kenelm CHILLINGLY 

 his adventures and opinion 


 by Edward Bulwer Lytton 

  LORD LYTTON 




 BOOK I. 



 CHAPTER I. 

 SIR PETER CHILLINGLY , of Exmundham , Baronet , F.R.S. and F.A.S. , be the 
 representative of an ancient family , and a land proprietor of some 
 importance . he have marry young ; not from any ardent inclination for 
 the connubial state , but in compliance with the request of his parent . 
 they take the pain to select his bride ; and if they might have choose 
 well , they might have choose bad , which be more than can be say for 
 many man who choose wife for themselves . Miss Caroline Brotherton be 
 in all respect a suitable connection . she have a pretty fortune , which 
 be of much use in buy a couple of farm , long desiderate by the 
 Chillinglys as necessary for the rounding of their property into a 
 ring - fence . she be highly connected , and bring into the county that 
 experience of fashionable life acquire by a young lady who have attend 
 a course of ball for three season , and go out in matrimonial 
 honour , with credit to herself and her chaperon . she be handsome 
 enough to satisfy a husband 's pride , but not so handsome as to keep 
 perpetually on the qui vive a husband 's jealousy . she be consider 
 highly accomplish ; that is , she play upon the pianoforte so that any 
 musician would say she " be very well teach ; " but no musician would 
 go out of his way to hear she a second time . she paint in 
 water - colours - well enough to amuse herself . she know french and italian 
 with an elegance so lady - like that , without have read more than 
 select extract from author in those language , she speak they both 
 with an accent more correct than we have any reason to attribute to 
 Rousseau or Ariosto . what else a young lady may acquire in order to be 
 style highly accomplished I do not pretend to know ; but I be sure that 
 the young lady in question fulfil that requirement in the opinion 
 of the good master . it be not only an eligible match for Sir 
 Peter Chillingly , - it be a brilliant match . it be also a very 
 unexceptionable match for Miss Caroline Brotherton . this excellent 
 couple get on together as most excellent couple do . a short time after 
 marriage , Sir Peter , by the death of his parent - who , have marry 
 their heir , have nothing leave in life worth the trouble of live 
 for - succeed to the hereditary estate ; he live for nine month of 
 the year at Exmundham , go to town for the other three month . Lady 
 Chillingly and himself be both very glad to go to town , be bore at 
 Exmundham ; and very glad to go back to Exmundham , be bore in town . 
 with one exception it be an exceedingly happy marriage , as marriage 
 go . Lady Chillingly have her way in small thing ; Sir Peter his way in 
 great . small thing happen every day ; great thing once in three year . 
 once in three year Lady Chillingly give way to Sir Peter ; household so 
 manage go on regularly . the exception to their connubial happiness be , 
 after all , but of a negative description . their affection be such 
 that they sigh for a pledge of it ; fourteen year have he and Lady 
 Chillingly remain unvisite by the little stranger . 

 now , in default of male issue , Sir Peter 's estate pass to a distant 
 cousin as heir - at - law ; and during the last four year this heir - at - law 
 have evince his belief that practically speak he be already 
 heir - apparent ; and   though Sir Peter be a much young man than 
 himself , and as healthy as any man well can be   have make his 
 expectation of a speedy succession unpleasantly conspicuous . he have 
 refuse his consent to a small exchange of land with a neighbouring 
 squire , by which Sir Peter would have obtain some good arable land , 
 for an outlying unprofitable wood that produce nothing but fagot and 
 rabbit , with the blunt declaration that he , the heir - at - law , be fond 
 of rabbit - shooting , and that the wood would be convenient to he next 
 season if he come into the property by that time , which he very possibly 
 might . he dispute Sir Peter 's right to make his customary fall of 
 timber , and have even threaten he with a bill in Chancery on that 
 subject . in short , this heir - at - law be exactly one of those person 
 to spite whom a land proprietor would , if single , marry at the age of 
 eighty in the hope of a family . 

 nor be it only on account of his very natural wish to frustrate the 
 expectation of this unamiable relation that Sir Peter Chillingly 
 lament the absence of the little stranger . although belong to that 
 class of country gentleman to whom certain political reasoner deny the 
 intelligence vouchsafe to other member of the community , Sir Peter be 
 not without a considerable degree of book - learning and a great taste 
 for speculative philosophy . he sigh for a legitimate inheritor to the 
 store of his erudition , and , be a very benevolent man , for a more 
 active and useful dispenser of those benefit to the human race which 
 philosopher confer by strike hard against each other ; just as , how 
 full soever of spark a flint may be , they might lurk conceal in the 
 flint till doomsday , if the flint be not hit by the steel . Sir Peter , 
 in short , long for a son amply endow with the combative quality , in 
 which he himself be deficient , but which be the first essential to all 
 seeker after renown , and especially to benevolent philosopher . 

 under these circumstance one may well conceive the joy that fill 
 the household of Exmundham and extend to all the tenantry on that 
 venerable estate , by whom the present possessor be much beloved and 
 the prospect of an heir - at - law with a special eye to the preservation 
 of rabbit much detest , when the medical attendant of the Chillinglys 
 declare that ' her ladyship be in an interesting way ; ' and to what 
 height that joy culminate when , in due course of time , a male baby be 
 safely entbrone in his cradle . to that cradle Sir Peter be summon . 
 he enter the room with a lively bind and a radiant countenance : he 
 quit it with a musing step and an overcloud brow . 

 yet the baby be no monster . it do not come into the world with two 
 head , as some baby be say to have do ; it be form as baby be 
 in general ; be on the whole a thrive baby , a fine baby . nevertheless , 
 its aspect awe the father as already it have awe the nurse . the 
 creature look so unutterably solemn . it fix its eye upon Sir Peter 
 with a melancholy reproachful stare ; its lip be compress and draw 
 downward as if discontentedly meditate its future destiny . the nurse 
 declare in a frightened whisper that it have utter no cry on face 
 the light . it have take possession of its cradle in all the dignity of 
 silent sorrow . a more saddened and a more thoughtful countenance a human 
 being could not exhibit if he be leave the world instead of enter 
 it . 

 " Hem ! " say Sir Peter to himself on regain the solitude of his 
 library ; " a philosopher who contribute a new inhabitant to this vale of 
 tear take upon himself very anxious responsibility - " 

 at that moment the joy - bell ring out from the neighbouring church 
 tower , the summer sun shine into the window , the bee hum among the 
 flower on the lawn . Sir Peter rouse himself and look forth , " after 
 all , " say he , cheerily , " the vale of tear be not without a smile . " 



 CHAPTER II . 

 a FAMILY council be hold at Exmundham Hall to deliberate on the name 
 by which this remarkable infant should be admit into the Christian 
 community . the junior branch of that ancient house consist , first , 
 of the obnoxious heir - at - law - a Scotch branch name Chillingly Gordon . 
 he be the widow father of one son , now of the age of three , and 
 happily unconscious of the injury inflict on his future prospect by 
 the advent of the new - bear , which could not be truthfully say of his 
 Caledonian father . Mr. Chillingly Gordon be one of those man who get on 
 in the world with out our be able to discover why . his parent die 
 in his infancy and leave he nothing ; but the family interest procure 
 he an admission into the Charterhouse School , at which illustrious 
 academy he obtain no remarkable distinction . nevertheless , as soon as 
 he leave it the State take he under its special care , and appoint he 
 to a clerkship in a public office . from that moment he continue to get 
 on in the world , and be now a Commissioner of Customs , with a salary of 
 L1500 a year . as soon as he have be thus enable to maintain a wife , 
 he select a wife who assist to maintain himself . she be an irish 
 peer 's widow , with a jointure of L2000 a year . 

 a few month after his marriage , Chillingly Gordon effect insurance 
 on his wife 's life , so as to secure himself an annuity of L1000 a year 
 in case of her decease . as she appear to be a fine healthy woman , some 
 year young than her husband , the deduction from his income effect 
 by the annual payment for the insurance seem an over - sacrifice of 
 present enjoyment to future contingency . the result bear witness to 
 his reputation for sagacity , as the lady die in the second year of 
 their wedding , a few month after the birth of her only child , and of a 
 heart - disease which have be latent to the doctor , but which , no doubt , 
 Gordon have affectionately discover before he have insure a life too 
 valuable not to need some compensation for its loss . he be now , then , 
 in the possession of L2500 a year , and be therefore very well off , 
 in the pecuniary sense of the phrase . he have , moreover , acquire a 
 reputation which give he a social rank beyond that accord to he by 
 a discerning State . he be consider a man of solid judgment , and 
 his opinion upon all matter , private and public , carry weight . the 
 opinion itself , critically examine , be not worth much , but the way he 
 announce it be impose . Mr. Fox say that ' no one ever be so wise as 
 Lord Thurlow look . ' Lord Thurlow could not have look wise than Mr. 
 Chillingly Gordon . he have a square jaw and large red bushy eyebrow , 
 which he lower down with great effect when he deliver judgment . 
 he have another advantage for acquire grave reputation . he be a very 
 unpleasant man . he could be rude if you contradict he ; and as few 
 person wish to provoke rudeness , so he be seldom contradict . 

 Mr. Chillingly Mivers , another cadet of the house , be also 
 distinguish , but in a different way . he be a bachelor , now about the 
 age of thirty - five . he be eminent for a supreme well - breed contempt for 
 everybody and everything . he be the originator and chief proprietor of 
 a public journal call " the Londoner , " which have lately be set up on 
 that principle of contempt , and we need not say , be exceedingly popular 
 with those lead member of the community who admire nobody and 
 believe in nothing . Mr. Chillingly Mivers be regard by himself and 
 by other as a man who might have achieve the high success in any 
 branch of literature , if he have deign to exhibit his talent therein . 
 but he do not so deign , and therefore he have full right to imply that , 
 if he have write an epic , a drama , a novel , a history , a metaphysical 
 treatise , Milton , Shakspeare , Cervantes , Hume , Berkeley would have be 
 nowhere . he hold greatly to the dignity of the anonymous ; and even in 
 the journal which he originate nobody could ever ascertain what he 
 write . but , at all event , Mr. Chillingly Mivers be what Mr. Chillingly 
 Gordon be not ; namely , a very clever man , and by no mean an unpleasant 
 one in general society . 

 the Rev. John Stalworth Chillingly be a decided adherent to the creed 
 of what be call " muscular Christianity , " and a very fine speciman 
 of it too . a tall stout man with broad shoulder , and that division of 
 low limb which intervene between the knee and the ankle powerfully 
 develop . he would have knock down a deist as soon as look at 
 he . it be tell by the Sieur de Joinville , in his Memoir of Louis , the 
 sainted king , that an assembly of divine and theologian convene the 
 Jews of an oriental city for the purpose of argue with they on the 
 truth of Christianity , and a certain knight , who be at that time 
 cripple , and support himself on crutch , ask and obtain 
 permission to be present at the debate . the Jews flock to the summon , 
 when a prelate , select a learn rabbi , mildly put to he the lead 
 question whether he own the divine conception of our Lord . " certainly 
 not , " reply the rabbi ; whereon the pious knight , shock by such 
 blasphemy , uplift his crutch and fell the rabbi , and then fling 
 himself among the other misbeliever , whom he soon disperse in 
 ignominious flight and in a very belabour condition . the conduct of 
 the knight be report to the sainted king , with a request that it 
 should be properly reprimand ; but the sainted king deliver himself 
 of this wise judgment : - 

 " if a pious knight be a very learn clerk , and can meet in fair 
 argument the doctrine of the misbeliever , by all mean let he argue 
 fairly ; but if a pious knight be not a learn clerk , and the argument 
 go against he , then let the pious knight cut the discussion short by 
 the edge of his good sword . " 

 the Rev. John Stalworth Chillingly be of the same opinion as Saint 
 Louis ; otherwise , he be a mild and amiable man . he encourage cricket 
 and other manly sport among his rural parishioner . he be a skilful 
 and bold rider , but he do not hunt ; a convivial man - and take his 
 bottle freely . but his taste in literature be of a refined and 
 peaceful character , contrast therein the tendency some might have 
 expect from his muscular development of Christianity . he be a great 
 reader of poetry , but he dislike Scott and Byron , whom he consider 
 flashy and noisy ; he maintain that Pope be only a versifier , and that 
 the great poet in the language be Wordsworth ; he do not care much 
 for the ancient classic ; he refuse all merit to the french poet ; he 
 know nothing of the italian , but he dabble in German , and be incline 
 to bore one about the " Hermann and Dorothea " of Goethe . he be marry 
 to a homely little wife , who revere he in silence , and think there 
 would be no schism in the church if he be in his right place as 
 Archbishop of Canterbury ; in this opinion he entirely agree with his 
 wife . 

 besides these three male specimen of the chillingly race , the fairer 
 sex be represent , in the absence of her ladyship , who still keep she 
 room , by three female Chillinglys , sister of Sir Peter , and all three 
 spinster . perhaps one reason why they have remain single be , that 
 externally they be so like each other that a suitor must have be 
 puzzle which to choose , and may have be afraid that if he do choose 
 one , he should be catch next day kiss another one in mistake . they 
 be all tall , all thin , with long throat - and beneath the throat a 
 fine development of bone . they have all pale hair , pale eyelid , pale 
 eye , and pale complexion . they all dress exactly alike , and their 
 favourite colour be a vivid green : they be so dressed on this 
 occasion . 

 as there be such similitude in their person , so , to an ordinary 
 observer , they be exactly the same in character and mind . very 
 well behave , with proper notion of female decorum : very distant and 
 reserve in manner to stranger ; very affectionate to each other and 
 their relation or favourite ; very good to the poor , whom they look 
 upon as a different order of creation , and treat with that sort of 
 benevolence which humane people bestow upon dumb animal . their mind 
 have be nourish on the same book - what one read the other have read . 
 the book be mainly divide into two class , - novel , and what they 
 call " good book . " they have a habit of take a speciman of each 
 alternately ; one day a novel , then a good book , then a novel again , and 
 so on . thus if the imagination be overwarme on Monday , on Tuesday it 
 be cool down to a proper temperature ; and if frost - bite on Tuesday , 
 it take a tepid bath on Wednesday . the novel they choose be indeed 
 rarely of a nature to raise the intellectual thermometer into blood 
 heat : the hero and heroine be model of correct conduct . Mr. 
 James 's novel be then in vogue , and they unite in say that those 
 " be novel a father might allow his daughter to read . " but though an 
 ordinary observer might have fail to recognize any distinction between 
 these three lady , and , find they habitually dress in green , would 
 have say they be as much alike as one pea be to another , they have 
 their idiosyncratic difference , when duly examine . Miss Margaret , the 
 eld , be the command one of the three ; it be she who regulate 
 their household   they all live together , keep the joint purse , and 
 decide every doubtful point that arise : whether they should or should 
 not ask Mrs. So - and - so to tea ; whether Mary should or should not be 
 discharge ; whether or not they should go to Broadstairs or to Sandgate 
 for the month of October . in fact , Miss Margaret be the WILL of the 
 body corporate . 

 Miss Sibyl be of milder nature and more melancholy temperament ; she have 
 a poetic turn of mind , and occasionally write verse . some of these 
 have be print on satin paper , and sell for object of beneficence 
 at charity bazaar . the county newspaper say that the verse " be 
 characterize by all the elegance of a cultured and feminine mind . " the 
 other two sister agree that Sibyl be the genius of the household , 
 but , like all genius , not sufficiently practical for the world . 
 Miss Sarah Chillingly , the young of the three , and now just in her 
 forty - fourth year , be look upon by the other as " a dear thing , 
 inclined to be naughty , but such a darling that nobody could have the 
 heart to scold she . " Miss Margaret say ' she be a giddy creature . " Miss 
 Sibyl write a poem on she , entitle , " warn to a young Lady against 
 the Pleasures of the World . " they all call she Sally ; the other 
 two sister have no diminutive synonyms . Sally be a name indicative of 
 fastness . but this Sally would not have be think fast in another 
 household , and she be now little likely to sally out of the one she 
 belong to . these sister , who be all many year old than Sir 
 Peter , live in a handsome , old - fashioned , red - brick house , with a large 
 garden at the back , in the principal street of the capital of their 
 native county . they have each L10,000 for portion ; and if he could have 
 marry all three , the heir - at - law would have marry they , and settle 
 the aggregate L30,000 on himself . but we have not yet come to recognize 
 Mormonism as legal , though if our social progress continue to slide in 
 the same groove as at present , Heaven only know what triumph over 
 the prejudice of our ancestor may not be achieve by the wisdom of our 
 descendant ! 



 CHAPTER III . 

 SIR PETER stand on his hearthstone , survey the guest seat in 
 semicircle , and say : " friend , - in Parliament , before anything 
 affect the fate of a Bill be discuss , it be , I believe , necessary 
 to introduce the Bill . " he pause a moment , rang the bell , and say to 
 the servant who enter , " tell Nurse to bring in the Baby . " 

 Mr. CHILLINGLY GORDON . - " I do not   see the necessity for that , Sir Peter . 
 we may take the existence of the Baby for grant . " 

 Mr. MIVERS . - " it be an advantage to the reputation of Sir Peter 's work 
 to preserve the incognito . Omne ignotum pro magnifico . " 

 the REV . JOHN STALWORTH CHILLINGLY . - " I do not   approve the cynical 
 levity of such remark . of course we must all be anxious to see , in the 
 early stage of be , the future representative of our name and race . 
 who would not wish to contemplate the source , however small , of the 
 Tigris or the Nile ! - " 

 MISS SALLY   titter . - " he ! he ! " 

 MISS MARGARET . - " for shame , you giddy thing ! " 

 the Baby enter in the nurse 's arm . all rise and gather round the Baby 
 with one exception , - Mr. Gordon , who have cease to be heir - at - law . 

 the Baby return the gaze of its relation with the most contemptuous 
 indifference . Miss Sibyl be the first to pronounce an opinion on the 
 Baby 's attribute . say she , in a solemn whisper , " what a heavenly 
 mournful expression ! it seem so grieve to have leave the angel ! " 

 the REV . JOHN . - " that be prettily say , Cousin Sibyl ; but the infant 
 must pluck up its courage and fight its way among mortal with a good 
 heart , if it want to get back to the angel again . and I think it will ; 
 a fine child . " he take it from the nurse , and move it deliberately up 
 and down , as if to weigh it , say cheerfully , " monstrous heavy ! by the 
 time it be twenty it will be a match for a prize - fighter of fifteen 
 stone ! " 

 Therewith he stride to Gordon , who as if to show that he now consider 
 himself wholly apart from all interest in the affair of a family who 
 have so ill - treat he in the birth of that Baby , have take up the 
 " Times " newspaper and conceal his countenance beneath the ample sheet . 
 the Parson abruptly snatch away the " Times " with one hand , and , 
 with the other substituting to the indignant eye of the ci - devant 
 heir - at - law the spectacle of the Baby , say , " kiss it . " 

 " kiss it ! " echo Chillingly Gordon , push back his chair - " kiss 
 it ! pooh , sir , stand off ! I never kiss my own baby : I shall not kiss 
 another man 's . take the thing away , sir : it be ugly ; it have black eye . " 

 Sir Peter , who be near - sighted , put on his spectacle and examine 
 the face of the new - bear . " true , " say he , " it have black eye , - very 
 extraordinary : portentous : the first Chillingly that ever have black 
 eye . " 

 " its mamma have black eye , " say Miss Margaret : " it take after its 
 mamma ; it have not the fair beauty of the Chillinglys , but it be not 
 ugly . " 

 " sweet infant ! " sigh Sibyl ; " and so good ; do not cry . " 

 " it have neither cry nor crow since it be bear , " say the nurse ; 
 " bless its little heart . " 

 she take the Baby from the Parson 's arm , and smooth back the frill of 
 its cap , which have get ruffle . 

 " you may go now , Nurse , " say Sir Peter . 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 " I agree with Mr. Shandy , " say Sir Peter , resume his stand on the 
 hearthstone , " that among the responsibility of a parent the choice of 
 the name which his child be to bear for life be one of the grave . and 
 this be especially so with those who belong to the order of baronet . 
 in the case of a peer his christian name , fuse into his titular 
 designation , disappear . in the case of a Mister , if his baptismal 
 be cacophonous or provocative of ridicule , he need not ostentatiously 
 parade it : he may drop it altogether on his visit card , and may be 
 imprint as Mr. Jones instead of Mr. Ebenezer Jones . in his signature , 
 save where the form of the law demand Ebenezer in full , he may only 
 use an initial and be your obedient servant E. Jones , leave it to be 
 conjecture that E. stand for Edward or Ernest , - name inoffensive , and 
 not suggestive of a Dissenting Chapel , like Ebenezer . if a man call 
 Edward or Ernest be detect in some youthful indiscretion , there be 
 no indelible stain on his moral character : but if an Ebenezer be so 
 detect he be set down as a hypocrite ; it produce that shock on the 
 public mind which be feel when a profess saint be prove to be a 
 bit of a sinner . but a baronet never can escape from his baptismal : it 
 can not lie perdu ; it can not shrink into an initial , it stand forth 
 glaringly in the light of day ; christen he Ebenezer , and he be Sir 
 Ebenezer in full , with all its perilous consequence if he ever succumb 
 to those temptation to which even baronet be expose . but , my 
 friend , it be not only the effect that the sound of a name have upon 
 other which be to be thoughtfully consider : the effect that his name 
 produce on the man himself be perhaps still more important . some name 
 stimulate and encourage the owner ; other deject and paralyze he : I 
 be a melancholy instance of that truth . Peter have be for many 
 generation , as you be aware , the baptismal to which the eldest - bear 
 of our family have be devote . on the altar of that name I have be 
 sacrifice . never have there be a Sir Peter Chillingly who have , in any 
 way , distinguish himself above his fellow . that name have be a dead 
 weight on my intellectual energy . in the catalogue of illustrious 
 englishman there be , I think , no immortal Sir Peter , except Sir Peter 
 Teazle , and he only exist on the comic stage . " 

 MISS sibyl . - " Sir Peter Lely ? " 

 SIR PETER CHILLINGLY . - " that painter be not an Englishman . he be bear 
 in Westphalia , famous for ham . I confine my remark to the child of 
 our native land . I be aware that in foreign country the name be not an 
 extinguisher to the genius of its owner . but why ? in other country its 
 sound be modify . Pierre Corneille be a great man ; but I put it to 
 you whether , have he be an Englishman , he could have be the father of 
 european tragedy as Peter Crow ? " 

 MISS sibyl . - " impossible ! " 

 MISS SALLY . - " he ! he ! " 

 MISS MARGARET . - " there be nothing to laugh at , you giddy child ! " 

 SIR PETER . - " my son shall not be petrify into Peter . " 

 MR . CHILLINGLY GORDON . - " if a man be such a fool - and I do not   say your 
 son will not be a fool , Cousin Peter - as to be influence by the sound 
 of his own name , and you want the booby to turn the world topsy - turvy , 
 you have well call he Julius Caesar or Hannibal or Attila or 
 Charlemagne . " 

 SIR PETER ,   who excel mankind in imperturbability of temper . - " on the 
 contrary , if you inflict upon a man the burden of one of those name , 
 the glory of which he can not reasonably expect to eclipse or even to 
 equal , you crush he beneath the weight . if a poet be call John 
 Milton or William Shakspeare , he could not dare to publish even a 
 sonnet . no : the choice of a name lie between the two extreme of 
 ludicrous insignificance and oppressive renown . for this reason I have 
 order the family pedigree to be suspend on yonder wall . let we 
 examine it with care , and see whether , among the Chillinglys themselves 
 or their alliance , we can discover a name that can be bear with 
 become dignity by the destine head of our house - a name neither too 
 light nor too heavy . " 

 Sir Peter here lead the way to the family tree - a goodly roll of 
 parchment , with the arm of the family emblazon at the top . those arm 
 be simple , as ancient heraldic coat be , - three fish argent on 
 a field azure ; the crest a mermaid 's head . all flock to inspect the 
 pedigree except Mr. Gordon , who resume the " Times " newspaper . 

 " I never could quite make out what kind of fish these be , " say 
 the Rev. John Stalworth . " they be certainly not pike which form the 
 emblematic blazon of the hotoft , and be still grim enough to frighten 
 future Shakspeares on the scutcheon of the Warwickshire Lucys . " 

 " I believe they be tench , " say Mr. Mivers . " the tench be a fish that 
 know how to keep itself safe by a philosophical taste for an obscure 
 existence in deep hole and slush . " 

 SIR PETER . - " no , Mivers ; the fish be dace , a fish that , once 
 introduce into any pond , never can be get out again . you may drag 
 the water ; you may let off the water ; you may say , ' those dace be 
 extirpate , ' - vain thought ! - the dace reappear as before ; and in this 
 respect the arm be really emblematic of the family . all the disorder 
 and revolution that have occur in England since the Heptarchy have 
 leave the Chillinglys the same race in the same place . somehow or other 
 the Norman Conquest do not despoil they ; they hold fief under Eudo 
 Dapifer as peacefully as they have hold they under King Harold ; they take 
 no part in the Crusades , nor the Wars of the Roses , nor the Civil Wars 
 between Charles the First and the Parliament . as the dace stick to the 
 water and the water stick by the dace , so the Chillinglys stick to the 
 land and the land stick by the Chillinglys . perhaps I be wrong to wish 
 that the new Chillingly may be a little less like a dace . " 

 " oh ! " cry Miss Margaret , who , mount on a chair , have be inspect 
 the pedigree through an eye - glass , " I do not   see a fine christian name 
 from the beginning , except Oliver . " 

 SIR PETER . - " that chillingly be bear in Oliver Cromwell 's Protectorate , 
 and name Oliver in compliment to he , as his father , bear in the reign 
 of James I. , be christen James . the three fish always swam with 
 the stream . oliver ! - Oliver not a bad name , but significant of radical 
 doctrine . " 

 Mr. MIVERS . - " I do not   think so . Oliver Cromwell make short work of 
 radical and their doctrine ; but perhaps we can find a name less awful 
 and revolutionary . " 

 " I have it ! I have it ! " cry the Parson . " here be a descent from Sir 
 Kenelm Digby and Venetia Stanley . Sir Kenelm Digby ! no fine speciman of 
 muscular Christianity . he fight as well as he write ; eccentric , it be 
 true , but always a gentleman . call the boy Kenelm ! " 

 " a sweet name , " say Miss Sibyl : " it breathe of romance . " 

 " Sir Kenelm Chillingly ! it sound well , - imposing ! " say Miss Margaret . 

 " and , " remark Mr. Mivers , " it have this advantage - that while it have 
 sufficient association with honourable distinction to affect the mind of 
 the namesake and rouse his emulation , it be not that of so stupendous 
 a personage as to defy rivalry . Sir Kenelm Digby be certainly an 
 accomplished and gallant gentleman ; but what with his silly superstition 
 about sympathetic powder , etc . , any man nowadays might be clever in 
 comparison without be a prodigy . yes , let we decide on Kenelm . " 

 Sir Peter meditate . " certainly , " say he , after a pause , " certainly 
 the name of Kenelm carry with it very crotchety association ; and I be 
 afraid that Sir Kenelm Digby do not make a prudent choice in marriage . 
 the fair Venetia be no well than she should be ; and I should wish 
 my heir not to be lead away by beauty but we d a woman of respectable 
 character and decorous conduct . " 

 Miss MARGARET . - " a british matron , of course ! " 

 three sister   in chorus . - " of course ! of course ! " 

 " but , " resume Sir Peter , " I be crotchety myself , and crotchet be 
 innocent thing enough ; and as for marriage the Baby can not marry 
 to - morrow , so that we have ample time to consider that matter . Kenelm 
 Digby be a man any family might be proud of ; and , as you say , sister 
 Margaret , Kenelm Chillingly do not sound amiss : Kenelm Chillingly it 
 shall be ! " 

 the Baby be accordingly christen Kenelm , after which ceremony its 
 face grow long than before . 



 CHAPTER V. 

 before his relation disperse , Sir Peter summon Mr. Gordon into his 
 library . 

 " Cousin , " say he , kindly , " I do not blame you for the want of family 
 affection , or even of humane interest , which you exhibit towards the 
 New - bear . " 

 " blame I , Cousin Peter ! I should think not . I exhibit as much 
 family affection and humane interest as could be expect from 
 I , - circumstance consider . " 

 " I own , " say Sir Peter , with all his wonte mildness , " that after 
 remain childless for fourteen year of wed life , the advent of 
 this little stranger must have occasion you a disagreeable surprise . 
 but , after all , as I be many year young than you , and in the course 
 of nature shall outlive you , the loss be less to yourself than to your 
 son , and upon that I wish to say a few word . you know too well the 
 condition on which I hold my estate not to be aware that I have 
 not legally the power to saddle it with any bequest to your boy . the 
 New - bear succeed to the fee - simple as last in tail . but I intend , 
 from this moment , to lay by something every year for your son out of my 
 income ; and , fond as I be of London for a part of the year , I shall now 
 give up my town - house . if I live to the year the Psalmist allot to 
 man , I shall thus accumulate something handsome for your son , which may 
 be take in the way of compensation . " 

 Mr. Gordon be by no mean soften by this generous speech . however , 
 he answer more politely than be his will not , " my son will be very much 
 oblige to you , should he ever need your intend bequest . " pause a 
 moment , he add with a cheerful smile , " a large percentage of infant 
 die before attain the age of twenty - one . " 

 " Nay , but I be tell your son be an uncommonly fine healthy child . " 

 " my son , Cousin Peter ! I be not think of my son , but of your . your 
 have a big head . I should not wonder if he have water in it . I do not   wish 
 to alarm you , but he may go off any day , and in that case it be not 
 likely that Lady Chillingly will condescend to replace he . so you will 
 excuse I if I still keep a watchful eye on my right ; and , however 
 painful to my feeling , I must still dispute your right to cut a stick 
 of the field timber . " 

 " that be nonsense , Gordon . I be tenant for life without impeachment of 
 waste , and can cut down all timber not ornamental . " 

 " I advise you not , Cousin Peter . I have tell you before that I shall try 
 the question at law , should you provoke it , amicably , of course . right 
 be right ; and if I be drive to maintain mine , I trust that you be of 
 a mind too liberal to allow your family affection for I and mine to be 
 influence by a decree of the Court of Chancery . but my fly be wait . 
 I must not miss the train . " 

 " well , good - by , Gordon . shake hand . " 

 " shake hand ! - of course , of course . by the by , as I come through the 
 lodge , it seem to I sadly out of repair . I believe you be liable for 
 dilapidation . good - by . " 

 " the man be a hog in armour , " soliloquize Sir Peter , when his cousin 
 be go ; " and if it be hard to drive a common pig in the way he do not 
 choose to go , a hog in armour be indeed undrivable . but his boy ought 
 not to suffer for his father 's hoggishness ; and I shall begin at once to 
 see what I can lay by for he . after all , it be hard upon Gordon . poor 
 Gordon ; poor fellow ! poor fellow ! still I hope he will not go to law 
 with I . I hate law . and a worm will turn , especially a worm that be put 
 into Chancery . " 



 CHAPTER VI . 

 despite the sinister semi - prediction of the ci - devant heir - at - law , 
 the youthful Chillingly pass with safety , and indeed with dignity , 
 through the infant stage of existence . he take his measle and 
 whooping - cough with philosophical equanimity . he gradually acquire 
 the use of speech , but he do not too lavishly exercise that special 
 attribute of humanity . during the early year of childhood he speak 
 as little as if he have be prematurely train in the school of 
 Pythagoras . but he evidently speak the less in order to reflect the 
 more . he observe closely and ponder deeply over what he observe . at 
 the age of eight he begin to converse more freely , and it be in that 
 year that he startle his mother with the question , " Mamma , be you not 
 sometimes overpower by the sense of your own identity ? " 

 Lady Chillingly , - I be about to say rush , but Lady chillingly never 
 rush , - Lady Chillingly glide less sedately than she will not to Sir 
 Peter , and repeat her son 's question , say , " the boy be grow 
 troublesome , too wise for any woman : he must go to school . " 

 Sir Peter be of the same opinion . but where on earth do the child get 
 hold of so long a word as " identity , " and how do so extraordinary and 
 puzzle a metaphysical question come into his head ? Sir Peter summon 
 Kenelm , and ascertain that the boy , have free access to the library , 
 have fasten upon Locke on the Human Understanding , and be prepared to 
 dispute with that philosopher upon the doctrine of innate idea . Quoth 
 Kenelm , gravely , " a want be an idea ; and if , as soon as I be bear , I 
 feel the want of food and know at once where to turn for it , without 
 be teach , surely I come into the world with an ' innate idea . ' " 

 Sir Peter , though he dabble in metaphysics , be pose , and scratch 
 his head without get out a proper answer as to the distinction 
 between idea and instinct . " my child , " he say at last , " you do not 
 know what you be talk about : go and take a good gallop on your black 
 pony ; and I forbid you to read any book that be not give to you by 
 myself or your mamma . Stick to ' Puss in Boots . ' " 



 CHAPTER VII . 

 SIR PETER order his carriage and drive to the house of the stout 
 parson . that doughty ecclesiastic hold a family live a few mile 
 distant from the Hall , and be the only one of the cousin with whom Sir 
 Peter habitually commune on his domestic affair . 

 he find the Parson in his study , which exhibit taste other 
 than clerical . over the chimney - piece be range fencing - foil , 
 boxing - glove , and staff for the athletic exercise of single - stick ; 
 cricket - bat and fishing - rod fill up the angle . there be 
 sundry print on the wall : one of Mr. Wordsworth , flank by two of 
 distinguished race - horse ; one of a Leicestershire short - horn , with 
 which the Parson , who farm his own glebe and breed cattle in its rich 
 pasture , have win a prize at the county show ; and on either side of that 
 animal be the portrait of Hooker and Jeremy Taylor . there be dwarf 
 book - case contain miscellaneous work very handsomely bound ; at 
 the open window , a stand of flower - pot , the flower in full bloom . the 
 Parson 's flower be famous . 

 the appearance of the whole room be that of a man who be tidy and neat 
 in his habit . 

 " Cousin , " say Sir Peter , " I have come to consult you . " and therewith he 
 relate the marvellous precocity of Kenelm Chillingly . " you see the name 
 begin to work on he rather too much . he must go to school ; and now 
 what school shall it be ? private or public ? " 

 the REV . JOHN STALWORTH . - " there be a great deal to be say for or 
 against either . at a public school the chance be that Kenelm will 
 no long be overpower by a sense of his own identity ; he will more 
 probably lose identity altogether . the bad of a public school be that 
 a sort of common character be substitute for individual character . 
 the master , of course , can not   attend to the separate development of each 
 boy 's idiosyncrasy . all mind be throw into one great mould , and come 
 out of it more or less in the same form . an Etonian may be clever or 
 stupid , but , as either , he remain emphatically etonian . a public school 
 ripen talent , but its tendency be to stifle genius . then , too , a public 
 school for an only son , heir to a good estate , which will be entirely at 
 his own disposal , be apt to encourage reckless and extravagant habit ; 
 and your estate require careful management , and leave no margin for an 
 heir 's note - of - hand and post - obit . on the whole , I be against a public 
 school for Kenelm . " 

 " well then , we will decide on a private one . " 

 " hold ! " say the Parson : " a private school have its drawback . you can 
 seldom produce large fish in small pond . in private school the 
 competition be narrow , the energy stint . the schoolmaster 's wife 
 interfere , and generally coddle the boy . there be not manliness 
 enough in those academy ; no fagging , and very little fighting . a 
 clever boy turn out a prig ; a boy of feebler intellect turn out a 
 well - behave young lady in trouser . nothing muscular in the system . 
 decidedly the namesake and descendant of Kenelm Digby should not go to a 
 private seminary . " 

 " so far as I gather from your reasoning , " say Sir Peter , with 
 characteristic placidity , " Kenelm Chillingly be not to go to school at 
 all . " 

 " it do look like it , " say the Parson , candidly ; " but , on 
 consideration , there be a medium . there be school which unite the good 
 quality of public and private school , large enough to stimulate and 
 develop energy mental and physical , yet not so framed as to melt all 
 character in one crucible . for instance , there be a school which have 
 at this moment one of the first scholar in Europe for head - master , - a 
 school which have turn out some of the most remarkable man of the 
 rise generation . the master see at a glance if a boy be clever , and 
 take pain with he accordingly . he be not a mere teacher of hexameter 
 and sapphic . his learning embrace all literature , ancient and modern . 
 he be a good writer and a fine critic ; admire Wordsworth . he wink at 
 fight : his boy know how to use their fist ; and they be not in the 
 habit of sign post - obit before they be fifteen . Merton School be 
 the place for Kenelm . " 

 " thank you , " say Sir Peter . " it be a great comfort in life to find 
 somebody who can decide for one . I be an irresolute man myself , and in 
 ordinary matter willingly let Lady chillingly govern I . " 

 " I should like to see a wife govern I , " say the stout Parson . 

 " but you be not married to Lady Chillingly . and now let we go into the 
 garden and look at your dahlia . " 



 CHAPTER VIII . 

 the youthful confuter of Locke be despatch to Merton School , and 
 rank , accord to his merit , as lag of the penultimate form . when he 
 come home for the Christmas holiday he be more saturnine than ever ; 
 in fact , his countenance bear the impression of some absorbing grief . 
 he say , however , that he like school very well , and elude all other 
 question . but early the next morning he mount his black pony and 
 ride to the Parson 's rectory . the reverend gentleman be in his farmyard 
 examine his bullock when Kenelm accost he thus briefly , - 

 " Sir , I be disgrace , and I shall die of it if you can not help to set I 
 right in my own eye . " 

 " my dear boy , do not   talk in that way . come into my study . " 

 as soon as they enter that room , and the Parson have carefully close 
 the door , he take the boy 's arm , turn he round to the light , and see 
 at once that there be something very grave on his mind . chuck he 
 under the chin , the Parson say cheerily , " hold up your head , Kenelm . I 
 be sure you have do nothing unworthy of a gentleman . " 

 " I do not   know that . I fight a boy very little big than myself , and 
 I have be lick . I do not give in , though ; but the other boy pick 
 I up , for I could not stand any long ; and the fellow be a great 
 bully ; and his name be Butt ; and he be the son of a lawyer ; and he get my 
 head into chancery ; and I have challenge he to fight again next 
 half ; and unless you can help I to lick he , I shall never be good for 
 anything in the world , - never . it will break my heart . " 

 " I be very glad to hear you have have the pluck to challenge he . just 
 let I see how you double your fist . well , that be not amiss . now , put 
 yourself into a fighting attitude , and hit out at I , - hard ! hard ! 
 Pooh ! that will never do . you should make your blow as straight as 
 an arrow . and that be not the way to stand . stop , - so : well on your 
 haunch ; weight on the left leg ; good ! now , put on these glove , and 
 I will give you a lesson in boxing . " 

 five minute afterwards Mrs. John Chillingly , enter the room to 
 summon her husband to breakfast , stand astounded to see he with his 
 coat off , and parry the blow of Kenelm , who fly at he like a young 
 tiger . the good pastor at that moment might certainly have appear a 
 fine type of muscular Christianity , but not of that kind of Christianity 
 out of which one make Archbishops of Canterbury . 

 " good gracious I ! " falter Mrs. John Chillingly ; and then , wife - like , 
 fly to the protection of her husband , she seize Kenelm by the 
 shoulder , and give he a good shaking . the Parson , who be sadly out 
 of breath , be not displeased at the interruption , but take that 
 opportunity to put on his coat , and say , " we will begin again to - morrow . 
 now , come to breakfast . " but during breakfast Kenelm 's face still 
 betray dejection , and he talk little and eat less . 

 as soon as the meal be over , he draw the Parson into the garden and 
 say , " I have be think , sir , that perhaps it be not fair to Butt 
 that I should be take these lesson ; and if it be not fair , I would rather 
 not - " 

 " give I your hand , my boy ! " cry the Parson , transport . " the name 
 of Kenelm be not throw away upon you . the natural desire of man in 
 his attribute of fight animal   an attribute in which , I believe , he 
 excel all other animate being , except a quail and a gamecock   be to 
 beat his adversary . but the natural desire of that culmination of man 
 which we call gentleman be to beat his adversary fairly . a gentleman 
 would rather be beat fairly than beat unfairly . be not that your 
 thought ? " 

 " yes , " reply Kenelm , firmly ; and then , begin to philosophize , he 
 add , " and it stand to reason ; because if I beat a fellow unfairly , I 
 do not   really beat he at all . " 

 " Excellent ! but suppose that you and another boy go into examination 
 upon Caesar 's Commentaries or the multiplication table , and the other 
 boy be clever than you , but you have take the trouble to learn the 
 subject and he have not : should you say you beat he unfairly ? " 

 Kenelm meditate a moment , and then say decidedly , " no . " 

 " that which apply to the use of your brain apply equally to the use 
 of your fist . do you comprehend I ? " 

 " yes , sir ; I do now . " 

 " in the time of your namesake , Sir Kenelm Digby , gentleman wear sword , 
 and they learn how to use they , because , in case of quarrel , they have 
 to fight with they . nobody , at least in England , fight with sword 
 now . it be a democratic age , and if you fight at all , you be reduce to 
 fist ; and if Kenelm Digby learn to fence , so Kenelm Chillingly must 
 learn to box ; and if a gentleman thrash a drayman twice his size , who 
 have not learn to box , it be not unfair ; it be but an exemplification 
 of the truth that knowledge be power . come and take another lesson on 
 box to - morrow . " 

 Kenelm remount his pony and return home . he find his father 
 saunter in the garden with a book in his hand . " Papa , " say Kenelm , 
 " how do one gentleman write to another with whom he have a quarrel , 
 and he do not   want to make it up , but he have something to say about the 
 quarrel which it be fair the other gentleman should know ? " 

 " I do not   understand what you mean . " 

 " well , just before I go to school I remember hear you say that you 
 have a quarrel with Lord Hautfort , and that he be an ass , and you would 
 write and tell he so . when you write do you say , ' you be an ass ' ? be 
 that the way one gentleman write to another ? " 

 " upon my honour , Kenelm , you ask very odd question . but you can not 
 learn too early this fact , that irony be to the high - breed what 
 billingsgate be to the vulgar ; and when one gentleman think another 
 gentleman an ass , he do not say it point - blank : he imply it in the 
 politest term he can invent . Lord Hautfort deny my right of free 
 warren over a trout - stream that run through his land . I do not   care a 
 rush about the trout - stream , but there be no doubt of my right to fish 
 in it . he be an ass to raise the question ; for , if he have not , I 
 should not have exercise the right . as he do raise the question , I be 
 oblige to catch his trout . " 

 " and you write a letter to he ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " how do you write , Papa ? what do you say ? " 

 " something like this . ' Sir Peter Chillingly present his compliment 
 to Lord Hautfort , and think it fair to his lordship to say that he have 
 take the good legal advice with regard to his right of free warren ; 
 and trust to be forgive if he presume to suggest that Lord Hautfort 
 might do well to consult his own lawyer before he decide on dispute 
 they . ' " 

 " thank you , Papa . I see . " 

 that evening Kenelm write the following letter : - 


 Mr. Chillingly present his compliment to Mr. Butt , and think it fair 
 to Mr. Butt to say that he be take lesson in boxing ; and trust to be 
 forgive if he presume to suggest that Mr. Butt might do well to take 
 lesson himself before fight with Mr. Chillingly next half . 


 " Papa , " say Kenelm the next morning , " I want to write to a schoolfellow 
 whose name be Butt ; he be the son of a lawyer who be call a serjeant . 
 I do not   know where to direct to he . " 

 " that be easily ascertain , " say Sir Peter . " Serjeant Butt be an 
 eminent man , and his address will be in the Court Guide . " 

 the address be find , - Bloomsbury Square ; and Kenelm direct his 
 letter accordingly . in due course he receive this answer , - 


 you be an insolent little fool , and I will thrash you within an inch of 
 your life . 

 ROBERT BUTT . 


 after the receipt of that polite epistle , Kenelm Chillingly 's scruple 
 vanish , and he take daily lesson in muscular Christianity . 

 Kenelm return to school with a brow clear from care , and three day 
 after his return he write to the Reverend John , - 


 DEAR SIR , - I have lick Butt . Knowledge be power . 

 your affectionate KENELM . 

 P. S. - now that I have lick Butt , I have make it up with he . 


 from that time Kenelm prosper . eulogistic letter from the illustrious 
 head - master shower in upon Sir Peter . at the age of sixteen Kenelm 
 Chillingly be the head of the school , and , quit it finally , 
 bring home the follow letter from his Orbilius to Sir Peter , mark 
 " confidential " : - 


 DEAR SIR PETER CHILLINGLY , - I have never feel more anxious for the 
 future career of any of my pupil than I do for that of your son . he be 
 so clever that , with ease to himself , he may become a great man . he be 
 so peculiar that it be quite as likely that he may only make himself 
 know to the world as a great oddity . that distinguished teacher Dr. 
 Arnold say that the difference between one boy and another be not so 
 much talent as energy . your son have talent , have energy : yet he want 
 something for success in life ; he want the faculty of amalgamation . he 
 be of a melancholic and therefore unsocial temperament . he will not act 
 in concert with other . he be lovable enough : the other boy like he , 
 especially the small one , with whom he be a sort of hero ; but he 
 have not one intimate friend . so far as school learning be concern , 
 he might go to college at once , and with the certainty of distinction 
 provide he choose to exert himself . but if I may venture to offer an 
 advice , I should say employ the next two year in let he see 
 a little more of real life and acquire a due sense of its practical 
 object . send he to a private tutor who be not a pedant , but a man 
 of letter or a man of the world , and if in the metropolis so much the 
 well . in a word , my young friend be unlike other people ; and , with 
 quality that might do anything in life , I fear , unless you can get 
 he to be like other people , that he will do nothing . excuse the freedom 
 with which I write , and ascribe it to the singular interest with which 
 your son have inspire I . I have the honour to be , dear Sir Peter , 

 your truly , WILLIAM HORTON . 


 upon the strength of this letter Sir Peter do not indeed summon another 
 family council ; for he do not consider that his three maiden sister 
 could offer any practical advice on the matter . and as to Mr. Gordon , 
 that gentleman have go to law on the great timber question , and 
 have be signally beat thereon , have inform Sir Peter that he 
 disown he as a cousin and despise he as a man ; not exactly in those 
 word , - more covertly , and therefore more stingingly . but Sir Peter 
 invite Mr. Mivers for a week 's shooting , and request the Reverend 
 John to meet he . 

 Mr. Mivers arrive . the sixteen year that have elapse since he be 
 first introduce to the reader have make no perceptible change in his 
 appearance . it be one of his maxim that in youth a man of the world 
 should appear old than he be ; and in middle age , and thence to his 
 die day , young . and he announce one secret for attain that art 
 in these word : " begin your wig early , thus you never become gray . " 

 unlike most philosopher , Mivers make his practice conform to his 
 precept ; and while in the prime of youth inaugurate a wig in a 
 fashion that defy the flight of time , not curly and hyacinthine , but 
 straight - haired and unassuming . he look five - and - thirty from the day 
 he put on that wig at the age of twenty - five . he look five - and - thirty 
 now at the age of fifty - one . 

 " I mean , " say he , " to remain thirty - five all my life . no well age to 
 stick at . People may choose to say I be more , but I shall not own it . no 
 one be bind to criminate himself . " 

 Mr. Mivers have some other aphorism on this important subject . one 
 be , " refuse to be ill . never tell people you be ill ; never own it to 
 yourself . illness be one of those thing which a man should resist on 
 principle at the onset . it should never be allow to get in the thin 
 end of the wedge . but take care of your constitution , and , have 
 ascertain the good habit for it , keep to they like clockwork . " Mr. 
 Mivers would not have miss his constitutional walk in the Park before 
 breakfast if , by go in a cab to St. Giles 's , he could have save the 
 city of London from conflagration . 

 another aphorism of his be , " if you want to keep young , live in a 
 metropolis ; never stay above a few week at a time in the country . take 
 two man of similar constitution at the age of twenty - five ; let one live 
 in London and enjoy a regular sort of club life ; send the other to some 
 rural district , preposterously call ' salubrious . ' look at these man 
 when they have both reach the age of forty - five . the London man have 
 preserve his figure : the rural man have a paunch . the London man have 
 an interesting delicacy of complexion : the face of the rural man be 
 coarse - grain and perhaps jowly . " 

 a third axiom be , " do not   be a family man ; nothing age one like 
 matrimonial felicity and paternal tie . never multiply care , and pack 
 up your life in the brief compass you can . why add to your carpet - bag 
 of trouble the content of a lady 's imperial and bonnet - box , and the 
 travel fourgon require by the nursery ? Shun ambition : it be so 
 gouty . it take a great deal out of a man 's life , and give he nothing 
 worth have till he have cease to enjoy it . " another of his aphorism 
 be this , " a fresh mind keep the body fresh . take in the idea of the 
 day , drain off those of yesterday . as to the morrow , time enough to 
 consider it when it become to - day . " 

 preserve himself by attention to these rule , Mr. Mivers appear at 
 Exmundham totus , tere , but not rotundus , - a man of middle height , 
 slender , upright , with well - cut , small , slight feature , thin lip , 
 enclose an excellent set of tooth , even , white , and not indebte 
 to the dentist . for the sake of those tooth he shun acid wine , 
 especially hock in all its variety , culinary sweet , and hot drink . 
 he drink even his tea cold . 

 " there be , " he say , " two thing in life that a sage must preserve at 
 every sacrifice , the coat of his stomach and the enamel of his tooth . 
 some evil admit of consolation : there be no comforter for dyspepsia 
 and toothache . " a man of letter , but a man of the world , he have so 
 cultivate his mind as both that he be fear as the one and like as 
 the other . as a man of letter he despise the world ; as a man of the 
 world he despise letter . as the representative of both he revere 
 himself . 



 CHAPTER IX . 

 on the evening of the third day from the arrival of Mr. Mivers , he , the 
 Parson , and Sir Peter be seat in the host 's parlour , the Parson in 
 an armchair by the ingle , smoke a short cutty - pipe ; miver at length 
 on the couch , slowly inhale the perfume of one of his own choice 
  trabuco . Sir Peter never smoke . there be spirit and hot water and 
 lemon on the table . the Parson be fame for skill in the composition 
 of toddy . from time to time the Parson sip his glass , and Sir Peter 
 less frequently do the same . it be needless to say that Mr. Mivers 
 eschew toddy ; but beside he , on a chair , be a tumbler and a large 
 carafe of iced water . 

 SIR PETER . - " Cousin Mivers , you have now have time to study Kenelm , 
 and to compare his character with that assign to he in the Doctor 's 
 letter . " 

 miver   languidly . - " ay . " 

 SIR PETER . - " I ask you , as a man of the world , what you think I have 
 good do with the boy . shall I send he to such a tutor as the Doctor 
 suggest ? Cousin John be not of the same mind as the Doctor , and think 
 that Kenelm 's oddity be fine thing in their way , and should not be 
 prematurely grind out of he by contact with worldly tutor and London 
 pavement . " 

 " ay , " repeat Mr. Mivers more languidly than before . after a pause he 
 add , " Parson John , let we hear you . " 

 the Parson lay aside his cutty - pipe and empty his fourth tumbler of 
 toddy ; then , throw back his head in the dreamy fashion of the great 
 Coleridge when he indulge in a monologue , he thus begin , speak 
 somewhat through his nose , - 

 " at the morning of life - " 

 here Mivers shrug his shoulder , turn round on his couch , and 
 close his eye with the sigh of a man resign himself to a homily . 

 " at the morning of life , when the dew - " 

 " I know the dew be come , " say miver . " dry they , if you please ; 
 nothing so unwholesome . we anticipate what you mean to say , which be 
 plainly this , when a fellow be sixteen he be very fresh : so he be ; pass 
 on ; what then ? " 

 " if you mean to interrupt I with your habitual cynicism , " say the 
 Parson , " why do you ask to hear I ? " 

 " that be a mistake I grant ; but who on earth could conceive that you 
 be go to commence in that florid style ? morning of life indeed ! 
 bosh ! " 

 " Cousin Mivers , " say Sir Peter , " you be not review John 's style in 
 ' the Londoner ; ' and I will beg you to remember that my son 's morning of 
 life be a serious thing to his father , and not to be nip in its bud 
 by a cousin . Proceed , John ! " 

 Quoth the Parson , good - humouredly , " I will adapt my style to the taste 
 of my critic . when a fellow be at the age of sixteen , and very fresh 
 to life , the question be whether he should begin thus prematurely to 
 exchange the idea that belong to youth for the idea that properly 
 belong to middle age , - whether he should begin to acquire that knowledge 
 of the world which middle - aged man have acquire and can teach . I think 
 not . I would rather have he yet a while in the company of the poet ; 
 in the indulgence of glorious hope and beautiful dream , form to 
 himself some type of the Heroic , which he will keep before his eye as 
 a standard when he go into the world as man . there be two school of 
 think for the formation of character , - the Real and the Ideal . I would 
 form the character in the Ideal school , in order to make it bold and 
 grander and lovelier when it take its place in that every - day life 
 which be call real . and therefore I be not for place the descendant 
 of Sir Kenelm Digby , in the interval between school and college , with a 
 man of the world , probably as cynical as Cousin Mivers and live in the 
 stony thoroughfare of London . " 

 MR . MIVERS   rouse himself . - " before we plunge into that Serbonian 
 bog - the controversy between the Realistic and the Idealistic 
 academician - I think the first thing to decide be what you want Kenelm 
 to be hereafter . when I order a pair of shoe , I decide beforehand what 
 kind of shoe they be to be , - court pump or strong walking shoe ; 
 and I do not   ask the shoemaker to give I a preliminary lecture upon the 
 different purpose of locomotion to which leather can be apply . if , 
 Sir Peter , you want Kenelm to scribble lackadaisical poem , listen to 
 Parson John ; if you want to fill his head with pastoral rubbish about 
 innocent love , which may end in marry the miller 's daughter , listen 
 to Parson John ; if you want he to enter life a soft - headed greenhorn , 
 who will sign any bill carry 50 per cent to which a young scamp ask 
 he to be security , listen to Parson John ; in fine , if you wish a clever 
 lad to become either a pigeon or a ring - dove , a credulous booby or a 
 sentimental milksop , Parson John be the good adviser you can have . " 

 " but I do not   want my son to ripen into either of those imbecile 
 development of specie . " 

 " then do not   listen to Parson John ; and there be an end of the 
 discussion . " 

 " no , there be not . I have not hear your advice what to do if John 's 
 advice be not to be take . " 

 Mr. Mivers hesitate . he seem puzzle . 

 " the fact be , " say the Parson , " that miver get up ' the Londoner ' 
 upon a principle that regulate his own mind , - find fault with the way 
 everything be do , but never commit yourself by say how anything can 
 be do well . " 

 " that be true , " say miver , candidly . " the destructive order of mind be 
 seldom ally to the constructive . I and ' the Londoner ' be destructive 
 by nature and by policy . we can reduce a building into rubbish , but we 
 do not   profess to turn rubbish into a building . we be critic , and , as 
 you say , not such fool as to commit ourselves to the proposition of 
 amendment that can be criticise by other . nevertheless , for your 
 sake , Cousin Peter , and on the condition that if I give my advice you 
 will never say that I give it , and if you take it that you will never 
 reproach I if it turn out , as most advice do , very ill , - I will 
 depart from my custom and hazard my opinion . " 

 " I accept the condition . " 

 " well then , with every new generation there spring up a new order of 
 idea . the early the age at which a man seize the idea that will 
 influence his own generation , the more he have a start in the race with 
 his contemporary . if Kenelm comprehend at sixteen those intellectual 
 sign of the time which , when he go up to college , he will find young 
 man of eighteen or twenty only just prepared to comprehend , he 
 will produce a deep impression of his power for reasoning and their 
 adaptation to actual life , which will be of great service to he later . 
 now the idea that influence the mass of the rise generation never 
 have their well - head in the generation itself . they have their source in 
 the generation before they , generally in a small minority , neglect or 
 contemn by the great majority which adopt they later . therefore a lad 
 at the age of sixteen , if he want to get at such idea , must come 
 into close contact with some superior mind in which they be conceive 
 twenty or thirty year before . I be consequently for place Kenelm with 
 a person from whom the new idea can be learn . I be also for his being 
 place in the metropolis during the process of this initiation . with 
 such introduction as be at our command , he may come in contact not 
 only with new idea , but with eminent man in all vocation . it be a 
 great thing to mix betime with clever people . one pick their brain 
 unconsciously . there be another advantage , and not a small one , in 
 this early entrance into good society . a youth learn manner , 
 self - possession , readiness of resource ; and he be much less likely to 
 get into scrape and contract taste for low vice and mean dissipation , 
 when he come into life wholly his own master , after have acquire 
 a predilection for refined companionship under the guidance of those 
 competent to select it . there , I have talk myself out of breath . and 
 you have well decide at once in favour of my advice ; for as I be of a 
 contradictory temperament , myself of to - morrow may probably contradict 
 myself of to - day . " 

 Sir Peter be greatly impressed with his cousin 's argumentative 
 eloquence . 

 the Parson smoke his cutty - pipe in silence until appeal to by Sir 
 Peter , and he then say , " in this programme of education for a Christian 
 gentleman , the part of Christian seem to I leave out . " 

 " the tendency of the age , " observe Mr. Mivers , calmly , " be towards that 
 omission . secular education be the necessary reaction from the special 
 theological training which arise in the dislike of one set of Christians 
 to the teaching of another set ; and as these antagonist will not agree 
 how religion be to be teach , either there must be no teaching at all , 
 or religion must be eliminate from the tuition . " 

 " that may do very well for some huge system of national education , " say 
 Sir Peter , " but it do not apply to Kenelm , as one of a family all of 
 whose member belong to the Established Church . he may be teach the 
 creed of his forefather without offend a dissenter . " 

 " which Established Church be he to belong to ? " ask Mr. Mivers , - " high 
 Church , Low Church , Broad Church , Puseyite Church , Ritualistic Church , 
 or any other Established Church that may be come into fashion ? " 

 " Pshaw ! " say the Parson . " that sneer be out of place . you know very 
 well that one merit of our Church be the spirit of toleration , which 
 do not magnify every variety of opinion into a heresy or a schism . 
 but if Sir Peter send his son at the age of sixteen to a tutor who 
 eliminate the religion of Christianity from his teaching , he deserve 
 to be thrash within an inch of his life ; and , " continue the Parson , 
 eye Sir Peter sternly , and mechanically turn up his cuff , " I 
 should like to thrash he . " 

 " gently , John , " say Sir Peter , recoiling ; " gently , my dear kinsman . my 
 heir shall not be educate as a heathen , and Mivers be only banter 
 we . Come , Mivers , do you happen to know among your London friend some 
 man who , though a scholar and a man of the world , be still a Christian ? " 

 " a Christian as by law establish ? " 

 " well - yes . " 

 " and who will receive Kenelm as a pupil ? " 

 " of course I be not put , such question to you out of idle 
 curiosity . " 

 " I know exactly the man . he be originally intend for order , and be 
 a very learn theologian . he relinquish the thought of the clerical 
 profession on succeed to a small land estate by the sudden death of 
 an eld brother . he then come to London and buy experience : that 
 be , he be naturally generous ; he become easily take in ; get into 
 difficulty ; the estate be transfer to trustee for the benefit of 
 creditor , and on the payment of L400 a year to himself . by this time 
 he be marry and have two child . he find the necessity of employ 
 his pen in order to add to his income , and be one of the able 
 contributor to the periodical press . he be an elegant scholar , an 
 effective writer , much court by public man , a thorough gentleman , have 
 a pleasant house , and receive the good society . have be once take 
 in , he defy any one to take he in again . his experience be not 
 buy too dearly . no more acute and accomplished man of the world . the 
 three hundred a year or so that you would pay for Kenelm would suit he 
 very well . his name be Welby , and he live in Chester Square . " 

 " no doubt he be a contributor to ' the Londoner , ' " say the Parson , 
 sarcastically . 

 " true . he write our classical , theological , and metaphysical article . 
 suppose I invite he to come here for a day or two , and you can see he 
 and judge for yourself , Sir Peter ? " 

 " do . " 



 CHAPTER X. 

 MR . WELBY arrive , and please everybody . a man of the happy manner , 
 easy and courteous . there be no pedantry in he , yet you could soon see 
 that his reading cover an extensive surface , and here and there 
 have dive deeply . he enchant the Parson by his comment on Saint 
 Chrysostom ; he dazzle Sir Peter with his lore in the antiquity of 
 ancient Britain ; he captivate Kenelm by his readiness to enter into 
 that most disputatious of science call metaphysic ; while for Lady 
 Chillingly , and the three sister who be invite to meet he , he be 
 more entertaining , but not less instructive . equally at home in novel 
 and in good book , he give to the spinster a list of innocent work 
 in either ; while for Lady Chillingly he sparkle with anecdote of 
 fashionable life , the new bon mot , the late scandal . in fact , 
 Mr. Welby be one of those brilliant person who adorn any society 
 amidst which they be throw . if at heart he be a disappointed man , 
 the disappointment be conceal by an even serenity of spirit ; he 
 have entertain high and justifiable hope of a brilliant career and a 
 last reputation as a theologian and a preacher ; the succession to 
 his estate at the age of twenty - three have change the nature of his 
 ambition . the charm of his manner be such that he spring at once into 
 the fashion , and become beguile by his own genial temperament into that 
 less but pleasanter kind of ambition which content itself with social 
 success and enjoy the present hour . when his circumstance compel 
 he to eke out his income by literary profit , he slide into the groove 
 of periodical composition , and resign all thought of the labour 
 require for any complete work , which might take much time and be 
 attend with scanty profit . he still remain very popular in society , 
 and perhaps his general reputation for ability make he fearful to 
 hazard it by any great undertaking . he be not , like Mivers , a despiser 
 of all man and all thing ; but he regard man and thing as an 
 indifferent though good - natured spectator regard the throng street 
 from a drawing - room window . he could not be call blase , but he be 
 thoroughly desillusionne . once over - romantic , his character now be so 
 entirely imbue with the neutral tint of life that romance offend his 
 taste as an obtrusion of violent colour into a sober woof . he be become 
 a thorough Realist in his code of criticism , and in his worldly mode 
 of action and thought . but Parson John do not perceive this , for 
 Welby listen to that gentleman 's eulogy on the Ideal school without 
 trouble himself to contradict they . he have grow too indolent to be 
 combative in conversation , and only as a critic betray such pugnacity 
 as remain to he by the polished cruelty of sarcasm . 

 he come off with fly colour through an examination into his Church 
 orthodoxy institute by the Parson and Sir Peter . amid a cloud of 
 ecclesiastical erudition , his own opinion vanish in those of the 
 father . in truth , he be a Realist , in religion as in everything else . 
 he regard Christianity as a type of existent civilization , which 
 ought to be reverence , as one might recognize the other type of that 
 civilization ; such as the liberty of the press , the representative 
 system , white neckcloth and black coat of an evening , etc . he 
 belong , therefore , to what he himself call the school of Eclectical 
 Christiology ; and accommodate the reasoning of deism to the doctrine 
 of the Church , if not as a creed , at least as an institution . finally , 
 he unite all the Chillingly vote in his favour ; and when he depart 
 from the Hall carry off Kenelm for his initiation into the new idea 
 that be to govern his generation . 



 CHAPTER XI . 

 KENELM remain a year and a half with this distinguished preceptor . 
 during that time he learn much in book - lore ; he see much , too , of the 
 eminent man of the day , in literature , the law , and the senate . he see , 
 also , a good deal of the fashionable world . fine lady , who have be 
 friend of his mother in her youth , take he up , counsel and pet 
 he , - one in especial , the Marchioness of Glenalvon , to whom he be 
 endear by grateful association , for her young son have be a 
 fellow - pupil of Kenelm at Merton School , and Kenelm have save his life 
 from drown . the poor boy die of consumption later , and her grief for 
 his loss make her affection for Kenelm yet more tender . Lady Glenalvon 
 be one of the queen of the London world . though in the fiftieth 
 year she be still very handsome : she be also very accomplished , very 
 clever , and very kind - hearted , as some of such queen be ; just one 
 of those woman invaluable in form the manner and elevate the 
 character of young man destine to make a figure in after - life . but she 
 be very angry with herself in think that she fail to arouse any 
 such ambition in the heir of the Chillinglys . 

 it may here be say that Kenelm be not without great advantage of form 
 and countenance . he be tall , and the youthful grace of his proportion 
 conceal his physical strength , which be extraordinary rather from the 
 iron texture than the bulk of his thews and sinew . his face , though it 
 certainly lack the roundness of youth , have a grave , sombre , haunt 
 sort of beauty , not artistically regular , but picturesque , peculiar , 
 with large dark expressive eye , and a certain indescribable combination 
 of sweetness and melancholy in his quiet smile . he never laugh 
 audibly , but he have a quick sense of the comic , and his eye would laugh 
 when his lip be silent . he would say queer , droll , unexpected thing 
 which pass for humour ; but , save for that gleam in the eye , he could 
 not have say they with more seeming innocence of intentional joke if he 
 have be a monk of La Trappe look up from the grave he be dig in 
 order to utter " memento mori . " 

 that face of his be a great " take in . " woman think it full of 
 romantic sentiment ; the face of one easily move to love , and whose love 
 would be replete alike with poetry and passion . but he remain as proof 
 as the youthful Hippolytus to all female attraction . he delight the 
 Parson by keep up his practice in athletic pursuit ; and obtain a 
 reputation at the pugilistic school , which he attend regularly , as the 
 good gentleman boxer about town . 

 he make many acquaintance , but still form no friendship . yet every 
 one who see he much conceive affection for he . if he do not return 
 that affection , he do not repel it . he be exceedingly gentle in voice 
 and manner , and have all his father 's placidity of temper : child and 
 dog take to he as by instinct . 

 on leave Mr. Welby 's , Kenelm carry to Cambridge a mind largely 
 stock with the new idea that be bud into leaf . he certainly 
 astonish the other freshman , and occasionally puzzle the mighty 
 Fellows of Trinity and St. John 's . but he gradually withdraw himself 
 much from general society . in fact , he be too old in mind for his 
 year ; and after have mix in the choice circle of a metropolis , 
 college supper and wine party have little charm for he . he maintain 
 his pugilistic renown ; and on certain occasion , when some delicate 
 undergraduate have be bully by some gigantic bargeman , his muscular 
 Christianity nobly develop itself . he do not do as much as he might 
 have do in the more intellectual way of academical distinction . 
 still , he be always among the first in the college examination ; he win 
 two university prize , and take a very creditable degree , after which 
 he return home , more odd , more saturnine - in short , less like other 
 people - than when he have leave Merton School . he have weave a solitude 
 round he out of his own heart , and in that solitude he sit still and 
 watchful as a spider sit in his web . 

 whether from natural temperament or from his educational training under 
 such teacher as Mr. Mivers , who carry out the new idea of reform by 
 revere nothing in the past , and Mr. Welby , who accept the routine of 
 the present as realistic , and pooh - poohe all vision of the future as 
 idealistic , Kenelm 's chief mental characteristic be a kind of tranquil 
 indifferentism . it be difficult to detect in he either of those 
 ordinary incentive to action , - vanity or ambition , the yearning for 
 applause or the desire of power . to all female fascination he have be 
 hitherto star - proof . he have never experience love , but he have read 
 a good deal about it ; and that passion seem to he an unaccountable 
 aberration of human reason , and an ignominious surrender of the 
 equanimity of thought which it should be the object of masculine nature 
 to maintain undisturbed . a very eloquent book in praise of celibacy , and 
 entitle " the Approach to the Angels , " write by that eminent Oxford 
 scholar , Decimus Roach , have produce so remarkable an effect upon his 
 youthful mind that , have he be a Roman Catholic , he might have become 
 a monk . where he most evince ardour it be a logician 's ardour for 
 abstract truth ; that be , for what he consider truth : and , as what 
 seem truth to one man be sure to seem falsehood to some other man , this 
 predilection of his be not without its inconvenience and danger , as 
 may probably be see in the following chapter . 

 meanwhile , rightly to appreciate his conduct therein , I entreat thee , O 
 candid reader   not that any reader ever be candid , to remember that he 
 be brimful of new idea , which , meet by a deep and hostile undercurrent 
 of old idea , become more provocatively billowy and surge . 



 CHAPTER XII . 

 there have be great festivity at Exmundham , in celebration of the 
 honour bestow upon the world by the fact that Kenelm Chillingly have 
 live twenty - one year in it . 

 the young heir have make a speech to the assemble tenant and other 
 admit reveller , which have by no mean add to the exhilaration of 
 the proceeding . he speak with a fluency and self - possession which be 
 surprising in a youth address a multitude for the first time . but his 
 speech be not cheerful . 

 the principal tenant on the estate , in propose his health , have 
 naturally refer to the long line of his ancestor . his father 's 
 merit as man and landlord have be enthusiastically commemorate ; and 
 many happy augury for his own future career have be draw , partly 
 from the excellence of his parentage , partly from his own youthful 
 promise in the honour achieve at the University . 

 Kenelm Chillingly in reply largely avail himself of those new idea 
 which be to influence the rise generation , and with which he have 
 be render familiar by the journal of Mr. Mivers and the conversation 
 of Mr. Welby . 

 he briefly dispose of the ancestral part of the question . he observe 
 that it be singular to note how long any give family or , dynasty could 
 continue to flourish in any give nook of matter in creation , without 
 any exhibition of intellectual power beyond those display by a 
 succession of vegetable crop . " it be certainly true , " he say , " that 
 the Chillinglys have live in this place from father to son for about a 
 fourth part of the history of the world , since the date which Sir Isaac 
 Newton assign to the Deluge . but , so far as can be judge by existent
