 record , the world have not be in any way wise or well for their 
 existence . they be bear to eat as long as they could eat , and when 
 they could eat no long they die . not that in this respect they be 
 a whit less insignificant than the generality of their fellow - creature . 
 Most of we now present , " continue the youthful orator , " be only bear 
 in order to die ; and the chief consolation of our wound pride in 
 admit this fact be in the probability that our posterity will not 
 be of more consequence to the scheme of Nature than we ourselves be . " 
 pass from that philosophical view of his own ancestor in particular , 
 and of the human race in general , Kenelm Chillingly then touch with 
 serene analysis on the eulogy lavish on his father as man and 
 landlord . 

 " as man , " he say , " my father no doubt deserve all that can be say 
 by man in favour of man . but what , at the good , be man ? a crude , 
 struggle , undeveloped embryo , of whom it be the high attribute that 
 he feel a vague consciousness that he be only an embryo , and can not 
 complete himself till he cease to be a man ; that be , until he become 
 another be in another form of existence . we can praise a dog as 
 a dog , because a dog be a complete ens , and not an embryo . but to 
 praise a man as man , forget that he be only a germ out of which a 
 form wholly different be ultimately to spring , be equally opposed 
 to Scriptural belief in his present crudity and imperfection , and to 
 psychological or metaphysical examination of a mental construction 
 evidently design for purpose that he can never fulfil as man . that my 
 father be an embryo not more incomplete than any present be quite true ; 
 but that , you will see on reflection , be say very little on his 
 behalf . even in the boasted physical formation of we man , you be 
 aware that the well - shaped amongst we , accord to the last scientific 
 discovery , be only a development of some hideous hairy animal , such 
 as a gorilla ; and the ancestral gorilla itself have its own aboriginal 
 forefather in a small marine animal shape like a two - neck bottle . the 
 probability be that , some day or other , we shall be exterminate by a 
 new development of specie . 

 " as for the merit assign to my father as landlord , I must 
 respectfully dissent from the panegyric so rashly bestow on he . for 
 all sound reasoner must concur in this , that the first duty of an owner 
 of land be not to the occupier to whom he lease it , but to the nation 
 at large . it be his duty to see that the land yield to the community 
 the utmost it can yield . in order to effect this object , a landlord 
 should put up his farm to competition , exact the high rent he can 
 possibly get from responsible competitor . competitive examination be 
 the enlightened order of the day , even in profession in which the good 
 man would have quality that defy examination . in agriculture , happily , 
 the principle of competitive examination be not so hostile to the choice 
 of the good man as it must be , for instance , in diplomacy , where a 
 Talleyrand would be exclude for know no language but his own ; and 
 still more in the army , where promotion would be deny to an officer 
 who , like Marlborough , could not spell . but in agriculture a landlord 
 have only to inquire who can give the high rent , have the large 
 capital , subject by the strict penalty of law to the condition of 
 a lease dictate by the most scientific agriculturist under penalty 
 fix by the most cautious conveyancer . by this mode of procedure , 
 recommend by the most liberal economist of our age , - bar those 
 still more liberal who deny that property in land be any property at 
 all , - by this mode of procedure , I say , a landlord do his duty to his 
 country . he secure tenant who can produce the most to the community by 
 their capital , test through competitive examination in their banker ' 
 account and the security they can give , and through the rigidity of 
 covenant suggest by a Liebig and reduce into law by a Chitty . but on 
 my father 's land I see a great many tenant with little skill and less 
 capital , ignorant of a Liebig and revolt from a Chitty , and no 
 filial enthusiasm can induce I honestly to say that my father be a good 
 landlord . he have prefer his affection for individual to his duty 
 to the community . it be not , my friend , a question whether a handful of 
 farmer like yourself go to the workhouse or not . it be a consumer 's 
 question . do you produce the maximum of corn to the consumer ? 

 " with respect to myself , " continue the orator , warm as the cold 
 he have engender in his audience become more freezingly feel , - " with 
 respect to myself , I do not deny that , owe to the accident of training 
 for a very faulty and contract course of education , I have obtain 
 what be call ' honour ' at the University of Cambridge ; but you must 
 not regard that fact as a promise of any worth in my future passage 
 through life . some of the most useless person - especially narrow - minded 
 and bigote - have acquire far high honour at the University than 
 have fall to my lot . 

 " I thank you no less for the civil thing you have say of I and of my 
 family ; but I shall endeavour to walk to that grave to which we be all 
 bind with a tranquil indifference as to what people may say of I in 
 so short a journey . and the soon , my friend , we get to our journey 's 
 end , the well our chance of escape a great many pain , trouble , 
 sin , and disease . so that when I drink to your good health , you must 
 feel that in reality I wish you an early deliverance from the ill to 
 which flesh be expose , and which so generally increase with our year 
 that good health be scarcely compatible with the decay faculty of 
 old age . gentleman , your good health ! " 



 CHAPTER XIII . 

 the morning after these birthday rejoicing , Sir Peter and Lady 
 chillingly hold a long consultation on the peculiarity of their heir , 
 and the good mode of instill into his mind the expediency either 
 of entertain more pleasing view , or at least of profess less 
 unpopular sentiment ; compatibly of course , though they do not say it , 
 with the new idea that be to govern his century . have come to an 
 agreement on this delicate subject , they go forth , arm in arm , in 
 search of their heir . Kenelm seldom meet they at breakfast . he be an 
 early riser , and accustom to solitary ramble before his parent be 
 out of bed . 

 the worthy pair find Kenelm seat on the bank of a trout - stream that 
 meander through Chillingly Park , dip his line into the water , and 
 yawning , with apparent relief in that operation . 

 " do fishing amuse you , my boy ? " say Sir Peter , heartily . 

 " not in the least , sir , " answer Kenelm . 

 " then why do you do it ? " ask Lady Chillingly . 

 " because I know nothing else that amuse I more . " 

 " ah ! that be it , " say Sir Peter : " the whole secret of Kenelm 's oddity 
 be to be find in these word , my dear ; he need amusement . voltaire 
 say truly , ' amusement be one of the want of man . ' and if Kenelm could 
 be amuse like other people , he would be like other people . " 

 " in that case , " say Kenelm , gravely , and extract from the water 
 a small but lively trout , which settle itself in Lady Chillingly 's 
 lap , - " in that case I would rather not be amuse . I have no interest 
 in the absurdity of other people . the instinct of self - preservation 
 compel I to have some interest in my own . " 

 " Kenelm , sir , " exclaim Lady Chillingly , with an animation into which 
 her tranquil ladyship be very rarely betray , " take away that horrid 
 damp thing ! put down your rod and attend to what your father say . your 
 strange conduct give we cause of serious anxiety . " 

 Kenelm unhook the trout , deposit the fish in his basket , and raise 
 his large eye to his father 's face , say , " what be there in my conduct 
 that occasion you displeasure ? " 

 " not displeasure , Kenelm , " say Sir Peter , kindly , " but anxiety ; your 
 mother have hit upon the right word . you see , my dear son , that it be 
 my wish that you should distinguish yourself in the world . you might 
 represent this county , as your ancestor have do before . I have look 
 forward to the proceeding of yesterday as an admirable occasion for 
 your introduction to your future constituent . Oratory be the talent 
 most appreciated in a free country , and why should you not be an orator ? 
 demosthene say that delivery , delivery , delivery , be the art of 
 oratory ; and your delivery be excellent , graceful , self - possess , 
 classical . " 

 " Pardon I , my dear father , demosthene do not say delivery , 
 nor action , as the word be commonly render ; he say , ' act , or 
 stage - play , ' - the art by which a man deliver a speech in a feigned 
 character , whence we get the word hypocrisy . Hypocrisy , hypocrisy , 
 hypocrisy ! be , accord to demosthene , the triple art of the orator . 
 do you wish I to become triply a hypocrite ? " 

 " Kenelm , I be ashamed of you . you know as well as I do that it be only 
 by metaphor that you can twist the word ascribe to the great Athenian 
 into the sense of hypocrisy . but assume it , as you say , to mean not 
 delivery , but act , I understand why your debut as an orator be 
 not successful . your delivery be excellent , your act defective . 
 an orator should please , conciliate , persuade , prepossess . you do the 
 reverse of all this ; and though you produce a great effect , the effect 
 be so decidedly to your disadvantage that it would have lose you an 
 election on any husting in England . " 

 " be I to understand , my dear father , " say Kenelm , in the mournful and 
 compassionate tone with which a pious minister of the Church reprove 
 some abandon and hoary sinner , - " be I to understand that you would 
 commend to your son the adoption of deliberate falsehood for the gain of 
 a selfish advantage ? " 

 " deliberate falsehood ! you impertinent puppy ! " 

 " Puppy ! " repeat Kenelm , not indignantly but musingly , - " puppy ! a 
 well - breed puppy take after its parent . " 

 Sir Peter burst out laugh . 

 Lady Chillingly rise with dignity , shake her gown , unfold her parasol , 
 and stalk away speechless . 

 " now , look you , Kenelm , " say Sir Peter , as soon as he have compose 
 himself . " these quip and humour of yours be amusing enough to an 
 eccentric man like myself , but they will not do for the world ; and 
 how at your age , and with the rare advantage you have have in an early 
 introduction to the good intellectual society , under the guidance of a 
 tutor acquaint with the new idea which be to influence the 
 conduct of statesman , you could have make so silly a speech as you do 
 yesterday , I can not understand . " 

 " my dear father , allow I to assure you that the idea I express be 
 the new idea most in vogue , - idea express in still plainer , or , if 
 you prefer the epithet , still silly term than I employ . you will 
 find they instill into the public mind by ' the Londoner ' and by most 
 intellectual journal of a liberal character . " 

 " Kenelm , Kenelm , such idea would turn the world topsy - turvy . " 

 " new idea always do tend to turn old idea topsy - turvy . and the world , 
 after all , be only an idea , which be turn topsy - turvy with every 
 successive century . " 

 " you make I sick of the word ' idea . ' leave off your metaphysics and 
 study real life . " 

 " it be real life which I do study under Mr. Welby . he be the 
 Archimandrite of Realism . it be sham life which you wish I to study . to 
 oblige you I be willing to commence it . I dare say it be very pleasant . 
 real life be not ; on the contrary - dull , " and Kenelm yawn again . 

 " have you no young friend among your fellow - collegian ? " 

 " friend ! certainly not , sir . but I believe I have some enemy , who 
 answer the same purpose as friend , only they do not   hurt one so much . " 

 " do you mean to say that you live alone at Cambridge ? " 

 " no , I live a good deal with Aristophanes , and a little with conic 
 Sections and Hydrostatics . " 

 " Books . Dry company . " 

 " more innocent , at least , than moist company . do you ever get drunk , 
 sir ? " 

 " Drunk ! " 

 " I try to do so once with the young companion whom you would commend 
 to I as friend . I do not   think I succeed , but I wake with a headache . 
 real life at college abound with headache . " 

 " Kenelm , my boy , one thing be clear : you must travel . " 

 " as you please , sir . Marcus Antoninus say that it be all one to a stone 
 whether it be throw upwards or downward . when shall I start ? " 

 " very soon . of course there be preparation to make ; you should have a 
 travel companion . I do not   mean a tutor , - you be too clever and 
 too steady to need one , - but a pleasant , sensible , well - mannered young 
 person of your own age . " 

 " my own age , - male or female ? " 

 Sir Peter try hard to frown . the utmost he could do be to reply 
 gravely , " FEMALE ! if I say you be too steady to need a tutor , it be 
 because you have hitherto seem little likely to be lead out of your 
 way by female allurement . among your other study may I inquire if you 
 have include that which no man have ever yet thoroughly master , - the 
 study of woman ? " 

 " certainly . do you object to my catch another trout ? " 

 " Trout be - bless , or the reverse . so you have study woman . I should 
 never have think it . where and when do you commence that department 
 of science ? " 

 " when ? ever since I be ten year old . where ? first in your own house , 
 then at college . hush ! - a bite , " and another trout leave its native 
 element and alight on Sir Peter 's nose , whence it be solemnly 
 transfer to the basket . 

 " at ten year old , and in my own house ! that flaunt hussy Jane , the 
 under - housemaid - " 

 " jane ! no , sir . Pamela , Miss Byron , Clarissa , - female in Richardson , 
 who , accord to Dr. Johnson , ' teach the passion to move at the 
 command of virtue . ' I trust for your sake that Dr. Johnson do not err 
 in that assertion , for I find all these female at night in your own 
 private apartment . " 

 " oh ! " say Sir Peter , " that be all ? " 

 " all I remember at ten year old , " reply Kenelm . 

 " and at Mr. Welby 's or at college , " proceed Sir Peter , timorously , 
 " be your acquaintance with female of the same kind ? " 

 Kenelm shake his head . " much worse : they be very naughty indeed at 
 college . " 

 " I should think so , with such a lot of young fellow run after 
 they . " 

 " very few fellow run after the female . I mean - rather avoid they . " 

 " so much the well . " 

 " no , my father , so much the bad ; without an intimate knowledge of 
 those female there be little use go to college at all . " 

 " explain yourself . " 

 " every one who receive a classical education be introduce into their 
 society , - Pyrrha and Lydia , Glycera and Corinna , and many more of the 
 same sort ; and then the female in Aristophanes , what do you say to 
 they , sir ? " 

 " be it only female who live two thousand or three thousand year ago , 
 or more probably never live at all , whose intimacy you have cultivate ? 
 have you never admire any real woman ? " 

 " real woman ! I never meet one . never meet a woman who be not a sham , 
 a sham from the moment she be tell to be pretty - behaved , conceal her 
 sentiment , and look fib when she do not speak they . but if I be to 
 learn sham life , I suppose I must put up with sham woman . " 

 " have you be cross in love that you speak so bitterly of the sex ? " 

 " I do not   speak bitterly of the sex . examine any woman on her oath , and 
 she will own she be a sham , always have be , and always will be , and be 
 proud of it . " 

 " I be glad your mother be not by to hear you . you will think differently 
 one of these day . meanwhile , to turn to the other sex , be there no 
 young man of your own rank with whom you would like to travel ? " 

 " certainly not . I hate quarrel . " 

 " as you please . but you can not go quite alone : I will find you a good 
 travel - servant . I must write to town to - day about your preparation , 
 and in another week or so I hope all will be ready . your allowance will 
 be whatever you like to fix it at ; you have never be extravagant , 
 and - boy - I love you . amuse yourself , enjoy yourself , and come back 
 cure of your oddity , but preserve your honour . " 

 Sir Peter bend down and kiss his son 's brow . Kenelm be move ; he 
 rise , put his arm round his father 's shoulder , and lovingly say , in 
 an undertone , " if ever I be tempt to do a base thing , may I remember 
 whose son I be : I shall be safe then . " he withdraw his arm as he say 
 this , and take his solitary way along the bank of the stream , forgetful 
 of rod and line . 



 CHAPTER XIV . 

 the young man continue to skirt the side of the stream until he reach 
 the boundary pale of the park . here , place on a rough grass mound , 
 some former proprietor , of a social temperament , have build a kind of 
 belvidere , so as to command a cheerful view of the high road below . 
 mechanically the heir of the Chillinglys ascend the mound , seat 
 himself within the belvidere , and lean his chin on his hand in a 
 thoughtful attitude . it be rarely that the building be honour by a 
 human visitor : its habitual occupant be spider . of those industrious 
 insect it be a well - populate colony . their webs , darken with 
 dust and ornament with the wing and leg and skeleton of many an 
 unfortunate traveller , clung thick to angle and window - sill , festoon 
 the rickety table on which the young man lean his elbow , and describe 
 geometrical circle and rhomboid between the gap rail that form 
 the back of venerable chair . one large black spider - who be probably 
 the old inhabitant , and hold possession of the good place by the 
 window , ready to offer perfidious welcome to every wing itinerant 
 who might be tempt to turn aside from the high road for the sake of 
 a little cool and repose - rush from its innermost penetralia at the 
 entrance of Kenelm , and remain motionless in the centre of its mesh , 
 star at he . it do not seem quite sure whether the stranger be too 
 big or not . 

 " it be a wonderful proof of the wisdom of Providence , " say Kenelm , 
 " that whenever any large number of its creature form a community 
 or class , a secret element of disunion enter into the heart of the 
 individual form the congregation , and prevent their co - operate 
 heartily and effectually for their common interest . ' the flea would 
 have drag I out of bed if they have be unanimous , ' say the great 
 Mr. Curran ; and there can be no doubt that if all the spider in this 
 commonwealth would unite to attack I in a body , I should fall a victim 
 to their combine nipper . but spider , though inhabit the same 
 region , constitute the same race , animate by the same instinct , 
 do not combine even against a butterfly : each seek his own special 
 advantage , and not that of the community at large . and how completely 
 the life of each thing resemble a circle in this respect , that it can 
 never touch another circle at more than one point . Nay , I doubt if it 
 quite touch it even there , - there be a space between every atom ; self 
 be always selfish : and yet there be eminent master in the Academe of 
 New Ideas who wish to make we believe that all the work class of a 
 civilized world could merge every difference of race , creed , intellect , 
 individual propensity and interest into the construction of a single 
 web , stock as a larder in common ! " here the soliloquist come to a dead 
 stop , and , lean out of the window , contemplate the high road . it be 
 a very fine high road , straight and level , keep in excellent order by 
 turn pike at every eight mile . a pleasant greensward border it on 
 either side , and under the belvidere the benevolence of some mediaeval 
 Chillingly have place a little drinking - fountain for the refreshment of 
 wayfarer . close to the fountain stand a rude stone bench , overshadow 
 by a large willow , and command from the high table - ground on which 
 it be place a wide view of cornfield , meadow , and distant hill , 
 suffuse in the mellow light of the summer sun . along that road there 
 come successively a wagon fill with passenger seat on straw , - an 
 old woman , a pretty girl , two child ; then a stout farmer go to 
 market in his dog - cart ; then three fly carry fare to the near 
 railway station ; then a handsome young man on horseback , a handsome 
 young lady by his side , a groom behind . it be easy to see that the 
 young man and young lady be lover . see it in his ardent look and 
 serious lip part but for whisper only to be hear by she ; see it 
 in her downcast eye and heighten colour . " ' alas ! regardless of 
 their doom , ' " mutter Kenelm , " what trouble those ' little victim ' 
 be prepare for themselves and their progeny ! would I could lend they 
 Decimus Roach 's ' approach to the Angels ' ! " the road now for some minute 
 become solitary and still , when there be hear to the right a sprightly 
 sort of carol , half sing , half recite , in musical voice , with a 
 singularly clear enunciation , so that the word reach Kenelm 's ear 
 distinctly . they run thus : - 


  " Black Karl look forth from his cottage door , 
  he look on the forest green ; 
  and down the path , with his dog before , 
  come the Ritter of Neirestein : 
  singing , singing , lustily sing , 
  down the path with his dog before , 
  come the Ritter of Neirestein . " 


 at a voice so English , attune to a strain so Germanic , Kenelm prick 
 up attentive ear , and , turn his eye down the road , beheld , emerge 
 from the shade of beech that overhang the park pale , a figure that 
 do not altogether harmonize with the idea of a Ritter of Neirestein . it 
 be , nevertheless , a picturesque figure enough . the man be attire in a 
 somewhat threadbare suit of Lincoln green , with a high - crown Tyrolese 
 hat ; a knapsack be sling behind his shoulder , and he be attend by a 
 white pomeranian dog , evidently foot - sore , but do his good to appear 
 proficient in the chase by limp some yard in advance of his master , 
 and sniff into the hedge for rat and mouse , and such small deer . 

 by the time the pedestrian have reach to the close of his refrain he 
 have gain the fountain , and greet it with an exclamation of pleasure . 
 slip the knapsack from his shoulder , he fill the iron ladle 
 attach to the basin . he then call the dog by the name of Max , and 
 hold the ladle for he to drink . not till the animal have satisfy his 
 thirst do the master assuage his own . then , lift his hat and bath 
 his temple and face , the pedestrian seat himself on the bench , 
 and the dog nestle on the turf at his foot . after a little pause the 
 wayfarer begin again , though in a low and slow tone , to chant his 
 refrain , and proceed , with abrupt snatch , to link the verse on 
 to another stanza . it be evident that he be either endeavour to 
 remember or to invent , and it seem rather like the latter and more 
 laborious operation of mind . 


  " ' why on foot , why on foot , Ritter Karl , ' quoth he , 
  ' and not on thy palfrey gray ? ' 


 Palfrey gray - hum - gray . 


  " ' the run of ill - luck be too strong for I , 
  ' and have gallop my steed away . ' 


 that will do : good ! " 

 " good indeed ! he be easily satisfied , " mutter Kenelm . " but such 
 pedestrian do not   pass the road every day . let we talk to he . " so 
 say he slip quietly out of the window , descend the mound , 
 and let himself into the road by a screen wicket - gate , take his 
 noiseless stand behind the wayfarer and beneath the bowery willow . 

 the man have now sink into silence . perhaps he have tire himself of 
 rhyme ; or perhaps the mechanism of verse - making have be replace by 
 that kind of sentiment , or that kind of revery , which be common to the 
 temperament of those who indulge in verse - making . but the loveliness 
 of the scene before he have catch his eye , and fix it into an intent 
 gaze upon woode landscape stretch far and far to the range 
 of hill on which the heaven seem to rest . 

 " I should like to hear the rest of that german ballad , " say a voice , 
 abruptly . 

 the wayfarer start , and , turn round , present to Kenelm 's view 
 a countenance in the ripe noon of manhood , with lock and beard of a 
 deep rich auburn , bright blue eye , and a wonderful nameless charm both 
 of feature and expression , very cheerful , very frank , and not without a 
 certain nobleness of character which seem to exact respect . 

 " I beg your pardon for my interruption , " say Kenelm , lift his hat : 
 " but I overheard you recite ; and though I suppose your verse be a 
 translation from the German , I do not   remember anything like they in such 
 popular german poet as I happen to have read . " 

 " it be not a translation , sir , " reply the itinerant . " I be only 
 try to string together some idea that come into my head this fine 
 morning . " 

 " you be a poet , then ? " say Kenelm , seat himself on the bench . 

 " I dare not say poet . I be a verse - maker . " 

 " Sir , I know there be a distinction . many poet of the present day , 
 consider very good , be uncommonly bad verse - maker . for my part , I 
 could more readily imagine they to be good poet if they do not make 
 verse at all . but can I not hear the rest of the ballad ? " 

 " alas ! the rest of the ballad be not yet make . it be rather a long 
 subject , and my flight be very brief . " 

 " that be much in their favour , and very unlike the poetry in fashion . 
 you do not belong , I think , to this neighbourhood . be you and your dog 
 travel far ? " 

 " it be my holiday time , and I ramble on through the summer . I be 
 travel far , for I travel till September . life amid summer field be 
 a very joyous thing . " 

 " be it indeed ? " say Kenelm , with much naivete . " I should have think 
 that long before September you would have get very much bored with the 
 field and the dog and yourself altogether . but , to be sure , you 
 have the resource of verse - making , and that seem a very pleasant and 
 absorb occupation to those who practise it , - from our old friend 
 Horace , knead laboured alcaic into honey in his summer ramble among 
 the water woodland of Tibur , to Cardinal Richelieu , employ himself 
 on french rhyme in the interval between chop off nobleman 's head . 
 it do not seem to signify much whether the verse be good or bad , 
 so far as the pleasure of the verse - maker himself be concern ; for 
 Richelieu be as much charm with his occupation as Horace be , and his 
 verse be certainly not horatian . " 

 " surely at your age , sir , and with your evident education - " 

 " say culture ; that be the word in fashion nowadays . " 

 " well , your evident culture , you must have make verse . " 

 " latin verse , yes ; and occasionally greek . I be oblige to do so at 
 school . it do not amuse I . " 

 " try English . " 

 Kenelm shake his head . " not i. every cobbler should stick to his last . " 

 " well , put aside the verse - making : do not   you find a sensible 
 enjoyment in those solitary summer walk , when you have nature all to 
 yourself , - enjoyment in mark all the mobile evanescent change in her 
 face , - her laugh , her smile , her tear , her very frown ! " 

 " assume that by nature you mean a mechanical series of external 
 phenomenon , I object to your speaking of a machinery as if it be a 
 person of the feminine gender , - her laugh , her smile , etc . as 
 well talk of the laugh and smile of a steam - engine . but to descend to 
 common - sense . I grant there be some pleasure in solitary ramble in fine 
 weather and amid vary scenery . you say that it be a holiday excursion 
 that you be enjoy . I presume , therefore , that you have some 
 practical occupation which consume the time that you do not devote to a 
 holiday ? " 

 " yes ; I be not altogether an idler . I work sometimes , though not so hard 
 as I ought . ' life be earnest , ' as the poet say . but I and my dog be 
 rest now , and as I have still a long walk before I I must wish you 
 good - day . " 

 " I fear , " say Kenelm , with a grave and sweet politeness of tone and 
 manner , which he could command at time , and which , in its difference 
 from merely conventional urbanity , be not without fascination , - " I 
 fear that I have offend you by a question that must have seem to you 
 inquisitive , perhaps impertinent ; accept my excuse : it be very rarely 
 that I meet any one who interest I ; and you do . " as he speak he 
 offer his hand , which the wayfarer shake very cordially . 

 " I should be a churl indeed if your question could have give I 
 offence . it be rather perhaps I who be guilty of impertinence , if I 
 take advantage of my seniority in year and tender you a counsel . do not 
 despise Nature or regard she as a steam - engine ; you will find in her 
 a very agreeable and conversable friend if you will cultivate she 
 intimacy . and I do not   know a well mode of do so at your age , and 
 with your strong limb , than put a knapsack on your shoulder and 
 turn foot - traveller like myself . " 

 " Sir , I thank you for your counsel ; and I trust we may meet again 
 and interchange idea as to the thing you call Nature , - a thing which 
 science and art never appear to see with the same eye . if to an artist 
 Nature have a soul , why , so have a steam - engine . art gift with soul all 
 matter that it contemplate : science turn all that be already gift 
 with soul into matter . good - day , sir . " 

 here Kenelm turn back abruptly , and the traveller go his way , 
 silently and thoughtfully . 



 CHAPTER XV . 

 KENELM retrace his step homeward under the shade of his " old 
 hereditary tree . " one might have think his path along the 
 greensward , and by the side of the babble rivulet , be pleasanter and 
 more conducive to peaceful thought than the broad , dusty thoroughfare 
 along which plod the wanderer he have quit . but the man addict to 
 revery form his own landscape and colour his own sky . 

 " it be , " soliloquize Kenelm Chillingly , " a strange yearning I have 
 long feel , - to get out of myself , to get , as it be , into another man 's 
 skin , and have a little variety of thought and emotion . one 's self be 
 always the same self ; and that be why I yawn so often . but if I can not 
 get into another man 's skin , the next good thing be to get as unlike 
 myself as I possibly can do . let I see what be myself . myself be 
 Kenelm Chillingly , son and heir to a rich gentleman . but a fellow with 
 a knapsack on his back , sleep at wayside inn , be not at all like 
 Kenelm Chillingly ; especially if he be very short of money and may come 
 to want a dinner . perhaps that sort of fellow may take a lively view 
 of thing : he can not   take a dull one . Courage , Myself : you and I can 
 but try . " 

 for the next two day Kenelm be observe to be unusually pleasant . he 
 yawn much less frequently , walk with his father , play piquet 
 with his mother , be more like other people . Sir Peter be charm : 
 he ascribe this happy change to the preparation he be make 
 for Kenelm 's travel in style . the proud father be in active 
 correspondence with his great London friend , seek letter of 
 introduction for Kenelm to all the court of Europe . portmanteau , with 
 every modern convenience , be order ; an experienced courier , who 
 could talk all language and cook french dish if require , be 
 invite to name his term . in short , every arrangement worthy a young 
 patrician 's entrance into the great world be in rapid progress , when 
 suddenly Kenelm Chillingly disappear , leave behind he on Sir 
 Peter 's library table the follow letter : - 


 MY VERY DEAR FATHER , - Obedient to your desire , I depart in search of 
 real life and real person , or of the good imitation of they . forgive 
 I , I beseech you , if I commence that search in my own way . I have see 
 enough of lady and gentleman for the present : they must be all very 
 much alike in every part of the world . you desire I to be amuse . I 
 go to try if that be possible . lady and gentleman be not amusing ; the 
 more ladylike or gentlemanlike they be , the more insipid I find they . 
 my dear father , I go in quest of adventure like Amadis of Gaul , like Don 
 Quixote , like Gil Blas , like Roderick Random ; like , in short , the only 
 people seek real life , the people who never exist except in book . 
 I go on foot ; I go alone . I have provide myself with a large amount of 
 money than I ought to spend , because every man must buy experience , 
 and the first fee be heavy . in fact , I have put fifty pound into my 
 pocket - book and into my purse five sovereign and seventeen shilling . 
 this sum ought to last I a year ; but I dare say inexperience will do 
 I out of it in a month , so we will count it as nothing . since you have 
 ask I to fix my own allowance , I will beg you kindly to commence it 
 this day in advance , by an order to your banker to cash my check to the 
 amount of five pound , and to the same amount monthly ; namely , at the 
 rate of sixty pound a year . with that sum I can not   starve , and if I 
 want more it may be amusing to work for it . pray do not   send after I , 
 or institute inquiry , or disturb the household and set all the 
 neighbourhood talk , by any mention either of my project or of your 
 surprise at it . I will not fail to write to you from time to time . 
 you will judge well what to say to my dear mother . if you tell she the 
 truth , which of course I should do do I tell she anything , my request 
 be virtually frustrated , and I shall be the talk of the county . you , 
 I know , do not   think tell fib be immoral when it happen to be 
 convenient , as it would be in this case . 

 I expect to be absent a year or eighteen month ; if I prolong my travel 
 it shall be in the way you propose . I will then take my place in polite 
 society , call upon you to pay all expense , and fib on my own account 
 to any extent require by that world of fiction which be people by 
 illusion and govern by sham . 

 Heaven bless you , my dear Father , and be quite sure that if I get into 
 any trouble require a friend , it be to you I shall turn . as yet I have 
 no other friend on earth , and with prudence and good luck I may escape 
 the infliction of any other friend . 

  your ever affectionately , 

  KENELM . 

 P. S. - Dear Father , I open my letter in your library to say again " bless 
 you , " and to tell you how fondly I kiss your old beaver glove , which 
 I find on the table . 


 when Sir Peter come to that postscript he take off his spectacle and 
 wipe they : they be very moist . 

 then he fall into a profound meditation . Sir Peter be , as I have say , 
 a learn man ; he be also in some thing a sensible man , and he have a 
 strong sympathy with the humorous side of his son 's crotchety character . 
 what be to be say to Lady Chillingly ? that matron be quite guiltless 
 of any crime which should deprive she of a husband 's confidence in 
 a matter relate to her only son . she be a virtuous matron ; moral 
 irreproachable , manner dignify , and she - baronety . any one see 
 she for the first time would intuitively say , " your ladyship . " be 
 this a matron to be suppress in any well - order domestic circle ? Sir 
 Peter 's conscience loudly answer , " no ; " but when , put conscience 
 into his pocket , he regard the question at issue as a man of the 
 world , Sir Peter feel that to communicate the content of his son 's 
 letter to Lady Chillingly would be the foolish thing he could 
 possibly do . do she know that Kenelm have abscond with the family 
 dignity invest in his very name , no marital authority short of such 
 abuse of power as constitute the offence of cruelty in a wife 's 
 action for divorce from social board and nuptial bed could prevent Lady 
 chillingly from summon all the groom , send they in all direction 
 with strict order to bring back the runaway dead or alive ; the wall 
 would be placard with hand - bill , " stray from his home , " etc . ; the 
 police would be telegraph private instruction from town to town ; 
 the scandal would stick to Kenelm Chillingly for life , accompany with 
 vague hint of criminal propensity and insane hallucination ; he would 
 be ever afterwards point out as " the MAN who have disappear . " and to 
 disappear and to turn up again , instead of be murder , be the most 
 hateful thing a man can do : all the newspaper bark at he , " Tray , 
 Blanche , Sweetheart , and all ; " strict explanation of the unseemly fact 
 of his safe existence be demand in the name of public decorum , and no 
 explanation be accept ; it be life save , character lose . 

 Sir Peter seize his hat and walk forth , not to deliberate whether to 
 fib or not to fib to the wife of his bosom , but to consider what kind of 
 fib would the most quickly sink into the bosom of his wife . 

 a few turn to and fro on the terrace suffice for the conception and 
 mature of the fib select ; a proof that Sir Peter be a practised 
 fibber . he re - enter the house , pass into her ladyship 's habitual 
 sitting - room , and say with careless gayety , " my old friend the Duke of 
 Clareville be just set off on a tour to Switzerland with his family . 
 his young daughter , Lady Jane , be a pretty girl , and would not be a 
 bad match for Kenelm . " 

 " Lady Jane , the young daughter with fair hair , whom I see last as 
 a very charming child , nurse a lovely doll present to she by the 
 Empress Eugenie , - a good match indeed for Kenelm . " 

 " I be glad you agree with I . would it not be a favourable step towards 
 that alliance , and an excellent thing for Kenelm generally , if he be 
 to visit the continent as one of the Duke 's travel party ? " 

 " of course it would . " 

 " then you approve what I have do ; the Duke start the day after 
 to - morrow , and I have pack Kenelm off to town , with a letter to my old 
 friend . you will excuse all leave take . you know that though the good 
 of son he be an odd fellow ; and see that I have talk he into it , 
 I strike while the iron be hot , and send he off by the express at nine 
 o'clock this morning , for fear that if I allow any delay he would talk 
 himself out of it . " 

 " do you mean to say Kenelm be actually go ? good gracious . " 

 Sir Peter steal softly from the room , and summon his valet , say , " I 
 have send Mr. Chillingly to London . pack up the clothe he be likely 
 to want , so that he can have they send at once , whenever he write for 
 they . " 

 and thus , by a judicious violation of truth on the part of his father , 
 that exemplary truth - teller Kenelm Chillingly save the honour of 
 his house and his own reputation from the breath of scandal and the 
 inquisition of the police . he be not " the MAN who have disappear . " 




 BOOK II . 



 CHAPTER I. 

 KENELM CHILLINGLY have quit the paternal home at daybreak before any 
 of the household be astir . " unquestionably , " say he , as he walk 
 along the solitary lane , - " unquestionably I begin the world as poet 
 begin poetry , an imitator and a plagiarist . I be imitate an itinerant 
 verse - maker , as , no doubt , he begin by imitate some other maker 
 of verse . but if there be anything in I , it will work itself out in 
 original form . and , after all , the verse - maker be not the inventor of 
 idea . adventure on foot be a notion that remount to the age of fable . 
 Hercules , for instance ; that be the way in which he get to heaven , as 
 a foot - traveller . how solitary the world be at this hour ! be it not for 
 that reason that this be of all hour the most beautiful ? " 

 here he pause , and look around and above . it be the very height of 
 summer . the sun be just rise over gentle slop upland . all the 
 dew on the hedgerow sparkle . there be not a cloud in the heaven . up 
 rise from the green blade of corn a solitary skylark . his voice wake up 
 the other bird . a few minute more and the joyous concert begin . 
 Kenelm reverently doff his hat , and bow his head in mute homage and 
 thanksgiving . 



 CHAPTER II . 

 about nine o'clock Kenelm enter a town some twelve mile distant from 
 his father 's house , and towards which he have designedly make his way , 
 because in that town he be scarcely if at all know by sight , and he 
 might there make the purchase he require without attract any 
 mark observation . he have select for his travel costume a 
 shooting - dress , as the simple and least likely to belong to his 
 rank as a gentleman . but still in its very cut there be an air of 
 distinction , and every labourer he have meet on the way have touch his 
 hat to he . besides , who wear a shooting - dress in the middle of June , 
 or a shooting - dress at all , unless he be either a game - keeper or a 
 gentleman license to shoot ? 

 Kenelm enter a large store - shop for ready - make clothe and purchase 
 a suit such as might be wear on Sundays by a small country yeoman or 
 tenant - farmer of a petty holding , - a stout coarse broadcloth upper 
 garment , half coat , half jacket , with waistcoat to match , strong 
 corduroy trouser , a smart Belcher neckcloth , with a small stock of 
 linen and woollen sock in harmony with the other raiment . he buy 
 also a leathern knapsack , just big enough to contain this wardrobe , and 
 a couple of book , which with his comb and brush he have bring away 
 in his pocket ; for among all his trunk at home there be no knapsack . 

 these purchase make and pay for , he pass quickly through the town , 
 and stop at a humble inn at the outskirt , to which he be attract 
 by the notice , " refreshment for man and beast . " he enter a little 
 sand parlour , which at that hour he have all to himself , call for 
 breakfast , and devour the good part of a fourpenny loaf with a couple 
 of hard egg . 

 thus recruit , he again sally forth , and deviate into a thick wood 
 by the roadside , he exchange the habiliment with which he have leave 
 home for those he have purchase , and by the help of one or two big 
 stone sink the relinquished garment into a small but deep pool which 
 he be lucky enough to find in a bush - grow dell much haunted by snipe 
 in the winter . 

 " now , " say Kenelm , " I really begin to think I have get out of myself . 
 I be in another man 's skin ; for what , after all , be a skin but a soul 's 
 clothing , and what be clothing but a decenter skin ? of its own natural 
 skin every civilized soul be ashamed . it be the height of impropriety 
 for any one but the low kind of savage to show it . if the pure 
 soul now existent upon earth , the Pope of Rome 's or the Archbishop of 
 Canterbury 's , be to pass down the Strand with the skin which nature 
 give to it bare to the eye , it would be bring up before a magistrate , 
 prosecute by the Society for the Suppression of Vice , and commit to 
 jail as a public nuisance . 

 " decidedly I be now in another man 's skin . Kenelm Chillingly , I no 
 long 

  " remain 

  " your faithfully ; 

 " but be , 

  " with profound consideration , 

  " your obedient humble servant . " 

 with light step and elated crest , the wanderer , thus transform , sprang 
 from the wood into the dusty thoroughfare . he have travel on for about 
 an hour , meeting but few other passenger , when he hear to the right a 
 loud shrill young voice , " help ! help ! I will not go ; I tell you , I will 
 not ! " just before he stand , by a high five - bar gate , a pensive gray 
 cob attach to a neat - look gig . the bridle be loose on the cob 's 
 neck . the animal be evidently accustomed to stand quietly when order 
 to do so , and glad of the opportunity . 

 the cry , " help , help ! " be renew , mingle with loud tone in a 
 rougher voice , tone of wrath and menace . evidently these sound do 
 not come from the cob . Kenelm look over the gate , and see a few yard 
 distant in a grass field a well - dress boy struggle violently against 
 a stout middle - aged man who be rudely haul he along by the arm . 

 the chivalry natural to a namesake of the valiant Sir Kenelm Digby 
 be instantly arouse . he vault over the gate , seize the man by the 
 collar , and exclaim , " for shame ! what be you do to that poor boy ? 
 let he go ! " 

 " why the devil do you interfere ? " cry the stout man , his eye glare 
 and his lip foam with rage . " ah , be you the villain ? yes , no doubt 
 of it . I will give it to you , jackanape , " and still grasp the boy with 
 one hand , with the other the stout man dart a blow at Kenelm , from 
 which nothing less than the practise pugilistic skill and natural 
 alertness of the youth thus suddenly assaulted could have save his eye 
 and nose . as it be , the stout man have the bad of it : the blow be 
 parried , return with a dexterous manoeuvre of Kenelm 's right foot in 
 cornish fashion , and procumbit humi bos ; the stout man lie sprawl 
 on his back . the boy , thus release , seize hold of Kenelm by the arm , 
 and hurry he along up the field , cry , " come , come before he get 
 up ! save I ! save I ! " Ere he have recover his own surprise , the boy 
 have drag Kenelm to the gate , and jump into the gig , sob forth , 
 " get in , get in , I can not   drive ; get in , and drive - you . quick ! quick ! " 

 " but - " begin Kenelm . 

 " get in , or I shall go mad . " Kenelm obey ; the boy give he the rein , 
 and seize the whip himself , apply it lustily to the cob . on sprang 
 the cob . " stop , stop , stop , thief ! villain ! Holloa ! thief ! thief ! 
 thief ! stop ! " cry a voice behind . Kenelm involuntarily turn his 
 head and beheld the stout man perch upon the gate and gesticulate 
 furiously . it be but a glimpse ; again the whip be ply , the cob 
 frantically break into a gallop , the gig jolt and bump and swerve , 
 and it be not till they have put a good mile between themselves and the 
 stout man that Kenelm succeed in obtain possession of the whip and 
 calm the cob into a rational trot . 

 " young gentleman , " then say Kenelm , " perhaps you will have the goodness 
 to explain . " 

 " by and by ; get on , that be a good fellow ; you shall be well pay for it , 
 well and handsomely . " 

 Quoth Kenelm , gravely , " I know that in real life payment and service 
 naturally go together . but we will put aside the payment till you tell 
 I what be to be the service . and first , whither be I to drive you ? we 
 be come to a place where three road meet ; which of the three shall I 
 take ? " 

 " oh , I do not   know ; there be a finger - post . I want to get to , - but it be 
 a secret ; you will not betray I ? promise , - swear . " 

 " I do not   swear except when I be in a passion , which , I be sorry to say , 
 be very seldom ; and I do not   promise till I know what I promise ; neither 
 do I go on drive runaway boy in other man 's gig unless I know that I 
 be take they to a safe place , where their papa and mamma can get at 
 they . " 

 " I have no papa , no mamma , " say the boy , dolefully and with quivering 
 lip . 

 " poor boy ! I suppose that burly brute be your schoolmaster , and you be 
 run away home for fear of a flogging . " 

 the boy burst out laugh ; a pretty , silvery , merry laugh : it thrill 
 through Kenelm Chillingly . " no , he would not flog I : he be not a 
 schoolmaster ; he be bad than that . " 

 " be it possible ? what be he ? " 

 " an uncle . " 

 " Hum ! uncle be proverbial for cruelty ; be so in the classical day , 
 and Richard III . be the only scholar in his family . " 

 " eh ! classical and Richard III . ! " say the boy , startle , and look 
 attentively at the pensive driver . " who be you ? you talk like a 
 gentleman . " 

 " I beg pardon . I will not do so again if I can help it . " - " decidedly , " 
 think Kenelm , " I be begin to be amuse . what a blessing it be to 
 get into another man 's skin , and another man 's gig too ! " Aloud , " here 
 we be at the fingerpost . if you be run away from your uncle , it be 
 time to inform I where you be run to . " 

 here the boy lean over the gig and examine the fingerpost . then he 
 clap his hand joyfully . 

 " all right ! I think so , ' to Tor - Hadham , eighteen mile . ' that be the 
 road to ' Tor - Hadham . " 

 " do you mean to say I be to drive you all that way , - eighteen mile ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " and to whom be you go ? " 

 " I will tell you by and by . do go on ; do , pray . I can not   drive - never 
 drive in my life - or I would not ask you . pray , pray , do not   desert I ! 
 if you be a gentleman you will not ; and if you be not a gentleman , 
 I have get L10 in my purse , which you shall have when I be safe at 
 Tor - Hadham . do not   hesitate : my whole life be at stake ! " and the boy 
 begin once more to sob . 

 Kenelm direct the pony 's head towards Tor - Hadham , and the boy cease 
 to sob . 

 " you be a good , dear fellow , " say the boy , wipe his eye . " I be 
 afraid I be take you very much out of your road . " 

 " I have no road in particular , and would as soon go to Tor - Hadham , which 
 I have never see , as anywhere else . I be but a wanderer on the face of 
 the earth . " 

 " have you lose your papa and mamma too ? why , you be not much old than 
 I be . " 

 " little gentleman , " say Kenelm , gravely , " I be just of age , and you , I 
 suppose , be about fourteen . " 

 " what fun ! " cry the boy , abruptly . " be not   it fun ? " 

 " it will not be fun if I be sentence to penal servitude for steal 
 your uncle 's gig , and rob his little nephew of L10 . by the by , that 
 choleric relation of yours mean to knock down somebody else when he 
 strike at I . he ask , ' be you the villain ? ' pray who be the villain ? 
 he be evidently in your confidence . " 

 " Villain ! he be the most honourable , high - minded - but no matter now : 
 I will introduce you to he when we reach Tor - Hadham . whip that pony : he 
 be crawl . " 

 " it be up hill : a good man spar his beast . " 

 no art and no eloquence could extort from his young companion any 
 further explanation than Kenelm have yet receive ; and indeed , as the 
 journey advance , and they approach their destination , both party 
 sink into silence . Kenelm be seriously consider that his first day 's 
 experience of real life in the skin of another have place in some peril 
 his own . he have knock down a man evidently respectable and well to do , 
 have carry off that man 's nephew , and make free with that man 's good 
 and chattel ; namely , his gig and horse . all this might be explain 
 satisfactorily to a justice of the peace , but how ? by return to his 
 former skin ; by avow himself to be Kenelm Chillingly , a distinguished 
 university medalist , heir to no ignoble name and some L10,000 a year . 
 but then what a scandal ! he who abhor scandal ; in vulgar parlance , 
 what a " row ! " he who deny that the very word " row " be sanction 
 by any classic authority in the english language . he would have to 
 explain how he come to be find disguise , carefully disguise , in 
 garment such as no baronet 's eld son - even though that baronet be 
 the least ancestral man of mark whom it suit the convenience of a First 
 Minister to recommend to the Sovereign for exaltation over the rank 
 of Mister - be ever beheld in , unless he have take flight to the 
 gold - digging . be this a position in which the heir of the Chillinglys , 
 a distinguished family , whose coat - of - arm date from the early 
 authenticate period of English heraldry under Edward III . as Three 
 Fishes azure , could be place without grievous slur on the cold and 
 ancient blood of the Three Fishes ? 

 and then individually to himself , Kenelm , irrespectively of the Three 
 Fishes , - what a humiliation ! he have put aside his respected father 's 
 deliberate preparation for his entrance into real life ; he have 
 perversely choose his own walk on his own responsibility ; and here , 
 before half the first day be over , what an infernal scrape he have 
 walk himself into ! and what be his excuse ? a wretched little boy , 
 sob and chuckle by turn , and yet who be clever enough to twist 
 Kenelm Chillingly round his finger ; twist he , a man who think 
 himself so much wise than his parent , - a man who have gain honour at 
 the University , - a man of the grave temperament , - a man of so nicely 
 critical a turn of mind that there be not a law of art or nature in 
 which he do not detect a flaw ; that he should get himself into this 
 mess be , to say the least of it , an uncomfortable reflection . 

 the boy himself , as Kenelm glance at he from time to time , become 
 impish and will - of - the - Wisp - ish . sometimes he laugh to himself loudly , 
 sometimes he weep to himself quietly ; sometimes , neither laugh nor 
 weeping , he seem absorb in reflection . twice as they come near to 
 the town of Tor - Hadham , Kenelm nudge the boy , and say , " my boy , I must 
 talk with you ; " and twice the boy , withdraw his arm from the nudge , 
 have answer dreamily , " Hush ! I be think . " 

 and so they enter the town of Tor - Hadham , the cob very much do up . 



 CHAPTER III . 

 " now , young sir , " say Kenelm , in a tone calm , but peremptory , - " now we 
 be in the town , where be I to take you ? and wherever it be , there to 
 say good - by . " 

 " no , not good - by . stay with I a little bit . I begin to feel frightened , 
 and I be so friendless ; " and the boy , who have before resent the 
 slight nudge on the part of Kenelm , now wound his arm into Kenelm 's , 
 and clung to he caressingly . 

 I do not   know what my reader have hitherto thought of Kenelm Chillingly : 
 but , amid all the curve and winding of his whimsical humour , there be 
 one way that go straight to his heart ; you have only to be weak than 
 himself and ask his protection . 

 he turn round abruptly ; he forget all the strangeness of his position , 
 and reply : " little brute that you be , I will be shoot if I forsake you 
 if in trouble . but some compassion be also due to the cob : for his sake 
 say where we be to stop . " 

 " I be sure I can not   say : I never be here before . let we go to a nice 
 quiet inn . drive slowly : we will look out for one . " 

 Tor - Hadham be a large town , not nominally the capital of the county , 
 but , in point of trade and bustle and life , virtually the capital . the 
 straight street , through which the cob go as slowly as if he have 
 be draw a triumphal car up the Sacred Hill , present an animate 
 appearance . the shop have handsome facade and plate - glass window ; 
 the pavement exhibit a lively concourse , evidently not merely of 
 business , but of pleasure , for a large proportion of the passer - by be 
 compose of the fair sex , smartly dress , many of they young and some 
 pretty . in fact a regiment of her Majesty 's - - -th hussar have be 
 send into the town two day before ; and , between the officer of that 
 fortunate regiment and the fair sex in that hospitable town , there be 
 a natural emulation which should make the great number of slain and 
 wound . the advent of these hero , professional subtracter from 
 hostile and multiplier of friendly population , give a stimulus to 
 the caterer for those amusement which bring young folk 
 together , - archery - meeting , rifle - shooting , concert , ball , announce 
 in bill attach to board and wall and expose at shop - windows . 

 the boy look eagerly forth from the gig , scan especially these 
 advertisement , till at length he utter an excited exclamation , " ah , I 
 be right : there it be ! " 

 " there what be ? " ask Kenelm , - " the inn ? " his companion do not answer , 
 but Kenelm follow the boy 's eye perceive an immense hand - bill . 


  " to - MORROW NIGHT THEATRE OPENS . 

  " RICHARD III . Mr. COMPTON . " 


 " do just ask where the theatre be , " say the boy , in a whisper , turn 
 away his head . 

 Kenelm stop the cob , make the inquiry , and be direct to take the 
 next turn to the right . in a few minute the compo portico of an ugly 
 dilapidated building , dedicate to the Dramatic Muses , present itself 
 at the angle of a dreary , deserted lane . the wall be placard with 
 play - bill , in which the name of Compton stand forth as gigantic as 
 capital could make it . the boy draw a sigh . " now , " say he , " let we 
 look out for an inn near here , - the near . " 

 no inn , however , beyond the rank of a small and questionable look 
 public - house be apparent , until at a distance somewhat remote from the 
 theatre , and in a quaint , old - fashion , desert square , a neat , 
 newly whitewash house display upon its frontispiece , in large black 
 letter of funereal aspect , " Temperance Hotel . " 

 " stop , " say the boy ; " do not   you think that would suit we ? it look 
 quiet . " 

 " could not look more quiet if it be a tombstone , " reply Kenelm . 

 the boy put his hand upon the rein and stop the cob . the cob be in 
 that condition that the slight touch suffice to stop he , though he 
 turn his head somewhat ruefully as if in doubt whether hay and corn 
 would be within the regulation of a Temperance Hotel . Kenelm descend 
 and enter the house . a tidy woman emerge from a sort of glass 
 cupboard which constitute the bar , minus the comfort drink 
 associate with the beau ideal of a bar , but which display instead 
 two large decanter of cold water with tumbler a discretion , and 
 sundry plate of thin biscuit and sponge - cake . this tidy woman 
 politely inquire what be his " pleasure . " 

 " pleasure , " answer Kenelm , with his usual gravity , " be not the word I 
 should myself have choose . but could you oblige my horse - I mean that 
 horse - with a stall and a feed of oat , and that young gentleman and 
 myself with a private room and a dinner ? " 

 " Dinner ! " echo the hostess , - " dinner ! " 

 " a thousand pardon , ma'am . but if the word ' dinner ' shock you I retract 
 it , and would say instead something to eat and drink . ' " 

 " drink ! this be strictly a Temperance Hotel , sir . " 

 " oh , if you do not   eat and drink here , " exclaim Kenelm , fiercely , for 
 he be famish , " I wish you good morning . " 

 " stay a bit , sir . we do eat and drink here . but we be very simple 
 folk . we allow no fermented liquor . " 

 " not even a glass of beer ? " 

 " only ginger - beer . Alcohols be strictly forbid . we have tea and 
 coffee and milk . but most of our customer prefer the pure liquid . as 
 for eating , sir , - anything you order , in reason . " 

 Kenelm shake his head and be retreat , when the boy , who have spring 
 from the gig and overheard the conversation , cry petulantly , " what 
 do it signify ? who want ferment liquor ? water will do very well . 
 and as for dinner , - anything convenient . please , ma'am , show we into a 
 private room : I be so tired . " the last word be say in a caressing 
 manner , and so prettily , that the hostess at once change her tone , 
 and mutter , " poor boy ! " and , in a still more subdued mutter , " what 
 a pretty face he have ! " nod , and lead the way up a very clean 
 old - fashioned staircase . 

 " but the horse and gig , where be they to go ? " say Kenelm , with a pang 
 of conscience on reflect how ill treat hitherto have be both horse 
 and owner . 

 " oh , as for the horse and gig , sir , you will find Jukes 's livery - stable 
 a few yard far down . we do not   take in horse ourselves ; our 
 customer seldom keep they : but you will find the good of accommodation 
 at Jukes 's . " 

 Kenelm conduct the cob to the livery - stable thus indicate , and 
 wait to see he walk about to cool , well rub down , and make 
 comfortable over half a peck of oats , - for Kenelm Chillingly be a 
 humane man to the brute creation , - and then , in a state of ravenous 
 appetite , return to the Temperance Hotel , and be usher into a small 
 drawing - room , with a small bit of carpet in the centre , six small chair 
 with cane seat , print on the wall descriptive of the various 
 effect of intoxicate liquor upon sundry specimen of mankind , - some 
 resemble ghost , other fiend , and all with a general aspect of 
 beggary and perdition ; contrast by Happy - Family picture , - smile 
 wife , portly husband , rosy infant , emblematic of the beatified 
 condition of member of the Temperance Society . 

 a table with a spotless cloth , and knife and fork for two , chiefly , 
 however , attract Kenelm 's attention . 

 the boy be stand by the window , seemingly gaze on a small aquarium 
 which be there place , and contain the usual variety of small fish , 
 reptile , and insect , enjoy the pleasure of Temperance in its 
 native element , include , of course , an occasional meal upon each 
 other . 

 " what be they go to give we to eat ? " inquire Kenelm . " it must be 
 ready by this time I should think . " 

 here he give a brisk tug at the bell - pull . the boy advance from 
 the window , and as he do so Kenelm be strike with the grace of his 
 bearing , and the improvement in his look , now that he be without his 
 hat , and rest and ablution have refresh from heat and dust the delicate 
 bloom of his complexion . there be no doubt about it that he be an 
 exceedingly pretty boy , and if he live to be a man would make many a 
 lady 's heart ache . it be with a certain air of gracious superiority 
 such as be seldom warrant by superior rank if it be less than royal , 
 and chiefly become a marked seniority in year , that this young 
 gentleman , approach the solemn heir of the Chillinglys , hold out his 
 hand and say , - 

 " Sir , you have behave extremely well , and I thank you very much . " 

 " your Royal Highness be condescend to say so , " reply Kenelm 
 Chillingly , bow low , " but have you order dinner ? and what be 
 they go to give we ? no one seem to answer the bell here . as it be a 
 Temperance Hotel , probably all the servant be drunk . " 

 " why should they be drunk at a Temperance Hotel ? " 

 " why ! because , as a general rule , people who flagrantly pretend to 
 anything be the reverse of that which they pretend to . a man who set 
 up for a saint be sure to be a sinner , and a man who boast that he be a 
 sinner be sure to have some feeble , maudlin , snivel bit of saintship 
 about he which be enough to make he a humbug . masculine honesty , 
 whether it be saint - like or sinner - like , do not label itself either 
 saint or sinner . Fancy Saint Augustine label himself saint , or 
 Robert Burns sinner ; and therefore , though , little boy , you have 
 probably not read the poem of Robert Burns , and have certainly not read 
 the ' Confessions ' of Saint Augustine , take my word for it , that both 
 those personage be very good fellow ; and with a little difference of 
 training and experience , Burns might have write the ' Confessions ' and 
 augustine the poem . power above ! I be starve . what do you order for 
 dinner , and when be it to appear ? " 

 the boy , who have open to an enormous width a naturally large pair of 
 hazel eye , while his tall companion in fustian trouser and Belcher 
 neckcloth speak thus patronizingly of Robert Burns and Saint Augustine , 
 now reply , with rather a deprecatory and shamefaced aspect , " I be 
 sorry I be not think of dinner . I be not so mindful of you as I 
 ought to have be . the landlady ask I what we would have . I say , 
 ' what you like ; ' and the landlady mutter something about - " here the 
 boy hesitate . 

 " yes . about what ? mutton - chop ? " 

 " no . Cauliflowers and rice - pudding . " 

 Kenelm Chillingly never swear , never rag . where ruder being of 
 human mould swear or rag , he vent displeasure in an expression of 
 countenance so pathetically melancholic and lugubrious that it would 
 have melt the heart of an hyrcanian tiger . he turn his countenance 
 now on the boy , and murmur " Cauliflower ! - Starvation ! " sink into 
 one of the cane - bottom chair , and add quietly , ' so much for human 
 gratitude . " 

 the boy be evidently smite to the heart by the bitter sweetness 
 of this reproach . there be almost tear in his voice , as he say 
 falteringly , " pray forgive I , I be ungrateful . I will run down and see 
 what there be ; " and , suit the action to the word , he disappear . 

 Kenelm remain motionless ; in fact he be plunge into one of those 
 revery , or rather absorption of inward and spiritual being , into 
 which it be say that the consciousness of the indian dervish can be by 
 prolong fasting preternaturally resolve . the appetite of all man 
 of powerful muscular development be of a nature far exceed the 
 property of any reasonable number of cauliflower and rice - pudding 
 to satisfy . Witness Hercules himself , whose craving for substantial 
 nourishment be the stand joke of the classic poet . I do not   know 
 that Kenelm Chillingly would have beat the Theban Hercules either in 
 fighting or in eat ; but , when he want to fight or when he want 
 to eat , Hercules would have have to put forth all his strength not to be 
 beat . 

 after ten minute ' absence , the boy come back radiant . he tap Kenelm 
 on the shoulder , and say playfully , " I make they cut a whole loin into 
 chop , besides the cauliflower ; and such a big rice - pudding , and egg 
 and bacon too ! cheer up ! it will be serve in a minute . " 

 " a - h ! " say Kenelm . 

 " they be good people ; they do not mean to stint you : but most of their 
 customer , it seem , live upon vegetable and farinaceous food . there 
 be a society here form upon that principle ; the landlady say they be 
 philosopher ! " 

 at the word " philosopher " Kenelm 's crest rise as that of a practised 
 hunter at the cry of " Yoiks ! Tally - ho ! " " philosopher ! " say he , 
 " philosopher indeed ! o ignoramus , who do not even know the structure 
 of the human tooth ! look you , little boy , if nothing be leave on this 
 earth of the present race of man , as we be assure upon great authority 
 will be the case one of these day , - and a mighty good riddance it will 
 be , - if nothing , I say , of man be leave except fossil of his tooth and 
 his thumb , a philosopher of that superior race which will succeed to 
 man would at once see in those relic all his characteristic and all 
 his history ; would say , compare his thumb with the talon of an eagle , 
 the claw of a tiger , the hoof of a horse , the owner of that thumb must 
 have be lord over creature with talon and claw and hoof . you may 
 say the monkey tribe have thumb . true ; but compare an ape 's thumb with 
 a man 's : could the big ape 's thumb have build Westminster Abbey ? but 
 even thumb be trivial evidence of man as compare with his tooth . 
 look at his tooth ! " - here Kenelm expand his jaw from ear to ear 
 and display semicircle of ivory , so perfect for the purpose of 
 mastication that the most artistic dentist might have despair of 
 his power to imitate they , - " look , I say , at his tooth ! " the 
 boy involuntarily recoil . " be the tooth those of a miserable 
 cauliflower - eater ? or be it purely by farinaceous food that the 
 proprietor of tooth like man 's obtain the rank of the sovereign 
 destroyer of creation ? no , little boy , no , " continue Kenelm , close 
 his jaw , but advance upon the infant , who at each stride recede 
 towards the aquarium , - " no ; man be the master of the world , because 
 of all create being he devour the great variety and the great 
 number of create thing . his tooth evince that man can live upon every 
 soil from the torrid to the frozen zone , because man can eat everything 
 that other creature can not eat . and the formation of his tooth prove 
 it . a tiger can eat a deer ; so can man : but a tiger can not   eat an eel ; 
 man can . an elephant can eat cauliflower and rice - pudding ; so can man ! 
 but an elephant can not   eat a beefsteak ; man can . in sum , man can 
 live everywhere , because he can eat anything , thank to his dental 
 formation ! " conclude Kenelm , make a prodigious stride towards the 
 boy . " Man , when everything else fail he , eat his own specie . " 

 " do not ; you frighten I , " say the boy . " aha ! " clap his hand with a 
 sensation of gleeful relief , " here come the mutton - chop ! " 

 a wonderfully clean , well - wash , indeed well - wash - out , middle - aged 
 parlour - maid now appear , dish in hand . put the dish on the table 
 and take off the cover , the handmaiden say civilly , though frigidly , 
 like one who live upon salad and cold water , " mistress be sorry to have 
 keep you wait , but she think you be Vegetarians . " 

 after help his young friend to a mutton - chop , Kenelm help himself , 
 and reply gravely , " tell your mistress that if she have only give 
 we vegetable , I should have eat you . tell she that though man be 
 partially graminivorous , he be principally carnivorous . tell she that 
 though a swine eat cabbage and such like , yet where a swine can get 
 a baby , it eat the baby . tell she , " continue Kenelm   now at his third 
 chop , " that there be no animal that in digestive organ more resemble 
 man than a swine . ask she if there be any baby in the house ; if so , it 
 would be safe for the baby to send up some more chop . " 

 as the acute observer could rarely be quite sure when Kenelm 
 Chillingly be in jest or in earnest , the parlour - maid pause a moment 
 and attempt a pale smile . Kenelm lift his dark eye , unspeakably sad 
 and profound , and say mournfully , " I should be so sorry for the baby . 
 bring the chop ! " the parlour - maid vanish . the boy lay down his 
 knife and fork , and look fixedly and inquisitively on Kenelm . Kenelm , 
 unheede the look , place the last chop on the boy 's plate . 

 " no more , " cry the boy , impulsively , and return the chop to the 
 dish . " I have din : I have have enough . " 

 " little boy , you lie , " say Kenelm ; " you have not have enough to keep 
 body and soul together . eat that chop or I shall thrash you : whatever I 
 say I do . " 

 somehow or other the boy feel quell ; he eat the chop in silence , again 
 look at Kenelm 's face , and say to himself , " I be afraid . " 

 the parlour - maid here enter with a fresh supply of chop and a dish of 
 bacon and egg , soon follow by a rice - pudding bake in a tin dish , and 
 of size sufficient to have nourish a charity school . when the repast 
 be finish , Kenelm seem to forget the dangerous property of the 
 carnivorous animal ; and stretch himself indolently out , appear 
 to be as innocently ruminative as the most domestic of animal 
 graminivorous . 

 then say the boy , rather timidly , " may I ask you another favour ? " 

 " be it to knock down another uncle , or to steal another gig and cob ? " 

 " no , it be very simple : it be merely to find out the address of a friend 
 here ; and when find to give he a note from I . " 

 " do the commission press ? ' after dinner , rest a while , ' saith the 
 proverb ; and proverb be so wise that no one can guess the author 
 of they . they be suppose to be fragment of the philosophy of the 
 antediluvian : come to we pack up in the ark . " 

 " really , indeed , " say the boy , seriously . " how interesting ! no , my 
 commission do not press for an hour or so . do you think , sir , they have 
 any drama before the Deluge ? " 

 " Drama ! not a doubt of it . man who live one or two thousand year have 
 time to invent and improve everything ; and a play could have have its 
 natural length then . it would not have be necessary to crowd the 
 whole history of Macbeth , from his youth to his old age , into an absurd 
 epitome of three hour . one can not trace a touch of real human nature in 
 any actor 's delineation of that very interesting Scotchman , because 
 the actor always come on the stage as if he be the same age when he 
 murder Duncan , and when , in his sear and yellow leaf , he be lop 
 off by Macduff . " 

 " do you think Macbeth be young when he murder Duncan ? " 

 " certainly . no man ever commit a first crime of violent nature , such as 
 murder , after thirty ; if he begin before , he may go on up to any age . 
 but youth be the season for commence those wrong calculation which 
 belong to irrational hope and the sense of physical power . you thus 
 read in the newspaper that the person who murder their sweetheart be 
 generally from two to six and twenty ; and person who murder from other 
 motif than love - that be , from revenge , avarice , or ambition - be 
 generally about twenty - eight , - Iago 's age . twenty - eight be the usual 
 close of the active season for get rid of one 's fellow - creature ; a 
 prize - fighter fall off after that age . I take it that Macbeth be about 
 twenty - eight when he murder Duncan , and from about fifty - four to sixty 
 when he begin to whine about miss the comfort of old age . but 
 can any audience understand that difference of year in see a 
 three - hour ' play ? or do any actor ever pretend to impress it on the 
 audience , and appear as twenty - eight in the first act and a sexagenarian 
 in the fifth ? " 

 " I never think of that , " say the boy , evidently interested . " but I 
 never see ' Macbeth . ' I have see ' Richard III . : ' be not that nice ? do not 
 you dote on the play ? I do . what a glorious life an actor 's must be ! " 

 Kenelm , who have be hitherto rather talk to himself than to his 
 youthful companion , here rouse his attention , look on the boy 
 intently , and say , - 

 " I see you be stage - stricken . you have run away from home in order to 
 turn player , and I should not wonder if this note you want I to give be 
 for the manager of the theatre or one of his company . " 

 the young face that encounter Kenelm 's dark eye become very flushed , 
 but set and defiant in its expression . 

 " and what if it be ? would not you give it ? " 

 " what ! help a child of your age run away from his home , to go upon the 
 stage against the consent of his relation ? certainly not . " 

 " I be not a child ; but that have nothing to do with it . I do not   want to 
 go on the stage , at all event without the consent of the person who 
 have a right to dictate my action . my note be not to the manager of 
 the theatre , nor to one of his company ; but it be to a gentleman who 
 condescend to act here for a few night ; a thorough gentleman , - a great 
 actor , - my friend , the only friend I have in the world . I say frankly I 
 have run away from home so that he may have that note , and if you will 
 not give it some one else will ! " 

 the boy have rise while he speak , and he stand erect beside the 
 recumbent Kenelm , his lip quivering , his eye suffuse with suppress 
 tear , but his whole aspect resolute and determine . evidently , if he 
 do not get his own way in this world , it would not be for want of will . 

 " I will take your note , " say Kenelm . 

 " there it be ; give it into the hand of the person it be address 
 to , - Mr. Herbert Compton . " 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 KENELM take his way to the theatre , and inquire of the door - keeper for 
 Mr. Herbert Compton . that functionary reply , " Mr. Compton do not act 
 to - night , and be not in the house . " 

 " where do he lodge ? " 

 the door - keeper point to a grocer 's shop on the other side of the way , 
 and say tersely , " there , private door ; knock and ring . " 

 Kenelm do as he be direct . a slatternly maid - servant open the 
 door , and , in answer to his interrogatory , say that Mr. Compton be at 
 home , but at supper . 

 " I be sorry to disturb he , " say Kenelm , raise his voice , for he 
 hear a clatter of knife and plate within a room hard by at his left , 
 " but my business require to see he forthwith ; " and , push the maid 
 aside , he enter at once the adjoining banquet - hall . 

 before a savoury stew smell strongly of onion sit a man very much at 
 his ease , without coat or neckcloth , - a decidedly handsome man , his hair 
 cut short and his face closely shaven , as befit an actor who have wig 
 and beard of all hue and form at his command . the man be not alone ; 
 opposite to he sit a lady , who might be a few year young , of a 
 somewhat faded complexion , but still pretty , with good stage feature 
 and a profusion of blond ringlet . 

 " Mr. Compton , I presume , " say Kenelm , with a solemn bow . 

 " my name be Compton : any message from the theatre ? or what do you want 
 with I ? " 

 " i - nothing ! " reply Kenelm ; and then deepen his naturally mournful 
 voice into tone ominous and tragic , continue , " by whom you be want 
 let this explain ; " therewith he place in Mr. Compton 's hand the letter 
 with which he be charge , and stretch his arm and interlace his 
 finger in the pose of Talma as Julius Caesar , add , " ' Qu'en dis - tu , 
 Brute ? ' " 

 whether it be from the sombre aspect and awe - inspire delivery of 
 the messenger , or the sight of the handwriting on the address of the 
 missive , Mr. Compton 's countenance suddenly fall , and his hand rest 
 irresolute , as if not dare to open the letter . 

 " never mind I , dear , " say the lady with blond ringlet , in a tone of 
 sting affability : " read your billet - doux ; do not   keep the young man 
 waiting , love ! " 

 " Nonsense , Matilda , nonsense ! billet - doux indeed ! more likely a bill 
 from Duke the tailor . excuse I for a moment , my dear . follow I , sir , " 
 and rise , still with shirtsleeve uncover , he quit the room , 
 close the door after he , motion Kenelm into a small parlour on the 
 opposite side of the passage , and by the light of a suspend gas - lamp 
 run his eye hastily over the letter , which , though it seem very short , 
 draw from he sundry exclamation . " good heaven , how very absurd ! 
 what be to be do ? " then , thrust the letter into his trouser - pocket , 
 he fix upon Kenelm a very brilliant pair of dark eye , which soon 
 drop before the steadfast look of that saturnine adventurer . 

 " be you in the confidence of the writer of this letter ? " ask Mr. 
 Compton , rather confusedly . 

 " I be not the confidant of the writer , " answer Kenelm , " but for the 
 time be I be the protector ! " 

 " Protector ! " 

 " Protector . " 

 Mr. Compton again eye the messenger , and this time fully realize the 
 gladiatorial development of that dark stranger 's physical form , he grow 
 many shade paler , and involuntarily retreat towards the bell - pull . 

 after a short pause , he say , " I be request to call on the writer . if 
 I do so , may I understand that the interview will be strictly private ? " 

 " so far as I be concern , yes : on the condition that no attempt be make 
 to withdraw the writer from the house . " 

 " certainly not , certainly not ; quite the contrary , " exclaim Mr. 
 Compton , with genuine animation . " say I will call in half an hour . " 

 " I will give your message , " say Kenelm , with a polite inclination of 
 his head ; " and pray pardon I if I remind you that I style myself the 
 protector of your correspondent , and if the slight advantage be
