 take of that correspondent 's youth and inexperience or the small 
 encouragement be give to plan of abduction from home and friend , the 
 stage will lose an ornament and Herbert Compton vanish from the scene . " 
 with these word Kenelm leave the player stand aghast . gain the 
 street - door , a lad with a band - box run against he and be nearly upset . 

 " Stupid , " cry the lad , " can not   you see where you be go ? give this 
 to Mrs. Compton . " 

 " I should deserve the title you give if I do for nothing the business 
 for which you be pay , " reply Kenelm , sententiously , and stride on . 



 CHAPTER V. 

 " I have fulfil my mission , " say Kenelm , on rejoin his travel 
 companion . " Mr. Compton say he would be here in half an hour . " 

 " you see he ? " 

 " of course : I promise to give your letter into his own hand . " 

 " be he alone ? " 

 " no ; at supper with his wife . " 

 " his wife ! what do you mean , sir ? - wife ! he have no wife . " 

 " appearance be deceitful . at least he be with a lady who call he 
 ' dear ' and ' love ' in as spiteful a tone of voice as if she have be his 
 wife ; and as I be come out of his street - door a lad who run against 
 I ask I to give a band - box to Mrs. Compton . " 

 the boy turn as white as death , stagger back a few step , and 
 drop into a chair . 

 a suspicion which during his absence have suggest itself to Kenelm 's 
 inquire mind now take strong confirmation . he approach softly , draw 
 a chair close to the companion whom fate have force upon he , and say 
 in a gentle whisper , - 

 " this be no boy 's agitation . if you have be deceive or mislead , and 
 I can in any way advise or aid you , count on I as woman under the 
 circumstance count on man and gentleman . " 

 the boy start to his foot , and pace the room with disorder step , 
 and a countenance work with passion which he attempt vainly to 
 suppress . suddenly arrest his step , he seize Kenelm 's hand , press 
 it convulsively , and say , in a voice struggle against a sob , - 

 " I thank you , - I bless you . leave I now : I would be alone . alone , too , 
 I must face this man . there may be some mistake yet ; go . " 

 " you will promise not to leave the house till I return ? " 

 " yes , I promise that . " 

 " and if it be as I fear , you will then let I counsel with and advise 
 you ? " 

 " heaven help I , if so ! whom else should I trust to ? go , go ! " 

 Kenelm once more find himself in the street , beneath the mingled light 
 of gas - lamp and the midsummer moon . he walk on mechanically till he 
 reach the extremity of the town . there he halt , and seat himself 
 on a milestone , indulge in these meditation : - 

 " Kenelm , my friend , you be in a still bad scrape than I think you 
 be an hour ago . you have evidently now get a woman on your hand . what 
 on earth be you to do with she ? a runaway woman , who , mean to run 
 off with somebody else - such be the cross and contradiction in human 
 destiny - have run off with you instead . what mortal can hope to be safe ? 
 the last thing I think could befall I when I get up this morning be 
 that I should have any trouble about the other sex before the day be 
 over . if I be of an amatory temperament , the Fates might have some 
 justification for lead I into this snare , but , as it be , those 
 meddle old maid have none . Kenelm , my friend , do you think you ever 
 can be in love ? and , if you be in love , do you think you could be a 
 great fool than you be now ? " 

 Kenelm have not decide this knotty question in the conference hold with 
 himself , when a light and soft strain of music come upon his ear . it be 
 but from a string instrument , and might have sound thin and tinkle 
 but for the stillness of the night , and that peculiar addition of 
 fulness which music acquire when it be bear along a tranquil air . 
 presently a voice in song be hear from the distance accompany 
 the instrument . it be a man 's voice , a mellow and a rich voice , but 
 Kenelm 's ear could not catch the word . mechanically he move on towards 
 the quarter from which the sound come , for Kenelm Chillingly have music 
 in his soul , though he be not quite aware of it himself . he see before 
 he a patch of greensward , on which grow a solitary elm with a seat 
 for wayfarer beneath it . from this sward the ground recede in a wide 
 semicircle border partly by shop , partly by the tea - garden of a 
 pretty cottage - like tavern . round the table scatter throughout the 
 garden be group quiet customer , evidently belong to the class 
 of small tradespeople or superior artisan . they have an appearance of 
 decorous respectability , and be listen intently to the music . so 
 be many person at the shop - door and at the window of upper room . 
 on the sward , a little in advance of the tree , but beneath its shadow , 
 stand the musician , and in that musician Kenelm recognize the wanderer 
 from whose talk he have conceive the idea of the pedestrian excursion 
 which have already bring he into a very awkward position . the 
 instrument on which the singer accompany himself be a guitar , and his 
 song be evidently a love - song , though , as it be now draw near to 
 its close , Kenelm could but imperfectly guess at its general meaning . 
 he hear enough to perceive that its word be at least free from the 
 vulgarity which generally characterize street ballad , and be yet 
 simple enough to please a very homely audience . 

 when the singer end there be no applause ; but there be evident 
 sensation among the audience , - a feeling as if something that have give 
 a common enjoyment have cease . presently the white pomeranian dog , who 
 have hitherto keep himself out of sight under the seat of the elm - tree , 
 advance , with a small metal tray between his tooth , and , after look 
 round he deliberately , as if to select whom of the audience should 
 be honour with the commencement of a general subscription , gravely 
 approach Kenelm , stand on his hind leg , star at he , and present 
 the tray . 

 Kenelm drop a shilling into that depository , and the dog , look 
 gratify , take his way towards the tea - garden . lift his hat , for he 
 be , in his way , a very polite man , Kenelm approach the singer , and , 
 trust to the alteration in his dress for not be recognize by a 
 stranger who have only once before encounter he he say , - 

 " judge by the little I hear , you sing very well , sir . may I ask who 
 compose the word ? " 

 " they be mine , " reply the singer . 

 " and the air ? " 

 " mine too . " 

 " accept my compliment . I hope you find these manifestation of genius 
 lucrative ? " 

 the singer , who have not hitherto vouchsafe more than a careless glance 
 at the rustic garb of the questioner , now fix his eye full upon 
 Kenelm , and say , with a smile , " your voice betray you , sir . we have 
 meet before . " 

 " true ; but I do not then notice your guitar , nor , though acquaint 
 with your poetical gift , suppose that you select this primitive 
 method of make they publicly know . " 

 " nor do I anticipate the pleasure of meet you again in the character 
 of Hobnail . Hist ! let we keep each other 's secret . I be know hereabouts 
 by no other designation than that of the ' Wandering Minstrel . ' " 

 " it be in the capacity of minstrel that I address you . if it be not an 
 impertinent question , do you know any song which take the other side of 
 the case ? " 

 " what case ? I do not   understand you , sir . " 

 " the song I hear seem in praise of that sham call love . do not   you 
 think you could say something more new and more true , treat that 
 aberration from reason with the contempt it deserve ? " 

 " not if I be to get my travel expense pay . " 

 " what ! the folly be so popular ? " 

 " do not your own heart tell you so ? " 

 " not a bit of it , - rather the contrary . your audience at present 
 seem folk who live by work , and can have little time for such idle 
 phantasy ; for , as it be well observe by Ovid , a poet who write much 
 on that subject , and profess the most intimate acquaintance with it , 
 ' idleness be the parent of love . ' can not   you sing something in praise of 
 a good dinner ? everybody who work hard have an appetite for food . " 

 the singer again fix on Kenelm his inquire eye , but not detect a 
 vestige of humour in the grave face he contemplate , be rather puzzle 
 how to reply , and therefore remain silent . 

 " I perceive , " resume Kenelm , " that my observation surprise you : the 
 surprise will vanish on reflection . it have be say by another poet , 
 more reflective than Ovid , that ' the world be govern by love and 
 hunger . ' but hunger certainly have the lion 's share of the government ; 
 and if a poet be really to do what he pretend to do , - namely , represent 
 nature , - the great part of his lay should be address to the 
 stomach . " here , warm with his subject , Kenelm familiarly lay his 
 band on the musician 's shoulder , and his voice take a tone border on 
 enthusiasm . " you will allow that a man in the normal condition of health 
 do not fall in love every day . but in the normal condition of health 
 he be hungry every day . Nay , in those early year when you poet say he 
 be most prone to love , he be so especially disposed to hunger that 
 less than three meal a day can scarcely satisfy his appetite . you may 
 imprison a man for month , for year , nay , for his whole life , - from 
 infancy to any age which Sir Cornewall Lewis may allow he to 
 attain , - without let he be in love at all . but if you shut he up 
 for a week without put something into his stomach , you will find he 
 at the end of it as dead as a door - nail . " 

 here the singer , who have gradually retreat before the energetic 
 advance of the orator , sink into the seat by the elm - tree and say 
 pathetically , " Sir , you have fairly argue I down . will you please to 
 come to the conclusion which you deduce from your premise ? " 

 " simply this , that where you find one human being who care about love , 
 you will find a thousand susceptible to the charm of a dinner ; and if 
 you wish to be the popular minne - singer or troubadour of the age , appeal 
 to nature , sir , - appeal to nature ; drop all hackneyed rhapsody about a 
 rosy cheek , and strike your lyre to the theme of a beefsteak . " 

 the dog have for some minute regain his master 's side , stand on 
 his hind leg , with the tray , tolerably well fill with copper coin , 
 between his tooth ; and now , justly aggrieve by the inattention which 
 detain he in that artificial attitude , drop the tray and growl 
 at Kenelm . 

 at the same time there come an impatient sound from the audience in the 
 tea - garden . they want another song for their money . 

 the singer rise , obedient to the summon . " excuse I , sir ; but I be 
 call upon to - " 

 " to sing again ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " and on the subject I suggest ? " 

 " no , indeed . " 

 " what ! love , again ? " 

 " I be afraid so . " 

 " I wish you good evening then . you seem a well - educate man , - more shame 
 to you . perhaps we may meet once more in our ramble , when the question 
 can be properly argue out . " 

 Kenelm lift his hat , and turn on his heel . before he reach the 
 street , the sweet voice of the singer again smite his ear ; but the only 
 word distinguishable in the distance , ring out at the close of the 
 refrain , be " love . " 

 " fiddle - de - dee , " say Kenelm . 



 CHAPTER VI . 

 AS Kenelm regain the street dignify by the edifice of the Temperance 
 Hotel , a figure , dress picturesquely in a spanish cloak , brush 
 hurriedly by he , but not so fast as to be unrecognized as the 
 tragedian . " Hem ! " mutter Kenelm , " I do not   think there be much triumph 
 in that face . I suspect he have be scold . " 

 the boy - if Kenelm 's travel companion be still to be so 
 designate - be lean against the mantelpiece as Kenelm re - enter

 listless attitude and in the droop tearless eye . 

 " my dear child , " say Kenelm , in the soft tone of his plaintive 
 voice , " do not honour I with any confidence that may be painful . but 
 let I hope that you have dismiss forever all thought of go on the 
 stage . " 

 " yes , " be the scarce audible answer . 

 " and now only remain the question , ' what be to be do ? ' " 

 " I be sure I do not   know , and I do not   care . " 

 " then you leave it to I to know and to care ; and assume for the 
 moment as a fact that which be one of the great lie in this 
 mendacious world - namely , that all man be brother - you will consider 
 I as an eld brother , who will counsel and control you as he would 
 an imprudent young - sister . I see very well how it be . somehow or other 
 you , have first admire Mr. Compton as Romeo or Richard III . , make his 
 acquaintance as Mr. Compton . he allow you to believe he a single 
 man . in a romantic moment you escape from your home , with the design of 
 adopt the profession of the stage and of become Mrs. Compton . " 

 " oh , " break out the girl , since her sex must now be declare , " oh , " she 
 exclaim , with a passionate sob , " what a fool I have be ! only do not

 would not have appear . I should not have know he have one and - and - " 
 here her voice be choke under her passion . 

 " but now you have discover the truth , let we thank Heaven that you be 
 save from shame and misery . I must despatch a telegram to your uncle : 
 give I his address . " 

 " no , no . " 

 " there be not a ' no ' possible in this case , my child . your reputation 
 and your future must be save . leave I to explain all to your uncle . 
 he be your guardian . I must send for he ; nay , nay , there be no option . 
 hate I now for enforce your will : you will thank I hereafter . and 
 listen , young lady ; if it do pain you to see your uncle , and encounter 
 his reproach , every fault must undergo its punishment . a brave nature 
 undergo it cheerfully , as a part of atonement . you be brave . submit , 
 and in submit rejoice ! " 

 there be something in Kenelm 's voice and manner at once so kindly and 
 so command that the wayward nature he address fairly succumb . 
 she give he her uncle 's address , " John Bovill , Esq . , Oakdale , near 
 Westmere . " and after give it , she fix her eye mournfully upon her 
 young adviser , and say with a simple , dreary pathos , " now , will you 
 esteem I more , or rather despise I less ? " 

 she look so young , nay , so childlike , as she thus speak , that Kenelm 
 feel a parental inclination to draw she on his lap and kiss away 
 her tear . but he prudently conquer that impulse , and say , with a 
 melancholy half - smile , - 

 " if human being despise each other for be young and foolish , the 
 soon we be exterminate by that superior race which be to succeed we 
 on earth the well it will be . adieu , till your uncle come . " 

 " what ! you leave I here - alone ? " 

 " Nay , if your uncle find I under the same roof , now that I know you 
 be his niece , do not   you think he would have a right to throw I out 
 of the window ? allow I to practise for myself the prudence I preach 
 to you . send for the landlady to show you your room , shut yourself in 
 there , go to bed , and do not   cry more than you can help . " 

 Kenelm shoulder the knapsack he have deposit in a corner of the room , 
 inquire for the telegraph - office , despatch a telegram to Mr. Bovill , 
 obtain a bedroom at the Commercial Hotel , and fall asleep , mutter 
 these sensible word , - 

 " Rouchefoucauld be perfectly right when he say , ' very few people would 
 fall in love if they have not hear it so much talk about . ' " 



 CHAPTER VII . 

 KENELM CHILLINGLY rise with the sun , accord to his usual custom , and 
 take his way to the Temperance Hotel . all in that sober building seem 
 still in the arm of Morpheus . he turn towards the stable in which he 
 have leave the gray cob , and have the pleasure to see that ill - use animal 
 in the healthful process of rub down . 

 " that be right , " say he to the hostler . " I be glad to see you be so 
 early a riser . " 

 " why , " quoth the hostler , " the gentleman as own the pony knock I 
 up at two o'clock in the morning , and please enough he be to see the 
 creature again lie down in the clean straw . " 

 " oh , he have arrive at the hotel , I presume ? - a stout gentleman ? " 

 " yes , stout enough ; and a passionate gentleman too . come in a yellow and 
 two poster , knock up the temperance and then knock up I to see 
 for the pony , and be much put out as he could not get any grog at the 
 temperance . " 

 " I dare say he be . I wish he have get his grog : it might have put he in 
 well humour . poor little thing ! " mutter Kenelm , turn away ; " I be 
 afraid she be in for a regular vituperation . my turn next , I suppose . 
 but he must be a good fellow to have come at once for his niece in the 
 dead of the night . " 

 about nine o'clock Kenelm present himself again at the Temperance

 have no other costume at hand . to Kenelm 's great relief , Mr. Bovill

 to Kenelm , say , - 

 " Sir , you be a gentleman ; sit down , sit down and take breakfast . " 

 then , as soon as the maid be out of the room , the uncle continue , - 

 " I have hear all your good conduct from this young simpleton . thing 
 might have be bad , sir . " 

 Kenelm bow his head , and draw the loaf towards he in silence . then , 
 consider that some apology be due to his entertainer , he say , - 

 " I hope you forgive I for that unfortunate mistake , when - " 

 " you knock I down , or rather trip I up . all right now . Elsie , 
 give the gentleman a cup of tea . pretty little rogue , be she not ? and a 
 good girl , in spite of her nonsense . it be all my fault let she go 
 to the play and be intimate with Miss Lockit , a stage - stricken , foolish 
 old maid , who ought to have know well than to lead she into all this 
 trouble . " 

 " no , uncle , " cry the girl , resolutely ; " do not   blame she , nor any one 
 but I . " 

 Kenelm turn his dark eye approvingly towards the girl , and see that 
 her lip be firmly set ; there be an expression , not of grief nor 
 shame , but compress resolution in her countenance . but when her eye 
 meet his they fall softly , and a blush mantle over her cheek up to she 
 very forehead . 

 " ah ! " say the uncle , " just like you , Elsie ; always ready to take 
 everybody 's fault on your own shoulder . well , well , say no more about 
 that . now , my young friend , what bring you across the country tramp 
 it on foot , eh ? a young man 's whim ? " as he speak , he eye Kenelm very 
 closely , and his look be that of an intelligent man not unaccustomed to 
 observe the face of those he converse with . in fact a more shrewd man 
 of business than Mr. Bovill be seldom meet with on ' Change or in market . 

 " I travel on foot to please myself , sir , " answer Kenelm , curtly , and 
 unconsciously set on his guard . 

 " of course you do , " cry Mr. Bovill , with a jovial laugh . " but it seem 
 you do not   object to a chaise and pony whenever you can get they for 
 nothing , - ha , ha ! - excuse I , - a joke . " 

 herewith Mr. Bovill , still in excellent good - humour , abruptly change 
 the conversation to general matter , - agricultural prospect , chance of 
 a good harvest , corn trade , money market in general , politic , state of 
 the nation . Kenelm feel there be an attempt to draw he out , to sound , 
 to pump he , and reply only by monosyllable , generally significant 
 of ignorance on the question broach ; and at the close , if the 
 philosophical heir of the Chillinglys be in the habit of allow 
 himself to be surprised he would certainly have be startle when Mr. 
 Bovill rise , slap he on the shoulder , and say in a tone of great 
 satisfaction , " just as I think , sir ; you know nothing of these 
 matter : you be a gentleman bear and breed ; your clothe can not   disguise 
 you , sir . Elsie be right . my dear , just leave we for a few minute : I 
 have something to say to our young friend . you can get ready meanwhile 
 to go with I . " Elsie leave the table and walk obediently towards the 
 doorway . there she halt a moment , turn round , and look timidly 
 towards Kenelm . he have naturally rise from his seat as she rise , and 
 advance some pace as if to open the door for she . thus their look 
 encounter . he could not interpret that shy gaze of her : it be 
 tender , it be deprecate , it be humble , it be plead ; a man 
 accustom to female conquest might have think it be something more , 
 something in which be the key to all . but that something more be an 
 unknown tongue to Kenelm Chillingly . 

 when the two man be alone , Mr. Bovill reseat himself and motion to 
 Kenelm to do the same . " now , young sir , " say the former , " you and I can 
 talk at our ease . that adventure of yours yesterday may be the lucky 
 thing that could happen to you . " 

 " it be sufficiently lucky if I have be of any service to your niece . 
 but her own good sense would have be her safeguard if she have be

 knowingly or not , mislead she to believe that he be a single man . " 

 " Hang Mr. Compton ! we have do with he . I be a plain man , and I come 
 to the point . it be you who have carry off my niece ; it be with you

 have behave , and that your language and manner be those of a real 
 gentleman , my mind be make up . I guess pretty well what you be ; you 
 be a gentleman 's son ; probably a college youth ; not overburden with 
 cash ; have a quarrel with your governor , and he keep you short . do not 
 interrupt I . well , Elsie be a good girl and a pretty girl , and will 
 make a good wife , as wife go ; and , hark ye , she have L20,000 . so just 
 confide in I ; and if you do not   like your parent to know about it till 
 the thing be do and they be only get to forgive and bless you , why , you 
 shall marry Elsie before you can say Jack Robinson . " 

 for the first time in his life Kenelm Chillingly be seize with

 arm , and with violence and assault elope with she ; and what would the 
 world say if it know ? would it believe in your prudent conduct ? - conduct 
 only to be explain by the respect you feel due to your future wife . 
 and where will you find a well ? where will you find an uncle who will 
 part with his ward and L20,000 without ask if you have a sixpence ? 
 and the girl have take a fancy to you ; I see it : would she have give up 
 that player so easily if you have not steal her heart ? would you break 
 that heart ? no , young man : you be not a villain . shake hand on it ! " 

 " Mr. Bovill , " say Kenelm , recover his wonted equanimity , " I be 
 inexpressibly flatter by the honour you propose to I , and I do not

 I have inconceivable prejudice against the connubial state . if it be 
 permit to a member of the Established Church to cavil at any sentence 
 write by Saint Paul , - and I think that liberty may be permit to a 
 simple layman , since eminent member of the clergy criticise the whole

 any reproach attach to Miss Elsie , since in my telegram I direct you

 here Kenelm be interrupt by a violent explosion of rage from the 
 uncle . he stamp his foot ; he almost foam at the mouth ; he double

 " Sir , you be mock I : John Bovill be not a man to be jeered in this

 she , and you shall keep she , or I will break every bone in your skin . " 

 " break they , " say Kenelm , resignedly , but at the same time fall back

 accuser . Mr. Bovill sink into his chair , and wipe his forehead . Kenelm 
 craftily pursue the advantage he have gain , and in mild accent 
 proceed to reason , - 

 " when you recover your habitual serenity of humour , Mr. Bovill , you 
 will see how much your very excusable desire to secure your niece 's 
 happiness , and , I may add , to reward what you allow to have be 
 forbearing and well - breed conduct on my part , have hurry you into an 
 error of judgment . you know nothing of I . I may be , for what you know , 
 an impostor or swindler ; I may have every bad quality , and yet you be 
 to be content with my assurance , or rather your own assumption , that 
 I be bear a gentleman , in order to give I your niece and her L20,000 . 
 this be temporary insanity on your part . allow I to leave you to 
 recover from your excitement . " 

 " stop , sir , " say Mr. Bovill , in a changed and sullen tone ; " I be not 
 quite the madman you think I . but I dare say I have be too hasty and 
 too rough . nevertheless the fact be as I have state they , and I do 
 not see how , as a man of honour , you can get off marry my niece . the

 your part : but still there it be ; and suppose the case come before a

 could mend it . come , come , I own I be too business - like in rush to 
 the point at once , and I no long say , ' marry my niece off - hand . ' you 
 have only see she disguised and in a false position . pay I a visit at 
 Oakdale ; stay with I a month ; and if at the end of that time you do not 
 like she well enough to propose , I will let you off and say no more about 
 it . " 

 while Mr. Bovill thus speak , and Kenelm listen , neither see that the 
 door have be noiselessly open and that Elsie stand at the threshold . 
 now , before Kenelm could reply , she advance into the middle of the 
 room , and , her small figure draw up to its full height , her cheek 
 glowing , her lip quivering , exclaim , - 

 " Uncle , for shame ! " then address Kenelm in a sharp tone of anguish ,

 both hand and stand mute . 

 all of chivalry that Kenelm have receive with his baptismal appellation 
 be arouse . he spring up , and , bend his knee as he draw one of her 
 hand into his own , he say , - 

 " I be as convince that your uncle 's word be abhorrent to you as I be

 be so , she would have commit no imprudence . if she have any female

 " I have ! I have ! " cry Elsie ; " my lost mother 's sister : let I go to 
 she . " 

 " the woman who keep a school ! " say Mr. Bovill sneeringly . 

 " why not ? " ask Kenelm . 

 " she never would go there . I propose it to she a year ago . the minx 
 would not go into a school . " 

 " I will now , Uncle . " 

 " well , then , you shall at once ; and I hope you will be put on bread and 
 water . Fool ! fool ! you have spoil your own game . Mr. Chillingly , now 
 that Miss Elsie have turn she back on herself , I can convince you that 
 I be not the mad man you think I . I be at the festive meeting hold

 not recognize your face immediately in the excitement of our encounter 
 and in your change of dress ; but in walk home it strike I that I have 
 see it before , and I know it at once when you enter the room to - day . 
 it have be a tussle between we which should beat the other . you have 
 beat I ; and thank to that idiot ! if she have not put she speak into my 
 wheel , she would have live to be ' my lady . ' now good - day , sir . " 

 " Mr. Bovill , you offer to shake hand : shake hand now , and promise

 it . Hark ye , my friend "   this in Mr. Bovill 's ear : " a man can never 
 manage a woman . till a woman marry , a prudent man leave she to woman ; 
 when she do marry , she manage her husband , and there be an end of it . " 

 Kenelm be go . 

 " oh , wise young man ! " murmur the uncle . " Elsie , dear , how can you go 
 to your aunt 's while you be in that dress ? " 

 Elsie start as from a trance , her eye direct towards the 
 doorway through which Kenelm have vanish . " this dress , " she say

 " Gad ! " mutter Mr. Bovill , " that youngster be a second Solomon ; and if

 CHAPTER VIII . 

 " by the power that guard innocence and celibacy , " soliloquize Kenelm 
 Chillingly , " but I have have a narrow escape ! and have that amphibious 
 creature be in girl 's clothe instead of boy 's , when she intervene 
 like the deity of the ancient drama , I might have plunge my armorial 
 fish into hot water . though , indeed , it be hard to suppose that a 
 young lady head - over - ear in love with Mr. Compton yesterday could have 
 consign her affection to I to - day . still she look as if she could , 
 which prove either that one be never to trust a woman 's heart or never 
 to trust a woman 's look . Decimus Roach be right . man must never relax 
 his flight from the woman , if he strive to achieve an ' approach to the 
 Angels . ' " 

 these reflection be make by Kenelm Chillingly as , have turn his 
 back upon the town in which such temptation and trial have befall 
 he , he take his solitary way along a footpath that wind through mead 
 and cornfield , and shorten by three mile the distance to a cathedral 
 town at which he propose to rest for the night . 

 he have travel for some hour , and the sun be begin to slope 
 towards a range of blue hill in the west , when he come to the margin 
 of a fresh rivulet , overshadow by feathery willow and the quivering

 their banquet in return for the appetite of youth . then , recline along

 woode covert , provide they be neighbour by water , no matter whether 
 in pool or rill , he resign himself to that intermediate state between 
 thought and dream - land which we call " revery . " at a little distance he 
 hear the low still sound of the mower 's scythe , and the air come to his 
 brow sweet with the fragrance of new - mow hay . 

 he be rouse by a gentle tap on the shoulder , and turn lazily round , 
 see a good - humour jovial face upon a pair of massive shoulder , and 
 hear a hearty and win voice say , - 

 " young man , if you be not too tired , will you lend a hand to get in 
 my hay ? we be very short of hand , and I be afraid we shall have rain 
 pretty soon . " 

 Kenelm rise and shake himself , gravely contemplate the stranger , and 
 reply in his customary sententious fashion , " man be bear to help his 
 fellow - man , - especially to get in hay while the sun shin . I be at your 
 service . " 

 " that be a good fellow , and I be greatly oblige to you . you see I have 
 count on a gang of rove haymaker , but they be buy up 
 by another farmer . this way ; " and lead on through a gap in the

 with person of both sex , toss and spread the cut grass . among

 toss and spread like the rest , with his usual melancholy 
 resignation of mien and aspect . though a little awkward at first in

 accomplishment bestow on he that invaluable quality which be term

 and neatness with which he perform his work . something - it might be in 
 his countenance or in the charm of his be a stranger - attract the 
 attention of the feminine section of haymaker , and one very pretty girl 
 who be near to he than the rest attempt to commence conversation . 

 " this be new to you , " she say smile . 

 " nothing be new to I , " answer Kenelm , mournfully . " but allow I to 
 observe that to do thing well you should only do one thing at a time . I 
 be here to make hay and not conversation . " 

 " my ! " say the girl , in amazed ejaculation , and turn off with a toss 
 of her pretty head . 

 " I wonder if that jade have get an uncle , " think Kenelm . the farmer , 
 who take his share of work with the man , halt now and then to look 
 round , notice Kenelm 's vigorous application with much approval , and at 
 the close of the day 's work shake he heartily by the hand , leave a 
 two - shilling piece in his palm . the heir of the Chillinglys gaze on 
 that honorarium , and turn it over with the finger and thumb of the 
 leave hand . 

 " be not   it eno ' ? " say the farmer , nettle . 

 " pardon I , " answer Kenelm . " but , to tell you the truth , it be the 
 first money I ever earn by my own bodily labour ; and I regard it with 
 equal curiosity and respect . but if it would not offend you , I would 
 rather that , instead of the money , you have offer I some supper ; for I 
 have taste nothing but bread and water since the morning . " 

 " you shall have the money and supper both , my lad , " say the farmer , 
 cheerily . " and if you will stay and help till I have get in the hay , I 
 dare say my good woman can find you a well bed than you will get in the 
 village inn ; if , indeed , you can get one there at all . " 

 " you be very kind . but before I accept your hospitality excuse one 
 question : have you any niece about you ? " 

 " niece ! " echo the farmer , mechanically thrust his hand into his 
 breech - pocket as if in search of something there , " niece about I ! 
 what do you mean ? be that a newfangled word for copper ? " 

 " not for copper , though perhaps for brass . but I speak without 
 metaphor . I object to niece upon abstract principle , confirm by the 
 test of experience . " 

 the farmer star , and think his new friend not quite so sound in his 
 mental as he evidently be in his physical conformation , but reply , 
 with a laugh , " make yourself easy , then . I have only one niece , and she 
 be married to an iron - monger and live in Exeter . " 

 on enter the farmhouse , Kenelm 's host conduct he straight into the 
 kitchen , and cry out , in a hearty voice , to a comely middle - aged dame ,

 woman , I have bring you a guest who have well earn his supper , for he 
 have do the work of two , and I have promise he a bed . " 

 the farmer 's wife turn sharply round . " he be heartily welcome to 
 supper . as to a bed , " she say doubtfully , " I do not   know . " but here her

 roost till he have sup . " 

 " certainly not , " say Kenelm , sniff a very agreeable odour .

 " they have be in these five minute , and go upstairs to tidy 
 themselves . "

 " but I do not say I have no daughter . why , you be not afraid of they , 
 be you ? "

 they be not dangerous . " 

 " come , " cry the farmer , look very much pleased , while his dame 
 smile and blush , " come , that be as nicely say as if you be 
 canvass the county . ' tis not among haymaker that you learn 
 manner , I guess ; and perhaps I have be make too free with my 
 better . " 

 " what ! " quoth the courteous Kenelm , " do you mean to imply that you be 
 too free with your shilling ? apologize for that , if you like , but I 
 do not   think you will get back the shilling . I have not see so much of 
 this life as you have , but , accord to my experience , when a man once 
 part with his money , whether to his better or his worser , the chance 
 be that he will never see it again . " 

 at this aphorism the farmer laugh ready to kill himself , his wife 
 chuckle , and even the maid - of - all - work grin . Kenelm , preserve his 
 unalterable gravity , say to himself , - 

 " wit consist in the epigrammatic expression of a commonplace truth , and 
 the dull remark on the worth of money be almost as sure of successful 
 appreciation as the dull remark on the worthlessness of woman . 
 certainly I be a wit without know it . " 

 here the farmer touch he on the shoulder - touch it , do not slap 
 it , as he would have do ten minute before - and say , - 

 " we must not disturb the Missis or we shall get no supper . I will just go 
 and give a look into the cow - sheds . do you know much about cow ? " 

 " yes , cow produce cream and butter . the good cow be those which 
 produce at the least cost the good cream and butter . but how the good 
 cream and butter can be produce at a price which will place they free 
 of expense on a poor man 's breakfast - table be a question to be settle

 let we not delay the supper . " 

 the farmer and his guest quit the kitchen and enter the farmyard . 

 " you be quite a stranger in these part ? " 

 " quite . " 

 " you do not   even know my name ? " 

 " no , except that I hear your wife call you John . " 

 " my name be John Saunderson . " 

 " ah ! you come from the North , then ? that be why you be so sensible and 
 shrewd . name that end in ' son ' be chiefly bear by the descendant of 
 the Danes , to whom King Alfred , Heaven bless he ! peacefully assign 
 no less than sixteen english county . and when a dane be call 
 somebody 's son , it be a sign that he be the son of a somebody . " 

 " by gosh ! I never hear that before . " 

 " if I think you have I should not have say it . " 

 " now I have tell you my name , what be yours ? " 

 " a wise man ask question and a fool answer they . suppose for a moment 
 that I be not a fool . " 

 Farmer Saunderson scratch his head , and look more puzzled than 
 become the descendant of a dane settle by King Alfred in the north of 
 England . 

 " dash it , " say he at last , " but I think you be Yorkshire too . " 

 " Man , who be the most conceited of all animal , say that he alone have 
 the prerogative of thought , and condemn the other animal to the mean 
 mechanical operation which he call instinct . but as instinct be 
 unerring and thought generally go wrong , man have not much to boast of 
 accord to his own definition . when you say you think , and take it 
 for grant , that I be Yorkshire , you err . I be not Yorkshire . confine 
 yourself to instinct , can you divine when we shall sup ? the cow you be 
 about to visit divine to a moment when they shall be feed . " 

 say the farmer , recover his sense of superiority to the guest whom

 Quoth Kenelm , " the first consideration be to sup at all . supper

 sup in his parlour than his kitchen . meanwhile , I see a pump ; while you 
 go to the cow I will stay here and wash my hand of they . "

 beer of himself . you would do I a service , and he too , if you would let he 
 down a peg or two . " 

 Kenelm , who be now hard at work at the pump - handle , only reply by a 
 gracious nod . but as he seldom lose an opportunity for reflection , he

 even fancy that he be not small beer . it be upon that principle in 
 human nature that criticism wisely relinquish its pretension as an

 CHAPTER IX . 

 it be a pretty , quaint farmhouse , such as might well go with two or 
 three hundred acre of tolerably good land , tolerably well farm by an 
 active old - fashioned tenant , who , though he do not use mowing - machine 
 nor steam - plough nor dabble in chemical experiment , still bring 
 an adequate capital to his land and make the capital yield a very fair 
 return of interest . the supper be lay out in a good - sized though 
 low - pitch parlour with a glaze door , now wide open , as be all the 
 lattice window , look into a small garden , rich in those straggling 
 old english flower which be nowadays banish from garden more 
 pretentious and ; infinitely less fragrant . at one corner be an arbour 
 cover with honeysuckle , and opposite to it a row of beehive . the room 
 itself have an air of comfort , and that sort of elegance which indicate 
 the preside genius of feminine taste . there be shelf suspend 
 to the wall by blue ribbon , and fill with small book neatly bind ; 
 there be flower - pot in all the window - sill ; there be a small 
 cottage piano ; the wall be grace partly with engraved portrait of 
 county magnate and prize oxen ; partly with sampler in worst - work , 
 comprise verse of moral character and the name and birthday of 
 the farmer 's grandmother , mother , wife , and daughter . over the 
 chimney - piece be a small mirror , and above that the trophy of a fox 's 
 brush ; while niche into an angle in the room be a glaze cupboard , 
 rich with specimen of old china , Indian and English . 

 the party consist of the farmer , his wife , three buxom daughter , and 
 a pale - face slender lad of about twenty , the only son , who do not take 
 willingly to farming : he have be educate at a superior grammar school , 
 and have high notion about the March of Intellect and the Progress of 
 the Age . 

 Kenelm , though among the gravest of mortal , be one of the least shy . 
 in fact shyness be the usual symptom of a keen amour propre ; and of 
 that quality the youthful Chillingly scarcely possess more than do 
 the three Fishes of his hereditary scutcheon . he feel himself perfectly 
 at home with his entertainer ; take care , however , that his attention 
 be so equally divide between the three daughter as to prevent all 
 suspicion of a particular preference . " there be safety in number , " 
 think he , " especially in odd number . the three Graces never marry , 
 neither do the nine mus . " 

 " I presume , young lady , that you be fond of music , " say Kenelm , 
 glance at the piano . 

 " yes , I love it dearly , " say the eld girl , speak for the other . 

 Quoth the farmer , as he heap the stranger 's plate with boil beef and 
 carrot , " thing be not what they be when I be a boy ; then it be 
 only great tenant - farmer who have their girl teach the piano , and 
 send their boy to a good school . now we small folk be for help our 
 child a step or two high than our own place on the ladder . " 

 " the schoolmaster be abroad , " say the son , with the emphasis of a sage 
 add an original aphorism to the store of philosophy . 

 " there be , no doubt , a great equality of culture than there be in 
 the last generation , " say Kenelm . " People of all rank utter the same 
 commonplace idea in very much the same arrangement of syntax . and in 
 proportion as the democracy of intelligence extend - a friend of mine , 
 who be a doctor , tell I that complaint formerly reserved to what be 
 call aristocracy   though what that word mean in plain English I do not 
 know   be equally share by the commonalty - tic - douloureux and other 
 neuralgic malady abound . and the human race , in England at least , be 
 become more slight and delicate . there be a fable of a man who , when 
 he become exceedingly old , be turn into a grasshopper . England 
 be very old , and be evidently approach the grasshopper state of 
 development . perhaps we do not   eat as much beef as our forefather do . 
 may I ask you for another slice ? " 

 Kenelm 's remark be somewhat over the head of his audience . but 
 the son , take they as a slur upon the enlightened spirit of the age , 
 colour up and say , with a knit brow , " I hope , sir , that you be 
 not an enemy to progress . " 

 " that depend : for instance , I prefer stay here , where I be well off , 
 to go far and fare bad . " 

 " well say ! " cry the farmer . 

 not deign to notice that interruption , the son take up Kenelm 's reply 
 with a sneer , " I suppose you mean that it be to fare bad , if you march 
 with the time . " 

 " I be afraid we have no option but to march with the time ; but when we 
 reach that stage when to march any far be to march into old age , we 
 should not be sorry if time would be kind enough to stand still ; and all 
 good doctor concur in advise we to do nothing to hurry he . " 

 " there be no sign of old age in this country , sir ; and thank Heaven we 
 be not stand still ! " 

 " grasshopper never do ; they be always hop and jump , and make 
 what they think ' progress , ' till   unless they hop into the water and be 
 swallow up prematurely by a carp or a frog   they die of the exhaustion 
 which hop and jump unremitting naturally produce . may I ask you , Mrs. 
 Saunderson , for some of that rice - pudding ? " 

 the farmer , who , though he do not quite comprehend Kenelm 's 
 metaphorical mode of argue , see delightedly that his wise son look 
 more posed than himself , cry with great glee , " Bob , my boy , - Bob , our 
 visitor be a little too much for you ! " 

 " oh , no , " say Kenelm , modestly . " but I honestly think Mr. Bob would be 
 a wise man , and a weighty man , and more remove from the grasshopper 
 state , if he would think less and eat more pudding . " 

 when the supper be over the farmer offer Kenelm a clay pipe fill 
 with shag , which that adventurer accept with his habitual resignation 
 to the ill of life ; and the whole party , except Mrs. Saunderson , 
 stroll into the garden . Kenelm and Mr. Saunderson seat themselves

 moon at her full . the farmer , seat face his hayfield , smoke on 
 placidly . Kenelm , at the third whiff , lay aside his pipe , and glance

 Young Saunderson walk restlessly by himself to and fro the path of

 " very much well , I take it , sir . Adam be as good as gold , and never 
 have a qualm of conscience or stomach till Eve seduce he into eating

 " young man , thou'st be cross in love . I see it now . that be why thou

 " the two shilling you give I ? yes - "

 judge from your talk that you be a college chap , - anyhow , a gentleman . 
 be not   it so ? " 

 " my dear Mr. Saunderson , I set out on my travel , which be not long 
 ago , with a strong dislike to tell lie . but I doubt if a man can get 
 along through this world without find that the faculty of lying be

 if you be go to ask I any question about myself , I be sure that

 flirt with you , and if you do not   mind she will bring you into trouble . " 

 " how ? do she want to run away from her uncle ? " 

 " Uncle ! bless you , she do not   live with he ! she live with her

 Wiles - that be her name - be , I believe , a very good girl , and everybody 
 like she , - perhaps a little too much ; but then she know she be a

 " no woman ever do , whether she be a beauty or not . but I do not   yet 
 understand why Jessie Wiles should bring I into trouble . " 

 " because there be a big hulking fellow who have go half out of his wit 
 for she ; and when he fancy he see any other chap too sweet on she he 
 thrash he into a jelly . so , youngster , you just keep your skin out of

 " Hem ! and what do the girl say to those proof of affection ? do she 
 like the man the well for thrash other admirer into jelly ? "

 " thank you very much for your information , " say Kenelm , in a soft tone ,

 " Do . I should be sorry if any harm come to thee ; and Tom Bowles in one

 " thank you for the hint . I see the young lady have already go in . 
 good - night . " 

 pass through the garden , Kenelm encounter the junior Saunderson . 

 " I fear , " say the Votary of Progress , " that you have find the governor 
 awful slow . what have you be talk about ? " 

 " girl , " say Kenelm , " a subject always awful , but not necessarily 
 slow . " 

 " girl , - the governor be talk about girl ? you joke . " 

 " I wish I do joke , but that be a thing I could never do since I come 
 upon earth . even in the cradle , I feel that life be a very serious 
 matter , and do not allow of joke . I remember too well my first dose 
 of castor - oil . you too , Mr. Bob , have doubtless imbibe that initiatory 
 preparation to the sweet of existence . the corner of your mouth have 
 not recover from the downward curve into which it so rigidly drag 
 they . like myself , you be of grave temperament , and not easily move 
 to jocularity , - nay , an enthusiast for Progress be of necessity a man 
 eminently dissatisfied with the present state of affair . and chronic 
 dissatisfaction resent the momentary relief of a joke . " 

 " give off chaff , if you please , " say Bob , lower the didascular 
 intonation of his voice , " and just tell I plainly , do not my father 
 say anything particular about I ? " 

 " not a word : the only person of the male sex of whom he say anything 
 particular be Tom Bowles . " 

 " what , fight Tom ! the terror of the whole neighbourhood ! ah , I guess 
 the old gentleman be afraid lest Tom may fall foul upon I . but Jessie 
 Wiles be not worth a quarrel with that brute . it be a cry shame in 
 the government - " 

 " what ! have the Government fail to appreciate the heroism of Tom 
 Bowles , or rather to restrain the excess of its ardour ? " 

 " Stuff ! it be a shame in the Government not to have compel his father 
 to put he to school . if education be universal - " 

 " you think there would be no brute in particular . it may be so ; but 
 education be universal in China , and so be the bastinado . I think , 
 however , that you say the schoolmaster be abroad , and that the age of 
 enlightenment be in full progress . " 

 " yes , in the town , but not in these obsolete rural district ; and that 
 bring I to the point . I feel lost , throw away here . I have something 
 in I , sir , and it can only come out by collision with equal mind . so 
 do I a favour , will you ? " 

 " with the great pleasure . " 

 " give the governor a hint that he can not   expect I , after the education 
 I have have , to follow the plough and fatten pig ; and that Manchester be 
 the place for I . " 

 " why Manchester ? " 

 " because I have a relation in business there who will give I a 
 clerkship if the governor will consent . and Manchester rule England . " 

 " Mr. Bob Saunderson , I will do my good to promote your wish . this be 
 a land of liberty , and every man should choose his own walk in it , so 
 that , at the last , if he go to the dog , he go to they without that 
 disturbance of temper which be naturally occasion by the sense of 
 be drive to their jaw by another man against his own will . he have 
 then no one to blame but himself . and that , Mr. Bob , be a great comfort . 
 when , have get into a scrape , we blame other , we unconsciously 
 become unjust , spiteful , uncharitable , malignant , perhaps revengeful . 
 we indulge in feeling which tend to demoralize the whole character . 
 but when we only blame ourselves , we become modest and penitent . we make 
 allowance for other . and indeed self - blame be a salutary exercise of 
 conscience , which a really good man perform every day of his life . and 
 now , will you show I the room in which I be to sleep , and forget for a 
 few hour that I be alive at all : the good thing that can happen to we 
 in this world , my dear Mr. Bob ! there be never much amiss with our day , 
 so long as we can forget about they the moment we lay our head on the 
 pillow . " 

 the two young man enter the house amicably , arm in arm . the girl have 
 already retire , but Mrs. Saunderson be still up to conduct her 
 visitor to the guest 's chamber , - a pretty room which have be furnish 
 twenty - two year ago on the occasion of the farmer 's marriage , at the 
 expense of Mrs. Saunderson 's mother , for her own occupation when she 
 pay they a visit , and with its dimity curtain and trellis paper it 
 still look as fresh and new as if decorate and furnish yesterday . 

 leave alone , Kenelm undressed , and before he get into bed , bar 
 his right arm , and double it , gravely contemplate its muscular 
 development , pass his left hand over that prominence in the upper 
 part which be vulgarly call the ball . satisfied apparently with the 
 size and the firmness of that pugilistic protuberance , he gently sigh 
 forth , " I fear I shall have to lick Thomas Bowles . " in five minute more 
 he be asleep . 



 CHAPTER X. 

 the next day the hay - mowing be complete , and a large portion of the 
 hay already make cart away to be stack . Kenelm acquit himself 
 with a credit not less praiseworthy than have previously win Mr. 
 Saunderson 's approbation . but instead of reject as before the 
 acquaintance of Miss Jessie Wiles , he contrive towards noon to place 
 himself near to that dangerous beauty , and commence conversation . " I be 
 afraid I be rather rude to you yesterday , and I want to beg pardon . " 

 " oh , " answer the girl , in that simple intelligible English which 
 be more frequent among our village folk nowadays than many popular 
 novelist would lead we into supposing , " oh , I ought to ask pardon for 
 take a liberty in speak to you . but I think you would feel strange , 
 and I intend it kindly . " 

 " I be sure you do , " return Kenelm , chivalrously rake her portion of 
 hay as well as his own , while he speak . " and I want to be good friend 
 with you . it be very near the time when we shall leave off for 
 dinner , and Mrs. Saunderson have fill my pocket with some excellent 
 beef - sandwich , which I shall be happy to share with you , if you do not 
 object to dine with I here , instead of go home for your dinner . " 

 the girl hesitate , and then shake her head in dissent from the 
 proposition . 

 " be you afraid that your neighbour will think it wrong ? " 

 Jessie curl up her lip with a pretty scorn , and say , " I do not   much 
 care what other folk say , but be not   it wrong ? " 

 " not in the least . let I make your mind easy . I be here but for a day 
 or two : we be not likely ever to meet again ; but , before I go , I should 
 be glad if I could do you some little service . " as he speak he have 
 pause from his work , and , lean on his rake , fix his eye , for the 
 first time attentively , on the fair haymaker . 

 yes , she be decidedly pretty , - pretty to a rare degree : luxuriant brown 
 hair neatly tie up , under a straw hat doubtless of her own plaiting ; 
 for , as a general rule , nothing more educate the village maid for the 
 destiny of flirt than the accomplishment of straw - plaiting . she have 
 large , soft blue eye , delicate small feature , and a complexion more 
 clear in its healthful bloom than rural beauty generally retain 
 against the influence of wind and sun . she smile and slightly colour 
 as he gaze on she , and , lift her eye , give he one gentle , trustful 
 glance , which might have bewitch a philosopher and deceive a roue . 
 and yet Kenelm by that intuitive knowledge of character which be often 
 truthful where it be least disturb by the doubt and cavil of 
 acquire knowledge , feel at once that in that girl 's mind coquetry , 
 perhaps unconscious , be conjoin with an innocence of anything 
 bad than coquetry as complete as a child 's . he bow his head , in 
 withdraw his gaze , and take she into his heart as tenderly as if she 
 have be a child appeal to it for protection . 

 " certainly , " he say inly , " certainly I must lick Tom Bowles ; yet stay , 
 perhaps after all she like he . " 

 " but , " he continue aloud , " you do not see how I can be of any service 
 to you . before I explain , let I ask which of the man in the field be 
 Tom Bowles ? " 

 " Tom Bowles ? " exclaim Jessie , in a tone of surprise and alarm , and 
 turn pale as she look hastily round ; " you frighten I , sir : but 
 he be not here ; he do not work in the field . but how come you to hear 
 of Tom Bowles ? " 

 " Dine with I and I will tell you . look , there be a quiet place in yon 
 corner under the thorn - tree by that piece of water . see , they be 
 leave off work : I will go for a can of beer , and then , pray , let I 
 join you there . " 

 Jessie pause for a moment as if doubtful still ; then again glance at 
 Kenelm , and assure by the grave kindness of his countenance , utter a 
 scarce audible assent and move away towards the thorn - tree . 

 as the sun now stand perpendicularly over their head , and the hand 
 of the clock in the village church tower , soar over the hedgerow , 
 reach the first hour after noon , all work cease in a sudden silence : 
 some of the girl go back to their home ; those who stay group 
 together , apart from the man , who take their way to the shadow of a 
 large oak - tree in the hedgerow , where beer keg and can await they . 



 CHAPTER XI . 

 " and now , " say Kenelm , as the two young person , have finish their 
 simple repast , sit under the thorn - tree and by the side of the water , 
 fringe at that part with tall reed through which the light summer 
 breeze stir with a pleasant murmur , " now I will talk to you about Tom 
 Bowles . be it true that you do not   like that brave young fellow ? I say 
 young , as I take his youth for grant . " 

 " like he ! I hate the sight of he . " 

 " do you always hate the sight of he ? you must surely at one time have 
 allow he to think that you do not ? " 

 the girl wince , and make no answer , but pluck a daffodil from the 
 soil , and tear it ruthlessly to piece . 

 " I be afraid you like to serve your admirer as you do that ill - fat 
 flower , " say Kenelm , with some severity of tone . " but conceal in 
 the flower you may sometimes find the sting of a bee . I see by your 
 countenance that you do not tell Tom Bowles that you hat he till it 
 be too late to prevent his lose his wit for you . " 

 " no ; I be not   so bad as that , " say Jessie , look , nevertheless , 
 rather ashamed of herself ; " but I be silly and giddy - like , I own ; and , 
 when he first take notice of I , I be pleased , without think much of 
 it , because , you see , Mr. Bowles   emphasis on Mr.    be high up than 
 a poor girl like I . he be a tradesman , and I be only a shepherd 's 
 daughter ; though , indeed , Father be more like Mr. Saunderson 's foreman 
 than a mere shepherd . but I never think anything serious of it , and 
 do not suppose he do ; that be , at first . " 

 " so Tom Bowles be a tradesman . what trade ? " 

 " a farrier , sir . " 

 " and , I be tell , a very fine young man . " 

 " I do not   know as to that : he be very big . " 

 " and what make you hate he ? " 

 " the first thing that make I hate he be that he insult Father , who 
 be a very quiet , timid man , and threaten I do not   know what if Father 
 do not make I keep company with he . make I indeed ! but Mr. Bowles be 
 a dangerous , bad - hearted , violent man , and - do not   laugh at I , sir , but 
 I dream one night he be murder I . and I think he will too , if he 
 stay here : and so do his poor mother , who be a very nice woman , and 
 want he to go away ; but he will not . " 

 " Jessie , " say Kenelm , softly , " I say I want to make friend with 
 you . do you think you can make a friend of I ? I can never be more than 
 friend . but I should like to be that . can you trust I as one ? " 

 " yes , " answer the girl , firmly , and , as she lift her eye to he , 
 their look be pure from all vestige of coquetry , - guileless , frank , 
 grateful . 

 " be there not another young man who court you more civilly than Tom 
 Bowles do , and whom you really could find it in your heart to like ? " 

 Jessie look round for another daffodil , and not find one , content 
 herself with a bluebell , which she do not tear to piece , but caress 
 with a tender hand . Kenelm bend his eye down on her charming face 
 with something in their gaze rarely see there , - something of that 
 unreasoning , inexpressible human fondness , for which philosopher of 
 his school have no excuse . have ordinary mortal , like you or myself , for 
 instance , peer through the leaf of the thorn - tree , we should have 
 sigh or frown , accord to our several temperament ; but we should 
 all have say , whether spitefully or envyingly , " happy young lover ! " 
 and should all have blunder lamentably in so say . 

 still , there be no deny the fact that a pretty face have a very unfair 
 advantage over a plain one . and , much to the discredit of Kenelm 's 
 philanthropy , it may be reasonably doubted whether , have Jessie Wiles 
 be endow by nature with a snub nose and a squint , Kenelm would have 
 volunteer his friendly service , or meditate battle with Tom Bowles 
 on her behalf . 

 but there be no touch of envy or jealousy in the tone with which he 
 say , - 

 " I see there be some one you would like well enough to marry , and 
 that you make a great difference in the way you treat a daffodil and a 
 bluebell . who and what be the young man whom the bluebell represent ? 
 come , confide . " 

 " we be much bring up together , " say Jessie , still look down , 
 and still smooth the leaf of the bluebell . " his mother live in the 
 next cottage ; and my mother be very fond of he , and so be Father 
 too ; and , before I be ten year old , they use to laugh when poor will 
 call I his little wife . " here the tear which have start to Jessie 's 
 eye begin to fall over the flower . " but now Father would not hear of 
 it ; and it can not   be . and I have   try to care for some one else , and I 
 can not , and that be the truth . " 

 " but why ? have he turn out ill ? - take to poach or drink ? " 

 " no , no , no ; he be as steady and good a lad as ever live . but - but - " 

 " yes ; but - " 

 " he be a cripple now ; and I love he all the well for it . " here Jessie 
 fairly sob . 

 Kenelm be greatly move , and prudently hold his peace till she have a 
 little recover herself ; then , in answer to his gentle questioning , he 
 learn that will Somers - till then a healthy and strong lad - have fall 
 from the height of a scaffolding , at the age of sixteen , and be so 
 seriously injure that he be move at once to the hospital . when he 
 come out of it - what with the fall , and what with the long illness which 
 have follow the effect of the accident - he be not only crippled for 
 life , but of health so delicate and weakly that he be no long fit for 
 outdoor labour and the hard life of a peasant . he be an only son of a 
 widow mother , and his sole mode of assist she be a very precarious 
 one . he have teach himself basket - making ; and though , Jessie say , his 
 work be very ingenious and clever , still there be but few customer 
 for it in that neighbourhood . and , alas ! even if Jessie 's father would 
 consent to give his daughter to the poor cripple , how could the poor 
 cripple earn enough to maintain a wife ? 

 " and , " say Jessie , ' still I be happy , walk out with he on Sunday 
 evening , or go to sit with he and his mother ; for we be both 
 young , and can wait . but I dare not   do it any more now : for Tom Bowles 
 have swear that if I do he will beat he before my eye ; and will have a 
 high spirit , and I should break my heart if any harm happen to he on 
 my account . " 

 " as for Mr. Bowles , we will not think of he at present . but if will could 
 maintain himself and you , your father would not object nor you either to 
 a marriage with the poor cripple ? " 

 " Father would not ; and as for I , if it be not   for disobey Father , 
 I would marry he to - morrow . I can work . " 

 " they be go back to the hay now ; but after that task be over , let I 
 walk home with you , and show I Will 's cottage and Mr. Bowles 's shop or 
 forge . " 

 " but you will not say anything to Mr. Bowles . he would not   mind your being 
 a gentleman , as I now see you be , sir ; and he be dangerous , - oh , so 
 dangerous ! - and so strong . " 

 " never fear , " answer Kenelm , with the near approach to a laugh he 
 have ever make since childhood ; " but when we be relieve , wait for I a 
 few minute at yon gate . " 



 CHAPTER XII . 

 KENELM speak no more to his new friend in the hayfield ; but when the 
 day 's work be over he look round for the farmer to make an excuse 
 for not immediately join the family supper . however , he do not see 
 either Mr. Saunderson or his son . both be busy in the stackyard . 
 well pleased to escape excuse and the question it might provoke , Kenelm 
 therefore put on the coat he have lay aside and join Jessie , who 
 have wait for he at the gate . they enter the lane side by side , 
 follow the stream of villager who be slowly wend their homeward 
 way . it be a primitive english village , not adorn on the one hand 
 with fancy or model cottage , nor on the other hand indicate penury 
 and squalor . the church rise before they gray and Gothic , back by the 
 red cloud in which the sun have set , and border by the glebe - land 
 of the half - see parsonage . then come the village green , with a 
 pretty schoolhouse ; and to this succeed a long street of scatter 
 whitewash cottage , in the midst of their own little garden . 

 as they walk the moon rise in full splendour , silver the road 
 before they . 

 " who be the squire here ? " ask Kenelm . " I should guess he to be a good 
 sort of man , and well off . " 

 " yes , Squire Travers ; he be a great gentleman , and they say very rich . 
 but his place be a good way from this village . you can see it if you 
 stay , for he give a harvest - home supper on Saturday , and Mr. Saunderson 
 and all his tenant be go . it be a beautiful park , and Miss Travers 
 be a sight to look at . oh , she be lovely ! " continue Jessie , with an 
 unaffected burst of admiration ; for woman be more sensible of the charm 
 of each other 's beauty than man give they credit for . 

 " as pretty as yourself ? " 

 " oh , pretty be not the word . she be a thousand time handsomer ! " 

 " Humph ! " say Kenelm , incredulously . 

 there be a pause , break by a quick sigh from Jessie . 

 " what be you sigh for ? - tell I . " 

 " I be think that a very little can make folk happy , but that 
 somehow or other that very little be as hard to get as if one set one 's 
 heart on a great deal . " 

 " that be very wisely say . everybody covet a little something for which , 
 perhaps , nobody else would give a straw . but what be the very little 
 thing for which you be sigh ? " 

 " Mrs. Bawtrey want to sell that shop of her . she be get old , and 
 have have fit ; and she can get nobody to buy ; and if will have that shop 
 and I could keep it , - but ' tis no use thinking of that . " 

 " what shop do you mean ? " 

 " there ! " 

 " where ? I see no shop . " 

 " but it be the shop of the village , - the only one , - where the 
 post - office be . " 

 " ah ! I see something at the window like a red cloak . what do they 
 sell ? " 

 " everything , - tea and sugar and candle and shawl and gown and cloak 
 and mouse - trap and letter - paper ; and Mrs. Bawtrey buy poor Will 's 
 basket , and sell they for a good deal more than she pay . " 

 " it seem a nice cottage , with a field and orchard at the back . " 

 " yes . Mrs. Bawtrey pay L8 a year for it ; but the shop can well afford 
 it . " 

 Kenelm make no reply . they both walk on in silence , and have now 
 reach the centre of the village street when Jessie , look up , 
 utter an abrupt exclamation , give an affrighted start , and then come 
 to a dead stop . 

 Kenelm 's eye follow the direction of her , and see , a few yard 
 distant , at the other side of the way , a small red brick house , with 
 thatched shed adjoin it , the whole standing in a wide yard , over 
 the gate of which lean a man smoke a small cutty - pipe . " it be Tom 
 Bowles , " whisper Jessie , and instinctively she twin her arm into 
 Kenelm 's ; then , as if on second thought , withdraw it , and say , still 
 in a whisper , " go back now , sir ; do . " 

 " not i. it be Tom Bowles whom I want to know . hush ! " 

 for here Tom Bowles have throw down his pipe and be come slowly 
 across the road towards they . 

 Kenelm eye he with attention . a singularly powerful man , not so tall 
 as Kenelm by some inch , but still above the middle height , herculean 
 shoulder and chest , the low limb not in equal proportion , - a sort 
 of slouch , shamble gait . as he advance the moonlight fall on his 
 face ; it be a handsome one . he wear no hat , and his hair , of a 
 light brown , curl close . his face be fresh - colour , with aquiline 
 feature ; his age apparently about six or seven and twenty . come 
 nearer and nearer , whatever favourable impression the first glance 
 at his physiognomy might have make on Kenelm be dispel , for the 
 expression of his face change and become fierce and lower . 

 Kenelm be still walk on , Jessie by his side , when Bowles rudely 
 thrust himself between they , and seize the girl 's arm with one hand , 
 he turn his face full on Kenelm , with a menacing wave of the other 
 hand , and say in a deep burly voice , 

 " who be you ? " 

 " let go that young woman before I tell you . " 

 " if you be not   a stranger , " answer Bowles , seem as if he try to 
 suppress a rise fit of wrath , " you would be in the kennel for those word . 
 but I s'pose you do not   know that I be Tom Bowles , and I do not   choose the 
 girl as I be after to keep company with any other man . so you be off . " 

 " and I do not   choose any other man to lay violent hand on any girl 
 walk by my side without tell he that he be a brute ; and that I 
 only wait till he have both his hand at liberty to let he know that he 
 have not a poor cripple to deal with . " 

 Tom Bowles could scarcely believe his ear . Amaze swallow up for 
 the moment every other sentiment . mechanically he loosen his hold of 
 Jessie , who flee off like a bird release . but evidently she think 
 of her new friend 's danger more than her own escape ; for instead of 
 shelter herself in her father 's cottage , she run towards a group 
 of labourer who , near at hand , have stop loiter before the 
 public - house , and return with those ally towards the spot in which 
 she have leave the two man . she be very popular with the villager , who , 
 strong in the sense of number , overcome their awe of Tom Bowles , and 
 arrive at the place half run , half stride , in time , they hop , 
 to interpose between his terrible arm and the bone of the unoffending 
 stranger . 

 meanwhile Bowles , have recover his first astonishment , and scarcely 
 notice Jessie 's escape , still leave his right arm extend towards the 
 place she have vacate , and with a quick back - stroke of the left level 
 at Kenelm 's face , growl contemptuously , " thou'lt find one hand enough 
 for thee . " 

 but quick as be his aim , Kenelm catch the lift arm just above the 
 elbow , cause the blow to waste itself on air , and with a simultaneous 
 advance of his right knee and foot dexterously trip up his bulky 
 antagonist , and lay he sprawl on his back . the movement be 
 so sudden , and the stun it occasion so utter , morally as well as 
 physically , that a minute or more elapse before Tom Bowles pick 
 himself up . and he then stand another minute glower at his 
 antagonist , with a vague sentiment of awe almost like a superstitious 
 panic . for it be noticeable that , however fierce and fearless a man or 
 even a wild beast may be , yet if either have hitherto be only familiar 
 with victory and triumph , never yet have meet with a foe that could 
 cope with its force , the first effect of a defeat , especially from 
 a despise adversary , unhinge and half paralyze the whole nervous 
 system . but as fight Tom gradually recover to the consciousness of 
 his own strength , and the recollection that it have be only foil by 
 the skilful trick of a wrestler , and not the hand - to - hand might of a 
 pugilist , the panic vanish , and Tom Bowles be himself again . " oh , 
 that be your sort , be it ? we do not   fight with our heel hereabouts , like 
 cornisher and donkey : we fight with our fist , youngster ; and since 
 you will have a bout at that , why , you must . " 

 " Providence , " answer Kenelm , solemnly , ' send I to this village 
 for the express purpose of lick Tom Bowles . it be a signal mercy 
 vouchsafe to yourself , as you will one day acknowledge . " 

 again a thrill of awe , something like that which the demagogue in 
 Aristophanes might have feel when brave by the sausage - maker , shoot 
 through the valiant heart of Tom Bowles . he do not like those ominous 
 word , and still less the lugubrious tone of voice in which they 
 be utter , but resolve , at least , to proceed to battle with 
 more preparation than he have at first design , he now deliberately 
 disencumber himself of his heavy fustian jacket and vest , roll up 
 his shirt - sleeve , and then slowly advanced towards the foe . 

 Kenelm have also , with still great deliberation , take off his 
 coat - which he fold up with care , as be both a new and an only one , 
 and deposit by the hedge - side - and bared arm , lean indeed and almost 
 slight , as compare with the vast muscle of his adversary , but firm in 
 sinew as the hind leg of a stag . 

 by this time the labourer , lead by Jessie , have arrive at the spot , and 
 be about to crowd in between the combatant , when Kenelm wave they 
 back and say in a calm and impressive voice , - 

 " stand round , my good friend , make a ring , and see that it be fair play 
 on my side . I be sure it will be fair on Mr. Bowles 's . he be big enough 
 to scorn what be little . and now , Mr. Bowles , just a word with you in 
 the presence of your neighbour . I be not go to say anything uncivil . 
 if you be rather rough and hasty , a man be not always master of 
 himself - at least so I be tell - when he think more than he ought to 
 do about a pretty girl . but I can not   look at your face even by this 
 moonlight , and though its expression at this moment be rather cross , 
 without be sure that you be a fine fellow at bottom , and that if you 
 give a promise as man to man you will keep it . be that so ? " 

 one or two of the bystander murmur assent ; the other press round 
 in silent wonder . 

 " what be all that soft - sawder about ? " say Tom Bowles , somewhat 
 falteringly . 

 " simply this : if in the fight between we I beat you , I ask you to 
 promise before your neighbour that you will not by word or deed mol 
 or interfere again with Miss Jessie Wiles . " 

 " eh ! " roar Tom . " be it that you be after she ? " 

 " suppose I be , if that please you ; and on my side , I promise that if 
 you beat I , I quit this place as soon as you leave I well enough to do 
 so , and will never visit it again . what ! do you hesitate to promise ? be 
 you really afraid I shall lick you ? " 

 " you ! I would smash a dozen of you to powder . " 

 " in that case , you be safe to promise . come , ' tis a fair bargain . be 
 not   it , neighbour ? " 

 win over by Kenelm 's easy show of good temper , and by the sense of 
 justice , the bystander join in a common exclamation of assent . 

 " come , Tom , " say an old fellow , " the gentleman can not   speak fair ; and 
 we shall all think you be afeard if you hold back . " 

 Tom 's face work : but at last he growl , " well , I promise ; that be , if 
 he beat I . " 

 " all right , " say Kenelm . " you hear , neighbour ; and Tom Bowles could 
 not show that handsome face of his among you if he break his word . shake 
 hand on it . " 

 fight Tom sulkily shake hand . 

 " well now , that be what I call English , " say Kenelm , " all pluck and no 
 malice . fall back , friend , and leave a clear space for we . " 

 the man all recede ; and as Kenelm take his ground , there be a supple 
 ease in his posture which at once bring out into clear evidence the 
 nervous strength of his build , and , contrast with Tom 's bulk of chest , 
 make the latter look clumsy and topheavy . 

 the two man face each other a minute , the eye of both vigilant and 
 steadfast . Tom 's blood begin to fire up as he gaze ; nor , with all his 
 outward calm ; be Kenelm insensible of that proud beat of the heart 
 which be arouse by the fierce joy of combat . Tom strike out first and 
 a blow be parry , but not return ; another and another blow , - still 
 parried , still unreturne . Kenelm , act evidently on the defensive , 
 take all the advantage for that strategy which he derive from superior 
 length of arm and light agility of frame . perhaps he wish to 
 ascertain the extent of his adversary 's skill , or to try the endurance 
 of his wind , before he venture on the hazard of attack . Tom , gall to 
 the quick that blow which might have fell an ox be thus ward 
 off from their mark , and dimly aware that he be encounter some 
 mysterious skill which turn his brute strength into waste force and 
 might overmaster he in the long run , come to a rapid conclusion that 
 the soon he bring that brute strength to bear the well it would be 
 for he . accordingly , after three round , in which without once break 
 the guard of his antagonist he have receive a few playful tap on 
 the nose and mouth , he draw back and make a bull - like rush at his 
 foe , - bull - like , for it butt full at he with the powerful down - bent 
 head , and the two fist do duty as horn . the rush spend , he find 
 himself in the position of a man mill . I take it for grant that 
 every Englishman who can call himself a man - that be , every man who 
 have be an english boy , and , as such , be compel to the use of 
 his fist - know what a " mill " be . but I sing not only " pueris , " but 
 " virginibus . " lady , " a mill , " - use with reluctance and contempt for 
 myself that slang in which ladywriter indulge , and girl of the Period 
 know much well than they do their Murray , - " a mill , " - speak not to 
 ladywriter , not to girl of the Period , but to innocent damsel , and in 
 explanation to those foreigner who only understand the english language 
 as teach by Addison and Macaulay , - a " mill " periphrastically mean 
 this : your adversary , in the noble encounter between fist and fist , have 
 so plunge his head that it get catch , as in a vice , between the side 
 and double left arm of the adversary , expose that head , unprotected 
 and helpless , to be pound out of recognizable shape by the right fist 
 of the opponent . it be a situation in which raw superiority of force 
 sometimes find itself , and be seldom spar by disciplined superiority 
 of skill . Kenelm , his right fist raise , pause for a moment , then , 
 loosen the left arm , release the prisoner , and give he a 
 friendly slap on the shoulder , he turn round to the spectator and 
 say apologetically , " he have a handsome face : it would be a shame to 
 spoil it . " 

 Tom 's position of peril be so obvious to all , and that good - humour 
 abnegation of the advantage which the position give to the adversary 
 seem so generous , that the labourer actually hurrah . Tom , himself 
 feel as if treat like a child ; and alas , and alas for he ! in wheel 
 round , and regathere himself up , his eye rest on Jessie 's face . her 
 lip be apart with breathless terror : he fancy they be apart with 
 a smile of contempt . and now he become formidable . he fight as fight 
 the bull in the presence of the heifer , who , as he know too well , will 
 go with the conqueror . 

 if Tom have never yet fight with a man teach by a prizefighter , so 
 never yet have Kenelm encounter a strength which , but for the lack of 
 that teaching , would have conquer his own . he could act no long on 
 the defensive ; he could no long play , like a dexterous fencer , with 
 the sledge - hammer of those mighty arm . they break through his guard ; 
 they sound on his chest as on an anvil . he feel that do they alight 
 on his head he be a lost man . he feel also that the blow spend on the 
 chest of his adversary be idle as the stroke of a cane on the hide 
 of a rhinoceros . but now his nostril dilate ; his eye flash fire : 
 Kenelm Chillingly have cease to be a philosopher . Crash come his 
 blow - how unlike the swing roundabout hit of Tom Bowles ! - straight 
 to its aim as the rifle - ball of a Tyrolese or a british marksman 
 at Aldershot , - all the strength of nerve , sinew , purpose , and mind 
 concentre in its vigour , - crash just at that part of the front where 
 the eye meet , and follow up with the rapidity of lightning , flash 
 upon flash , by a more restrained but more disabling blow with the left 
 hand just where the left ear meet throat and jaw - bone . 

 at the first blow Tom Bowles have reel and stagger , at the second he 
 throw up his hand , make a jump in the air as if shoot through the heart , 
 and then heavily fall forwards , an inert mass . 

 the spectator press round he in terror . they think he be dead . 
 Kenelm knelt , pass quickly his hand over Tom 's lip , pulse , and heart , 
 and then rise , say , humbly and with an air of apology , - 

 " if he have be a less magnificent creature , I assure you on my honour 
 that I should never have venture that second blow . the first would have 
 do for any man less splendidly endow by nature . lift he gently ; 
 take he home . tell his mother , with my kind regard , that I will call and
