 see she and he to - morrow . and , stop , do he ever drink too much beer ? " 

 " well , " say one of the villager , " Tom can drink . " 

 " I think so . too much flesh for that muscle . go for the near 
 doctor . you , my lad ? good ; off with you ; quick . no danger , but perhaps 
 it may be a case for the lancet . " 

 Tom Bowles be lift tenderly by four of the stout man present and 
 bear into his home , evince no sign of consciousness ; but his face , 
 where not clout with blood , be very pale , very calm , with a slight 
 froth at the lip . 

 Kenelm pull down his shirt - sleeve , put on his coat , and turn to 
 Jessie , - 

 " now , my young friend , show I Will 's cottage . " 

 the girl come to he , white and trembling . she do not dare to speak . 
 the stranger have become a new man in her eye . perhaps he frighten she 
 as much as Tom Bowles have do . but she quicken her pace , leave the 
 public - house behind till she come to the farth end of the village . 
 Kenelm walk beside she , mutter to himself : and though Jessie catch 
 his word , happily she do not understand ; for they repeat one of 
 those bitter reproach on her sex as the main cause of all strife , 
 bloodshed , and mischief in general , with which the classic author 
 abound . his spleen soothe by that recourse to the lesson of the 
 ancient , Kenelm turn at last to his silent companion , and say kindly 
 but gravely , - 

 " Mr. Bowles have give I his promise , and it be fair that I should now 
 ask a promise from you . it be this : just consider how easily a girl so 
 pretty as you can be the cause of a man 's death . have Bowles strike I 
 where I strike he I should have be past the help of a surgeon . " 

 " oh ! " groan Jessie , shuddering , and cover her face with both hand . 

 " and , put aside that danger , consider that a man may be hit mortally 
 on the heart as well as on the head , and that a woman have much to answer 
 for who , no matter what her excuse , forget what misery and what guilt 
 can be inflict by a word from her lip and a glance from her eye . 
 consider this , and promise that , whether you marry Will Somers or not , 
 you will never again give a man fair cause to think you can like he 
 unless your own heart tell you that you can . will you promise that ? " 

 " I will , indeed , - indeed . " Poor Jessie 's voice die in sob . 

 " there , my child , I do not   ask you not to cry , because I know how much 
 woman like cry ; and in this instance it do you a great deal 
 of good . but we be just at the end of the village ; which be Will 's 
 cottage ? " 

 Jessie lift her head , and point to a solitary , small thatch 
 cottage . 

 " I would ask you to come in and introduce I ; but that might look too 
 much like crow over poor Tom Bowles . so good - night to you , Jessie , 
 and forgive I for preach . " 



 CHAPTER XIII . 

 KENELM knock at the cottage door ; a voice say faintly , " come in . " 

 he stoop his head , and step over the threshold . 

 since his encounter with Tom Bowles his sympathy have go with that 
 unfortunate lover : it be natural to like a man after you have beat 
 he ; and he be by no mean predispose to favour Jessie 's preference 
 for a sickly cripple . 

 yet , when two bright , soft , dark eye , and a pale intellectual 
 countenance , with that nameless aspect of refinement which delicate 
 health so often give , especially to the young , greet his quiet gaze , 
 his heart be at once win over to the side of the rival . will Somers be 
 seat by the hearth , on which a few live ember despite the warmth of 
 the summer evening still burn ; a rude little table be by his side , 
 on which be lay osi twig and white peeled chip , together with an 
 open book . his hand , pale and slender , be at work on a small basket 
 half finish . his mother be just clear away the tea - thing from 
 another table that stand by the window . will rose , with the good 
 breeding that belong to the rural peasant , as the stranger enter ; the 
 widow look round with surprise , and drop her simple courtesy , - a 
 little thin woman , with a mild , patient face . 

 the cottage be very tidily keep , as it be in most village home where 
 the woman have it her own way . the deal dresser opposite the door have 
 its display of humble crockery . the whitewashed wall be relieve with 
 coloured print , chiefly scriptural subject from the New Testament , 
 such as the Return of the Prodigal Son , in a blue coat and yellow 
 inexpressible , with his stocking about his heel . 

 at one corner there be pile up basket of various size , and at 
 another corner be an open cupboard contain book , - an article of 
 decorative furniture find in cottage much more rarely than colour 
 print and gleam crockery . 

 all this , of course , Kenelm could not at a glance comprehend in detail . 
 but as the mind of a man accustom to generalization be marvellously 
 quick in form a sound judgment , whereas a mind accustom to dwell 
 only on detail be wonderfully slow at arrive at any judgment at all , 
 and when it do , the probability be that it will arrive at a wrong one , 
 Kenelm judge correctly when he come to this conclusion : " I be among 
 simple english peasant ; but , for some reason or other , not to be 
 explain by the relative amount of wage , it be a favourable speciman 
 of that class . " 

 " I beg your pardon for intrude at this hour , Mrs. Somers , " say 
 Kenelm , who have be too familiar with peasant from his early 
 childhood not to know how quickly , when in the presence of their 
 household god , they appreciate respect , and how acutely they feel the 
 want of it . " but my stay in the village be very short , and I should not 
 like to leave without see your son 's basket - work , of which I have 
 hear much . " 

 " you be very good , sir , " say will , with a pleased smile that 
 wonderfully brighten up his face . " it be only just a few common thing 
 that I keep by I . any fine sort of work I mostly do by order . " 

 " you see , sir , " say Mrs. Somers , " it take so much more time for pretty 
 work - basket , and such like ; and unless do to order , it might be 
 a chance if he could get it sell . but pray be seat , sir , " and Mrs. 
 Somers place a chair for her visitor , " while I just run up stair for 
 the work - basket which my son have make for Miss Travers . it be to go home 
 to - morrow , and I put it away for fear of accident . " 

 Kenelm seat himself , and , draw his chair near to Will 's , take up 
 the half - finish basket which the young man have lay down on the table . 

 " this seem to I very nice and delicate workmanship , " say Kenelm ; " and 
 the shape , when you have finish it , will be elegant enough to please 
 the taste of a lady . " 

 " it be for Mrs. Lethbridge , " say will : ' she want something to hold 
 card and letter ; and I take the shape from a book of drawing which 
 Mr. Lethbridge kindly lend I . you know Mr. Lethbridge , sir ? he be a 
 very good gentleman . " 

 " no , I do not   know he . who be he ? " 

 " our clergyman , sir . this be the book . " 

 to Kenelm 's surprise , it be a work on Pompeii , and contain woodcut 
 of the implement and ornament , mosaic and fresco , find in that 
 memorable little city . 

 " I see this be your model , " say Kenelm ; " what they call a patera , 
 and rather a famous one . you be copy it much more truthfully than I 
 should have suppose it possible to do in substitute basket - work for 
 bronze . but you observe that much of the beauty of this shallow bowl 
 depend on the two dove perch on the brim . you can not   manage that 
 ornamental addition . " 

 " Mrs. Lethbridge think of put there two little stuff 
 canary - bird . " 

 " do she ? good heaven ! " exclaim Kenelm . 

 " but somehow , " continue will , " I do not like that , and I make bold to 
 say so . " 

 " why do not you do it ? " 

 " well , I do not   know ; but I do not think it would be the right thing . " 

 " it would have be very bad taste , and spoil the effect of your 
 basket - work ; and I will endeavour to explain why . you see here , in the 
 next page , a drawing of a very beautiful statue . of course this statue 
 be intend to be a representation of nature , but nature idealize . you 
 do not   know the meaning of that hard word , idealize , and very few people 
 do . but it mean the performance of a something in art accord to the 
 idea which a man 's mind form to itself out of a something in nature . 
 that something in nature must , of course , have be carefully study 
 before the man can work out anything in art by which it be faithfully 
 represent . the artist , for instance , who make that statue , must have 
 know the proportion of the human frame . he must have make study 
 of various part of it , - head and hand , and arm and leg , and so 
 forth , - and have do so , he then put together all his various 
 study of detail , so as to form a new whole , which be intend to 
 personate an idea form in his own mind . do you go with I ? " 

 " partly , sir ; but I be puzzle a little still . " 

 " of course you be ; but you will puzzle yourself right if you think over 
 what I say . now if , in order to make this statue , which be compose of 
 metal or stone , more natural , I stick on it a wig of real hair , would 
 not you feel at once that I have spoil the work ; that as you clearly 
 express it , ' it would not be the right thing ' ? and instead of make the 
 work of art more natural , I should have make it laughably unnatural , by 
 force insensibly upon the mind of he who look at it the contrast 
 between the real life , represent by a wig of actual hair , and the 
 artistic life , represent by an idea embody in stone or metal . the 
 high the work of art   that be , the high the idea it represent as a 
 new combination of detail take from nature , the more it be degrade 
 or spoilt by an attempt to give it a kind of reality which be out 
 of keep with the material employ . but the same rule apply to 
 everything in art , however humble . and a couple of stuff canary - bird 
 at the brim of a basket - work imitation of a greek drinking - cup would be 
 as bad taste as a wig from the barber 's on the head of a marble statue 
 of Apollo . " 

 " I see , " say will , his head downcast , like a man ponder , - " at least 
 I think I see ; and I be very much oblige to you , sir . " 

 Mrs. Somers have long since return with the work - basket , but stand with 
 it in her hand , not dare to interrupt the gentleman , and listen to 
 his discourse with as much patience and as little comprehension as if it 
 have be one of the controversial sermon upon ritualism with which on 
 great occasion Mr. Lethbridge favour his congregation . 

 Kenelm have now exhaust his critical lecture - from which certain 
 poet and novelist who contrive to caricature the ideal by their 
 attempt to put wig of real hair upon the head of stone statue might 
 borrow a useful hint or two if they would condescend to do so , which 
 be not likely - perceive Mrs. Somers stand by he , take from her 
 the basket , which be really very pretty and elegant , subdivide 
 into various compartment for the implement in use among lady , and 
 bestow on it a well - merit eulogium . 

 " the young lady mean to finish it herself with ribbon , and line it 
 with satin , " say Mrs. Somers , proudly . 

 " the ribbon will not be amiss , sir ? " say will , interrogatively . 

 " not at all . your natural sense of the fitness of thing tell you 
 that ribbon go well with straw and light straw - like work such as this ; 
 though you would not put ribbon on those rude hamper and game - basket 
 in the corner . like to like ; a stout cord go suitably with they : just 
 as a poet who understand his art employ pretty expression for poem 
 intend to be pretty and suit a fashionable drawing - room , and carefully 
 shun they to substitute a simple cord for poem intend to be strong 
 and travel far , despite of rough usage by the way . but you really 
 ought to make much more money by this fancy - work than you could as a 
 day - labourer . " 

 will sigh . " not in this neighbourhood , sir ; I might in a town . " 

 " why not move to a town , then ? " 

 the young man colour , and shake his head . 

 Kenelm turn appealingly to Mrs. Somers . " I will be willing to go 
 wherever it would be good for my boy , sir . but - " and here she check 
 herself , and a tear trickle silently down her cheek . 

 will resume , in a more cheerful tone , " I be get a little know 
 now , and work will come if one wait for it . " Kenelm do not deem it 
 courteous or discreet to intrude far on Will 's confidence in the 
 first interview ; and he begin to feel , more than he have do at first , 
 not only the dull pain of the bruise he have receive in the recent 
 combat , but also somewhat more than the weariness which follow long 
 summer - day 's work in the open air . he therefore , rather abruptly , now 
 take his leave , say that he should be very glad of a few specimen of 
 Will 's ingenuity and skill , and would call or write to give direction 
 about they . 

 just as he come in sight of Tom Bowles 's house on his way back to Mr. 
 Saunderson 's , Kenelm see a man mount a pony that stand tie up at the 
 gate , and exchange a few word with a respectable - look woman 
 before he ride on . he be pass by Kenelm without notice , when that 
 philosophical vagrant stop he , say , " if I be not mistaken , sir , 
 you be the doctor . there be not much the matter with Mr. Bowles ? " 

 the doctor shake his head . " I can not   say yet . he have have a very ugly blow 
 somewhere . " 

 " it be just under the left ear . I do not aim at that exact spot : 
 but Bowles unluckily swerve a little aside at the moment , perhaps in 
 surprise at a tap between his eye immediately precede it : and so , as 
 you say , it be an ugly blow that he receive . but if it cure he of 
 the habit of give ugly blow to other people who can bear they less 
 safely , perhaps it may be all for his good , as , no doubt , sir , your 
 schoolmaster say when he flog you . " 

 " bless my soul ! be you the man who fight with he , - you ? I can not 
 believe it . " 

 " why not ? " 

 " why not ! so far as I can judge by this light , though you be a tall 
 fellow , Tom Bowles must be a much heavy weight than you be . " 

 " Tom Spring be the champion of England ; and accord to the record of 
 his weight , which history have preserve in her archive , Tom Spring be 
 a light weight than I be . " 

 " but be you a prize - fighter ? " 

 " I be as much that as I be anything else . but to return to Mr. Bowles , 
 be it necessary to bleed he ? " 

 " yes ; he be unconscious , or nearly so , when I come . I take away a few 
 ounce ; and I be happy to say he be now sensible , but must be keep very 
 quiet . " 

 " no doubt ; but I hope he will be well enough to see I to - morrow . " 

 " I hope so too ; but I can not   say yet . Quarrel about a girl , - eh ? " 

 " it be not about money . and I suppose if there be no money and no 
 woman in the world , there would be no quarrel and very few doctor . 
 good - night , Sir . " 

 " it be a strange thing to I , " say Kenelm , as he now open the 
 garden - gate of Mr. Saunderson 's homestead , " that though I have   have nothing 
 to eat all day , except a few pitiful sandwich , I do not   feel the least 
 hungry . such arrest of the lawful duty of the digestive organ never 
 happen to I before . there must be something weird and ominous in it . " 

 on enter the parlour , the family party , though they have long since 
 finish supper , be still seat round the table . they all rise at 
 the sight of Kenelm . the fame of his achievement have precede he . he 
 check the congratulation , the compliment , and the question 
 which the hearty farmer rapidly heap upon he , with a melancholic 
 exclamation , " but I have lose my appetite ! no honour can compensate for 
 that . let I go to bed peaceably , and perhaps in the magic land of sleep 
 Nature may restore I by a dream of supper . " 



 CHAPTER XIV . 

 KENELM rise betime the next morning somewhat stiff and uneasy , but 
 sufficiently recover to feel ravenous . fortunately , one of the 
 young lady , who attend specially to the dairy , be already up , and 
 supply the starve hero with a vast bowl of bread and milk . he then 
 stroll into the hayfield , in which there be now very little left 
 to do , and but few hand besides his own be employ . Jessie be not 
 there . Kenelm be glad of that . by nine o'clock his work be over , and 
 the farmer and his man be in the yard complete the rick . Kenelm 
 steal away unobserved , bent on a round of visit . he call first at the 
 village shop keep by Mrs. Bawtrey , which Jessie have point out to 
 he , on pretence of buy a gaudy neckerchief ; and soon , thank to his 
 habitual civility , make familiar acquaintance with the shopwoman . she 
 be a little sickly old lady , her head shake , as with palsy , somewhat 
 deaf , but still shrewd and sharp , render mechanically so by long 
 habit of shrewdness and sharpness . she become very communicative , speak 
 freely of her desire to give up the shop , and pass the rest of her day 
 with a sister , widow like herself , in a neighbouring town . since she 
 have lose her husband , the field and orchard attach to the shop have 
 cease to be profitable , and become a great care and trouble ; and the 
 attention the shop require be wearisome . but she have twelve year 
 unexpired of the lease grant for twenty - one year to her husband on 
 low term , and she want a premium for its transfer , and a purchaser 
 for the stock of the shop . Kenelm soon draw from she the amount of the 
 sum she require for all , - L45 . 

 " you be not   think of it for yourself ? " she ask , put on her 
 spectacle , and examine he with care . 

 " perhaps so , if one could get a decent living out of it . do you keep a 
 book of your loss and your gain ? " 

 " in course , sir , " she say proudly . " I keep the book in my goodman 's 
 time , and he be one who could find out if there be a farthe wrong , 
 for he have be in a lawyer 's office when a lad . " 

 " why do he leave a lawyer 's office to keep a little shop ? " 

 " well , he be bear a farmer 's son in this neighbourhood , and he always 
 have a hankering after the country , and - and besides that - " 

 " yes . " 

 " I will tell you the truth ; he have get into a way of drinking speerrit , 
 and he be a good young man , and want to break himself of it , and he 
 take the temperance oath ; but it be too hard on he , for he could not 
 break himself of the company that lead he into liquor . and so , one time 
 when he come into the neighbourhood to see his parent for the Christmas 
 holiday , he take a bit of like to I ; and my father , who be squire 
 Travers 's bailiff , have just die , and leave I a little money . and so , 
 somehow or other , we come together , and get this house and the land 
 from the Squire on lease very reasonable ; and my goodman be well 
 eddyeate , and much thought of , and never be tempt to drink , now 
 that he have a missis to keep he in order , have a many little thing put 
 into his way . he could help to measure timber , and know about drain , 
 and he get some bookkeeping from the farmer about ; and we keep cow 
 and pig and poultry , and so we do very well , specially as the Lord be 
 merciful and send we no child . " 

 " and what do the shop bring in a year since your husband die ? " 

 " you have good judge for yourself . will you look at the book , and take 
 a peep at the land and apple - tree ? but they be be neglect since my 
 goodman die . " 

 in another minute the heir of the Chillinglys be seat in a neat 
 little back parlour , with a pretty though confine view of the orchard 
 and grass slope behind it , and bend over Mrs. Bawtrey 's ledger . 

 some customer for cheese and bacon come now into the shop , the old 
 woman leave he to his study . though they be not of a nature familiar 
 to he , he bring to they , at least , that general clearness of head and 
 quick seizure of important point which be common to most man who have 
 go through some disciplined training of intellect , and be accustom 
 to extract the pith and marrow out of many book on many subject . the 
 result of his examination be satisfactory ; there appear to he a 
 clear balance of gain from the shop alone of somewhat over L40 a year , 
 take the average of the last three year . close the book , he then 
 let himself out of the window into the orchard , and thence into the 
 neighbour grass field . both be , indeed , much neglect ; the tree 
 want pruning , the field manure . but the soil be evidently of rich 
 loam , and the fruit - tree be abundant and of ripe age , generally 
 look healthy in spite of neglect . with the quick intuition of a man 
 bear and breed in the country , and pick up scrap of rural knowledge 
 unconsciously , Kenelm convince himself that the land , properly manage , 
 would far more than cover the rent , rate , tithe , and all incidental 
 outgoing , leave the profit of the shop as the clear income of the 
 occupier . and no doubt with clever young people to manage the shop , its 
 profit might be increase . 

 not think it necessary to return at present to Mrs. Bawtrey 's , Kenelm 
 now bent his way to Tom Bowles 's . 

 the house - door be close . at the summon of his knock it be quickly 
 open by a tall , stout , remarkably fine - look woman , who might have 
 tell fifty year , and carry they off lightly on her ample shoulder . 
 she be dress very respectably in black , her brown hair braid simply 
 under a neat tight - fitting cap . her feature be aquiline and 
 very regular : altogether there be something about her majestic and 
 Cornelia - like . she might have sit for the model of that roman matron , 
 except for the fairness of her anglo - Saxon complexion . 

 " what be your pleasure ? " she ask , in a cold and somewhat stern voice . 

 " Ma'am , " answer Kenelm , uncovering , " I have call to see Mr. Bowles , 
 and I sincerely hope he be well enough to let I do so . " 

 " no , sir , he be not well enough for that ; he be lie down in his own 
 room , and must be keep quiet . " 

 " may I then ask you the favour to let I in ? I would say a few word to 
 you , who be his mother if I mistake not . " Mrs. Bowles pause a moment 
 as if in doubt ; but she be at no loss to detect in Kenelm 's manner 
 something superior to the fashion of his dress , and suppose the visit 
 might refer to her son 's professional business , she open the door 
 wide , draw aside to let he pass first , and when he stand midway in 
 the parlour , request he to take a seat , and , to set he the example , 
 seat herself . 

 " Ma'am , " say Kenelm , " do not regret to have admit I , and do not 
 think hardly of I when I inform you that I be the unfortunate cause of 
 your son 's accident . " 

 Mrs. Bowles rise with a start . " you be the man who beat my boy ? " 

 " no , ma'am , do not say I beat he . he be not beat . he be so brave 
 and so strong that he would easily have beat I if I have not , by good 
 luck , knock he down before he have time to do so . Pray , ma'am , retain 
 your seat and listen to I patiently for a few moment . " 

 Mrs. Bowles , with an indignant heave of her Juno - like bosom , and with 
 a superbly haughty expression of countenance which suit well with its 
 aquiline formation , tacitly obey . 

 " you will allow , ma'am , " recommence Kenelm , " that this be not the first 
 time by many that Mr. Bowles have come to blow with another man . be I 
 not right in that assumption ? " 

 " my son be of hasty temper , " reply Mrs. Bowles , reluctantly , " and 
 people should not aggravate he . " 

 " you grant the fact , then ? " say Kenelm , imperturbably , but with a 
 polite inclination of head . " Mr. Bowles have often be engage in these 
 encounter , and in all of they it be quite clear that he provoke the 
 battle ; for you must be aware that he be not the sort of man to whom any 
 other would be dispose to give the first blow . yet , after these little 
 incident have occur , and Mr. Bowles have , say , half kill the person 
 who aggravate he , you do not feel any resentment against that person , 
 do you ? Nay , if he have want nursing , you would have go and nurse 
 he . " 

 " I do not   know as to nursing , " say Mrs. Bowles , begin to lose she 
 dignity of mien ; " but certainly I should have be very sorry for he . 
 and as for Tom , - though I say it who should not say , - he have no more 
 malice than a baby : he would go and make it up with any man , however badly 
 he have beat he . " 

 " just as I suppose ; and if the man have sulk and would not make it up , 
 Tom would have call he a bad fellow , and feel inclined to beat he 
 again . " 

 Mrs. Bowles 's face relax into a stately smile . 

 " well , then , " pursue Kenelm , " I do but humbly imitate Mr. Bowles , and I 
 come to make it up and shake hand with he . " 

 " no , sir , - no , " exclaim Mrs. Bowles , though in a low voice , and 
 turn pale . " do not   think of it . ' tis not the blow ; he will get over 
 those fast enough : ' tis his pride that be hurt ; and if he see you there 
 might be mischief . but you be a stranger , and go away : do go soon ; do 
 keep out of his way ; do ! " and the mother clasp her hand . 

 " Mrs. Bowles , " say Kenelm , with a change of voice and aspect , - a 
 voice and aspect so earnest and impressive that they still and awe 
 she , - " will you not help I to save your son from the danger into which 
 that hasty temper and that mischievous pride may at any moment hurry 
 he ? do it never occur to you that these be the cause of terrible 
 crime , bring terrible punishment ; and that against brute force , 
 impel by savage passion , society protect itself by the hulk and 
 the gallow ? " 

 " Sir ; how dare you - " 

 " hush ! if one man kill another in a moment of ungovernable wrath , that 
 be a crime which , though heavily punish by the conscience , be gently 
 deal with by the law , which call it only manslaughter ; but if a motive 
 to the violence , such as jealousy or revenge , can be assign , and there 
 should be no witness by to prove that the violence be not premeditate , 
 then the law do not call it manslaughter , but murder . be it not that 
 think which make you so imploringly exclaim , ' go soon ; keep out of his 
 way ' ? " 

 the woman make no answer , but , sink back in her chair , gasp for 
 breath . 

 " Nay , madam , " resume Kenelm , mildly ; " banish your fear . if you will 
 help I I feel sure that I can save your son from such peril , and I 
 only ask you to let I save he . I be convinced that he have a good and 
 a noble nature , and he be worth save . " and as he thus say he take she 
 hand . she resign it to he and return the pressure , all her pride 
 soften as she begin to weep . 

 at length , when she recover voice , she say , - 

 " it be all along of that girl . he be not so till she cross he , and 
 make he half mad . he be not the same man since then , - my poor Tom ! " 

 " do you know that he have give I his word , and before his 
 fellow - villager , that if he have the bad of the fight he would never 
 mol Jessie Wiles again ? " 

 " yes , he tell I so himself ; and it be that which weigh on he now . he 
 brood and brood and mutter , and will not be comfort ; and - and I do 
 fear that he mean revenge . and again , I implore you to keep out of his 
 way . " 

 " it be not revenge on I that he think of . suppose I go and be see no 
 more , do you think in your own heart that that girl 's life be safe ? " 

 " what ! my Tom kill a woman ! " 

 " do you never read in your newspaper of a man who kill his sweetheart , 
 or the girl who refuse to be his sweetheart ? at all event , you 
 yourself do not approve this frantic suit of his . if I have hear 
 rightly , you have wish to get Tom out of the village for some time , 
 till Jessie Wiles be - we will say , marry , or go elsewhere for good . " 

 " yes , indeed , I have wish and pray for it many be the time , both for 
 her sake and for his . and I be sure I do not   know what we shall do if 
 he stay , for he have be lose custom fast . the squire have take away 
 his , and so have many of the farmer ; and such a trade as it be in his 
 good father 's time ! and if he would go , his uncle , the veterinary at 
 Luscombe , would take he into partnership ; for he have no son of his own , 
 and he know how clever Tom be : there be not   a man who know more about 
 horse ; and cow , too , for the matter of that . " 

 " and if Luscombe be a large place , the business there must be more 
 profitable than it can be here , even if Tom get back his custom ? " 

 " oh yes ! five time as good , - if he would but go ; but he will not hear of 
 it . " 

 " Mrs. Bowles , I be very much oblige to you for your confidence , and I 
 feel sure that all will end happily now we have have this talk . I will not 
 press far on you at present . Tom will not stir out , I suppose , till 
 the evening . " 

 " ah , sir , he seem as if he have no heart to stir out again , unless for 
 something dreadful . " 

 " Courage ! I will call again in the evening , and then you just take I 
 up to Tom 's room , and leave I there to make friend with he , as I have 
 with you . do not   say a word about I in the meanwhile . " 

 " but - " 

 " ' but , ' Mrs. Bowles , be a word that cool many a warm impulse , stifle 
 many a kindly thought , put a dead stop to many a brotherly deed . nobody 
 would ever love his neighbour as himself if he listen to all the but 
 that could be say on the other side of the question . " 

 CHAPTER XV . 

 KENELM now bent his way towards the parsonage , but just as he near 
 its glebe - land he meet a gentleman whose dress be so evidently clerical 
 that he stop and say , - 

 " have I the honour to address Mr. Lethbridge ? " 

 " that be my name , " say the clergyman , smile pleasantly . " anything I 
 can do for you ? " 

 " yes , a great deal , if you will let I talk to you about a few of your 
 parishioner . " 

 " my parishioner ! I beg your pardon , but you be quite a stranger to I , 
 and , I should think , to the parish . " 

 " to the parish , - no , I be quite at home in it ; and I honestly believe 
 that it have never know a more officious busybody , thrust himself 
 into its most private affair . " 

 Mr. Lethbridge star , and , after a short pause , say , " I have hear of 
 a young man who have be stay at Mr. Saunderson 's , and be indeed at 
 this moment the talk of the village . you be - " 

 " that young man . alas ! yes . " 

 " Nay , " say Mr. Lethbridge , kindly , " I can not myself , as a minister 
 of the Gospel , approve of your profession , and , if I might take the 
 liberty , I would try and dissuade you from it ; but still , as for the 
 one act of free a poor girl from the most scandalous persecution , and 
 administering , though in a rough way , a lesson to a savage brute who 
 have long be the disgrace and terror of the neighbourhood , I can not 
 honestly say that it have my condemnation . the moral sense of a community 
 be generally a right one : you have win the praise of the village . under 
 all the circumstance , I do not withhold mine . you wake this morning and 
 find yourself famous . do not sigh ' alas . ' " 

 " Lord Byron wake one morning and find himself famous , and the result 
 be that he sigh ' alas ' for the rest of his life . if there be two 
 thing which a wise man should avoid , they be fame and love . Heaven 
 defend I from both ! " 

 again the parson star ; but be of compassionate nature , and incline 
 to take mild view of everything that belong to humanity , he say , with 
 a slight inclination of his head , - 

 " I have always hear that the Americans in general enjoy the advantage 
 of a well education than we do in England , and their reading public 
 be infinitely large than our ; still , when I hear one of a calling 
 not highly consider in this country for intellectual cultivation or 
 ethical philosophy cite Lord Byron , and utter a sentiment at variance 
 with the impetuosity of inexperienced youth , but which have much to 
 commend it in the eye of a reflective Christian impress with the 
 nothingness of the object mostly covet by the human heart , I be 
 surprised , and - oh , my dear young friend , surely your education might 
 fit you for something well ! " 

 it be among the maxim of Kenelm Chillingly 's creed that a sensible man 
 should never allow himself to be surprised ; but here he be , to use 
 a popular idiom , " take aback , " and lower himself to the rank of 
 ordinary mind by say , simply , " I do not   understand . " 

 " I see , " resume the clergyman , shake his head gently , " as I always 
 suspect , that in the vaunted education bestow on Americans , the 
 elementary principle of Christian right and wrong be more neglect 
 than they be among our own humble class . yes , my young friend , you 
 may quote poet , you may startle I by remark on the nothingness of 
 human fame and human love , derive from the precept of heathen poet , 
 and yet not understand with what compassion , and , in the judgment 
 of most sober - minded person , with what contempt , a human being who 
 practise your vocation be regard . " 

 " have I a vocation ? " say Kenelm . " I be very glad to hear it . what be my 
 vocation ? and why must I be an American ? " 

 " why , surely I be not misinformed ? you be the American - I forget his 
 name - who have come over to contest the belt of prize - fighting with 
 the champion of England . you be silent ; you hang your head . by your 
 appearance , your length of limb , your gravity of countenance , your 
 evident education , you confirm the impression of your birth . your 
 prowess have prove your profession . " 

 " Reverend sir , " say Kenelm , with his unutterable seriousness of aspect , 
 " I be on my travel in search of truth and in flight from sham , but 
 so great a take - in as myself I have not yet encounter . remember I in 
 your prayer . I be not an American ; I be not a prize - fighter . I 
 honour the first as the citizen of a grand republic try his good to 
 accomplish an experiment in government in which he will find the 
 very prosperity he tend to create will soon or later destroy his 
 experiment . I honour the last because strength , courage , and sobriety 
 be essential to the prize - fighter , and be among the chief ornament 
 of king and hero . but I be neither one nor the other . and all I 
 can say for myself be , that I belong to that very vague class commonly 
 call english gentleman , and that , by birth and education , I have a 
 right to ask you to shake hand with I as such . " 

 Mr. Lethbridge star again , raise his hat , bow , and shake hand . 

 " you will allow I now to speak to you about your parishioner . you take 
 an interest in Will Somers ; so do I. he be clever and ingenious . but it 
 seem there be not sufficient demand here for his basket , and he would , 
 no doubt , do well in some neighbouring town . why do he object to 
 move ? " 

 " I fear that poor will would pine away to death if he lose sight of that 
 pretty girl for whom you do such chivalrous battle with Tom Bowles . " 

 " the unhappy man , then , be really in love with Jessie Wiles ? and do you 
 think she no less really care for he ? " 

 " I be sure of it . " 

 " and would make he a good wife ; that be , as wife go ? " 

 " a good daughter generally make a good wife . and there be not a father 
 in the place who have a well child than Jessie be to hers . she really 
 be a girl of a superior nature . she be the cleverest pupil at our 
 school , and my wife be much attach to she . but she have something 
 well than mere cleverness : she have an excellent heart . " 

 " what you say confirm my own impression . and the girl 's father have no 
 other objection to Will Somers than his fear that will could not support 
 a wife and family comfortably . 

 " he can have no other objection save that which would apply equally to 
 all suitor . I mean his fear lest Tom Bowles might do she some mischief , 
 if he know she be about to marry any one else . " 

 " you think , then , that Mr. Bowles be a thoroughly bad and dangerous 
 person ? " 

 " thoroughly bad and dangerous , and bad since he have take to 
 drink . " 

 " I suppose he do not take to drinking till he lose his wit for Jessie 
 Wiles ? " 

 " no , I do not   think he do . " 

 " but , Mr. Lethbridge , have you never use your influence over this 
 dangerous man ? " 

 " of course , I do try , but I only get insult . he be a godless animal , 
 and have not be inside a church for year . he seem to have get 
 a smattering of such vile learning as may be find in infidel 
 publication , and I doubt if he have any religion at all . " 

 " Poor Polyphemus ! no wonder his Galatea shun he . " 

 " Old Wiles be terribly frightened , and ask my wife to find Jessie a 
 place as servant at a distance . but Jessie can not   bear the thought of 
 leave . " 

 " for the same reason which attach will Somers to the native soil ? " 

 " my wife think so . " 

 " do you believe that if Tom Bowles be out of the way , and Jessie 
 and will be man and wife , they could earn a sufficient livelihood as 
 successor to Mrs. Bawtrey , will add the profit of his basket - work 
 to those of the shop and land ? " 

 " a sufficient livelihood ! of course . they would be quite rich . I know 
 the shop use to turn a great deal of money . the old woman , to be sure , 
 be no long up to the business , but still she retain a good custom . " 

 " will Somers seem in delicate health . perhaps if he have a less weary 
 struggle for a livelihood , and no fear of lose Jessie , his health 
 would improve . " 

 " his life would be save , sir . " 

 " then , " say Kenelm , with a heavy sigh and a face as long as an 
 undertaker 's , " though I myself entertain a profound compassion for that 
 disturbance to our mental equilibrium which go by the name of ' love , ' 
 and I be the last person who ought to add to the care and sorrow which 
 marriage entail upon its victim , - I say nothing of the woe 
 destine to those whom marriage usually add to a population already 
 overcrowded , - I fear that I must be the mean of bring these two 
 love - bird into the same cage . I be ready to purchase the shop and its 
 appurtenance on their behalf , on the condition that you will kindly 
 obtain the consent of Jessie 's father to their union . as for my brave 
 friend Tom Bowles , I undertake to deliver they and the village from that 
 exuberant nature , which require a large field for its energy . Pardon 
 I for not let you interrupt I . I have not yet finish what I have 
 to say . allow I to ask if Mrs. Grundy reside in this village . " 

 " Mrs. Grundy ! oh , I understand . of course ; wherever a woman have a 
 tongue , there Mrs. Grundy have a home . " 

 " and see that Jessie be very pretty , and that in walk with she I 
 encounter Mr. Bowles , might not Mrs. Grundy say , with a toss of her 
 head , ' that it be not out of pure charity that the stranger have be so 
 liberal to Jessie Wiles ' ? but if the money for the shop be pay through 
 you to Mrs. Bawtrey , and you kindly undertake all the contingent 
 arrangement , Mrs. Grundy will have nothing to say against any one . " 

 Mr. Lethbridge gaze with amaze at the solemn countenance before he . 

 " Sir , " he say , after a long pause , " I scarcely know how to express my 
 admiration of a generosity so noble , so thoughtful , and accompany with 
 a delicacy , and , indeed , with a wisdom , which - which - " 

 " pray , my dear sir , do not make I still more ashamed of myself than I 
 be at present for an interference in love matter quite alien to my own 
 conviction as to the good mode of make an ' approach to the Angels . ' 
 to conclude this business , I think it well to deposit in your hand 
 the sum of L45 , for which Mrs. Bawtrey have agree to sell the remainder 
 of her lease and stock - in - hand ; but , of course , you will not make 
 anything public till I be go , and Tom Bowles too . I hope I may get 
 he away to - morrow ; but I shall know to - night when I can depend on his 
 departure , and till he go I must stay . " 

 as he speak , Kenelm transfer from his pocket - book to Mr. Lethbridge 's 
 hand bank - note to the amount specify . 

 " may I at least ask the name of the gentleman who honour I with his 
 confidence , and have bestow so much happiness on member of my flock ? " 

 " there be no great reason why I should not tell you my name , but I see 
 no reason why I should . you remember Talleyrand 's advice , ' if you be 
 in doubt whether to write a letter or not , do not . ' the advice apply to 
 many doubt in life besides that of letter - writing . Farewell , sir ! " 

 " a most extraordinary young man , " mutter the parson , gaze at the 
 recede form of the tall stranger ; then gently shake his head , he 
 add , " quite an original . " he be content with that solution of the 
 difficulty which have puzzle he . may the reader be the same . 



 CHAPTER XVI . 

 after the family dinner , at which the farmer 's guest display more 
 than his usual power of appetite , Kenelm follow his host towards the 
 stackyard , and say , - 

 " my dear Mr. Saunderson , though you have no long any work for I to 
 do , and I ought not to trespass far on your hospitality , yet if I 
 might stay with you another day or so , I should be very grateful . " 

 " my dear lad , " cry the farmer , in whose estimation Kenelm have rise 
 prodigiously since the victory over Tom Bowles , " you be welcome to stay 
 as long as you like , and we shall be all sorry when you go . indeed , at 
 all event , you must stay over Saturday , for you shall go with we to 
 the squire 's harvest - supper . it will be a pretty sight , and my girl be 
 already count on you for a dance . " 

 " Saturday , - the day after to - morrow . you be very kind ; but merrymaking 
 be not much in my way , and I think I shall be on my road before you set 
 off to the squire 's supper . " 

 " Pooh ! you shall stay ; and , I say , young ' un , if you want more to do , I 
 have a job for you quite in your line . " 

 " what be it ? " 

 " thrash my ploughman . he have be insolent this morning , and he be the 
 big fellow in the county , next to Tom Bowles . " 

 here the farmer laugh heartily , enjoy his own joke . 

 " thank you for nothing , " say Kenelm , rub his bruise . " a burn 
 child dread the fire . " 

 the young man wander alone into the field . the day be become 
 overcast , and the cloud threaten rain . the air be exceedingly still ; 
 the landscape , miss the sunshine , wear an aspect of gloomy solitude . 
 Kenelm come to the bank of the rivulet not far from the spot on which 
 the farmer have first find he . there he sit down , and lean his cheek 
 on his hand , with eye fix on the still and darken stream lapsing 
 mournfully away : sorrow enter into his heart and ting its musing . 

 " be it then true , " say he , soliloquize , " that I be bear to pass 
 through life utterly alone ; ask , indeed , for no sister - half of 
 myself , disbelieve its possibility , shrink from the thought 
 of it , - half scorning , half pity those who sigh for it ? - thing 
 unattainable , - well sigh for the moon ! 

 " yet if other man sigh for it , why do I stand apart from they ? if the 
 world be a stage , and all the man and woman in it merely player , be I 
 to be the solitary spectator , with no part in the drama and no interest 
 in the vicissitude of its plot ? many there be , no doubt , who covet as 
 little as I do the part of ' Lover , ' ' with a woful ballad , make to his 
 mistress 's eyebrow ; ' but then they covet some other part in the drama , 
 such as that of soldier ' bearded as a pard , ' or that of justice ' in fair 
 round belly with fat capon line . ' but I no ambition fire : I have no 
 longing either to rise or to shine . I do not   desire to be a colonel , nor 
 an admiral , nor a member of Parliament , nor an alderman ; I do not yearn 
 for the fame of a wit , or a poet , or a philosopher , or a diner - out , or 
 a crack shot at a rifle - match or a battue . decidedly , I be the one 
 looker - on , the one bystander , and have no more concern with the active 
 world than a stone have . it be a horrible phantasmal crotchet of Goethe , 
 that originally we be all monad , little segregated atom adrift in 
 the atmosphere , and carry hither and thither by force over which we 
 have no control , especially by the attraction of other monad , so 
 that one monad , compel by porcine monad , crystallize into a pig ; 
 another , hurry along by heroic monad , become a lion or an Alexander . 
 now it be quite clear , " continue Kenelm , shift his position and 
 cross the right leg over the left , " that a monad intend or 
 fit for some other planet may , on its way to that destination , be 
 encounter by a current of other monad blow earthward , and be 
 catch up in the stream and whirl on , till , to the marring of 
 its whole proper purpose and scene of action , it settle 
 here , - conglomerate into a baby . probably that lot have befall I : my 
 monad , mean for another region in space , have be drop into this , 
 where it can never be at home , never amalgamate with other monad nor 
 comprehend why they be in such a perpetual fidget . I declare I know 
 no more why the mind of human being should be so restlessly agitated 
 about thing which , as most of they own , give more pain than pleasure , 
 than I understand why that swarm of gnat , which have such a very short 
 time to live , do not give itself a moment 's repose , but go up and 
 down , rise and fall as if it be on a seesaw , and make as much 
 noise about its insignificant alternation of ascent and descent as if 
 it be the hum of man . and yet , perhaps , in another planet my monad 
 would have frisk and jump and dance and seesaw with congenial 
 monad , as contentedly and as sillily as do the monad of man and gnat 
 in this alien Vale of Tears . " 

 Kenelm have just arrive at that conjectural solution of his perplexity 
 when a voice be hear singing , or rather modulate to that kind of 
 chant between recitative and song , which be so pleasingly effective 
 where the intonation be pure and musical . they be so in this 
 instance , and Kenelm 's ear catch every word in the following song : - 


  CONTENT . 

  " there be time when the trouble of life be still ; 
  the bee wander lose in the depth of June , 
  and I pause where the chime of a silver rill 
  sing the linnet and lark to their rest at noon . 

  " say my soul , ' see how calmly the wavelet glide , 
  though so narrow their way to their ocean vent ; 
  and the world that I traverse be wide , be wide , 
  and yet be too narrow to hold content ' 

  " o my son , never say that the world be wide ; 
  the rill in its bank be less closely pent : 
  it be thou who art shoreless on every side , 
  and thy width will not let thee enclose content . " 


 as the voice cease Kenelm lift his head . but the bank of the brook 
 be so curve and so clothe with brushwood that for some minute the 
 singer be invisible . at last the bough before he be put aside , and 
 within a few pace of himself pause the man to whom he have commend 
 the praise of a beefsteak , instead of those which minstrelsy in its 
 immemorial error dedicate to love . 

 " Sir , " say Kenelm , half rise , " well meet once more . have you ever 
 listen to the cuckoo ? " 

 " Sir , " answer the minstrel , " have you ever feel the presence of the 
 summer ? " 

 " permit I to shake hand with you . I admire the question by which you 
 have countermet and rebuke my own . if you be not in a hurry , will you 
 sit down and let we talk ? " 

 the minstrel incline his head and seat himself . his dog - now emerge 
 from the brushwood - gravely approach Kenelm , who with great gravity 
 regard he ; then , wag his tail , repose on his haunch , 
 intent with ear erect on a stir in the neighbouring reed , evidently 
 consider whether it be cause by a fish or a water - rat . 

 " I ask you , sir , if you have ever listen to the cuckoo from no 
 irrelevant curiosity ; for often on summer day , when one be talk with 
 one 's self , - and , of course , puzzle one 's self , - a voice break out , 
 as it be from the heart of Nature , so far be it and yet so near ; and 
 it say something very quieting , very musical , so that one be tempt 
 inconsiderately and foolishly to exclaim , ' nature reply to I . ' the 
 cuckoo have serve I that trick pretty often . your song be a well 
 answer to a man 's self - questioning than he can ever get from a cuckoo . " 

 " I doubt that , " say the minstrel . " Song , at the good , be but the echo 
 of some voice from the heart of Nature . and if the cuckoo 's note seem 
 to you such a voice , it be an answer to your questioning perhaps more 
 simply truthful than man can utter , if you have rightly construe the 
 language . " 

 " my good friend , " answer Kenelm , " what you say sound very prettily ; 
 and it contain a sentiment which have be amplify by certain critic 
 into that measureless domain of dunderhead which be vulgarly call 
 BOSH . but though nature be never silent , though she abuse the privilege 
 of her age in be tediously gossip and garrulous , Nature never 
 reply to our question : she can not   understand an argument ; she have 
 never read Mr. Mill 's work on Logic . in fact , as it be truly say by a 
 great philosopher , ' nature have no mind . ' every man who address she be 
 compel to force upon she for a moment the loan of his own mind . and 
 if she answer a question which his own mind put to she , it be only 
 by such a reply as his own mind teach to her parrot - like lip . and as 
 every man have a different mind , so every man get a different answer . 
 Nature be a lie old humbug . " 

 the minstrel laugh merrily ; and his laugh be as sweet as his chant . 

 " poet would have a great deal to unlearn if they be to look upon 
 nature in that light . " 

 " bad poet would , and so much the well for they and their reader . " 

 " be not good poet student of Nature ? " 

 " student of Nature , certainly , as surgeon study anatomy by dissect 
 a dead body . but the good poet , like the good surgeon , be the man who 
 consider that study merely as the necessary A b C , and not as the 
 all - in - all essential to skill in his practice . I do not give the fame 
 of a good surgeon to a man who fill a book with detail , more or less 
 accurate , of fibre and nerve and muscle ; and I do not   give the fame of 
 a good poet to a man who make an inventory of the Rhine or the Vale of 
 Gloucester . the good surgeon and the good poet be they who understand 
 the live man . what be that poetry of drama which Aristotle justly 
 rank as the high ? be it not a poetry in which description of 
 inanimate Nature must of necessity be very brief and general ; in which 
 even the external form of man be so indifferent a consideration that it 
 will vary with each actor who perform the part ? a Hamlet may be fair 
 or dark . a Macbeth may be short or tall . the merit of dramatic poetry 
 consist in the substituting for what be commonly call Nature   namely , 
 external and material Nature   creature intellectual , emotional , but 
 so purely immaterial that they may be say to be all mind and soul , 
 accept the temporary loan of any such body at hand as actor may 
 offer , in order to be make palpable and visible to the audience , but 
 need no such body to be palpable and visible to reader . the 
 high kind of poetry be therefore that which have least to do with 
 external Nature . but every grade have its merit more or less genuinely 
 great , accord as it instil into Nature that which be not there , - the 
 reason and the soul of man . " 

 " I be not much disposed , " say the minstrel , " to acknowledge any one 
 form of poetry to be practically high than another ; that be , so far as 
 to elevate the poet who cultivate what you call the high with some 
 success above the rank of the poet who cultivate what you call a very 
 inferior school with a success much more triumphant . in theory , dramatic 
 poetry may be high than lyric , and ' Venice Preserved ' be a very 
 successful drama ; but I think Burns a great poet than Otway . " 

 " possibly he may be ; but I know of no lyrical poet , at least among the 
 modern , who treat less of nature as the mere outward form of thing , 
 or more passionately animate her framework with his own human heart , 
 than do Robert Burns . do you suppose when a Greek , in some perplexity 
 of reason or conscience , address a question to the oracular oak - leaf 
 of Dodona that the oak - leaf answer he ? do not   you rather believe 
 that the question suggest by his mind be answer by the mind of 
 his fellow - man , the priest , who make the oak - leave the mere vehicle 
 of communication , as you and I might make such vehicle in a sheet of 
 writing - paper ? be not the history of superstition a chronicle of the 
 folly of man in attempt to get answer from external Nature ? " 

 " but , " say the minstrel , " have I not somewhere hear or read that the 
 experiment of Science be the answer make by Nature to the question 
 put to she by man ? " 

 " they be the answer which his own mind suggest to she , - nothing more . 
 his mind study the law of matter , and in that study make experiment 
 on matter ; out of those experiment his mind , accord to its previous 
 knowledge or natural acuteness , arrive at its own deduction , and 
 hence arise the science of mechanic and chemistry , etc . but the matter 
 itself give no answer : the answer vary accord to the mind that 
 put the question ; and the progress of science consist in the perpetual 
 correction of the error and falsehood which precede mind conceive 
 to be the correct answer they receive from Nature . it be the 
 supernatural within we , - namely , Mind , - which can alone guess at the 
 mechanism of the natural , namely , Matter . a stone can not question a 
 stone . " 

 the minstrel make no reply . and there be a long silence , break but by 
 the hum of the insect , the ripple of onward wave , and the sigh of the 
 wind through reed . 



 CHAPTER XVII . 

 SAID Kenelm , at last break silence - 


  " ' Rapiamus , amici , 
  Occasionem de die , dumque virent genu , 
  Et decet , obducta solvatur fronte senectus ! ' " 


 " be not that quotation from Horace ? " ask the minstrel . 

 " yes ; and I make it insidiously , in order to see if you have not acquire 
 what be call a classical education . " 

 " I might have receive such education , if my taste and my destiny 
 have not withdraw I in boyhood from study of which I do not then 
 comprehend the full value . but I do pick up a smattering of Latin at 
 school ; and from time to time since I leave school I have endeavour to 
 gain some little knowledge of the most popular latin poet ; chiefly , I 
 own to my shame , by the help of literal english translation . " 

 " as a poet yourself , I be not sure that it would be an advantage to know 
 a dead language so well that its form and mode of thought run , 
 though perhaps unconsciously , into those of the live one in which you 
 compose . Horace might have be a still well poet if he have not know 
 Greek well than you know Latin . " 

 " it be at least courteous in you to say so , " answer the singer , with a 
 pleased smile . 

 " you would be still more courteous , " say Kenelm , " if you would pardon 
 an impertinent question , and tell I whether it be for a wager that you 
 wander through the land , Homer - like , as a wandering minstrel , and allow 
 that intelligent quadrupe your companion to carry a tray in his mouth 
 for the reception of penny ? " 

 " no , it be not for a wager ; it be a whim of mine , which I fancy from 
 the tone of your conversation you could understand , be apparently 
 somewhat whimsical yourself . " 

 " so far as whim go , be assure of my sympathy . " 

 " well , then , though I follow a calling by the exercise of which I secure 
 a modest income , my passion be verse . if the season be always summer , 
 and life be always youth , I should like to pass through the world 
 singing . but I have never venture to publish any verse of mine . if 
 they fall still - bear it would give I more pain than such wound to 
 vanity ought to give to a bearded man ; and if they be assail or 
 ridicule it might seriously injure I in my practical vocation . that 
 last consideration , be I quite alone in the world , might not much 
 weigh on I ; but there be other for whose sake I should like to make 
 fortune and preserve station . many year ago - it be in Germany - I fall 
 in with a german student who be very poor , and who do make money by 
 wander about the country with lute and song . he have since become a 
 poet of no mean popularity , and he have tell I that he be sure he find 
 the secret of that popularity in habitually consult popular taste 
 during his rove apprenticeship to song . his example strongly impress 
 I . so I begin this experiment ; and for several year my summer have 
 be all partly spend in this way . I be only know , as I think I tell 
 you before , in the round I take as ' the Wandering Minstrel ; ' I receive 
 the trifling money that be bestow on I as proof of a certain 
 merit . I should not be pay by poor people if I do not please ; and the 
 song which please they good be generally those I love good myself . 
 for the rest , my time be not throw away , - not only as regard bodily 
 health , but healthfulness of mind : all the current of one 's idea 
 become so freshen by month of playful exercise and varied 
 adventure . " 

 " yes , the adventure be varied enough , " say Kenelm , somewhat ruefully ; 
 for he feel , in shift his posture , a sharp twinge of his bruised 
 muscle . " but do not   you find those mischief - maker , the woman , always 
 mix themselves up with adventure ? " 

 " bless they ! of course , " say the minstrel , with a ring laugh . " in 
 life , as on the stage , the petticoat interest be always the strong . " 

 " I do not   agree with you there , " say Kenelm , dryly . " and you seem to 
 I to utter a claptrap beneath the rank of your understanding . however , 
 this warm weather indispose one to disputation ; and I own that a 
 petticoat , provide it be red , be not without the interest of colour in 
 a picture . " 

 " well , young gentleman , " say the minstrel , rise , " the day be wear 
 on , and I must wish you good - by ; probably , if you be to ramble about 
 the country as I do , you would see too many pretty girl not to teach 
 you the strength of petticoat interest , - not in picture alone ; and 
 should I meet you again I may find you write love - verse yourself . " 

 " after a conjecture so unwarrantable , I part company with you less 
 reluctantly than I otherwise might do . but I hope we shall meet again . " 

 " your wish flatter I much ; but , if we do , pray respect the confidence 
 I have place in you , and regard my wandering minstrelsy and my dog 's 
 tray as sacred secret . should we not so meet , it be but a prudent 
 reserve on my part if I do not give you my right name and address . " 

 " there you show the cautious common - sense which belong rarely to lover 
 of verse and petticoat interest . what have you do with your guitar ? " 

 " I do not pace the road with that instrument : it be forward to I 
 from town to town under a borrow name , together with other raiment 
 that this , should I have cause to drop my character of wandering 
 minstrel . " 

 the two man here exchange a cordial shake of the hand . and as the 
 minstrel go his way along the river - side , his voice in chanting seem 
 to lend to the wavelet a lively murmur , to the reed a less plaintive 
 sigh . 



 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 in his room , solitary and brooding , sit the defeat hero of a hundred 
 fight . it be now twilight ; but the shutter have be partially close 
 all day , in order to exclude the sun , which have never before be 
 unwelcome to Tom Bowles , and they still remain so , make the twilight 
 doubly twilight , till the harvest moon , rise early , shoot its ray 
 through the crevice , and force a silvery track amid the shadow of the 
 floor . 

 the man 's head droop on his breast ; his strong hand rest 
 listlessly on his knee : his attitude be that of utter despondency and 
 prostration . but in the expression of his face there be the sign of 
 some dangerous and restless thought which belie not the gloom but the 
 stillness of the posture . his brow , which be habitually open and 
 frank , in its defying aggressive boldness , be now contract into deep 
 furrow , and lower darkly over his downcast , half - close eye . his 
 lip be so tightly compressed that the face lose its roundness , and 
 the massive bone of the jaw stand out hard and salient . now and then , 
 indeed , the lip open , give vent to a deep , impatient sigh , but they 
 reclose as quickly as they have part . it be one of those crisis in 
 life which find all the element that make up a man 's former self in 
 lawless anarchy ; in which the Evil One seem to enter and direct the 
 storm ; in which a rude untutored mind , never before harbour a thought 
 of crime , see the crime start up from an abyss , feel it to be an 
 enemy , yet yield to it as a fate . so that when , at the last , some 
 wretch , sentence to the gibbet , shudderingly look back to the moment 
 " that tremble between two world , " - the world of the man guiltless , 
 the world of the man guilty , - he say to the holy , highly educated , 
 rational , passionless priest who confess he and call he " brother , " 
 " the devil put it into my head . " 

 at that moment the door open ; at its threshold there stand the man 's 
 mother - whom he have never allow to influence his conduct , though he 
 love she well in his rough way - and the hat fellow - man whom he long 
 to see dead at his foot . the door reclose : the mother be go , without 
 a word , for her tear choke she ; the fellow - man be alone with he . Tom 
 Bowles look up , recognize his visitor , clear his brow , and rub 
 his mighty hand . 



 CHAPTER XIX . 

 KENELM CHILLINGLY draw a chair close to his antagonist 's , and silently 
 lay a hand on his . 

 Tom Bowles take up the hand in both his own , turn it curiously towards 
 the moonlight , gaze at it , poise it , then with a sound between groan 
 and laugh toss it away as a thing hostile but trivial , rise and lock 
 the door , come back to his seat and say bluffly , - 

 " what do you want with I now ? " 

 " I want to ask you a favour . " 

 " Favour ? " 

 " the great which man can ask from man , - friendship . you see , my dear 
 Tom , " continue Kenelm , make himself quite at home , throw his arm 
 over the back of Tom 's chair , and stretch his leg comfortably as 
 one do by one 's own fireside ; " you see , my dear Tom , that man like 
 us - young , single , not on the whole bad - look as man go - can 
 find sweetheart in plenty . if one do not like we , another will ; 
 sweetheart be sow everywhere like nettle and thistle . but the 
 rare thing in life be a friend . now , tell I frankly , in the course 
 of your wandering do you ever come into a village where you could not 
 have get a sweetheart if you have ask for one ; and if , have get 
 a sweetheart , you have lose she , do you think you would have have any 
 difficulty in find another ? but have you such a thing in the world , 
 beyond the pale of your own family , as a true friend , - a man friend ; and 
 suppose that you have such a friend , - a friend who would stand by you 
 through thick and thin ; who would tell you your fault to your face , and 
 praise you for your good quality behind your back ; who would do all 
 he could to save you from a danger , and all he could to get you out of 
 one , - suppose you have such a friend and lose he , do you believe that 
 if you live to the age of Methuselah you could find another ? you do not 
 answer I ; you be silent . well , Tom , I ask you to be such a friend to 
 I , and I will be such a friend to you . " 

 Tom be so thoroughly " take aback " by this address that he remain 
 dumfounde . but he feel as if the cloud in his soul be break , and 
 a ray of sunlight be force its way through the sullen darkness . 
 at length , however , the recede rage within he return , though with 
 vacillate step , and he growl between his tooth , - 

 " a pretty friend indeed , rob I of my girl ! go along with you ! " 

 " she be not your girl any more than she be or ever can be mine . " 

 " what , you be not   after she ? " 

 " certainly not ; I be go to Luscombe , and I ask you to come with I . 
 do you think I be go to leave you here ? " 

 " what be it to you ? " 

 " everything . Providence have permit I to save you from the most 
 lifelong of all sorrow . for - think ! can any sorrow be more lasting 
 than have be yours if you have attain your wish ; if you have force or 
 frighten a woman to be your partner till death do part , - you love 
 she , she loathe you ; you conscious , night and day , that your very love 
 have insure her misery , and that misery haunt you like a ghost ! - that 
 sorrow I have save you . may Providence permit I to complete my work , 
 and save you also from the most irredeemable of all crime ! look into 
 your soul , then recall the thought which all day long , and not least at 
 the moment I cross this threshold , be rise up , make reason dumb 
 and conscience blind , and then lie your hand on your heart and say , ' I 
 be guiltless of a dream of murder . ' " 

 the wretched man spring up erect , menacing , and , meet Kenelm 's calm , 
 steadfast , pity gaze , drop no less suddenly , - drop on the 
 floor , cover his face with his hand , and a great cry come forth 
 between sob and howl . 

 " Brother , " say Kenelm , kneel beside he , and twin his arm round 
 the man 's heaving breast , " it be over now ; with that cry the demon that 
 madden you have flee forever . " 



 CHAPTER XX . 

 when , some time after , Kenelm quit the room and join Mrs. Bowles

 as he walk towards the farmhouse , Kenelm encounter Mr. Lethbridge ,

 " the squire be a good man of business , and , though no one can call he

 the Parson muse , and be silent .
