 CHAPTER XXI . 

 MR . SAUNDERSON and Kenelm sit in the arbour : the former sip his grog 
 and smoke his pipe ; the latter look forth into the summer night 
 sky with an earnest yet abstract gaze , as if he be try to count 
 the star in the Milky Way . 

 " ha ! " say Mr. Saunderson , who be conclude an argument ; " you see it 
 now , do not   you ? " 

 " I ? not a bit of it . you tell I that your grandfather be a farmer , 
 and your father be a farmer , and that you have be a farmer for thirty 
 year ; and from these premise you deduce the illogical and irrational 
 conclusion that therefore your son must be a farmer . " 

 " young man , you may think yourself very know 'cause you have be at 
 the ' varsity , and sweep away a headful of book - learning . " 

 " stop , " quoth Kenelm . " you grant that a university be learn . " 

 " well , I suppose so . " 

 " but how could it be learn if those who quit it bring the 
 learning away ? we leave it all behind we in the care of the tutor . but 
 I know what you be go to say , - that it be not because I have read 
 more book than you have that I be to give myself air and pretend to 
 have more knowledge of life than a man of your year and experience . 
 agree , as a general rule . but do not every doctor , however wise and 
 skilful , prefer take another doctor 's opinion about himself , even 
 though that other doctor have just start in practice ? and see that 
 doctor , take they as a body , be monstrous clever fellow , be not 
 the example they set we worth follow ? do it not prove that no man , 
 however wise , be a good judge of his own case ? now , your son 's case 
 be really your case : you see it through the medium of your liking and 
 disliking ; and insist upon force a square peg into a round hole , 
 because in a round hole you , be a round peg , feel tight and 
 comfortable . now I call that irrational . " 

 " I do not   see why my son have any right to fancy himself a square peg , " 
 say the farmer , doggedly , " when his father and his grandfather and his 
 great - grandfather have be round peg ; and it be agin ' nature for 
 any creature not to take after its own kind . a dog be a pointer or 
 a sheep - dog accord as its forebear be pointer or sheep - dog . 
 there , " cry the farmer , triumphantly , shake the ash out of his 
 pipe . " I think I have pose you , young master ! " 

 " no ; for you have take it for grant that the breed have not be 
 cross . but suppose that a sheep - dog have marry a pointer , be you 
 sure that his son will not be more of a pointer than a sheep - dog ? " 

 Mr. Saunderson arrest himself in the task of refill his pipe , and 
 scratch his head . 

 " you see , " continue Kenelm , " that you have cross the breed . you 
 marry a tradesman 's daughter , and I dare say her grandfather and 
 great - grandfather be tradesman too . now , most son take after their 
 mother , and therefore Mr. Saunderson junior take after his kind on the 
 distaff side , and come into the world a square peg , which can only be 
 tight and comfortable in a square hole . it be no use arguing , farmer : 
 your boy must go to his uncle ; and there be an end of the matter . " 

 " by gole ! " say the farmer , " you seem to think you can talk I out of 
 my sens . " 

 " no ; but I think if you have your own way you would talk your son into 
 the workhouse . " 

 " what ! by stick to the land like his father before he ? let a man 
 stick by the land , and the land will stick by he . " 

 " let a man stick in the mud , and the mud will stick to he . you put 
 your heart in your farm , and your son would only put his foot into it . 
 courage ! do not   you see that Time be a whirligig , and all thing come 
 round ? every day somebody leave the land and go off into trade . by 
 and by he grow rich , and then his great desire be to get back to 
 the land again . he leave it the son of a farmer : he return to it as a 
 squire . your son , when he get to be fifty , will invest his saving in 
 acre , and have tenant of his own . Lord , how he will lay down the law 
 to they ! I would not advise you to take a farm under he . " 

 " catch I at it ! " say the farmer . " he would turn all the content of 
 the ' pothecary 's shop into my fallow , and call it ' progress . ' " 

 " let he physic the fallow when he have farm of his own : keep yours out 
 of his chemical clutch . come , I shall tell he to pack up and be off 
 to his uncle 's next week ? " 

 " well , well , " say the farmer , in a resign tone : " a wilful man must 
 e'en have his way . " 

 " and the good thing a sensible man can do be not to cross it . Mr. 
 Saunderson , give I your honest hand . you be one of those man who put 
 the son of good father in mind of their own ; and I think of mine when 
 I say ' God bless you ! ' " 

 quit the farmer , Kenelm re - enter the house , and seek Mr. 
 Saunderson junior in his own room . he find that young gentleman still 
 up , and read an eloquent tract on the Emancipation of the Human Race 
 from all Tyrannical Control , - Political , Social , Ecclesiastical , and 
 Domestic . 

 the lad look up sulkily , and say , on encounter Kenelm 's 
 melancholic visage , " ah ! I see you have talk with the old governor , 
 and he will not hear of it . " 

 " in the first place , " answer Kenelm , ' since you value yourself on 
 a superior education , allow I to advise you to study the English 
 language , as the form of it be maintain by the eld author , whom , 
 in spite of an Age of Progress , man of superior education esteem . no one 
 who have go through that study ; no one , indeed , who have study the Ten 
 Commandments in the vernacular , - commit the mistake of suppose that 
 ' the old governor ' be a synonymous expression for ' father . ' in the 
 second place , since you pretend to the superior enlightenment which 
 result from a superior education , learn to know well your own self 
 before you set up as a teacher of mankind . excuse the liberty I take , 
 as your sincere well - wisher , when I tell you that you be at present 
 a conceited fool , - in short , that which make one boy call another an 
 ' ass . ' but when one have a poor head he may redeem the average balance of 
 humanity by increase the wealth of the heart . try and increase your . 
 your father consent to your choice of your lot at the sacrifice of 
 all his own inclination . this be a sore trial to a father 's pride , a 
 father 's affection ; and few father make such sacrifice with a good 
 grace . I have thus keep my promise to you , and enforce your wish on 
 Mr. Saunderson 's judgment , because I be sure you would have be a very 
 bad farmer . it now remain for you to show that you can be a very good 
 tradesman . you be bind in honour to I and to your father to try your 
 good to be so ; and meanwhile leave the task of upset the world 
 to those who have no shop in it , which would go crash in the general 
 tumble . and so good - night to you . " 

 to these admonitory word , sacro digna silentio , Saunderson junior 
 listen with a dropping jaw and fascinated star eye . he feel like 
 an infant to whom the nurse have give a hasty shake , and who be too 
 stupefy by that operation to know whether he be hurt or not . 

 a minute after Kenelm have quit the room he reappear at the door , 
 and say in a conciliatory whisper , " do not   take it to heart that I 
 call you a conceited fool and an ass . these term be no doubt just as 
 applicable to myself . but there be a more conceited fool and a great 
 ass than either of we ; and that be the Age in which we have the 
 misfortune to be bear , - an Age of Progress , Mr. Saunderson , junior ! - an 
 Age of Prigs . " 




 BOOK III . 



 CHAPTER I. 

 if there be a woman in the world who might be form and fit 
 to reconcile Kenelm Chillingly to the sweet trouble of love and the 
 pleasant bickering of wed life , one might reasonably suppose that 
 that woman could be find in Cecilia Travers . an only daughter and 
 lose her mother in childhood , she have be raise to the mistress - ship 
 of a household at an age in which most girl be still put their 
 doll to bed ; and thus have early acquire that sense of responsibility , 
 accompany with the habit of self - reliance , which seldom fail to give 
 a certain nobility to character ; though almost as often , in the case of 
 woman , it steal away the tender gentleness which constitute the charm 
 of their sex . 

 it have not do so in the instance of Cecilia Travers , because she be 
 so womanlike that even the exercise of power could not make she manlike . 
 there be in the depth of her nature such an instinct of sweetness that 
 wherever her mind toil and wander it gather and hoard honey . 

 she have one advantage over most girl in the same rank of life , - she 
 have not be teach to fritter away such capacity for culture as 
 Providence give she in the sterile nothingness which be call 
 feminine accomplishment . she do not paint figure out of draw 
 in meagre water - colour ; she have not devote year of her life to the 
 inflicting on polite audience the boredom of italian bravura , which 
 they could hear well sing by a third - rate professional singer in 
 a metropolitan music - hall . I be afraid she have no other female 
 accomplishment than those by which the sempstress or embroideress earn 
 her daily bread . that sort of work she love , and she do it deftly . 

 but if she have not be profitlessly plague by master , Cecilia Travers 
 have be singularly favour by her father 's choice of a teacher : no 
 great merit in he either . he have a prejudice against professional 
 governess , and it chance that among his own family connection be a 
 certain Mrs. Campion , a lady of some literary distinction , whose husband 
 have hold a high situation in one of our public office , and living , much 
 to his satisfaction , up to a very handsome income , have die , much to the 
 astonishment of other , without leave a farthing behind he . 

 fortunately , there be no child to provide for . a small government 
 pension be allot to the widow ; and as her husband 's house have be 
 make by her one of the pleasantest in London , she be popular enough to 
 be invite by numerous friend to their country seat ; among other , by 
 Mr. Travers . she come intend to stay a fortnight . at the end of that 
 time she have grow so attach to Cecilia , and Cecilia to she , and her 
 presence have become so pleasant and so useful to her host , that 
 the Squire entreat she to stay and undertake the education of his 
 daughter . Mrs. Campion , after some hesitation , gratefully consent ; and 
 thus Cecilia , from the age of eight to her present age of nineteen , have 
 the inestimable advantage of live in constant companionship with a 
 woman of richly cultivate mind , accustom to hear the good criticism 
 on the good book , and add to no small accomplishment in literature 
 the refinement of manner and that sort of prudent judgment which result 
 from habitual intercourse with an intellectual and gracefully world - wise 
 circle of society : so that Cecilia herself , without be at all blue or 
 pedantic , become one of those rare young woman with whom a well - educate 
 man can converse on equal term ; from whom he gain as much as he can 
 impart to she ; while a man who , not care much about book , be still 
 gentleman enough to value good breeding , feel a relief in exchange the 
 form of his native language without the shock of hear that a bishop 
 be " a swell " or a croquet - party " awfully jolly . " 

 in a word , Cecilia be one of those woman whom Heaven form for man 's 
 helpmate ; who , if he be bear to rank and wealth , would , as his 
 partner , reflect on they a new dignity , and add to their enjoyment by 
 bring forth their duty ; who , not less if the husband she choose be 
 poor and struggling , would encourage , sustain , and soothe he , take she 
 own share of his burden , and temper the bitterness of life with the 
 all - recompense sweetness of her smile . 

 little , indeed , as yet have she ever think of love or of lover . she 
 have not even form to herself any of those ideal which float before 
 the eye of most girl when they enter their teen . but of two thing 
 she feel inly convince : first , that she could never we d where she do 
 not love ; and secondly , that where she do love it would be for life . 

 and now I close this sketch with a picture of the girl herself . she have 
 just come into her room from inspect the preparation for the evening 
 entertainment which her father be to give to his tenant and rural 
 neighbour . 

 she have throw aside her straw hat , and put down the large basket which 
 she have empty of flower . she pause before the glass , smooth back 
 the ruffle band of her hair , - hair of a dark , soft chestnut , silky 
 and luxuriant , - never pollute , and never , so long as she live , to be 
 pollute by auricomous cosmetic , far from that delicate darkness , every 
 tint of the colour traditionally dedicated to the lock of Judas . 

 her complexion , usually of that soft bloom which incline to paleness , 
 be now heighten into glow by exercise and sunlight . the feature be 
 small and feminine ; the eye dark with long lash ; the mouth singularly 
 beautiful , with a dimple on either side , and part now in a half - smile 
 at some pleasant recollection , give a glimpse of small tooth 
 glistening as pearl . but the peculiar charm of her face be in an 
 expression of serene happiness , that sort of happiness which seem as if 
 it have never be interrupt by a sorrow , have never be trouble by a 
 sin , - that holy kind of happiness which belong to innocence , the light 
 reflect from a heart and conscience alike at peace . 



 CHAPTER II . 

 it be a lovely summer evening for the squire 's rural entertainment . Mr. 
 Travers have some guest stay with he : they have din early for 
 the occasion , and be now group with their host a little before six 
 o'clock on the lawn . the house be of irregular architecture , alter 
 or add to at various period from the reign of Elizabeth to that of 
 Victoria : at one end , the old part , a gable with mullion window ; 
 at the other , the new part , a flat - roof wing , with modern sash 
 opening to the ground , the intermediate part much hide by a veranda 
 cover with creeper in full bloom . the lawn be a spacious table - land 
 face the west , and back by a green and gentle hill , crown with 
 the ruin of an ancient priory . on one side of the lawn stretch a 
 flower - garden and pleasure - ground , originally plan by Repton ; on the 
 opposite angle of the sward be place two large marquee , - one for 
 dancing , the other for supper . towards the south the view be leave open , 
 and command the prospect of an old English park , not of the stateliest 
 character ; not intersect with ancient avenue , nor clothe 
 with profitless fern as lair for deer : but the park of a careful 
 agriculturist , unite profit with show , the sward duly drain and 
 nourish , fit to fatten bullock in an incredibly short time , and 
 somewhat spoilt to the eye by subdivision of wire fence . Mr. Travers 
 be renowned for skilful husbandry , and the general management of 
 land to the good advantage . he have come into the estate while still in 
 childhood , and thus enjoy the accumulation of a long minority . he have 
 enter the Guards at the age of eighteen , and have more command of 
 money than most of his contemporary , though they might be of high 
 rank and the son of rich man , he have be much court and much 
 plunder . at the age of twenty - five he find himself one of the leader 
 of fashion , renowne chiefly for reckless daring where - ever honour could 
 be pluck out of the nettle danger : a steeple - chaser , whose exploit 
 make a quiet man 's hair stand on end ; a rider across country , take 
 leap which a more cautious huntsman carefully avoid . know at Paris 
 as well as in London , he have be admire by lady whose smile have 
 cost he duel , the mark of which still remain in glorious scar 
 on his person . no man ever seem more likely to come to dire 
 grief before attain the age of thirty , for at twenty - seven all the 
 accumulation of his minority be go ; and his estate , which , when he 
 come of age , be scarcely three thousand a year , but entirely at his own 
 disposal , be mortgage up to its eye . 

 his friend begin to shake their head and call he " poor fellow ; " but , 
 with all his wild fault , Leopold Travers have be wholly pure from the 
 two vice out of which a man do not often redeem himself . he have never 
 drunk and he have never gamble . his nerve be not break , his brain 
 be not besotte . there be plenty of health in he yet , mind and body . 
 at the critical period of his life he marry for love , and his choice 
 be a most felicitous one . the lady have no fortune ; but though handsome 
 and high - bear , she have no taste for extravagance , and no desire for 
 other society than that of the man she love . so when he say , " let we 
 settle in the country and try our good to live on a few hundred , lie 
 by , and keep the old place out of the market , " she consent with a 
 joyful heart : and marvel it be to all how this wild Leopold Travers 
 do settle down ; do take to cultivate his home farm with his man from 
 sunrise to sunset like a common tenant - farmer ; do contrive to pay the 
 interest on the mortgage , and keep his head above water . after some 
 year of pupilage in this school of thrift , during which his habit 
 become form and his whole character brace , Leopold Travers suddenly 
 find himself again rich , through the wife whom he have so prudently 
 married without other dower than her love and her virtue . her only 
 brother , Lord Eagleton , a Scotch peer , have be engage in marriage to a 
 young lady , consider to be a rare prize in the lottery of wedlock . 
 the marriage be break off under very disastrous circumstance ; but the 
 young lord , good - looking and agreeable , be naturally expect to seek 
 speedy consolation in some other alliance . nevertheless he do not 
 do so : he become a confirmed invalid , and die single , leave to his 
 sister all in his power to save from the distant kinsman who succeed 
 to his land and title , - a goodly sum , which not only suffice to pay 
 off the mortgage on Neesdale Park but bestow on its owner a surplus 
 which the practical knowledge of country life that he have acquire 
 enable he to devote with extraordinary profit to the general 
 improvement of his estate . he replace tumble - down old farm building 
 with new construction on the most approve principle ; buy or 
 pension off certain slovenly incompetent tenant ; throw sundry petty 
 holding into large farm suit to the building he construct ; 
 purchase here and there small bit of land , commodious to the farm 
 they adjoin , and complete the integrity of his ring - fence ; stub 
 up profitless wood which diminish the value of neighbouring arable 
 by obstruct sun and air and harbour legion of rabbit ; and 
 then , seek tenant of enterprise and capital , more than double his 
 original yearly rental , and perhaps more than triple the market value 
 of his property . simultaneously with this acquisition of fortune , he 
 emerge from the inhospitable and unsocial obscurity which his previous 
 poverty have compel , take an active part in county business , prove 
 himself an excellent speaker at public meeting , subscribe liberally to 
 the hunt , and occasionally join in it , - a less bold but a wise rider 
 than of yore . in short , as Themistocles boast that he could make a 
 small state great , so Leopold Travers might boast with equal truth , 
 that , by his energy , his judgment , and the weight of his personal 
 character , he have make the owner of a property which have be at 
 his accession to it of third - rate rank in the county a personage so 
 considerable that no knight of the shire against whom he declare could 
 have be elect , and if he have determine to stand himself he would 
 have be choose free of expense . 

 but he say , on be solicit to become a candidate , " when a man once 
 give himself up to the care and improvement of a land estate , he 
 have no time and no heart for anything else . an estate be an income or 
 a kingdom , accord as the owner choose to take it . I take it as a 
 kingdom , and I can not be roi faineant , with a steward for maire du 
 palais . a king do not go into the House of Commons . " 

 three year after this rise in the social ladder , Mrs. Travers be 
 seize with congestion of the lung follow by pleurisy , and die after 
 less than a week 's illness . leopold never wholly recover her loss . 
 though still young and always handsome , the idea of another wife , the 
 love of another woman , be notion which he dismiss from his , mind 
 with a quiet scorn . he be too masculine a creature to parade grief . 
 for some week , indeed , he shut himself up in his own room , so rigidly 
 seclude that he would not see even his daughter . but one morning he 
 appear in his field as usual , and from that day resume his old 
 habit , and gradually renew that cordial interchange of hospitality 
 which have popularly distinguish he since his accession to wealth . 
 still people feel that the man be change ; he be more taciturn , 
 more grave : if always just in his dealing , he take the hard side of 
 justice , where in his wife 's time he have take the gentler . perhaps , to 
 a man of strong will , the habitual intercourse with an amiable woman be 
 essential for those occasion in which will well prove the fineness of 
 its temper by the facility with which it can be bent . 

 it may be say that Leopold Travers might have find such intercourse in 
 the intimate companionship of his own daughter . but she be a mere child 
 when his wife die , and she grow up to womanhood too insensibly for 
 he to note the change . besides , where a man have find a wife his 
 all - in - all , a daughter can never supply her place . the very reverence 
 due to child preclude unrestrained confidence ; and there be not 
 that sense of permanent fellowship in a daughter which a man have in a 
 wife , - any day a stranger may appear and carry she off from he . at all 
 event Leopold do not own in Cecilia the soften influence to 
 which he have yield in her mother . he be fond of she , proud of she , 
 indulgent to she ; but the indulgence have its set limit . whatever she 
 ask solely for herself he grant ; whatever she wish for matter 
 under feminine control - the domestic household , the parish school , the 
 alms - receive poor - obtain his gentle consideration . but when she 
 have be solicit by some offend out - of - door dependant or some petty 
 default tenant to use her good office in favour of the culprit , Mr. 
 Travers check her interference by a firm " no , " though utter in a 
 mild accent , and accompany with a masculine aphorism to the effect 
 that " there would be no such thing as strict justice and discipline 
 order in the world if a man yield to a woman 's pleading in any matter 
 of business between man and man . " from this it will be see that 
 Mr. Lethbridge have overrate the value of Cecilia 's alliance in the 
 negotiation respect Mrs. Bawtrey 's premium and shop . 



 CHAPTER III . 

 if , have just peruse what have thus be write on the biographical 
 antecedent and mental characteristic of Leopold Travers , you , my dear 
 reader , be to be personally present to that gentleman as he now 
 stand , the central figure of the group gather round he , on his 
 terrace , you would probably be surprised , - nay , I have no doubt you 
 would say to yourself , " not at all the sort of man I expect . " in that 
 slender form , somewhat below the middle height ; in that fair countenance 
 which still , at the age of forty - eight , retain a delicacy of feature 
 and of colour which be of almost womanlike beauty , and , from the 
 quiet placidity of its expression , convey at first glance the notion of 
 almost womanlike mildness , - it would be difficult to recognize a man who 
 in youth have be renowned for reckless daring , in mature year more 
 honourably distinguish for steadfast prudence and determined purpose , 
 and who , alike in fault or in merit , be as emphatically masculine as 
 a biped in trouser can possibly be . 

 Mr. Travers be listen to a young man of about two and twenty , the 
 eld son of the rich nobleman of the county , and who intend to 
 start for the representation of the shire at the next general election , 
 which be close at hand . the Hon . George Belvoir be tall , incline to be 
 stout , and will look well on the husting . he have have those pain take 
 with his education which an english peer generally do take with the 
 son intend to succeed to the representation of an honourable name and 
 the responsibility of high station . if eld son do not often make 
 as great a figure in the world as their young brother , it be not 
 because their mind be less cultivate , but because they have less 
 motive power for action . George Belvoir be well read , especially 
 in that sort of reading which befit a future senator , - history , 
 statistic , political economy , so far as that dismal science be 
 compatible with the agricultural interest . he be also well - principle , 
 have a strong sense of discipline and duty , be prepare in politic 
 firmly to uphold as right whatever be propose by his own party , and 
 to reject as wrong whatever be propose by the other . at present he be 
 rather loud and noisy in the assertion of his opinion , - young man fresh 
 from the University generally be . it be the secret wish of Mr. Travers 
 that George Belvoir should become his son - in - law ; less because of his 
 rank and wealth   though such advantage be not of a nature to be 
 despise by a practical man like Leopold Travers   than on account of 
 those quality in his personal character which be likely to render 
 he an excellent husband . 

 seat on wire bench , just without the veranda , but shade by its 
 fragrant festoon , be Mrs. Campion and three lady , the wife of 
 neighbouring squire . Cecilia stand a little apart from they , bend

 but see , the company be arrive ! how suddenly that green space , ten 
 minute ago so solitary , have become animate and populous ! 

 indeed the park now present a very lively appearance : van , cart , and 
 farmer ' chaise be see in crowded procession along the wind road ;

 host impart a respect for order to his ruder visitor ; not even a 
 turbulent boy attempt to scale the fence , or creep through their 
 wire ; all thread the narrow turnstile which give egress from one 
 subdivision of the sward to another . 

 Mr. Travers turn to George Belvoir : " I see old farmer Steen 's 
 yellow gig . mind how you talk to he , George . he be full of whim and 
 crotchet , and if you once brush his feather the wrong way he will be

 class . " 

 " I suppose , " say George , " that if Mr. Steen be the good man to second

 " a good speaker ? in one sense he be . he never say a word too much . the 
 last time he second the nomination of the man you be to succeed , this 
 be his speech : ' Brother Electors , for twenty year I have be one of 
 the judge at our county cattle - show . I know one animal from another . 
 look at the specimen before we to - day none of they be as good 
 of their kind as I have   see elsewhere . but if you choose Sir John Hogg 
 you will not get the wrong sow by the ear ! ' " 

 " at least , " say George , after a laugh at this sample of eloquence 
 unadorned , " Mr. Steen do not err on the side of flattery in his 
 commendation of a candidate . but what make he such an authority with 
 the farmer ? be he a first - rate agriculturist ? " 

 " in thrift , yes ! - in spirit , no ! he say that all expensive experiment 
 should be leave to gentleman farmer . he be an authority with other 
 tenant : firstly , because he be a very keen censor of their landlord ; 
 secondly , because he hold himself thoroughly independent of his own ; 
 thirdly , because he be suppose to have study the political bearing 
 of question that affect the land interest , and have more than once 
 be summon to give his opinion on such subject to Committees of both 
 Houses of Parliament . here he come . Observe , when I leave you to talk 
 to he : firstly , that you confess utter ignorance of practical farming ; 
 nothing enrage he like the presumption of a gentleman farmer like 
 myself : secondly , that you ask his opinion on the publication of 
 Agricultural Statistics , just modestly intimate that you , as at 
 present advise , think that inquisitorial research into a man 's 
 business involve principle oppose to the British Constitution . and on

 of your father in particular , make no reply , but listen with an air of 
 melancholy conviction . how do you do , Mr. Steen , and how be the mistress ? 
 why have you not bring she with you ? " 

 " my good woman be in the straw again , squire . who be that youngster ? " 

 " Hist ! let I introduce Mr. Belvoir . " 

 Mr. Belvoir offer his hand . 

 " no , sir ! " vociferate Steen , put both his own hand behind he . " no 
 offence , young gentleman . but I do not   give my hand at first sight to

 against you . but , if you be a farmer 's friend rabbit be not , and my 
 lord your father be a great one for rabbit . "

 the guest now arrive fast and thick . they consist chiefly not only

 it be not a supper intend to include the labour class ; for Mr.

 " well , Lethbridge , " say Mr. Travers , " where be the young gladiator you

 " meanwhile , " say George , with a timid attempt at gallantry , as they now

 have the good wish of Miss Travers . if lady have vote , as Mr. Mill 
 recommend , why , then - " 

 " why , then , I should vote as Papa do , " say Miss Travers , simply . " and 
 if woman have vote , I suspect there would be very little peace in any 
 household where they do not vote as the man at the head of it wish 
 they . " 

 " but I believe , after all , " say the aspirant to Parliament , seriously , 
 " that the advocate for female suffrage would limit it to woman 
 independent of masculine control , widow and spinster vote in right

 " in that case , " say Cecilia , " I suppose they would still generally go

 " you underrate the good sense of your sex . " 

 " I hope not . do you underrate the good sense of your , if , in far more

  cavalier seul . " 

 " by the way , " say George , in another interval of the dance , " do

 Westshire ? " 

 " no ; why do you ask ? " 

 " because I think I catch a glimpse of his face : it be just as Mr.

 " chillingly ! but surely some person be talk yesterday at dinner 
 about a young gentleman of that name as be likely to stand for 
 Westshire at the next election , but who have make a very unpopular and 
 eccentric speech on the occasion of his come of age . " 

 " the same man : I be at college with he , - a very singular character . he 
 be think clever ; win a prize or two ; take a good degree : but it be 
 generally say that he would have deserve a much high one if some of 
 his paper have not contain covert jest either on the subject or the

 quality in his parliamentary speech . just like Chillingly , to turn

 " and , once see , you would not easily forget he , - a dark , handsome 
 face , with large melancholy eye , and with one of those spare slender 
 figure which enable a man to disguise his strength , as a fraudulent 
 billiard - player disguise his play . " 

 the dance have cease during this conversation , and the speaker be now

 most equal and most polished of all democracy , - the good society of 
 large capital . and to such acquire advantage Leopold Travers add the 
 inborn quality that please . 

 later in the evening Travers , again accost Mr. Lethbridge , say , " I

 Tom Bowles ; and all I hear so confirm the interest your own

 but I have my own theory as to the management of an estate , and my
