 CHAPTER IV . 

 quitting Mr. Lethbridge , Travers turn with quick step towards the more 
 solitary part of the ground . he do not find the object of his search 
 in the walk of the plantation ; and , on take the circuit of his 
 demesne , wind his way back towards the lawn through a sequester rocky 
 hollow in the rear of the marquee , which have be devote to a fernery . 
 here he come to a sudden pause ; for , seat a few yard before he on 
 a gray crag , and the moonlight full on his face , he see a solitary man , 
 look upwards with a still and mournful gaze , evidently absorb in 
 abstract contemplation . 

 recall the description of the stranger which he have hear from Mr. 
 Lethbridge and the saunderson , Mr. Travers feel sure that he have come 
 on he at last . he approach gently ; and , be much conceal by the 
 tall fern , Kenelm   for that itinerant it be   do not see he advance , 
 until he feel a hand on his shoulder , and , turn round , beheld a 
 win smile and hear a pleasant voice . 

 " I think I be not mistaken , " say Leopold Travers , " in assume you to 
 be the gentleman whom Mr. Lethbridge promise to introduce to I , and 
 who be stay with my tenant , Mr. Saunderson ? " 

 Kenelm rise and bow . traver see at once that it be the bow of a man 
 in his own world , and not in keep with the Sunday costume of a petty 
 farmer . " Nay , " say he , " let we talk seat ; " and place himself on the 
 crag , he make room for Kenelm beside he . 

 " in the first place , " resume Travers , " I must thank you for have 
 do a public service in put down the brute force which have long 
 tyrannize over the neighbourhood . often in my young day I have feel 
 the disadvantage of height and sinew , whenever it would have be a 
 great convenience to terminate dispute or chastise insolence by a 
 resort to man 's primitive weapon ; but I never more lament my physical 
 inferiority than on certain occasion when I would have give my ear to 
 be able to thrash Tom Bowles myself . it have be as great a disgrace to 
 my estate that that bully should so long have infest it as it be 
 to the King of Italy not to be able with all his army to put down a 
 brigand in Calabria . " 

 " Pardon I , Mr. Travers , but I be one of those rare person who do not 
 like to hear ill of their friend . Mr. Thomas Bowles be a particular 
 friend of mine . " 

 " eh ! " cry Travers , aghast . " ' friend ! ' you be joke . 

 " you would not accuse I of joke if you know I well . but surely you 
 have feel that there be few friend one like more cordially , and ought 
 to respect more heedfully , than the enemy with whom one have just make it 
 up . " 

 " you say well , and I accept the rebuke , " say Travers , more and more 
 surprised . " and I certainly have less right to abuse Mr. Bowles than 
 you have , since I have not the courage to fight he . to turn to another 
 subject less provocative . Mr. Lethbridge have tell I of your amiable 
 desire to serve two of his young parishioner , will Somers and Jessie 
 Wiles , and of your generous offer to pay the money Mrs. Bawtrey demand 
 for the transfer of her lease . to that negotiation my consent be 
 necessary , and that consent I can not give . shall I tell you why ? " 

 " pray do . your reason may admit of argument . " 

 " every reason admit of argument , " say Mr. Travers , amuse at the calm 
 assurance of a youthful stranger in anticipate argument with a skilful 
 proprietor on the management of his own property . " I do not , however , 
 tell you my reason for the sake of argument , but in vindication of my 
 seeming want of courtesy towards yourself . I have have a very hard and a 
 very difficult task to perform in bring the rental of my estate up 
 to its proper value . in do so , I have be compel to adopt one 
 uniform system , equally apply to my large and my pettiest holding . 
 that system consist in secure the good and safe tenant I can , 
 at the rent compute by a valuer in whom I have confidence . to this 
 system , universally adopt on my estate , though it incur much 
 unpopularity at first , I have at length succeed in reconcile the 
 public opinion of my neighbourhood . People begin by say I be 
 hard ; they now acknowledge I be just . if I once give way to favour or 
 sentiment , I unhinge my whole system . every day I be subject to move 
 solicitation . Lord Twostars , a keen politician , beg I to give a 
 vacant farm to a tenant because he be an excellent canvasser , and have 
 always vote straight with the party . Mrs. Fourstars , a most benevolent 
 woman , entreat I not to dismiss another tenant , because he be in 
 distressed circumstance and have a large family ; very good reason 
 perhaps for my excuse he an arrear , or allow he a retire 
 pension , but the bad reason in the world for let he continue to 
 ruin himself and my land . now , Mrs. Bawtrey have a small holding on lease 
 at the inadequate rent of l8 a year . she ask L45 for its transfer , but 
 she can not   transfer the lease without my consent ; and I can get L12 a 
 year as a moderate rental from a large choice of competent tenant . it 
 will well answer I to pay she the L45 myself , which I have no doubt 
 the incoming tenant would pay I back , at least in part ; and if he do 
 not , the additional rent would be good interest for my expenditure . 
 now , you happen to take a sentimental interest , as you pass through the 
 village , in the love of a needy cripple whose utmost industry have but 
 serve to save himself from parish relief , and a giddy girl without a 
 sixpence , and you ask I to accept these very equivocal tenant instead 
 of substantial one , and at a rent one - third less than the market value . 
 suppose that I yield to your request , what become of my reputation 
 for practical , business - like justice ? I shall have make an inroad into 
 the system by which my whole estate be manage , and have invite all 
 manner of solicitation on the part of friend and neighbour , which I 
 could no long consistently refuse , have show how easily I can be 
 persuade into compliance by a stranger whom I may never see again . and 
 be you sure , after all , that , if you do prevail on I , you would do 
 the individual good you aim at ? it be , no doubt , very pleasant to think 
 one have make a young couple happy . but if that young couple fail in 
 keep the little shop to which you would transplant they   and 
 nothing more likely : peasant seldom become good shopkeeper , and find 
 themselves , with a family of child , dependent solely , not on the arm 
 of a strong labourer , but the ten finger of a sickly cripple , who make 
 clever basket , for which there be but slight and precarious demand in 
 the neighbourhood , may you not have insure the misery of the couple you 
 wish to render happy ? " 

 " I withdraw all argument , " say Kenelm , with an aspect so humiliated and 
 deject , that it would have soften a Greenland bear , or a Counsel for 
 the prosecution . " I be more and more convinced that of all the sham in 
 the world that of benevolence be the great . it seem so easy to 
 do good , and it be so difficult to do it . everywhere , in this hateful 
 civilized life , one run one 's head against a system . a system , Mr. 
 Travers , be man 's servile imitation of the blind tyranny of what in our 
 ignorance we call ' Natural Laws , ' a mechanical something through which 
 the world be rule by the cruelty of General Principles , to the utter 
 disregard of individual welfare . by Natural Laws creature prey on each 
 other , and big fish eat little one upon system . it be , nevertheless , 
 a hard thing for the little fish . every nation , every town , every 
 hamlet , every occupation , have a system , by which , somehow or other , the 
 pond swarm with fish , of which a great many inferior contribute to 
 increase the size of a superior . it be an idle benevolence to keep 
 one solitary gudgeon out of the jaw of a pike . here be I do what I 
 think the simple thing in the world , ask a gentleman , evidently 
 as good - natured as myself , to allow an old woman to let she premise to 
 a deserve young couple , and pay what she ask for it out of my own 
 money . and I find that I be run against a system , and invade all 
 the law by which a rental be increase and an estate improve . Mr. 
 Travers , you have no cause for regret in not have beat Tom Bowles . 
 you have beat his victor , and I now give up all dream of further 
 interference with the Natural Laws that govern the village which I 
 have visit in vain . I have mean to remove Tom Bowles from that quiet 
 community . I shall now leave he to return to his former habit , - to 
 marry Jessie Wiles , which he certainly will do , and - " 

 " hold ! " cry Mr. Travers . " do you mean to say that you can induce Tom 
 Bowles to leave the village ? " 

 " I have induce he to do it , provide Jessie Wiles marry the 
 basket - maker ; but , as that be out of the question , I be bind to tell 
 he so , and he will stay . " 

 " but if he leave , what would become of his business ? his mother could 
 not keep it on ; his little place be a freehold ; the only house in the 
 village that do not belong to I , or I should have eject he long 
 ago . would he sell the premise to I ? " 

 " not if he stay and marry Jessie Wiles . but if he go with I to 
 Luscombe and settle in that town as a partner to his uncle , I suppose 
 he would be too glad to sell a house of which he can have no pleasant 
 recollection . but what then ? you can not violate your system for the 
 sake of a miserable forge . " 

 " it would not violate my system if , instead of yield to a sentiment , 
 I gain an advantage ; and , to say truth , I should be very glad to buy 
 that forge and the field that go with it . " 

 " ' tis your affair now , not mine , Mr. Travers . I no long presume to 
 interfere . I leave the neighbourhood to - morrow : see if you can negotiate 
 with Mr. Bowles . I have the honour to wish you a good evening . " 

 " Nay , young gentleman , I can not allow you to quit I thus . you have 
 decline apparently to join the dancer , but you will at least join the 
 supper . come ! " 

 " thank you sincerely , no . I come here merely on the business which your 
 system have settle . " 

 " but I be not sure that it be settle . " here Mr. Travers wind his arm 
 within Kenelm 's , and look he full in the face , say , " I know that 
 I be speak to a gentleman at least equal in rank to myself , but as 
 I enjoy the melancholy privilege of be the old man , do not think 
 I take an unwarrantable liberty in ask if you object to tell I your 
 name . I should like to introduce you to my daughter , who be very partial 
 to Jessie Wiles and to Will Somers . but I can not   venture to inflame her 
 imagination by designate you as a prince in disguise . " 

 " Mr. Travers , you express yourself with exquisite delicacy . but I 
 be just start in life , and I shrink from mortify my father by 
 associate my name with a signal failure . suppose I be an anonymous 
 contributor , say , to ' the Londoner , ' and I have just bring that 
 highly intellectual journal into discredit by a feeble attempt at 
 a good - natured criticism or a generous sentiment , would that be the 
 fitting occasion to throw off the mask , and parade myself to a mocking 
 world as the imbecile violator of an establish system ? should I not , 
 in a moment so untoward , more than ever desire to merge my insignificant 
 unit in the mysterious importance which the small Singular obtain 
 when he make himself a Plural , and speak not as ' I , ' but as ' we ' ? 
  we be insensible to the charm of young lady ; we be not bribe 
 by supper ; we , like the witch of ' Macbeth , ' have no name on earth ; 
  we be the great wisdom of the great number ; we be so upon 
 system ; we salute you , Mr. Travers , and depart unassailable . " 

 here Kenelm rise , doff and replace his hat in majestic salutation , 
 turn towards the entrance of the fernery , and find himself suddenly 
 face to face with George Belvoir , behind whom follow , with a throng 
 of guest , the fair form of Cecilia . George Belvoir catch Kenelm by the 
 hand , and exclaim , " chillingly ! I think I could not be mistake . " 

 " chillingly ! " echo Leopold Travers from behind . " be you the son of my 
 old friend Sir Peter ? " 

 thus discover and environ , Kenelm do not lose his wonted presence 
 of mind ; he turn round to Leopold Travers , who be now close in his 
 rear , and whisper , " if my father be your friend , do not disgrace his 
 son . do not say I be a failure . deviate from your system , and let will 
 Somers succeed Mrs. Bawtrey . " then revert his face to Mr. Belvoir , he 
 say tranquilly , " yes ; we have meet before . " 

 " Cecilia , " say Travers , now interpose , " I be happy to introduce to 
 you as Mr. Chillingly , not only the son of an old friend of mine , 
 not only the knight - errant of whose gallant conduct on behalf of your 
 protegee Jessie Wiles we have hear so much , but the eloquent arguer who 
 have conquer my well judgment in a matter on which I think myself 
 infallible . tell Mr. Lethbridge that I accept Will Somers as a tenant 
 for Mrs. Bawtrey 's premise . " 

 Kenelm grasp the Squire 's hand cordially . " may it be in my power to do 
 a kind thing to you , in spite of any system to the contrary ! " 

 " Mr. Chillingly , give your arm to my daughter . you will not now object 
 to join the dancer ? " 



 CHAPTER V. 

 CECILIA steal a shy glance at Kenelm as the two emerge from the fernery 
 into the open space of the lawn . his countenance please she . she 
 think she discover much latent gentleness under the cold and 
 mournful gravity of its expression ; and , attribute the silence he 
 maintain to some painful sense of an awkward position in the abrupt 
 betrayal of his incognito , seek with womanly tact to dispel his 
 suppose embarrassment . 

 " you have choose a delightful mode of see the country this lovely 
 summer weather , Mr. Chillingly . I believe such pedestrian exercise be 
 very common with university student during the long vacation . " 

 " very common , though they generally wander in pack like wild dog or 
 Australian dingoe . it be only a tame dog that one find on the road 
 travel by himself ; and then , unless he behave very quietly , it be 
 ten to one that he be stone as a mad dog . " 

 " but I be afraid , from what I hear , that you have not be travel 
 very quietly . " 

 " you be quite right , Miss Travers , and I be a sad dog if not a mad one . 
 but pardon I : we be near the marquee ; the band be strike up , and , 
 alas ! I be not a dancing dog . " 

 he release Cecilia 's arm , and bow . 

 " let we sit here a while , then , " say she , motion to a garden - bench . 
 " I have no engagement for the next dance , and , as I be a little tired , I 
 shall be glad of a reprieve . " 

 Kenelm sigh , and , with the air of a martyr stretch himself on the 
 rack , take his place beside the fair girl in the county . 

 " you be at college with Mr. Belvoir ? " 

 " I be . " 

 " he be think clever there ? " 

 " I have not a doubt of it . " 

 " you know he be canvass our county for the next election . my father 
 take a warm interest in his success , and think he will be a useful 
 member of Parliament . " 

 " of that I be certain . for the first five year he will be call 
 pushing , noisy , and conceited , much sneered at by man of his own age , 
 and cough down on great occasion ; for the five follow year he 
 will be consider a sensible man in committee , and a necessary feature 
 in debate ; at the end of those year he will be an under - secretary ; in 
 five year more he will be a Cabinet Minister , and the representative of 
 an important section of opinion ; he will be an irreproachable private 
 character , and his wife will be see wear the family diamond at all 
 the great party . she will take an interest in politic and theology ; 
 and if she die before he , her husband will show his sense of wed 
 happiness by choose another lady , equally fit to wear the family 
 diamond and to maintain the family consequence . " 

 in spite of her laughter , Cecilia feel a certain awe at the solemnity of 
 voice and manner with which Kenelm deliver these oracular sentence , 
 and the whole prediction seem strangely in unison with her own 
 impression of the character whose fate be thus shadow out . 

 " be you a fortune - teller , Mr. Chillingly ? " she ask , falteringly , and 
 after a pause . 

 " as good a one as any whose hand you could cross with a shilling . " 

 " will you tell I my fortune ? " 

 " no ; I never tell the fortune of lady , because your sex be credulous , 
 and a lady might believe what I tell she . and when we believe such and 
 such be to be our fate , we be too apt to work out our life into the 
 verification of the belief . if Lady Macbeth have disbelieve in the 
 witch , she would never have persuade she lord to murder Duncan . " 

 " but can you not predict I a more cheerful fortune than that tragical 
 illustration of yours seem to threaten ? " 

 " the future be never cheerful to those who look on the dark side of 
 the question . Mr. Gray be too good a poet for people to read nowadays , 
 otherwise I should refer you to his line in the ' Ode to Eton 
 College , ' - 


  " ' see how all around we wait 
  the minister of human fate , 
  and black Misfortune 's baleful train . ' 


 " meanwhile it be something to enjoy the present . we be young ; we 
 be listen to music ; there be no cloud over the summer star ; our 
 conscience be clear ; our heart untrouble : why look forward in search 
 of happiness ? shall we ever be happy than we be at this moment ? " 

 here Mr. Travers come up . " we be go to supper in a few minute , " 
 say he ; " and before we lose sight of each other , Mr. Chillingly , I wish 
 to impress on you the moral fact that one good turn deserve another . I 
 have yield to your wish , and now you must yield to mine . come and stay 
 a few day with I , and see your benevolent intention carry out . " 

 Kenelm pause . now that he be discover , why should he not pass a few 
 day among his equal ? reality or sham might be study with squire 
 no less than with farmer ; besides , he have take a liking to Travers . 
 that graceful ci - devant Wildair , with the slight form and the delicate 
 face , be unlike rural squire in general . Kenelm pause , and then say 
 frankly , - 

 " I accept your invitation . would the middle of next week suit you ? " 

 " the soon the well . why not to - morrow ? " 

 " to - morrow I be pre - engaged to an excursion with Mr. Bowles . that may 
 occupy two or three day , and meanwhile I must write home for other 
 garment than those in which I be a sham . " 

 " come any day you like . " 

 " agree . " 

 " agree ; and , hark ! the supper - bell . " 

 " supper , " say Kenelm , offer his arm to Miss Travers , - ' supper be a 
 word truly interesting , truly poetical . it associate itself with the 
 entertainment of the ancient , with the Augustan age , with Horace and 
 Maecenas ; with the only elegant but too fleeting period of the modern 
 world ; with the noble and wit of Paris , when Paris have wit and 
 noble ; with Moliere and the warm - hearted Duke who be say to have be 
 the original of Moliere 's misanthrope ; with Madame de Sevigne and the 
 Racine whom that inimitable letter - writer deny to be a poet ; with 
 Swift and Bolingbroke ; with Johnson , Goldsmith , and Garrick . epoch be 
 signalize by their eating . I honour he who revive the Golden Age of 
 supper . " so say , his face brighten . 



 CHAPTER VI . 


 KENELM CHILLINGLY , ESQ . , to SIR PETER CHILLINGLY , BART . , ETC . 

 MY DEAR FATHER , - I be alive and unmarried . Providence have watch over 
 I in these respect ; but I have have narrow escape . Hitherto I have not 
 acquire much worldly wisdom in my travel . it be true that I have be 
 pay two shilling as a day labourer , and , in fact , have fairly earn 
 at least six shilling more ; but against that additional claim I 
 generously set off , as an equivalent , my board and lodge . on the other 
 hand , I have spend forty - five pound out of the fifty which I devote 
 to the purchase of experience . but I hope you will be a gainer by 
 that investment . send an order to Mr. William Somers , basket - maker , 
 Graveleigh , - - -shire , for the hamper and game - basket you require , 
 and I undertake to say that you will save twenty per cent on that 
 article   all expense of carriage deduct   and do a good action into 
 the bargain . you know , from long habit , what a good action be worth 
 well than I do . I dare say you will be more pleased to learn than I 
 be to record the fact that I have be again decoy into the society of 
 lady and gentleman , and have accept an invitation to pass a few day 
 at Neesdale Park with Mr. Travers , - christen Leopold , who call you 
 " his old friend , " - a term which I take for grant belong to that class 
 of poetic exaggeration in which the " dear " and " darling " of conjugal 
 intercourse may be categorize . have for that visit no suitable 
 garment in my knapsack , kindly tell Jenkes to forward I a portmanteau 
 full of those which I habitually wear as Kenelm Chillingly , direct 
 to I at " Neesdale Park , near Beaverston . " let I find it there on 
 Wednesday . 

 I leave this place to - morrow morning in company with a friend of the 
 name of Bowles : no relation to the reverend gentleman of that name who 
 hold the doctrine that a poet should bore we to death with fiddle - faddle 
 minutia of natural object in preference to that study of the 
 insignificant creature man , in his relation to his specie , to which 
 Mr. Pope limit the range of his inferior muse ; and who , practise as 
 he preach , write some very nice verse , to which the Lake school and 
 its successor be largely indebted . my Mr. Bowles have exercise his 
 faculty upon man , and have a powerful inborn gift in that line which 
 only require cultivation to render he a match for any one . his more 
 masculine nature be at present much obscure by that pass cloud 
 which , in conventional language , be call " a hopeless attachment . " but 
 I trust , in the course of our excursion , which be to be take on foot , 
 that this vapour may consolidate by motion , as some old - fashioned 
 astronomer hold that the nebula do consolidate into a matter - of - fact 
 world . be it Rochefoucauld who say that a man be never more likely to 
 form a hopeful attachment for one than when his heart be soften by a 
 hopeless attachment to another ? may it be long , my dear father , before 
 you condole with I on the first or congratulate I on the second . 

  your affectionate son , 

  KENELM . 

 direct to I at Mr. Travers 's . kind love to my mother . 


 the answer to this letter be here subjoin as the most convenient place 
 for its insertion , though of course it be not receive till some day 
 after the date of my next chapter . 


 SIR PETER CHILLINGLY , BART . , TO KENELM CHILLINGLY , ESQ . 

 MY DEAR boy , - with this I despatch the portmanteau you require to the 
 address that you give . I remember well Leopold Travers when he be in 
 the Guards , - a very handsome and a very wild young fellow . but he 
 have much more sense than people give he credit for , and frequent 
 intellectual society ; at least I meet he very often at my friend 
 Campion 's , whose house be then the favourite rendezvous of 
 distinguished person . he have very win manner , and one could not 
 help take an interest in he . I be very glad when I hear he have 
 marry and reform . here I beg to observe that a man who contract a 
 taste for low company may indeed often marry , but he seldom reform when 
 he do so . and , on the whole , I should be much pleased to hear that the 
 experience which have cost you forty - five pound have convince you that 
 you might be well employ than earn two , or even six shilling as 
 a day - labourer . 

 I have not give your love to your mother , as you request . in fact , 
 you have place I in a very false position towards that other author of 
 your eccentric being . I could only guard you from the inquisition of the 
 police and the notoriety of descriptive hand - bill by allow my lady 
 to suppose that you have go abroad with the Duke of Clairville and his 
 family . it be easy to tell a fib , but it be very difficult to untell 
 it . however , as soon as you have make up your mind to resume your normal 
 position among lady and gentleman , I should be greatly oblige if 
 you would apprise I . I do not   wish to keep a fib on my conscience a 
 day long than may be necessary to prevent the necessity of tell 
 another . 

 from what you say of Mr. Bowles 's study of Man , and his inborn talent 
 for that scientific investigation , I suppose that he be a professed 
 Metaphysician , and I should be glad of his candid opinion upon the 
 Primary Basis of Morals , a subject upon which I have for three year 
 meditate the consideration of a critical paper . but have lately read 
 a controversy thereon between two eminent philosopher , in which each 
 accuse the other of not understand he , I have resolve for the 
 present to leave the Basis in its unsettled condition . 

 you rather alarm I when you say you have have a narrow escape from 
 marriage . should you , in order to increase the experience you set out 
 to acquire , decide on try the effect of a Mrs. Chillingly upon your 
 nervous system , it would be well to let I know a little beforehand , so 
 that I might prepare your mother 's mind for that event . such household 
 trifle be within her special province ; and she would be much put out 
 if a Mrs. Chillingly drop on her unawares . 

 this subject , however , be too serious to admit of a jest even between 
 two person who understand , so well as you and I do , the secret cipher 
 by which each other 's outward style of jest be to be gravely interpret 
 into the irony which say one thing and mean another . my dear boy , you 
 be very young ; you be wander about in a very strange manner , and 
 may , no doubt , meet with many a pretty face by the way , with which you 
 may fancy that you fall in love . you can not think I a barbarous , tyrant 
 if I ask you to promise I , on your honour , that you will not propose 
 to any young lady before you come first to I and submit the case to my 
 examination and approval . you know I too well to suppose that I should 
 unreasonably withhold my consent if convince that your happiness be 
 at stake . but while what a young man may fancy to be love be often a 
 trivial incident in his life , marriage be the great event in it ; 
 if on one side it may involve his happiness , on the other side it 
 may insure his misery . Dearest , good , and oddest of son , give I the 
 promise I ask , and you will free my breast from a terribly anxious 
 thought which now sit on it like a nightmare . 

 your recommendation of a basket - maker come opportunely . all such 
 matter go through the bailiff 's hand , and it be but the other day 
 that Green be complain of the high price of the man he employ for 
 hamper and game - basket . Green shall write to your protege . 

 keep I informed of your proceeding as much as your anomalous character 
 will permit ; so that nothing may diminish my confidence that the man who 
 have the honour to be christen Kenelm will not disgrace his name , but 
 acquire the distinction deny to a Peter . 

 your affectionate father . 



 CHAPTER VII . 

 villager lie abe on Sundays later than on workday , and no shutter be 
 unclosed in a window of the rural street through which Kenelm Chillingly 
 and Tom Bowles go , side by side , in the still soft air of the Sabbath 
 morn . side by side they go on , cross the pastoral glebe - land , 
 where the kine still drowsily recline under the bowery shade of 
 glint chestnut leaf ; and diving thence into a narrow lane or 
 by - road , wind deep between lofty bank all tangle with convolvulus 
 and wild - rose and honeysuckle . 

 they walk in silence , for Kenelm , after one or two vain attempt at 
 conversation , have the tact to discover that his companion be in no mood 
 for talk ; and be himself one of those creature whose mind glide 
 easily into the dreamy monologue of revery , he be not displeased to 
 muse on undisturbed , drink quietly into his heart the subdue joy of 
 the summer morn , with the freshness of its sparkling dew , the wayward 
 carol of its early bird , the serene quietude of its limpid breezy 
 air . only when they come to fresh turning in the road that lead towards 
 the town to which they be bind , Tom Bowles step before his 
 companion , indicate the way by a monosyllable or a gesture . thus they 
 journey for hour , till the sun attain power , and a little wayside 
 inn near a hamlet invite Kenelm to the thought of rest and food . 

 " Tom , " say he then , rouse from his revery , " what do you say to 
 breakfast ? " 

 Answered Tom sullenly , " I be not hungry ; but as you like . " 

 " thank you , then we will stop here a while . I find it difficult to 
 believe that you be not hungry , for you be very strong , and there be 
 two thing which generally accompany great physical strength : the one be 
 a keen appetite ; the other be - though you may not suppose it , and it be 
 not commonly know - a melancholic temperament . " 

 " eh ! - a what ? " 

 " a tendency to melancholy . of course you have hear of Hercules : you 
 know the saying ' as strong as Hercules ' ? " 

 " yes , of course . " 

 " well , I be first lead to the connection between strength , appetite , and 
 melancholy , by read in an old author name Plutarch that Hercules 
 be among the most notable instance of melancholy temperament which the 
 author be enable to quote . that must have be the traditional notion 
 of the Herculean constitution ; and as for appetite , the appetite of 
 Hercules be a standard joke of the comic writer . when I read that 
 observation it set I think , be myself melancholic and have 
 an exceedingly good appetite . sure enough , when I begin to collect 
 evidence , I find that the strong man with whom I make acquaintance , 
 include prize - fighter and irish drayman , be disposed to look upon 
 life more on the shady than the sunny side of the way ; in short , they 
 be melancholic . but the kindness of Providence allow they to enjoy 
 their meal , as you and I be about to do . " in the utterance of this 
 extraordinary crotchet Kenelm have halt his step ; but now stride 
 briskly forward he enter the little inn , and after a glance at its 
 larder , order the whole content to be bring out and place within a 
 honeysuckle arbour which he spy in the angle of a bowling - green at the 
 rear of the house . 

 in addition to the ordinary condiment of loaf and butter and egg and 
 milk and tea , the board soon groan beneath the weight of pigeon - pie , 
 cold rib of beef , and shoulder of mutton , remain of a feast which the 
 member of a monthly rustic club have hold there the day before . Tom eat 
 little at first ; but example be contagious , and gradually he vie with 
 his companion in the diminution of the solid viand before he . then he 
 call for brandy . 

 " no , " say Kenelm . " no , Tom ; you have promise I friendship , and that 
 be not compatible with brandy . Brandy be the bad enemy a man like 
 you can have ; and would make you quarrel even with I . if you want a 
 stimulus I allow you a pipe . I do not   smoke myself , as a rule , but there 
 have be time in my life when I require soothe , and then I have 
 feel that a whiff of tobacco still and soften one like the kiss of a 
 little child . bring this gentleman a pipe . " 

 Tom grunt , but take to the pipe kindly , and in a few minute , during 
 which Kenelm leave he in silence , a lower furrow between his brow 
 smooth itself away . 

 gradually he feel the sweetening influence of the day and the place , of 
 the merry sunbeam at play amid the leaf of the arbour , of the frank 
 perfume of the honeysuckle , of the warble of the bird before they sink 
 into the taciturn repose of a summer noon . 

 it be with a reluctant sigh that he rise at last , when Kenelm say , " we 
 have yet far to go : we must push on . " 

 the landlady , indeed , have already give they a hint that she and 
 the family want to go to church , and to shut up the house in their 
 absence . Kenelm draw out his purse , but Tom do the same with a return 
 of cloud on his brow , and Kenelm see that he would be mortally offend 
 if suffer to be treat as an inferior ; so each pay his due share , 
 and the two man resume their wandering . this time it be along a 
 by - path amid field , which be a short cut than the lane they have 
 previously follow , to the main road to Luscombe . they walk 
 slowly till they come to a rustic foot - bridge which span a gloomy 
 trout - stream , not noisy , but with a low , sweet murmur , doubtless the 
 same stream beside which , many mile away , Kenelm have converse with the 
 minstrel . just as they come to this bridge there float to their ear 
 the distant sound of the hamlet church - bell . 

 " now let we sit here a while and listen , " say Kenelm , seat himself 
 on the baluster of the bridge . " I see that you bring away your pipe 
 from the inn , and provide yourself with tobacco : refill the pipe and 
 listen . " 

 Tom half smile and obey . 

 " o friend , " say Kenelm , earnestly , and after a long pause of thought , 
 " do you not feel what a blessed thing it be in this mortal life to be 
 ever and anon remind that you have a soul ? " 

 Tom , startle , withdraw the pipe from his lip , and mutter , - 

 " eh ! " 

 Kenelm continue , - 

 " you and I , Tom , be not so good as we ought to be : of that there be no 
 doubt ; and good people would say justly that we should now be within 
 yon church itself rather than listen to its bell . grant , my friend , 
 grant ; but still it be something to hear that bell , and to feel by the 
 train of thought which begin in our innocent childhood , when we say 
 our prayer at the knee of a mother , that we be lift beyond this 
 visible nature , beyond these field and wood and water , in which , fair 
 though they be , you and I miss something ; in which neither you nor I be 
 as happy as the kine in the field , as the bird on the bough , as the 
 fish in the water : lift to a consciousness of a sense vouchsafe to 
 you and to I , not vouchsafe to the kine , to the bird , and the fish , - a 
 sense to comprehend that Nature have a God , and Man have a life hereafter . 
 the bell say that to you and to I . be that bell a thousand time 
 more musical it could not say that to beast , bird , and fish . do you 
 understand I , Tom ? " 

 Tom remain silent for a minute , and then reply , " I never think of 
 it before ; but , as you put it , I understand . " 

 " nature never give to a live thing capacity not practically mean 
 for its benefit and use . if Nature give to we capacity to believe 
 that we have a creator whom we never see , of whom we have no direct 
 proof , who be kind and good and tender beyond all that we know of kind 
 and good and tender on earth , it be because the endowment of capacity 
 to conceive such a being must be for our benefit and use : it would not 
 be for our benefit and use if it be a lie . again , if Nature have give 
 to we a capacity to receive the notion that we live again , no matter 
 whether some of we refuse so to believe , and argue against it , - why , the 
 very capacity to receive the idea   for unless we receive it we could 
 not argue against it   prove that it be for our benefit and use ; and if 
 there be no such life hereafter , we should be govern and influence , 
 arrange our mode of life , and mature our civilization , by obedience 
 to a lie , which Nature falsify herself in give we the capacity to 
 believe . you still understand I ? " 

 " yes ; it bother I a little , for you see I be not a parson 's man ; but I 
 do understand . " 

 " then , my friend , study to apply , - for it require constant 
 study , - study to apply that which you understand to your own case . you 
 be something more than Tom Bowles , the smith and doctor of horse ; 
 something more than the magnificent animal who rag for his mate and 
 fight every rival : the bull do that . you be a soul endow with the 
 capacity to receive the idea of a creator so divinely wise and great 
 and good that , though act by the agency of general law , he can 
 accommodate they to all individual case , so that - take into account 
 the life hereafter , which he grant to you the capacity to believe - all 
 that trouble you now will be prove to you wise and great and good 
 either in this life or the other . Lay that truth to your heart , friend , 
 now - before the bell stop ring ; recall it every time you hear the 
 church - bell ring again . and oh , Tom , you have such a noble nature ! - " 

 " I - I ! do not   jeer I , - do not . " 

 " such a noble nature ; for you can love so passionately , you can war so 
 fiercely , and yet , when convince that your love would be misery to 
 she you love , can resign it ; and yet , when beat in your war , can so 
 forgive your victor that you be walk in this solitude with he as a 
 friend , know that you have but to drop a foot behind he in order to 
 take his life in an unguarded moment ; and rather than take his life , you 
 would defend it against an army . do you think I be so dull as not to see 
 all that ? and be not all that a noble nature ? " 

 Tom Bowles cover his face with his hand , and his broad breast heave . 

 " well , then , to that noble nature I now trust . I myself have do little 
 good in life . I may never do much ; but let I think that I have not 
 cross your life in vain for you and for those whom your life can 
 colour for good or for bad . as you be strong , be gentle ; as you 
 can love one , be kind to all ; as you have so much that be grand as 
 Man , - that is , the high of God 's work on earth , - let all your act 
 attach your manhood to the idea of he , to whom the voice of the bell 
 appeal . ah ! the bell be hush ; but not your heart , Tom , - that speak 
 still . " 

 Tom be weep like a child . 



 CHAPTER VIII . 

 now when our two traveller resume their journey , the relationship 
 between they have undergo a change ; nay , you might have say that their 
 character be also change . for Tom find himself pour out his 
 turbulent heart to Kenelm , confide to this philosophical scoffer at 
 love all the passionate humanity of love , - its hope , its anguish , its 
 jealousy , its wrath , - the all that link the gentle of emotion to 
 tragedy and terror . and Kenelm , listen tenderly , with softened eye , 
 utter not one cynic word , - nay , not one playful jest . he , feel that 
 the gravity of all he hear be too solemn for mockery , too deep even 
 for comfort . true love of this sort be a thing he have never know , 
 never wish to know , never think he could know , but he sympathize 
 in it not the less . strange , indeed , how much we do sympathize , on 
 the stage , for instance , or in a book , with passion that have never 
 agitate ourselves ! have Kenelm jest or reason or preach , Tom would 
 have shrink at once into dreary silence ; but Kenelm say nothing , save 
 now and then , as he rest his arm , brother - like , on the strong man 's 
 shoulder , he murmur , " poor fellow ! " so , then , when Tom have finish 
 his confession , he feel wondrously relieve and comfort . he have 
 cleanse his bosom of the perilous stuff that weigh upon the heart . 

 be this good result effect by Kenelm 's artful diplomacy , or by that 
 insight into human passion vouchsafe unconsciously to himself , by 
 gleam or in flash , to this strange man who survey the object and 
 pursuit of his fellow with a yearning desire to share they , murmur 
 to himself , " I can not , I do not stand in this world ; like a ghost I 
 glide beside it , and look on " ? 

 thus the two man continue their way slowly , amid soft pasture and 
 yellowing cornfield , out at length into the dusty thoroughfare of 
 the main road . that gain , their talk insensibly change its tone : it 
 become more commonplace ; and Kenelm permit himself the license of 
 those crotchet by which he extract a sort of quaint pleasantry out of 
 commonplace itself ; so that from time to time Tom be startle into the 
 mirth of laughter . this big fellow have one very agreeable gift , which 
 be only grant , I think , to man of genuine character and affectionate 
 disposition , - a spontaneous and sweet laugh , manly and frank , but not 
 boisterous , as you might have suppose it would be . but that sort of 
 laugh have not before come from his lip , since the day on which his love 
 for Jessie Wiles have make he at war with himself and the world . 

 the sun be set when from the brow of a hill they behold the spire 
 of luscombe , imbed amid the level meadow that stretch below , 
 water by the same stream that have wind along their more rural 
 pathway , but which now expand into stately width , and need , to span 
 it , a mighty bridge fit for the convenience of civilized traffic . the 
 town seem near , but it be full two mile off by road . 

 " there be a short cut across the field beyond that stile , which lead 
 straight to my uncle 's house , " say Tom ; " and I dare say , sir , that you 
 will be glad to escape the dirty suburb by which the road pass before 
 we get into the town . " 

 " a good thought , Tom . it be very odd that fine town always be 
 approach by dirty suburb ; a covert symbolical satire , perhaps , on the 
 way to success in fine town . avarice or ambition go through very mean 
 little street before they gain the place which they jostle the crowd to 
 win , - in the Townhall or on ' Change . happy the man who , like you , Tom , 
 find that there be a short and a clean and a pleasanter way to goal 
 or to rest - place than that through the dirty suburb ! " 

 they meet but few passenger on their path through the field , - a 
 respectable , staid , elderly couple , who have the air of a Dissenting 
 minister and his wife ; a girl of fourteen lead a little boy seven 
 year young by the hand ; a pair of lover , evidently lover at 
 least to the eye of Tom Bowles ; for , on regard they as they pass 
 unheede he , he wince , and his face change . even after they have 
 pass , Kenelm see on the face that pain linger there : the lip be 
 tightly compressed , and their corner gloomily draw down . 

 just at this moment a dog rush towards they with a short quick 
 bark , - a pomeranian dog with pointed nose and prick ear . it hush 
 its bark as it near Kenelm , sniff his trouser , and wag its tail . 

 " by the sacred Nine , " cry Kenelm , " thou art the dog with the tin tray ! 
 where be thy master ? " 

 the dog seem to understand the question , for it turn its head 
 significantly ; and Kenelm see , seat under a lime - tree , at a good 
 distance from the path , a man , with book in hand , evidently employ in 
 sketching . 

 " come this way , " he say to Tom : " I recognize an acquaintance . you 
 will like he . " Tom desire no new acquaintance at that moment , but he 
 follow Kenelm submissively . 



 CHAPTER IX . 

 " you see we be fat to meet again , " say Kenelm , stretch himself 
 at his ease beside the Wandering Minstrel , and motion Tom to do the 
 same . " but you seem to add the accomplishment of draw to that of 
 verse - making ! you sketch from what you call Nature ? " 

 " from what I call Nature ! yes , sometimes . " 

 " and do you not find in drawing , as in verse - making , the truth that I 
 have before seek to din into your reluctant ear ; namely , that nature 
 have no voice except that which man breathe into she out of his mind ? 
 I would lay a wager that the sketch you be now take be rather an 
 attempt to make she embody some thought of your own , than to present she 
 outline as they appear to any other observer . permit I to judge for 
 myself . " and he bend over the sketch - book . it be often difficult for 
 one who be not himself an artist nor a connoisseur to judge whether the 
 pencil jotting in an impromptu sketch be by the hand of a profess 
 master or a mere amateur . Kenelm be neither artist nor connoisseur , but 
 the mere pencil - work seem to he much what might be expect from any 
 man with an accurate eye who have take a certain number of lesson from 
 a good drawing - master . it be enough for he , however , that it furnish 
 an illustration of his own theory . " I be right , " he cry triumphantly . 
 " from this height there be a beautiful view , as it present itself to 
 I ; a beautiful view of the town , its meadow , its river , harmonize by 
 the sunset ; for sunset , like gilding , unite conflict colour , and 
 soften they in unite . but I see nothing of that view in your sketch . 
 what I do see be to I mysterious . " 

 " the view you suggest , " say the minstrel , " be no doubt very fine , but 
 it be for a Turner or a Claude to treat it . my grasp be not wide enough 
 for such a landscape . " 

 " I see indeed in your sketch but one figure , a child . " 

 " Hist ! there she stand . Hist ! while I put in this last touch . " 

 Kenelm strain his sight , and see far off a solitary little girl , who 
 be toss something in the air   he could not distinguish what , and 
 catch it as it fall . she seem stand on the very verge of the 
 upland , back by rose - cloud gather round the set sun ; below lay 
 in confused outline the great town . in the sketch those outline seem 
 infinitely more confused , be only indicate by a few bold stroke ; 
 but the figure and face of the child be distinct and lovely . there 
 be an ineffable sentiment in her solitude ; there be a depth of quiet 
 enjoyment in her mirthful play , and in her upturned eye . 

 " but at that distance , " ask Kenelm , when the wanderer have finish his 
 last touch , and , after contemplate it , silently close his book , and 
 turn round with a genial smile , " but at that distance , how can you 
 distinguish the girl 's face ? how can you discover that the dim object 
 she have just throw up and recaught be a ball make of flower ? do you 
 know the child ? " 

 " I never see she before this evening ; but as I be seat here she be 
 stray around I alone , weave into chain some wild - flower which 
 she have gather by the hedgerow yonder , next the high road ; and as she 
 string they she be chant to herself some pretty nursery rhyme . 
 you can well understand that when I hear she thus chant I become 
 interested , and as she come near I I speak to she , and we soon make 
 friend . she tell I she be an orphan , and bring up by a very old man 
 distantly related to she , who have be in some small trade and now live 
 in a crowded lane in the heart of the town . he be very kind to she , and 
 be confine himself to the house by age or ailment he send she out to 
 play in the field on summer sunday . she have no companion of her own 
 age . she say she do not like the other little girl in the lane ; and 
 the only little girl she like at school have a grander station in life , 
 and be not allow to play with she , and so she come out to play alone ; 
 and as long as the sun shin and the flower bloom , she say she never 
 want other society . " 

 " Tom , do you hear that ? as you will be reside in Luscombe , find out 
 this strange little girl , and be kind to she , Tom , for my sake . " 

 Tom put his large hand upon Kenelm 's , make no other answer ; but he 
 look hard at the minstrel , recognize the genial charm of his voice 
 and face , and slide along the grass nearer to he . 

 the minstrel continue : " while the child be talk to I I 
 mechanically take the flower - chain from her hand , and not think 
 what I be about , gather they up into a ball . suddenly she see what 
 I have do , and instead of scold I for spoil her pretty chain , 
 which I richly deserve , be delighted to find I have twist they into a 
 new plaything . she run off with the ball , toss it about till , excited 
 with her own joy , she get to the brow of the hill , and I begin my 
 sketch . " 

 " be that charming face you have draw like her ? " 

 " no ; only in part . I be think of another face while I sketch , but 
 it be not like that either ; in fact , it be one of those patchwork which 
 we call ' fancy head , ' and I mean it to be another version of a thought 
 that I have just put into rhyme when the child come across I . " 

 " may we hear the rhyme ? " 

 " I fear that if it do not bore yourself it would bore your friend . " 

 " I be sure not . Tom , do you sing ? " 

 " well , I have sing , " say Tom , hang his head sheepishly , " and I 
 should like to hear this gentleman . " 

 " but I do not know these verse , just make , well enough to sing they ; it 
 be enough if I can recall they well enough to recite . " here the minstrel 
 pause a minute or so as if for recollection , and then , in the sweet 
 clear tone and the rare purity of enunciation which characterize his 
 utterance , whether in recital or song , give to the follow verses a 
 touching and a varied expression which no one could discover in merely 
 read they . 


  the FLOWER - GIRL by the crossing . 

  " by the muddy crossing in the crowd street 
  stand a little maid with her basket full of posy , 
  proffer all who pass her choice of knit sweet , 
  Tempting age with heart' - ease , court Youth with rose . 

  " age disdain the heart's - ease , 
  Love reject the rose ; 
  London life be busy , - 
  who can stop for posy ? 

  " one man be too grave , another be too gay ; 
  this man have his hothouse , that man not a penny : 
  floweret too be common in the month of May , 
  and the thing most common least attract the many . 

  " Ill , on London crossing , 
  fare the sale of posy ; 
  Age disdain the heart's - ease , 
  Youth reject the rose . " 


 when the verse - maker have do , he do not pause for approbation , nor 
 look modestly down , as do most people who recite their own verse , but 
 unaffectedly think much more of his art than his audience , hurry on 
 somewhat disconsolately , - 

 " I see with great grief that I be well at sketch than rhyme . can 
 you "   appeal to Kenelm   " even comprehend what I mean by the verse ? " 

 KENELM . - " do you comprehend , Tom ? " 

 TOM   in a whisper . - " no . " 

 KENELM . - " I presume that by his flower - girl our friend mean to 
 represent not only poetry , but a poetry like his own , which be not at 
 all the sort of poetry now in fashion . I , however , expand his meaning , 
 and by his flower - girl I understand any image of natural truth or beauty 
 for which , when we be live the artificial life of crowded street , we 
 be too busy to give a penny . " 

 " take it as you please , " say the minstrel , smile and sigh at the 
 same time ; " but I have not express in word that which I do mean half 
 so well as I have express it in my sketch - book . " 

 " ah ! and how ? " ask Kenelm . 

 " the image of my thought in the sketch , be it poetry or whatever you 
 prefer to call it , do not stand forlorn in the crowded street : the 
 child stand on the brow of the green hill , with the city stretch in 
 confused fragment below , and , thoughtless of penny and passer - by , 
 she be play with the flower she have gather ; but in play cast 
 they heavenward , and follow they with heavenward eye . " 

 " good ! " mutter Kenelm , " good ! " and then , after a long pause , he add , 
 in a still low mutter , " pardon I that remark of mine the other day 
 about a beefsteak . but own that I be right : what you call a sketch from 
 Nature be but a sketch of your own thought . " 



 CHAPTER X. 

 the child with the flower - ball have vanish from the brow of the hill ; 
 sink down amid the street below , the rose - clouds have fade from the 
 horizon ; and night be close round , as the three man enter the 
 thick of the town . Tom press Kenelm to accompany he to his uncle 's , 
 promise he a hearty welcome and bed and board , but Kenelm decline . 
 he entertain a strong persuasion that it would be well for the 
 desire effect on Tom 's mind that he should be leave alone with his 
 relation that night , but propose that they should spend the next day 
 together , and agree to call at the veterinary surgeon 's in the morning . 

 when Tom quit they at his uncle 's door , Kenelm say to the minstrel , 
 " I suppose you be go to some inn ; may I accompany you ? we can sup 
 together , and I should like to hear you talk upon poetry and Nature . " 

 " you flatter I much ; but I have friend in the town , with whom I lodge ,

 to - morrow . "

 " but one word more , " say Kenelm . " your host or hostess , if resident

 passionate heart like Tom 's , now ache with a horrible void ,

 the minstrel change colour : he even start . " Sir , be you a wizard 
 that you say that to I ? " 

 " I be not a wizard , but I guess from your question that you have a 
 little child of your own . so much the well : the child may keep you out 
 of much mischief . remember the little child . good evening . " 

 Kenelm cross the threshold of the Golden Lamb , engage his room , make 
 his ablution , order , and , with his usual zest , partook of his evening 
 meal ; and then , feel the pressure of that melancholic temperament 
 which he so strangely associate with Herculean constitution , rouse 
 himself up , and , seek a distraction from thought , saunter forth 
 into the gaslit street . 

 it be a large handsome town , - handsomer than Tor - Hadham , on account of 
 its site in a valley surround by woode hill , and water by the fair 
 stream whose winding we have see as a brook , - handsomer , also , because 
 it boast a fair cathedral , well clear to the sight , and surround 
 by venerable old house , the residence of the clergy or of the quiet 
 lay gentry with mediaeval taste . the main street be throng with 
 passenger , - some soberly return home from the evening service ; some , 
 the young , linger in pleasant promenade with their sweetheart or 
 family , or arm in arm with each other , and have the air of 
 bachelor or maiden unattached . through this street Kenelm pass with 
 inattentive eye . a turn to the right take he towards the cathedral and 
 its surrounding . there all be solitary . the solitude please he , 
 and he linger long , gaze on the noble church lift its spire and 
 turret into the deep blue starry air . 

 musingly , then , he stray on , enter a labyrinth of gloomy lane , in 
 which , though the shop be closed , many a door stand open , with man 
 of the work class loll against the threshold , idly smoke their 
 pipe , or woman seat on the doorstep gossip , while noisy child 
 be play or quarrel in the kennel . the whole do not present the 
 indolent side of an English Sabbath in the pleasantest and rosy point 
 of view . somewhat quicken his step , he enter a broad street , 
 attract to it involuntarily by a bright light in the centre . on 
 near the light he find that it shine forth from a gin - palace , of

 that of the cathedral . " the new civilization versus the old , " murmur

 be not that of Nature 's giving . " be you kind to - night ? " ask a husky 
 voice . 

 " Kind ! " say Kenelm , with mournful tone and soften eye , " kind ! alas , 
 my poor sister mortal ! if pity be kindness , who can see you and not be 
 kind ? " 

 the girl release his arm , and he walk on . she stand some moment 
 gaze after he till out of sight , then she draw her hand suddenly 
 across her eye , and retrace her step , be , in her turn , catch hold 
 of by a rougher hand than her , as she pass the gin - palace . she shake 
 off the grasp with a passionate scorn , and go straight home . home ! be 
 that the right word ? poor sister mortal ! 



 CHAPTER XI . 

 and now Kenelm find himself at the extremity of the town , and on the

 stretching towards the river . 

 all around in the thoroughfare be silent and desert . by this time

 " do not   be so tiresome , Fritz , " say the handsome lady , half petulantly ,

 its be know that he be addict in his holiday to taste and habit

 while he be thus think , the lady have draw her chair close to the

 Kenelm sigh . be it from envy , from pity , from fear ? I know not ; but

 " Love 's quarrel . "

 follow line : - 


  LOVE 's QUARREL . 

  " stand by the river , gaze on the river , 
  see it pave with starbeam , - heaven be at our foot ; 
  now the wave be trouble , now the rush quiver ; 
  Vanished be the starlight : it be a deceit . 

  " come a little cloudlet ' twixt ourselves and heaven , 
  and from all the river fade the silver track ; 
  put thine arm around I , whisper low , ' forgive ! ' 
  see how on the river starlight settle back . " 


 when he have finish , still with face turn aside , the lady do not ,

 there come to his ear , - he know not from whence , from whom , -

 CHAPTER XII . 

 the next morning betime Kenelm visit Tom at his uncle 's home . a 
 comfortable and respectable home it be , like that of an owner in easy 
 circumstance . the veterinary surgeon himself be intelligent , and 
 apparently educate beyond the range of his calling ; a childless 
 widower , between sixty and seventy , live with a sister , an old maid . 
 they be evidently much attach to Tom , and delight by the hope of 
 keep he with they . Tom himself look rather sad , but not sullen , 
 and his face brighten wonderfully at first sight of Kenelm . that 
 oddity make himself as pleasant and as much like other people as he 
 could in converse with the old widower and the old maid , and take 
 leave , engage Tom to be at his inn at half past twelve , and spend the 
 day with he and the minstrel . he then return to the Golden Lamb , and 
 wait there for his first visitant ; the minstrel . that votary of the 
 muse arrive punctually at twelve o'clock . his countenance be less 
 cheerful and sunny than usual . Kenelm make no allusion to the scene 
 he have witness , nor do his visitor seem to suspect that Kenelm have 
 witness it or be the utterer of that warning voice . 

 KENELM . - " I have ask my friend Tom Bowles to come a little later , 
 because I wish you to be of use to he , and , in order to be so , I 
 should suggest how . " 

 THE MINSTREL . - " pray do . " 

 KENELM . - " you know that I be not a poet , and I do not have much 
 reverence for verse - making merely as a craft . " 

 THE MINSTREL . - " neither have I. " 

 KENELM . - " but I have a great reverence for poetry as a priesthood . I 
 feel that reverence for you when you sketch and talk priesthood 
 last evening , and place in my heart - I hope forever while it beat - the 
 image of the child on the sunlit hill , high above the abode of man , 
 toss her flower - ball heavenward and with heavenward eye . " 

 the singer 's cheek colour high , and his lip quiver : he be very 
 sensitive to praise ; most singer be . 

 Kenelm resume , " I have be educate in the realistic school , and with 
 realism I be discontent , because in realism as a school there be no 
 truth . it contain but a bit of truth , and that the cold and hard 
 bit of it , and he who utter a bit of truth and suppresse the rest of 
 it tell a lie . " 

 the MINSTREL   slyly . - " do the critic who say to I , ' sing of 
 beefsteak , because the appetite for food be a real want of daily life , 
 and do not   sing of art and glory and love , because in daily life a man 
 may do without such idea , ' - tell a lie ? " 

 KENELM . - " thank you for that rebuke . I submit to it . no doubt I do tell 
 a lie , - that be , if I be quite in earnest in my recommendation , and if 
 not in earnest , why - " 

 THE MINSTREL . - " you belie yourself . " 

 KENELM . - " very likely . I set out on my travel to escape from sham , and 
 begin to discover that I be a sham par excellence . but I suddenly 
 come across you , as a boy dull by his syntax and his vulgar fraction 
 suddenly come across a pleasant poem or a picture - book , and feel his 
 wit brighten up . I owe you much : you have do I a world of good . " 

 " I can not guess how . " 

 " possibly not , but you have show I how the realism of Nature herself 
 take colour and life and soul when see on the ideal or poetic side 
 of it . it be not exactly the word that you say or sing that do I the 
 good , but they awaken within I new train of thought , which I seek 
 to follow out . the good teacher be the one who suggest rather than 
 dogmatize , and inspire his listener with the wish to teach himself . 
 therefore , o singer ! whatever be the worth in critical eye of your 
 song , I be glad to remember that you would like to go through the world 
 always sing . " 

 " pardon I : you forget that I add , ' if life be always young , and the 
 season be always summer . ' " 

 " I do not forget . but if youth and summer fade for you , you leave youth 
 and summer behind you as you pass along , - behind in heart which mere 
 realism would make always old , and count their slothful beat under 
 the gray of a sky without sun or star ; wherefore I pray you to consider 
 how magnificent a mission the singer 's be , - to harmonize your life with 
 your song , and toss your flower , as your child do , heavenward , with 
 heavenward eye . think only of this when you talk with my sorrowing 
 friend , and you will do he good , as you have do I , without be 
 able to guess how a seeker after the beautiful , such as you , carry we 
 along with he on his way ; so that we , too , look out for beauty , and see 
 it in the wild - flower to which we have be blind before . " 

 here Tom enter the little sand parlour where this dialogue have be 
 hold , and the three man sally forth , take the short cut from the 
 town into the field and woodland . 



 CHAPTER XIII . 

 whether or not his spirit be raise by Kenelm 's praise and 
 exhortation , the minstrel that day talk with a charm that spellbound 
 Tom , and Kenelm be satisfied with brief remark on his side tend to 
 draw out the principal performer . 

 the talk be draw from outward thing , from natural object , - object 
 that interest child , and man who , like Tom Bowles , have be 
 accustom to view surrounding more with the heart 's eye than the 
 mind 's eye . this rover about the country know much of the habit of 
 bird and beast and insect , and tell anecdote of they with a mixture 
 of humour and pathos , which fascinate Tom 's attention , make he laugh 
 heartily , and sometimes bring tear into his big blue eye . 

 they din at an inn by the wayside , and the dinner be mirthful ; then 
 they wend their way slowly back . by the decline daylight their talk 
 grow somewhat graver , and Kenelm take more part in it . Tom listen 
 mute , - still fascinated . at length , as the town come in sight , they 
 agree to halt a while , in a bosky nook soft with moss and sweet with 
 wild thyme . 

 there , as they lie stretch at their ease , the bird hymn vesper 
 song amid the bough above , or dropping , noiseless and fearless , for 
 their evening food on the sward around they , the wanderer say to 
 Kenelm , " you tell I that you be no poet , yet I be sure you have a 
 poet 's perception : you must have write poetry ? " 

 " not I ; as I before tell you , only school verse in dead language : but 
 I find in my knapsack this morning a copy of some rhyme , make by a 
 fellow - collegian , which I put into my pocket meaning to read they to 
 you both . they be not verse like your , which evidently burst from you 
 spontaneously , and be not imitate from any other poet . these verse 
 be write by a Scotchman , and smack of imitation from the old ballad 
 style . there be little to admire in the word themselves , but there 
 be something in the idea which strike I as original , and impress I 
 sufficiently to keep a copy , and somehow or other it get into the leaf 
 of one of the two book I carry with I from home . " 

 " what be those book ? book of poetry both , I will venture to wager - " 

 " Wrong ! both metaphysical , and dry as a bone . Tom , light your pipe , and 
 you , sir , lean more at ease on your elbow ; I should warn you that the 
 ballad be long . patience ! " 

 " attention ! " say the minstrel . 

 " Fire ! " add Tom . 

 Kenelm begin to read , - and he read well . 


  LORD RONALD 'S BRIDE . 

  PART I. 

  " why gather the crowd in the market - place 
  Ere the star have yet leave the sky ? " 
  " for a holiday show and an act of grace , - 
  at the sunrise a witch shall die . " 

  " what deed have she do to deserve that doom ? 
  have she blight the stand corn , 
  or rifle for philter a dead man 's tomb , 
  or rid mother of babe new - bear ? " 

  " her pact with the fiend be not thus reveal , 
  she teach sinner the Word to hear ; 
  the hungry she feed , and the sick she heal , 
  and be hold as a Saint last year . 

  " but a holy man , who at Rome have be , 
  have discover , by book and bell , 
  that the marvel she work be through arts unclean , 
  and the lie of the Prince of Hell . 

  " and our Mother the Church , for the dame be rich , 
  and her husband be Lord of Clyde , 
  would fain have be mild to this saint - like witch 
  if her sin she have not deny . 

  " but hush , and come near to see the sight , 
  Sheriff , halberd , and torchman , - look ! 
  that be the witch stand mute in her garb of white , 
  by the priest with his bell and book . " 

  so the witch be consume on the sacred pyre , 
  and the priest grow in power and pride , 
  and the witch leave a son to succeed his sire 
  in the hall and the land of Clyde . 

  and the infant wax comely and strong and brave , 
  but his manhood have scarce begin , 
  when his vessel be launch on the northern wave 
  to the shore which be near the sun . 

  PART II . 

  Lord Ronald have come to his hall in Clyde 
  with a bride of some unknown race ; 
  compare with the man who would kiss that bride 
  Wallace wight be a coward base . 

  her eye have the glare of the mountain - cat 
  when it spring on the hunter 's spear , 
  at the head of the board when that lady sate 
  hungry man could not eat for fear . 

  and the tone of her voice have that deadly growl 
  of the bloodhound that scent its prey ; 
  no storm be so dark as that lady 's scowl 
  under tress of wintry gray . 

  " Lord Ronald ! man marry for love or gold , 
  mickle rich must have be thy bride ! " 
  " Man 's heart may be buy , woman 's hand be sell , 
  on the bank of our northern Clyde . 

  " my bride be , in sooth , mickle rich to I 
  though she bring not a groat in dower , 
  for her face , couldst thou see it as I do see , 
  be the fair in hall or bower ! " 

  Quoth the bishop one day to our lord the king , 
  " Satan reign on the Clyde alway , 
  and the taint in the blood of the witch doth cling 
  to the child that she bring to day . 

  " Lord Ronald hath come from the Paynim land 
  with a bride that appal the sight ; 
  like his dam she hath mole on her dread right hand , 
  and she turn to a snake at night . 

  " it be plain that a Scot who can blindly dote 
  on the face of an eastern ghoul , 
  and a ghoul who be worth not a silver groat , 
  be a Scot who have lose his soul . 

  " it be wise to have do with this demon tree 
  which have teem with such caukere fruit ; 
  add the soil where it stand to my holy See , 
  and consign to the flame its root . " 

  " holy man ! " quoth King James , and he laugh , " we know 
  that thy tongue never wag in vain , 
  but the Church cist be full , and the king 's be low , 
  and the Clyde be a fair domain . 

  " yet a knight that be bewitch by a laidly fere 
  need not much to dissolve the spell ; 
  we will summon the bride and the bridegroom here 
  be at hand with thy book and bell . " 

  PART III . 

  Lord Ronald stand up in King James 's court , 
  and his dame by his dauntless side ; 
  the baron who come in the hope of sport 
  shake with fright when they see the bride . 

  the bishop , though arm with his bell and book , 
  grow as white as if turn to stone ; 
  it be only our king who could face that look , 
  but he speak with a trembling tone . 

  " Lord Ronald , the knight of thy race and mine 
  should have mate in their own degree ; 
  what parentage , say , hath that bride of thine 
  who hath come from the far countree ? 

  " and what be she dowry in gold or land , 
  or what be the charm , I pray , 
  that a comely young gallant should woo the hand 
  of the ladye we see to - day ? " 

  and the lord would have laugh , but that awful dame 
  strike they dumb with her thunder - frown : 
  " Saucy king , do I utter my father 's name , 
  Thou wouldst kneel as his liegeman down . 

  " though I bring to Lord Ronald nor land nor gold , 
  nor the bloom of a fading cheek ; 
  yet , be I a widow , both young and old 
  would my hand and my dowry seek . 

  " for the wish that he covet the most below , 
  and would hide from the saint above , 
  which he dare not to pray for in weal or woe , 
  be the dowry I bring my love . 

  " let every man look in his heart and see 
  what the wish he most lust to win , 
  and then let he fasten his eye on I 
  while he think of his darling sin . " 

  and every man - bishop , and lord , and king 
  Thought of what he most wish to win , 
  and , fix his eye on that grewsome thing , 
  he behold his own darling sin . 

  no long a ghoul in that face he see ; 
  it be fair as a boy 's first love : 
  the voice that have curdle his vein with awe 
  be the coo of the woodland dove . 

  each heart be on flame for the peerless dame 
  at the price of the husband 's life ; 
  bright claymore flash out , and loud voice shout , 
  " in thy widow shall be my wife . " 

  then darkness fall over the palace hall , 
  more dark and more dark it fall , 
  and a death - groan boom hoarse underneath the pall , 
  and be drown amid roar and yell . 

  when light through the lattice - pane stole once more , 
  it be gray as a wintry dawn , 
  and the bishop lie cold on the regal floor , 
  with a stain on his robe of lawn . 

  Lord Ronald be stand beside the dead , 
  in the scabbard he plunge his sword , 
  and with visage as wan as the corpse , he say , 
  " Lo ! my ladye hath keep her word . 

  " now I leave she to other to woo and win , 
  for no long I find she fair ; 
  could I look on the face of my darling sin , 
  I should see but a dead man 's there . 

  " and the dowry she bring I be here return , 
  for the wish of my heart have die , 
  it be quench in the blood of the priest who burn 
  my sweet mother , the Saint of Clyde . " 

  Lord Ronald stride over the stony floor , 
  not a hand be outstretche to stay ; 
  Lord Ronald have pass through the gap door , 
  not an eye ever trace the way . 

  and the ladye , leave widow , be prize above 
  all the maiden in hall and bower , 
  many barter their life for that ladye 's love , 
  and their soul for that ladye 's dower . 

  God grant that the wish which I dare not pray 
  be not that which I lust to win , 
  and that ever I look with my first dismay 
  on the face of my darling sin ! 


 as he cease , Kenelm 's eye fall on Tom 's face upturned to his own , with 
 open lip , an intent stare , and paled cheek , and a look of that high 
 sort of terror which belong to awe . the man , then recover himself , 
 try to speak , and attempt a sickly smile , but neither would do . 
 he rise abruptly and walk away , creep under the shadow of a dark 
 beech - tree , and stand there lean against the trunk . 

 " what say you to the ballad ? " ask Kenelm of the singer . 

 " it be not without power , " answer he . 

 " ay , of a certain kind . " 

 the minstrel look hard at Kenelm , and drop his eye , with a 
 heighten glow on his cheek . 

 " the Scotch be a thoughtful race . the Scot who write this thing may 
 have think of a day when he see beauty in the face of a darling sin ; 
 but , if so , it be evident that his sight recover from that glamoury . 
 shall we walk on ? Come , Tom . " 

 the minstrel leave they at the entrance of the town , say , " I regret 
 that I can not see more of either of you , as I quit Luscombe at daybreak . 
 here , by the by , I forgot to give it before , be the address you want . " 

 KENELM . - " of the little child . I be glad you remember she . " 

 the minstrel again look hard at Kenelm , this time without drop his 
 eye . Kenelm 's expression of face be so simply quiet that it might be 
 almost call vacant . 

 Kenelm and Tom continue to walk on towards the veterinary surgeon 's 
 house , for some minute silently . then Tom say in a whisper , " do you 
 not mean those rhyme to hit I here - here ? " and he strike his breast . 

 " the rhyme be write long before I see you , Tom ; but it be well if 
 their meaning strike we all . of you , my friend , I have no fear now . be 
 you not already a change man ? " 

 " I feel as if I be go through a change , " answer Tom , in slow , 
 dreary accent . " in hear you and that gentleman talk so much of 
 thing that I never think of , I feel something in I , - you will laugh 
 when I tell you , - something like a bird . " 

 " like a bird , - good ! - a bird have wing . " 

 " just so . " 

 " and you feel wing that you be unconscious of before , flutter and 
 beat themselves as against the wire of a cage . you be true to 
 your instinct then , my dear fellow - man , - instinct of space and Heaven . 
 Courage ! - the cage - door will open soon . and now , practically speak , 
 I give you this advice in parting : you have a quick and sensitive mind 
 which you have allow that strong body of yours to incarcerate and 
 suppress . give that mind fair play . attend to the business of your 
 call diligently ; the craving for regular work be the healthful 
 appetite of mind : but in your spare hour cultivate the new idea which 
 your talk with man who have be accustom to cultivate the mind more 
 than the body have sow within you . belong to a book - club , and interest 
 yourself in book . a wise man have say , ' book widen the present by 
 add to it the past and the future . ' seek the company of educate man 
 and educate woman too ; and when you be angry with another , reason 
 with he : do not   knock he down ; and do not   be knock down yourself by an 
 enemy much strong than yourself , - drink . do all this , and when I see 
 you again you will be - " 

 " stop , sir , - you will see I again ? " 

 " yes , if we both live , I promise it . " 

 " when ? " 

 " you see , Tom , we have both of we something in our old self which we 
 must work off . you will work off your something by repose , and I must 
 work off mine , if I can , by move about . so I be on my travel . May 
 we both have new self well than the old self , when we again shake 
 hand ! for your part try your good , dear Tom , and Heaven prosper you . " 

 " and Heaven bless you ! " cry Tom , fervently , with tear roll 
 unheeded from his bold blue eye . 



 CHAPTER XIV . 

 THOUGH Kenelm leave Luscombe on Tuesday morning , he do not appear at 
 Neesdale Park till the Wednesday , a little before the dressing - bell for 
 dinner . his adventure in the interim be not worth repeat . he have 
 hop he might fall in again with the minstrel , but he do not . 

 his portmanteau have arrive , and he heave a sigh as he case himself in 
 a gentleman 's evening dress . " alas ! I have soon get back again into my 
 own skin . " 

 there be several other guest in the house , though not a 
 large party , - they have be ask with an eye to the approach 
 election , - consist of squire and clergy from remoter part of the 
 county . chief among the guest in rank and importance , and render by 
 the occasion the central object of interest , be George Belvoir . 

 Kenelm bear his part in this society with a resignation that partook of 
 repentance . 

 the first day he speak very little , and be consider a very dull young 
 man by the lady he take in to dinner . Mr. Travers in vain try to draw 
 he out . he have anticipate much amusement from the eccentricity of 
 his guest , who have talk volubly enough in the fernery , and be sadly 
 disappoint . " I feel , " he whisper to Mrs. Campion , " like poor Lord 
 Pomfret , who , charm with Punch 's lively conversation , buy he , and 
 be greatly surprised that , when he have once bring he home , Punch 
 would not talk . " 

 " but your Punch listen , " say Mrs. Campion , " and he observe . " 

 George Belvoir , on the other hand , be universally declare to be very 
 agreeable . though not naturally jovial , he force himself to appear 
 so , - laugh loud with the squire , and enter heartily with 
 their wife and daughter into such topic as county - ball and 
 croquet - party ; and when after dinner he have , Cato - like , ' warm his 
 virtue with wine , ' the virtue come out very lustily in praise of good 
 man , - namely , man of his own party , - and anathema on bad man , - namely , 
 man of the other party . 

 now and then he appeal to Kenelm , and Kenelm always return the same 
 answer , " there be much in what you say . " 

 the first evening close in the usual way in country house . there be 
 some lounge under moonlight on the terrace before the house ; then 
 there be some singing by young lady amateur , and a rubber of whist for 
 the elder ; then wine - and - water , hand - candlestick , a smoking - room for 
 those who smoke , and bed for those who do not . 

 in the course of the evening , Cecilia , partly in obedience to the duty 
 of hostess and partly from that compassion for shyness which kindly and 
 high - breed person entertain , have go a little out of her way to allure 
 Kenelm forth from the estranged solitude he have contrive to weave 
 around he . in vain for the daughter as for the father . he reply to 
 she with the quiet self - possession which should have convince she that 
 no man on earth be less entitled to indulgence for the gentlemanlike 
 infirmity of shyness , and no man less need the duty of any hostess 
 for the augmentation of his comfort , or rather for his diminished sense 
 of discomfort ; but his reply be in monosyllable , and make with the 
 air of a man who say in his heart , " if this creature would but leave I 
 alone ! " 

 Cecilia , for the first time in her life , be pique , and , strange to 
 say , begin to feel more interest about this indifferent strange than 
 about the popular , animate , pleasant George Belvoir , who she know by 
 womanly instinct be as much in love with she as he could be . 

 Cecilia Travers that night on retire to rest tell she maid , smilingly , 
 that she be too tired to have her hair do ; and yet , when the maid 
 be dismiss , she look at herself in the glass more gravely and more 
 discontentedly than she have ever look there before ; and , tired though 
 she be , stand at the window gaze into the moonlit night for a good 
 hour after the maid leave she . 



 CHAPTER XV . 

 KENELM CHILLINGLY have now be several day a guest at Neesdale Park . 
 he have recover speech ; the other guest have go , include George 
 Belvoir . Leopold Travers have take a great fancy to Kenelm . Leopold 
 be one of those man , not uncommon perhaps in England , who , with great 
 mental energy , have little book - knowledge , and when they come
