 in contact with a book - reader who be not a pedant feel a pleasant 
 excitement in his society , a source of interest in compare note with 
 he , a constant surprise in finding by what venerable authority the 
 deduction which their own mother - wit have draw from life be support , 
 or by what cogent argument derive from book those deduction be 
 contravene or upset . Leopold Travers have in he that sense of humour 
 which generally accompany a strong practical understanding   no man , 
 for instance , have more practical understanding than a Scot , and no man 
 have a keener susceptibility to humour , and not only enjoy Kenelm 's 
 odd way of express himself , but very often mistook Kenelm 's irony for 
 opinion speak in earnest . 

 since his early removal from the capital and his devotion to 
 agricultural pursuit , it be so seldom that Leopold Travers meet a man 
 by whose conversation his mind be divert to other subject than those 
 which be incidental to the commonplace routine of his life that he 
 find in Kenelm 's view of man and thing a source of novel amusement , 
 and a stirring appeal to such metaphysical creed of his own as have be 
 form unconsciously , and have long repose unexamined in the recess of 
 an intellect shrewd and strong , but more accustomed to dictate than to 
 argue . Kenelm , on his side , see much in his host to like and to admire ; 
 but , reverse their relative position in point of year , he converse 
 with Travers as with a mind young than his own . indeed , it be one 
 of his crotchety theory that each generation be in substance mentally 
 old than the generation precede it , especially in all that relate 
 to science ; and , as he would say , " the study of life be a science , and 
 not an art . " 

 but Cecilia , - what impression do she create upon the young visitor ? 
 be he alive to the charm of her rare beauty , to the grace of a mind 
 sufficiently store for commune with those who love to think and to 
 imagine , and yet sufficiently feminine and playful to seize the sportive 
 side of reality , and allow their proper place to the trifle which 
 make the sum of human thing ? an impression she do make , and that 
 impression be new to he and pleasing . Nay , sometimes in her presence 
 and sometimes when alone , he fall into abstract consultation with 
 himself , say , " Kenelm Chillingly , now that thou hast get back into 
 thy proper skin , dost thou not think that thou hadst well remain 
 there ? couldst thou not be content with thy lot as err descendant 
 of Adam , if thou couldst win for thy mate so faultless a descendant of 
 Eve as now flit before thee ? " but he could not abstract from himself 
 any satisfactory answer to the question he have address to himself . 

 once he say abruptly to Travers , as , on their return from their 
 ramble , they catch a glimpse of Cecilia 's light form bend over the 
 flower - bed on the lawn , " do you admire Virgil ? " 

 " to say truth I have not read Virgil since I be a boy ; and , between you 
 and I , I then think he rather monotonous . " 

 " perhaps because his verse be so smooth in its beauty ? " 

 " probably . when one be very young one 's taste be faulty ; and if a poet 
 be not faulty , we be apt to think he want vivacity and fire . " 

 " thank you for your lucid explanation , " answer Kenelm , add musingly 
 to himself , " I be afraid I should yawn very often if I be married to a 
 Miss Virgil . " 



 CHAPTER XVI . 

 the house of Mr. Travers contain a considerable collection of family 
 portrait , few of they well paint , but the squire be evidently proud 
 of such evidence of ancestry . they not only occupy a considerable 
 space on the wall of the reception room , but swarm into the 
 principal sleeping - chamber , and smile or frown on the beholder from 
 dark passage and remote lobby . one morning , Cecilia , on her way 
 to the china closet , find Kenelm gaze very intently upon a female 
 portrait consign to one of those obscure receptacle by which through 
 a back staircase he gain the only approach from the hall to his 
 chamber . 

 " I do not   pretend to be a good judge of painting , " say Kenelm , as 
 Cecilia pause beside he ; " but it strike I that this picture be very 
 much well than most of those to which place of honour be assign in 
 your collection . and the face itself be so lovely that it would add an 
 embellishment to the princely gallery . " 

 " yes , " say Cecilia , with a half - sigh . " the face be lovely , and the 
 portrait be consider one of Lely 's rare masterpiece . it use to 
 hang over the chimney - piece in the drawing - room . my father have it place 
 here many year ago . " 

 " perhaps because he discover it be not a family portrait ? " 

 " on the contrary , - because it grieve he to think it be a family 
 portrait . hush ! I hear his footstep : do not   speak of it to he ; do not   let 
 he see you look at it . the subject be very painful to he . " 

 here Cecilia vanish into the china closet and Kenelm turn off to his 
 own room . 

 what sin commit by the original in the time of Charles II . but only 
 discover in the reign of Victoria could have justify Leopold Travers 
 in remove the most pleasing portrait in the house from the honour 
 place it have occupy , and banish it to so obscure a recess ? Kenelm 
 say no more on the subject , and indeed an hour afterwards have dismiss 
 it from his thought . the next day he ride out with Travers and 
 Cecilia . their way pass through quiet shady lane without any purposed 
 direction , when suddenly , at the spot where three of those lane meet on 
 an angle of common ground , a lonely gray tower , in the midst of a wide 
 space of grass - land which look as if it have once be a park , with 
 huge bole of pollarded oak dot the space here and there , rise 
 before they . 

 " Cissy ! " cry Travers , angrily rein in his horse and stop short 
 in a political discussion which he have force upon Kenelm , " Cissy ! 
 how come this ? we have take the wrong turn ! no matter , I see there , " 
 point to the right , " the chimney - pot of old Mondell 's homestead . he 
 have not yet promise his vote to George Belvoir . I will go and have a talk 
 with he . turn back , you and Mr. Chillingly , - meet I at Terner 's Green , 
 and wait for I there till I come . I need not excuse myself to you , 
 chillingly . a vote be a vote . " so say , the Squire , whose ordinary 
 riding - horse be an old hunter , halt , turn , and , no gate be 
 visible , put the horse over a stiff fence and vanish in the direction 
 of old Mondell 's chimney - pot . Kenelm , scarcely hear his host 's 
 instruction to Cecilia and excuse to himself , remain still and 
 gaze on the old tower thus abruptly obtrude on his view . 

 though no learn antiquarian like his father , Kenelm have a strange 
 fascinating interest in all relic of the past ; and old gray tower , 
 where they be not church tower , be very rarely to be see in England . 
 all around the old gray tower speak with an unutterable mournfulness 
 of a past in ruin : you could see remain of some large Gothic building 
 once attach to it , rise here and there in fragment of deeply 
 buttress wall ; you could see in a dry ditch , between high ridge , 
 where there have be a fortified moat : nay , you could even see where 
 once have be the bailey hill from which a baron of old have dispense 
 justice . seldom indeed do the most acute of antiquarian discover 
 that remnant of Norman time on land still hold by the old of 
 anglo - Norman family . then , the wild nature of the demesne around ; 
 those range of sward , with those old giant oak - trunk , hollow within 
 and pollard at top , - all speak , in unison with the gray tower , of a 
 past as remote from the reign of Victoria as the pyramid be from the 
 sway of the Viceroy of Egypt . 

 " let we turn back , " say Miss Travers ; " my father would not like I to 
 stay here . " 

 " pardon I a moment . I wish my father be here ; he would stay till 
 sunset . but what be the history of that old tower ? a history it must 
 have . " 

 " every home have a history , even a peasant 's hut , " say Cecilia . " but do 
 pardon I if I ask you to comply with my father 's request . I at least 
 must turn back . " 

 thus command , Kenelm reluctantly withdraw his gaze from the ruin and 
 regain Cecilia , who be already some pace in return down the lane . 

 " I be far from a very inquisitive man by temperament , " say Kenelm , ' so 
 far as the affair of the living be concern . but I should not care to 
 open a book if I have no interest in the past . pray indulge my curiosity 
 to learn something about that old tower . it could not look more 
 melancholy and solitary if I have build it myself . " 

 " its most melancholy association be with a very recent past , " answer 
 Cecilia . " the tower , in remote time , form the keep of a castle 
 belong to the most ancient and once the most powerful family in these 
 part . the owner be baron who take active share in the Wars of the 
 Roses . the last of they side with Richard III . , and after the battle 
 of Bosworth the title be attaint , and the large portion of the land 
 be confiscate . loyalty to a Plantagenet be of course treason to 
 a Tudor . but the regeneration of the family rest with their direct 
 descendant , who have save from the general wreck of their fortune what 
 may be call a good squire 's estate , - about , perhaps , the same rental 
 as my father 's , but of much large acreage . these squire , however , 
 be more look up to in the county than the wealthy peer . they 
 be still by far the old family in the county ; and trace in their 
 pedigree alliance with the most illustrious house in english history . 
 in themselves too for many generation they be a high - spirit , 
 hospitable , popular race , live unostentatiously on their income , and 
 content with their rank of squire . the castle , ruin by time and 
 siege , they do not attempt to restore . they dwelt in a house near to 
 it , build about Elizabeth 's time , which you could not see , for it lie 
 in a hollow behind the tower , - a moderate - sized , picturesque , country 
 gentleman 's house . our family intermarry with they , - the portrait you 
 see be a daughter of their house , - and very proud be any squire in the 
 county of intermarriage with the Fletwodes . " 

 " Fletwode , - that be their name ? I have a vague recollection of have 
 hear the name connect with some disastrous - oh , but it can not   be the 
 same family : pray go on . " 

 " I fear it be the same family . but I will finish the story as I have 
 hear it . the property descend at last to one Bertram Fletwode , who , 
 unfortunately , obtain the reputation of be a very clever man of 
 business . there be some mining company in which , with other gentleman 
 in the county , he take great interest ; invest largely in share ; 
 become the head of the direction - " 

 " I see ; and be of course ruin . " 

 " no ; bad than that : he become very rich ; and , unhappily , become 
 desirous of be rich still . I have hear that there be a great 
 mania for speculation just about that time . he embark in these , and 
 prosper , till at last he be induce to invest a large share of the 
 fortune thus acquire in the partnership of a bank which enjoy a high 
 character . up to that time he have retain popularity and esteem in 
 the county ; but the squire who share in the adventure of the mining 
 company , and know little or nothing about other speculation in which 
 his name do not appear , profess to be shock at the idea of a 
 Fletwode of Fletwode be ostensibly join in partnership with a Jones 
 of Clapham in a London bank . " 

 " slow folk , those country squire , - behind the progress of the age . 
 well ? " 

 " I have hear that Bertram Fletwode be himself very reluctant to take 
 this step , but be persuade to do so by his son . this son , Alfred , be 
 say to have still great talent for business than the father , and 
 have be not only associate with but consult by he in all the later 
 speculation which have prove so fortunate . Mrs. Campion know Alfred 
 Fletwode very well . she describe he as handsome , with quick , eager 
 eye ; showy and impose in his talk ; immensely ambitious , more 
 ambitious than avaricious , - collect money less for its own sake than 
 for that which it could give , - rank and power . accord to she it be 
 the dear wish of his heart to claim the old barony , but not before 
 there could go with the barony a fortune adequate to the lustre of a 
 title so ancient , and equal to the wealth of modern peer with high 
 nominal rank . " 

 " a poor ambition at the good ; of the two I should prefer that of a poet 
 in a garret . but I be no judge . thank Heaven I have no ambition . 
 still , all ambition , all desire to rise , be interesting to he who be 
 ignominiously content if he do not fall . so the son have his way , 
 and Fletwode join company with Jones on the road to wealth and the 
 peerage ; meanwhile do the son marry ? if so , of course the daughter of 
 a duke or a millionnaire . tuft - hunting , or money - making , at the risk of 
 degradation and the workhouse . progress of the age ! " 

 " no , " reply Cecilia , smile at this outburst , but smile sadly , 
 " Fletwode do not marry the daughter of a duke or a millionnaire ; but 
 still his wife belong to a noble family , - very poor , but very proud . 
 perhaps he marry from motif of ambition , though not of gain . her 
 father be of much political influence that might perhaps assist his 
 claim to the barony . the mother , a woman of the world , enjoy a high 
 social position , and nearly related to a connection of ours , - Lady 
 Glenalvon . " 

 " Lady Glenalvon , the dearest of my lady friend ! you be connect with 
 she ? " 

 " yes ; Lord Glenalvon be my mother 's uncle . but I wish to finish my 
 story before my father join we . Alfred Fletwode do not marry till long 
 after the partnership in the bank . his father , at his desire , have buy 
 up the whole business , Mr. Jones have die . the bank be carry on 
 in the name of Fletwode and Son . but the father have become merely a 
 nominal or what I believe be call a ' sleep ' partner . he have long 
 cease to reside in the county . the old house be not grand enough for 
 he . he have purchase a palatial residence in one of the home county ; 
 live there in great splendour ; be a munificent patron of science 
 and art ; and in spite of his early addiction to business - like 
 speculation he appear to have be a singularly accomplish , 
 high - breed gentleman . some year before his son 's marriage , Mr. Fletwode 
 have be afflict with partial paralysis , and his medical attendant 
 enjoin rigid abstention from business . from that time he never 
 interfere with his son 's management of the bank . he have an only 
 daughter , much young than Alfred . Lord Eagleton , my mother 's brother , 
 be engage to be married to she . the wedding - day be fix , - when the 
 world be startle by the news that the great firm of Fletwode and Son 
 have stop payment ; be that the right phrase ? " 

 " I believe so . " 

 " a great many people be ruin in that failure . the public indignation 
 be very great . of course all the Fletwode property go to the 
 creditor . old Mr. Fletwode be legally acquit of all other offence 
 than that of overconfidence in his son . Alfred be convict of 
 fraud , - of forgery . I do not , of course , know the particular , they be 
 very complicated . he be sentence to a long term of servitude , but 
 die the day he be condemn ; apparently by poison , which he have long 
 secrete about his person . now you can understand why my father , who 
 be almost gratuitously sensitive on the point of honour , remove into a 
 dark corner the portrait of Arabella Fletwode , - his own ancestress , but 
 also the ancestress of a convict felon : you can understand why the 
 whole subject be so painful to he . his wife 's brother be to have 
 marry the felon 's sister ; and though , of course , that marriage be 
 tacitly break off by the terrible disgrace that have befall the 
 Fletwodes , yet I do not   think my poor uncle ever recover the blow to 
 his hope . he go abroad , and die in Madeira of a slow decline . " 

 " and the felon 's sister , do she die too ? " 

 " no ; not that I know of . Mrs. Campion say that she see in a newspaper 
 the announcement of old Mr. Fletwode 's death , and a paragraph to the 
 effect that after that event Miss Fletwode have sail from Liverpool to 
 New York . " 

 " Alfred Fletwode 's wife go back , of course , to her family ? " 

 " alas ! no , - poor thing ! she have not be many month marry when the 
 bank break ; and among his friend her wretched husband appear to have 
 forge the name of the trustee to her marriage settlement , and sell 
 out the sum which would otherwise have serve she as a competence . 
 her father , too , be a great sufferer by the bankruptcy , have by 
 his son - in - law 's advice place a considerable portion of his moderate 
 fortune in Alfred 's hand for investment , all of which be involve in 
 the general wreck . I be afraid he be a very hard - hearted man : at all 
 event his poor daughter never return to he . she die , I think , even 
 before the death of Bertram Fletwode . the whole story be very dismal . " 

 " dismal indeed , but pregnant with salutary warning to those who live 
 in an age of progress . here you see a family of fair fortune , living 
 hospitably , beloved , revere , more look up to by their neighbour than 
 the wealthy noble ; no family not proud to boast alliance with it . 
 all at once , in the tranquil record of this happy race , appear that 
 darling of the age , that hero of progress , - a clever man of business . he 
 be content to live as his father ! he be content with such trifle 
 as competence , respect , and love ! much too clever for that . the age be 
 money - making , - go with the age ! he go with the age . bear a gentleman 
 only , he exalt himself into a trader . but at least he , it seem , if 
 greedy , be not dishonest . he be bear a gentleman , but his son be 
 bear a trader . the son be a still cleverer man of business ; the son be 
 consult and trust . aha ! he too go with the age ; to greed he 
 link ambition . the trader 's son wish to return - what ? to the rank of 
 gentleman ? - gentleman ! nonsense ! everybody be a gentleman nowadays , - to 
 the title of Lord . how end it all ! could I sit but for twelve hour in 
 the innermost heart of that Alfred Fletwode ; could I see how , step by 
 step from his childhood , the dishonest son be avariciously lead on by 
 the honest father to depart from the old vestigia of Fletwodes of 
 Fletwode , - scorn the Enough to covet the More , gain the More to 
 sigh , ' it be not the Enough , ' - I think I might show that the age live 
 in a house of glass , and have well not for its own sake throw stone on 
 the felon ! " 

 " ah , but , Mr. Chillingly , surely this be a very rare exception in the 
 general - " 

 " Rare ! " interrupt Kenelm , who be excited to a warmth of passion which 
 would have startle his most intimate friend,-if indeed an intimate 
 friend have ever be vouchsafe to he , - " rare ! nay , how common - I do not 
 say to the extent of forgery and fraud , but to the extent of degradation 
 and ruin - be the greed of a little More to those who have the Enough ! be 
 the discontent with competence , respect , and love , when catch sight 
 of a money - bag ! how many well - descend county family , curse with 
 an heir who be call a clever man of business , have vanish from the 
 soil ! a company start , the clever man join it one bright day . Pouf ! 
 the old estate and the old name be powder . ascend higher . take noble 
 whose ancestral title ought to be to english ear like the sound of 
 clarion , awaken the most slothful to the scorn of money - bag and 
 the passion for renown . Lo ! in that mocking dance of death call 
 the Progress of the Age , one who do not find Enough in a sovereign 's 
 revenue , and seek the little More as a gambler on the turf by the 
 advice of blackleg ! lo ! another , with land wide than his great 
 ancestor ever possess , must still go in for the little More , add 
 acre to acre , heap debt upon debt ! Lo ! a third , whose name , bear by 
 his ancestor , be once the terror of England 's foe , - the landlord of 
 a hotel ! a fourth , - but why go on through the list ? another and another 
 still succeed ; each on the Road to Ruin , each in the Age of Progress . 
 ah , Miss Travers ! in the old time it be through the Temple of Honour 
 that one pass to the Temple of Fortune . in this wise age the process 
 be reverse . but here come your father . " 

 " a thousand pardon ! " say Leopold Travers . " that numskull Mondell keep 
 I so long with his old - fashioned Tory doubt whether liberal politic 
 be favourable to agricultural prospect . but as he owe a round sum to 
 a Whig lawyer I have to talk with his wife , a prudent woman ; convince 
 she that his own agricultural prospect be safe on the Whig side of 
 the question ; and , after kiss his baby and shake his hand , book 
 his vote for George Belvoir , - a plumper . " 

 " I suppose , " say Kenelm to himself , and with that candour which 
 characterize he whenever he talk to himself , " that Travers have take 
 the right road to the Temple , not of Honour , but of honour , in every 
 country , ancient or modern , which have adopt the system of popular 
 suffrage . " 



 CHAPTER XVII . 

 the next day Mrs. Campion and Cecilia be seat under the veranda . 
 they be both ostensibly employ on two several piece of embroidery , 
 one intend for a screen , the other for a sofa - cushion ; but the mind of 
 neither be on her work . 

 MRS . CAMPION . - " have Mr. Chillingly say when he mean to take leave ? " 

 CECILIA . - " not to I . how much my dear father enjoy his conversation ! " 

 MRS . CAMPION . - " cynicism and mockery be not so much the fashion among 
 young man in your father 's day as I suppose they be now , and therefore 
 they seem new to Mr. Travers . to I they be not new , because I see 
 more of the old than the young when I live in London , and cynicism and 
 mockery be more natural to man who be leave the world than to those 
 who be enter it . " 

 CECILIA . - " Dear Mrs. Campion , how bitter you be , and how unjust ! 
 you take much too literally the jesting way in which Mr. Chillingly 
 express himself . there can be no cynicism in one who go out of his 
 way to make other happy . " 

 MRS . CAMPION . - " you mean in the whim of make an ill - assorted marriage 
 between a pretty village flirt and a sickly cripple , and settle a 
 couple of peasant in a business for which they be wholly unfit . " 

 CECILIA . - " Jessie Wiles be not a flirt , and I be convinced that she will 
 make Will Somers a very good wife , and that the shop will be a great 
 success . " 

 MRS . CAMPION . - " we shall see . still , if Mr. Chillingly 's talk belie his 
 action , he may be a good man , but he be a very affected one . " 

 CECILIA . - " have I not hear you say that there be person so natural 
 that they seem affect to those who do not understand they ? " 

 Mrs. Campion raise her eye to Cecilia 's face , drop they again over 
 her work , and say , in grave undertone , - " take care , Cecilia . " 

 " take care of what ? " 

 " my dear child , forgive I ; but I do not like the warmth with which 
 you defend Mr. Chillingly . " 

 " would not my father defend he still more warmly if he have hear you ? " 

 " man judge of man in their relation to man . I be a woman , and judge of 
 man in their relation to woman . I should tremble for the happiness of 
 any woman who join her fate with that of Kenelm Chillingly . " 

 " my dear friend , I do not understand you to - day . " 

 " Nay ; I do not mean to be so solemn , my love . after all , it be nothing 
 to we whom Mr. Chillingly may or may not marry . he be but a pass 
 visitor , and , once go , the chance be that we may not see he again 
 for year . " 

 thus speak , Mrs. Campion again raise her eye from her work , 
 steal a sidelong glance at Cecilia ; and her mother - like heart sink 
 within she , on notice how suddenly pale the girl have become , and how 
 her lip quiver . Mrs. Campion have enough knowledge of life to feel 
 aware that she have commit a grievous blunder . in that early stage 
 of virgin affection , when a girl be unconscious of more than a certain 
 vague interest in one man which distinguish he from other in her 
 thought , - if she hear he unjustly disparage , if some warn against 
 he be imply , if the probability that he will never be more to she 
 than a pass acquaintance be forcibly obtrude on she , - suddenly that 
 vague interest , which might otherwise have fade away with many another 
 girlish fancy , become arrest , consolidated ; the quick pang it 
 occasion make she involuntarily , and for the first time , question 
 herself , and ask , " do I love ? " but when a girl of a nature so delicate 
 as that of Cecilia Travers can ask herself the question , " do I love ? " 
 her very modesty , her very shrink from acknowledge that any power 
 over her thought for weal or for woe can be acquire by a man , except 
 through the sanction of that love which only become divine in her eye 
 when it be earnest and pure and self - devote , make she prematurely 
 disposed to answer " yes . " and when a girl of such a nature in her own 
 heart answer " yes " to such a question , even if she deceive herself at 
 the moment , she begin to cherish the deceit till the belief in her love 
 become a reality . she have adopt a religion , false or true , and she 
 would despise herself if she could be easily convert . 

 Mrs. Campion have so contrive that she have force that question upon 
 Cecilia , and she fear , by the girl 's change of countenance , that the 
 girl 's heart have answer " yes . " 



 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 while the conversation just narrated take place , Kenelm have walk forth 
 to pay a visit to Will Somers . all obstacle to Will 's marriage be now 
 clear away ; the transfer of lease for the shop have be sign , and 
 the bann be to be publish for the first time on the follow 
 Sunday . we need not say that Will be very happy . Kenelm then pay a 
 visit to Mrs. Bowles , with whom he stay an hour . on reentere the 
 Park , he see Travers , walk slowly , with downcast eye and his hand 
 clasp behind he   his habit when in thought . he do not observe 
 Kenelm 's approach till within a few foot of he , and he then greet his 
 guest in listless accent , unlike his usual cheerful tone . 

 " I have be visit the man you have make so happy , " say Kenelm . 

 " who can that be ? " 

 " will Somers . do you make so many people happy that your reminiscence of 
 they be lose in their number ? " 

 Travers smile faintly , and shake his head . 

 Kenelm go on . " I have also see Mrs. Bowles , and you will be please 
 to hear that Tom be satisfied with his change of abode : there be no 
 chance of his return to Graveleigh ; and Mrs. Bowles take very kindly 
 to my suggestion that the little property you wish for should be sell 
 to you , and , in that case , she would remove to Luscombe to be near her 
 son . " 

 " I thank you much for your thought of I , " say Travers , " and the affair 
 shall be see to at once , though the purchase be no long important to 
 I . I ought to have tell you three day ago , but it slip my memory , 
 that a neighbouring squire , a young fellow just come into his property , 
 have offer to exchange a capital farm , much near to my residence , 
 for the land I hold in Graveleigh , include Saunderson 's farm and the 
 cottage : they be quite at the outskirt of my estate , but run into 
 his , and the exchange will be advantageous to both . still I be glad that 
 the neighbourhood should be thoroughly rid of a brute like Tom Bowles . " 

 " you would not call he brute if you know he ; but I be sorry to hear 
 that will Somers will be under another landlord . " 

 " it do not matter , since his tenure be secure for fourteen year . " 

 " what sort of man be the new landlord ? " 

 " I do not   know much of he . he be in the army till his father die , 
 and have only just make his appearance in the county . he have , however , 
 already earn the character of be too fond of the other sex : it be 
 well that pretty Jessie be to be safely marry . " 

 Travers then relapse into a moody silence from which Kenelm find it 
 difficult to rouse he . at length the latter say kindly , - 

 " my dear Mr. Travers , do not think I take a liberty if I venture to 
 guess that something have happen this morning which trouble or vexe 
 you . when that be the case , it be often a relief to say what it be , even 
 to a confidant so unable to advise or to comfort as myself . " 

 " you be a good fellow , chillingly , and I know not , at least in these 
 part , a man to whom I would unburden myself more freely . I be put out , 
 I confess ; disappoint unreasonably , in a cherish wish , and , " he 
 add , with a slight laugh , " it always annoy I when I do not   have my 
 own way . " 

 " so it do I . " 

 " do not   you think that George Belvoir be a very fine young man ? " 

 " certainly . " 

 " I call he handsome ; he be steady , too , than most man of his 
 age , and of his command of money ; and yet he do not want spirit nor 
 knowledge of life . to every advantage of rank and fortune he add the 
 industry and the ambition which attain distinction in public life . " 

 " quite true . be he go to withdraw from the election after all ? " 

 " good heaven , no ! " 

 " then how do he not let you have your own way ? " 

 " it be not he , " say Travers , peevishly ; " it be Cecilia . do not   you 
 understand that George be precisely the husband I would choose for she ; 
 and this morning come a very well write manly letter from he , ask 
 my permission to pay his address to she . " 

 " but that be your own way so far . " 

 " yes , and here come the balk . of course I have to refer it to Cecilia , 
 and she positively decline , and have no reason to give ; do not deny 
 that George be good - look and sensible , that he be a man of whose 
 preference any girl might be proud ; but she choose to say she can not 
 love he , and when I ask why she can not love he , have no other answer 
 than that ' she can not say . ' it be too provoking . " 

 " it be provoke , " answer Kenelm ; " but then Love be the most 
 dunderheade of all the passion ; it never will listen to reason . the 
 very rudiment of logic be unknown to it . ' love have no wherefore , ' say 
 one of those latin poet who write love - verse call elegy , - a name 
 which we modern appropriate to funeral dirge . for my own part , I can not 
 understand how any one can be expect voluntarily to make up his mind 
 to go out of his mind . and if Miss Travers can not go out of her mind 
 because George Belvoir do , you could not argue she into do so if 
 you talk till doomsday . " 

 Travers smile in spite of himself , but he answer gravely , " certainly , 
 I would not wish Cissy to marry any man she dislike , but she do not 
 dislike George ; no girl could : and where that be the case , a girl so 
 sensible , so affectionate , so well bring up , be sure to love , after 
 marriage , a thoroughly kind and estimable man , especially when she have 
 no previous attachment , - which , of course , Cissy never have . in fact , 
 though I do not wish to force my daughter 's will , I be not yet dispose 
 to give up my own . do you understand ? " 

 " perfectly . " 

 " I be the more inclined to a marriage so desirable in every way , because 
 when Cissy come out in London , which she have not yet do , she be 
 sure to collect round her face and her presumptive inheritance all the 
 handsome fortune - hunter and title vaurien ; and if in love there 
 be no wherefore , how can I be sure that she may not fall in love with a 
 scamp ? " 

 " I think you may be sure of that , " say Kenelm . " Miss Travers have too 
 much mind . " 

 " yes , at present ; but do you not say that in love people go out of 
 their mind ? " 

 " true ! I forget that . " 

 " I be not then disposed to dismiss poor George 's offer with a decided 
 negative , and yet it would be unfair to mislead he by encouragement . in 
 fact , I will be hang if I know how to reply . " 

 " you think Miss Travers do not dislike George Belvoir , and if she see 
 more of he may like he well , and it would be good for she as well as 
 for he not to put an end to that , chance ? " 

 " exactly so . " 

 " why not then write : ' my dear George , - you have my good wish , but my 
 daughter do not seem disposed to marry at present . let I consider 
 your letter not write , and continue on the same term as we be 
 before . ' perhaps , as George know Virgil , you might find your own 
 schoolboy recollection of that poet useful here , and add , varium et 
 mutabile semper femina ; hackneyed , but true . " 

 " my dear Chillingly , your suggestion be capital . how the deuce at your 
 age have you contrive to know the world so well ? " 

 Kenelm answer in the pathetic tone so natural to his voice , " by be 
 only a looker - on ; alas ! " 

 Leopold Travers feel much relieved after he have write his reply 
 to George . he have not be quite so ingenuous in his revelation to 
 chillingly as he may have seem . conscious , like all proud and 
 fond father , of his daughter 's attraction , he be not without some 
 apprehension that Kenelm himself might entertain an ambition at variance 
 with that of George Belvoir : if so , he deem it well to put an end to 
 such ambition while yet in time : partly because his interest be already 
 pledge to George ; partly because , in rank and fortune , George be the 
 well match ; partly because George be of the same political party as 
 himself , - while Sir Peter , and probably Sir Peter 's heir , espouse the 
 opposite side ; and partly also because , with all his personal liking to 
 Kenelm , Leopold Travers , as a very sensible , practical man of the world , 
 be not sure that a baronet 's heir who tramp the country on foot in 
 the dress of a petty farmer , and indulge pugilistic propensity in 
 martial encounter with stalwart farrier , be likely to make a safe 
 husband and a comfortable son - in - law . Kenelm 's word , and still more his 
 manner , convince Travers that any apprehension of rivalry that he have 
 previously conceive be utterly groundless . 



 CHAPTER XIX . 

 the same evening , after dinner   during that lovely summer month they 
 din at Neesdale Park at an unfashionably early hour , Kenelm , in 
 company with Travers and Cecilia , ascend a gentle eminence at the back 
 of the garden , on which there be some picturesque ivy - grow ruin of 
 an ancient priory , and command the good view of a glorious sunset and 
 a subject landscape of vale and wood , rivulet and distant hill . 

 " be the delight in scenery , " say Kenelm , " really an acquire gift , 
 as some philosopher tell we ? be it true that young child and rude 
 savage do not feel it ; that the eye must be educate to comprehend its 
 charm , and that the eye can be only educate through the mind ? " 

 " I should think your philosopher be right , " say Travers . " when I be 
 a schoolboy , I think no scenery be like the flat of a cricket ground ; 
 when I hunt at Melton , I think that unpicturesque country more 
 beautiful than Devonshire . it be only of late year that I feel a 
 sensible pleasure in scenery for its own sake , apart from association 
 of custom or the us to which we apply they . " 

 " and what say you , Miss Travers ? " 

 " I scarcely know what to say , " answer Cecilia , musingly . " I can 
 remember no time in my childhood when I do not feel delight in that 
 which seem to I beautiful in scenery , but I suspect that I vaguely 
 distinguish one kind of beauty from another . a common field with 
 daisy and buttercup be beautiful to I then , and I doubt if I see 
 anything more beautiful in extensive landscape . " 

 " true , " say Kenelm : " it be not in early childhood that we carry the 
 sight into distance : as be the mind so be the eye ; in early childhood 
 the mind revel in the present , and the eye rejoice most in the thing 
 near to it . I do not   think in childhood that we - 

  " ' watch with wistful eye the set sun . ' " 

 " ah ! what a world of thought in that word ' wistful ' ! " murmur Cecilia , 
 as her gaze rivet itself on the western heaven , towards which Kenelm 
 have point as he speak , where the enlarging orb rest half its disk on 
 the rim of the horizon . 

 she have seat herself on a fragment of the ruin , back by the hollow 
 of a broken arch . the last ray of the sun linger on her young face , 
 and then lose themselves in the gloom of the arch behind . there be a 
 silence for some minute , during which the sun have sink . rosy cloud in 
 thin flake still float , momently wan : and the eve - star steal forth 
 steadfast , bright , and lonely , - nay , lonely not now ; that sentinel have 
 arouse a host . 

 say a voice , " no sign of rain yet , Squire . what will become of the 
 turnip ? " 

 " real life again ! who can escape it ? " mutter Kenelm , as his eye rest 
 on the burly figure of the squire 's bailiff . 

 " ha ! north , " say Travers , " what bring you here ? no bad news , I hope ? " 

 " indeed , yes , Squire . the Durham bull - " 

 " the Durham bull ! what of he ? you frighten I . " 

 " take bad . Colic . " 

 " excuse I , chillingly , " cry Travers ; " I must be off . a most valuable 
 animal , and no one I can trust to doctor he but myself . " 

 " that be true enough , " say the bailiff , admiringly . " there be not a 
 veterinary in the county like the squire . " 

 Travers be already go , and the pant bailiff have hard work to catch 
 he up . 

 Kenelm seat himself beside Cecilia on the ruin fragment . 

 " how I envy your father ! " say he . 

 " why just at this moment , - because he know how to doctor the bull ? " 
 say Cecilia , with a sweet low laugh . 

 " well , that be something to envy . it be a pleasure to relieve from pain 
 any of God 's creature , - even a Durham bull . " 

 " indeed , yes . I be justly rebuke . " 

 " on the contrary you be to be justly praise . your question suggest 
 to I an amiable sentiment in place of the selfish one which be 
 uppermost in my thought . I envy your father because he create for 
 himself so many object of interest ; because while he can appreciate the 
 mere sensuous enjoyment of a landscape and a sunset , he can find mental 
 excitement in turnip crop and bull . happy , Miss Travers , be the 
 Practical Man . " 

 " when my dear father be as young as you , Mr. Chillingly , I be sure that 
 he have no more interest in turnip and bull than you have . I do not 
 doubt that some day you will be as practical as he be in that respect . " 

 " do you think so - sincerely ? " 

 Cecilia make no answer . 

 Kenelm repeat the question . 

 " sincerely , then , I do not know whether you will take interest in 
 precisely the same thing that interest my father ; but there be other 
 thing than turnip and cattle which belong to what you call ' practical 
 life , ' and in these you will take interest , as you take in the fortune 
 of Will Somers and Jessie Wiles . " 

 " that be no practical interest . I get nothing by it . but even if that 
 interest be practical , - I mean productive , as cattle and turnip crop 
 be , - a succession of Somerses and Wileses be not to be hop for . 
 history never repeat itself . " 

 " may I answer you , though very humbly ? " 

 " Miss Travers , the wise man that ever exist never be wise enough 
 to know woman ; but I think most man ordinarily wise will agree in this , 
 that woman be by no mean a humble creature , and that when she say she 
 ' answer very humbly , ' she do not mean what she say . permit I to 
 entreat you to answer very loftily . " 

 Cecilia laugh and blush . the laugh be musical ; the blush be - what ? 
 let any man , seat beside a girl like Cecilia at starry twilight , find 
 the right epithet for that blush . I pass it by epithetless . but she 
 answer , firmly though sweetly , - 

 " be there not thing very practical , and affect the happiness , not 
 of one or two individual , but of innumerable thousand , in which a man 
 like Mr. Chillingly can not fail to feel interest , long before he be my 
 father 's age ? " 

 " forgive I : you do not answer ; you question . I imitate you , and ask 
 what be those thing as applicable to a man like Mr. Chillingly ? " 

 Cecilia gather herself up , as with the desire to express a great deal 
 in short substance , and then say , - 

 " in the expression of thought , literature ; in the conduct of action , 
 politic . " 

 Kenelm Chillingly star , dumfounde . I suppose the great enthusiast 
 for woman 's right could not assert more reverentially than he do the 
 cleverness of woman ; but among the thing which the cleverness of woman 
 do not achieve , he have always place " laconic . " " no woman , " he be 
 will not to say , " ever invent an axiom or a proverb . " 

 " Miss Travers , " he say at last , " before we proceed far , vouchsafe 
 to tell I if that very terse reply of yours be spontaneous and 
 original ; or whether you have not borrow it from some book which I 
 have not chance to read ? " 

 Cecilia ponder honestly , and then say , " I do not   think it be from any 
 book ; but I owe so many of my thought to Mrs. Campion , and she live so 
 much among clever man , that - " 

 " I see it all , and accept your definition , no matter whence it come . 
 you think I might become an author or a politician . do you ever read an 
 essay by a live author call ' Motive Power ' ? " 

 " no . " 

 " that essay be design to intimate that without motive power a man , 
 whatever his talent or his culture , do nothing practical . the 
 mainspring of motive power be Want and Ambition . they be absent 
 from my mechanism . by the accident of birth I do not require bread and 
 cheese ; by the accident of temperament and of philosophical culture 
 I care nothing about praise or blame . but without want of bread and 
 cheese , and with a most stolid indifference to praise and blame , do you 
 honestly think that a man will do anything practical in literature or 
 politic ? ask Mrs. Campion . " 

 " I will not ask she . be the sense of duty nothing ? " 

 " alas ! we interpret duty so variously . of mere duty , as we commonly 
 understand the word , I do not think I shall fail more than other man . 
 but for the fair development of all the good that be in we , do you 
 believe that we should adopt some line of conduct against which our 
 whole heart rebel ? can you say to the clerk , ' be a poet ' ? can you say 
 to the poet , ' be a clerk ' ? it be no more to the happiness of a man 's 
 be to order he to take to one career when his whole heart be set 
 on another , than it be to order he to marry one woman when it be to 
 another woman that his heart will turn . " 

 Cecilia here wince and look away . Kenelm have more tact than most man 
 of his age , - that is , a keener perception of subject to avoid ; but then 
 Kenelm have a wretched habit of forget the person he talk to and 
 talk to himself . utterly oblivious of George Belvoir , he be talk 
 to himself now . not then observe the effect his mal - a - propos dogma 
 have produce on his listener , he go on , " happiness be a word very 
 lightly use . it may mean little ; it may mean much . by the word 
 happiness I would signify , not the momentary joy of a child who get 
 a plaything , but the last harmony between our inclination and our 
 object ; and without that harmony we be a discord to ourselves , we be 
 incompletion , we be failure . yet there be plenty of adviser who say 
 to we , ' it be a duty to be a discord . ' I deny it . " 

 here Cecilia rise and say in a low voice , " it be get late . we must 
 go homeward . " 

 they descend the green eminence slowly , and at first in silence . 
 the bat , emerge from the ivied ruin they leave behind , flit and 
 skim before they , chase the insect of the night . a moth , escape 
 from its pursuer , alight on Cecilia 's breast , as if for refuge . 

 " the bat be practical , " say Kenelm ; " they be hungry , and their 
 motive power to - night be strong . their interest be in the insect they 
 chase . they have no interest in the star ; but the star lure the moth . " 

 Cecilia draw her slight scarf over the moth , so that it might not 
 fly off and become a prey to the bat . " yet , " say she , " the moth be 
 practical too . " 

 " ay , just now , since it have find an asylum from the danger that 
 threaten it in its course towards the star . " 

 Cecilia feel the beating of her heart , upon which lie the moth 
 conceal . do she think that a deep and more tender meaning than they 
 outwardly express be couch in these word ? if so , she err . they 
 now near the garden gate , and Kenelm pause as he open it . " see , " 
 he say , " the moon have just rise over those dark fir , make the still 
 night stiller . be it not strange that we mortal , place amid perpetual 
 agitation and tumult and strife , as if our natural element , conceive a 
 sense of holiness in the image antagonistic to our real life ; I mean 
 in image of repose ? I feel at the moment as if I suddenly be 
 make well , now that heaven and earth have suddenly become yet more 
 tranquil . I be now conscious of a purer and sweet moral than either I 
 or you draw from the insect you have shelter . I must come to the poet 
 to express it , - 


  " ' the desire of the moth for the star , 
  of the night for the morrow ; 
  the devotion to something afar 
  from the sphere of our sorrow . ' 


 " oh , that something afar ! that something afar ! never to be reach on 
 this earth , - never , never ! " 

 there be such a wail in that cry from the man 's heart that Cecilia 
 could not resist the impulse of a divine compassion . she lay her hand 
 on his , and look on the dark wildness of his upward face with eye 
 that Heaven mean to be well of comfort to grieve man . at the light 
 touch of that hand Kenelm start , look down , and meet those soothe 
 eye . 

 " I be happy to tell you that I have save my Durham , " cry out Mr. 
 Travers from the other side of the gate . 



 CHAPTER XX . 

 AS Kenelm that night retire to his own room , he pause on the 
 landing - place opposite to the portrait which Mr. Travers have consign 
 to that desolate exile . this daughter of a race dishonour in its 
 extinction might well have be the glory of the house she have enter 
 as a bride . the countenance be singularly beautiful , and of a character 
 of beauty eminently patrician ; there be in its expression a gentleness 
 and modesty not often find in the female portrait of Sir Peter 
 Lely , and in the eye and in the smile a wonderful aspect of innocent 
 happiness . 

 " what a speak homily , " soliloquize Kenelm , address the picture , 
 " against the ambition thy fair descendant would awake in I , art thou , 
 o lovely image ! for generation thy beauty live in this canvas , a thing 
 of joy , the pride of the race it adorn . Owner after owner say 
 to admire guest , ' yes , a fine portrait , by lely ; she be my 
 ancestress , - a Fletwode of Fletwode . ' now , lest guest should remember 
 that a Fletwode marry a Travers thou art thrust out of sight ; not even 
 Lely 's art can make thee of value , can redeem thine innocent self from 
 disgrace . and the last of the Fletwodes , doubtless the most ambitious of 
 all , the most bent on restore and regilde the old lordly name , die 
 a felon ; the infamy of one live man be so large that it can blot 
 out the honour of the dead . " he turn his eye from the smile of 
 the portrait , enter his own room , and , seat himself by the 
 writing - table , draw blotting - book and note - paper towards he , take 
 up the pen , and instead of writing fall into deep revery . there be a 
 slight frown on his brow , on which frown be rare . he be very angry 
 with himself . 

 " Kenelm , " he say , enter into his customary dialogue with that self , 
 " it become you , forsooth , to moralize about the honour of race which 
 have no affinity with you . Son of Sir Peter Chillingly , look at home . 
 be you quite sure that you have not say or do or look a something 
 that may bring trouble to the hearth on which you be receive as guest ? 
 what right have you to be moan forth your egotism , not remember 
 that your word fall on compassionate ear , and that such word , hear 
 at moonlight by a girl whose heart they move to pity , may have danger 
 for her peace ? shame on you , Kenelm ! shame ! know too what her 
 father 's wish be ; and know too that you have not the excuse of 
 desire to win that fair creature for yourself . what do you mean , 
 Kenelm ? I do not   hear you ; speak out . oh , ' that I be a vain coxcomb to 
 fancy that she could take a fancy to I : ' well , perhaps I be ; I hope so 
 earnestly ; and at all event , there have be and shall be no time for 
 much mischief . we be off to - morrow , Kenelm ; bestir yourself and pack 
 up , write your letter , and then ' put out the light , - put out the 
 light ! ' " 

 but this converser with himself do not immediately set to work , as 
 agree upon by that twofold one . he rise and walk restlessly to and 
 fro the floor , stop ever and anon to look at the picture on the 
 wall . 

 several of the worst paint of the family portrait have be consign 
 to the room tenant by Kenelm , which , though both the old and 
 large bed - chamber in the house , be always appropriate to a bachelor 
 male guest , partly because it be without dressing - room , remote , and 
 only approach by the small back - staircase , to the landing - place of 
 which Arabella have be banish in disgrace ; and partly because it have 
 the reputation of be haunt , and lady be more alarmed by that 
 superstition than man be suppose to be . the portrait on which Kenelm 
 now pause to gaze be of various date , from the reign of Elizabeth to 
 that of George III . , none of they by eminent artist , and none of they 
 the effigy of ancestor who have leave name in history , - in short , such 
 portrait as be often see in the country house of well - bear squire . 
 one family type of feature or expression pervade most of these 
 portrait ; feature clear - cut and hardy , expression open and honest . 
 and though not one of those dead man have be famous , each of they have 
 contribute his unostentatious share , in his own simple way , to the 
 movement of his time . that worthy in ruff and corselet have man his 
 own ship at his own cost against the Armada ; never have be repay by 
 the thrifty Burleigh the expense which have harass he and diminish 
 his patrimony ; never have be even knight . that gentleman with short 
 straight hair , which overhang his forehead , lean on his sword 
 with one hand , and a book open in the other hand , have serve as 
 representative of his county town in the Long Parliament , fight under 
 Cromwell at Marston Moor , and , resist the Protector when he remove 
 the " bauble , " be one of the patriot incarcerate in " Hell hole . " he , 
 too , have diminish his patrimony , maintain two trooper and two 
 horse at his own charge , and " Hell hole " be all he get in return . 
 a third , with a sleeker expression of countenance , and a large wig , 
 flourish in the quiet time of Charles II . , have only be a justice 
 of the peace , but his alert look show that he have be a very active 
 one . he have neither increase nor diminish his ancestral fortune . a 
 fourth , in the costume of William III . 's reign , have somewhat add to 
 the patrimony by become a lawyer . he must have be a successful one . 
 he be inscribe " sergeant - at - law . " a fifth , a lieutenant in the army , 
 be kill at Blenheim ; his portrait be that of a very young and 
 handsome man , take the year before his death . his wife 's portrait be 
 place in the drawing - room because it be paint by Kneller . she be 
 handsome too , and marry again a nobleman , whose portrait , of course , 
 be not in the family collection . here there be a gap in chronological 
 arrangement , the lieutenant 's heir be an infant ; but in the time 
 of George II . another Travers appear as the governor of a West India 
 colony . his son take part in a very different movement of the age . he be 
 represent old , venerable , with white hair , and underneath his 
 effigy be inscribe , " follower of Wesley . " his successor complete the 
 collection . he be in naval uniform ; he be in full length , and one of his 
 leg be a wooden one . he be Captain , R.N. , and inscribe , " fight under 
 Nelson at Trafalgar . " that portrait would have find more dignified 
 place in the reception - room if the face have not be forbiddingly ugly , 
 and the picture itself a villanous daub . 

 " I see , " say Kenelm , stop short , " why Cecilia Travers have be 
 rear to talk of duty as a practical interest in life . these man of a 
 former time seem to have live to discharge a duty , and not to follow 
 the progress of the age in the chase of a money - bag , - except perhaps 
 one , but then to be sure he be a lawyer . Kenelm , rouse up and listen 
 to I ; whatever we be , whether active or indolent , be not my favourite 
 maxim a just and a true one ; namely , ' a good man do good by live ' ? 
 but , for that , he must be a harmony and not a discord . Kenelm , you lazy 
 dog , we must pack up . " 

 Kenelm then refill his portmanteau , and label and direct it to 
 Exmundham , after which he write these three note : - 


 NOTE I. 

 to the marchioness of GLENALVON . 

 MY DEAR FRIEND and MONITRESS , - I have leave your last letter a month 
 unanswered . I could not reply to your congratulation on the event of my 
 attain the age of twenty - one . that event be a conventional sham , 
 and you know how I abhor sham and convention . the truth be that I be 
 either much young than twenty - one or much old . as to all design on 
 my peace in stand for our county at the next election , I wish to 
 defeat they , and I have do so ; and now I have commence a course of 
 travel . I have intend on start to confine it to my native country . 
 intention be mutable . I be go abroad . you shall hear of my 
 whereabout . I write this from the house of Leopold Travers , who , I 
 understand from his fair daughter , be a connection of your ; a man to be 
 highly esteemed and cordially like . 

 no , in spite of all your flattering prediction , I shall never be 
 anything in this life more distinguished than what I be now . Lady 
 Glenalvon allow I to sign myself her grateful friend , 

 K. C. 


 NOTE II . 

 DEAR COUSIN MIVERS , - I be go abroad . I may want money ; for , in order 
 to rouse motive power within I , I mean to want money if I can . when I 
 be a boy of sixteen you offer I money to write attack upon veteran 
 author for " the Londoner . " will you give I money now for a similar 
 display of that grand New Idea of our generation ; namely , that the less 
 a man know of a subject the well he understand it ? I be about to 
 travel into country which I have never see , and among race I have 
 never know . my arbitrary judgment on both will be invaluable to " the 
 Londoner " from a Special Correspondent who share your respect for the 
 anonymous , and whose name be never to be divulge . direct your answer by 
 return to I , poste restante , Calais . 

 your truly , 

 K. C. 


 NOTE III . 

 MY DEAR FATHER , - I find your letter here , whence I depart to - morrow . 
 Excuse haste . I go abroad , and shall write to you from Calais . 

 I admire Leopold Travers very much . after all , how much of self - balance 
 there be in a true english gentleman ! toss he up and down where you 
 will , and he always alight on his foot , - a gentleman . he have one child , 
 a daughter name Cecilia , - handsome enough to allure into wedlock any 
 mortal whom Decimus Roach have not convince that in celibacy lie the 
 right " approach to the Angels . " moreover , she be a girl whom one can 
 talk with . even you could talk with she . traver wish she to marry 
 a very respectable , good - look , promising gentleman , in every way 
 ' suitable , " as they say . and if she do , she will rival that pink and 
 perfection of polished womanhood , Lady Glenalvon . I send you back my 
 portmanteau . I have pretty well exhausted my experience - money , but have 
 not yet encroach on my monthly allowance . I mean still to live upon 
 that , eke it out , if necessary , by the sweat of my brow or brain . but 
 if any case require extra fund should occur , - a case in which that 
 extra would do such real good to another that I feel you would do 
 it , - why , I must draw a check on your banker . but understand that be 
 your expense , not mine , and it be you who be to be repay in Heaven . 
 Dear father , how I do love and honour you every day more and more ! 
 promise you not to propose to any young lady till I come first to you 
 for consent ! - oh , my dear father , how could you doubt it ? how doubt 
 that I could not be happy with any wife whom you could not love as a 
 daughter ? accept that promise as sacred . but I wish you have ask I 
 something in which obedience be not much too facile to be a test of 
 duty . I could not have obey you more cheerfully if you have ask I to 
 promise never to propose to any young lady at all . have you ask I to 
 promise that I would renounce the dignity of reason for the frenzy of 
 love , or the freedom of man for the servitude of husband , then I might 
 have seek to achieve the impossible ; but I should have die in the 
 effort ! - and thou wouldst have know that remorse which haunt the bed 
 of the tyrant . 

 your affectionate son , 

 K. C. 



 CHAPTER XXI . 

 the next morning Kenelm surprise the party at breakfast by appear 
 in the coarse habiliment in which he have first make his host 's 
 acquaintance . he do not glance towards Cecilia when he announce his 
 departure ; but , his eye rest on Mrs. Campion , he smile , perhaps a 
 little sadly , at see her countenance brighten up and hear her give 
 a short sigh of relief . traver try hard to induce he to stay a few 
 day long , but Kenelm be firm . " the summer be wear away , " say he , 
 " and I have far to go before the flower fade and the snow fall . on the 
 third night from this I shall sleep on foreign soil . " 

 " you be go abroad , then ? " ask Mrs. Campion . 

 " yes . " 

 " a sudden resolution , Mr. Chillingly . the other day you talk of 
 visit the Scotch lake . " 

 " true ; but , on reflection , they will be crowd with holiday tourist , 
 many of whom I shall probably know . abroad I shall be free , for I shall 
 be unknown . " 

 " I suppose you will be back for the hunting season , " say Travers . 

 " I think not . I do not hunt fox . " 

 " probably we shall at all event meet in London , " say Travers . " I 
 think , after long rustication , that a season or two in the bustling 
 capital may be a salutary change for mind as well as for body ; and it 
 be time that Cecilia be present and her court - dress specially 
 commemorate in the column of the ' Morning Post . ' " 

 Cecilia be seemingly too busied behind the tea - urn to heed this 
 reference to her debut . 

 " I shall miss you terribly , " cry Travers , a few moment afterwards , 
 and with a hearty emphasis . " I declare that you have quite unsettle I . 
 your quaint saying will be ring in my ear long after you be go . " 

 there be a rustle as of a woman 's dress in sudden change of movement 
 behind the tea - urn . 

 " Cissy , " say Mrs. Campion , " be we ever to have our tea ? " 

 " I beg pardon , " answer a voice behind the urn . " I hear Pompey "   the 
 skye terrier   " whine on the lawn . they have shut he out . I will be 
 back presently . " 

 Cecilia rise and be go . Mrs. Campion take her place at the tea - urn . 

 " it be quite absurd of Cissy to be so fond of that hideous dog , " say 
 Travers , petulantly . 

 " its hideousness be its beauty , " return Mrs. Campion , laugh . " Mr. 
 Belvoir select it for she as have the long back and the short 
 leg of any dog he could find in Scotland . " 

 " ah , George give it to she ; I forget that , " say Travers , laugh 
 pleasantly . 

 it be some minute before Miss Travers return with the Skye 
 terrier , and she seem to have recover her spirit in regain that 
 ornamental accession to the party ; talk very quickly and gayly , and 
 with flushed cheek , like a young person excite by her own overflow of 
 mirth . 

 but when , half an hour afterwards , Kenelm take leave of she and Mrs. 
 Campion at the hall - door , the flush be go , her lip be tightly 
 compress , and her parting word be not audible . then , as his figure 
  side by side with her father , who accompany his guest to the lodge 
 swiftly pass across the lawn and vanish amid the tree beyond , Mrs. 
 Campion wind a mother - like arm around her waist and kiss she . 
 Cecilia shiver and turn her face to her friend smile ; but such a 
 smile , - one of those smile that seem brimful of tear . 

 " thank you , dear , " she say meekly ; and , glide away towards the 
 flower - garden , linger a while by the gate which Kenelm have open 
 the night before . then she go with languid step up the green slope 
 towards the ruin priory . 




 BOOK IV . 



 CHAPTER I. 

 it be somewhat more than a year and a half since Kenelm Chillingly leave 
 England , and the scene now be in London , during that early and more 
 sociable season which precede the Easter holiday , - season in which 
 the charm of intellectual companionship be not yet wither away in the 
 heated atmosphere of crowd room , - season in which party be small , 
 and conversation extend beyond the interchange of commonplace with 
 one 's next neighbour at a dinner - table , - season in which you have a 
 fair chance of find your warm friend not absorb by the superior 
 claim of their chilly acquaintance . 

 there be what be call a conversazione at the house of one of those 
 Whig nobleman who yet retain the graceful art of bring agreeable 
 people together , and collect round they the true aristocracy , which 
 combine letter and art and science with hereditary rank and political 
 distinction , - that art which be the happy secret of the Lansdownes and 
 holland of the last generation . Lord Beaumanoir be himself a genial , 
 well - read man , a good judge of art , and a pleasant talker . he have a 
 charming wife , devote to he and to her child , but with enough love 
 of general approbation to make herself as popular in the fashionable 
 world as if she seek in its gayetie a refuge from the dulness of 
 domestic life . 

 amongst the guest at the Beaumanoirs , this evening be two man , seat 
 apart in a small room , and converse familiarly . the one might be about 
 fifty - four ; he be tall , strongly build , but not corpulent , somewhat 
 bald , with black eyebrow , dark eye , bright and keen , mobile lip round 
 which there play a shrewd and sometimes sarcastic smile . 

 this gentleman , the Right Hon . Gerard Danvers , be a very influential 
 member of Parliament . he have , when young for english public life , 
 attain to high office ; but - partly from a great distaste to the 
 drudgery of administration ; partly from a pride of temperament , which 
 unfit he for the subordination that a Cabinet owe to its chief ; 
 partly , also , from a not uncommon kind of epicurean philosophy , at once 
 joyous and cynical , which seek the pleasure of life and hold very 
 cheap its honours - he have obstinately decline to re - enter office , and 
 only speak on rare occasion . on such occasion he carry great weight , 
 and , by the brief expression of his opinion , command more vote than 
 many an orator infinitely more eloquent . despite his want of ambition , 
 he be fond of power in his own way , - power over the people who have 
 power ; and , in the love of political intrigue , he find an amusement for 
 an intellect very subtle and very active . at this moment he be bent on 
 a new combination among the leader of different section in the same 
 party , by which certain veteran be to retire , and certain young man 
 to be admit into the Administration . it be an amiable feature in his 
 character that he have a sympathy with the young , and have help to 
 bring into Parliament , as well as into office , some of the ablest of 
 a generation later than his own . he give they sensible counsel , 
 be pleased when they succeed , and encourage they when they 
 fail , - always provide that they have stuff enough in they to redeem 
 the failure ; if not , he gently drop they from his intimacy , but 
 maintain sufficiently familiar term with they to be pretty sure that 
 he could influence their vote whenever he so desire . 

 the gentleman with whom he be now converse be young , about 
 five - and - twenty ; not yet in Parliament , but with an intense desire to 
 obtain a seat in it , and with one of those reputation which a youth 
 carry away from school and college , justify , not by honour purely 
 academical , but by an impression of ability and power create on the 
 mind of his contemporary and endorse by his elder . he have do 
 little at the University beyond take a fair degree , except acquire 
 at the debating society the fame of an exceedingly ready and adroit 
 speaker . on quit college he have write one or two political 
 article in a quarterly review , which create a sensation ; and though 
 belong to no profession , and have but a small yet independent 
 income , society be very civil to he , as to a man who would some day or 
 other attain a position in which he could damage his enemy and serve 
 his friend . something in this young man 's countenance and bearing 
 tend to favour the credit give to his ability and his promise . in his 
 countenance there be no beauty ; in his bear no elegance . but in that 
 countenance there be vigour , there be energy , there be audacity . a 
 forehead wide but low , protuberant in those organ over the brow which 
 indicate the quality fit for perception and judgment , - quality 
 for every - day life ; eye of the clear english blue , small , somewhat 
 sunken , vigilant , sagacious , penetrate ; a long straight upper 
 lip , significant of resolute purpose ; a mouth in which a student 
 of physiognomy would have detect a dangerous charm . the smile 
 be captivate , but it be artificial , surround by dimple , and 
 display tooth white , small , strong , but divide from each other . the 
 expression of that smile would have be frank and candid to all who 
 fail to notice that it be not in harmony with the brood forehead 
 and the steely eye ; that it seem to stand distinct from the rest 
 of the face , like a feature that have learn its part . there be that 
 physical power in the back of the head which belong to man who make 
 their way in life , - combative and destructive . all gladiator have it ; 
 so have great debater and great reformer , - that is , reformer who can 
 destroy , but not necessarily reconstruct . so , too , in the bearing of the 
 man there be a hardy self - confidence , much too simple and unaffected 
 for his bad enemy to call it self - conceit . it be the bearing of one 
 who know how to maintain personal dignity without seem to care about 
 it . never servile to the great , never arrogant to the little ; so little 
 over - refined that it be never vulgar , - a popular bearing . 

 the room in which these gentleman be seat be separate from the 
 general suite of apartment by a lobby off the landing - place , and serve 
 for Lady Beaumanoir 's boudoir . very pretty it be , but simply furnish , 
 with chintz drapery . the wall be adorn with drawing in 
 water - colour , and precious specimen of china on fanciful Parian 
 bracket . at one corner , by a window that look southward and open 
 on a spacious balcony , glaze in and fill with flower , stand one of 
 those high trellis screen , first invent , I believe , in Vienna , and 
 along which ivy be so train as to form an arbour . 

 the recess thus construct , and which be completely out of sight from 
 the rest of the room , be the hostess 's favourite writing - nook . the two 
 man I have describe be seat near the screen , and have certainly no 
 suspicion that any one could be behind it . 

 " yes , " say Mr. Danvers , from an ottoman niche in another recess of the 
 room , " I think there will be an opening at Saxboro ' soon : Milroy want a 
 Colonial Government ; and if we can reconstruct the Cabinet as I propose , 
 he would get one . Saxboro ' would thus be vacant . but , my dear fellow , 
 Saxboro ' be a place to be woo through love , and only win through 
 money . it demand liberalism from a candidate , - two kind of liberalism 
 seldom united ; the liberalism in opinion which be natural enough to a 
 very poor man , and the liberalism in expenditure which be scarcely to 
 be obtain except from a very rich one . you may compute the cost of 
 Saxboro ' at L3000 to get in , and about L2000 more to defend your seat 
 against a petition , - the defeat candidate nearly always petition . 
 L5000 be a large sum ; and the bad of it be , that the extreme opinion 
 to which the member for Saxboro ' must pledge himself be a drawback to 
 an official career . violent politician be not the good raw material 
 out of which to manufacture fortunate placeman . " 

 " the opinion do not so much matter ; the expense do . I can not afford 
 L5000 , or even L3000 . " 

 " would not Sir Peter assist ? he have , you say , only one son ; and if 
 anything happen to that son , you be the next heir . " 

 " my father quarrel with Sir Peter , and harass he by an imprudent 
 and ungracious litigation . I scarcely think I could apply to he for 
 money to obtain a seat in Parliament upon the democratic side of the 
 question ; for , though I know little of his politic , I take it for 
 grant that a country gentleman of old family and L10,000 a year can not 
 well be a democrat . " 

 " then I presume you would not be a democrat if , by the death of your 
 cousin , you become heir to the Chillinglys . " 

 " I be not sure what I might be in that case . there be time when a 
 democrat of ancient lineage and good estate could take a very high 
 place amongst the aristocracy . " 

 " Humph ! my dear Gordon , vous irez loin . " 

 " I hope to do so . measure myself against the man of my own day , I do 
 not see many who should outstrip I . " 

 " what sort of a fellow be your cousin Kenelm ? I meet he once or twice 
 when he be very young , and read with Welby in London . People then 
 say that he be very clever ; he strike I as very odd . " 

 " I never see he , but from all I hear , whether he be clever or whether 
 he be odd , he be not likely to do anything in life , - a dreamer . " 

 " write poetry perhaps ? " 

 " capable of it , I dare say . " 

 just then some other guest come into the room , amongst they a lady 
 of an appearance at once singularly distinguished and singularly 
 prepossessing , rather above the common height , and with a certain 
 indescribable nobility of air and presence . Lady Glenalvon be one of 
 the queen of the London world , and no queen of that world be ever 
 less worldly or more queen - like . side by side with the lady be Mr. 
 Chillingly Mivers . Gordon and Mivers interchange friendly nod , and the 
 former saunter away and be soon lose amid a crowd of other young 
 man , with whom , as he could converse well and lightly on thing which 
 interested they , he be rather a favourite , though he be not an 
 intimate associate . Mr. Danvers retire into a corner of the adjoining 
 lobby , where he favour the french ambassador with his view on the 
 state of Europe and the reconstruction of Cabinets in general . 

 " but , " say Lady Glenalvon to Chillingly Mivers , " be you quite sure 
 that my old young friend Kenelm be here ? since you tell I so , I have 
 look everywhere for he in vain . I should so much like to see he 
 again . " 

 " I certainly catch a glimpse of he half an hour ago ; but before I 
 could escape from a geologist who be bore I about the silurian 
 system , Kenelm have vanish . " 

 " perhaps it be his ghost ! " 

 " well , we certainly live in the most credulous and superstitious age 
 upon record ; and so many people tell I that they converse with the 
 dead under the table that it seem impertinent in I to say that I do not 
 believe in ghost . " 

 " tell I some of those incomprehensible story about table - rap , " 
 say Lady Glenalvon . " there be a charming , snug recess here behind the 
 screen . " 

 Scarcely have she enter the recess when she draw back with a start and 
 an exclamation of amaze . seat at the table within the recess , his chin 
 rest on his hand , and his face cast down in abstract revery , be a 
 young man . so still be his attitude , so calmly mournful the expression 
 of his face , so estranged do he seem from all the motley but brilliant 
 assemblage which circle around the solitude he have make for himself , 
 that he might well have be deem one of those visitant from another 
 world whose secret the intruder have wish to learn . of that intruder 's 
 presence he be evidently unconscious . recover her surprise , she 
 steal up to he , place her hand on his shoulder , and utter his name 
 in a low gentle voice . at that sound Kenelm Chillingly look up . 

 " do you not remember I ? " ask Lady Glenalvon . before he could answer , 
 miver , who have follow the marchioness into the recess , interpose . 

 " my dear Kenelm , how be you ? when do you come to London ? why have you 
 not call on I ; and what on earth be you hide yourself for ? " 

 Kenelm have now recover the self - possession which he rarely lose long 
 in the presence of other . he return cordially his kinsman 's greeting , 
 and kiss with his wonted chivalrous grace the fair hand which the lady 
 withdraw from his shoulder and extend to his pressure . " remember you ! " 
 he say to Lady Glenalvon with the kindliest expression of his soft dark 
 eye ; " I be not so far advanced towards the noon of life as to forget 
 the sunshine that brighten its morning . my dear miver , your question 
 be easily answer . I arrive in England two week ago , stay at 
 Exmundham till this morning , to - day din with Lord Thetford , whose 
 acquaintance I make abroad , and be persuade by he to come here and 
 be introduce to his father and mother , the Beaumanoirs . after I have 
 undergo that ceremony , the sight of so many strange face frighten 
 I into shyness . enter this room at a moment when it be quite 
 deserted , I resolve to turn hermit behind the screen . " 

 " why , you must have see your cousin Gordon as you come into the room . " 

 " but you forget I do not   know he by sight . however , there be no one in 
 the room when I enter ; a little later some other come in , for I hear 
 a faint buzz , like that of person talk in a whisper . however , I be 
 no eavesdropper , as a person behind a screen be on the dramatic stage . " 

 this be true . even have Gordon and Danvers talk in a loud tone , 
 Kenelm have be too absorb in his own thought to have hear a word of 
 their conversation . 

 " you ought to know young Gordon ; he be a very clever fellow , and have an 
 ambition to enter Parliament . I hope no old family quarrel between his 
 bear of a father and dear Sir Peter will make you object to meet he . " 

 " Sir Peter be the most forgiving of man , but he would scarcely forgive 
 I if I decline to meet a cousin who have never offend he . " 

 " well say . come and meet Gordon at breakfast to - morrow , - ten o'clock . I 
 be still in the old room . " 

 while the kinsman thus converse , Lady Glenalvon have seat herself on 
 the couch beside Kenelm , and be quietly observe his countenance . 
 now she speak . " my dear Mr. Mivers , you will have many opportunity of 
 talk with Kenelm ; do not grudge I five minute ' talk with he now . " 

 " I leave your ladyship alone in your hermitage . how all the man in this 
 assembly will envy the hermit ! " 



 CHAPTER II . 

 " I be glad to see you once more in the world , " say Lady Glenalvon ; " and 
 I trust that you be now prepared to take that part in it which ought to 
 be no mean one if you do justice to your talent and your nature . " 

 KENELM . - " when you go to the theatre , and see one of the piece which 
 appear now to be the fashion , which would you rather be , - an actor or a 
 looker - on ? " 

 LADY GLENALVON . - " my dear young friend , your question sadden I . " 
  after a pause .   - " but though I use a stage metaphor when I express 
 my hope that you would take no mean part in the world , the world be not 
 really a theatre . life admit of no looker - on . speak to I frankly , as 
 you use to do . your face retain its old melancholy expression . be you 
 not happy ? " 

 KENELM . - " Happy , as mortal go , I ought to be . I do not think I be 
 unhappy . if my temper be melancholic , melancholy have a happiness of its 
 own . Milton show that there be as many charm in life to be find on 
 the Penseroso side of it as there be on the Allegro . " 

 LADY GLENALVON . - " Kenelm , you save the life of my poor son , and when , 
 later , he be take from I , I feel as if he have commend you to my 
 care . when at the age of sixteen , with a boy 's year and a man 's heart , 
 you come to London , do I not try to be to you almost as a mother ? and 
 do you not often tell I that you could confide to I the secret of 
 your heart more readily than to any other ? " 

 " you be to I , " say Kenelm , with emotion , " that most precious and 
 sustain good genius which a youth can find at the threshold of 
 life , - a woman gently wise , kindly sympathize , sham he by the 
 spectacle of her own purity from all gross error , elevate he from 
 mean taste and object by the exquisite , ineffable loftiness of soul 
 which be only find in the noble order of womanhood . come , I will open 
 my heart to you still . I fear it be more wayward than ever . it still 
 feel estranged from the companionship and pursuit natural to my age 
 and station . however , I have be seek to brace and harden my nature , 
 for the practical end of life , by travel and adventure , chiefly among 
 rougher variety of mankind than we meet in drawing - room . now , in 
 compliance with the duty I owe to my dear father 's wish , I come back 
 to these circle , which under your auspex I enter in boyhood , and 
 which even then seem to I so inane and artificial . take a part in the 
 world of these circle ; such be your wish . my answer be brief . I have 
 be do my good to acquire a motive power , and have not succeed . I 
 see nothing that I care to strive for , nothing that I care to gain . the 
 very time in which we live be to I , as to Hamlet , out of joint ; and 
 I be not bear like Hamlet to set they right . ah ! if I could look on 
 society through the spectacle with which the poor hidalgo in ' Gil Blas ' 
 look on his meagre board , - spectacle by which cherry appear the
