 size of peach , and tomtit as large as turkey ! the imagination which 
 be necessary to ambition be a great magnifier . " 

 " I have know more than one man , now very eminent , very active , who 
 at your age feel the same estrangement from the practical pursuit of 
 other . " 

 " and what reconcile those man to such pursuit ? " 

 " that diminish sense of individual personality , that unconscious 
 fusion of one 's own be into other existence , which belong to home 
 and marriage . " 

 " I do not   object to home , but I do to marriage . " 

 " depend on it there be no home for man where there be no woman . " 

 " Prettily say . in that case I resign the home . " 

 " do you mean seriously to tell I that you never see the woman you could 
 love enough to make she your wife , and never enter any home that you do 
 not quit with a touch of envy at the happiness of married life ? " 

 " seriously , I never see such a woman ; seriously , I never enter such a 
 home . " 

 " Patience , then ; your time will come , and I hope it be at hand . listen 
 to I . it be only yesterday that I feel an indescribable longing to 
 see you again , - to know your address that I might write to you ; for 
 yesterday , when a certain young lady leave my house after a week 's visit , 
 I say this girl would make a perfect wife , and , above all , the exact 
 wife to suit Kenelm Chillingly . " 

 " Kenelm Chillingly be very glad to hear that this young lady have leave 
 your house . " 

 " but she have not leave London : she be here to - night . she only stay 
 with I till her father come to town , and the house he have take for the 
 season be vacant ; those event happen yesterday . " 

 " fortunate event for I : they permit I to call on you without danger . " 

 " have you no curiosity to know , at least , who and what be the young lady 
 who appear to I so well suited to you ? " 

 " no curiosity , but a vague sensation of alarm . " 

 " well , I can not talk pleasantly with you while you be in this 
 irritate mood , and it be time to quit the hermitage . come , there be 
 many person here , with some of whom you should renew old acquaintance , 
 and to some of whom I should like to make you know . " 

 " I be prepared to follow Lady Glenalvon wherever she deign to lead 
 I , - except to the altar with another . " 



 CHAPTER III . 

 the room be now full , - not overcrowded , but full , - and it be rarely 
 even in that house that so many distinguished person be collect 
 together . a young man thus honour by so grande a dame as Lady 
 Glenalvon could not but be cordially welcome by all to whom she 
 present he , Ministers and parliamentary leader , ball - giver , and 
 beauty in vogue , - even author and artist ; and there be something in 
 Kenelm Chillingly , in his strike countenance and figure , in that calm 
 ease of manner natural to his indifference to effect , which seem to 
 justify the favour show to he by the brilliant princess of fashion and 
 mark he out for general observation . 

 that first evening of his reintroduction to the polite world be 
 a success which few young man of his year achieve . he produce a 
 sensation . just as the room be thin , Lady Glenalvon whisper to 
 Kenelm , - 

 " come this way : there be one person I must reintroduce you to ; thank I 
 for it hereafter . " 

 Kenelm follow the marchioness , and find himself face to face with 
 Cecilia Travers . she be lean on her father 's arm , look very 
 handsome , and her beauty be heighten by the blush which overspread 
 her cheek as Kenelm Chillingly approach . 

 Travers greet he with great cordiality ; and Lady Glenalvon ask he 
 to escort she to the refreshment - room , Kenelm have no option but to offer 
 his arm to Cecilia . 

 Kenelm feel somewhat embarrassed . " have you be long in town , Miss 
 Travers ? " 

 " a little more than a week , but we only settle into our house 
 yesterday . " 

 " ah , indeed ! be you then the young lady who - " he stop short , and 
 his face grow gentle and graver in its expression . 

 " the young lady who - what ? " ask Cecilia with a smile . 

 " who have be stay with Lady Glenalvon ? " 

 " yes ; do she tell you ? " 

 " she do not mention your name , but praise that young lady so justly 
 that I ought to have guess it . " 

 Cecilia make some not very audible answer , and on enter the 
 refreshment - room other young man gather round she , and Lady Glenalvon 
 and Kenelm remain silent in the midst of a general small - talk . when 
 Travers , after give his address to Kenelm , and , of course , press 
 he to call , leave the house with Cecilia , Kenelm say to Lady Glenalvon , 
 musingly , " so that be the young lady in whom I be to see my fate : you 
 know that we have meet before ? " 

 " yes , she tell I when and where . besides , it be not two year since you 
 write to I from her father 's house . do you forget ? " 

 " ah , " say Kenelm , so abstractedly that he seem to be dream , " no 
 man with his eye open rush on his fate : when he do so his sight be 
 go . love be blind . they say the blind be very happy , yet I never meet 
 a blind man who would not recover his sight if he could . " 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 Mr. CHILLINGLY miver never give a dinner at his own room . when he 
 do give a dinner it be at Greenwich or Richmond . but he give 
 breakfast - party pretty often , and they be consider pleasant . 
 he have handsome bachelor apartment in Grosvenor Street , daintily 
 furnish , with a prevalent air of exquisite neatness , a good library 
 store with book of reference , and adorn with presentation copy 
 from author of the day , very beautifully bind . though the room serve 
 for the study of the professed man of letter , it have none of the untidy 
 litter which generally characterize the study of one whose vocation it 
 be to deal with book and paper . even the implement for writing 
 be not apparent , except when require . they lie conceal in a vast 
 cylinder bureau , French make , and French polish . within that bureau 
 be numerous pigeon - hole and secret drawer , and a profound well with 
 a separate patent lock . in the well be deposit the article intend 
 for publication in " the Londoner , " proof - sheet , etc . ; pigeon - hole 
 be devoted to ordinary correspondence ; secret drawer to confidential 
 note , and outline of biography of eminent man now live , but 
 intend to be complete for publication the day after their death . 

 no man write such funeral composition with a lively pen than that 
 of Chillingly Mivers ; and the large and miscellaneous circle of 
 his visit acquaintance allow he to ascertain , whether by 
 authoritative report or by personal observation , the sign of mortal 
 disease in the illustrious friend whose dinner he accept , and whose 
 fail pulse he instinctively feel in return the pressure of their 
 hand ; so that he be often able to put the finishing - stroke to their 
 obituary memorial day , week , even month , before their fate take the 
 public by surprise . that cylinder bureau be in harmony with the secrecy 
 in which this remarkable man shroud the production of his brain . in 
 his literary life Mivers have no " I , " there he be ever the inscrutable , 
 mysterious " we . " he be only " I " when you meet he in the world , and 
 call he Mivers . 

 Adjoining the library on one side be a small dining or rather breakfast 
 room , hang with valuable picture , - present from live painter . 
 many of these painter have be severely handle by Mr. Mivers in his 
 existence as " we , " - not always in " the Londoner . " his most pungent 
 criticism be often contribute to other intellectual journal 
 conduct by member of the same intellectual clique . painter know not 
 how contemptuously " we " have treat they when they meet Mr. Mivers . 
 his " I " be so complimentary that they send he a tribute of their 
 gratitude . 

 on the other side be his drawing - room , also enrich by many gift , 
 chiefly from fair hand , - embroider cushion and table - cover , bit 
 of Sevres or old Chelsea , elegant knick - knack of all kind . fashionable 
 authoress pay great court to Mr. Mivers ; and in the course of his 
 life as a single man , he have other female adorer besides fashionable 
 authoress . 

 Mr. Mivers have already return from his early constitutional walk 
 in the Park , and be now seat by the cylinder secretaire with a 
 mild - look man , who be one of the most merciless contributor to " the 
 Londoner " and no unimportant councillor in the oligarchy of the clique 
 that go by the name of the " Intellectuals . " 

 " well , " say Mivers , languidly , " I can not   even get through the book ; 
 it be as dull as the country in November . but , as you justly say , 
 the writer be an ' Intellectual , ' and a clique would be anything 
 but intellectual if it do not support its member . review the book 
 yourself ; mind and make the dulness of it the signal proof of its merit . 
 say : ' to the ordinary class of reader this exquisite work may appear 
 less brilliant than the flippant smartness of ' - any other author you 
 like to name ; ' but to the well educate and intelligent every line be 
 pregnant with , ' etc . by the way , when we come by and by to review the 
 exhibition at Burlington House , there be one painter whom we must try 
 our good to crush . I have not see his picture myself , but he be a new 
 man ; and our friend , who have see he , be terribly jealous of he , and 
 say that if the good judge do not put he down at once , the villanous 
 taste of the public will set he up as a prodigy . a low - live fellow 
 too , I hear . there be the name of the man and the subject of the 
 picture . see to it when the time come . meanwhile , prepare the way for 
 onslaught on the picture by occasional sneer at the painter . " here 
 Mr. Mivers take out of his cylinder a confidential note from the jealous 
 rival and hand it to his mild - look confrere ; then rise , he 
 say , " I fear we must suspend our business till to - morrow ; I expect two 
 young cousin to breakfast . " 

 as soon as the mild - look man be go , Mr. Mivers saunter to his 
 drawing - room window , amiably offer a lump of sugar to a canary - bird 
 send to he as a present the day before , and who , in the gild cage 
 which make part of the present , scan he suspiciously and refuse the 
 sugar . 

 Time have remain very gentle in its dealing with Chillingly Mivers . 
 he scarcely look a day old than when he be first present to the 
 reader on the birth of his kinsman Kenelm . he be reap the fruit of 
 his own sage maxim . free from whisker and safe in wig , there be no 
 sign of gray , no suspicion of dye . superiority to passion , abnegation of 
 sorrow , indulgence of amusement , avoidance of excess , have keep away the 
 crow' - foot , preserve the elasticity of his frame and the unflushed 
 clearness of his gentlemanlike complexion . the door open , and a 
 well - dress valet , who have live long enough with Mivers to grow very 
 much like he , announce Mr. Chillingly Gordon . 

 " good morning , " say miver ; " I be much pleased to see you talk so 
 long and so familiarly with danver : other , of course , observe it , and 
 it add a step to your career . it do you great good to be see in a 
 drawing - room talk apart with a somebody . but may I ask if the talk 
 itself be satisfactory ? " 

 " not at all : Danvers throw cold water on the notion of Saxboro ' , and 
 do not even hint that his party will help I to any other opening . 
 Party have few opening at its disposal nowadays for any young man . the 
 schoolmaster be abroad have sweep away the school for statesman as 
 he have sweep away the school for actor , - an evil , and an evil of a far 
 great consequence to the destiny of the nation than any good likely 
 to be get from the system that succeed it . " 

 " but it be of no use rail against thing that can not   be help . if I 
 be you , I would postpone all ambition of Parliament and read for the 
 bar . " 

 " the advice be sound , but too unpalatable to be take . I be resolve to 
 find a seat in the House , and where there be a will there be a way . " 

 " I be not so sure of that . " 

 " but I be . " 

 " judge by what your contemporary at the University tell I of your 
 speech at the Debating Society , you be not then an ultra - radical . 
 but it be only an ultra - radical who have a chance of success at 
 Saxboro ' . " 

 " I be no fanatic in politic . there be much to be say on all side : 
  coeteris paribus , I prefer the win side to the losing ; nothing 
 succeed like success . " 

 " ay , but in politic there be always reaction . the win side one day 
 may be the lose side another . the lose side represent a minority , 
 and a minority be sure to comprise more intellect than a majority : in 
 the long run intellect will force its way , get a majority and then lose 
 it , because with a majority it will become stupid . " 

 " Cousin Mivers , do not the history of the world show you that a single 
 individual can upset all theory as to the comparative wisdom of the 
 few or the many ? take the wise few you can find , and one man of genius 
 not a tithe so wise crush they into powder . but then that man of 
 genius , though he despise the many , must make use of they . that 
 do , he rule they . do not   you see how in free country political 
 destination resolve themselves into individual impersonation ? at 
 a general election it be one name around which elector rally . the 
 candidate may enlarge as much as he please on political principle , but 
 all his talk will not win he vote enough for success , unless he say , 
 ' I go with Mr. A. , ' the minister , or with Mr. Z. , the chief of the 
 opposition . it be not the Tories who beat the whig when Mr. Pitt 
 dissolve Parliament . it be Mr. Pitt who beat Mr. Fox , with whom in 
 general political principle - slave - trade , roman catholic emancipation , 
 parliamentary reform - he certainly agree much more than he do with any 
 man in his own cabinet . " 

 " take care , my young cousin , " cry Mivers , in accent of alarm ; " do not 
 set up for a man of genius . Genius be the bad quality a public man can 
 have nowadays : nobody heed it , and everybody be jealous of it . " 

 " pardon I , you mistake ; my remark be purely objective , and intend 
 as a reply to your argument . I prefer at present to go with the many 
 because it be the win side . if we then want a man of genius to keep 
 it the win side , by subjugate its partisan to his will , he will 
 be sure to come . the few will drive he to we , for the few be always 
 the enemy of the one man of genius . it be they who distrust , - it be 
 they who be jealous , - not the many . you have allow your judgment , 
 usually so clear , to be somewhat dim by your experience as a critic . 
 the critic be the few . they have infinitely more culture than the 
 many . but when a man of real genius appear and assert himself , the 
 critic be seldom such fair judge of he as the many be . if he be not 
 one of their oligarchical clique , they either abuse , or disparage , or 
 affect to ignore he ; though a time at last come when , have gain 
 the many , the critic acknowledge he . but the difference between the 
 man of action and the author be this , that the author rarely find this 
 acknowledgment till he be dead , and it be necessary to the man of action 
 to enforce it while he be alive . but enough of this speculation : you ask 
 I to meet Kenelm ; be he not come ? " 

 " yes , but I do not ask he till ten o'clock . I ask you at half - past 
 nine , because I wish to hear about Danvers and Saxboro ' , and also to 
 prepare you somewhat for your introduction to your cousin . I must be 
 brief as to the last , for it be only five minute to the hour , and he 
 be a man likely to be punctual . Kenelm be in all way your opposite . I 
 do not   know whether he be cleverer or less clever ; there be no scale of 
 measurement between you : but he be wholly void of ambition , and might 
 possibly assist your . he can do what he like with Sir Peter ; 
 and consider how your poor father - a worthy man , but 
 cantankerous - harass and persecute Sir Peter , because Kenelm come 
 between the estate and you , it be probable that Sir Peter bear you a 
 grudge , though Kenelm declare he incapable of it ; and it would be 
 well if you could annul that grudge in the father by conciliate the 
 goodwill of the son . " 

 " I should be glad so to annul it ; but what be Kenelm 's weak side ? - the 
 turf ? the hunting - field ? woman ? poetry ? one can only conciliate a man by 
 get on his weak side . " 

 " Hist ! I see he from the window . Kenelm 's weak side be , when I know 
 he some year ago , and I rather fancy it still be - " 

 " well , make haste ! I hear his ring at your door - bell . " 

 " a passionate longing to find ideal truth in real life . " 

 " ah ! " say Gordon , " as I think , - a mere dreamer " 



 CHAPTER V. 

 KENELM enter the room . the young cousin be introduce , shake hand , 
 recede a step , and gaze at each other . it be scarcely possible 
 to conceive a great contrast outwardly than that between the two 
 Chillingly representative of the rise generation . each be silently 
 impress by the sense of that contrast . each feel that the contrast 
 imply antagonism , and that if they two meet in the same arena it must 
 be as rival combatant ; still , by some mysterious intuition , each feel a 
 certain respect for the other , each divine in the other a power that 
 he could not fairly estimate , but against which his own power would 
 be strongly tasked to contend . so might exchange look a thorough - breed 
 deer - hound and a half - breed mastiff : the bystander could scarcely doubt 
 which be the nobler animal ; but he might hesitate which to bet on , if 
 the two come to deadly quarrel . meanwhile the thorough - breed deer - hound 
 and the half - breed mastiff sniff at each other in polite salutation . 
 Gordon be the first to give tongue . 

 " I have long wish to know you personally , " say he , throw into his 
 voice and manner that delicate kind of deference which a well - bear cadet 
 owe to the destine head of his house . " I can not conceive how I miss 
 you last night at Lady Beaumanoir 's , where miver tell I he meet you ; 
 but I leave early . " 

 here Mivers lead the way to the breakfast - room , and , there seat , the 
 host become the principal talker , run with lively glibness over the 
 principal topic of the day , - the last scandal , the last new book , the 
 reform of the army , the reform of the turf , the critical state of Spain , 
 and the debut of an italian singer . he seem an embodied Journal , 
 include the lead article , the Law Reports , Foreign Intelligence , 
 the Court Circular , down to the Births , Deaths , and marriage . Gordon 
 from time to time interrupt this flow of soul with brief , trenchant 
 remark , which evince his own knowledge of the subject treat , and 
 a habit of look on all subject connect with the pursuit and 
 business of mankind from a high ground appropriate to himself , and 
 through the medium of that blue glass which convey a wintry aspect to 
 summer landscape . Kenelm say little , but listen attentively . 

 the conversation arrest its discursive nature , to settle upon a 
 political chief , the high in fame and station of that party to which 
 miver profess - not to belong , he belong to himself alone , but to 
 appropinquate . miver speak of this chief with the great distrust , 
 and in a spirit of general depreciation . Gordon acquiesce in the 
 distrust and the depreciation , add , " but he be master of the 
 position , and must , of course , be support through thick and thin for 
 the present . " 

 " yes , for the present , " say miver , " one have no option . but you will 
 see some clever article in ' the Londoner ' towards the close of the 
 session , which will damage he greatly , by praise he in the wrong 
 place , and deepen the alarm of important follower , - an alarm now at 
 work , though suppress . " 

 here Kenelm ask , in humble tone , why Gordon think that a minister 
 he consider so untrustworthy and dangerous must for the present be 
 support through thick and thin . 

 " because at present a member elect so to support he would lose his 
 seat if he do not : need must when the devil drive . " 

 KENELM . - " when the devil drive , I should have think it well to 
 resign one 's seat on the coach ; perhaps one might be of some use , out of 
 it , in help to put on the drag . " 

 miver . - " Cleverly say , Kenelm . but , metaphor apart , Gordon be right . 
 a young politician must go with his party ; a veteran journalist like 
 myself be more independent . so long as the journalist blame everybody , 
 he will have plenty of reader . " 

 Kenelm make no reply , and Gordon change the conversation from man 
 to measure . he speak of some Bills before Parliament with remarkable 
 ability , evince much knowledge of the subject , much critical 
 acuteness , illustrate their defect , and prove the danger of their 
 ultimate consequence . 

 Kenelm be greatly strike with the vigour of this cold , clear mind , and 
 own to himself that the House of Commons be a fitting place for its 
 development . 

 " but , " say miver , " would you not be oblige to defend these Bills if 
 you be member for Saxboro ' ? " 

 " before I answer your question , answer I this : dangerous as the Bills 
 be , be it not necessary that they shall pass ? have not the public so 
 resolve ? " 

 " there can be no doubt of that . " 

 " then the member for Saxboro ' can not be strong enough to go against the 
 public . " 

 " progress of the age ! " say Kenelm , musingly . " do you think the class of 
 gentleman will long last in England ? " 

 " what do you call gentleman ? the aristocracy by birth ? - the 
  gentilshomme ? " 

 " Nay , I suppose no law can take away a man 's ancestor , and a class of 
 well - bear man be not to be exterminate . but a mere class of well - bear 
 man - without duty , responsibility , or sentiment of that which 
 become good birth in devotion to country or individual honour - do no 
 good to a nation . it be a misfortune which statesman of democratic creed 
 ought to recognize , that the class of the well - bear can not be destroy : 
 it must remain as it remain in Rome and remain in France , after all 
 effort to extirpate it , as the most dangerous class of citizen when 
 you deprive it of the attribute which make it the most serviceable . 
 I be not speak of that class ; I speak of that unclassified order 
 peculiar to England , which , no doubt , form itself originally from 
 the ideal standard of honour and truth suppose to be maintain by the 
  gentilshomme , or well - bear , no long require pedigree and acre to 
 confer upon its member the designation of gentleman ; and when I hear 
 a ' gentleman ' say that he have no option but to think one thing and say 
 another , at whatever risk to his country , I feel as if in the progress 
 of the age the class of gentleman be about to be supersede by some 
 fine development of specie . " 

 Therewith Kenelm rise , and would have take his departure , if Gordon have 
 not seize his hand and detain he . 

 " my dear cousin , if I may so call you , " he say , with the frank manner 
 which be usual to he , and which suit well the bold expression of his 
 face and the clear ring of his voice , " I be one of those who , from an 
 over - dislike to sentimentality and can not , often make those not intimately 
 acquaint with they think bad of their principle than they deserve . 
 it may be quite true that a man who go with his party dislike the 
 measure he feel bind to support , and say so openly when among 
 friend and relation , yet that man be not therefore devoid of loyalty 
 and honour ; and I trust , when you know I well , you will not think it 
 likely I should derogate from that class of gentleman to which we both 
 belong . " 

 " pardon I if I seem rude , " answer Kenelm ; " ascribe it to my 
 ignorance of the necessity of public life . it strike I that where a 
 politician think a thing evil , he ought not to support it as good . but 
 I dare say I be mistaken . " 

 " entirely mistaken , " say miver , " and for this reason : in politic 
 formerly there be a direct choice between good and evil . that rarely 
 exist now . man of high education , have to choose whether to accept or 
 reject a measure force upon their option by constituent body of very 
 low education , be call upon to weigh evil against evil , - the evil of 
 accept or the evil of reject ; and if they resolve on the first , it 
 be as the less evil of the two . " 

 " your definition be perfect , " say Gordon , " and I be content to rest 
 on it my excuse for what my cousin deem insincerity . " 

 " I suppose that be real life , " say Kenelm , with his mournful smile . 

 " of course it be , " say miver . 

 " every day I live , " sigh Kenelm , ' still more confirm my conviction 
 that real life be a phantasmal sham . how absurd it be in philosopher to 
 deny the existence of apparition ! what apparition we , live man , must 
 seem to the ghost ! 


  " ' the spirit of the wise 
  Sit in the cloud and mock we . ' " 



 CHAPTER VI . 

 CHILLINGLY GORDON do not fail to confirm his acquaintance with Kenelm . 
 he very often look in upon he of a morning , sometimes join he 
 in his afternoon ride , introduce he to man of his own set who be 
 mostly busy member of Parliament , rise barrister , or political 
 journalist , but not without a proportion of brilliant idler , - club 
 man , sporting man , man of fashion , rank , and fortune . he do so with a 
 purpose , for these person speak well of he , - speak well not only 
 of his talent , but of his honourable character . his general nickname 
 amongst they be " HONEST GORDON . " Kenelm at first think this sobriquet 
 must be ironical ; not a bit of it . it be give to he on account of 
 the candour and boldness with which he express opinion embody that 
 sort of cynicism which be vulgarly call " the absence of humbug . " the 
 man be certainly no hypocrite ; he affect no belief which he do not 
 entertain . and he have very few belief in anything , except the first 
 half of the adage , " every man for himself , - and God for we all . " 

 but whatever Chillingly Gordon 's theoretical disbelief in thing which 
 make the current creed of the virtuous , there be nothing in his conduct 
 which evince predilection for vice : he be strictly upright in all 
 his dealing , and in delicate matter of honour be a favourite umpire 
 amongst his coeval . though so frankly ambitious , no one could accuse 
 he of attempt to climb on the shoulder of patron . there be 
 nothing servile in his nature ; and , though he be perfectly prepared to 
 bribe elector if necessary , no money could have buy himself . his one 
 master - passion be the desire of power . he sneer at patriotism as a 
 wear - out prejudice , at philanthropy as a sentimental catch - word . he do 
 not want to serve his country , but to rule it . he do not want to 
 raise mankind , but to rise himself . he be therefore unscrupulous , 
 unprincipled , as hungerer after power for itself too often be ; yet 
 still if he get power he would probably use it well , from the clearness 
 and strength of his mental perception . the impression he make on Kenelm 
 may be see in the following letter : - 


 to SIR PETER CHILLINGLY , BART . , ETC . 

 MY DEAR FATHER , - you and my dear mother will be pleased to hear that 
 London continue very polite to I : that " arida nutrix leonum " enroll 
 I among the pet class of lion which lady of fashion admit into the 
 society of their lapdog . it be somewhere about six year since I be 
 allow to gaze on this peep - show through the loophole of Mr. Welby 's 
 retreat . it appear to I , perhaps erroneously , that even within that 
 short space of time the tone of ' society " be perceptibly change . that 
 the change be for the well be an assertion I leave to those who belong 
 to the progressista party . 

 I do not   think nearly so many young lady six year ago paint their 
 eyelid and dye their hair : a few of they there might be , imitator of 
 the slang invent by schoolboy and circulate through the medium of 
 small novelist ; they might use such expression as ' stunning , " " cheek , " 
 " awfully jolly , " etc . but now I find a great many who have advance to 
 a slang beyond that of verbal expression , - a slang of mind , a slang 
 of sentiment , a slang in which very little seem leave of the woman and 
 nothing at all of the lady . 

 Newspaper essayist assert that the young man of the day be to blame 
 for this ; that the young man like it ; and the fair husband - angler dress 
 their fly in the colour most likely to attract a nibble . whether this 
 excuse be the true one I can not pretend to judge ; but it strike I that 
 the man about my own age who affect to be fast be a more languid race 
 than the man from ten to twenty year old , whom they regard as slow . 
 the habit of dram - drinking in the morning be a very new idea , an idea 
 greatly in fashion at the moment . Adonis call for a " pick - me - up " before 
 he have strength enough to answer a billet - doux from Venus . Adonis 
 have not the strength to get nobly drunk , but his delicate constitution 
 require stimulant , and he be always tipple . 

 the man of high birth or renown for social success belong , my 
 dear father , to your time , be still distinguish by an air of good 
 breeding , by a style of conversation more or less polished and not 
 without evidence of literary culture , from man of the same rank in 
 my generation , who appear to pride themselves on respect nobody and 
 know nothing , not even grammar . still we be assure that the world 
 go on steadily improve . that new idea be in full vigour . 

 society in the concrete have become wonderfully conceited as to its 
 own progressive excellence , and the individual who form the concrete 
 entertain the same complacent opinion of themselves . there be , of 
 course , even in my brief and imperfect experience , many exception to 
 what appear to I the prevalent characteristic of the rise generation 
 in ' society . " of these exception I must content myself with name the 
 most remarkable . Place aux dames , the first I name be Cecilia Travers . 
 she and her father be now in town , and I meet they frequently . I 
 can conceive no civilized era in the world which a woman like Cecilia 
 Travers would not grace and adorn , because she be essentially the type 
 of woman as man like to imagine woman ; namely , on the fair side of 
 the womanly character . and I say " woman " rather than " girl , " because 
 among " girl of the Period " Cecilia Travers can not be class . you might 
 call her damsel , virgin , maiden , but you could no more call her girl 
 than you could call a well - bear french demoiselle fille . she be 
 handsome enough to please the eye of any man , however fastidious , but 
 not that kind of beauty which dazzle all man too much to fascinate one 
 man ; for - speak , thank Heaven , from mere theory - I apprehend that the 
 love for woman have in it a strong sense of property ; that one require 
 to individualize one 's possession as be wholly one 's own , and not 
 a possession which all the public be invite to admire . I can readily 
 understand how a rich man , who have what be call a show place , in which 
 the splendid room and the stately garden be open to all inspector , 
 so that he have no privacy in his own demesne , run away to a pretty 
 cottage which he have all to himself , and of which he can say , " this be 
 home ; this be all mine . " 

 but there be some kind of beauty which be eminently show 
 place , - which the public think they have as much a right to admire as 
 the owner have ; and the show place itself would be dull and perhaps fall 
 out of repair , if the public could be exclude from the sight of it . 

 the beauty of Cecilia Travers be not that of a show place . there be a 
 feeling of safety in she . if Desdemona have be like she , Othello would 
 not have be jealous . but then Cecilia would not have deceive she 
 father ; nor I think have tell a blackamoor that she wish " Heaven 
 have make she such a man . " her mind harmonize with her person : it be 
 a companionable mind . her talent be not showy , but , take they 
 altogether , they form a pleasant whole : she have good sense enough in 
 the practical affair of life , and enough of that ineffable womanly gift 
 call tact to counteract the effect of whimsical nature like mine , 
 and yet enough sense of the humouristic view of life not to take too 
 literally all that a whimsical man like myself may say . as to temper , 
 one never know what a woman 's temper be - till one put she out of it . 
 but I imagine her , in its normal state , to be serene , and dispose to 
 be cheerful . now , my dear father , if you be not one of the clever 
 of man you would infer from this eulogistic mention of Cecilia Travers 
 that I be in love with she . but you no doubt will detect the truth that 
 a man in love with a woman do not weigh her merit with so steady a 
 hand as that which guide this steel pen . I be not in love with Cecilia 
 Travers . I wish I be . when Lady Glenalvon , who remain wonderfully 
 kind to I , say , day after day , " Cecilia Travers would make you a 
 perfect wife , " I have no answer to give ; but I do not   feel the least 
 inclined to ask Cecilia Travers if she would waste her perfection on one 
 who so coldly concede it . 

 I find that she persist in reject the man whom her father wish 
 she to marry , and that he have console himself by marry somebody 
 else . no doubt other suitor as worthy will soon present themselves . 

 oh , dearest of all my friend , - sole friend whom I regard as a 
 confidant , - shall I ever be in love ? and if not , why not ? sometimes 
 I feel as if , with love as with ambition , it be because I have some 
 impossible ideal in each , that I must always remain indifferent to the 
 sort of love and the sort of ambition which be within my reach . I have 
 an idea that if I do love , I should love as intensely as Romeo , and 
 that think inspire I with vague foreboding of terror ; and if I 
 do find an object to arouse my ambition , I could be as earnest in its 
 pursuit as - whom shall I name ? - Caesar or Cato ? I like Cato 's 
 ambition the well of the two . but people nowadays call ambition an 
 impracticable crotchet , if it be invest on the lose side . Cato would 
 have save Rome from the mob and the dictator ; but Rome could not be 
 save , and Cato fall on his own sword . have we a Cato now , the verdict 
 at a coroner 's inquest would be , ' suicide while in a state of unsound 
 mind ; " and the verdict would have be prove by his senseless 
 resistance to a mob and a dictator ! talk of ambition , I come to the 
 other exception to the youth of the day ; I have name a demoiselle , I 
 now name a damoiseau . imagine a man of about five - and - twenty , and who 
 be morally about fifty year old than a healthy man of sixty , - imagine 
 he with the brain of age and the flower of youth ; with a heart absorb 
 into the brain , and give warm blood to frigid idea : a man who sneer 
 at everything I call lofty , yet would do nothing that he think mean ; to 
 whom vice and virtue be as indifferent as they be to the Aesthetics 
 of Goethe ; who would never jeopardize his career as a practical reasoner 
 by an imprudent virtue , and never sully his reputation by a degrading 
 vice . imagine this man with an intellect keen , strong , ready , 
 unscrupulous , dauntless , - all cleverness and no genius . imagine this 
 man , and then do not be astonish when I tell you he be a Chillingly . 

 the Chillingly race culminate in he , and become Chillinglyest . in 
 fact , it seem to I that we live in a day precisely suited to the 
 Chillingly idiosyncrasy . during the ten century or more that our 
 race have hold local habitation and a name , it have be as airy nothing . 
 its representative live in hot - blooded time , and be compel to 
 skulk in still water with their emblematic dace . but the time now , my 
 dear father , be so cold - blooded that you can not   be too cold - blooded to 
 prosper . what could chillingly Mivers have be in an age when people 
 care twopence - halfpenny about their religious creed , and their 
 political party deem their cause be sacred and their leader be 
 hero ? chillingly Mivers would not have find five subscriber to 
 " the Londoner . " but now " the Londoner " be the favourite organ of the 
 intellectual public ; it sneer away all the foundation of the social 
 system , without an attempt at reconstruction ; and every new journal set 
 up , if it keep its head above water , model itself on " the Londoner . " 
 chillingly Mivers be a great man , and the most potent writer of the age , 
 though nobody know what he have write . chillingly Gordon be a still 
 more notable instance of the rise of the Chillingly worth in the modern 
 market . 

 there be a general impression in the most authoritative circle that 
 chillingly Gordon will have high rank in the van of the come man . his 
 confidence in himself be so thorough that it infect all with whom he 
 come into contact , - myself include . 

 he say to I the other day , with a sang - froid worthy of the icy 
 Chillingly , " I mean to be Prime Minister of England : it be only a 
 question of time . " now , if chillingly Gordon be to be Prime Minister , it 
 will be because the increase cold of our moral and social atmosphere 
 will exactly suit the development of his talent . 

 he be the man above all other to argue down the declaimer of 
 old - fashioned sentimentality , - love of country , care for its position 
 among nation , zeal for its honour , pride in its renown .   oh , if 
 you could hear he philosophically and logically sneer away the word 
 " prestige " !   such notion be fast be classify as " bosh . " and 
 when that classification be complete , - when England have no colony to 
 defend , no navy to pay for , no interest in the affair of other nation , 
 and have attain to the happy condition of Holland , - then Chillingly 
 Gordon will be her Prime Minister . 

 yet while , if ever I be sting into political action , it will be by 
 abnegation of the Chillingly attribute , and in opposition , however 
 hopeless , to Chillingly Gordon , I feel that this man can not be 
 suppress , and ought to have fair play ; his ambition will be infinitely 
 more dangerous if it become sour by delay . I propose , my dear father , 
 that you should have the honour of lay this clever kinsman under 
 an obligation , and enable he to enter Parliament . in our last 
 conversation at Exmundham , you tell I of the frank resentment of Gordon 
  pere , when my come into the world shut he out from the Exmundham 
 inheritance ; you confide to I your intention at that time to lay 
 by yearly a sum that might ultimately serve as a provision for Gordon 
  fil , and as some compensation for the loss of his expectation when 
 you realize your hope of an heir ; you tell I also how this generous 
 intention on your part have be frustrate by a natural indignation at 
 the eld Gordon 's conduct in his harass and costly litigation , and 
 by the addition you have be tempt to make to the estate in a purchase 
 which add to its acreage , but at a rate of interest which diminish 
 your own income , and preclude the possibility of further saving . now , 
 chance to meet your lawyer , Mr. Vining , the other day , I learn from 
 he that it have be long a wish which your delicacy prevent your 
 naming to I , that I , to whom the fee - simple descend , should join with 
 you in cut off the entail and resettle the estate . he show I 
 what an advantage this would be to the property , because it would leave 
 your hand free for many improvement in which I heartily go with the 
 progress of the age , for which , as merely tenant for life , you could not 
 raise the money except upon ruinous term ; new cottage for labourer , 
 new building for tenant , the consolidation of some old mortgage and 
 charge on the rent - roll , etc . and allow I to add that I should like 
 to make a large increase to the jointure of my dear mother . vining say , 
 too , that there be a part of the outlying land which , as be near a 
 town , could be sell to considerable profit if the estate be resettle . 

 let we hasten to complete the necessary deed , and so obtain the L20,000 
 require for the realization of your noble and , let I add , your just 
 desire to do something for Chillingly Gordon . in the new deed of 
 settlement we could insure the power of will the estate as we 
 please , and I be strongly against devise it to Chillingly Gordon . 
 it may be a crotchet of mine , but one which I think you share , that the 
 owner of english soil should have a son 's love for the native land , and 
 Gordon will never have that . I think , too , that it will be good for his 
 own career , and for the establishment of a frank understanding between 
 we and himself , that he should be fairly tell that he would not be 
 benefit in the event of our death . twenty thousand pound give to he 
 now would be a great boon to he than ten time the sum twenty year 
 later . with that at his command , he can enter Parliament , and have an 
 income , add to what he now possess , if modest , still sufficient to 
 make he independent of a minister 's patronage . 

 pray humour I , my dear father , in the proposition I venture to 
 submit to you . 

  your affectionate son , KENELM . 


 from SIR PETER CHILLINGLY to KENELM CHILLINGLY . 

 my DEAR BOY , - you be not worthy to be a Chillingly ; you be decidedly 
 warm - blooded : never be a load lift off a man 's mind with a gentler 
 hand . yes , I have wish to cut off the entail and resettle the 
 property ; but , as it be eminently to my advantage to do so , I shrink 
 from ask it , though eventually it would be almost as much to your own 
 advantage . what with the purchase I make of the Faircleuch land - which 
 I could only effect by money borrow at high interest on my personal 
 security , and pay off by yearly instalment , eat largely into 
 income - and the old mortgage , etc . , I own I have be pinch of late 
 year . but what rejoice I the most be the power to make home for our 
 honest labourer more comfortable , and near to their work , which last 
 be the chief point , for the old cottage in themselves be not bad ; the 
 misfortune be , when you build an extra room for the child , the silly 
 people let it out to a lodger . 

 my dear boy , I be very much touch by your wish to increase your 
 mother 's jointure , - a very proper wish , independently of filial feeling , 
 for she bring to the estate a very pretty fortune , which , the trustee 
 consent to my investing in land ; and though the land complete our 
 ring - fence , it do not bring in two per cent , and the condition of 
 the entail limit the right of jointure to an amount below that which a 
 widow Lady Chillingly may fairly expect . 

 I care more about the provision on these point than I do for the 
 interest of old Chillingly Gordon 's son . I have mean to behave very 
 handsomely to the father ; and when the return for behave handsomely 
 be be put into Chancery - A Worm will turn . nevertheless , I agree with 
 you that a son should not be punish for his father 's fault ; and , if 
 the sacrifice of L20,000 make you and myself feel that we be well 
 Christians and truer gentleman , we shall buy that feeling very cheaply . 


 Sir Peter then proceed , half jestingly , half seriously , to combat 
 Kenelm 's declaration that he be not in love with Cecilia Travers ; and , 
 urge the advantage of marriage with one whom Kenelm allow would be 
 a perfect wife , astutely remark that unless Kenelm have a son of his 
 own it do not seem to he quite just to the next of kin to will the 
 property from he , upon no well plea than the want of love for his 
 native country . " he would love his country fast enough if he have 10,000 
 acre in it . " 

 Kenelm shake his head when he come to this sentence . 

 " be even then love for one 's country but cupboard - love after all ? " say 
 he ; and he postpone finish the perusal of his father 's letter . 



 CHAPTER VII . 

 KENELM CHILLINGLY do not exaggerate the social position he have acquire 
 when he class himself amongst the lion of the fashionable world . I 
 dare not count the number of three - cornered note shower upon he by 
 the fine lady who grow romantic upon any kind of celebrity ; or the 
 carefully seal envelope , contain letter from fair Anonymas , who 
 ask if he have a heart , and would be in such a place in the Park at 
 such an hour . what there be in Kenelm Chillingly that should make he 
 thus favour , especially by the fair sex , it would be difficult to say , 
 unless it be the two - fold reputation of be unlike other people , and 
 of be unaffectedly indifferent to the gain of any reputation at all . 
 he might , have he so pleased , have easily establish a proof that 
 the prevalent though vague belief in his talent be not altogether 
 unjustified . for the article he have send from abroad to " the Londoner " 
 and by which his travel expense be defray , have be stamp 
 by that sort of originality in tone and treatment which rarely fail to 
 excite curiosity as to the author , and meet with more general praise 
 than perhaps it deserve . 

 but Mivers be true to his contract to preserve inviolable the incognito 
 of the author , and Kenelm regard with profound contempt the article 
 themselves and the reader who praise they . 

 just as misanthropy with some person grow out of benevolence 
 disappoint , so there be certain nature - and Kenelm Chillingly 's be 
 perhaps one of they - in which indifferentism grow out of earnestness 
 baffle . 

 he have promise himself pleasure in renew acquaintance with his old 
 tutor , Mr. Welby , - pleasure in refresh his own taste for metaphysic 
 and casuistry and criticism . but that accomplished professor of realism 
 have retire from philosophy altogether , and be now enjoy a holiday 
 for life in the business of a public office . a minister in favour of 
 whom , when in opposition , Mr. Welby , in a moment of whim , write some 
 very able article in a lead journal , have , on accede to power , 
 present the realist with one of those few good thing still leave to 
 ministerial patronage , - a place worth about L1,200 a year . his morning 
 thus engage in routine work , Mr. Welby enjoy his evening in a 
 convivial way . 

 " inveni portum , " he say to Kenelm ; " I plunge into no troubled water 
 now . but come and dine with I to - morrow , tete - a - tete . my wife be at 
 St. Leonard 's with my youngest bear for the benefit of sea - air . " Kenelm 
 accept the invitation . 

 the dinner would have content a Brillat - Savarin : it be faultless ; and 
 the claret be that rare nectar , the Lafitte of 1848 . 

 " I never share this , " say Welby , " with more than one friend at a time . " 

 Kenelm seek to engage his host in discussion on certain new work in 
 vogue , and which be compose accord to purely realistic canon of 
 criticism . " the more realistic ; these book pretend to be , the less 
 real they be , " say Kenelm . " I be half inclined to think that the whole 
 school you so systematically seek to build up be a mistake , and that 
 realism in art be a thing impossible . " 

 " I dare say you be right . I take up that school in earnest because I 
 be in a passion with pretender to the Idealistic school ; and whatever 
 one take up in earnest be generally a mistake , especially if one be in 
 a passion . I be not in earnest and I be not in a passion when I write 
 those article to which I be indebted for my office . " Mr. Welby here 
 luxuriously stretch his limb , and lift his glass to his lip , 
 voluptuously inhale its bouquet . 

 " you sadden I , " return Kenelm . " it be a melancholy thing to find that 
 one 's mind be influence in youth by a teacher who mock at his own 
 teaching . " 

 Welby shrug his shoulder . " life consist in the alternate process of 
 learning and unlearning ; but it be often wise to unlearn than to learn . 
 for the rest , as I have cease to be a critic , I care little whether I 
 be wrong or right when I play that part . I think I be right now as a 
 placeman . let the world go its own way , provide the world let you live 
 upon it . I drain my wine to the lee , and cut down hope to the brief 
 span of life . reject realism in art if you please , and accept realism in 
 conduct . for the first time in my life I be comfortable : my mind , have 
 wear out its walking - shoe , be now enjoy the luxury of slipper . who 
 can deny the realism of comfort ? " 

 " have a man a right , " Kenelm say to himself , as he enter his brougham , 
 " to employ all the brilliancy of a rare wit , all the acquisition of as 
 rare a scholarship , to the scaring of the young generation out of the 
 safe old road which youth leave to itself would take , - old road skirt 
 by romantic river and bowery tree , - direct they into new path on 
 long sandy flat , and then , when they be faint and footsore , to tell 
 they that he care not a pin whether they have wear out their shoe in 
 right path or wrong path , for that he have attain the summum bonum 
 of philosophy in the comfort of easy slipper ? " 

 before he could answer the question he thus put to himself , his brougham 
 stop at the door of the minister whom Welby have contribute to bring 
 into power . 

 that night there be a crowded muster of the fashionable world at the 
 great man 's house . it happen to be a very critical moment for the 
 minister . the fate of his cabinet depend on the result of a motion 
 about to be make the following week in the House of Commons . the great 
 man stand at the entrance of the apartment to receive his guest , and 
 among the guest be the framer of the hostile motion and the leader 
 of the opposition . his smile be not less gracious to they than to his 
 dear friend and stanch supporter . 

 " I suppose this be realism , " say Kenelm to himself ; " but it be not 
 truth , and it be not comfort . " lean against the wall near the 
 doorway , he contemplate with grave interest the strike countenance 
 of his distinguished host . he detect beneath that courteous smile 
 and that urbane manner the sign of care . the eye be absent , the cheek 
 pinch , the brow furrow . Kenelm turn away his look , and glance 
 over the animate countenance of the idle lounger along commoner 
 thoroughfare in life . their eye be not absent ; their brow be 
 not furrow ; their mind seem quite at home in exchange nothing . 
 interest many of they have in the approach struggle , but it be 
 much such an interest as better of small sum may have on the Derby 
 day , - just enough to give piquancy to the race ; nothing to make gain a 
 great joy , or loss a keen anguish . 

 " our host be look ill , " say Mivers , accost Kenelm . " I detect 
 symptom of suppress gout . you know my aphorism , ' nothing so gouty as 
 ambition , ' especially parliamentary ambition . " 

 " you be not one of those friend who press on my choice of life that 
 source of disease ; allow I to thank you . " 

 " your thank be misplace . I strongly advise you to devote yourself to 
 a political career . " 

 " despite the gout ? " 

 " despite the gout . if you could take the world as I do , my advice might 
 be different . but your mind be overcrowded with doubt and fantasy and 
 crotchet , and you have no choice but to give they vent in active life . " 

 " you have something to do in make I what I be , - an idler ; something 
 to answer for as to my doubt , fantasy , and crotchet . it be by your 
 recommendation that I be place under the tuition of Mr. Welby , and at 
 that critical age in which the bent of the twig form the shape of the 
 tree . " 

 " and I pride myself on that counsel . I repeat the reason for which I 
 give it : it be an incalculable advantage for a young man to start in 
 life thoroughly initiate into the New Ideas which will more or less 
 influence his generation . Welby be the able representative of these 
 idea . it be a wondrous good fortune when the propagandist of the 
 New Ideas be something more than a bookish philosopher , - when he be 
 a thorough ' man of the world , ' and be what we emphatically call 
 ' practical . ' yes , you owe I much that I secure to you such tuition , 
 and save you from twaddle and sentiment , the poetry of Wordsworth and 
 the muscular Christianity of Cousin John . " 

 " what you say that you save I from might have do I more good than 
 all you confer on I . I suspect that when education succeed 
 in place an old head upon young shoulder the combination be not 
 healthful : it clog the blood and slacken the pulse . however , I must 
 not be ungrateful ; you mean kindly . yes , I suppose Welby be practical : 
 he have no belief , and he have get a place . but our host , I presume , be 
 also practical ; his place be a much high one than Welby 's , and yet he 
 be surely not without belief ? " 

 " he be bear before the new idea come into practical force ; but 
 in proportion as they have do so , his belief have necessarily 
 disappear . I do not   suppose that he believe in much now , except the 
 two proposition : firstly , that if he accept the new idea he will have 
 power and keep it , and if he do not accept they power be out of the 
 question ; and , secondly , that if the new idea be to prevail he be the 
 good man to direct they safely , - belief quite enough for a minister . no 
 wise minister should have more . " 

 " do he not believe that the motion he be to resist next week be a bad 
 one ? " 

 " a bad one of course , in its consequence , for if it succeed it will 
 upset he ; a good one in itself I be sure he must think it , for he would 
 bring it on himself if he be in opposition . " 

 " I see that Pope 's definition be still true , ' party be the madness of 
 the many for the gain of the few . ' " 

 " no , it be not true . madness be a wrong word apply to the many : the 
 many be sane enough ; they know their own object , and they make use of 
 the intellect of the few in order to gain their object . in each party 
 it be the many that control the few who nominally lead they . a man 
 become Prime Minister because he seem to the many of his party the 
 fit person to carry out their view . if he presume to differ from 
 these view , they put he into a moral pillory , and pelt he with their 
 dirty stone and their rottenest egg . " 

 " then the maxim should be reverse , and party be rather the madness of 
 the few for the gain of the many ? 

 " of the two , that be the more correct definition . " 

 " let I keep my sens and decline to be one of the few . " 

 Kenelm move away from his cousin 's side , and enter one of the less 
 crowd room , see Cecilia Travers seat there in a recess with Lady 
 Glenalvon . he join they , and after a brief interchange of a few 
 commonplace , Lady Glenalvon quit her post to accost a foreign 
 ambassadress , and Kenelm sink into the chair she vacate . 

 it be a relief to his eye to contemplate Cecilia 's candid brow ; to 
 his ear to hearken to the soft voice that have no artificial tone , and 
 utter no cynical witticism . 

 " do not   you think it strange , " say Kenelm , " that we English should so 
 mould all our habit as to make even what we call pleasure as little 
 pleasurable as possible ? we be now in the beginning of June , the fresh 
 outburst of summer , when every day in the country be a delight to eye 
 and ear , and we say , ' the season for hot room be begin . ' we alone 
 of civilized race spend our summer in a capital , and cle to the 
 country when the tree be leafless and the brooks freeze . " 

 " certainly that be a mistake ; but I love the country in all season , 
 even in winter . " 

 " provide the country house be full of London people ? " 

 " No ; that be rather a drawback . I never want companion in the country . " 

 " true ; I should have remember that you differ from young lady in 
 general , and make companion of book . they be always more conversable 
 in the country than they be in town ; or rather , we listen there to they 
 with less distracted attention . ha ! do I not recognize yonder the fair 
 whisker of George Belvoir ? who be the lady lean on his arm ? " 

 " do not   you know ? - Lady Emily Belvoir , his wife . " 

 " ah ! I be tell that he have marry . the lady be handsome . she will 
 become the family diamond . do she read Blue - book ? " 

 " I will ask she if you wish . " 

 " Nay , it be scarcely worth while . during my ramble abroad I see but 
 few english newspaper . I do , however , learn that George have win his 
 election . have he yet speak in Parliament ? " 

 " yes ; he move the answer to the Address this session , and be much 
 compliment on the excellent tone and taste of his speech . he speak 
 again a few week afterwards , I fear not so successfully . " 

 " cough down ? " 

 " something like it . " 

 " do he good ; he will recover the cough , and fulfil my prophecy of his 
 success . " 

 " have you do with poor George for the present ? if so , allow I to ask 
 whether you have quite forget Will Somers and Jessie Wiles ? " 

 " forgotten they ! no . " 

 " but you have never ask after they ? " 

 " I take it for grant that they be as happy as could be expect . 
 pray assure I that they be . " 

 " I trust so now ; but they have have trouble , and have leave Graveleigh . " 

 " Trouble ! leave Graveleigh ! you make I uneasy . pray explain . " 

 " they have not be three month marry and instal in the home they 
 owe to you , when poor will be seize with a rheumatic fever . he be 
 confine to his bed for many week ; and , when at last he could move from 
 it , be so weak as to be still unable to do any work . during his illness 
 Jessie have no heart and little leisure to attend to the shop . of course 
 i - that be , my dear father - give they all necessary assistance ; but - " 

 " I understand ; they be reduce to object of charity . Brute that I be , 
 never to have think of the duty I owe to the couple I have bring 
 together . but pray go on . " 

 " you be aware that just before you leave we my father receive a 
 proposal to exchange his property at Graveleigh for some land more 
 desirable to he ? " 

 " I remember . he close with that offer . " 

 " yes ; Captain Stavers , the new landlord of Graveleigh , seem to be 
 a very bad man ; and though he could not turn the Somerses out of the 
 cottage so long as they pay rent , which we take care they do pay , - yet 
 out of a very wicked spite he set up a rival shop in one of his other 
 cottage in the village , and it become impossible for these poor young 
 people to get a livelihood at Graveleigh . " 

 " what excuse for spite against so harmless a young couple could Captain 
 Stavers find or invent ? " 

 Cecilia look down and colour . " it be a revengeful feeling against 
 Jessie . " 

 " ah , I comprehend . " 

 " but they have now leave the village , and be happily settle elsewhere . 
 will have recover his health , and they be prosper much more than 
 they could ever have do at Graveleigh . " 

 " in that change you be their benefactress , Miss Travers ? " say Kenelm , 
 in a more tender voice and with a soft eye than he have ever before 
 evince towards the heiress . 

 " no , it be not I whom they have to thank and bless . " 

 " who , then , be it ? your father ? " 

 " no . do not question I . I be bind not to say . they do not themselves 
 know ; they rather believe that their gratitude be due to you . " 

 " to I ! be I to be forever a sham in spite of myself ? my dear Miss 
 Travers , it be essential to my honour that I should undeceive this 
 credulous pair ; where can I find they ? " 

 " I must not say ; but I will ask permission of their conceal 
 benefactor , and send you their address . " 

 a touch be lay on Kenelm 's arm , and a voice whisper , " may I ask you 
 to present I to Miss Travers ? " 

 " Miss Travers , " say Kenelm , " I entreat you to add to the list of your 
 acquaintance a cousin of mine , - Mr. Chillingly Gordon . " 

 while Gordon address to Cecilia the well - breed conventionalism with 
 which acquaintance in London drawing - room usually commence , Kenelm , 
 obedient to a sign from Lady Glenalvon , who have just re - enter the 
 room , quit his seat , and join the marchioness . 

 " be not that young man whom you leave talk with Miss Travers your 
 clever cousin Gordon ? " 

 " the same . " 

 " she be listen to he with great attention . how his face brighten up 
 as he talk ! he be positively handsome , thus animate . " 

 " yes , I could fancy he a dangerous wooer . he have wit and liveliness and 
 audacity ; he could be very much in love with a great fortune , and talk 
 to the owner of it with a fervour rarely exhibit by a Chillingly . 
 well , it be no affair of mine . " 

 " it ought to be . " 

 Alas and alas ! that " ought to be ; " what depth of sorrowful meaning lie 
 within that simple phrase ! how happy would be our life , how grand our 
 action , how pure our soul , if all could be with we as it ought to be ! 



 CHAPTER VIII . 

 we often form cordial intimacy in the confine society of a country 
 house , or a quiet watering - place , or a small Continental town , which 
 fade away into remote acquaintanceship in the mighty vortex of London 
 life , neither party be to blame for the estrangement . it be so with 
 Leopold Travers and Kenelm Chillingly . traver , as we have see , have 
 feel a powerful charm in the converse of the young stranger , so in 
 contrast with the routine of the rural companionship to which his alert 
 intellect have for many year circumscribe its range . but on reappear 
 in London the season before Kenelm again meet he , he have renew old 
 friendship with man of his own standing , - officer in the regiment of 
 which he have once be a popular ornament , some of they still unmarried , 
 a few of they like himself widow , other who have be his rival 
 in fashion , and be still pleasant idler about town ; and it rarely 
 happen in a metropolis that we have intimate friendship with those of 
 another generation , unless there be some common tie in the cultivation 
 of art and letter , or the action of kindre sympathy in the party 
 strife of politic . therefore Travers and Kenelm have have little familiar 
 communication with each other since they first meet at the Beaumanoirs ' . 
 now and then they find themselves at the same crowded assembly , and 
 interchanged nod and salutation . but their habit be different ; the 
 house at which they be intimate be not the same , neither do they 
 frequent the same club . Kenelm 's chief bodily exercise be still that 
 of long and early ramble into rural suburb ; Leopold 's be that of 
 a late ride in the row . of the two , Leopold be much more the man of 
 pleasure . once restore to metropolitan life , a temper constitutionally 
 eager , ardent , and convivial take kindly , as in early youth , to its 
 light range of enjoyment . 

 have the intercourse between the two man be as frankly familiar as it 
 have be at Neesdale Park , Kenelm would probably have see much more of 
 Cecilia at her own home ; and the admiration and esteem with which she 
 already inspire he might have ripen into much warm feeling , have 
 he thus be bring into clear comprehension of the soft and womanly 
 heart , and its tender predisposition towards himself . 

 he have say somewhat vaguely in his letter to Sir Peter , that ' sometimes 
 he feel as if his indifference to love , as to ambition , be because he 
 have some impossible ideal in each . " take that conjecture to task , 
 he could not honestly persuade himself that he have form any ideal of 
 woman and wife with which the reality of Cecilia Travers be at war . on 
 the contrary , the more he think over the characteristic of Cecilia , 
 the more they seem to correspond to any ideal that have float before 
 he in the twilight of dreamy revery ; and yet he know that he be not 
 in love with she , that his heart do not respond to his reason ; and 
 mournfully he resign himself to the conviction that nowhere in 
 this planet , from the normal pursuit of whose inhabitant he feel so 
 estranged , be there wait for he the smile playmate , the earnest 
 helpmate . as this conviction strengthen , so an increase weariness 
 of the artificial life of the metropolis , and of all its object and 
 amusement , turn his thought with an intense yearning towards the 
 Bohemian freedom and fresh excitement of his foot rambling . he often 
 think with envy of the wandering minstrel , and wonder whether , if he 
 again traverse the same range of country , he might encounter again that 
 vagrant singer . 



 CHAPTER IX . 

 it be nearly a week since Kenelm have meet Cecilia , and he be sit 
 in his room with Lord Thetford at that hour of three in the afternoon 
 which be find the most difficult to dispose of by idler about town . 
 amongst young man of his own age and class with whom Kenelm assort in 
 the fashionable world , perhaps the one whom he like the good , and of 
 whom he see the most , be this young heir of the Beaumanoirs ; and though 
 Lord Thetford have nothing to do with the direct stream of my story , it 
 be worth pause a few minute to sketch an outline of one of the 
 good whom the last generation have produce for a part that , owe to 
 accident of birth and fortune , young man like Lord Thetford must play 
 on that stage from which the curtain be not yet draw up . destine to 
 be the head of a family that unite with princely possession and a 
 historical name a keen though honourable ambition for political power , 
 Lord Thetford have be care fully educate , especially in the new idea 
 of his time . his father , though a man of no ordinary talent , have never 
 take a prominent part in public life . he desire his eld son to do 
 so . the Beaumanoirs have be whig from the time of William III . they 
 have share the good and the ill fortune of a party which , whether we 
 side with it or not , no politician who dread extreme in the government 
 of a State so pre - eminently artificial that a prevalent extreme at 
 either end of the balance would be fatal to equilibrium , can desire to 
 become extinct or feeble so long as a constitutional monarchy exist 
 in England . from the reign of George I. to the death of George IV . , the 
 Beaumanoirs be in the ascendant . visit their family portrait gallery , 
 and you must admire the eminence of a house which , during that interval 
 of less than a century , contribute so many man to the service of the 
 State or the adornment of the Court , - so many Ministers , Ambassadors , 
 general , Lord Chamberlains , and Masters of the horse . when the young 
 Pitt beat the great Whig Houses , the Beaumanoirs vanish into comparative 
 obscurity ; they reemerge with the accession of William IV . , and once 
 more produce bulwark of the state and ornament of the Crown . the 
 present Lord of Beaumanoir , poco curante in politic though he be , have 
 at least hold high office at Court ; and , as a matter of course , he be 
 Lord Lieutenant of his county , as well as Knight of the Garter . he be 
 a man whom the chief of his party have be accustom to consult on 
 critical question . he give his opinion confidentially and modestly , 
 and when they be reject never take offence . he think that a time 
 be come when the head of the Beaumanoirs should descend into the list 
 and fight hand - to - hand with any Hodge or Hobson in the cause of his 
 country for the benefit of the Whigs . too lazy or too old to do this 
 himself , he say to his son , " you must do it : without effort of mine the 
 thing may last my life . it need effort of yours that the thing may last 
 through your own . " 

 Lord Thetford cheerfully respond to the paternal admonition . he curb 
 his natural inclination , which be neither inelegant nor unmanly ; for , 
 on the one side , he be very fond of music and painting , an accomplished 
 amateur , and deem a sound connoisseur in both ; and , on the other side , 
 he have a passion for all field sport , and especially for hunting . he 
 allow no such attraction to interfere with diligent attention to the 
 business of the House of Commons . he serve in Committees , he take the 
 chair at public meeting on sanitary question or project for social 
 improvement , and acquit himself well therein . he have not yet speak in 
 debate , but he have only be two year in Parliament , and he take his 
 father 's wise advice not to speak till the third . but he be not without 
 weight among the well - bear youth of the party , and have in he the stuff 
 out of which , when it become season , the corinthian capital of a 
 Cabinet may be very effectively carve . in his own heart he be convince 
 that his party be go too far and too fast ; but with that party he 
 go on light - heartedly , and would continue to do so if they go to 
 Erebus . but he would prefer their go the other way . for the rest , a 
 pleasant , bright - eyed young fellow , with vivid animal spirit ; and , in 
 the holiday moment of reprieve from public duty he bring sunshine into 
 draggle hunting - field , and a fresh breeze into heated ballroom . 

 " my dear fellow , " say Lord Thetford , as he throw aside his cigar , " I 
 quite understand that you bore yourself : you have nothing else to do . " 

 " what can I do ? " 

 " work . " 

 " work ! " 

 " yes , you be clever enough to feel that you have a mind ; and mind be a 
 restless inmate of body : it crave occupation of some sort , and regular 
 occupation too ; it need its daily constitutional exercise . do you give 
 your mind that ? " 

 " I be sure I do not   know , but my mind be always busy itself about 
 something or other . " 

 " in a desultory way , - with no fix object . " 

 " true . " 

 " write a book , and then it will have its constitutional . " 

 " Nay , my mind be always write a book   though it may not publish 
 one , always jot down impression , or invent incident , or 
 investigate character ; and between you and I , I do not think that I 
 do bear myself so much as I do formerly . other people bear I more than 
 they do . " 

 " because you will not create an object in common with other people : come 
 into Parliament , side with a party , and you have that object . " 

 " do you mean seriously to tell I that you be not bored in the House of 
 Commons ? " 

 " with the speaker very often , yes ; but with the strife between the 
 speaker , no . the House of Commons life have a peculiar excitement 
 scarcely understand out of it ; but you may conceive its charm when you 
 observe that a man who have once be in the thick of it feel forlorn 
 and shelve if he lose his seat , and even repine when the accident 
 of birth transfer he to the serener air of the Upper House . try that 
 life , Chillingly . " 

 " I might if I be an ultra - radical , a Republican , a Communist , a 
 Socialist , and wish to upset everything exist , for then the strife 
 would at least be a very earnest one . " 

 " but could not you be equally in earnest against those revolutionary 
 gentleman ? " 

 " be you and your leader in earnest against they ? they do not   appear to 
 I so . " 

 Thetford be silent for a minute . " well , if you doubt the principle of 
 my side , go with the other side . for my part , I and many of our party 
 would be glad to see the Conservatives strong . " 

 " I have no doubt they would . no sensible man like to be carry off his 
 leg by the rush of the crowd behind he ; and a crowd be less headlong 
 when it see a strong force array against it in front . but it seem 
 to I that , at present , Conservatism can but be what it now be , - a party 
 that may combine for resistance , and will not combine for inventive 
 construction . we be live in an age in which the process of 
 unsettlement be go blindly at work , as if impel by a Nemesis as 
 blind as itself . new idea come beat into surf and surge against 
 those which former reasoner have consider as fix bank and 
 breakwater ; and the new idea be so mutable , so fickle , that those 
 which be consider novel ten year ago be deem obsolete to - day , 
 and the new one of to - day will in their turn be obsolete to - morrow . 
 and , in a sort of fatalism , you see statesman yield way to these 
 successive mockery of experiment , - for they be experiment against 
 experience , - and say to each other with a shrug of the shoulder , 
 ' Bismillah ! it must be so ; the country will have it , even though it 
 send the country to the dog . ' I do not   feel sure that the country will 
 not go there the soon , if you can only strengthen the conservative 
 element enough to set it up in office , with the certainty of knock 
 it down again . alas ! I be too dispassionate a looker - on to be fit for a 
 partisan : would I be not ! address yourself to my cousin Gordon . " 

 " ay , Chillingly Gordon be a come man , and have all the earnestness you 
 find absent in party and in yourself . " 

 " you call he earnest ? " 

 " thoroughly , in the pursuit of one object , - the advancement of 
 Chillingly Gordon . if he get into the House of Commons , and succeed 
 there , I hope he will never become my leader ; for if he think 
 Christianity in the way of his promotion , he would bring in a bill for 
 its abolition . " 

 " in that case would he still be your leader ? " 

 " my dear Kenelm , you do not   know what be the spirit of party , and how 
 easily it make excuse for any act of its leader . of course , if Gordon 
 bring in a bill for the abolition of Christianity , it would be on the 
 plea that the abolition be good for the Christians , and his follower 
 would cheer that enlighten sentiment . " 

 " ah , " say Kenelm , with a sigh , " I own myself the dull of blockhead ; 
 for instead of tempt I into the field of party politic , your talk 
 leave I in stolid amaze that you do not take to your heel , where 
 honour can only be save by flight . " 

 " Pooh ! my dear Chillingly , we can not run away from the age in which we 
 live : we must accept its condition and make the good of they ; and if 
 the House of Commons be nothing else , it be a famous debating society 
 and a capital club . think over it . I must leave you now . I be go 
 to see a picture at the exhibition which have be most truculently 
 criticise in ' the Londoner , ' but which I be assure , on good authority , 
 be a work of remarkable merit . I can not   bear to see a man snarl and 
 sneer down , no doubt by jealous rival , who have their influence in 
 journal , so I shall judge of the picture for myself . if it be really 
 as good as I be tell , I shall talk about it to everybody I meet ; and in 
 matter of art I fancy my word go for something . study art , my dear 
 Kenelm . no gentleman 's education be complete if he do not   know a good 
 picture from a bad one . after the exhibition I shall just have time for 
 a canter round the Park before the debate of the session , which begin 
 to - night . " 

 with a light step the young man quit the room , hum an air from 
 the " Figaro " as he descend the stair . from the window Kenelm watch 
 he swing himself with careless grace into his saddle and riding 
 briskly down the street , - in form and face and bear a very model of 
 young , high - bear , high - breed manhood . " the Venetians , " mutter Kenelm , 
 " decapitate Marino Faliero for conspire against his own order , - the 
 noble . the Venetians love their institution , and have faith in they . 
 be there such love and such faith among the English ? " 

 as he thus soliloquize he hear a shrilling sort of squeak ; and a 
 showman station before his window the stage on which Punch satirize 
 the law and morality of the world , " kill the beadle and defy the 
 devil . " 



 CHAPTER X. 

 KENELM turn from the sight of Punch and Punch 's friend the cur , as his 
 servant , enter , say a person from the country , who would not give 
 his name , ask to see he . 

 think it might be some message from his father , Kenelm order the 
 stranger to be admit , and in another minute there enter a young man 
 of handsome countenance and powerful frame , in whom , after a surprised 
 stare , Kenelm recognize Tom Bowles . difficult indeed would have be 
 that recognition to an unobservant beholder : no trace be leave of the 
 sullen bully or the village farrier ; the expression of the face be mild 
 and intelligent , - more bashful than hardy ; the brute strength of the 
 form have lose its former clumsiness , the simple dress be that of a 
 gentleman , - to use an expressive idiom , the whole man be wonderfully 
 " tone down . " 

 " I be afraid , sir , I be take a liberty , " say Tom , rather nervously , 
 twiddle his hat between his finger . 

 " I should be a great friend to liberty than I be if it be always 
 take in the same way , " say Kenelm , with a touch of his saturnine 
 humour ; but then yield at once to the warm impulse of his nature , 
 he grasp his old antagonist 's hand and exclaim , " my dear Tom , you 
 be so welcome . I be so glad to see you . sit down , man ; sit down : make 
 yourself at home . " 

 " I do not know you be back in England , sir , till within the last few 
 day ; for you do say that when you come back I should see or hear from 
 you , " and there be a tone of reproach in the last word . 

 " I be to blame , forgive I , " say Kenelm , remorsefully . " but how do 
 you find I out ? you do not then , I think , even know my name . that , 
 however , it be easy enough to discover ; but who give you my address in 
 this lodging ? " 

 " well , sir , it be Miss Travers ; and she bid I come to you . otherwise , 
 as you do not send for I , it be scarcely my place to call uninvited . " 

 " but , my dear Tom , I never dream that you be in London . one do not 
 ask a man whom one suppose to be more than a hundred mile off to pay 
 one an afternoon call . you be still with your uncle , I presume ? and I 
 need not ask if all thrive well with you : you look a prosperous man , 
 every inch of you , from crown to toe . " 

 " yes , " say Tom ; " thank you kindly , sir , I be do well in the way of 
 business , and my uncle be to give I up the whole concern at Christmas . " 

 while Tom thus speak Kenelm have summon his servant , and order up 
 such refreshment as could be find in the larder of a bachelor in 
 lodging . " and what bring you to town , Tom ? " 

 " Miss Travers write to I about a little business which she be good 
 enough to manage for I , and say you wish to know about it ; and so , 
 after turn it over in my mind for a few day , I resolve to come to 
 town : indeed , " add Tom , heartily , " I do wish to see your face again . " 

 " but you talk riddle . what business of yours could Miss Travers imagine 
 I wish to know about ? " 

 Tom colour high , and look very embarrassed . luckily , the servant 
 here enter with the refreshment - tray allow he time to recover
