 himself . Kenelm help he to a liberal slice of cold pigeon - pie , 
 press wine on he , and do not renew the subject till he think his 
 guest 's tongue be likely to be more freely set loose ; then he say , 
 lay a friendly hand on Tom 's shoulder , " I have be think over 
 what pass between I and Miss Travers . I wish to have the new 
 address of Will Somers ; she promise to write to his benefactor to ask 
 permission to give it . you be that benefactor ? " 

 " do not   say benefactor , sir . I will tell how it come about if you will 
 let I . you see , I sell my little place at Graveleigh to the new Squire , 
 and when Mother remove to Luscombe to be near I , she tell I how 
 poor Jessie have be annoy by Captain Stavers , who seem to think 
 his purchase include the young woman on the property along with the 
 standing timber ; and I be half afraid that she have give some cause for 
 his persecution , for you know she have a blink of those soft eye of 
 her that might charm a wise man out of his skin and put a fool there 
 instead . " 

 " but I hope she have do with those blink since her marriage . " 

 " well , and I honestly think she have . it be certain she do not encourage 
 Captain Stavers , for I go over to Graveleigh myself on the sly , and 
 lodge conceal with one of the cottager who owe I a kindness ; and 
 one day , as I be at watch , I see the Captain peer over the stile 
 which divide Holmwood from the glebe , - you remember Holmwood ? " 

 " I can not   say I do . " 

 " the footway from the village to Squire Travers 's go through the 
 wood , which be a few hundred yard at the back of Will Somers 's orchard . 
 presently the Captain draw himself suddenly back from the stile , and 
 disappear among the tree , and then I see Jessie come from the 
 orchard with a basket over her arm , and walk quick towards the wood . 
 then , sir , my heart sink . I feel sure she be go to meet the Captain . 
 however , I creep along the hedgerow , hide myself , and get into the 
 wood almost as soon as Jessie get there , by another way . under the cover 
 of the brushwood I steal on till I see the Captain come out from the 
 copse on the other side of the path , and plant himself just before 
 Jessie . then I see at once I have wrong she . she have not expect to 
 see he , for she hastily turn back , and begin to run homeward ; but he 
 catch she up , and seize she by the arm . I could not hear what he say , 
 but I hear her voice quite sharp with fright and anger . and then he 
 suddenly seize her round the waist , and she scream , and I spring 
 forward - " 

 " and thrash the Captain ? " 

 " no , I do not , " say Tom ; " I have make a vow to myself that I never 
 would be violent again if I could help it . so I take he with one hand 
 by the cuff of the neck , and with the other by the waistband , and just 
 pitch he on a bramble bush , - quite mildly . he soon pick himself up , 
 for he be a dapper little chap , and become very blustering and abusive . 
 but I keep my temper , and say civilly , ' little gentleman , hard word 
 break no bone ; but if ever you molest Mrs. Somers again , I will carry 
 you into her orchard , souse you into the duck - pond there , and call all 
 the villager to see you scramble out of it again ; and I will do it 
 now if you be not off . I dare say you have hear of my name : I be Tom 
 Bowles . ' upon that his face , which be before very red , grow very white , 
 and mutter something I do not hear , he walk away . 

 " Jessie - I mean Mrs. Somers - seem at first as much frighten at I 
 as she have be at the Captain ; and though I offer to walk with she to 
 Miss Travers 's , where she be go with a basket which the young lady 
 have order , she refuse , and go back home . I feel hurt , and return 
 to my uncle 's the same evening ; and it be not for month that I hear 
 the Captain have be spiteful enough to set up an opposition shop , and 
 that poor will have be take ill , and his wife be confine about the 
 same time , and the talk be that they be in distress and might have to 
 be sell up . 

 " when I hear all this , I think that after all it be my rough tongue 
 that have so anger the Captain and be the cause of his spite , and so 
 it be my duty to make it up to poor Will and his wife . I do not know 
 how to set about mend matter , but I think I would go and talk to Miss 
 Travers ; and if ever there be a kind heart in a girl 's breast , hers be 
 one . " 

 " you be right there , I guess . what do Miss Travers say ? " 

 " Nay ; I hardly know what she do say , but she set I think , and it 
 strike I that Jessie - Mrs. Somers - have well move to a distance , and 
 out of the Captain 's reach , and that will would do well in a less 
 out - of - the - way place . and then , by good luck , I read in the newspaper 
 that a stationary and a fancywork business , with a circulate library , 
 be to be sell on moderate term at Moleswich , the other side of London . 
 so I take the train and go to the place , and think the shop would 
 just suit these young folk , and not be too much work for either ; then I 
 go to Miss Travers , and I have a lot of money lie by I from the sale 
 of the old forge and premise , which I do not know what to do with ; and 
 so , to cut short a long story , I buy the business , and Will and his 
 wife be settle at Moleswich , thriving and happy , I hope , sir . " 

 Tom 's voice quiver at the last word , and he turn aside quickly , 
 pass his hand over his eye . 

 Kenelm be greatly move . 

 " and they do not   know what you do for they ? " 

 " to be sure not . I do not   think will would have let he self be 
 beholden to I . ah ! the lad have a spirit of his own , and Jessie - Mrs. 
 Somers - would have feel pain and humble that I should even think of 
 such a thing . Miss Travers manage it all . they take the money as a loan 
 which be to be pay by instalment . they have send Miss Travers more 
 than one instalment already , so I know they be do well . " 

 " a loan from Miss Travers ? " 

 " no ; Miss Travers want to have a share in it , but I beg she not . it 
 make I happy to do what I do all myself ; and Miss Travers feel for I 
 and do not press . they perhaps think it be Squire Travers   though he be 
 not a man who would like to say it , for fear it should bring applicant 
 on he , or some other gentleman who take an interest in they . " 

 " I always say you be a grand fellow , Tom . but you be grander still 
 than I think you . " 

 " if there be any good in I , I owe it to you , sir . think what a drunken , 
 violent brute I be when I first meet you . those walk with you , and I 
 may say that other gentleman 's talk , and then that long kind letter I 
 have from you , not sign in your name , and write from abroad , - all 
 these change I , as the child be change at nurse . " 

 " you have evidently read a good deal since we part . " 

 " yes ; I belong to our young man 's library and institute ; and when of an 
 evening I get hold of a book , especially a pleasant story - book , I do not 
 care for other company . " 

 " have you never see any other girl you could care for , and wish to 
 marry ? " 

 " ah , sir , " answer Tom , " a man do not go so mad for a girl as I 
 do for Jessie Wiles , and when it be all over , and he have come to his 
 sens , put his heart into joint again as easily as if it be only a 
 broken leg . I do not   say that I may not live to love and to marry another 
 woman : it be my wish to do so . but I know that I shall love Jessie to my 
 die day ; but not sinfully , sir , - not sinfully . I would not wrong she 
 by a thought . " 

 there be a long pause . 

 at last Kenelm say , " you promise to be kind to that little girl with 
 the flower - ball ; what have become of she ? " 

 " she be quite well , thank you , sir . my aunt have take a great fancy to 
 she , and so have my mother . she come to they very often of an evening , 
 and bring she work with she . a quick , intelligent little thing , and 
 full of pretty thought . on Sundays , if the weather be fine , we stroll 
 out together in the field . " 

 " she have be a comfort to you , Tom . " 

 " oh , yes . " 

 " and love you ? " 

 " I be sure she do ; an affectionate , grateful child . " 

 " she will be a woman soon , Tom , and may love you as a woman then . " 

 Tom look indignant and rather scornful at that suggestion , and 
 hasten to revert to the subject more immediately at his heart . 

 " Miss Travers say you would like to call on Will Somers and his wife ; 
 will you ? Moleswich be not far from London , you know . " 

 " certainly , I will call . " 

 " I do hope you will find they happy ; and if so , perhaps you will kindly 
 let I know ; and - and - I wonder whether Jessie 's child be like she ? it 
 be a boy ; somehow or other I would rather it have be a girl . " 

 " I will write you full particular . but why not come with I ? " 

 " no , I do not   think I could do that , just at present . it unsettle I 
 sadly when I do again see her sweet face at Graveleigh , and she be 
 still afraid of I too ! that be a sharp pang . " 

 " she ought to know what you have do for she , and will . " 

 " on no account , sir ; promise I that . I should feel mean if I humble 
 they , - that way . " 

 " I understand , though I will not as yet make you any positive promise . 
 meanwhile , if you be stay in town , lodge with I ; my landlady can 
 find you a room . " 

 " thank you heartily , sir ; but I go back by the evening train ; and , bless 
 I ! how late it be now ! I must wish you good - by . I have some commission 
 to do for my aunt , and I must buy a new doll for Susey . " 

 " Susey be the name of the little girl with the flower - ball ? " 

 " yes . I must run off now ; I feel quite light at heart see you again 
 and find that you receive I still so kindly , as if we be equal . " 

 " ah , Tom , I wish I be your equal , - nay , half as noble as Heaven have 
 make you ! " 

 Tom laugh incredulously , and go his way . 

 " this mischievous passion of love , " say Kenelm to himself , " have its 
 good side , it seem , after all . if it be nearly make a wild beast of 
 that brave fellow , - nay , bad than wild beast , a homicide doom to 
 the gibbet , - so , on the other hand , what a refined , delicate , chivalrous 
 nature of gentleman it have develop out of the stormy element of its 
 first madness ! yes , I will go and look at this new - married couple . I 
 dare say they be already snarl and spit at each other like cat 
 and dog . Moleswich be within reach of a walk . " 





 BOOK V. 



 CHAPTER I. 

 two day after the interview record in the last chapter of the 
 previous Book , Travers , chance to call at Kenelm 's lodging , be tell 
 by his servant that Mr. Chillingly have leave London , alone , and have give 
 no order as to forward letter . the servant do not know where he 
 have go , or when he would return . 

 traver repeat this news incidentally to Cecilia , and she feel 
 somewhat hurt that he have not write she a line respect Tom 's visit . 
 she , however , guess that he have go to see the somerse , and would 
 return to town in a day or so . but week pass , the season draw to its 
 close , and of Kenelm Chillingly she see or hear nothing : he have 
 wholly vanish from the London world . he have but write a line to his 
 servant , order he to repair to Exmundham and await he there , and 
 enclose he a check to pay outstanding bill . 

 we must now follow the devious step of the strange being who have grow 
 into the hero of this story . he have leave his apartment at daybreak long 
 before his servant be up , with his knapsack , and a small portmanteau , 
 into which he have thrust - besides such additional article of dress as 
 he think he might possibly require , and which his knapsack could not 
 contain - a few of his favourite book . drive with these in a hack - cab 
 to the Vauxhall station , he direct the portmanteau to be forward 
 to Moleswich , and fling the knapsack on his shoulder , walk slowly 
 along the drowsy suburb that stretch far into the landscape , before , 
 breathe more freely , he find some evidence of rural culture on 
 either side of the high road . it be not , however , till he have leave the 
 roof and tree of pleasant Richmond far behind he that he begin to 
 feel he be out of reach of the metropolitan disquieting influence . 
 finding at a little inn , where he stop to breakfast , that there be 
 a path along field , and in sight of the river , through which he could 
 gain the place of his destination , he then quit the high road , 
 and traverse one of the lovely district in one of our lovely 
 county , he reach Moleswich about noon . 



 CHAPTER II . 

 ON enter the main street of the pretty town , the name of Somers , 
 in gilt capital , be sufficiently conspicuous over the door of a very 
 imposing shop . it boast two plate - glass window , at one of which be 
 tastefully exhibit various article of fine stationery , embroidery 
 pattern , etc . ; at the other , no less tastefully , sundry specimen of 
 ornamental basket - work . 

 Kenelm cross the threshold and recognize behind the counter - fair 
 as ever , but with an expression of face more staid , and a figure more 
 rounded and matron - like - his old friend Jessie . there be two or three 
 customer before she , between whom she be divide her attention . while 
 a handsome young lady , seat , be say , in a somewhat loud but cheery 
 and pleasant voice , " do not mind I , Mrs. Somers : I can wait , " Jessie 's 
 quick eye dart towards the stranger , but too rapidly to distinguish 
 his feature , which , indeed , he turn away , and begin to examine the 
 basket . 

 in a minute or so the other customer be serve and have depart ; and 
 the voice of the lady be again hear , " now , Mrs. Somers , I want to see 
 your picture - book and toy . I be give a little child 's party this 
 afternoon , and I want to make they as happy as possible . " 

 " somewhere or other , on this planet , or before my Monad be whisk 
 away to it , I have hear that voice , " mutter Kenelm . while Jessie be 
 alertly bring forth her toy and picture - book , she say , " I be sorry 
 to keep you wait , sir ; but if it be the basket you come about , I can 
 call my husband . " 

 " do , " say Kenelm . 

 " William , William , " cry Mrs. Somers ; and after a delay long enough to 
 allow he to slip on his jacket , William Somers emerge from the back 
 parlour . 

 his face have lose its old trace of suffering and ill health ; it be 
 still somewhat pale , and retain its expression of intellectual 
 refinement . 

 " how you have improve in your art ! " say Kenelm , heartily . 

 William start , and recognize Kenelm at once . he spring forward and 
 take Kenelm 's outstretched hand in both his own , and , in a voice between 
 laugh and crying , exclaim , " Jessie , Jessie , it be he ! - he whom we 
 pray for every night . God bless you ! God bless and make you as happy as 
 he permit you to make I ! " 

 before this little speech be falter out , Jessie be by her husband 's 
 side , and she add , in a low voice , but tremulous with deep feeling , 
 " and I too ! " 

 " by your leave , will , " say Kenelm , and he salute Jessie 's white 
 forehead with a kiss that could not have be kindly or cold if it 
 have be her grandfather 's . 

 meanwhile the lady have rise noiselessly and unobserve , and steal up 
 to Kenelm , look he full in the face . 

 " you have another friend here , sir , who have also some cause to thank 
 you - " 

 " I think I remember your voice , " say Kenelm , look puzzle . " but 
 pardon I if I can not recall your feature . where have we meet before ? " 

 " give I your arm when we go out , and I will bring myself to your 
 recollection . but no : I must not hurry you away now . I will call 
 again in half an hour . Mrs. Somers , meanwhile put up the thing I 
 have select . I will take they away with I when I come back from the 
 vicarage , where I have leave the pony - carriage . " so , with a parting nod 
 and smile to Kenelm , she turn away , and leave he bewildered . 

 " but who be that lady , will ? " 

 " a Mrs. Braefield . she be a new comer . " 

 " she may well be that , will , " say Jessie , smile , " for she have only 
 be marry six month . " 

 " and what be her name before she marry ? " 

 " I be sure I do not   know , sir . it be only three month since we come 
 here , and she have be very kind to we and an excellent customer . 
 everybody like she . Mr. Braefield be a city gentleman and very rich ; 
 and they live in the fine house in the place , and see a great deal of 
 company . " 

 " well , I be no wise than I be before , " say Kenelm . " People who ask 
 question very seldom be . " 

 " and how do you find we out , sir ? " say Jessie . " oh ! I guess , " she 
 add , with an arch glance and smile . " of course , you have see Miss 
 Travers , and she tell you . " 

 " you be right . I first learn your change of residence from she , and 
 think I would come and see you , and be introduce to the baby , - a boy , 
 I understand ? like you , will ? " 

 " no , sir , the picture of Jessie . " 

 " Nonsense , will ; it be you all over , even to its little hand . " 

 " and your good mother , will , how do you leave she ? " 

 " oh , sir ! " cry Jessie , reproachfully ; " do you think we could have the 
 heart to leave Mother , - so lone and rheumatic too ? she be tend baby 
 now , - always do while I be in the shop . " 

 here Kenelm follow the young couple into the parlour , where , seat by 
 the window , they find old Mrs. Somers read the Bible and rock the 
 baby , who sleep peacefully in its cradle . 

 " will , " say Kenelm , bend his dark face over the infant , " I will 
 tell you a pretty thought of a foreign poet 's , which have be thus badly 
 translate : 


  " ' blest babe , a boundless world this bed so narrow seem to thee ; 
  grow man , and narrower than this bed the boundless world shall 
  be . ' " 1 


   1   Schiller . 


 " I do not   think that be true , sir , " say will , simply ; " for a happy home 
 be a world wide enough for any man . " 

 tear start into Jessie 's eye ; she bend down and kiss - not the 
 baby , but the cradle . " will made it . " she add blushing , " I mean the

 Time fly past while Kenelm talk with Will and the old mother , for

 " good - by , will ; I shall come to see you again soon ; and my mother give 
 I a commission to buy I do not   know how many specimen of your craft . " 



 CHAPTER III . 

 a SMART pony - phaeton , with a box for a driver in livery equally smart , 
 stand at the shop - door . 

 " now , Mr. Chillingly , " say Mrs. Braefield , " it be my turn to run away 
 with you ; get in ! " 

 " eh ! " murmur Kenelm , gaze at she with large dreamy eye . " be it 
 possible ? " 

 " quite possible ; get in . Coachman , home ! yes , Mr. Chillingly , you meet 
 again that giddy creature whom you threaten to thrash ; it would have 
 serve her right . I ought to feel so ashamed to recall myself to your 
 recollection , and yet I be not a bit ashamed . I be proud to show you 
 that I have turn out a steady , respectable woman , and , my husband 
 tell I , a good wife . " 

 " you have only be six month marry , I hear , " say Kenelm , dryly . " I 
 hope your husband will say the same six year hence . " 

 " he will say the same sixty year hence , if we live as long . " 

 " how old be he now ? " 

 " thirty - eight . " 

 " when a man want only two year of his hundredth , he probably have 
 learn to know his own mind ; but then , in most case , very little mind 
 be leave to he to know . " 

 " do not   be satirical , sir ; and do not   talk as if you be rail at 
 marriage , when you have just leave as happy a young couple as the sun 
 ever shine upon ; and owe , - for Mrs. Somers have tell I all about her 
 marriage , - owe their happiness to you . " 

 " their happiness to I ! not in the least . I help they to marry , and in 
 spite of marriage they help each other to be happy . " 

 " you be still unmarried yourself ? " 

 " yes , thank Heaven ! " 

 " and be you happy ? " 

 " no ; I can not   make myself happy : myself be a discontent brute . " 

 " then why do you say ' thank Heaven ' ? " 

 " because it be a comfort to think I be not make somebody else 
 unhappy . " 

 " do you believe that if you love a wife who love you , you should make 
 her unhappy ? " 

 " I be sure I do not   know ; but I have not see a woman whom I could love 
 as a wife . and we need not push our inquiry far . what have become 
 of that ill - treat gray cob ? " 

 " he be quite well , thank you , when I last hear of he . " 

 " and the uncle who would have inflict I upon you , if you have not so 
 gallantly defend yourself ? " 

 " he be live where he do live , and have marry his housekeeper . he 
 feel a delicate scruple against take that step till I be marry 
 myself and out of the way . " 

 here Mrs. Braefield , begin to speak very hurriedly , as woman who 
 seek to disguise emotion often do , inform Kenelm how unhappy she have 
 feel for week after have find an asylum with her aunt , - how she have 
 be sting by remorse and oppress by a sense of humiliation at the 
 thought of her folly and the odious recollection of Mr. Compton , - how 
 she have declare to herself that she would never marry any one 
 now - never ! how Mr. Braefield happen to be on a visit in the 
 neighbourhood , and see she at church , - how he have seek an introduction 
 to she , - and how at first she rather dislike he than not ; but he be 
 so good and so kind , and when at last he propose - and she have frankly 
 tell he all about her girlish flight and infatuation - how generously he 
 have thank she for a candour which have place she as high in his esteem 
 as she have be before in his love . " and from that moment , " say Mrs. 
 Braefield , passionately , " my whole heart leap to he . and now you know 
 all ; and here we be at the Lodge . " 

 the pony - phaeton go with great speed up a broad gravel - drive , border 
 with rare evergreen , and stop at a handsome house with a portico in 
 front , and a long conservatory at the garden side , - one of those house 
 which belong to " city gentleman , " and often contain more comfort and 
 exhibit more luxury than many a stately manorial mansion . 

 Mrs. Braefield evidently feel some pride as she lead Kenelm through 
 the handsome hall , pave with malvern tile and adorn with Scagliola 
 column , and into a drawing - room furnish with much taste and opening 
 on a spacious flower - garden . 

 " but where be Mr. Braefield ? " ask Kenelm . 

 " oh , he have take the rail to his office ; but he will be back long 
 before dinner , and of course you dine with we . " 

 " you be very hospitable , but - " 

 " no but : I will take no excuse . do not   fear that you shall have only 
 mutton - chop and a rice - pudding ; and , besides , I have a child 's party 
 come at two o'clock , and there will be all sort of fun . you be fond 
 of child , I be sure ? " 

 " I rather think I be not . but I have never clearly ascertain my own 
 inclination upon that subject . " 

 " well , you shall have ample opportunity to do so to - day . and oh ! I 
 promise you the sight of the lovely face that you can picture to 
 yourself when you think of your future wife . " 

 " my future wife , I hope , be not yet bear , " say Kenelm , wearily , and 
 with much effort suppress a yawn . " but at all event , I will stay 
 till after two o'clock ; for two o'clock , I presume , mean luncheon . " 

 Mrs. Braefield laugh . " you retain your appetite ? " 

 " Most single man do , provide they do not   fall in love and become double 
 up . " 

 at this abominable attempt at a pun , Mrs. Braefield disdain to laugh ; 
 but turn away from its perpetrator she take off her hat and glove 
 and pass her hand lightly over her forehead , as if to smooth back 
 some vagrant tress in lock already sufficiently sheen and trim . she be 
 not quite so pretty in female attire as she have appear in boy 's 
 dress , nor do she look quite as young . in all other respect she be 
 wonderfully improve . there be a serener , a more settle intelligence 
 in her frank bright eye , a milder expression in the play of her part 
 lip . Kenelm gaze at she with pleased admiration . and as now , turn

 clear delicacy of her cheek , and the frank eye moisten . she come up 
 to he as he sit , and take his hand in both her , press it warmly . 
 " ah , Mr. Chillingly , " she say , with impulsive tremulous tone , " look 
 round , look round this happy , peaceful home ! - the life so free from a 
 care , the husband whom I so love and honour ; all the blessing that I 
 might have so recklessly lose forever have I not meet with you , have I be 
 punish as I deserve . how often I think of your word , that ' you 
 would be proud of my friendship when we meet again ' ! what strength they 
 give I in my hour of humble self - reproach ! " her voice here die away 
 as if in the effort to suppress a sob . 

 she release his hand , and , before he could answer , pass quickly 
 through the open sash into the garden . 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 the child have come , - some thirty of they , pretty as english child 
 generally be , happy in the joy of the summer sunshine , and the 
 flower lawn , and the feast under cover of an awning suspend between 
 chestnut - tree , and carpet with sward . 

 no doubt Kenelm hold his own at the banquet , and do his good to 
 increase the general gayety , for whenever he speak the child listen 
 eagerly , and when he have do they laugh mirthfully . 

 " the fair face I promise you , " whisper Mrs. Braefield , " be not here 
 yet . I have a little note from the young lady to say that Mrs. Cameron 
 do not feel very well this morning , but hop to recover sufficiently 
 to come later in the afternoon . " 

 " and pray who be Mrs. Cameron ? " 

 " ah ! I forget that you be a stranger to the place . Mrs. Cameron be the 
 aunt with whom Lily resides . be it not a pretty name , Lily ? " 

 " very ! emblematic of a spinster that do not spin , with a white head 
 and a thin stalk . " 

 " then the name belie my Lily , as you will see . " 

 the child now finish their feast , and betook themselves to dance 
 in an alley smooth for a croquet - ground , and to the sound of a violin 
 play by the old grandfather of one of the party . while Mrs. Braefield 
 be busy herself with form the dance , Kenelm seize the occasion 
 to escape from a young nymph of the age of twelve who have sit next he 
 at the banquet , and take so great a fancy to he that he begin to fear 
 she would vow never to forsake his side , and steal away undetected . 

 there be time when the mirth of other only sadden we , especially 
 the mirth of child with high spirit , that jar on our own quiet mood . 
 glide through a dense shrubbery , in which , though the lilac be 
 fade , the laburnum still retain here and there the wan gold of its 
 cluster , Kenelm come into a recess which bound his step and invite 
 he to repose . it be a circle , so form artificially by slight 
 trellis , to which clung parasite rose heavy with leaf and flower . 
 in the midst play a tiny fountain with a silvery murmur sound ; at 
 the background , dominate the place , rise the crest of stately tree , 
 on which the sunlight shimmer , but which rampire out all horizon 
 beyond . even as in life do the great dominant passion - love , ambition , 
 desire of power or gold or fame or knowledge - form the proud background 
 to the brief - live floweret of our youth , lift our eye beyond the 
 smile of their bloom , catch the glint of a loftier sunbeam , and yet , 
 and yet , exclude our sight from the length and the width of the space 
 which extend behind and beyond they . 

 Kenelm throw himself on the turf beside the fountain . from afar come the 
 whoop and the laugh of the child in their sport or their dance . at 
 the distance their joy do not sadden he , - he marvel why ; and thus , 
 in muse revery , think to explain the why to himself . 

 " the poet , " so run his lazy thinking , " have tell we that ' distance lend 
 enchantment to the view , ' and thus compare to the charm of distance 
 the illusion of hope . but the poet narrow the scope of his own 
 illustration . distance lend enchantment to the ear as well as to the 
 sight ; nor to these bodily sens alone . memory no less than hope owe 
 its charm to ' the far away . ' 

 " I can not imagine myself again a child when I be in the midst of 
 young noisy child . but as their noise reach I here , subdue and 
 mellow , and know , thank Heaven , that the urchin be not within 
 reach of I , I could readily dream myself back into childhood , and into 
 sympathy with the lose playfield of school . 

 " so surely it must be with grief : how different the terrible agony for 
 a beloved one just go from earth , to the soft regret for one who 
 disappear into Heaven year ago ! so with the art of poetry : how 
 imperatively , when it deal with the great emotion of tragedy , it must 
 remove the actor from we , in proportion as the emotion be to elevate , 
 and the tragedy be to please we by the tear it draw ! imagine our shock 
 if a poet be to place on the stage some wise gentleman with whom we 
 din yesterday , and who be discover to have kill his father and 
 marry his mother . but when Oedipus commit those unhappy mistake 
 nobody be shock . Oxford in the nineteenth century be a long way off 
 from Thebes three thousand or four thousand year ago . 

 " and , " continue Kenelm , plunge deeply into the maze of metaphysical 
 criticism , " even where the poet deal with person and thing close upon 
 our daily sight , - if he would give they poetic charm he must resort to 
 a sort of moral or psychological distance ; the nearer they be to we 
 in external circumstance , the far they must be in some internal 
 peculiarity . werter and Clarissa Harlowe be describe as 
 contemporary of their artistic creation , and with the minute detail 
 of apparent realism ; yet they be at once remove from our daily life 
 by their idiosyncrasy and their fate . we know that while Werter 
 and Clarissa be so near to we in much that we sympathize with they as 
 friend and kinsfolk , they be yet as much remote from we in the poetic 
 and idealize side of their nature as if they belong to the age of 
 Homer ; and this it be that invest with charm the very pain which their 
 fate inflict on we . thus , I suppose , it must be in love . if the love 
 we feel be to have the glamour of poetry , it must be love for some 
 one morally at a distance from our ordinary habitual self ; in short , 
 differ from we in attribute which , however near we draw to the 
 possessor , we can never approach , never blend , in attribute of our 
 own ; so that there be something in the love one that always remain an 
 ideal , - a mystery , - ' a sun - bright summit mingle with the sky ' ! " 

 herewith the soliloquist 's musing glide vaguely into mere revery . he 
 close his eye drowsily , not asleep , nor yet quite awake ; as sometimes 
 in bright summer day when we recline on the grass we do close our eye , 
 and yet dimly recognize a golden light bath the drowsy lid ; and 
 athwart that light image come and go like dream , though we know that 
 we be not dream . 



 CHAPTER V. 

 from this state , half comatose , half unconscious , Kenelm be rouse 
 slowly , reluctantly . something strike softly on his cheek , - again a 
 little less softly ; he open his eye , they fall first upon two tiny 
 rosebud , which , on strike his face , have fall on his breast ; and 
 then look up , he see before he , in an opening of the trellis 
 circle , a female child 's laugh face . her hand be still uplifted 
 charge with another rosebud , but behind the child 's figure , look 
 over her shoulder and hold back the menacing arm , be a face as 
 innocent but lovelier far , - the face of a girl in her first youth , 
 frame round with the blossom that festoon the trellise . how the face 
 become the flower ! it seem the fairy spirit of they . 

 Kenelm start and rise to his foot . the child , the one whom he have so 
 ungallantly escape from ran towards he through a wicket in the circle . 
 her companion disappear . 

 " be it you ? " say Kenelm to the child , " you who pelt I so cruelly ? 
 ungrateful creature ! do I not give you the good strawberry in the 
 dish and all my own cream ? " 

 " but why do you run away and hide yourself when you ought to be dance 
 with I ? " reply the young lady , evade , with the instinct of her sex ,

 " I do not run away , and it be clear that I do not mean to hide myself , 
 since you so easily find I out . but who be the young lady with you ?

 herself . " 

 " no , she do not pelt you ; she want to stop I , and you would have have 
 another rosebud - oh , so much big ! - if she have not hold back my arm . 
 do not   you know she , - do not   you know Lily ? " 

 " no ; so that be lily ? you shall introduce I to she . " 

 by this time they have pass out of the circle through the little wicket 
 opposite the path by which Kenelm have enter , and opening at once on

 on the grass , some walk to and fro , in the interval of the dance . 

 in the space between the group and the trellise Lily be walk alone 
 and quickly . the child leave Kenelm 's side and run after her friend , soon 
 overtook , but do not succeed in arrest her step . Lily do not pause 
 till she have reach the grassy ball - room , and here all the child 
 come round she and shut out her delicate form from Kenelm 's sight . 

 before he have reach the place , Mrs. Braefield meet he . 

 " Lily be come ! " 

 " I know it : I have see she . " 

 " be not she beautiful ? " 

 " I must see more of she if I be to answer critically ; but before you 
 introduce I , may I be permit to ask who and what be lily ? " 

 Mrs. Braefield pause a moment before she answer , and yet the 
 answer be brief enough not to need much consideration . " she be a Miss 
 Mordaunt , an orphan ; and , as I before tell you , reside with her aunt , 
 Mrs. Cameron , a widow . they have the prettiest cottage you ever see on 
 the bank of the river , or rather rivulet , about a mile from this place . 
 Mrs. Cameron be a very good , simple - hearted woman . as to Lily , I can 
 praise her beauty only with safe conscience , for as yet she be a mere 
 child , - her mind quite unformed . " 

 " do you ever meet any man , much less any woman , whose mind be form ? " 
 mutter Kenelm . " I be sure mine be not , and never will be on this

 Mrs. Braefield do not hear this low - voice observation . she be 
 look about for Lily ; and perceive she at last as the child who 
 surround she be disperse to renew the dance , she take Kenelm 's 
 arm , lead he to the young lady , and a formal introduction take place . 

 Formal as it could be on those sunlit sward , amidst the joy of summer 
 and the laugh of child . in such scene and such circumstance formality 
 do not last long . I know not how it be , but in a very few minute 
 Kenelm and Lily have cease to be stranger to each other . they find 
 themselves seat apart from the rest of the merry - maker , on the bank 
 shadow by lime - tree ; the man listen with downcast eye , the girl 
 with mobile shifting glance now on earth , now on heaven , and talk 
 freely ; gayly , - like the babble of a happy stream , with a silvery dulcet 
 voice and a sparkle of ripple smile . 

 no doubt this be a reversal of the formality of well - breed life , and 
 conventional narrating thereof . accord to they , no doubt , it be for 
 the man to talk and the maid to listen ; but I state the fact as they 
 be , honestly . and Lily know no more of the formality of drawing - room 
 life than a skylark fresh from its nest know of the song - teacher and 
 the cage . she be still so much of a child . Mrs. Braefield be right : 
 her mind be still so unformed . 

 what she do talk about in that first talk between they that could make 
 the meditative Kenelm listen so mutely , so intently , I know not , at 
 least I could not jot it down on paper . I fear it be very egotistical , 
 as the talk of child generally be , - about herself and her aunt , and 
 her home and her friend ; all her friend seem child like herself , 
 though young , - Clemmy the chief of they . Clemmy be the one who have 
 take a fancy to Kenelm . and amidst all this ingenuous prattle there 
 come flash of a quick intellect , a lively fancy , - nay , even a poetry 
 of expression or of sentiment . it might be the talk of a child , but 
 certainly not of a silly child . but as soon as the dance be over , 
 the little one again gather round Lily . evidently she be the prime 
 favourite of they all ; and as her companion have now become tired 
 of dancing , new sport be propose , and Lily be carry off to 
 " Prisoner 's Base . " 

 " I be very happy to make your acquaintance , Mr. Chillingly , " say a 
 frank , pleasant voice ; and a well - dress , good - look man hold out his 
 hand to Kenelm . 

 " my husband , " say Mrs. Braefield , with a certain pride in her look . 

 Kenelm respond cordially to the civility of the master of the house , 
 who have just return from his city office , and leave all its care 
 behind he . you have only to look at he to see that he be prosperous , 
 and deserve to be so . there be in his countenance the sign of strong 
 sense , of good - humour , - above all , of an active energetic temperament . a 
 man of broad smooth forehead , keen hazel eye , firm lip and jaw ; with a 
 happy contentment in himself , his house , the world in general , mantle 
 over his genial smile , and outspoken in the metallic ring of his voice . 

 " you will stay and dine with we , of course , " say Mr. Braefield ; " and , 
 unless you want very much to be in town to - night , I hope you will take a 
 bed here . " 

 Kenelm hesitate . 

 " do stay at least till to - morrow , " say Mrs. Braefield . Kenelm hesitate 
 still ; and while hesitate his eye rest on Lily , lean on the arm 
 of a middle - aged lady , and approach the hostess , - evidently to take 
 leave . 

 " I can not resist so tempt an invitation , " say Kenelm , and he fall 
 back a little behind Lily and her companion . 

 " thank you much for so pleasant a day , " say Mrs. Cameron to the 
 hostess . " Lily have enjoy herself extremely . I only regret we could not 
 come early . " 

 " if you be walk home , " say Mr. Braefield , " let I accompany you . I 
 want to speak to your gardener about his heart's - ease : it be much fine 
 than mine . " 

 " if so , " say Kenelm to Lily , " may I come too ? of all flower that grow , 
 heart' - ease be the one I most prize . " 

 a few minute afterwards Kenelm be walk by the side of Lily along 
 the bank of a little stream , tributary to the Thames ; Mrs. Cameron and 
 Mr. Braefield in advance , for the path only hold two abreast . 

 suddenly Lily leave his side , allure by a rare butterfly - I think it be 
 call the Emperor of Morocco - that be sun its yellow wing upon 
 a group of wild reed . she succeed in capture this wanderer in her 
 straw hat , over which she draw her sun - veil . after this notable capture 
 she return demurely to Kenelm 's side . 

 " do you collect insect ? " say that philosopher , as much surprised as it 
 be his nature to be at anything . 

 " only butterfly , " answer lily ; " they be not insect , you know ; they 
 be soul . " 

 " emblem of soul you mean , - at least , so the Greeks prettily 
 represent they to be . " 

 " no , real soul , - the soul of infant that die in their cradle 
 unbaptized ; and if they be take care of , and not eat by bird , and 
 live a year then they pass into fairy . " 

 " it be a very poetical idea , Miss Mordaunt , and found on evidence 
 quite as rational as other assertion of the metamorphosis of one 
 creature into another . perhaps you can do what the philosopher 
 can not , - tell I how you learn a new idea to be an incontestable 
 fact ? " 

 " I do not   know , " reply Lily , look very much puzzle ; " perhaps I 
 learn it in a book , or perhaps I dream it . " 

 " you could not make a wise answer if you be a philosopher . but you 
 talk of take care of butterfly ; how do you do that ? do you impale 
 they on pin stick into a glass case ? " 

 " impale they ! how can you talk so cruelly ? you deserve to be pinch by 
 the fairy . " 

 " I be afraid , " think Kenelm , compassionately , " that my companion have 
 no mind to be form ; what be euphoniously call ' an innocent . ' " 

 he shake his head and remain silent . Lily resume , - 

 " I will show you my collection when we get home ; they seem so happy . I 
 be sure there be some of they who know I : they will feed from my hand . 
 I have only have one die since I begin to collect they last summer . " 

 " then you have keep they a year : they ought to have turn into 
 fairy . " 

 " I suppose many of they have . of course I let out all those that have 
 be with I twelve month : they do not   turn to fairy in the cage , 
 you know . now I have only those I catch this year , or last autumn ; the 
 prettiest do not   appear till the autumn . " 

 the girl here bend her uncovered head over the straw hat , her tress 
 shadow it , and utter love word to the prisoner . then again she 
 look up and around she , and abruptly stop , and exclaim , - 

 " how can people live in town ? how can people say they be ever dull in 
 the country ? look , " she continue , gravely and earnestly , " look at that 
 tall pine - tree , with its long branch sweep over the water ; see how , 
 as the breeze catch it , it change its shadow , and how the shadow 
 change the play of the sunlight on the brook : - 


  " ' wave your top , ye pine ; 
  with every plant , in sign of worship wave . ' 


 " what an interchange of music there must be between Nature and a poet ! " 

 Kenelm be startle . this " an innocent " ! - this a girl who have no mind to 
 be form ! in that presence he could not be cynical ; could not speak of 
 nature as a mechanism , a lie humbug , as he have do to the man poet . 
 he reply gravely , - 

 " the Creator have gift the whole universe with language , but few be 
 the heart that can interpret it . happy those to whom it be no foreign 
 tongue , acquire imperfectly with care and pain , but rather a native 
 language , learn unconsciously from the lip of the great mother . to 
 they the butterfly 's wing may well buoy into heaven a fairy 's soul ! " 

 when he have thus say Lily turn , and for the first time attentively 
 look into his dark soft eye ; then instinctively she lay her light 
 hand on his arm , and say in a low voice , " talk on ; talk thus : I like to 
 hear you . " 

 but Kenelm do not talk on . they have now arrive at the garden - gate of 
 Mrs. Cameron 's cottage , and the elder person in advance pause at the 
 gate and walk with they to the house . 

 it be a long , low , irregular cottage , without pretension to 
 architectural beauty , yet exceedingly picturesque , - a flower - garden , 
 large , but in proportion to the house , with parterre in which the 
 colour be exquisitely assorted , slop to the grassy margin of the 
 rivulet , where the stream expand into a lake - like basin , narrow 
 at either end by lock , from which with gentle sound flow shallow 
 waterfall . by the bank be a rustic seat , half overshadow by the 
 droop bough of a vast willow . 

 the inside of the house be in harmony with the exterior , - cottage - like , 
 but with an unmistakable air of refinement about the room , even in the 
 little entrance - hall , which be paint in pompeian fresco . 

 " come and see my butterfly - cage , " say Lily , whisperingly . 

 Kenelm follow she through the window that open on the garden ; and 
 at one end of a small conservatory , or rather greenhouse , be the 
 habitation of these singular favourite . it be as large as a small 
 room ; three side of it form by minute wirework , with occasional 
 drapery of muslin or other slight material , and cover at interval , 
 sometimes within , sometimes without , by dainty creeper ; a tiny cistern 
 in the centre , from which upsprang a sparkling jet . lily cautiously 
 lift a sash - door and glide in , close it behind she . her entrance 
 set in movement a multitude of gossamer wing , some flutter round 
 she , some more boldly settle on her hair or dress . Kenelm think 
 she have not vainly boast when she say that some of the creature have 
 learn to know she . she release the Emperor of Morocco from her hat ; 
 it circle round she fearlessly , and then vanish amidst the leaf of 
 the creeper . lily open the door and come out . 

 " I have hear of a philosopher who tame a wasp , " say Kenelm , " but 
 never before of a young lady who tame butterfly . " 

 " no , " say Lily , proudly ; " I believe I be the first who attempt it . 
 I do not   think I should have attempt it if I have be tell that other 
 have succeed before I . not that I have succeed quite . no matter ; if 
 they do not   love I , I love they . " 

 they re - enter the drawing - room , and Mrs. Cameron address Kenelm . 

 " do you know much of this part of the country , Mr. Chillingly ? " 

 " it be quite new to I , and more rural than many district far from 
 London . " 

 " that be the good fortune of most of our home county , " say Mr. 
 Braefield ; " they escape the smoke and din of manufacturing town , and 
 agricultural science have not demolish their leafy hedgerow . the 
 walk through our green lane be as much border with convolvulus and 
 honeysuckle as they be when Izaak Walton saunter through they to 
 angle in that stream ! " 

 " do tradition say that he angle in that stream ? I think his haunt 
 be rather on the other side of London . " 

 " possibly ; I be not learn in Walton or in his art , but there be an old 
 summer - house , on the other side of the lock yonder , on which be carve 
 the name of Izaak Walton , but whether by his own hand or another be who 
 shall say ? have Mr. Melville be here lately , Mrs. Cameron ? " 

 " no , not for several month . " 

 " he have have a glorious success this year . we may hope that at last his 
 genius be acknowledge by the world . I mean to buy his picture , but I 
 be not in time : a Manchester man be before I . " 

 " who be Mr. Melville ? any relation to you ? " whisper Kenelm to Lily . 

 " Relation , - I scarcely know . yes , I suppose so , because he be my 
 guardian . but if he be the near relation on earth , I could not love 
 he more , " say Lily , with impulsive eagerness , her cheek flush , her 
 eye fill with tear . 

 " and he be an artist , - a painter ? " ask Kenelm . 

 " oh , yes ; no one paint such beautiful picture , - no one so clever , no 
 one so kind . " 

 Kenelm strove to recollect if he have ever hear the name of Melville as 
 a painter , but in vain . Kenelm , however , know but little of painter : 
 they be not in his way ; and he own to himself , very humbly , that 
 there might be many a live painter of eminent renown whose name and 
 work would be strange to he . 

 he glance round the wall ; Lily interpret his look . " there be no 
 picture of his here , " say she ; " there be one in my own room . I will 
 show it you when you come again . " 

 " and now , " say Mr. Braefield , rise , " I must just have a word with 
 your gardener , and then go home . we dine early here than in London , 
 Mr. Chillingly . " 

 as the two gentleman , after take leave , re - enter the hall , Lily 
 follow they and say to Kenelm , " what time will you come to - morrow to 
 see the picture ? " 

 Kenelm avert his head , and then reply , not with his wonte courtesy , 
 but briefly and brusquely , - 

 " I fear I can not call to - morrow . I shall be far away by sunrise . " 

 Lily make no answer , but turn back into the room . 

 Mr. Braefield find the gardener water a flower - border , confer 
 with he about the heart's - ease , and then join Kenelm , who have halt 
 a few yard beyond the garden - gate . 

 " a pretty little place that , " say Mr. Braefield , with a sort of lordly 
 compassion , as become the owner of Braefieldville . " what I call quaint . " 

 " yes , quaint , " echo Kenelm , abstractedly . 

 " it be always the case with house enlarge by degree . I have hear my 
 poor mother say that when Melville or Mrs. Cameron first buy it , it 
 be little well than a mere labourer 's cottage , with a field attach 
 to it . and two or three year afterwards a room or so more be build , 
 and a bit of the field take in for a garden ; and then by degree the 
 whole part now inhabit by the family be build , leave only the old 
 cottage as a scullery and washhouse ; and the whole field be turn 
 into the garden , as you see . but whether it be Melville 's money or the 
 aunt 's that do it , I do not   know . more likely the aunt 's . I do not   see 
 what interest Melville have in the place : he do not go there often , I 
 fancy ; it be not his home . " 

 " Mr. Melville , it seem , be a painter , and , from what I hear you say , a 
 successful one . " 

 " I fancy he have little success before this year . but surely you see his 
 picture at the exhibition ? " 

 " I be ashamed to say I have not be to the exhibition . " 

 " you surprise I . however , Melville have three picture there , - all very 
 good ; but the one I wish to buy make much more sensation than the 
 other , and have suddenly lift he from obscurity into fame . " 

 " he appear to be a relation of Miss Mordaunt 's , but so distant a 
 one that she could not even tell I what grade of cousinship he could 
 claim . " 

 " nor can I. he be her guardian , I know . the relationship , if any , must , 
 as you say , be very distant ; for Melville be of humble extraction , while 
 any one can see that Mrs. Cameron be a thorough gentlewoman , and Lily 
 Mordaunt be her sister 's child . I have hear my mother say that it be 
 Melville , then a very young man , who buy the cottage , perhaps with 
 Mrs. Cameron 's money ; say it be for a widow lady , whose husband 
 have leave she with very small mean . and when Mrs. Cameron arrive with 
 Lily , then a mere infant , she be in deep mourning , and a very young 
 woman herself , - pretty too . if Melville have be a frequent visitor 
 then , of course there would have be scandal ; but he very seldom come , 
 and when he do , he lodge in a cottage , Cromwell Lodge , on the other 
 side of the brook ; now and then bring with he a fellow - lodger , - some 
 other young artist , I suppose , for the sake of angle . so there could 
 be no cause for scandal , and nothing can be more blameless than poor 
 Mrs. Cameron 's life . my mother , who then reside at Braefieldville , take 
 a great fancy to both Lily and her aunt , and when by degree the cottage 
 grow into a genteel sort of place , the few gentry in the neighbourhood 
 follow my mother 's example and be very kind to Mrs. Cameron , so that 
 she have now her place in the society about here , and be much like . " 

 " and Mr. Melville ? - do he still very seldom come here ? " 

 " to say truth , he have not be at all since I settle at Braefieldville . 
 the place be leave to my mother for her life , and I be not much there 
 during her occupation . in fact , I be then a junior partner in our firm , 
 and conduct the branch business in New York , come over to England 
 for my holiday once a year or so . when my mother die , there be much to 
 arrange before I could settle personally in England , and I do not come 
 to settle at Braefieldville till I marry . I do see Melville on one of 
 my visit to the place some year ago ; but , between ourselves , he be not 
 the sort of person whose intimate acquaintance one would wish to court . 
 my mother tell I he be an idle , dissipated man , and I have hear from 
 other that he be very unsteady . Mr. - - - , the great painter , tell 
 I that he be a loose fish ; and I suppose his habit be against his 
 get on , till this year , when , perhaps , by a lucky accident , he have 
 paint a picture that raise he to the top of the tree . but be not 
 Miss Lily wondrously nice to look at ? what a pity her education have be 
 so much neglect ! " 

 " have it ? " 

 " have not you discover that already ? she have not have even a 
 music - master , though my wife say she have a good ear , and can sing 
 prettily enough . as for reading I do not   think she have read anything but 
 fairy tale and poetry , and such silly stuff . however , she be very young 
 yet ; and now that her guardian can sell his picture , it be to be hop 
 that he will do more justice to his ward . painter and actor be not so 
 regular in their private life as we plain man be , and great allowance 
 be to be make for they ; still , every one be bind to do his duty . I be 
 sure you agree with I ? " 

 " certainly , " say Kenelm , with an emphasis which startle the merchant . 
 " that be an admirable maxim of your : it seem a commonplace , yet how 
 often , when it be put into our head , it strike as a novelty ! a duty 
 may be a very difficult thing , a very disagreeable thing , and , what 
 be strange , it be often a very invisible thing . it be present , - close 
 before we , and yet we do not   see it ; somebody shout its name in our 
 ear , ' duty , ' and straight it tower before we a grim giant . pardon I 
 if I leave you : I can not   stay to dine . duty summon I elsewhere . make my 
 excuse to Mrs. Braefield . " 

 before Mr. Braefield could recover his self - possession , Kenelm have 
 vault over a stile and be go . 



 CHAPTER VI . 

 KENELM walk into the shop keep by the somerse , and find Jessie 
 still at the counter . " give I back my knap sack . thank you , " he say , 
 fling the knapsack across his shoulder . " now , do I a favour . a 
 portmanteau of mine ought to be at the station . send for it , and keep it 
 till I give further direction . I think of go to Oxford for a day 
 or two . Mrs. Somers , one more word with you . think , answer frankly , be 
 you , as you say this morning , thoroughly happy , and yet married to the 
 man you love ? " 

 " oh , so happy ! " 

 " and wish for nothing beyond ? do not wish will to be other than he be ? " 

 " God forbid ! you frighten I , sir . " 

 " frighten you ! be it so . everyone who be happy should be frightened 
 lest happiness fly away . do your good to chain it , and you will , for you 
 attach Duty to happiness ; and , " mutter Kenelm , as he turn from the 
 shop , " duty be sometimes not a rose - colour tie , but a heavy iron - hue 
 clog . " 

 he stride on through the street towards the sign - post with " To Oxford " 
 inscribe thereon . and whether he speak literally of the knapsack , or 
 metaphorically of duty , he murmur , as he strode , - 


  " a pedlar 's pack that bow the bearer down . " 



 CHAPTER VII . 



 KENELM might have reach Oxford that night , for he be a rapid and 
 untirable pedestrian ; but he halt a little after the moon rise , and 
 lay himself down to rest beneath a new - mow haystack , not very far from 
 the high road . 

 he do not sleep . meditatingly prop on his elbow , he say to 
 himself , - 

 " it be long since I have wonder at nothing . I wonder now : can this be 
 love , - really love , - unmistakably love ? Pooh ! it be impossible ; the 
 very last person in the world to be in love with . let we reason upon 
 it , - you , myself , and i. to begin with , - face ! what be face ? in a few 
 year the most beautiful face may be very plain . take the Venus at 
 Florence . animate she ; see she ten year after ; a chignon , front tooth 
  blue or artificially white , mottled complexion , double chin , - all that 
 sort of plump prettiness go into double chin . face , bah ! what man of 
 sense - what pupil of Welby , the realist - can fall in love with a face ? 
 and even if I be simpleton enough to do so , pretty face be as common 
 as daisy . Cecilia Travers have more regular feature ; Jessie Wiles a 
 rich colouring . I be not in love with they , - not a bit of it . myself , 
 you have nothing to say there . well , then , mind ? talk of mind , indeed ! 
 a creature whose favourite companionship be that of butterfly , and who 
 tell I that butterfly be the soul of infant unbaptize . what an 
 article for ' the Londoner , ' on the culture of young woman ! what a girl 
 for Miss Garrett and Miss Emily Faithfull ! put aside Mind as we have 
 do face . what rest ? - the Frenchman 's ideal of happy marriage ? 
 congenial circumstance of birth , fortune , taste , habit . worse still . 
 myself , answer honestly , be you not floor ? " 

 Whereon " Myself " take up the parable and answer , " o thou fool ! why 
 wert thou so ineffably bless in one presence ? why , in quit that 
 presence , do Duty become so grim ? why dost thou address to I those 
 inept pedantic questioning , under the light of yon moon , which have 
 suddenly cease to be to thy thought an astronomical body and have 
 become , forever and forever , identify in thy heart 's dream with 
 romance and poesy and first love ? why , instead of gaze on that 
 uncomfortable orb , art thou not quicken thy step towards a cozy inn 
 and a good supper at Oxford ? Kenelm , my friend , thou art in for it . no 
 disguise the fact : thou art in love ! " 

 " I will be hang if I be , " say the Second in the Dualism of Kenelm 's 
 mind ; and therewith he shift his knapsack into a pillow , turn his 
 eye from the moon , and still could not sleep . the face of Lily still 
 haunt his eye ; the voice of Lily still ring in his ear . 

 oh , my reader ! dost thou here ask I to tell thee what Lily be 
 like ? - be she dark ? be she fair ? be she tall ? be she short ? never 
 shalt thou learn these secret from I . imagine to thyself the be to 
 which thine whole of life , body and mind and soul , move irresistibly as 
 the needle to the pole . let she be tall or short , dark or fair , she be 
 that which out of all womankind have suddenly become the one woman for 
 thee . fortunate art thou , my reader , if thou chance to have hear the 
 popular song of " my Queen " sing by the one lady who alone can sing it 
 with expression worthy the verse of the poetess and the music of the 
 composition , by the sister of the exquisite songstress . but if thou hast 
 not hear the verse thus sing , to an accompaniment thus compose , still 
 the word themselves be , or ought to be , familiar to thee , if thou art , 
 as I take for grant , a lover of the true lyrical muse . Recall then 
 the word suppose to be utter by he who know himself destine to do 
 homage to one he have not yet beheld : - 


  " she be stand somewhere , - she I shall honour , 
  she that I wait for , my queen , my queen ; 
  whether her hair be golden or raven , 
  whether her eye be hazel or blue , 
  I know not now , it will be engraven 
  some day hence as my lovely hue . 
  she may be humble or proud , my lady , 
  or that sweet calm which be just between ; 
  but whenever she come , she will find I ready 
  to do her homage , my queen , my queen . " 


 be it possible that the cruel boy - god " who sharpen his arrow on the 
 whetstone of the human heart " have find the moment to avenge himself 
 for the neglect of his altar and the scorn of his power ? must that 
 redoubte knight - errant , the hero of this tale , despite the Three Fishes 
 on his charmed shield , at last veil the crest and bow the knee , and 
 murmur to himself , " she have come , my queen " ? 



 CHAPTER VIII . 

 the next morning Kenelm arrive at Oxford , - " Verum secretumque 
 Mouseion . " 

 if there be a place in this busy island which may distract the 
 passion of youth from love to scholarship , to ritualism , to mediaeval 
 association , to that sort of poetical sentiment or poetical fanaticism 
 which a Mivers and a Welby and an advocate of the Realistic School would 
 hold in contempt , - certainly that place be Oxford , - home ; nevertheless , 
 of great thinker and great actor in the practical world . 

 the vacation have not yet commence , but the commencement be near at 
 hand . Kenelm think he could recognize the lead man by their slow 
 walk and more abstracted expression of countenance . among the Fellows 
 be the eminent author of that book which have so powerfully fascinate 
 the early adolescence of Kenelm Chillingly , and who have himself be 
 subject to the fascination of a yet strong spirit . the Rev. Decimus 
 Roach have be ever an intense and reverent admirer of John Henry 
 Newman , - an admirer , I mean , of the pure and lofty character of the 
 man , quite apart from sympathy with his doctrine . but although Roach 
 remain an unconverted protestant of orthodox , if High Church , creed , 
 yet there be one tenet he do hold in common with the author of the 
 " Apologia . " he rank celibacy among the virtue most dear to Heaven . 
 in that eloquent treatise , " the Approach to the Angels , " he not only 
 maintain that the state of single blessedness be strictly incumbent 
 on every member of a christian priesthood , but to be commend to the 
 adoption of every conscientious layman . 

 it be the desire to confer with this eminent theologian that have 
 induce Kenelm to direct his step to Oxford . 

 Mr. Roach be a friend of Welby , at whose house , when a pupil , 
 Kenelm have once or twice meet he , and be even more charm by his 
 conversation than by his treatise . 

 Kenelm call on Mr. Roach , who receive he very graciously , and , not 
 be a tutor or examiner , place his time at Kenelm 's disposal ; take 
 he the round of the college and the Bodleian ; invite he to dine in 
 his college - hall ; and after dinner lead he into his own room , and give 
 he an excellent bottle of Chateau Margeaux . 

 Mr. Roach be somewhere about fifty , - a good - look man and evidently 
 think himself so ; for he wear his hair long behind and part in the 
 middle , which be not do by man who form modest estimate of their 
 personal appearance . 

 Kenelm be not long in draw out his host on the subject to which that 
 profound thinker have devote so much meditation . 

 " I can scarcely convey to you , " say Kenelm , " the intense admiration 
 with which I have study your noble work , ' approach to the Angels . ' it 
 produce a great effect on I in the age between boyhood and youth . but 
 of late some doubt on the universal application of your doctrine have 
 creep into my mind . " 

 " ay , indeed ? " say Mr. Roach , with an expression of interest in his 
 face . 

 " and I come to you for their solution . " 

 Mr. Roach turn away his head , and push the bottle to Kenelm . 

 " I be quite willing to concede , " resume the heir of the Chillinglys , 
 " that a priesthood should stand apart from the distracting care of a 
 family , and pure from all carnal affection . " 

 " Hem , hem , " grunt Mr. Roach , take his knee on his lap and caressing 
 it . 

 " I go far , " continue Kenelm , " and suppose with you that the 
 Confessional have all the importance , whether in its monitory or its 
 cheering effect upon repentant sinner , which be attach to it by 
 the Roman Catholics , and that it ought to be no less cultivate by the 
 Reformed Church , it seem to I essential that the Confessor should have 
 no well half to whom it can be even suspect he may , in an unguarded 
 moment , hint at the frailty of one of her female acquaintance . " 

 " I push that argument too far , " murmur Roach . 

 " not a bit of it . celibacy in the Confessor stand or fall with the 
 Confessional . your argument there be as sound as a bell . but when it 
 come to the layman , I think I detect a difference . " 

 Mr. Roach shake his head , and reply stoutly , " no ; if celibacy be 
 incumbent on the one , it be equally incumbent on the other . I say ' if . ' " 

 " permit I to deny that assertion . do not fear that I shall insult your 
 understanding by the popular platitude ; namely , that if celibacy be 
 universal , in a very few year the human race would be extinct . as you 
 have justly observe , in answer to that fallacy , ' it be the duty of each 
 human soul to strive towards the high perfection of the spiritual 
 state for itself , and leave the fate of the human race to the care of 
 the Creator . ' if celibacy be necessary to spiritual perfection , how do 
 we know but that it may be the purpose and decree of the all Wise that 
 the human race , have attain to that perfection , should disappear 
 from earth ? universal celibacy would thus be the euthanasia of mankind . 
 on the other hand , if the Creator decide that the human race , have 
 culminate to this crowning but barren flower of perfection , should 
 nevertheless continue to increase and multiply upon earth , have you not 
 victoriously exclaim , ' presumptuous mortal ! how canst thou presume 
 to limit the resource of the Almighty ? would it not be easy for he to 
 continue some other mode , unexpose to trouble and sin and passion , as 
 in the nuptial of the vegetable world , by which the generation will 
 be renew ? can we suppose that the angel - the immortal company 
 of heaven - be not hourly increase in number , and extend their 
 population throughout infinity ? and yet in heaven there be no marrying 
 nor give in marriage . ' all this , clothe by you in word which my 
 memory only serve I to quote imperfectly , - all this I unhesitatingly 
 concede . " 

 Mr. Roach rise and bring another bottle of the Chateau Margeaux from 
 his cellaret , fill Kenelm 's glass , reseat himself , and take the 
 other knee into his lap to caress . 

 " but , " resume Kenelm , " my doubt be this . " 

 " ah ! " cry Mr. Roach , " let we hear the doubt . " 

 " in the first place , be celibacy essential to the high state of 
 spiritual perfection ; and , in the second place , if it be , be mortal , 
 as at present constituted , capable of that culmination ? " 

 " very well put , " say Mr. Roach , and he toss off his glass with more 
 cheerful aspect than he have hitherto exhibit . 

 " you see , " say Kenelm , " we be compel in this , as in other question 
 of philosophy , to resort to the inductive process , and draw our theory 
 from the fact within our cognizance . now look round the world , be it 
 the fact that old maid and old bachelor be so much more spiritually 
 advanced than married folk ? do they pass their time , like an indian 
 dervish , in serene contemplation of divine excellence and beatitude ? 
 be they not quite as worldly in their own way as person who have be 
 marry as often as the Wife of Bath , and , generally speak , more 
 selfish , more frivolous , and more spiteful ? I be sure I do not   wish to 
 speak uncharitably against old maid and old bachelor . I have three 
 aunt who be old maid , and fine specimen of the genus ; but I be sure 
 they would all three have be more agreeable companion , and quite as 
 spiritually gifted , if they have be happily marry , and be caress 
 their child , instead of lapdog . so , too , I have an old bachelor 
 cousin , Chillingly Mivers , whom you know . as clever as a man can be . 
 but , Lord bless you ! as to be wrap in spiritual meditation , he 
 could not be more devoted to the thing of earth if he have marry as 
 many wife as Solomon , and have as many child as Priam . finally , have 
 not half the mistake in the world arise from a separation between the 
 spiritual and the moral nature of man ? be it not , after all , through his 
 dealing with his fellow - man that man make his safe ' approach to the 
 angel ' ? and be not the moral system a very muscular system ? do it not 
 require for healthful vigour plenty of continued exercise , and do it 
 not get that exercise naturally by the relationship of family , with 
 all the wide collateral struggle with life which the care of family 
 necessitate ? 

 " I put these question to you with the humblest diffidence . I expect to 
 hear such answer as will thoroughly convince my reason , and I shall be 
 delighted if so . for at the root of the controversy lie the passion of 
 love . and love must be a very disquieting , troublesome emotion , and have 
 lead many hero and sage into wonderful weakness and folly . " 

 " gently , gently , Mr. Chillingly ; do not   exaggerate . love , no doubt , 
 be - ahem - a disquieting passion . still , every emotion that change life 
 from a stagnant pool into the freshness and play of a running stream be 
 disquieting to the pool . not only love and its fellow - passion , such as 
 ambition , but the exercise of the reasoning faculty , which be always at 
 work in change our idea , be very disquieting . love , Mr. Chillingly , 
 have its good side as well as its bad . pass the bottle . " 

 KENELM   pass the bottle . - " yes , yes ; you be quite right in 
 put the adversary 's case strongly , before you demolish it : all good 
 rhetorician do that . pardon I if I be up to that trick in argument . 
 assume that I know all that can be say in favour of the abnegation of 
 common - sense , euphoniously call ' love , ' and proceed to the demolition 
 of the case . " 

 the REV . DECIMUS ROACH   hesitatingly . - " the demolition of the case ? 
 humph ! the passion be ingraft in the human system as part and parcel 
 of it , and be not to be demolish so easily as you seem to think . 
 Love , take rationally and morally by a man of good education and sound 
 principle , be - be - " 

 KENELM . - " well , be what ? " 

 the REV . DECIMUS ROACH . - " a - a - a - thing not to be despise . like the 
 sun , it be the great colourist of life , Mr. Chillingly . and you be so 
 right : the moral system do require daily exercise . what can give that 
 exercise to a solitary man , when he arrive at the practical age in 
 which he can not sit for six hour at a stretch musing on the divine 
 essence ; and rheumatism or other ailment forbid his adventure into 
 the wild of Africa as a missionary ? at that age , Nature , which will 
 be hear , Mr. Chillingly , demand her right . a sympathize female 
 companion by one 's side ; innocent little child climb one 's 
 knee , - lovely , bewitch picture ! who can be Goth enough to rub it out , 
 who fanatic enough to paint over it the image of a Saint Simeon sit 
 alone on a pillar ? take another glass . you do not   drink enough , Mr. 
 Chillingly . " 

 " I have drunk enough , " reply Kenelm , in a sullen voice , " to think I 
 see double . I imagine that before I sit the austere adversary of the 
 insanity of love and the misery of wedlock . now , I fancy I listen to 
 a pule sentimentalist utter the platitude which the other Decimus 
 Roach have already refute . certainly either I see double , or you amuse 
 yourself with mock my appeal to your wisdom . " 

 " not so , Mr. Chillingly . but the fact be , that when I write that book 
 of which you speak I be young , and youth be enthusiastic and one - side . 
 now , with the same disdain of the excess to which love may hurry weak 
 intellect , I recognize its benignant effect when take , as I before 
 say , rationally , - take rationally , my young friend . at that period 
 of life when the judgment be mature , the soothe companionship of 
 an amiable female can not but cheer the mind , and prevent that morose 
 hoar - frost into which solitude be chill and make rigid by increase 
 year . in short , Mr. Chillingly , have convince myself that I err 
 in the opinion once too rashly put forth , I owe it to Truth , I owe it to 
 Mankind , to make my conversion know to the world . and I be about next 
 month to enter into the matrimonial state with a young lady who - " 

 " say no more , say no more , Mr. Roach . it must be a painful subject to 
 you . let we drop it . " 

 " it be not a painful subject at all ! " exclaim Mr. Roach , with warmth . 
 " I look forward to the fulfilment of my duty with the pleasure which 
 a well - train mind always ought to feel in recant a fallacious 
 doctrine . but you do I the justice to understand that of course I do 
 not take this step I propose - for my personal satisfaction . no , sir , 
 it be the value of my example to other which purify my motif and 
 animate my soul . " 

 after this concluding and noble sentence , the conversation droop . host 
 and guest both feel they have have enough of each other . Kenelm soon rise 
 to depart . 

 Mr. Roach , on take leave of , he at the door , say , with marked 
 emphasis , - 

 " not for my personal satisfaction , - remember that . whenever you hear my 
 conversion discuss in the world , say that from my own lip you hear 
 these word , - not for MY PERSONAL satisfaction . no ! my kind regard to 
 Welby , - a married man himself , and a father : he will understand I . " 



 CHAPTER IX . 

 on quit Oxford , Kenelm wander for several day about the country , 
 advance to no definite goal , meet with no noticeable adventure . 
 at last he find himself mechanically retrace his step . a magnetic 
 influence he could not resist draw he back towards the grassy mead and 
 the sparkling rill of Moleswich . 

 " there must be , " say he to himself , " a mental , like an optical , 
 illusion . in the last , we fancy we have see a spectre . if we dare not 
 face the apparition , - dare not attempt to touch it , - run superstitiously 
 away from it , - what happen ? we shall believe to our die day that it 
 be not an illusion , that it be a spectre ; and so we may be craze for 
 life . but if we manfully walk up to the phantom , stretch our hand 
 to seize it , oh ! it fade into thin air , the cheat of our eyesight be 
 dispel , and we shall never be ghost - ride again . so it must be with 
 this mental illusion of mine . I see an image strange to my experience : 
 it seem to I , at first sight , clothe with a supernatural charm ; like 
 an unreasone coward , I run away from it . it continue to haunt I ; I 
 can not shut out its apparition . it pursue I by day alike in the haunt 
 of man , - alike in the solitude of nature ; it visit I by night in my 
 dream . I begin to say this must be a real visitant from another world : 
 it must be love ; the love of which I read in the Poets , as in the poet 
 I read of witchcraft and ghost . surely I must approach that apparition 
 as a philosopher like Sir David Brewster would approach the black 
 cat seat on a hearth - rug , which he tell we that some lady of his 
 acquaintance constantly see till she go into a world into which black 
 cat be not hold to be admit . the more I think of it the less 
 it appear to I possible that I can be really in love with a wild , 
 half - educate , anomalous creature , merely because the apparition of 
 her face haunt I . with perfect safety , therefore , I can approach the 
 creature ; in proportion as I see more of she the illusion will vanish . I 
 will go back to Moleswich manfully . " 

 thus say Kenelm to himself , and himself answer , - " go ; for thou canst 
 not help it . think thou that dace can escape the net that have mesh 
 a Roach ? no , - 


  ' come it will , the day decree by fate , ' 


 when thou must succumb to the ' nature which will be hear . ' well 
 succumb now , and with a good grace , than resist till thou hast reach 
 thy fiftieth year , and then make a rational choice not for thy personal 
 satisfaction . " 

 Whereupon Kenelm answer to himself , indignantly , " Pooh ! thou flippant . 
 my alter ego , thou know not what thou art talk about ! it be 
 not a question of nature ; it be a question of the supernatural , - an 
 illusion , - a phantom ! " thus Kenelm and himself continue to quarrel with 
 each other ; and the more they quarrel , the nearer they approach 
 to the haunted spot in which have be see , and flee from , the fatal 
 apparition of first love . 




 BOOK VI . 



 CHAPTER I. 

 SIR PETER have not hear from Kenelm since a letter inform he that 
 his son have leave town on an excursion , which would probably be short , 
 though it might last a few week ; and the good Baronet now resolve to 
 go to London himself , take his chance of Kenelm 's return , and if 
 still absent , at least learn from Mivers and other how far that very 
 eccentric planet have contrive to steer a regular course amidst the 
 fix star of the metropolitan system . he have other reason for his 
 journey . he wish to make the acquaintance of Chillingly Gordon 
 before hand he over the L20,000 which Kenelm have release in that 
 resettlement of estate , the necessary deed of which the young heir have 
 sign before quit London for Moleswich . Sir Peter wish still more 
 to see Cecilia Travers , in whom Kenelm 's account of she have inspire a 
 very strong interest . 

 the day after his arrival in town Sir Peter breakfast with miver . 

 " upon my word you be very comfortable here , " say Sir Peter , glance 
 at the well - appoint table , and round the well - furnish room . 

 " naturally so : there be no one to prevent my be comfortable . I be not 
 marry ; taste that omelette . " 

 " some man declare they never know comfort till they be marry , Cousin 
 Miners . " 

 " some man be reflect body , and catch a pallid gleam from the 
 comfort which a wife concentre on herself . with a fortune so modest and 
 secure , what comfort , possess by I now , would not a Mrs. Chillingly 
 Mivers ravish from my hold and appropriate to herself ! instead of these 
 pleasant room , where should I be lodge ? in a dingy den look on 
 a backyard exclude from the sun by day and vocal with cat by night ; 
 while Mrs. Mivers luxuriate in two drawing - room with southern aspect 
 and perhaps a boudoir . my brougham would be tear from my us and 
 monopolize by ' the angel of my hearth , ' cloud in her crinoline and 
 halve by her chignon . no ! if ever I marry - and I never deprive myself 
 of the civility and needlework which single lady waste upon I by 
 say I shall not marry - it will be when woman have fully establish 
 their right ; for then man may have a chance of vindicate their own . 
 then if there be two drawing - room in the house I shall take one ; 
 if not , we will toss up who shall have the back parlour ; if we keep a 
 brougham , it will be exclusively mine three day in the week ; if Mrs. M. 
 want l200 a year for her wardrobe she must be content with one , the

 lot , while I take my holiday on the croquet ground at Wimbledon . yes , 
 when the present wrong of woman be exchange for equality with man , I

 " I do not   deny that there be such a thing as humbug . and whenever I meet

 this come of live in the country . "

 take it altogether , and include the australian bushman ; and I do not

 ah ! on this , a word for what it be worth to you , my young reader . you ,
