 ring for his servant , give order for their delivery , and then follow 
 Sir Peter down stair and into the brougham . not a word would he say 
 more about Gordon , and Sir Peter shrink from tell he about the 
 L20,000 . chillingly Mivers be perhaps the last person to whom Sir 
 Peter would be tempt to parade an act of generosity . miver might not 
 unfrequently do a generous act himself , provide it be not divulge ; 
 but he have always a sneer for the generosity of other . 



 CHAPTER II . 

 wander back towards Moleswich , Kenelm find himself a little before 
 sunset on the bank of the garrulous brook , almost opposite to the house 
 inhabit by Lily Mordaunt . he stand long and silently by the grassy 
 margin , his dark shadow fall over the stream , break into fragment 
 by the eddy and strife of wave , fresh from their leap down the 
 neighbouring waterfall . his eye rest on the house and the garden lawn 
 in the front . the upper window be open . " I wonder which be her , " he 
 say to himself . at last he catch a glimpse of the gardener , bend 
 over a flower border with his watering - pot , and then move slowly 
 through the little shrubbery , no doubt to his own cottage . now the lawn 
 be solitary , save that a couple of thrush drop suddenly on the 
 sward . 

 " good evening , sir , " say a voice . " a capital spot for trout this . " 

 Kenelm turn his head , and beheld on the footpath , just behind he , 
 a respectable elderly man , apparently of the class of a small retail 
 tradesman , with a fishing - rod in his hand and a basket belt to his 
 side . 

 " for trout , " reply Kenelm ; " I dare say . a strangely attractive spot 
 indeed . " 

 " be you an angler , sir , if I may make bold to inquire ? " ask the 
 elderly man , somewhat perhaps puzzle as to the rank of the stranger ; 
 noticing , on the one hand , his dress and his mien , on the other , sling 
 to his shoulder , the worn and shabby knapsack which Kenelm have carry , 
 at home and abroad , the precede year . 

 " ay , I be an angler . " 

 " then this be the good place in the whole stream . look , sir , there 
 be Izaak Walton 's summer - house ; and far down you see that white , 
 neat - look house . well , that be my house , sir , and I have an apartment 
 which I let to gentleman angler . it be generally occupy throughout 
 the summer month . I expect every day to have a letter to engage it , 
 but it be vacant now . a very nice apartment , sir , - sit - room and 
 bedroom . " 

 " Descende ceolo , et dic age tibia , " say Kenelm . 

 " Sir ? " say the elderly man . 

 " I beg you ten thousand pardon . I have have the misfortune to have be 
 at the university , and to have learn a little Latin , which sometimes 
 come back very inopportunely . but , speak in plain English , what 
 I mean to say be this : I invoke the Muse to descend from heaven and 
 bring with she - the original say a fife , but I mean - a fishing - rod . I 
 should think your apartment would suit I exactly ; pray show it to I . " 

 " with the great pleasure , " say the elderly man . " the Muse need not 
 bring a fishing - rod ! we have all sort of tackle at your service , and a 
 boat too , if you care for that . the stream hereabout be so shallow and 
 narrow that a boat be of little use till you get far down . " 

 " I do not   want to get far down ; but should I want to get to the 
 opposite bank , without wad across , would the boat take I or be there 
 a bridge ? " 

 " the boat can take you . it be a flat - bottomed punt , and there be a 
 bridge too for foot - passenger , just opposite my house ; and between 
 this and Moleswich , where the stream widen , there be a ferry . the stone 
 bridge for traffic be at the farth end of the town . " 

 " good . let we go at once to your house . " 

 the two man walk on . 

 " by the by , " say Kenelm , as they walk , " do you know much of the 
 family that inhabit the pretty cottage on the opposite side , which we 
 have just leave behind ? " 

 " Mrs. Cameron 's . yes , of course , a very good lady ; and Mr. Melville , the 
 painter . I be sure I ought to know , for he have often lodge with I 
 when he come to visit Mrs. Cameron . he recommend my apartment to his 
 friend , and they be my good lodger . I like painter , sir , though I 
 do not   know much about painting . they be pleasant gentleman , and easily 
 content with my humble roof and fare . " 

 " you be quite right . I do not   know much about painting myself ; but I be 
 inclined to believe that painter , judge not from what I have see of 
 they , for I have not a single acquaintance among they personally , but 
 from what I have read of their life , be , as a general rule , not only 
 pleasant but noble gentleman . they form within themselves desire to 
 beautify or exalt commonplace thing , and they can only accomplish their 
 desire by a constant study of what be beautiful and what be exalt . 
 a man constantly so engaged ought to be a very noble gentleman , even 
 though he may be the son of a shoeblack . and live in a high world 
 than we do , I can conceive that he be , as you say , very well content 
 with humble roof and fare in the world we inhabit . " 

 " exactly , sir ; I see - I see now , though you put it in a way that never 
 strike I before . " 

 " and yet , " say Kenelm , look benignly at the speaker , " you seem to 
 I a well - educate and intelligent man ; reflective on thing in general , 
 without be unmindful of your interest in particular , especially when 
 you have lodging to let . do not be offend . that sort of man be not 
 perhaps bear to be a painter , but I respect he highly . the world , sir , 
 require the vast majority of its inhabitant to live in it , - to live 
 by it . ' each for himself , and God for we all . ' the great happiness 
 of the great number be well secure by a prudent consideration for 
 Number one . " 

 somewhat to Kenelm 's surprise   allow that he have now learn enough 
 of life to be occasionally surprise   the elderly man here make a dead 
 halt , stretch out his hand cordially , and cry , " hear , hear ! I see 
 that , like I , you be a decide democrat . " 

 " Democrat ! Pray , may I ask , not why you be one , - that would be a 
 liberty , and democrats resent any liberty take with themselves ; but why 
 you suppose I be ? " 

 " you speak of the great happiness of the great number . that be 
 a democratic sentiment surely ! besides , do not you say , sir , that 
 painter , - painter , sir , painter , even if they be the son of 
 shoeblack , be the true gentleman , - the true nobleman ? " 

 " I do not say that exactly , to the disparagement of other gentleman and 
 noble . but if I do , what then ? " 

 " Sir , I agree with you . I despise rank ; I despise duke and earl and 
 aristocrat . ' an honest man 's the noble work of God . ' some poet say 
 that . I think Shakspeare . wonderful man , Shakspeare . a tradesman 's 
 son , - butcher , I believe . eh ! my uncle be a butcher , and might have 
 be an alderman . I go along with you heartily , heartily . I be a 
 democrat , every inch of I . shake hand , sir , shake hand ; we be all 
 equal . ' each man for himself , and God for we all . ' " 

 " I have no objection to shake hand , " say Kenelm ; " but do not   let I owe 
 your condescension to false pretence . though we be all equal before 
 the law , except the rich man , who have little chance of justice as 
 against a poor man when submit to an english jury , yet I utterly deny 
 that any two man you select can be equal . one must beat the other 
 in something ; and , when one man beat another , democracy cease and 
 aristocracy begin . " 

 " Aristocracy ! I do not   see that . what do you mean by aristocracy ? " 

 " the ascendency of the well man . in a rude State the well man be 
 the strong ; in a corrupt State , perhaps the more roguish ; in modern 
 republic the jobber get the money and the lawyer get the power . in 
 well - order States alone aristocracy appear at its genuine worth : 
 the well man in birth , because respect for ancestry secure a high 
 standard of honour ; the well man in wealth , because of the immense 
 use to enterprise , energy , and the fine art , which rich man must be if 
 they follow their natural inclination ; the well man in character , the 
 well man in ability , for reason too obvious to define ; and these two 
 last will beat the other in the government of the State , if the state 
 be flourish and free . all these four class of well man constitute 
 true aristocracy ; and when a well government than a true aristocracy 
 shall be devise by the wit of man , we shall not be far off from 
 the Millennium and the reign of saint . but here we be at the 
 house , - your , be it not ? I like the look of it extremely . " 

 the elderly man now enter the little porch , over which clamber 
 honeysuckle and ivy intertwine , and usher Kenelm into a pleasant 
 parlour , with a bay window , and an equally pleasant bedroom behind it . 

 " will it do , sir ? " 

 " perfectly . I take it from this moment . my knapsack contain all I shall 
 need for the night . there be a portmanteau of mine at Mr. Somers 's shop , 
 which can be send here in the morning . " 

 " but we have not settle about the term , " say the elderly man , 
 begin to feel rather doubtful whether he ought thus to have 
 instal in his home a stalwart pedestrian of whom he know nothing , 
 and who , though talk glibly enough on other thing , have preserve an 
 ominous silence on the subject of payment . 

 " term ? true , name they . " 

 " include board ? " 

 " certainly . chameleon live on air ; democrat on wind bag . I have a 
 more vulgar appetite , and require mutton . " 

 " meat be very dear now - a - day , " say the elderly man , " and I be afraid , 
 for board and lodge I can not charge you less than L3 3s . , - say L3 a 
 week . my lodger usually pay a week in advance . " 

 " agree , " say Kenelm , extract three sovereign from his purse . " I 
 have din already : I want nothing more this evening ; let I detain you 
 no far . be kind enough to shut the door after you . " 

 when he be alone , Kenelm seat himself in the recess of the bay 
 window , against the casement , and look forth intently . yes ; he be 
 right : he could see from thence the home of Lily . not , indeed , more than 
 a white gleam of the house through the interstice of tree and shrub , 
 but the gentle lawn slop to the brook , with the great willow at the 
 end dip its bough into the water , and shut out all view beyond 
 itself by its bower of tender leaf . the young man bend his face on his 
 hand and muse dreamily : the evening deepen ; the star come forth ; 
 the ray of the moon now peer aslant through the arching dip of the 
 willow , silver their way as they steal to the wave below . 

 " shall I bring light , sir ? or do you prefer a lamp or candle ? " ask 
 a voice behind , - the voice of the elderly man 's wife . " do you like the 
 shutter close ? " 

 the question startle the dreamer . they seem mock his own old 
 mocking on the romance of love . lamp or candle , practical light for 
 prosaic eye , and shutter close against moon and star ! 

 " thank you , ma'am , not yet , " he say ; and rise quietly he place his 
 hand on the window - sill , swing himself through the open casement , and 
 pass slowly along the margin of the rivulet , by a path checker 
 alternately with shade and starlight ; the moon yet more slowly rise 
 above the willow , and lengthen its track along the wavelet . 



 CHAPTER III . 

 THOUGH Kenelm do not think it necessary at present to report to his 
 parent or his London acquaintance his recent movement and his present 
 resting - place , it never enter into his head to lurk perdu in the 
 immediate vicinity of Lily 's house , and seek opportunity of meet 
 she clandestinely . he walk to Mrs. Braefield 's the next morning , find 
 she at home , and say in rather a more off - hand manner than be habitual 
 to he , " I have hire a lodging in your neighbourhood , on the bank of 
 the brook , for the sake of its trout - fishing . so you will allow I to 
 call on you sometimes , and one of these day I hope you will give I the 
 dinner I so unceremoniously reject some day ago . I be then summon 
 away suddenly , much against my will . " 

 " yes ; my husband say that you shoot off from he with a wild exclamation 
 about duty . " 

 " quite true ; my reason , and I may say my conscience , be greatly 
 perplex upon a matter extremely important and altogether new to I . I 
 go to Oxford , - the place above all other in which question of 
 reason and conscience be most deeply consider , and perhaps 
 least satisfactorily solve . relieve in my mind by my visit to a 
 distinguished ornament of that university , I feel I might indulge in a 
 summer holiday , and here I be . " 

 " ah ! I understand . you have religious doubt , - think perhaps of turn 
 Roman Catholic . I hope you be not go to do so ? " 

 " my doubt be not necessarily of a religious nature . pagan have 
 entertain they . " 

 " whatever they be I be pleased to see they do not prevent your 
 return , " say Mrs. Braefield , graciously . " but where have you find a 
 lodging ; why not have come to we ? my husband would have be scarcely 
 less glad than myself to receive you . " 

 " you say that so sincerely , and so cordially , that to answer by a brief 
 ' I thank you ' seem rigid and heartless . but there be time in life 
 when one yearn to be alone , - to commune with one 's own heart , and , if 
 possible , be still ; I be in one of those moody time . Bear with I . " 

 Mrs. Braefield look at he with affectionate , kindly interest . she 
 have go before he through the solitary road of young romance . she 
 remember her dreamy , dangerous girlhood , when she , too , have yearn to 
 be alone . 

 " bear with you ; yes , indeed . I wish , Mr. Chillingly , that I be your 
 sister , and that you would confide in I . something trouble you . " 

 " trouble I , - no . my thought be happy one , and they may sometimes 
 perplex I , but they do not trouble . " 

 Kenelm say this very softly ; and in the warm light of his musing 
 eye , the sweet play of his tranquil smile , there be an expression 
 which do not belie his word . 

 " you have not tell I where you have find a lodging , " say Mrs. 
 Braefield , somewhat abruptly . 

 " do I not ? " reply Kenelm , with an unconscious start , as from an 
 abstract reverie . " with no undistinguished host , I presume , for when 
 I ask he this morning for the right address of this cottage , in order 
 to direct such luggage as I have to be send there , he give I his card 
 with a grand air , say , ' I be pretty well know at Moleswich , by 
 and beyond it . ' I have not yet look at his card . oh , here it 
 be , - ' Algernon Sidney Gale Jones , Cromwell Lodge ; ' you laugh . what do 
 you know of he ? " 

 " I wish my husband be here ; he would tell you more about he . Mr. 
 Jones be quite a character . " 

 " so I perceive . " 

 " a great radical , - very talkative and troublesome at the vestry ; but our 
 vicar , Mr. Emlyn , say there be no real harm in he , that his bark be 
 bad than his bite , and that his republican or radical notion must be 
 lay to the door of his godfather ! in addition to his name of Jones , he 
 be unhappily christen Gale ; Gale Jones be a noted radical orator 
 at the time of his birth . and I suppose Algernon Sidney be prefix 
 to Gale in order to devote the new - bear more emphatically to republican 
 principle . " 

 " naturally , therefore , Algernon Sidney Gale Jones baptize his house 
 Cromwell Lodge , see that Algernon Sidney hold the Protectorate in 
 especial abhorrence , and that the original Gale Jones , if an honest 
 radical , must have do the same , consider what rough usage the 
 advocate of Parliamentary Reform meet with at the hand of his Highness . 
 but we must be indulgent to man who have be unfortunately christen 
 before they have any choice of the name that be to rule their fate . 
 I myself should have be less whimsical have I not be name after a 
 Kenelm who believe in sympathetic powder . apart from his political 
 doctrine , I like my landlord : he keep his wife in excellent order . she 
 seem frightened at the sound of her own footstep , and glide to and 
 fro , a pallid image of submissive womanhood in list slipper . " 

 " great recommendation certainly , and Cromwell Lodge be very prettily 
 situate . by the by , it be very near Mrs. Cameron 's . " 

 " now I think of it , so it be , " say Kenelm , innocently . ah ! my friend 
 Kenelm , enemy of sham , and truth - teller , par excellence , what hast 
 thou come to ? how be the mighty fall ! " since you say you will dine 
 with we , suppose we fix the day after to - morrow , and I will ask Mrs. 
 Cameron and Lily . " 

 " the day after to - morrow : I shall be delighted . " 

 " an early hour ? " 

 " the early the well . " 

 " be six o'clock too early ? " 

 " too early ! certainly not ; on the contrary . good - day : I must now go to 
 Mrs. Somers ; she have charge of my portmanteau . " 

 then Kenelm rise . 

 " poor dear Lily ! " say Mrs. Braefield ; " I wish she be less of a 
 child . " 

 Kenelm reseat himself . 

 " be she a child ? I do not   think she be actually a child . " 

 " not in year ; she be between seventeen and eighteen : but my husband 
 say that she be too childish to talk to , and always tell I to take 
 she off his hand ; he would rather talk with Mrs. Cameron . " 

 " indeed ! " 

 " still I find something in she . " 

 " indeed ! " 

 " not exactly childish , nor quite womanish . " 

 " what then ? " 

 " I can not   exactly define . but you know what Mr. Melville and Mrs. Cameron 
 call she as a pet name ? " 

 " no . " 

 " Fairy ! fairy have no age ; fairy be neither child nor woman . " 

 " Fairy . she be call fairy by those who know she good ? Fairy ! " 

 " and she believe in fairy . " 

 " do she ? - so do I. Pardon I , I must be off . the day after 
 to - morrow , - six o'clock . " 

 " wait one moment , " say Elsie , go to her writing - table . " since you 
 pass Grasmere on your way home , will you kindly leave this note ? " 

 " I think Grasmere be a lake in the north ? " 

 " yes ; but Mr. Melville choose to call the cottage by the name of the 
 lake . I think the first picture he ever sell be a view of Wordsworth 's 
 house there . here be my note to ask Mrs. Cameron to meet you ; but if you 
 object to be my messenger - " 

 " object ! my dear Mrs. Braefield . as you say , I pass close by the 
 cottage . " 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 KENELM go with somewhat rapid pace from Mrs. Braefield 's to the shop 
 in the High Street keep by Will Somers . Jessie be behind the counter , 
 which be throng with customer . Kenelm give she a brief direction 
 about his portmanteau , and then pass into the back parlour , where 
 her husband be employ on his basket , - with the baby 's cradle in 
 the corner , and its grandmother rock it mechanically , as she read a 
 wonderful missionary tract full of tale of miraculous conversion : into 
 what sort of Christians we will not pause to inquire . 

 " and so you be happy , will ? " say Kenelm , seat himself between the 
 basket - maker and the infant ; the dear old mother beside he , read the 
 tract which link her dream of life eternal with life just open 
 in the cradle that she rock . he not happy ! how he pity the man who 
 could ask such a question . 

 " Happy , sir ! I should think so , indeed . there be not a night on which 
 Jessie and I , and mother too , do not pray that some day or other you may 
 be as happy . by and by the baby will learn to pray ' God bless papa , and 
 mamma , grandmamma , and Mr. Chillingly . ' " 

 " there be some one else much more deserving of prayer than I , though 
 need they less . you will know some day : pass it by now . to return to 
 the point : you be happy ; if I ask why , would you not say , ' because I 
 have marry the girl I love , and have never repent ' ? " 

 " well , sir , that be about it ; though , beg your pardon , I think it 
 could be put more prettily somehow . " 

 " you be right there . but perhaps love and happiness never yet find any 
 word that could fitly express they . good - bye , for the present . " 

 ah ! if it be as mere materialist , or as many middle - aged or elderly 
 folk , who , if materialist , be so without know it , unreflectingly 
 say , " the main element of happiness be bodily or animal health and 
 strength , " that question which chillingly put would appear a very 
 unmeaning or a very insulting one address to a pale cripple , who 
 however improve of late in health , would still be sickly and ail 
 all his life , - put , too , by a man of the rare conformation of physical 
 power that nature can adapt to physical enjoyment , - a man who , since 
 the age in which memory commence , have never know what it be to 
 be unwell , who could scarcely understand you if you talk of a 
 finger - ache , and whom those refinement of mental culture which 
 multiply the delight of the sens have endow with the most exquisite 
 conception of such happiness as mere nature and its instinct can give ! 
 but will do not think the question unmeaning or insulting . he , the poor 
 cripple , feel a vast superiority on the scale of joyous be over the 
 young Hercules , well bear , cultured , and wealthy , who could know so 
 little of happiness as to ask the crippled basket - maker if he be 
 happy . - he , bless husband and father ! 



 CHAPTER V. 

 LILY be seat on the grass under a chestnut - tree on the lawn . a white 
 cat , not long emerge from kittenhood , curl itself by her side . on her 
 lap be an open volume , which she be read with the great delight . 

 Mrs. Cameron come from the house , look round , perceive the girl , and 
 approach ; and either she move so gently , or Lily be so absorb in 
 the book , that the latter be not aware of her presence till she feel 
 a light hand on her shoulder , and , look up , recognize her aunt 's 
 gentle face . 

 " ah ! Fairy , Fairy , that silly book , when you ought to be at your French 
 verb . what will your guardian say when he come and find you have so 
 waste time ? " 

 " he will say that fairy never waste their time ; and he will scold you 
 for say so . " Therewith Lily throw down the book , spring to her foot , 
 wound her arm round Mrs. Cameron 's neck , and kiss she fondly . " there ! 
 be that waste time ? I love you so , aunty . in a day like this I think I 
 love everybody and everything ! " as she say this , she draw up her lithe 
 form , look into the blue sky , and with part lip seem to drink in 
 air and sunshine . then she wake up the doze cat , and begin chase it 
 round the lawn . 

 Mrs. Cameron stand still , regard she with moistened eye . just at 
 that moment Kenelm enter through the garden gate . he , too , stand 
 still , his eye fix on the undulating movement of Fairy 's exquisite 
 form . she have arrest her favourite , and be now at play with it , 
 shake off her straw hat , and draw the ribbon attach to it 
 tantalizingly along the smooth grass . her rich hair , thus release and 
 dishevel by the exercise , fall partly over her face in wavy ringlet ; 
 and her musical laugh and word of sportive endearment sound on 
 Kenelm 's ear more joyously than the thrill of the skylark , more sweetly 
 than the coo of the ring - dove . 

 he approach towards Mrs. Cameron . Lily turn suddenly and see he . 
 instinctively she smooth back her loosened tress , replace the straw 
 hat , and come up demurely to his side just as he have accost her aunt . 

 " Pardon my intrusion , Mrs. Cameron . I be the bearer of this note from 
 Mrs. Braefield . " while the aunt read the note , he turn to the niece . 

 " you promise to show I the picture , Miss Mordaunt . " 

 " but that be a long time ago . " 

 " too long to expect a lady 's promise to be keep ? " 

 Lily seem to ponder that question , and hesitate before she answer . 

 " I will show you the picture . I do not   think I ever break a promise yet , 
 but I shall be more careful how I make one in future . " 

 " why so ? " 

 " because you do not value mine when I make it , and that hurt I . " Lily 
 lift up her head with a bewitching stateliness , and add gravely , " I 
 be offend . " 

 " Mrs. Braefield be very kind , " say Mrs. Cameron ; ' she ask we to dine 
 the day after to - morrow . you would like to go , Lily ? " 

 " all grow - up people , I suppose ? no , thank you , dear aunt . you go alone , 
 I would rather stay at home . may I have little Clemmy to play with ? she 
 will bring Juba , and Blanche be very partial to Juba , though she do 
 scratch he . " 

 " very well , my dear , you shall have your playmate , and I will go by 
 myself . " 

 Kenelm stand aghast . " you will not go , Miss Mordaunt ; Mrs. Braefield 
 will be so disappointed . and if you do not   go , whom shall I have to talk 
 to ? I do not   like grow - up people well than you do . " 

 " you be go ? " 

 " certainly . " 

 " and if I go you will talk to I ? I be afraid of Mr. Braefield . he be so 
 wise . " 

 " I will save you from he , and will not utter a grain of wisdom . " 

 " Aunty , I will go . " 

 here Lily make a bound and catch up Blanche , who , take her kiss 
 resignedly , star with evident curiosity upon Kenelm . 

 here a bell within the house ring the announcement of luncheon . Mrs. 
 Cameron invite Kenelm to partake of that meal . he feel as Romulus might 
 have feel when first invite to taste the ambrosia of the god . yet 
 certainly that luncheon be not such as might have please Kenelm 
 Chillingly in the early day of the Temperance Hotel . but somehow or 
 other of late he have lose appetite ; and on this occasion a very modest 
 share of a very slender dish of chicken fricasseed , and a few cherry 
 daintily arrange on vine leaf , which Lily select for he , content 
 he , - as probably a very little ambrosia content Romulus while 
 feast his eye on Hebe . 

 Luncheon over , while Mrs. Cameron write her reply to Elsie , Kenelm 
 be conduct by Lily into her own own room , in vulgar parlance she 
  boudoir , though it do not look as if any one ever bouder'd there . 
 it be exquisitely pretty , - pretty not as a woman 's , but as a child 's 
 dream of the own own room she would like to have , - wondrously neat and 
 cool , and pure - looking ; a trellis paper , the trellis gay with rose and 
 woodbine , and bird and butterfly ; drapery of muslin , festoon with 
 dainty tassel and ribbon ; a dwarf bookcase , that seem well store , 
 at least as to binding ; a dainty little writing - table in french 
  marqueterie , look too fresh and spotless to have know hard 
 service . the casement be open , and in keep with the trellis paper ; 
 woodbine and rose from without encroached on the window - side , gently 
 stir by the faint summer breeze , and waft sweet odour into the 
 little room . Kenelm go to the window , and glance on the view beyond . 
 " I be right , " he say to himself ; " I divine it . " but though he 
 speak in a low inward whisper , Lily , who have watch his movement in 
 surprise , overheard . 

 " you divine it . divine what ? " 

 " nothing , nothing ; I be but talk to myself . " 

 " tell I what you divine : I insist upon it ! " and Fairy petulantly 
 stamp her tiny foot on the floor . 

 " do you ? then I obey . I have take a lodging for a short time on the 
 other side of the brook , - Cromwell Lodge , - and see your house as I 
 pass , I divine that your room be in this part of it . how soft here 
 be the view of the water ! ah ! yonder be Izaak Walton 's summer - house . " 

 " do not   talk about Izaak Walton , or I shall quarrel with you , as I do 
 with Lion when he want I to like that cruel book . " 

 " who be Lion ? " 

 " Lion , - of course , my guardian . I call he Lion when I be a little 
 child . it be on see in one of his book a print of a lion play 
 with a little child . " 

 " ah ! I know the design well , " say Kenelm , with a slight sigh . " it be 
 from an antique greek gem . it be not the lion that play with the child , 
 it be the child that master the lion , and the Greeks call the child 
 ' love . ' " 

 this idea seem beyond Lily 's perfect comprehension . she pause before 
 she answer , with the naivete of a child six year old , - 

 " I see now why I master Blanche , who will not make friend with any 
 one else : I love Blanche . ah , that remind I , - come and look at the 
 picture . " 

 she go to the wall over the writing - table , draw a silk curtain aside 
 from a small painting in a dainty velvet framework , and point to it , 
 cry with triumph , " look there ! be it not beautiful ? " 

 Kenelm have be prepare to see a landscape , or a group , or anything but 
 what he do see : it be the portrait of Blanche when a kitten . 

 little elevate though the subject be , it be treat with graceful 
 fancy . the kitten have evidently cease from play with the cotton 
 reel that lie between her paw , and be fix her gaze intently on a 
 bulfinch that have light on a spray within her reach . 

 " you understand , " say Lily , place her hand on his arm , and draw 
 he towards what she think the good light for the picture ; " it be 
 Blanche 's first sight of a bird . look well at her face ; do not   you see a 
 sudden surprise , - half joy , half fear ? she cease to play with the reel . 
 her intellect - or , as Mr. Braefield would say , ' her instinct ' - be for 
 the first time arouse . from that moment Blanche be no long a mere 
 kitten . and it require , oh , the most careful education , to teach she 
 not to kill the poor little bird . she never do now , but I have such 
 trouble with she . " 

 " I can not say honestly that I do see all that you do in the picture ; 
 but it seem to I very simply paint , and be , no doubt , a striking 
 likeness of Blanche at that early age . " 

 " so it be . Lion draw the first sketch from life with his pencil ; and 
 when he see how pleased I be with it - he be so good - he put it on 
 canvas , and let I sit by he while he paint it . then he take it away , 
 and bring it back finished and frame as you see , last May , a present 
 for my birthday . " 

 " you be bear in May - with the flower . " 

 " the good of all the flower be bear in May , - violet . " 

 " but they be bear in the shade , and cle to it . surely , as a child of 
 May , you love the sun ! " 

 " I love the sun ; it be never too bright nor too warm for I . but I do not

 as she thus shyly end , the character of her whole countenance be

 advance close to he , again lay her hand on his arm , and look up

 not mirthful , - 

 " how true ! you have feel that too ? where be that innermost self ,

 not tame the butterfly ; it long to get to the star . and then , - and

 puzzle you ? " 

 " very much . " 

 " be there no wise book about it that help to explain ? " 

 " no wise book in my very limited reading even hint at the puzzle . I

 " very much . I be no great judge of the art . but it please I at once ,

 " Lily choose to interpret his intention in her own way , and insist

 " do not   you see ? if you be call upon to describe truthfully the

 " admirably put ! " say Kenelm . " there be no doubt that a much fiercer 
 animal than a cat - a tiger , for instance , or a conquer hero - may be

 it be its natural instinct to prey . " 

 " yes , yes ; hear that , aunty ! you remember the Happy Family that we 
 see eight year ago , at Moleswich fair , with a cat not half so nice as

 " be not one 's innermost self one 's good self ? " 

 Kenelm smile approvingly . the fairy be rapidly deepen her spell

 every man 's heart , that , if you know it , would make you hate he . " what 
 Goethe say , still more what Goethe be report to have say , be never 
 to be take quite literally . no comprehensive genius - genius at once

 but the sun have no predilection for a dunghill . it only comprehend a 
 dunghill as it do a rose . still Kenelm have always regard that loose

 pass in the rear of the garden towards the bridge which lead to his

 " ah ! " he murmur at last , " if , as I hold , a man but tolerably good

 reckless wind or a vagrant bird , which , where it pass , leave behind 
 it the oak , the corn - sheaf , or the flower , - ah , if that be so , how 
 tenfold the good must be , if the man find the gentle and purer

 Shakspeares and day - labourer equally agree to call love ; which Newton 
 never recognize , and which descarte   his only rival in the realm

 CHAPTER IX . 

 there be a good many pretty villa in the outskirt of Moleswich , and 
 the owner of they be generally well off , and yet there be little of 
 what be call visit society ; owe perhaps to the fact that there

 himself as a stock - jobber , turn up its nose at the family of Mr. 
 B - - - , who have enrich himself still more as a linen - draper , while the 
 family of Mr. B - - - show a very cold shoulder to the family of Mr. 
 C - - - , who have become rich than either of they as a pawnbroker , 
 and whose wife wear diamond , but drop she h 's . England would be a 
 community so aristocratic that there would be no living in it , if one 
 could exterminate what be now call " aristocracy . " the Braefields be

 " I must not keep you long from your amusement . the sky be just cloud

 trout , and I prefer ramble about the lane and by - path to 


  " ' the noiseless angler 's solitary stand . ' 

 " I be an indefatigable walker , and the home scenery round the place have 
 many charm for I . besides , " add Kenelm , feel conscious that he

 " you be not intend , I presume , for any of the learn profession ? "

 all profession now - a - day be to have much about the same amount of 
 learn . the learning of the military profession be to be level

 Cabinet minister sneer at the us of Greek and Latin . and even

 " you seem to be badly provide with book here , " say the vicar ,

 half - a - dozen old - look volume , evidently belong not to the lodger

 translation of Chaucer or take lodging in Wardour Street . if you have

 the new idea in science , be our semi - human progenitor in the

 much edify by the loan . "

 " no such book ? you must be mistake . there must be plenty of they 
 somewhere . I grant all the wonderful power of invention bestow on 
 the creator of poetic romance ; still not the sovereign master in that 
 realm of literature - not Scott , not Cervantes , not Goethe , not even 
 Shakspeare - could have presume to rebuild the past without such 
 material as they find in the book that record it . and though I , no 
 less cheerfully , grant that we have now live among we a creator of 
 poetic romance immeasurably more inventive than they , - appeal to our 
 credulity in portent the most monstrous , with a charm of style the 
 most conversationally familiar , - still I can not conceive that even that 
 unrivalle romance - writer can so bewitch our understanding as to make 
 we believe that , if Miss Mordaunt 's cat dislike to wet her foot , it be 
 probably because in the prehistoric age her ancestor live in the dry 
 country of Egypt ; or that when some lofty orator , a Pitt or a gladstone , 
 rebut with a polished smile which reveal his canine tooth the rude 
 assault of an opponent , he betray his descent from a ' semi - human 
 progenitor ' who be accustomed to snap at his enemy . surely , surely 
 there must be some book still extant write by philosopher before the 
 birth of Adam , in which there be authority , even though but in mythic 
 fable , for such poetic invention . surely , surely some early chronicler 
 must depose that they see , see with their own eye , the great gorilla 
 who scratch off their hairy covering to please the eye of the young 
 lady of their specie , and that they note the gradual metamorphosis 
 of one animal into another . for , if you tell I that this illustrious 
 romance - writer be but a cautious man of science , and that we must accept 
 his invention accord to the sober law of evidence and fact , there 
 be not the most incredible ghost story which do not well satisfy the 
 common sense of a sceptic . however , if you have no such book , lend 
 I the most unphilosophical you possess , - on magic , for instance , - the 
 philosopher 's stone " - 

 " I have some of they , " say the vicar , laugh ; " you shall choose for 
 yourself . " 

 " if you be go homeward , let I accompany you part of the way : I 
 do not   yet know where the church and the vicarage be , and I ought to 
 know before I come in the evening . " 

 Kenelm and the vicar walk side by side , very sociably , across the 
 bridge and on the side of the rivulet on which stand Mrs. Cameron 's 
 cottage . as they skirt the garden pale at the rear of the cottage , 
 Kenelm suddenly stop in the middle of some sentence which have 
 interested Mr. Emlyn , and as suddenly arrest his step on the turf 
 that border the lane . a little before he stand an old peasant woman , 
 with whom lily , on the opposite side of the garden pale , be converse . 
 Mr. Emlyn do not at first see what Kenelm see ; turn round rather 
 to gaze on his companion , surprise by his abrupt halt and silence . the 
 girl put a small basket into the old woman 's hand , who then drop a 
 low curtsy , and utter low a " God bless you . " low though it be , Kenelm 
 overhear it , and say abstractedly to Mr. Emlyn , " be there a great 
 link between this life and the next than God 's blessing on the young , 
 breathe from the lip of the old ? " 



 CHAPTER X. 

 " and how be your good man , Mrs. Haley ? " say the vicar , who have now 
 reach the spot on which the old woman stand , - with Lily 's fair face 
 still bend down to she , - while Kenelm slowly follow he . 

 " thank you kindly , sir , he be well ; out of his bed now . the young lady 
 have do he a power of good - " 

 " hush ! " say Lily , colour . " make haste home now ; you must not keep 
 he wait for his dinner . " 

 the old woman again curtsy , and go off at a brisk pace . 

 " do you know , Mr. Chillingly , " say Mr. Emlyn , " that Miss Mordaunt be 
 the good doctor in the place ? though if she go on make so many cure 
 she will find the number of her patient rather burdensome . " 

 " it be only the other day , " say Lily , " that you scold I for the 
 good cure I have yet make . " 

 " I ? - oh ! I remember ; you lead that silly child Madge to believe that 
 there be a fairy charm in the arrowroot you send she . own you deserve 
 a scolding there . " 

 " no , I do not . I dress the arrowroot , and be I not Fairy ? I have just 
 get such a pretty note from Clemmy , Mr. Emlyn , ask I to come up this 
 evening and see her new magic lantern . will you tell she to expect I ? 
 and , mind , no scolding . " 

 " and all magic ? " say Mr. Emlyn ; " be it so . " 

 Lily and Kenelm have not hitherto exchange a word . she have reply with 
 a grave inclination of her head to his silent bow . but now she turn to 
 he shyly and say , " I suppose you have be fish all the morning ? " 

 " no ; the fish hereabout be under the protection of a Fairy , - whom I 
 dare not displease . " 

 Lily 's face brighten , and she extend her hand to he over the 
 paling . " good - day ; I hear aunty 's voice : those dreadful french verb ! " 

 she disappear among the shrub , amid which they hear the thrill of 
 her fresh young voice sing to herself . 

 " that child have a heart of gold , " say Mr. Emlyn , as the two man walk 
 on . " I do not exaggerate when I say she be the good doctor in the 
 place . I believe the poor really do believe that she be a fairy . of 
 course we send from the vicarage to our ail parishioner who require 
 it , food and wine ; but it never seem to do they the good that her 
 little dish make by her own tiny hand do ; and I do not   know if you 
 notice the basket that old woman take away , - Miss Lily teach Will 
 Somers to make the prettiest little basket ; and she put her jelly or 
 other savoury into dainty porcelain gallipot nicely fit into the 
 basket , which she trim with ribbon . it be the look of the thing that 
 tempt the appetite of the invalid , and certainly the child may well be 
 call Fairy at present ; but I wish Mrs. Cameron would attend a little 
 more strictly to her education . she can not   be a fairy forever . " 

 Kenelm sigh , but make no answer . 

 Mr. Emlyn then turn the conversation to erudite subject , and so they 
 come in sight of the town , when the vicar stop and point towards 
 the church , of which the spire rise a little to the left , with two aged 
 yew - tree half shadow the burial - ground , and in the rear a glimpse of 
 the vicarage see amid the shrub of its garden ground . 

 " you will know your way now , " say the vicar ; " excuse I if I quit you : 
 I have a few visit to make ; among other , to poor Haley , husband to the 
 old woman you see . I read to he a chapter in the Bible every day ; yet 
 still I fancy that he believe in fairy charm . " 

 " well believe too much , than too little , " say Kenelm ; and he turn 
 aside into the village and spend half - an - hour with Will , look at the 
 pretty basket Lily have teach Will to make . then , as he go slowly 
 homeward , he turn aside into the churchyard . 

 the church , build in the thirteenth century , be not large , but it 
 probably suffice for its congregation , since it betray no sign of 
 modern addition ; restoration or repair it need not . the century have 
 but mellow the tint of its solid wall , as little injure by the huge 
 ivy stem that shoot forth their aspire leaf to the very summit of 
 the stately tower as by the slender rose which have be train 
 to climb up a foot or so of the massive buttress . the site of the 
 burial - ground be unusually picturesque : shelter towards the north by 
 a rise ground clothe with wood , slop down at the south towards 
 the glebe pasture - ground through which run the brooklet , sufficiently 
 near for its brawling gurgle to be hear on a still day . Kenelm sit 
 himself on an antique tomb , which be evidently appropriate to some one 
 of high than common rank in bygone day , but on which the sculpture 
 be wholly obliterate . 

 the stillness and solitude of the place have their charm for his 
 meditative temperament ; and he remain there long , forgetful of time , 
 and scarcely hear the boom of the clock that warn he of its lapse . 

 when suddenly , a shadow - the shadow of a human form - fall on the grass 
 on which his eye dreamily rest . he look up with a start , and beheld 
 Lily stand before he mute and still . her image be so present in his 
 thought at the moment that he feel a thrill of awe , as if the thought 
 have conjure up her apparition . she be the first to speak . 

 " you here , too ? " she say very softly , almost whisperingly . " too ! " 
 echo Kenelm , rise ; " too ! ' tis no wonder that I , a stranger to 
 the place , should find my step attract towards its most venerable 
 building . even the most careless traveller , halt at some remote 
 abode of the living , turn aside to gaze on the burial - ground of the 
 dead . but my surprise be that you , Miss Mordaunt , should be attract 
 towards the same spot . " 

 " it be my favourite spot , " say Lily , " and always have be . I have sit 
 many an hour on that tombstone . it be strange to think that no one know 
 who sleep beneath it . the ' Guide Book to Moleswich , ' though it give 
 the history of the church from the reign in which it be first build , 
 can only venture a guess that this tomb , the grand and old in the 
 burial - ground , be tenant by some member of a family name Montfichet , 
 that be once very powerful in the county , and have become extinct since 
 the reign of Henry VI . but , " add Lily , " there be not a letter of the 
 name Montfichet leave . I find out more than any one else have do ; I 
 learn black - letter on purpose ; look here , " and she point to a small 
 spot in which the moss have be remove . " do you see those figure ? 
 be they not xviii ? and look again , in what be once the line above 
 the figure , ELE . it must have be an Eleanor , who die at the age of 
 eighteen - " 

 " I rather think it more probable that the figure refer to the date 
 of the death , 1318 perhaps ; and so far as I can decipher black - letter , 
 which be more in my father 's line than mine , I think it be AL , not EL , 
 and that it seem as if there have be a letter between L and the second 
 E , which be now effaced . the tomb itself be not likely to belong to any 
 powerful family then resident at the place . their monument , accord 
 to usage , would have be within the church , - probably in their own 
 mortuary chapel . " 

 " do not   try to destroy my fancy , " say Lily , shake her head ; " you 
 can not succeed , I know her history too well . she be young , and some one 
 love she , and build over her the fine tomb he could afford ; and see 
 how long the epitaph must have be ! how much it must have speak in 
 her praise and of his grief . and then he go his way , and the tomb be 
 neglect , and her fate forget . " 

 " my dear Miss Mordaunt , this be indeed a wild romance to spin out of so 
 slender a thread . but even if true , there be no reason to think that a 
 life be forget , though a tomb be neglect . " 

 " perhaps not , " say Lily , thoughtfully . " but when I be dead , if I can 
 look down , I think it would please I to see my grave not neglect by 
 those who have love I once . " 

 she move from he as she say this , and go to a little mound that 
 seem not long since raise ; there be a simple cross at the head and 
 a narrow border of flower round it . Lily knelt beside the flower and 
 pull out a stray weed . then she rise , and say to Kenelm , who have 
 follow , and now stand beside she , - 

 " she be the little grandchild of poor old Mrs. Hales . I could not cure 
 she , though I try hard : she be so fond of I , and die in my arm . 
 no , let I not say ' die , ' - surely there be no such thing as die . ' Tis 
 but a change of life , - 


  ' Less than the void between two wave of air , 
  the space between existence and a soul . ' " 


 " whose line be those ? " ask Kenelm . 

 " I do not   know ; I learn they from Lion . do not   you believe they to be 
 true ? " 

 " yes . but the truth do not render the thought of quit this scene 
 of life for another more pleasing to most of we . see how soft and gentle 
 and bright be all that live summer land beyond ; let we find subject 
 for talk from that , not from the graveyard on which we stand . " 

 " but be there not a summer land fair than that we see now ; and which 
 we do see , as in a dream , well when we take subject of talk from the 
 graveyard ? " without wait for a reply , Lily go on . " I plant these 
 flower : Mr. Emlyn be angry with I ; he say it be ' Popish . ' but he 
 have not the heart to have they take up ; I come here very often to see 
 to they . do you think it wrong ? poor little Nell ! she be so fond of 
 flower . and the Eleanor in the great tomb , she too perhaps know some 
 one who call she Nell ; but there be no flower round her tomb . poor 
 Eleanor ! " 

 she take the nosegay she wear on her bosom , and as she repasse the tomb 
 lay it on the mouldering stone . 



 CHAPTER XI . 

 they quit the burial - ground , take their way to Grasmere . Kenelm 
 walk by Lily 's side ; not a word pass between they till they come in 
 sight of the cottage . 

 then Lily stop abruptly , and lift towards he her charming face , 
 say , - 

 " I tell you I would think over what you say to I last night . I have 
 do so , and feel I can thank you honestly . you be very kind : I never 
 before think that I have a bad temper ; no one ever tell I so . but I 
 see now what you mean ; sometimes I feel very quickly , and then I show 
 it . but how do I show it to you , Mr. Chillingly ? " 

 " do you not turn your back to I when I seat myself next you in 
 Mrs. Braefield 's garden , vouchsafe I no reply when I ask if I have 
 offend ? " 

 Lily 's face become bath in blush , and her voice falter , as she 
 answer , - 

 " I be not offend ; I be not in a bad temper then : it be bad than 
 that . " 

 " bad ? what could it possibly be ? " 

 " I be afraid it be envy . " 

 " envy of what ? of whom ? " 

 " I do not   know how to explain ; after all , I fear aunty be right , and the 
 fairy tale put very silly , very naughty thought into one 's head . when 
 Cinderella 's sister go to the king 's ball , and Cinderella be leave 
 alone , do not she long to go too ? do not she envy her sister ? " 

 " ah ! I understand now : Sir Charles speak of the Court Ball . " 

 " and you be there talk with handsome lady - and - oh ! I be so 
 foolish and feel sore . " 

 " you , who when we first meet wonder how people who could live in 
 the country prefer to live in town , do then sometimes contradict 
 yourself , and sigh for the great world that lie beyond these quiet 
 water bank . you feel that you have youth and beauty , and wish to be 
 admire ! " 

 " it be not that exactly , " say Lily , with a perplexed look in her 
 ingenuous countenance , " and in my well moment , when the ' bettermost 
 self ' come forth , I know that I be not make for the great world you 
 speak of . but you see - " here she pause again , and as they have now 
 enter the garden , drop wearily on a bench beside the path . Kenelm 
 seat himself there too , wait for she to finish her broken sentence . 

 " you see , " she continue , look down embarrassed , and describe vague 
 circle on the gravel with her fairy - like foot , " that at home , ever 
 since I can remember , they have treat I as if - well , as if I 
 be - what shall I say ? the child of one of your great lady . even 
 Lion , who be so noble , so grand , seem to think when I be a mere 
 infant that I be a little queen : once when I tell a fib he do not 
 scold I ; but I never see he look so sad and so angry as when he say , 
 ' never again forget that you be a lady . ' and , but I tire you - " 

 " tire I , indeed ! go on . " 

 " no , I have say enough to explain why I have at time proud thought , 
 and vain thought ; and why , for instance , I say to myself , ' perhaps my 
 place of right be among those fine lady whom he - ' but it be all over 
 now . " she rise hastily with a pretty laugh , and bound towards Mrs. 
 Cameron , who be walk slowly along the lawn with a book in her hand . 



 CHAPTER XII . 

 it be a very merry party at the vicarage that evening . Lily have not 
 be prepare to meet Kenelm there , and her face brighten wonderfully 
 as at her entrance he turn from the book - shelf to which Mr. Emlyn 
 be direct his attention . but instead of meet his advance , she 
 dart off to the lawn , where Clemmy and several other child greet 
 she with a joyous shout . 

 " not acquaint with Macleane 's Juvenal ? " say the reverend scholar ; 
 " you will be greatly pleased with it ; here it be , - a posthumous work , 
 edit by George Long . I can lend you Munro 's Lucretius , ' 69 . aha ! we 
 have some scholar yet to pit against the Germans . " 

 " I be heartily glad to hear it , " say Kenelm . " it will be a long time 
 before they will ever wish to rival we in that game which Miss Clemmy 
 be now form on the lawn , and in which England have recently acquire a 
 european reputation . " 

 " I do not   take you . what game ? " 

 " Puss in the Corner . with your leave I will look out and see whether 
 it be a win game for puss - in the long - run . " Kenelm join the 
 child , amidst whom Lily seem not the least childlike . resist all 
 overture from Clemmy to join their play , he seat himself on a sloping 
 bank at a little distance , - an idle looker - on . his eye follow Lily 's 
 nimble movement , his ear drink in the music of her joyous laugh . could 
 that be the same girl whom he have see tend the flower - bed amid the 
 gravestone ? Mrs. Emlyn come across the lawn and join he , seat 
 herself also on the bank . Mrs. Emlyn be an exceedingly clever woman : 
 nevertheless she be not formidable , - on the contrary , pleasing ; and 
 though the lady in the neighbourhood say ' she talk like a book , ' 
 the easy gentleness of her voice carry off that offence . 

 " I suppose , Mr. Chillingly , " say she , " I ought to apologize for 
 my husband 's invitation to what must seem to you so frivolous an 
 entertainment as a child 's party . but when Mr. Emlyn ask you to come 
 to we this evening , he be not aware that Clemmy have also invite she 
 young friend . he have look forward to rational conversation with you 
 on his own favourite study . " 

 " it be not so long since I leave school , but that I prefer a half holiday 
 to lesson , even from a tutor so pleasant as Mr. Emlyn , - 


  " ' ah , happy year , - once more who would not be a boy ! ' " 


 " Nay , " say Mrs. Emlyn , with a grave smile . " who that have start so 
 fairly as Mr. Chillingly in the career of man would wish to go back and 
 resume a place among boy ? " 

 " but , my dear Mrs. Emlyn , the line I quote be wring from the heart of 
 a man who have already outstrip all rival in the race - ground he have 
 choose , and who at that moment be in the very maytime of youth and of 
 fame . and if such a man at such an epoch in his career could sigh to ' be 
 once more a boy , ' it must have be when he be think of the boy 's 
 half holiday , and recoil from the task work he be condemn to learn 
 as man . " 

 " the line you quote be , I think , from ' Childe Harold , ' and surely 
 you would not apply to mankind in general the sentiment of a poet so 
 peculiarly self - reflect   if I may use that expression , and in whom 
 sentiment be often so morbid . " 

 " you be right , Mrs. Emlyn , " say Kenelm , ingenuously . " still a boy 's 
 half holiday be a very happy thing ; and among mankind in general 
 there must be many who would be glad to have it back again , - Mr. Emlyn 
 himself , I should think . " 

 " Mr. Emlyn have his half holiday now . do you not see he stand just 
 outside the window ? do you not hear he laugh ? he be a child again 
 in the mirth of his child . I hope you will stay some time in the 
 neighbourhood ; I be sure you and he will like each other . and it be such 
 a rare delight to he to get a scholar like yourself to talk to . " 

 " Pardon I , I be not a scholar ; a very noble title that , and not to be 
 give to a lazy trifl on the surface of book - lore like myself . " 

 " you be too modest . my husband have a copy of your Cambridge prize 
 verse , and say ' the Latinity of they be quite beautiful . ' I quote his 
 very word . " 

 " latin verse - making be a mere knack , little more than a proof that one 
 have an elegant scholar for one 's tutor , as I certainly have . but it be by 
 special grace that a real scholar can send forth another real scholar , 
 and a Kennedy produce a Munro . but to return to the more interesting 
 question of half holiday ; I declare that Clemmy be lead off your 
 husband in triumph . he be actually go to be Puss in the Corner . " 

 " when you know more of Charles , - I mean my husband , - you will discover 
 that his whole life be more or less of a holiday . perhaps because he be 
 not what you accuse yourself of be : he be not lazy ; he never wish 
 to be a boy once more ; and taskwork itself be holiday to he . he enjoy 
 shut himself up in his study and reading ; he enjoy a walk with 
 the child ; he enjoy visit the poor ; he enjoy his duty as a 
 clergyman . and though I be not always content for he , though I think 
 he should have have those honour in his profession which have be 
 lavish on man with less ability and less learning , yet he be never 
 discontent himself . shall I tell you his secret ? " 

 " do . " 

 " he be a thanks - give Man . you , too , must have much to thank God 
 for , Mr. Chillingly ; and in thanksgiving to God do there not blend 
 usefulness to man , and such sense of pastime in the usefulness as make 
 each day a holiday ? " 

 Kenelm look up into the quiet face of this obscure pastor 's wife with 
 a startled expression in his own . 

 " I see , ma'am , " say he , " that you have devote much thought to the 
 study of the aesthetical philosophy as expound by german thinker , 
 whom it be rather difficult to understand . " 

 " I , Mr. Chillingly ! good gracious ! no ! what do you mean by your 
 aesthetical philosophy ? " 

 " accord to aesthetic , I believe man arrive at his high state 
 of moral excellence when labour and duty lose all the harshness of 
 effort , - when they become the impulse and habit of life ; when as the 
 essential attribute of the beautiful , they be , like beauty , enjoy 
 as pleasure ; and thus , as you express , each day become a holiday : a 
 lovely doctrine , not perhaps so lofty as that of the Stoics , but more 
 bewitching . only , very few of we can practically merge our care and our 
 worry into so serene an atmosphere . " 

 " some do so without know anything of aesthetic and with no pretence 
 to be stoic ; but , then , they be Christians . " 

 " there be some such Christians , no doubt ; but they be rarely to be meet 
 with . take Christendom altogether , and it appear to comprise the most 
 agitated population in the world ; the population in which there be the 
 great grumbling as to the quantity of labour to be do , the 
 loud complaint that duty instead of a pleasure be a very hard and 
 disagreeable struggle , and in which holiday be fewest and the moral 
 atmosphere least serene . perhaps , " add Kenelm , with a deep shade of 
 thought on his brow , " it be this perpetual consciousness of struggle ; 
 this difficulty in merge toil into ease , or stern duty into placid 
 enjoyment ; this refusal to ascend for one 's self into the calm of an air 
 aloof from the cloud which darken , and the hail - storm which beat 
 upon , the fellow - man we leave below , - that make the troubled life of 
 Christendom dearer to Heaven , and more conducive to Heaven 's design in 
 render earth the wrestling - ground and not the resting - place of man , 
 than be that of the Brahmin , ever seek to abstract himself from 
 the Christian 's conflict of action and desire , and to carry into its 
 extreme practice the aesthetic theory , of bask undisturbed in the 
 contemplation of the most absolute beauty human thought can reflect from 
 its idea of divine good ! " 

 whatever Mrs. Emlyn might have say in reply be interrupt by the rush 
 of the child towards she ; they be tired of play , and eager for tea 
 and the magic lantern . 



 CHAPTER XIII . 

 the room be duly obscure and the white sheet attach to the wall ; the 
 child be seat , hush , and awe - stricken . and Kenelm be place next 
 to Lily . 

 the trite thing in our mortal experience be among the most 
 mysterious . there be more mystery in the growth of a blade of grass than 
 there be in the wizard 's mirror or the feat of a spirit medium . Most of 
 we have know the attraction that draw one human being to another , and 
 make it so exquisite a happiness to sit quiet and mute by another 's 
 side ; which still for the moment the busy thought in our brain , the 
 most turbulent desire in our heart , and render we but conscious of a 
 present ineffable bliss . Most of we have know that . but who have ever 
 be satisfied with any metaphysical account of its why or wherefore ? we 
 can but say it be love , and love at that early section of its history 
 which have not yet escape from romance ; but by what process that other 
 person have become single out of the whole universe to attain such 
 special power over one be a problem that , though many have attempt to 
 solve it , have never attain to solution . in the dim light of the room 
 Kenelm could only distinguish the outline of Lily 's delicate face , but 
 at each new surprise in the show , the face intuitively turn to his , 
 and once , when the terrible image of a sheeted ghost , pursue a guilty 
 man , pass along the wall , she draw close to he in her childish 
 fright , and by an involuntary innocent movement lay her hand on his . he 
 detain it tenderly , but , alas ! it be withdraw the next moment ; 
 the ghost be succeed by a couple of dancing dog . and Lily 's ready 
 laugh - partly at the dog , partly at her own previous alarm - vex 
 Kenelm 's ear . he wish there have be a succession of ghost , each more 
 appalling than the last . 

 the entertainment be over , and after a slight refreshment of cake 
 and wine - and - water the party break up ; the child visitor go away 
 attend by servant - maid who have come for they . Mrs. Cameron and Lily 
 be to walk home on foot . 

 " it be a lovely night , Mrs. Cameron , " say Mr. Emlyn , " and I will attend 
 you to your gate . " 

 " permit I also , " say Kenelm . 

 " ay , " say the vicar , " it be your own way to Cromwell Lodge . " 

 the path lead they through the churchyard as the near approach to the 
 brook - side . the moonbeam shimmer through the yew - tree and rest on 
 the old tomb ; play , as it be , round the flower which Lily 's hand 
 have that day drop upon its stone . she be walk beside Kenelm , the 
 eld two a few pace in front . 

 " how silly I be , " say she , " to be so frightened at the false ghost ! I 
 do not   think a real one would frighten I , at least if see here , in this 
 love moonlight , and on God 's ground ! " 

 " ghost , be they permit to appear except in a magic lantern , could 
 not harm the innocent . and I wonder why the idea of their apparition 
 should always have be associate with such phantasy of horror , 
 especially by sinless child , who have the least reason to dread 
 they . " 

 " oh , that be true , " cry lily ; " but even when we be grow up there 
 must be time in which we should so long to see a ghost , and feel what a 
 comfort , what a joy it would be . " 

 " I understand you . if some one very dear to we have vanish from our 
 life ; if we feel the anguish of the separation so intensely as to efface 
 the thought that life , as you say so well , ' never die ; ' well , yes , 
 then I can conceive that the mourner would yearn to have a glimpse of 
 the vanish one , be it but to ask the sole and only question he could 
 desire to put , ' art thou happy ? may I hope that we shall meet again , 
 never to part , - never ? ' " 

 Kenelm 's voice tremble as he speak , tear stand in his eye . a 
 melancholy - vague , unaccountable , overpowering - pass across his heart , 
 as the shadow of some dark - wing bird pass over a quiet stream . 

 " you have never yet feel this ? " ask Lily doubtingly , in a soft voice , 
 full of tender pity , stop short and look into his face . 

 " I ? no . I have never yet lose one whom I so love and so yearn to see 
 again . I be but think that such loss may befall we all ere we too 
 vanish out of sight . " 

 " Lily ! " call forth Mrs. Cameron , halt at the gate of the 
 burial - ground . 

 " yes , auntie ? " 

 " Mr. Emlyn want to know how far you have get in ' Numa Pompilius . ' come 
 and answer for yourself . " 

 " oh , those tiresome grow - up people ! " whisper Lily , petulantly , to 
 Kenelm . " I do like Mr. Emlyn ; he be one of the very good of man . but 
 still he be grow up , and his ' Numa Pompilius ' be so stupid . " 

 " my first french lesson - book . no , it be not stupid . read on . it have 
 hint of the prettiest fairy tale I know , and of the fairy in especial 
 who bewitch my fancy as a boy . " 

 by this time they have gain the gate of the burial - ground . 

 " what fairy tale ? what fairy ? " ask Lily , speak quickly . 

 " she be a fairy , though in heathen language she be call a 
 nymph , - Egeria . she be the link between man and god to he she love ; 
 she belong to the race of god . true , she , too , may vanish , but she can 
 never die . " 

 " well , Miss Lily , " say the vicar , " and how far in the book I lend 
 you , - ' Numa Pompilius . ' " 

 " ask I this day next week . " 

 " I will ; but mind you be to translate as you go on . I must see the 
 translation . " 

 " very well . I will do my good , " answer Lily meekly . lily now walk 
 by the vicar 's side , and Kenelm by Mrs. Cameron 's , till they reach 
 grasmere . 

 " I will go on with you to the bridge , Mr. Chillingly , " say the vicar , 
 when the lady have disappear within their garden . " we have little 
 time to look over my book , and , by the by , I hope you at least take the 
 Juvenal . " 

 " no , Mr. Emlyn ; who can quit your house with an inclination for satire ? 
 I must come some morning and select a volume from those work which give 
 pleasant view of life and bequeath favourable impression of mankind . 
 your wife , with whom I have have an interesting conversation , upon the 
 principle of aesthetical philosophy - " 

 " my wife ! Charlotte ! she know nothing about aesthetical philosophy . " 

 " she call it by another name , but she understand it well enough to 
 illustrate the principle by example . she tell I that labour and duty 
 be so take up by you - 


  ' in den heitern Regionen 
  wo die reinen Formen wohnen , ' 


 that they become joy and beauty , - be it so ? " 

 " I be sure that Charlotte never say anything half so poetical . but , in 
 plain word , the day pass with I very happily . I should be ungrateful 
 if I be not happy . Heaven have bestow on I so many source of 
 love , - wife , child , book , and the calling which , when one quit 
 one 's own threshold , carry love along with it into the world beyond ; 
 a small world in itself , - only a parish , - but then my call link it 
 with infinity . " 

 " I see ; it be from the source of love that you draw the supply for 
 happiness . " 

 " surely ; without love one may be good , but one could scarcely be happy . 
 no one can dream of a heaven except as the abode of love . what writer be 
 it who say , ' how well the human heart be understand by he who first 
 call God by the name of Father ' ? " 

 " I do not remember , but it be beautifully say . you evidently do not 
 subscribe to the argument in Decimus Roach 's ' approach to the Angels . ' " 

 " ah , Mr. Chillingly ! your word teach I how lacerate a man 's happiness 
 may be if he do not keep the claw of vanity closely par . I actually 
 feel a keen pang when you speak to I of that eloquent panegyric on 
 celibacy , ignorant that the only thing I ever publish which I fancy 
 be not without esteem by intellectual reader be a Reply to ' the 
 Approach to the Angels , ' - a youthful book , write in the first year 
 of my marriage . but it obtain success : I have just revise the tenth 
 edition of it . " 

 " that be the book I will select from your library . you will be please 
 to hear that Mr. Roach , whom I see at Oxford a few day ago , recant his 
 opinion , and , at the age of fifty , be about to be marry ; he beg I 
 to add , ' not for his own personal satisfaction . ' " 

 " go to be marry ! - Decimus Roach ! I think my Reply would convince 
 he at last . " 

 " I shall look to your Reply to remove some linger doubt in my own 
 mind . " 

 " Doubts in favour of celibacy ? " 

 " well , if not for layman , perhaps for a priesthood . " 

 " the most forcible part of my Reply be on that head : read it 
 attentively . I think that , of all section of mankind , the clergy be 
 those to whom , not only for their own sake , but for the sake of the 
 community , marriage should be most commend . why , sir , " continue the 
 vicar , warm up into oratorical enthusiasm , " be you not aware that 
 there be no home in England from which man who have serve and adorn 
 their country have issue forth in such prodigal number as those of 
 the clergy of our Church ? what other class can produce a list so crowd 
 with eminent name as we can boast in the son we have rear and send 
 forth into the world ? how many statesman , soldier , sailor , lawyer , 
 physician , author , man of science , have be the son of us village 
 pastor ? naturally : for with we they receive careful education ; they 
 acquire of necessity the simple taste and discipline habit which lead 
 to industry and perseverance ; and , for the most part , they carry with 
 they throughout life a purer moral code , a more systematic reverence for 
 thing and thought religious , associate with their early image 
 of affection and respect , than can be expect from the son of layman 
 whose parent be wholly temporal and worldly . Sir , I maintain that this 
 be a cogent argument , to be consider well by the nation , not only in 
 favour of a married clergy , - for , on that score , a million of Roaches 
 could not convert public opinion in this country , - but in favour of the 
 Church , the Established Church , which have be so fertile a nursery 
 of illustrious layman ; and I have often think that one main and 
 undetected cause of the low tone of morality , public and private , 
 of the great corruption of manner , of the more prevalent scorn 
 of religion which we see , for instance , in a country so civilized as 
 France , be , that its clergy can train no son to carry into the contest 
 of earth the steadfast belief in accountability to Heaven . " 

 " I thank you with a full heart , " say Kenelm . " I shall ponder well over 
 all that you have so earnestly say . I be already disposed to give up 
 all linger crotchet as to a bachelor clergy ; but , as a layman , 
 I fear that I shall never attain to the purified philanthropy of Mr. 
 Decimus Roach , and , if ever I do marry , it will be very much for my 
 personal satisfaction . " 

 Mr. Emlyn laugh good - humouredly , and , as they have now reach the 
 bridge , shake hand with Kenelm , and walk homeward , along the 
 brook - side and through the burial - ground , with the alert step and the 
 uplifted head of a man who have joy in life and admit of no fear in 
 death . 



 CHAPTER XIV . 

 for the next two week or so Kenelm and Lily meet not indeed so often 
 as the reader might suppose , but still frequently ; five time at Mrs. 
 Braefield 's , once again at the vicarage , and twice when Kenelm have 
 call at Grasmere ; and , be invite to stay to tea at one of those 
 visit , he stay the whole evening . Kenelm be more and more fascinated 
 in proportion as he see more and more of a creature so exquisitely 
 strange to his experience . she be to he not only a poem , but a poem in 
 the Sibylline Books ; enigmatical , perplex conjecture , and somehow or 
 other mysteriously blend its interest with vision of the future . 

 Lily be indeed an enchanting combination of opposite rarely blend 
 into harmony . her ignorance of much that girl know before they number 
 half her year be so relieved by candid , innocent simplicity , so 
 adorn by pretty fancy and sweet belief , and so contrast and light 
 up by gleam of a knowledge that the young lady we call well educate 
 seldom exhibit , - knowledge derive from quick observation of external 
 Nature , and impressionable susceptibility to its varying and subtle 
 beauty . this knowledge have be perhaps first instill , and 
 subsequently nourish , by such poetry as she have not only learn by 
 heart , but take up as inseparable from the healthful circulation of her 
 thought ; not the poetry of our own day , - most young lady know enough 
 of that , - but select fragment from the verse of old , most of they 
 from poet now little read by the young of either sex , poet dear to 
 spirit like Coleridge or Charles Lamb , - none of they , however , so dear 
 to she as the solemn melody of Milton . much of such poetry she have 
 never read in book : it have be teach she in childhood by her guardian 
 the painter . and with all this imperfect , desultory culture , there be 
 such dainty refinement in her every look and gesture , and such deep 
 woman - tenderness of heart . since Kenelm have commend " Numa Pompilius " 
 to her study , she have take very lovingly to that old - fashioned romance , 
 and be fond of talk to he about Egeria as of a creature who have 
 really exist . 

 but what be the effect that he , - the first man of year correspondent 
 to her own with whom she have ever familiarly converse , - what be the 
 effect that Kenelm Chillingly produce on the mind and the heart of 
 Lily ? 

 this be , after all , the question that puzzle he the most , - not 
 without reason : it might have puzzle the shrewd bystander . the 
 artless candour with which she manifest she like to he be at 
 variance with the ordinary character of maiden love ; it seem more the 
 fondness of a child for a favourite brother . and it be this uncertainty 
 that , in his own thought , justify Kenelm for linger on , and 
 believe that it be necessary to win , or at least to learn more of , 
 her secret heart before he could venture to disclose his own . he do not 
 flatter himself with the pleasing fear that he might be endanger 
 her happiness ; it be only his own that be risk . then , in all those 
 meeting , all those conversation to themselves , there have pass none

 man 's eye love would force its way , Lily 's frank , innocent gaze chill 
 it back again to its inward cell . joyously as she would spring 
 forward to meet he , there be no tell - tale blush on her cheek , no 
 self - betray tremor in her clear , sweet - toned voice . no ; there have not 
 yet be a moment when he could say to himself , " she love I . " often he 
 say to himself , " she know not yet what love be . " 

 in the interval of time not pass in Lily 's society , Kenelm would 
 take long ramble with Mr. Emlyn , or saunter into Mrs. Braefield 's 
 drawing - room . for the former he conceive a more cordial sentiment of 
 friendship than he entertain for any man of his own age , - a friendship 
 that admit the noble element of admiration and respect . 

 Charles Emlyn be one of those character in which the colour appear 
 pale unless the light be bring very close to they , and then each 
 tint seem to change into a warm and rich one . the manner which , at 
 first , you would call merely gentle , become unaffectedly genial ; 
 the mind you at first might term inert , though well - inform , you now 
 acknowledge to be full of disciplined vigour . Emlyn be not , however , 
 without his little amiable foible ; and it be , perhaps , these that make 
 he lovable . he be a great believer in human goodness , and very easily 
 impose upon by cunning appeal to " his well - know benevolence . " he 
 be dispose to overrate the excellence of all that he once take to his 
 heart . he think he have the good wife in the world , the good child , 
 the good servant , the good beehive , the good pony , and the good 
 house - dog . his parish be the most virtuous , his church the most 
 picturesque , his vicarage the prettiest , certainly , in the whole 
 shire , - perhaps , in the whole kingdom . probably it be this philosophy 
 of optimism which contribute to lift he into the serene realm of 
 aesthetic joy . 

 he be not without his dislikes as well as liking . though a liberal 
 Churchman towards protestant dissenter , he cherish the odium 
 theologicum for all that savoured of Popery . perhaps there be another 
 cause for this besides the purely theological one . early in life a young 
 sister of his have be , to use his phrase , ' secretly entrap " into 
 conversion to the roman catholic faith , and have since enter a convent . 
 his affection have be deeply wound by this loss to the range of 
 they . Mr. Emlyn have also his little infirmity of self - esteem rather 
 than of vanity . though he have see very little of any world beyond that 
 of his parish , he pique himself on his knowledge of human nature and of 
 practical affair in general . certainly no man have read more about they , 
 especially in the book of the ancient classic . perhaps it be owe 
 to this that he so little understand Lily , - a character to which the 
 ancient classic afford no counterpart nor clue ; and perhaps it be 
 this also that make Lily think he ' so terribly grow up . " thus , despite 
 his mild good - nature , she do not get on very well with he . 

 the society of this amiable scholar please Kenelm the more , because 
 the scholar evidently have not the remotest idea that Kenelm 's sojourn at 
 Cromwell Lodge be influence by the vicinity to Grasmere . Mr. Emlyn be 
 sure that he know human nature , and practical affair in general , too

 for wife a girl without fortune or rank , the orphan ward of a low - bear 
 artist only just struggle into reputation ; or , indeed , that a 
 Cambridge prizeman , who have evidently read much on grave and dry 
 subject , and who have no less evidently see a great deal of polished

 pretty wayward innocent child , the companion and friend of his Clemmy . 

 Mrs. Braefield be more discerning ; but she have a good deal of tact , and 
 do not as yet scare Kenelm away from her house by let he see how 
 much she have discern . she would not even tell her husband , who , absent

 appearance and manner of the aunt , and the exquisite delicacy of the 
 niece 's form and feature , with the undefinable air of distinction

 Braefield also have the wit to discover that , under the infantine way

 " I be so glad to think , " she say one day , when Kenelm have join she
