 have make such friend with Mr. Emlyn . though all hereabout like he so 
 much for his goodness , there be few who can appreciate his learning . 
 to you it must be a surprise as well as pleasure to find , in this quiet 
 humdrum place , a companion so clever and well - inform : it compensate 
 for your disappointment in discover that our brook yield such bad 
 sport . " 

 " do not   disparage the brook ; it yield the pleasantest bank on which 
 to lie down under old pollard oak at noon , or over which to saunter 
 at morn and eve . where those charm be absent even a salmon could 
 not please . yes ; I rejoice to have make friend with Mr. Emlyn . I have 
 learn a great deal from he , and be often ask myself whether I 
 shall ever make peace with my conscience by put what I have learn 
 into practice . " 

 " may I ask what special branch of learning be that ? " 

 " I scarcely know how to define it . suppose we call it ' worth - whileism . ' 
 among the New Ideas which I be recommend to study as those that must 
 govern my generation , the not - worth - while Idea hold a very high rank ; 
 and be myself naturally of calm and equable constitution , that new 
 idea make the basis of my philosophical system . but since I have become 
 intimate with Charles Emlyn I think there be a great deal to be say in 
 favour of worth - whileism , old idea though it be . I see a man who , with 
 very commonplace material for interest or amusement at his command , 
 continue to be always interested or generally amuse ; I ask myself why 
 and how ? and it seem to I as if the cause start from fix belief 
 which settle his relation with God and man , and that settlement he will 
 not allow any speculation to disturb . be those belief questionable or 
 not by other , at least they be such as can not displease a deity , and 
 can not fail to be kindly and useful to fellow - mortal . then he plant 
 these belief on the soil of a happy and genial home , which tend to 
 confirm and strengthen and call they into daily practice ; and when he 
 go forth from home , even to the farth verge of the circle that 
 surround it , he carry with he the home influence of kindliness 
 and use . possibly my line of life may be draw to the verge of a wide 
 circle than his ; but so much the well for interest and amusement , if 
 it can be draw from the same centre ; namely , fix belief daily warm 
 into vital action in the sunshine of a congenial home . " 

 Mrs. Braefield listen to this speech with pleased attention , and as 
 it come to its close , the name of Lily tremble on her tongue , for she 
 divine that when he speak of home Lily be in his thought ; but she 
 check the impulse , and reply by a generalized platitude . 

 " certainly the first thing in life be to secure a happy and congenial 
 home . it must be a terrible trial for the good of we if we marry without 
 love . " 

 " terrible , indeed , if the one love and the other do not . " 

 " that can scarcely be your case , Mr. Chillingly , for I be sure you could 
 not marry where you do not love ; and do not think I flatter you when I 
 say that a man far less gifted than you can scarcely fail to be love by 
 the woman he woo and win . " 

 Kenelm , in this respect one of the modest of human being , shake his 
 head doubtingly , and be about to reply in self - disparagement , when , 
 lift his eye and look round , he halt mute and still as if 
 root to the spot . they have enter the trellis circle through the 
 rose of which he have first catch sight of the young face that have 
 haunt he ever since . 

 " ah ! " he say abruptly ; " I can not stay long here , dream away the 
 work - day hour in a fairy ring . I be go to town to - day by the next

 " Yoa be come back ? " 

 " of course , - this evening . I leave no address at my lodging in London .

 have listen to I ! " 

 " shall we fix a day next week for see the remain of the old Roman

 CHAPTER XV . 

 KENELM do indeed find a huge pile of letter and note on reach his 
 forsaken apartment in Mayfair ; many of they merely invitation for day 
 long past , none of they of interest except two from Sir Peter , three 
 from his mother , and one from Tom Bowles . 

 Sir Peter 's be short . in the first he gently scold Kenelm for go 
 away without communicate any address ; and state the acquaintance he 
 have form with Gordon , the favourable impression that young gentleman 
 have make on he , the transfer of the L20,000 and the invitation give to 
 Gordon , the traverse , and Lady Glenalvon . the second , date much later , 
 note the arrival of his invite guest , dwelt with warmth unusual to 
 Sir Peter on the attraction of Cecilia , and take occasion to refer , 
 not the less emphatically because as it be incidentally , to the sacred

 consent to propose to Cecilia Travers hold I a tyrant and rebel . " 

 Lady Chillingly 's letter be much long . they dwelt more 
 complainingly on his persistence in eccentric habit ; so exceedingly 
 unlike other people , quit London at the very height of the season , 
 go without even a servant nobody know where : she do not wish to 
 wound his feeling ; but still those be not the way natural to a young

 Miss Chillinglys . they have be ask by Sir Peter , unknown to she - so 
 like he - to meet their guest ; to meet Lady Glenalvon and Miss Travers , 
 whose dress be so perfect   here she describe their dress ; and they 
 come in pea - green with pelerine of mock blonde , and Miss Sally with 
 corkscrew ringlet and a wreath of jessamine , " which no girl after 
 eighteen would venture to wear . " 

 " but , my dear , " add her ladyship , " your poor father 's family be 
 certainly great oddity . I have more to put up with than any one know . 
 I do my good to carry it off . I know my duty , and will do they . " 

 family grievance thus duly record and lament , Lady Chillingly 
 return to her guest . 

 evidently unconscious of her husband 's design on Cecilia , she dismiss

 " not at all spoilt by the education of the great world , which , alas ! 
 obedient to the duty of wife and mother , however little my sacrifice 
 be appreciate , I have long since relinquish . Lady Glenalvon suggest 
 turn that hideous old moat into a fernery , - a great improvement . of 
 course your poor father make objection . " 

 Tom 's letter be write on black - edge paper , and run thus : - 


 DEAR SIR , - since I have the honour to see you in London I have have a sad 
 loss : my poor uncle be no more . he die very suddenly after a hearty 
 supper . one doctor say it be apoplexy , another valvular disease of the 
 heart . he have leave I his heir , after provide for his sister : no one 
 have an idea that he have save so much money . I be quite a rich man now . 
 and I shall leave the veterinary business , which of late - since I 
 take to reading , as you kindly advise - be not much to my like the 
 principal corn - merchant here have offer to take I into partnership ; 
 and , from what I can see , it will be a very good thing and a great rise 
 in life . but , sir , I can not   settle to it at present ; I can not   settle , as 
 I would wish to anything . I know you will not laugh at I when I say I 
 have a strange longing to travel for a while . I have be read book 
 of travel , and they get into my head more than any other book . but I 
 do not   think I could leave the country with a contented heart till I have 
 have just another look at you know whom , - just to see she , and know she 
 be happy . I be sure I could shake hand with Will and kiss her little 
 one without a wrong thought . what do you say to that , dear sir ? you 
 promise to write to I about she . but I have not hear from you . susey , 
 the little girl with the flower - ball , have have a loss too : the poor old 
 man she live with die within a few day of my dear uncle 's decease . 
 Mother move here , as I think you know , when the forge at Graveleigh be 
 sell ; and she be go to take Susey to live with she . she be quite fond 
 of Susey . pray let I hear from you soon ; and do , dear sir , give I your 
 advice about travel - and about she . you see I should like she to 
 think of I more kindly when I be in distant part . 

  I remain , dear sir , 

  your grateful servant , 

  T. BOWLES . 

 P.S. - Miss Travers have send I Will be last remittance . there be 
 very little owe I now ; so they must be thrive . I hope she be not 
 overwork . 


 on return by the train that evening , Kenelm go to the house of Will 
 Somers . the shop be already close , but he be admit by a trusty 
 servant - maid to the parlour , where he find they all at supper , except 
 indeed the baby , who have long since retire to the cradle , and the

 sleeping flower . 

 " you have a pleasant home here , Mrs. Somers . " 

 " we have , indeed , and know how to bless he we owe it to . " 

 " I be rejoice to think that . how often when God design a 
 special kindness to we he put the kindness into the heart of a 
 fellow - man , - perhaps the last fellow - man we should have think of ; but 
 in bless he we thank God who inspire he . now , my dear friend , I 
 know that you all three suspect I of be the agent whom God choose for 
 his benefit . you fancy that it be from I come the loan which enable 
 you to leave Graveleigh and settle here . you be mistaken , - you look 
 incredulous . " 

 " it could not be the squire , " exclaim Jessie . " Miss Travers assure 
 I that it be neither he nor herself . oh , it must be you , sir . I beg 
 pardon , but who else could it be ? " 

 " your husband shall guess . suppose , will , that you have behave ill 
 to some one who be nevertheless dear to you , and on think over it 
 afterwards feel very sorry and much ashamed of yourself , and suppose 
 that later you have the opportunity and the power to render a service to 
 that person , do you think you would do it ? " 

 " I should be a bad man if I do not . " 

 " Bravo ! and suppose that when the person you thus serve come to know 
 it be you who render the service , he do not feel thankful , he do 
 not think it handsome of you , thus to repair any little harm he might 
 have do you before , but become churlish and sore and cross - grained , 
 and with a wretched false pride say that because he have offend you 
 once he resent your take the liberty of befriend he now , would 
 you not think that person an ungrateful fellow ; ungrateful not only to 
 you his fellow - man , - that be of less moment , - but ungrateful to the 
 God who put it into your heart to be his human agent in the benefit 
 receive ? " 

 " well , sir , yes , certainly , " say will , with all the superior refinement 
 of his intellect to that of Jessie , unaware of what Kenelm be drive 
 at ; while Jessie , press her hand tightly together , turn pale , 
 and with a frightened hurried glance towards Will 's face , answer , 
 impulsively , - 

 " oh , Mr. Chillingly , I hope you be not think , not speak , of Mr. 
 Bowles ? " 

 " whom else should I think or speak of ? " 

 will rise nervously from his chair , all his feature writhe . 

 " Sir , sir , this be a bitter blow , - very bitter , very . " 

 Jessie rush to Will , fling her arm round he and sob . Kenelm 
 turn quietly to old Mrs. Somers , who have suspend the work on which 
 since supper she have be employ , knit sock for the baby , - 

 " my dear Mrs. Somers , what be the good of be a grandmother and 
 knitting sock for baby grandchild , if you can not assure those silly 
 child of yours that they be too happy in each other to harbour any 
 resentment against a man who would have part they , and now repent ? " 

 somewhat to Kenelm 's admiration , I dare not say surprise , old Mrs. 
 Somers , thus appeal to , rise from her seat , and , with a dignity of 
 thought or of feel no one could have anticipate from the quiet 
 peasant woman , approach the wed pair , lift Jessie 's face with one 
 hand , lay the other on Will 's head , and say , " if you do not   long to see 
 Mr. Bowles again and say ' the Lord bless you , sir ! ' you do not   deserve 
 the Lord 's blessing upon you . " Therewith she go back to her seat , and 
 resume her knitting . 

 " thank Heaven , we have pay back the good part of the loan , " say will , 
 in very agitated tone , " and I think , with a little pinch , Jessie , 
 and with sell off some of the stock , we might pay the rest ; and 
 then , " - and then he turn to Kenelm , - " and then , sir , we will "   here a 
 gulp   " thank Mr. Bowles . " 

 " this do not   satisfy I at all , will , " answer Kenelm ; " and since I 
 help to bring you two together , I claim the right to say I would never 
 have do so could I have guess you could have trust your wife so 
 little as to allow a remembrance of Mr. Bowles to be a thought of pain . 
 you do not feel humiliate when you imagine that it be to I you owe 
 some money which you have be honestly pay off . well , then , I will 
 lend you whatever trifle remain to discharge your whole debt to Mr. 
 Bowles , so that you may soon be able to say to he , ' thank you . ' 
 but between you and I , will , I think you will be a fine fellow and a 
 manly fellow if you decline to borrow that trifle of I ; if you feel 
 you would rather say ' thank you ' to Mr. Bowles , without the silly 
 notion that when you have pay he his money you owe he nothing for his 
 kindness . " 

 will look away irresolutely . Kenelm go on : " I have receive a letter 
 from Mr. Bowles to - day . he have come into a fortune , and think of go 
 abroad for a time ; but before he go , he say he should like to shake

 that to remain always a secret . but between friend there need be no 
 secret . what say you , Will ? as head of this household , shall Mr. Bowles

 " kindly welcome , " say old Mrs. Somers , look up from the sock . 

 " Sir , " say will , with sudden energy , " look here ; you have never be in 
 love , I dare say . if you have , you would not be so hard on I . Mr. Bowles 
 be in love with my wife there . Mr. Bowles be a very fine man , and I be 
 a cripple . " 

 " oh , Will ! will ! " cry Jessie . 

 " but I trust my wife with my whole heart and soul ; and , now that

 welcome , - heartily welcome . " 

 " shake hand . now you speak like a man , Will . I hope to bring Bowles 
 here to supper before many day be over . " 

 and that night Kenelm write to Mr. Bowles : 


 MY dear TOM , - come and spend a few day with I at Cromwell Lodge , 
 Moleswich . Mr. and Mrs. Somers wish much to see and to thank you . I

  your true friend , 

  K. C. 



 CHAPTER XVI . 

 MRS . CAMERON be seat alone in her pretty drawing - room , with a book 
 lie open , but unheeded , on her lap . she be look away from its 
 page , seemingly into the garden without , but rather into empty space . 

 to a very acute and practise observer , there be in her countenance an 
 expression which baffle the common eye . 

 to the common eye it be simply vacant ; the expression of a quiet , 
 humdrum woman , who might have be think of some quiet humdrum 
 household detail , - find that too much for she , and be now not think 
 at all . 

 but to the true observer , there be in that face indication of 
 a troubled past , still haunt with ghost never to be lay at 
 rest , - indication , too , of a character in herself that have undergo 
 some revolutionary change ; it have not always be the character of a 
 woman quiet and humdrum . the delicate outline of the lip and nostril 
 evince sensibility , and the deep and downward curve of it bespoke 
 habitual sadness . the softness of the look into space do not tell of 
 a vacant mind , but rather of a mind subdue and over - burden by the 
 weight of a secret sorrow . there be also about her whole presence , in 
 the very quiet which make her prevalent external characteristic , the 
 evidence of manner form in a high - breed society , - the society in which 
 quiet be connect with dignity and grace . the poor understand this 
 well than her rich acquaintance at Moleswich , when they say , " Mrs. 
 Cameron be every inch a lady . " to judge by her feature she must once 
 have be pretty , not a showy prettiness , but decidedly pretty . now , 
 as the feature be small , all prettiness have fade away in cold gray 
 colouring , and a sort of tame and slumber timidity of aspect . she 
 be not only not demonstrative , but must have impose on herself as a 
 duty the suppression of demonstration . who could look at the formation 
 of those lip , and not see that they belong to the nervous , quick , 
 demonstrative temperament ? and yet , observe she again more closely , 
 that suppression of the constitutional tendency to candid betrayal of 
 emotion would the more enlist our curiosity or interest ; because , if 
 physiognomy and phrenology have any truth in they , there be little 
 strength in her character . in the womanly yieldingness of the 
 short curved upper lip , the plead timidity of the regard , the 
 disproportionate but elegant slenderness of the head between the ear 
 and the neck , there be the token of one who can not resist the will , 
 perhaps the whim , of another whom she either love or trust . 

 the book open on her lap be a serious book on the doctrine of grace , 
 write by a popular clergyman of what be term " the Low Church . " she 
 seldom read any but serious book , except where such care as she give 
 to Lily 's education compel she to read " Outlines of history and 
 Geography , " or the elementary french book use in seminary for 
 young lady . yet if any one have decoy Mrs. Cameron into familiar 
 conversation , he would have discover that she must early have receive 
 the education give to young lady of station . she could speak 
 and write french and italian as a native . she have read , and still 
 remember , such classic author in either language as be concede to 
 the use of pupil by the well - regulate taste of orthodox governess . 
 she have a knowledge of botany , such as botany be teach twenty year 
 ago . I be not sure that , if her memory have be fairly arouse , she 
 might not have come out strong in divinity and political economy , as 
 expound by the popular manual of Mrs. Marcet . in short , you could see 
 in she a thoroughbred English lady , who have be teach in a generation 
 before Lily 's , and immeasurably superior in culture to the ordinary run 
 of english young lady teach nowadays . so , in what after all be very 
 minor accomplishment , - now make major accomplishment , - such as music , 
 it be impossible that a connoisseur should hear her play on the piano 
 without remark , " that woman have have the good master of her time . " 
 she could only play piece that belong to her generation . she have 
 learn nothing since . in short , the whole intellectual culture have come 
 to a dead stop long year ago , perhaps before Lily be bear . 

 now , while she be gaze into space Mrs. Braefield be announce . Mrs. 
 Cameron do not start from revery . she never start . but she make a 
 weary movement of annoyance , resettle herself , and lay the serious 
 book on the sofa table . elsie enter , young , radiant , dress in all the 
 perfection of the fashion , that is , as ungracefully as in the eye of an 
 artist any gentlewoman can be ; but rich merchant who be proud of their 
 wife so insist , and their wife , in that respect , submissively obey 
 they . 

 the lady interchange customary salutation , enter into the customary 
 preliminary of talk , and after a pause Elsie begin in earnest . 

 " but sha'n't   I see Lily ? where be she ? " 

 " I fear she have go into the town . a poor little boy , who do our 
 errand , have meet with an accident , - fall from a cherry - tree . " 

 " which he be rob ? " 

 " probably . " 

 " and Lily have go to lecture he ? " 

 " I do not   know as to that ; but he be much hurt , and Lily have go to see 
 what be the matter with he . " 

 Mrs. Braefield , in her frank outspoken way , - " I do not   take much to girl 
 of Lily 's age in general , though I be passionately fond of child . you 
 know how I do take to Lily ; perhaps because she be so like a child . but 
 she must be an anxious charge to you . " 

 Mrs. Cameron reply by an anxious " no ; she be still a child , a very 
 good one ; why should I be anxious ? " 

 Mrs. Braefield , impulsively , - " why , your child must now be eighteen . " 

 Mrs. Cameron , - " Eighteen - be it possible ! how time fly ! though in a 
 life so monotonous as mine , time do not seem to fly , it slip on 
 like the lapse of water . let I think , - eighteen ? no , she be but 
 seventeen , - seventeen last May . " 

 Mrs. Braefield , - " Seventeen ! a very anxious age for a girl ; an age in 
 which doll cease and lover begin . " 

 Mrs. Cameron , not so languidly , but still quietly , - " Lily never care 
 much for doll , - never much for lifeless pet ; and as to lover , she 
 do not dream of they . " 

 Mrs. Braefield , briskly , - " there be no age after six in which girl do 
 not dream of lover . and here another question arise . when a girl so 
 lovely as Lily be eighteen next birthday , may not a lover dream of she ? " 

 Mrs. Cameron , with that wintry cold tranquillity of manner , which 
 imply that in put such question an interrogator be take a 
 liberty , - " as no lover have appear , I can not trouble myself about his 
 dream . " 

 say Elsie inly to herself , " this be the stupid woman I ever meet ! " 
 and aloud to Mrs. Cameron , - " do you not think that your neighbour , Mr. 
 Chillingly , be a very fine young man ? " 

 " I suppose he would be generally consider so . he be very tall . " 

 " a handsome face ? " 

 " handsome , be it ? I dare say . " 

 " what do Lily say ? " 

 " about what ? " 

 " about Mr. Chillingly . do she not think he handsome ? " 

 " I never ask she . " 

 " my dear Mrs. Cameron , would it not be a very pretty match for Lily ? the 
 Chillinglys be among the old family in Burke 's ' Landed Gentry , ' 
 and I believe his father , Sir Peter , have a considerable property . " 

 for the first time in this conversation Mrs. Cameron betray emotion . 
 a sudden flush overspread her countenance , and then leave it pale 
 than before . after a pause she recover her accustomed composure , and 
 reply , rudely , - 

 " it would be no friend to Lily who could put such notion into her 
 head ; and there be no reason to suppose that they have enter into Mr. 
 Chillingly 's . " 

 " would you be sorry if they do ? surely you would like your niece to 
 marry well , and there be few chance of she do so at Moleswich . " 

 " Pardon I , Mrs. Braefield , but the question of Lily 's marriage I have 
 never discuss , even with her guardian . nor , consider the childlike 
 nature of her taste and habit , rather than the year she have number , 
 can I think the time have yet come for discuss it at all . " 

 Elsie , thus rebuke , change the subject to some newspaper topic which 
 interest the public mind at the moment and very soon rise to depart . 
 Mrs. Cameron detain the hand that her visitor hold out , and say in 
 low tone , which , though embarrassed , be evidently earnest , " my dear 
 Mrs. Braefield , let I trust to your good sense and the affection with 
 which you have honour my niece not to incur the risk of unsettling 
 her mind by a hint of the ambitious project for her future on which you 
 have speak to I . it be extremely improbable that a young man of 
 Mr. Chillingly 's expectation would entertain any serious thought of 
 marry out of his own sphere of life , and - " 

 " Stop , Mrs. Cameron , I must interrupt you . Lily 's personal attraction 
 and grace of manner would adorn any station ; and have I not rightly 
 understand you to say that though her guardian , Mr. Melville , be , as we 
 all know , a man who have rise above the rank of his parent , your niece , 
 Miss Mordaunt , be like yourself , by birth a gentlewoman ? " 

 " yes , by birth a gentlewoman , " say Mrs. Cameron , raise her head with 
 a sudden pride . but she add , with as sudden a change to a sort of 
 freeze humility , " what do that matter ? a girl without fortune , 
 without connection , bring up in this little cottage , the ward of a 
 professional artist , who be the son of a city clerk , to whom she owe 
 even the home she have find , be not in the same sphere of life as Mr. 
 Chillingly , and his parent could not approve of such an alliance for 
 he . it would be most cruel to she , if you be to change the innocent 
 pleasure she may take in the conversation of a clever and well - inform 
 stranger into the troubled interest which , since you remind I of she 
 age , a girl even so childlike and beautiful as Lily might conceive in 
 one represent to she as the possible partner of her life . do not   commit 
 that cruelty ; do not   - do not , I implore you ! " 

 " trust I , " cry the warm - hearted Elsie , with tear rush to her 
 eye . " what you say so sensibly , so nobly , never strike I before . I 
 do not know much of the world , - know nothing of it till I marry , - and 
 be very fond of Lily , and have a strong regard for Mr. Chillingly , 
 I fancy I could not serve both well than - than - but I see now ; he 
 be very young , very peculiar ; his parent might object , not to lily 
 herself , but to the circumstance you name . and you would not wish 
 she to enter any family where she be not as cordially welcome as she 
 deserve to be . I be glad to have have this talk with you . happily , I 
 have do no mischief as yet . I will do none . I have come to propose 
 an excursion to the remain of the Roman Villa , some mile off , and to 
 invite you and Mr. Chillingly . I will no long try to bring he and 
 Lily together . " 

 " thank you . but you still misconstrue I . I do not think that Lily care 
 half so much for Mr. Chillingly as she do for a new butterfly . I do 
 not fear their come together , as you call it , in the light in which 
 she now regard he , and in which , from all I observe , he regard she . 
 my only fear be that a hint might lead she to regard he in another way , 
 and that way impossible . " 

 Elsie leave the house extremely bewilder , and with a profound contempt 
 for Mrs. Cameron 's knowledge of what may happen to two young person 
 " bring together . " 



 CHAPTER XVII . 

 now , on that very day , and about the same hour in which the conversation 
 just record between Elsie and Mrs. Cameron take place , Kenelm , in his 
 solitary noonday wandering , enter the burial - ground in which Lily have 
 some short time before surprise he . and there he find she , stand 
 beside the flower border which she have place round the grave of the 
 child whom she have tend and nurse in vain . 

 the day be cloudless and sunless ; one of those day that so often 
 instil a sentiment of melancholy into the heart of an english summer . 

 " you come here too often , Miss Mordaunt , " say Kenelm , very softly , as 
 he approach . 

 Lily turn her face to he , without any start of surprise , with no 
 brightening change in its pensive expression , - an expression rare to the 
 mobile play of her feature . 

 " not too often . I promise to come as often as I could ; and , as I tell 
 you before , I have never break a promise yet . " 

 Kenelm make no answer . presently the girl turn from the spot , and 
 Kenelm follow she silently till she halt before the old tombstone 
 with its effaced inscription . 

 " see , " she say , with a faint smile , " I have put fresh flower there . 
 since the day we meet in this churchyard , I have think so much of that 
 tomb , so neglected , so forgotten , and - " she pause a moment , and go 
 on abruptly , " do you not often find that you be much too - what be 
 the word ? ah ! too egotistical , consider and ponder and dream 
 greatly too much about yourself ? " 

 " yes , you be right there ; though , till you so accuse I , my conscience 
 do not detect it . " 

 " and do not   you find that you escape from be so haunted by the thought 
 of yourself , when you think of the dead ? they can never have any 
 share in your existence here . when you say , ' I shall do this or that 
 to - day ; ' when you dream , ' I may be this or that to - morrow , ' you be 
 think and dream , all by yourself , for yourself . but you be out of 
 yourself , beyond yourself , when you think and dream of the dead , who can 
 have nothing to do with your to - day or your to - morrow . " 

 as we all know , Kenelm Chillingly make it one of the rule of his life 
 never to be take by surprise . but when the speech I have write down 
 come from the lip of that tamer of butterfly , he be so startled that 
 all it occur to he to say , after a long pause , be , - 

 " the dead be the past ; and with the past rest all in the present or 
 the future that can take we out of our natural self . the past decide 
 our present . by the past we divine our future . history , poetry , science , 
 the welfare of state , the advancement of individual , be all connect 
 with tombstone of which inscription be effaced . you be right to 
 honour the moulder tombstone with fresh flower . it be only in the 
 companionship of the dead that one cease to be an egotist . " 

 if the imperfectly educate Lily have be above the quick comprehension 
 of the academical Kenelm in her speech , so Kenelm be now above the 
 comprehension of Lily . she , too , pause before she reply , - 

 " if I know you well , I think I could understand you well . I wish you 
 know Lion . I should like to hear you talk with he . " 

 while thus converse , they have leave the burial - ground , and be in the 
 pathway tread by the common wayfarer . 

 Lily resume , - " yes , I should like to hear you talk with Lion . " 

 " you mean your guardian , Mr. Melville ? " 

 " yes , you know that . " 

 " and why should you like to hear I talk to he ? " 

 " because there be some thing in which I doubt if he be altogether 
 right , and I would ask you to express my doubt to he ; you would , would 
 you not ? " 

 " but why can you not express they yourself to your guardian ; be you 
 afraid of he ? " 

 " Afraid , no indeed ! but - ah , how many people there be come this way ! 
 there be some tiresome public meeting in the town to - day . let we take 
 the ferry : the other side of the stream be much pleasanter ; we shall 
 have it more to ourselves . " 

 turn aside to the right while she thus speak , Lily descend a 
 gradual slope to the margin of the stream , on which they find an old 
 man dozily recline in his ferry - boat . 

 as , seat side by side , they be slowly bear over the still water 
 under a sunless sky , Kenelm would have renew the subject which his 
 companion have begin , but she shake her head , with a significant glance 
 at the ferryman . evidently what she have to say be too confidential to 
 admit of a listener , not that the old ferryman seem likely to take 
 the trouble of listen to any talk that be not address to he . 
 Lily soon do address her talk to he , " so , Brown , the cow have quite 
 recover . " 

 " yes , Miss , thank to you , and God bless you . to think of your beating 
 the old witch like that ! " 

 " ' tis not I who beat the witch , Brown ; ' tis the fairy . fairy , you 
 know , be much more powerful than witch . " 

 " so I find , Miss. " 

 Lily here turn to Kenelm ; " Mr. Brown have a very nice milch - cow that 
 be suddenly take very ill , and both he and his wife be convince 
 that the cow be bewitch . " 

 " of course it be , that stand to reason . do not Mother Wright tell my 
 old woman that she would repent of sell milk , and abuse her dreadful ; 
 and be not the cow take with shiver that very night ? " 

 " gently , Brown . Mother Wright do not say that your wife would repent of 
 sell milk , but of put water into it . " 

 " and how do she know that , if she be not a witch ? we have the good of 
 customer among the gentlefolk , and never any one that complain . " 

 " and , " answer Lily to Kenelm , unheede this last observation , which 
 be make in a sullen manner , " Brown have a horrid notion of entice 
 Mother Wright into his ferry - boat and throw she into the water , in 
 order to break the spell upon the cow . but I consult the fairy , and 
 give he a fairy charm to tie round the cow 's neck . and the cow be quite 
 well now , you see . so , Brown , there be no necessity to throw mother 
 Wright into the water , because she say you put some of it into the 
 milk . but , " she add , as the boat now touch the opposite bank , ' shall 
 I tell you , Brown , what the fairy say to I this morning ? " 

 " do , Miss. " 

 " it be this : if Brown 's cow yield milk without any water in it , and 
 if water get into it when the milk be sell , we , the fairy , will pinch 
 Mr. Brown black and blue ; and when Brown have his next fit of rheumatic 
 he must not look to the fairy to charm it away . " 

 Herewith Lily drop a silver groat into Brown 's hand , and sprang 
 lightly ashore , follow by Kenelm . 

 " you have quite convert he , not only as to the existence , but as to 
 the beneficial power of fairy , " say Kenelm . 

 " ah , " answer Lily very gravely , " ah , but would it not be nice if there 
 be fairy still ? good fairy , and one could get at they ? tell they 
 all that trouble and puzzle we , and win from they charm against the 
 witchcraft we practise on ourselves ? " 

 " I doubt if it would be good for we to rely on such supernatural 
 counsellor . our own soul be so boundless that the more we explore 
 they the more we shall find world spread upon world into 
 infinity ; and among the world be Fairyland . " he add , inly to 
 himself , " be I not in Fairyland now ? " 

 " hush ! " whisper Lily . " do not   speak more yet awhile . I be think over 
 what you have just say , and try to understand it . " 

 thus walk silently they gain the little summer - house which 
 tradition dedicate to the memory of Izaak Walton . Lily enter it and 
 seat herself ; Kenelm take his place beside she . it be a small octagon 
 building which , judge by its architecture , might have be build 
 in the troubled reign of Charles I. ; the wall plaster within be 
 thickly cover with name and date , and inscription in praise of 
 angling , in tribute to Izaak , or with quotation from his book . on 
 the opposite side they could see the lawn of Grasmere , with its great 
 willow dip into the water . the stillness of the place , with its 
 association of the angler 's still life , be in harmony with the quiet 
 day , its breezeless air , and cloud - vest sky . 

 " you be to tell I your doubt in connection with your guardian , 
 doubt if he be right in something which you leave unexplained , which 
 you could not yourself explain to he . " 

 Lily start as from thought alien to the subject thus reintroduce . 
 " yes , I can not mention my doubt to he because they relate to I , and 
 he be so good . I owe he so much that I could not bear to vex he by a 
 word that might seem like reproach or complaint . you remember , " here she 
 draw near to he ; and with that ingenuous confide look and movement 
 which have , not unfrequently , enrapture he at the moment , and sadden 
 he on reflection , - too ingenuous , too confiding , for the sentiment 
 with which he yearn to inspire she , - she turn towards he her frank 
 untimorous eye , and lay her hand on his arm : " you remember that I say 
 in the burial - ground how much I feel that one be constantly think 
 too much of one 's self . that must be wrong . in talk to you only about 
 myself I know I be wrong , but I can not help it : I must do so . do not 
 think ill of I for it . you see I have not be bring up like other 
 girl . be my guardian right in that ? perhaps if he have insist upon 
 not let I have my own wilful way , if he have make I read the book 
 which Mr. and Mrs. Emlyn want to force on I , instead of the poem and 
 fairy tale which he give I , I should have have so much more to think of 
 that I should have think less of myself . you say that the dead be 
 the past ; one forget one 's self when one think of the dead . if I have 
 read more of the past , have more subject of interest in the dead whose 
 history it tell , surely I should be less shut up , as it be , in my 
 own small , selfish heart ? it be only very lately I have think of this , 
 only very lately that I have feel sorrow and shame in the thought that I 
 be so ignorant of what other girl know , even little Clemmy . and I dare 
 not say this to Lion when I see he next , lest he should blame himself , 
 when he only mean to be kind , and use to say , ' I do not   want Fairy to 
 be learn , it be enough for I to think she be happy . ' and oh , I be so 
 happy , till - till of late ! " 

 " because till of late you only know yourself as a child . but , now that 
 you feel the desire of knowledge , childhood be vanish . do not vex 
 yourself . with the mind which nature have bestow on you , such learning 
 as may fit you to converse with those dread ' grow - up folk ' will come 
 to you very easily and quickly . you will acquire more in a month now 
 than you would have acquire in a year when you be a child , and 
 task - work be loathe , not court . your aunt be evidently well 
 instruct , and if I might venture to talk to she about the choice of 
 book - " 

 " no , do not   do that . Lion would not like it . " 

 " your guardian would not like you to have the education common to other 
 young lady ? " 

 " Lion forbid my aunt to teach I much that I rather wish to learn . 
 she want to do so , but she have give it up at his wish . she only now 
 tease I with those horrid french verbs , and that I know be a mere 
 make - belief . of course on Sunday it be different ; then I must not read 
 anything but the Bible and sermon . I do not   care so much for the sermon 
 as I ought , but I could read the Bible all day , every week - day as well 
 as Sunday ; and it be from the Bible that I learn that I ought to think 
 less about myself . " 

 Kenelm involuntarily press the little hand that lie so innocently on 
 his arm . 

 " do you know the difference between one kind of poetry and another ? " 
 ask Lily , abruptly . 

 " I be not sure . I ought to know when one kind be good and another kind 
 be bad . but in that respect I find many people , especially profess 
 critic , who prefer the poetry which I call bad to the poetry I think 
 good . " 

 " the difference between one kind of poetry and another , suppose they 
 both to be good , " say Lily , positively , and with an air of triumph , " be 
 this , - I know , for Lion explain it to I , - in one kind of poetry the 
 writer throw himself entirely out of his existence , he put himself 
 into other existence quite strange to his own . he may be a very good 
 man , and he write his good poetry about very wicked man : he would not 
 hurt a fly , but he delight in describe murderer . but in the other 
 kind of poetry the writer do not put himself into other existence , he 
 express his own joy and sorrow , his own individual heart and mind . 
 if he could not hurt a fly , he certainly could not make himself at home 
 in the cruel heart of a murderer . there , Mr. Chillingly , that be the 
 difference between one kind of poetry and another . " 

 " very true , " say Kenelm , amuse by the girl 's critical definition . 
 " the difference between dramatic poetry and lyrical . but may I ask what 
 that definition have to do with the subject into which you so suddenly 
 introduce it ? " 

 " much ; for when Lion be explain this to my aunt , he say , ' a perfect 
 woman be a poem ; but she can never be a poem of the one kind , never can 
 make herself at home in the heart with which she have no connection , 
 never feel any sympathy with crime and evil ; she must be a poem of the 
 other kind , weave out poetry from her own thought and fancy . ' and , 
 turn to I , he say , smile , ' that be the poem I wish Lily to be . 
 too many dry book would only spoil the poem . ' and you now see why I be 
 so ignorant , and so unlike other girl , and why Mr. and Mrs. Emlyn look 
 down upon I . " 

 " you wrong at least Mr. Emlyn , for it be he who first say to I , ' lily 
 Mordaunt be a poem . ' " 

 " do he ? I shall love he for that . how pleased Lion will be ! " 

 " Mr. Melville seem to have an extraordinary influence over your mind , " 
 say Kenelm , with a jealous pang . 

 " of course . I have neither father nor mother : Lion have be both to 
 I . Aunty have often say , ' you can not be too grateful to your guardian ; 
 without he I should have no home to shelter you , no bread to give you . ' 
 he never say that : he would be very angry with aunty if he know she have 
 say it . when he do not call I Fairy he call I Princess . I would 
 not displease he for the world . " 

 " he be very much old than you ; old enough to be your father , I hear . " 

 " I dare say . but if he be twice as old I could not love he well . " 

 Kenelm smile : the jealousy be go . certainly not thus could any girl , 
 even Lily , speak of one with whom , however she might love he , she be 
 likely to fall in love . 

 Lily now rise up , rather slowly and wearily . " it be time to go home : 
 aunty will be wonder what keep I away , - come . " 

 they take their way towards the bridge opposite to Cromwell Lodge . 

 it be not for some minute that either break silence . Lily be the 
 first to do so , and with one of those abrupt change of topic which be 
 common to the restless play of her secret thought . 

 " you have father and mother still live , Mr. Chillingly ? " 

 " thank Heaven , yes . " 

 " which do you love the good ? " 

 " that be scarcely a fair question . I love my mother very much ; but my 
 father and I understand each other well than - " 

 " I see : it be so difficult to be understand . no one understand I . " 

 " I think I do . " 

 Lily shake her head with an energetic movement of dissent . 

 " at least as well as a man can understand a young lady . " 

 " what sort of young lady be Miss Cecilia Travers ? " 

 " Cecilia Travers ! when and how do you ever hear that such a person 
 exist ? " 

 " that big London man whom they call Sir Thomas mention her name the 
 day we din at Braefieldville . " 

 " I remember , - as have be at the Court ball . " 

 " he say she be very handsome . " 

 " so she be . " 

 " be she a poem too ? " 

 " No ; that never strike I . " 

 " Mr. Emlyn , I suppose , would call she perfectly bring up , - well 
 educate . he would not raise his eyebrow at she as he do at I , - poor 
 I , Cinderella ! " 

 " ah , Miss Mordaunt , you need not envy she . again let I say that you 
 could very soon educate yourself to the level of any young lady who 
 adorn the Court ball . " 

 " ay ; but then I should not be a poem , " say Lily , with a shy , arch 
 side - glance at his face . 

 they be now on the bridge , and before Kenelm could answer Lily resume 
 quickly , " you need not come any far ; it be out of your way . " 

 " I can not be so disdainfully dismiss , Miss Mordaunt ; I insist on 
 see you to at least your garden gate . " 

 Lily make no objection and again speak , - 

 " what sort of country do you live in when at home ; be it like this ? " 

 " not so pretty ; the feature be large , more hill and dale and 
 woodland : yet there be one feature in our ground which remind I a 
 little of this landscape , - a light stream , somewhat wide , indeed , 
 than your brooklet ; but here and there the bank be so like those by 
 Cromwell Lodge that I sometimes start and fancy myself at home . I have 
 a strange love for rivulet and all running water , and in my foot 
 wandering I find myself magnetically attract towards they . " 

 Lily listen with interest , and after a short pause say , with a 
 half - suppress sigh , " your home be much fine than any place here , even 
 than Braefieldville , be it not ? Mrs. Braefield say your father be very 
 rich . " 

 " I doubt if he be rich than Mr. Braefield ; and , though his house may 
 be large than Braefieldville , it be not so smartly furnish , and have 
 no such luxurious hothouse and conservatory . my father 's taste be 
 like mine , very simple . give he his library , and he would scarcely miss 
 his fortune if he lose it . he have in this one immense advantage over 
 I . " 

 " you would miss fortune ? " say Lily , quickly . 

 " not that ; but my father be never tired of book . and shall I own it ? 
 there be day when book tire I almost as much as they do you . " 

 they be now at the garden gate . lily , with one hand on the latch , hold 
 out the other to Kenelm , and her smile light up the dull sky like a burst 
 of sunshine , as she look in his face and vanish . 




 BOOK VII . 



 CHAPTER I. 

 KENELM do not return home till dusk , and just as he be sit down to 
 his solitary meal there be a ring at the bell , and Mrs. Jones usher 
 in Mr. Thomas Bowles . 

 though that gentleman have never write to announce the day of his 
 arrival , he be not the less welcome . 

 " only , " say Kenelm , " if you preserve the appetite I have lose , I fear 
 you will find meagre fare to - day . sit down , man . " 

 " thank you , kindly , but I din two hour ago in London , and I really 
 can eat nothing more . " 

 Kenelm be too well - breed to press unwelcome hospitality . in a very few 
 minute his frugal repast be end ; the cloth remove , the two man be 
 leave alone . 

 " your room be here , of course , Tom ; that be engage from the day I 
 ask you , but you ought to have give I a line to say when to expect 
 you , so that I could have put our hostess on her mettle as to dinner or 
 supper . you smoke still , of course : light your pipe . " 

 " thank you , Mr. Chillingly , I seldom smoke now ; but if you will excuse a 
 cigar , " and Tom produce a very smart cigar - case . 

 " do as you would at home . I shall send word to Will Somers that you and 
 I sup there to - morrow . you forgive I for let out your secret . 
 all straightforward now and henceforth . you come to their hearth as a 
 friend , who will grow dearer to they both every year . ah , Tom , this love 
 for woman seem to I a very wonderful thing . it may sink a man into 
 such deep of evil , and lift a man into such height of good . " 

 " I do not   know as to the good , " say Tom , mournfully , and lay aside 
 his cigar . 

 " go on smoking : I should like to keep you company ; can you spare I one 
 of your cigar ? " 

 Tom offer his case . Kenelm extract a cigar , light it , draw a few 
 whiff , and , when he see that Tom have resume his own cigar , recommenced 
 conversation . 

 " you do not   know as to the good ; but tell I honestly , do you think if 
 you have not love Jessie Wiles , you would be as good a man as you be 
 now ? " 

 " if I be well than I be , it be not because of my love for the girl . " 

 " what then ? " 

 " the loss of she . " 

 Kenelm start , turn very pale , throw aside the cigar , rise , and 
 walk the room to and fro with very quick but very irregular stride . 

 Tom continue quietly . " suppose I have win Jessie and marry she , I 
 do not   think any idea of improve myself would have enter my head . my 
 uncle would have be very much offend at my marrying a day - labourer 's 
 daughter , and would not have invite I to Luscombe . I should have 
 remain at Graveleigh , with no ambition of be more than a common 
 farrier , an ignorant , noisy , quarrelsome man ; and if I could not have 
 make Jessie as fond of I as I wish , I should not have break myself 
 of drinking , and I shudder to think what a brute I might have be , when 
 I see in the newspaper an account of some drunken wife - beater . how do 
 we know but what that wife - beater love his wife dearly before marriage , 
 and she do not care for he ? his home be unhappy , and so he take to 
 drink and to wife - beating . " 

 " I be right , then , " say Kenelm , halt his stride , " when I tell you 
 it would be a miserable fate to be married to a girl whom you love to 
 distraction , and whose heart you could never warm to you , whose life you 
 could never render happy . " 

 " so right ! " 

 " let we drop that part of the subject at present , " say Kenelm , 
 reseat himself , " and talk about your wish to travel . though content 
 that you do not marry Jessie , though you can now , without anguish , 
 greet she as the wife of another , still there be some lingering 
 thought of she that make you restless ; and you feel that you could more 
 easily wrench yourself from these thought in a marked change of scene 
 and adventure , that you might bury they altogether in the soil of a 
 strange land . be it so ? " 

 " ay , something of that , sir . " 

 then Kenelm rouse himself to talk of foreign land , and to map out a 
 plan of travel that might occupy some month . he be pleased to find 
 that Tom have already learn enough of French to make himself understand 
 at least upon commonplace matter , and still more pleased to discover 
 that he have be not only read the proper guide - book or manual 
 descriptive of the principal place in Europe worth visit , but that 
 he have acquire an interest in the place ; interest in the fame attach 
 to they by their history in the past , or by the treasure of art they 
 contain . 

 so they talk far into the night ; and when Tom retire to his room , 
 Kenelm let himself out of the house noiselessly , and walk with slow 
 step towards the old summer - house in which he have sit with Lily . the 
 wind have rise , scatter the cloud that have veil the precede day , 
 so that the star be see in far chasm of the sky beyond , - see for 
 a while in one place , and , when the swift cloud roll over they there , 
 shin out elsewhere . amid the vary sound of the tree , through 
 which sweep the night gust , Kenelm fancy he could distinguish the 
 sigh of the willow on the opposite lawn of Grasmere . 



 CHAPTER II . 

 KENELM despatch a note to Will Somers early the next morning , invite 
 himself and Mr. Bowles to supper that evening . his tact be sufficient 
 to make he aware that in such social meal there would be far less 
 restraint for each and all concerned than in a more formal visit 
 from Tom during the day - time ; and when Jessie , too , be engage with 
 customer to the shop . 

 but he lead Tom through the town and show he the shop itself , with 
 its pretty good at the plate - glass window , and its general air of 
 prosperous trade ; then he carry he off into the lane and field of 
 the country , draw out the mind of his companion , and impress with 
 great admiration of its marked improvement in culture , and in the train 
 of thought which culture open out and enriche . 

 but throughout all their multiform range of subject Kenelm could 
 perceive that Tom be still preoccupy and abstract : the idea of the 
 come interview with Jessie weigh upon he . 

 when they leave Cromwell Lodge at nightfall , to repair to the supper at 
 Will be ; Kenelm notice that Bowles have avail himself of the content 
 of his carpet - bag to make some refined alteration in his dress . the 
 alteration become he . 

 when they enter the parlour , will rise from his chair with the 
 evidence of deep emotion on his face , advance to Tom , take his hand and 
 grasp and drop it without a word . Jessie salute both guest alike , 
 with droop eyelid and an elaborate curtsy . the old mother alone be 
 perfectly self - possess and up to the occasion . 

 " I be heartily glad to see you , Mr. Bowles , " say she , " and so all three 
 of we be , and ought to be ; and if baby be old , there would be four . " 

 " and where on earth have you hide baby ? " cry Kenelm . " surely he 
 might have be keep up for I to - night , when I be expect ; the last

 complain of baby 's want of respect to her parent ' friend . " 

 Jessie raise the window - curtain , and point to the cradle behind it . 
 Kenelm link his arm in Tom 's , lead he to the cradle , and , leave 
 he alone to gaze on the sleep inmate , seat himself at the table , 
 between old Mrs. Somers and Will . Will 's eye be turn away towards 
 the curtain , Jessie hold its fold aside , and the formidable Tom , 
 who have be the terror of his neighbourhood , bend smile over 
 the cradle : till at last he lay his large hand on the pillow , gently , 
 timidly , careful not to awake the helpless sleeper , and his lip move , 
 doubtless with a blessing ; then he , too , come to the table , seat 
 himself , and Jessie carry the cradle upstairs . 

 will fix his keen , intelligent eye on his bygone rival ; and notice 
 the changed expression of the once aggressive countenance , the change 
 costume in which , without tinge of rustic foppery , there be the token 
 of a certain gravity of station scarcely compatible with a return to old 
 love and old habit in the village world , the last shadow of jealousy 
 vanish from the clear surface of Will 's affectionate nature . 

 " Mr. Bowles , " he exclaim , impulsively , " you have a kind heart , and a 
 good heart , and a generous heart . and your corning here to - night on this 
 friendly visit be an honour which - which " - " which , " interrupt Kenelm , 
 compassionate Will 's embarrassment , " be on the side of we single man . 
 in this free country a married man who have a male baby may be father 
 to the Lord Chancellor or the Archbishop of Canterbury . but - well , my 
 friend , such a meeting as we have to - night do not come often ; and 
 after supper let we celebrate it with a bowl of punch . if we have 
 headache the next morning none of we will grumble . " 

 Old Mrs. Somers laugh out jovially . " bless you , sir , I do not think 
 of the punch ; I will go and see about it , " and , baby 's sock still in 
 her hand , she hasten from the room . 

 what with the supper , what with the punch , and what with Kenelm 's art 
 of cheery talk on general subject , all reserve , all awkwardness , all 
 shyness between the convivialist , rapidly disappear . Jessie mingle 
 in the talk ; perhaps   except only Kenelm   she talk more than the 
 other , artlessly , gayly , no vestige of the old coquetry ; but , now and 
 then , with a touch of genteel finery , indicative of her rise in life , 
 and of the contact of the fancy shopkeeper with noble customer . it be 
 a pleasant evening ; Kenelm have resolve that it should be so . not a 
 hint of the obligation to Mr. Bowles escape until Will , follow his 
 visitor to the door , whisper to Tom , " you do not   want thank , and I 
 can not   express they . but when we say our prayer at night , we have always 
 ask God to bless he who bring we together , and have since make we 
 so prosperous , - I mean Mr. Chillingly . to - night there will be another 
 besides he , for whom we shall pray , and for whom baby , when he be 
 old , will pray too . " 

 Therewith Will 's voice thicken ; and he prudently recede , with no 
 unreasonable fear lest the punch might make he too demonstrative of 
 emotion if he say more . 

 Tom be very silent on the return to Cromwell Lodge ; it do not seem the 
 silence of depressed spirit , but rather of quiet meditation , from which 
 Kenelm do not attempt to rouse he . 

 it be not till they reach the garden pale of Grasmere that Tom , 
 stop short , and turn his face to Kenelm , say , " I be very 
 grateful to you for this evening , - very . " 

 " it have revive no painful thought then ? " 

 " no ; I feel so much calm in mind than I ever believe I could have 
 be , after see she again . " 

 " be it possible ! " say Kenelm , to himself . " how should I feel if I ever 
 see in Lily the wife of another man , the mother of his child ? " at that 
 question he shudder , and an involuntary groan escape from his lip . 
 just then have , willingly in those precinct , arrest his step when

 rest on the garden pale . he look , and see that it be Blanche . 
 the creature , impel by its instinct towards night - wandering , have , 
 somehow or other , escape from its own bed within the house , and hear

 the shrub behind upon the edge of the pale . there it stand , with arch 
 back , purr low as in pleased salutation . 

 Kenelm bend down and cover with kiss the blue ribbon which Lily 's 
 hand have bind round the favourite 's neck . Blanche submit to the 
 caress for a moment , and then catch a slight rustle among the shrub 
 make by some awake bird , spring into the thick of the quivering leaf

 Kenelm move on with a quick impatient stride , and no further word be 
 exchange between he and his companion till they reach their lodging 
 and part for the night . 



 CHAPTER III . 

 the next day , towards noon , Kenelm and his visitor , walk together 
 along the brook - side , stop before Izaak Walton 's summer - house , and , 
 at Kenelm 's suggestion , enter therein to rest , and more at their ease 
 to continue the conversation they have begin . 

 " you have just tell I , " say Kenelm , " that you feel as if a load be 
 take off your heart , now that you have again meet Jessie Somers , and 
 that you find she so change that she be no long the woman you love . 
 as to the change , whatever it be , I own , it seem to I for the well , 
 in person , in manner , in character ; of course I should not say this , if 
 I be not convinced of your perfect sincerity when you assure I that 
 you be cure of the old wound . but I feel so deeply interested in the 
 question how a fervent love , once entertain and enthrone in the heart 
 of a man so earnestly affectionate and so warm - blooded as yourself , can 
 be , all of a sudden , at a single interview , expel or transfer into 
 the calm sentiment of friendship , that I pray you to explain . " 

 " that be what puzzle I , sir , " answer Tom , pass his hand over his 
 forehead . " and I do not   know if I can explain it . 

 " think over it , and try . " 

 Tom muse for some moment and then begin . " you see , sir , that I be 
 a very different man myself when I fall in love with Jessie Wiles , and 
 say , ' come what may , that girl shall be my wife . nobody else shall have 
 she . ' " 

 " agree ; go on . " 

 " but while I be become a different man , when I think of she - and I 
 be always think of she - I still picture she to myself as the same 
 Jessie Wiles ; and though , when I do see she again at Graveleigh , after 
 she have marry - the day - " 

 " you save she from the insolence of the squire . " 

 " she be but very recently married . I do not realize she as married . I 
 do not see her husband , and the difference within myself be only 
 then begin . well , so all the time I be read and thinking , and 
 strive to improve my old self at Luscombe , still Jessie Wiles haunt 
 I as the only girl I have ever love , ever could love ; I could not 
 believe it possible that I could ever marry any one else . and lately I 
 have be much pressed to marry some one else ; all my family wish it : 
 but the face of Jessie rise up before I , and I say to myself , ' I 
 should be a base man if I marry one woman , while I could not get 
 another woman out of my head . ' I must see Jessie once more , must learn 
 whether her face be now really the face that haunt I when I sit alone ; 
 and I have see she , and it be not that face : it may be handsomer , but 
 it be not a girl 's face , it be the face of a wife and a mother . and , 
 last evening , while she be talk with an open - heartedness which I 
 have never find in she before , I become strangely conscious of the 
 difference in myself that have be silently at work within the last two 
 year or so . then , sir , when I be but an ill - condition , uneducated , 
 petty village farrier , there be no inequality between I and a peasant 
 girl ; or , rather , in all thing except fortune , the peasant girl 
 be much above I . but last evening I ask myself , watch she and 
 listen to her talk , ' if Jessie be now free , should I press she to 
 be my wife ? ' and I answer myself , ' no . ' " 

 Kenelm listen with rapt attention , and exclaim briefly , but 
 passionately , " why ? " 

 " it seem as if I be give myself air to say why . but , sir , lately I 
 have be throw among person , woman as well as man , of a high class 
 than I be bear in ; and in a wife I should want a companion up to their 
 mark , and who would keep I up to mine ; and ah , sir , I do not   feel as if 
 I could find that companion in Mrs. Somers . " 

 " I understand you now , Tom . but you be spoil a silly romance of 
 mine . I have fancy the little girl with the flower face would grow up 
 to supply the loss of Jessie ; and , I be so ignorant of the human heart , 
 I do think it would take all the year require for the little girl to 
 open into a woman , before the loss of the old love could be supply . I 
 see now that the poor little child with the flower face have no chance . " 

 " Chance ? why , Mr. Chillingly , " cry Tom , evidently much nettle , " Susey 
 be a dear little thing , but she be scarcely more than a mere charity 
 girl . Sir , when I last see you in London you touch on that matter as 
 if I be still the village farrier 's son , who might marry a village 
 labourer 's daughter . but , " add Tom , soften down his irritated tone 
 of voice , " even if Susey be a lady bear I think a man would make a 
 very great mistake , if he think he could bring up a little girl to 
 regard he as a father ; and then , when she grow up , expect she to accept 
 he as a lover . " 

 " ah , you think that ! " exclaim Kenelm , eagerly , and turn eye that 
 sparkle with joy towards the lawn of Grasmere . " you think that ; it be 
 very sensibly say , - well , and you have be press to marry , and have 
 hang back till you have see again Mrs. Somers . now you will be well 
 dispose to such a step ; tell I about it ? " 

 " I say , last evening , that one of the principal capitalist at 
 Luscombe , the lead corn - merchant , have offer to take I into 
 partnership . and , sir , he have an only daughter , she be a very amiable 
 girl , have have a first - rate education , and have such pleasant manner and 
 way of talk , quite a lady . if I marry she I should soon be the first 
 man in Luscombe , and Luscombe , as you be no doubt aware , return two 
 member to Parliament ; who know , but that some day the farrier 's son 
 might be - " Tom stop abruptly , abash at the aspire thought which , 
 while speak , have deepen his hardy colour and flash from his 
 honest eye . 

 " ah ! " say Kenelm , almost mournfully , " be it so ? must each man in his 
 life play many part ? ambition succeed to love , the reasoning brain to 
 the passionate heart . true , you be change ; my Tom Bowles be go . " 

 " not go in his undying gratitude to you , sir , " say Tom , with great 
 emotion . " your Tom Bowles would give up all his dream of wealth or of 
 rise in life , and go through fire and water to serve the friend who 
 first bid he be a new Tom Bowles ! do not   despise I as your own work : 
 you say to I that terrible day , when madness be on my brow and crime 
 within my heart , ' I will be to you the true friend man ever find in 
 man . ' so you have be . you command I to read ; you command I to 
 think ; you teach I that body should be the servant of mind . " 

 " hush , hush , time be alter ; it be you who can teach I now . teach 
 I , teach I ; how do ambition replace love ? how do the desire to 
 rise in life become the all - master passion , and , should it prosper , 
 the all - atone consolation of our life ? we can never be as happy , 
 though we rise to the throne of the Caesars , as we dream that we 
 could have be , have Heaven but permit we to dwell in the obscure 
 village , side by side with the woman we love . " 

 Tom be exceedingly startle by such a burst of irrepressible passion 
 from the man who have tell he that , though friend be find only once 
 in a life , sweetheart be as plentiful as blackberry . 

 again he sweep his hand over his forehead , and reply hesitatingly : " I 
 can not   pretend to say what maybe the case with other . but to judge by my 
 own case , it seem to I this : a young man who , out of his own business , 
 have nothing to interest or excite he , find content , interest , and 
 excitement when he fall in love ; and then , whether for good or ill , he 
 think there be nothing like love in the world , he do not   care a fig for 
 ambition then . over and over again do my poor uncle ask I to come to 
 he at Luscombe , and represent all the worldly advantage it would be 
 to I ; but I could not leave the village in which Jessie live , and , 
 besides , I feel myself unfit to be anything high than I be . but 
 when I have be some time at Luscombe , and gradually get accustom to 
 another sort of people , and another sort of talk , then I begin to feel 
 interest in the same object that interested those about I ; and when , 
 partly by mix with well educate man , and partly by the pain I 
 take to educate myself , I feel that I might now more easily rise above 
 my uncle 's rank of life than two year ago I could have rise above 
 a farrier 's forge , then the ambition to rise do stir in I , and grow 
 strong every day . Sir , I do not   think you can wake up a man 's intellect 
 but what you wake with it emulation . and , after all , emulation be 
 ambition . " 

 " then , I suppose , I have no emulation in I , for certainly I have no 
 ambition . " 

 " that I can not   believe , sir ; other thought may cover it over and keep it 
 down for a time . but soon or later , it will force its way to the top , 
 as it have do with I . to get on in life , to be respect by those who 
 know you , more and more as you grow old , I call that a manly desire . I 
 be sure it come as naturally to an Englishman as - as - " 

 " as the wish to knock down some other Englishman who stand in his way 
 do . I perceive now that you be always a very ambitious man , Tom ; the 
 ambition have only take another direction . Caesar might have be 


  " ' but the first wrestler on the green . ' 


 " and now , I suppose , you abandon the idea of travel : you will return to 
 Luscombe , cure of all regret for the loss of Jessie ; you will marry the 
 young lady you mention , and rise , through progressive step of alderman 
 and mayor , into the rank of member for Luscombe . " 

 " all that may come in good time , " answer Tom , not resent the tone 
 of irony in which he be address , " but I still intend to travel : a 
 year so spend must render I all the more fit for any station I aim at . 
 I shall go back to Luscombe to arrange my affair , come to term with 
 Mr. Leland the corn - merchant , against my return , and - " 

 " the young lady be to wait till then . " 

 " emily - " 

 " oh , that be the name ? emily ! a much more elegant name than Jessie . " 

 " emily , " continue Tom , with an unruffled placidity , - which , consider 
 the aggravating bitterness for which Kenelm have exchange his wonte 
 dulcitude of indifferentism , be absolutely saintlike , " Emily know 
 that if she be my wife I should be proud of she , and will esteem I 
 the more if she feel how resolved I be that she shall never be ashamed 
 of I . " 

 " Pardon I , Tom , " say Kenelm soften , and lay his hand on his 
 friend 's shoulder with brotherlike tenderness . " nature have make you a 
 thorough gentleman ; and you could not think and speak more nobly if you 
 have come into the world as the head of all the howard . " 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 TOM go away the next morning . he decline to see Jessie again , say 
 curtly , " I do not   wish the impression make on I the other evening to 
 incur a chance of be weaken . " 

 Kenelm be in no mood to regret his friend 's departure . despite all 
 the improvement in Tom 's manner and culture , which raise he so 
 much near to equality with the polite and instruct heir of the 
 Chillinglys , Kenelm would have feel more in sympathy and rapport with 
 the old disconsolate fellow - wanderer who have recline with he on the 
 grass , listen to the minstrel 's talk or verse , than he do with 
 the practical , rise citizen of Luscombe . to the young lover of Lily 
 Mordaunt there be a discord , a jar , in the knowledge that the human 
 heart admit of such well - reason , well - justify transfer of 
 allegiance ; a Jessie to - day , or an Emily to - morrow ; " La reine est morte : 
 vive la reine " 

 an hour or two after Tom have go , Kenelm find himself almost 
 mechanically lead towards Braefieldville . he have instinctively divine 
 Elsie 's secret wish with regard to himself and Lily , however skilfully 
 she think she have conceal it . 

 at Braefieldville he should hear talk of Lily , and in the scene where 
 Lily have be first beheld . 

 he find Mrs. Braefield alone in the drawing - room , seat by a table 
 cover with flower , which she be assort and intermix for the 
 vas to which they be destine . 

 it strike he that her manner be more reserved than usual and somewhat 
 embarrassed ; and when , after a few preliminary matter of small talk , 
 he rush boldly in media re and ask if she have see Mrs. Cameron 
 lately , she reply briefly , " yes , I call there the other day , " 
 and immediately change the conversation to the troubled state of the 
 Continent . 

 Kenelm be resolve not to be so put off , and presently return to the 
 charge . 

 " the other day you propose an excursion to the site of the roman villa , 
 and say you would ask Mrs. Cameron to be of the party . perhaps you have 
 forget it ? " 

 " no ; but Mrs. Cameron decline . we can ask the Emlyns instead . he will 
 be an excellent cicerone . " 

 " Excellent ! why do Mrs. Cameron decline ? " 

 Elsie hesitate , and then lift her clear brown eye to his face , with 
 a sudden determination to bring matter to a crisis . 

 " I can not say why Mrs. Cameron decline , but in decline she act very 
 wisely and very honourably . listen to I , Mr. Chillingly . you know how 
 highly I esteem , and how cordially I like you , and judge by what I 
 feel for some week , perhaps long , after we part at Tor Hadham - " 
 here again she hesitate , and , with a half laugh and a slight blush , 
 again go resolutely on . " if I be Lily 's aunt or eld sister , I 
 should do as Mrs. Cameron do ; decline to let Lily see much more of a 
 young gentleman too much above she in wealth and station for - " 

 " stop , " cry Kenelm , haughtily , " I can not allow that any man 's wealth 
 or station would warrant his presumption in think himself above Miss 
 Mordaunt . " 

 " above she in natural grace and refinement , certainly not . but in the 
 world there be other consideration which , perhaps , Sir Peter and Lady 
 Chillingly might take into account . " 

 " you do not think of that before you last see Mrs. Cameron . " 

 " honestly speak , I do not . assured that Miss Mordaunt be a 
 gentlewoman by birth , I do not sufficiently reflect upon other 
 disparity . " 

 " you know , then , that she be by birth a gentlewoman ? " 

 " I only know it as all here do , by the assurance of Mrs. Cameron , whom 
 no one could suppose not to be a lady . but there be different degree 
 of lady and of gentleman , which be little heed in the ordinary 
 intercourse of society , but become very perceptible in question of 
 matrimonial alliance ; and Mrs. Cameron herself say very plainly that 
 she do not consider her niece to belong to that station in life from 
 which Sir Peter and Lady Chillingly would naturally wish their son 
 should select his bride . then   hold out her hand   pardon I if I have 
 wound or offend you . I speak as a true friend to you and to Lily 
 both . earnestly I advise you , if Miss Mordaunt be the cause of your 
 linger here , earnestly I advise you to leave while yet in time for 
 her peace of mind and your own . " 

 " her peace of mind , " say Kenelm , in low falter tone , scarcely 
 hear the rest of Mrs. Braefield 's speech . " her peace of mind ? do 
 you sincerely think that she care for I , - could care for I , - if I 
 stay ? " 

 " I wish I could answer you decidedly . I be not in the secret of her 
 heart . I can but conjecture that it might be dangerous for the peace of 
 any young girl to see too much of a man like yourself , to divine that he 
 love she , and not to be aware that he could not , with the approval of 
 his family , ask she to become his wife . " 

 Kenelm bend his face down , and cover it with his right hand . he do 
 not speak for some moment . then he rise , the fresh cheek very pale , and 
 say , - 

 " you be right . Miss Mordaunt 's peace of mind must be the first 
 consideration . excuse I if I quit you thus abruptly . you have give I 
 much to think of , and I can only think of it adequately when alone . " 



 CHAPTER V. 


 from KENELM CHILLINGLY to SIR PETER CHILLINGLY . 


 MY FATHER , MY DEAR FATHER , - this be no reply to your letter . I know 
 not if itself can be call a letter . I can not yet decide whether it be 
 mean to reach your hand . tired with talk to myself , I sit down to 
 talk to you . often have I reproach myself for not see every fitting 
 occasion to let you distinctly know how warmly I love , how deeply I 
 reverence you ; you , o friend , o father . but we Chillinglys be not a 
 demonstrative race . I do not   remember that you , by word , ever express 
 to I the truth that you love your son infinitely more than he 
 deserve . yet , do I not know that you would send all your beloved old 
 book to the hammer rather than I should pine in vain for some untried , 
 if sinless , delight on which I have set my heart ? and do you not 
 know equally well , that I would part with all my heritage , and turn 
 day - labourer , rather than you should miss the beloved old book ? 

 that mutual knowledge be take for grant in all that my heart yearn 
 to pour forth to your own . but , if I divine aright , a day be come 
 when , as between you and I , there must be a sacrifice on the part of 
 one to the other . if so , I implore that the sacrifice may come from 
 you . how be this ? how be I so ungenerous , so egotistical , so selfish , so 
 ungratefully unmindful of all I already owe to you , and may never repay ? 
 I can only answer , " it be fate , it be nature , it be love " - 

      

 here I must break off . it be midnight , the moon halt opposite to the 
 window at which I sit , and on the stream that run below there be a long 
 narrow track on which every wave tremble in her light ; on either side 
 of the moonlit track all the other wave , run equally to their grave 
 in the invisible deep , seem motionless and dark . I can write no more . 

 ......... 

   date two day later . 

 they say she be beneath we in wealth and station . be we , my father - we , 
 two well - bear gentleman - coveter of gold or lackey of the great ? when 
 I be at college , if there be any there more heartily despise than 
 another it be the parasite and the tuft - hunter ; the man who choose his 
 friend accord as their money or their rank might be of use to he . 
 if so mean where the choice be so little important to the happiness and 
 career of a man who have something of manhood in he , how much more mean 
 to be the parasite and tuft - hunter in decide what woman to love , what 
 woman to select as the sweetener and ennobler of one 's everyday life ! 
 could she be to my life that sweetener , that ennobler ? I firmly believe 
 it . already life itself have gain a charm that I never even guess in 
 it before ; already I begin , though as yet but faintly and vaguely , to 
 recognize that interest in the object and aspiration of my fellow - man 
 which be strong in those whom posterity rank among its ennobler . in 
 this quiet village it be true that I might find example enough to prove 
 that man be not mean to meditate upon life , but to take active part in 
 it , and in that action to find his us . but I doubt if I should have 
 profit by such example ; if I should not have look on this 
 small stage of the world as I have look on the large one , with the 
 indifferent eye of a spectator on a trite familiar play carry on 
 by ordinary actor , have not my whole being suddenly leap out of 
 philosophy into passion , and , at once make warmly human , sympathize 
 with humanity wherever it burn and glow . ah , be there to be 
 any doubt of what station , as mortal bride , be due to she , - she , my 
 princess , my fairy ? if so , how contented you shall be , my father , with 
 the worldly career of your son ! how perseveringly he will strive 
  and when do perseverance fail ?   to supply all his deficiency of 
 intellect , genius , knowledge , by the energy concentrate on a single 
 object which - more than intellect , genius , knowledge , unless they attain 
 to equal energy equally concentrate - command what the world call 
 honour . 

 yes , with she , with she as the bearer of my name , with she to whom I , 
 whatever I might do of good or of great , could say , " it be thy work , " 
 I promise that you shall bless the day when you take to your arm a 
 daughter .

 " thou art in contact with the beloved in all that thou feelest elevated 
 above thee . " so it be write by one of those weird german who search 
 in our bosom for the seed of bury truth , and conjure they into 
 flower before we ourselves be even aware of the seed . 

 every thought that associate itself with my beloved seem to I bear 
 with wing .

 I have just see she , just part from she . since I have be 
 tell - kindly , wisely tell - that I have no right to hazard her peace of 
 mind unless I be privileged to woo and to win she , I promise myself 
 that I would shun her presence until I have bar my heart to you , as I 
 be do now , and receive that privilege from yourself ; for even have I 
 never make the promise that bind my honour , your consent and blessing 
 must hallow my choice . I do not feel as if I could dare to ask one 
 so innocent and fair to we d an ungrateful , disobedient son . but this 
 evening I meet she , unexpectedly , at the vicar 's , an excellent man , from 
 whom I have learn much ; whose precept , whose example , whose delight 
 in his home , and his life at once active and serene , be in harmony with 
 my own dream when I dream of she . 

 I will tell you the name of the beloved ; hold it as yet a profound 
 secret between you and I . but oh for the day when I may hear you call 
 she by that name , and print on her forehead the only kiss by man of 
 which I should not be jealous . 

 it be Sunday , and after the evening service it be my friend 's custom 
 to gather his child round he , and , without any formal sermon or 
 discourse , engage their interest in subject harmonious to association 
 with the sanctity of the day ; often not directly bear upon religion ; 
 more often , indeed , playfully start from some little incident or some 
 slight story - book which have amuse the child in the course of the 
 past week , and then gradually wind into reference to some sweet moral 
 precept or illustration from some divine example . it be a maxim with 
 he that , while much that child must learn they can only learn well 
 through conscious labour , and as positive task - work , yet religion should 
 be connect in their mind not with labour and task - work , but should 
 become insensibly infuse into their habit of thought , blend 
 itself with memory and image of peace and love ; with the indulgent 
 tenderness of the early teacher , the sinless mirthfulness of the 
 early home ; with consolation in after sorrow , support through after
