 trial , and never part company with its twin sister , Hope . 

 I enter the vicar 's room this evening just as the group have collect 
 round he . by the side of his wife sit a lady in whom I feel a keen 
 interest . her face wear that kind of calm which speak of the lassitude 
 bequeath by sorrow . she be the aunt of my beloved one . Lily have 
 nestle herself on a low ottoman , at the good pastor 's foot , with one 
 of his little girl , round whose shoulder she have wind her arm . she be 
 much more fond of the companionship of child than that of girl of 
 her own age . the vicar 's wife , a very clever woman , once , in my hearing , 
 take she to task for this preference , ask she why she persist in 
 group herself with mere infant who could teach she nothing ? ah ! 
 could you have see the innocent , angel - like expression of her face when 
 she answer simply , " I suppose because with they I feel safe , I mean 
 nearer to God . " 

 Mr. Emlyn - that be the name of the vicar - deduce his homily this 
 evening from a pretty fairy tale which Lily have be tell to his 
 child the day before , and which he draw she on to repeat . 

 take , in brief , the substance of the story : - 

 " once on a time , a king and queen make themselves very unhappy because 
 they have no heir to their throne ; and they pray for one ; and lo , on 
 some bright summer morning , the queen , wake from sleep , see a cradle 
 beside her bed , and in the cradle a beautiful sleeping babe . great 
 day throughout the kingdom ! but as the infant grow up , it become very 
 wayward and fretful : it lose its beauty ; it would not learn its lesson ; 
 it be as naughty as a child could be . the parent be very sorrowful ; 
 the heir , so long for , promise to be a great plague to themselves 
 and their subject . at last one day , to add to their trouble , two little 
 bump appear on the prince 's shoulder . all the doctor be consult 
 as to the cause and the cure of this deformity . of course they try 
 the effect of back - band and steel machine , which give the poor little 
 prince great pain , and make he more unamiable than ever . the bump , 
 nevertheless , grow large , and as they increase , so the prince sicken 
 and pin away . at last a skilful surgeon propose , as the only chance 
 of save the prince 's life , that the bump should be cut out ; and the 
 next morning be fix for that operation . but at night the queen see , 
 or dream she see , a beautiful shape stand by her bedside . and it 
 say to she reproachfully , ' ungrateful woman ! how wouldst thou repay I 
 for the precious boon that my favour bestow on thee ! in I behold the 
 queen of the Fairies . for the heir to thy kingdom , I consign to thy 
 charge an infant from Fairyland , to become a blessing to thee and to 
 thy people ; and thou wouldst inflict upon it a death of torture by the 
 surgeon 's knife . ' and the queen answer , ' precious indeed thou mayest 
 call the boon , - a miserable , sickly , feverish changeling . ' 

 " ' art thou so dull , ' say the beautiful visitant , ' as not to comprehend 
 that the early instinct of the fairy child would be those of 
 discontent , at the exile from its native home ? and in that discontent it 
 would have pin itself to death , or grow up , soured and malignant , 
 a fairy still in its power but a fairy of wrath and evil , have not the 
 strength of its inborn nature suffice to develop the growth of its 
 wing . that which thy blindness condemn as the deformity of the 
 human - bear , be to the fairy - bear the crown perfection of its beauty . 
 Woe to thee , if thou suffer not the wing of the fairy child to grow . ' 

 " and the next morning the queen send away the surgeon when he come with 
 his horrible knife , and remove the back - board and the steel machine 
 from the prince 's shoulder , though all the doctor predict that the 
 child would die . and from that moment the royal heir begin to recover 
 bloom and health . and when at last , out of those deform bump , 
 bud delicately forth the plumage of snow - white wing , the wayward 
 peevishness of the prince give place to sweet temper . instead of 
 scratch his teacher , he become the quick and most docile of 
 pupil , grow up to be the joy of his parent and the pride of their 
 people ; and people say , ' in he we shall have hereafter such a king as 
 we have never yet know . ' " 

 here end Lily 's tale . I can not convey to you a notion of the pretty , 
 playful manner in which it be tell . then she say , with a grave shake 
 of the head , " but you do not seem to know what happen afterwards . do 
 you suppose that the prince never make use of his wing ? listen to I . 
 it be discover by the courtier who attend on his Royal Highness 
 that on certain night , every week , he disappear . in fact , on these 
 night , obedient to the instinct of the wing , he fly from palace 
 hall into Fairyland ; come back thence all the more lovingly dispose 
 towards the human home from which he have escape for a while . " 

 " oh , my child , " interpose the preacher earnestly , " the wing would 
 be give to we in vain if we do not obey the instinct which allure we 
 to soar ; vain , no less , would be the soar , be it not towards 
 the home whence we come , bear back from its native air a strong 
 health , and a serener joy ; more reconcile to the duty of earth by 
 every new flight into heaven . " 

 as he thus complete the moral of Lily 's fairy tale , the girl rise 
 from her low seat , take his hand , kiss it reverently , and walk away 
 towards the window . I could see that she be affect even to tear , 
 which she seek to conceal . later in the evening , when we be 
 disperse on the lawn , for a few minute before the party break up , Lily 
 come to my side timidly and say , in a low whisper , - 

 " be you angry with I ? what have I do to displease you ? " 

 " angry with you ; displeased ? how can you think of I so unjustly ? " 

 " it be so many day since you have call , since I have see you , " 
 she say so artlessly , look up at I with eye in which tear still 
 seem to tremble . 

 before I could trust myself to reply , her aunt approach , and notice 
 I with a cold and distant " good - night , " lead away her niece . 

 I have calculate on walk back to their home with they , as I generally 
 have do when we meet at another house . but the aunt have probably 
 conjecture I might be at the vicarage that evening , and in order to 
 frustrate my intention have engage a carriage for their return . no doubt 
 she have be warn against permit further intimacy with her niece . 

 my father , I must come to you at once , discharge my promise , and receive 
 from your own lip your consent to my choice ; for you will consent , will 
 you not ? but I wish you to be prepare beforehand , and I shall therefore 
 put up these disjoint fragment of my commune with my own heart and 
 with your , and post they to - morrow . expect I to follow they after 
 leave you a day free to consider they alone , - alone , my dear father : 
 they be mean for no eye but your . 

 K. C. 



 CHAPTER VI . 

 the next day Kenelm walk into the town , post his voluminous letter 
 to Sir Peter , and then look in at the shop of Will Somers , mean to 
 make some purchase of basket - work or trifle fancy good in Jessie 's 
 pretty store of such article , that might please the taste of his 
 mother . 

 on enter the shop his heart beat quick . he see two young form 
 bend over the counter , examine the content of a glass case . one 
 of these customer be Clemmy ; in the other there be no mistaking the 
 slight graceful shape of Lily Mordaunt . Clemmy be exclaim , " oh , it 
 be so pretty , Mrs. Somers ! but , " turn her eye from the counter to a 
 silk purse in her hand , she add sorrowfully , " I can not   buy it . I have 
 not get enough , not by a great deal . " 

 " and what be it , Miss Clemmy ? " ask Kenelm . 

 the two girl turn round at his voice , and Clemmy 's face brighten . 

 " look here , " she say , " be it not too lovely ? " 

 the object thus admire and coveted be a little gold locket , enrich 
 by a cross compose of small pearl . 

 " I assure you , miss , " say Jessie , who have acquire all the coax art 
 of her trade , " it be really a great bargain . Miss Mary Burrows , who be 
 here just before you come , buy one not nearly so pretty and give ten 
 shilling more for it . " 

 Miss Mary Burrows be the same age as Miss Clementina Emlyn , and there 
 be a rivalry as to smartness between those youthful beauty . " Miss 
 burrow ! " sigh Clemmy , very scornfully . 

 but Kenelm 's attention be distract from Clemmy 's locket to a little 
 ring which Lily have be persuade by Mrs. Somers to try on , and which 
 she now draw off and return with a shake of the head . Mrs. Somers , who 
 see that she have small chance of sell the locket to Clemmy , be now 
 address herself to the eld girl more likely to have sufficient 
 pocket - money , and whom , at all event , it be quite safe to trust . 

 " the ring fit you so nicely , Miss Mordaunt , and every young lady of 
 your age wear at least one ring ; allow I to put it up . " she add in 
 a low voice , " though we only sell the article in this case on 
 commission , it be all the same to we whether we be pay now or at 
 Christmas . " 

 " ' tis no use tempt I , Mrs. Somers , " say Lily , laughing , and then 
 with a grave air , " I promise Lion , I mean my guardian , never to run 
 into debt , and I never will . " 

 Lily turn resolutely from the perilous counter , take up a paper 
 that contain a new ribbon she have buy for Blanche , and Clemmy 
 reluctantly follow she out of the shop . 

 Kenelm linger behind and select very hastily a few trifle , to be 
 send to he that evening with some specimen of basket - work leave to 
 Will be tasteful discretion ; then purchase the locket on which Clemmy 
 have set her heart ; but all the while his thought be fix on the ring 
 which Lily have try on . it be no sin against etiquette to give the 
 locket to a child like Clemmy , but would it not be a cruel impertinence 
 to offer a gift to Lily ? 

 Jessie speak : " Miss Mordaunt take a great fancy to this ring , Mr. 
 Chillingly . I be sure her aunt would like she to have it . I have a great 
 mind to put it by on the chance of Mrs. Cameron 's calling here . it would 
 be a pity if it be buy by some one else . " 

 " I think , " say Kenelm , " that I will take the liberty of show it to 
 Mrs. Cameron . no doubt she will buy it for her niece . add the price 
 of it to my bill . " he seize the ring and carry it off ; a very poor 
 little simple ring , with a single stone shape as a heart , not half the 
 price of the locket . 

 Kenelm rejoin the young lady just where the path split into two , the 
 one lead direct to Grasmere , the other through the churchyard to 
 the vicarage . he present the locket to Clemmy with brief kindly word 
 which easily remove any scruple she might have have in accept it ; 
 and , delight with her acquisition , she bound off to the vicarage , 
 impatient to show the prize to her mamma and sister , and more 
 especially to Miss Mary Burrows , who be come to lunch with they . 

 Kenelm walk on slowly by Lily 's side . 

 " you have a good heart , Mr. Chillingly , " say she , somewhat abruptly . 
 " how it must please you to give such pleasure ! dear little Clemmy ! " 

 this artless praise , and the perfect absence of envy or thought of self 
 evince by her joy that her friend 's wish be gratify , though her own 
 be not , enchant Kenelm . 

 " if it please to give pleasure , " say he , " it be your turn to be 
 pleased now ; you can confer such pleasure upon I . " 

 " how ? " she ask , falteringly , and with quick change of colour . 

 " by concede to I the same right your little friend have allow . " 

 and he draw forth the ring . 

 Lily rear her head with a first impulse of haughtiness . but when 
 her eye meet his the head droop down again , and a slight shiver run 
 through her frame . 

 " Miss Mordaunt , " resume Kenelm , master his passionate longing to 
 fall at her foot and say , " but , oh ! in this ring it be my love that 
 I offer , - it be my troth that I pledge ! " " Miss Mordaunt , spare I the 
 misery of think that I have offend you ; least of all would I do so 
 on this day , for it may be some little while before I see you again . 
 I be go home for a few day upon a matter which may affect the 
 happiness of my life , and on which I should be a bad son and an 
 unworthy gentleman if I do not consult he who , in all that concern 
 my affection , have train I to turn to he , the father ; in all that 
 concern my honour to he , the gentleman . " 

 a speech more unlike that which any delineator of manner and moral in 
 the present day would put into the mouth of a lover , no critic in " The 
 Londoner " could ridicule . but , somehow or other , this poor little tame 
 of butterfly and teller of fairy tale comprehend on the instant all 
 that this most eccentric of human being thus frigidly leave untold . 
 into her innermost heart it sink more deeply than would the most ardent 
 declaration put into the lip of the booby or the scamp in whom 
 delineator of manner in the present day too often debase the 
 magnificent chivalry embody in the name of " lover . " 

 where these two have , while speak , halt on the path along the 
 brook - side , there be a bench , on which it so happen that they have 
 seat themselves week before . a few moment later on that bench they 
 be seat again . 

 and the trumpery little ring with its turquoise heart be on Lily 's 
 finger , and there they continue to sit for nearly half an hour ; 
 not talk much , but wondrously happy ; not a single vow of troth 
 interchange . no , not even a word that could be construe into " I love . " 
 and yet when they rise from the bench , and go silently along the 
 brook - side , each know that the other be belove . 

 when they reach the gate that admit into the garden of Grasmere , 
 Kenelm make a slight start . Mrs. Cameron be lean over the gate . 
 whatever alarm at the appearance Kenelm might have feel be certainly 
 not share by Lily ; she advance lightly before he , kiss her aunt on 
 the cheek , and pass on across the lawn with a bind in her step and 
 the carol of a song upon her lip . 

 Kenelm remain by the gate , face to face with Mrs. Cameron . she open 
 the gate , put her arm in his , and lead he back along the brook - side . 

 " I be sure , Mr. Chillingly , " she say , " that you will not impute to my 
 word any meaning more grave than that which I wish they to convey , 
 when I remind you that there be no place too obscure to escape from the 
 ill - nature of gossip , and you must own that my niece incur the chance 
 of its notice if she be see walk alone in these by - path with a 
 man of your age and position , and whose sojourn in the neighbourhood , 
 without any ostensible object or motive , have already begin to excite 
 conjecture . I do not for a moment assume that you regard my niece in any 
 other light than that of an artless child , whose originality of taste 
 or fancy may serve to amuse you ; and still less do I suppose that she 
 be in danger of misrepresent any attention on your part . but for her 
 sake I be bind to consider what other may say . excuse I , then , if I 
 add that I think you be also bind in honour and in good feeling to do 
 the same . Mr. Chillingly , it would give I a great sense of relief if it 
 suit your plan to move from the neighbourhood . " 

 " my dear Mrs. Cameron , " answer Kenelm , who have listen to this speech 
 with imperturbable calm of visage , " I thank you much for your candour , 
 and I be glad to have this opportunity of inform you that I be about 
 to move from this neighbourhood , with the hope of return to it in 
 a very few day and rectify your mistake as to the point of view 
 in which I regard your niece . in a word , " here the expression of his 
 countenance and the tone of his voice undergo a sudden change , " it be 
 the dear wish of my heart to be empower by my parent to assure you 
 of the warmth with which they will welcome your niece as their daughter , 
 should she deign to listen to my suit and intrust I with the charge of 
 her happiness . " 

 Mrs. Cameron stop short , gaze into his face with a look of 
 inexpressible dismay . 

 " no ! Mr. Chillingly , " she exclaim , " this must not be , - can not be . put 
 out of your mind an idea so wild . a young man 's senseless romance . 
 your parent can not consent to your union with my niece ; I tell you 
 beforehand they can not . " 

 " but why ? " ask Kenelm , with a slight smile , and not much impressed by 
 the vehemence of Mrs. Cameron 's adjuration . 

 " why ? " she repeat passionately ; and then recover something of her 
 habitual weariness of quiet . " the why be easily explain . Mr. Kenelm 
 Chillingly be the heir of a very ancient house and , I be tell , of 
 considerable estate . Lily Mordaunt be a nobody , an orphan , without 
 fortune , without connection , the ward of a humbly bear artist , to 
 whom she owe the roof that shelter she ; she be without the ordinary 
 education of a gentlewoman ; she have see nothing of the world in which 
 you move . your parent have not the right to allow a son so young 
 as yourself to throw himself out of his proper sphere by a rash and 
 imprudent alliance . and , never would I consent , never would Walter 
 Melville consent , to she enter into any family reluctant to receive 
 she . there , - that be enough . dismiss the notion so lightly entertain . 
 and farewell . " 

 " Madam , " answer Kenelm very earnestly , " believe I , that have I not 
 entertain the hope approach to conviction that the reason you urge 
 against my presumption will not have the weight with my parent which 
 you ascribe to they , I should not have speak to you thus frankly . Young 
 though I be , still I might fairly claim the right to choose for myself 
 in marriage . but I give to my father a very binding promise that I would 
 not formally propose to any one till I have acquaint he with my desire 
 to do so , and obtain his approval of my choice ; and he be the last man 
 in the world who would withhold that approval where my heart be set on 
 it as it be now . I want no fortune with a wife , and should I ever care 
 to advance my position in the world , no connection would help I like 
 the approve smile of the woman I love . there be but one qualification 
 which my parent would deem they have the right to exact from my 
 choice of one who be to bear our name . I mean that she should have the 
 appearance , the manner , the principle , and - my mother at least might 
 add - the birth of a gentlewoman . well , as to appearance and manner , I 
 have see much of fine society from my boyhood , and find no one among 
 the highest bear who can excel the exquisite refinement of every look , 
 and the inborn delicacy of every thought , in she of whom , if mine , I 
 shall be as proud as I shall be fond . as to defect in the frippery 
 and tinsel of a boarding - school education , they be very soon remedie . 
 remain only the last consideration , - birth . Mrs. Braefield inform I 
 that you have assure she that , though circumstance into which as yet 
 I have no right to inquire , have make she the ward of a man of humble 
 origin , Miss Mordaunt be of gentle birth . do you deny that ? " 

 " no , " say Mrs. Cameron , hesitating , but with a flash of pride in her 
 eye as she go on . " no . I can not deny that my niece be descend from 
 those who , in point of birth , be not unequal to your own ancestor . 
 but what of that ? " she add , with a bitter despondency of tone . 
 " equality of birth cease when one fall into poverty , obscurity , 
 neglect , nothingness ! " 

 " really this be a morbid habit on your part . but , since we have thus 
 speak so confidentially , will you not empower I to answer the question 
 which will probably be put to I , and the answer to which will , I doubt 
 not , remove every obstacle in the way of my happiness ? whatever the 
 reason which might very sufficiently induce you to preserve , whilst 
 live so quietly in this place , a discreet silence as to the parentage 
 of Miss Mordaunt and your own , - and I be well aware that those whom 
 alter circumstance of fortune have compel to alter mode of life 
 may disdain to parade to stranger the pretension to a high station 
 than that to which they reconcile their habit , - whatever , I say , 
 such reason for silence to stranger , should they preclude you from 
 confide to I , an aspirant to your niece 's hand , a secret which , after 
 all , can not be conceal from her future husband ? " 

 " from her future husband ? of course not , " answer Mrs. Cameron . " but I 
 decline to be question by one whom I may never see again , and of whom 
 I know so little . I decline , indeed , to assist in remove any obstacle 
 to a union with my niece , which I hold to be in every way unsuited to 
 either party . I have no cause even to believe that my niece would accept 
 you if you be free to propose to she . you have not , I presume , speak 
 to she as an aspirant to her hand . you have not address to she 
 any declaration of your attachment , or seek to extract from her 
 inexperience any word that warrant you in think that her heart will 
 break if she never see you again . " 

 " I do not merit such cruel and taunt question , " say Kenelm , 
 indignantly . " but I will say no more now . when we again meet let I hope 
 you will treat I less unkindly . adieu ! " 

 " Stay , sir . a word or two more . you persist in ask your father and 
 Lady Chillingly to consent to your proposal to Miss Mordaunt ? " 

 " certainly I do . " 

 " and you will promise I , on your word as a gentleman , to state fairly 
 all the cause which might fairly operate against their consent , - the 
 poverty , the humble rearing , the imperfect education of my niece , - so 
 that they might not hereafter say you have entrap their consent , and 
 avenge themselves for your deceit by contempt for she ? " 

 " ah , madam , madam , you really try my patience too far . but take my 
 promise , if you can hold that of value from one whom you can suspect of 
 deliberate deceit . " 

 " I beg your pardon , Mr. Chillingly . Bear with my rudeness . I have be 
 so take by surprise , I scarcely know what I be say . but let we 
 understand each other completely before we part . if your parent 
 withhold their consent you will communicate it to I ; I only , not to 
 Lily . I repeat I know nothing of the state of her affection . but it 
 might embitter any girl 's life to be lead on to love one whom she could 
 not marry . " 

 " it shall be as you say . but if they do consent ? " 

 " then you will speak to I before you seek an interview with Lily , for 
 then come another question : will her guardian consent ? - and - and - " 

 " and what ? " 

 " no matter . I rely on your honour in this request , as in all else . 
 good - day . " 

 she turn back with hurried footstep , mutter to herself , " but they 
 will not consent . Heaven grant that they will not consent , or if they 
 do , what - what be to be say or do ? oh , that Walter Melville be 
 here , or that I know where to write to he ! " 

 on his way back to Cromwell Lodge , Kenelm be overtake by the vicar . 

 " I be come to you , my dear Mr. Chillingly , first to thank you for the 
 very pretty present with which you have gladden the heart of my little 
 Clemmy , and next to ask you to come with I quietly to - day to meet Mr. 
  - - - , the celebrate antiquarian , who come to Moleswich this morning 
 at my request to examine that old Gothic tomb in our churchyard . only 
 think , though he can not read the inscription any well than we can , he 
 know all about its history . it seem that a young knight renowne for 
 feat of valour in the reign of Henry IV . marry a daughter of one of 
 those great Earls of Montfichet who be then the most powerful family 
 in these part . he be slay in defend the church from an assault by 
 some disorderly rioter of the Lollard faction ; he fall on the very spot 
 where the tomb be now place . that account for its situation in the 
 churchyard , not within the fabric . Mr. - - - discover this fact in 
 an old memoir of the ancient and once famous family to which the young 
 knight Albert belong , and which come , alas ! to so shameful an end , 
 the Fletwodes , baron of Fletwode and Malpas . what a triumph over pretty 
 Lily Mordaunt , who always choose to imagine that the tomb must be that of 
 some heroine of her own romantic invention ! do come to dinner ; Mr. - - - 
 be a most agreeable man , and full of interesting anecdote . " 

 " I be so sorry I can not . I be oblige to return home at once for a few 
 day . that old family of Fletwode ! I think I see before I , while we 
 speak , the gray tower in which they once hold sway ; and the last of the 
 race follow Mammon along the Progress of the Age , - a convict felon ! 
 what a terrible satire on the pride of birth ! " 

 Kenelm leave Cromwell Lodge that evening , but he still keep on his 
 apartment there , say he might be back unexpectedly any day in the 
 course of the next week . 

 he remain two day in London , wish all that he have communicate to 
 Sir Peter in write to sink into his father 's heart before a personal 
 appeal to it . 

 the more he revolve the ungracious manner in which Mrs. Cameron have 
 receive his confidence , the less importance he attach to it . an 
 exaggerated sense of disparity of fortune in a person who appear 
 to he to have the pride so common to those who have know well day , 
 couple with a nervous apprehension lest his family should ascribe to 
 she any attempt to ensnare a very young man of considerable worldly 
 pretension into a marriage with a penniless niece , seem to account 
 for much that have at first perplexed and anger he . and if , as he 
 conjecture , Mrs. Cameron have once hold a much high position in the 
 world than she do now , - a conjecture warrant by a certain peculiar 
 conventional undeniable elegance which characterize her habitual 
 manner , - and be now , as she imply , actually a dependant on the bounty 
 of a painter who have only just acquire some professional distinction , 
 she might well shrink from the mortification of become an object of 
 compassion to her rich neighbour ; nor , when he come to think of it , 
 have he any more right than those neighbour to any confidence as to 
 her own or Lily 's parentage , so long as he be not formally entitle to 
 claim admission into her privity . 

 London seem to he intolerably dull and wearisome . he call nowhere 
 except at Lady Glenalvon 's ; he be glad to hear from the servant that 
 she be still at Exmundham . he rely much on the influence of the queen 
 of the fashion with his mother , whom he know would be more difficult to 
 persuade than Sir Peter , nor do he doubt that he should win to his side 
 that sympathize and warm - hearted queen . 



 CHAPTER VII . 

 it be somewhere about three week since the party invite by Sir Peter 
 and Lady Chillingly assemble at Exmundham , and they be still there , 
 though people invite to a country house have seldom compassion 
 enough for the dulness of its owner to stay more than three day . Mr. 
 Chillingly Mivers , indeed , have not exceed that orthodox limit . quietly 
 observant , during his stay , of young Gordon 's manner towards Cecilia , 
 and her towards he , he have satisfy himself that there be no 
 cause to alarm Sir Peter , or induce the worthy baronet to regret the 
 invitation he have give to that clever kinsman . for all the visitor 
 remain Exmundham have a charm . 

 to Lady Glenalvon , because in the hostess she meet she most familiar 
 friend when both be young girl , and because it please she to note 
 the interest which Cecilia Travers take in the place so associate with 
 memory of the man to whom it be Lady Glenalvon 's hope to see she 
 united . to Chillingly Gordon , because no opportunity could be so 
 favourable for his own well - conceal design on the hand and heart of 
 the heiress . to the heiress herself the charm need no explanation . 

 to Leopold Travers the attraction of Exmundham be unquestionably less 
 fascinating . still even he be well pleased to prolong his stay . his 
 active mind find amusement in wander over an estate the acreage of 
 which would have warrant a much large rental , and lecture Sir Peter 
 on the old - fashioned system of husbandry which that good - natured easy 
 proprietor permit his tenant to adopt , as well as on the number of 
 superfluous hand that be employ on the pleasure - ground and in the 
 general management of the estate , such as carpenter , sawyer , woodman , 
 bricklayer , and smith . 

 when the squire say , " you could do just as well with a third of those 
 costly dependant , " Sir Peter , unconsciously plagiarize the answer of 
 the old french grand seigneur , reply , " very likely . but the question 
 be , could the rest do just as well without I ? " 

 Exmundham , indeed , be a very expensive place to keep up . the house , 
 build by some ambitious Chillingly three century ago , would have be 
 large for an owner of thrice the revenue ; and though the flower - garden 
 be small than that at Braefieldville , there be path and drive 
 through mile of young plantation and old woodland that furnish lazy 
 occupation to an army of labourer . no wonder that , despite his nominal 
 ten thousand a year , Sir Peter be far from be a rich man . Exmundham 
 devour at least half the rental . the active mind of Leopold Travers 
 also find ample occupation in the store of his host 's extensive 
 library . 

 traver , never much of a reader , be by no mean a despiser of learning , 
 and he soon take to historical and archaeological research with the 
 ardour of a man who must always throw energy into any pursuit that 
 occasion present as an escape from indolence . Indolent Leopold Travers 
 never could be . but , more than either of these resource of occupation , 
 the companionship of Chillingly Gordon excite his interest and 
 quicken the current of his thought . always fond of renew his own 
 youth in the society of the young , and of the sympathize temperament 
 which belong to cordial nature , he have , as we have see , enter very 
 heartily into the ambition of George Belvoir , and reconcile himself 
 very pliably to the humour of Kenelm Chillingly . but the first of these 
 two be a little too commonplace , the second a little too eccentric , to 
 enlist the complete good - fellowship which , be alike very clever and 
 very practical , Leopold Travers establish with that very clever and 
 very practical representative of the rise generation , Chillingly 
 Gordon . between they there be this meeting - ground , political and 
 worldly , a great contempt for innocuous old - fashioned notion ; add to 
 which , in the mind of Leopold Travers , be a contempt - which would 
 have be complete , but that the contempt admit dread - of harmful 
 new - fashioned notion which , interpret by his thought , threaten 
 ruin to his country and downfall to the folly of existent society , 
 and which , interpret by his language , tame itself into the man of the 
 world 's phrase , " go too far for I . " notion which , by the much 
 more cultivate intellect and the immeasurably more soaring ambition of 
 Chillingly Gordon , might be view and criticise thus : " could I accept 
 these doctrine ? I do not   see my way to be Prime Minister of a country 
 in which religion and capital be still power to be consult . and , 
 put aside religion and capital , I do not   see how , if these doctrine 
 pass into law , with a good coat on my back I should not be a sufferer . 
 either I , as have a good coat , should have it tear off my back as a 
 capitalist , or , if I remonstrate in the name of moral honesty , be put 
 to death as a religionist . " 

 therefore when Leopold Travers say , " of course we must go on , " 
 chillingly Gordon smile and answer , " certainly , go on . " and when 
 Leopold Travers add , " but we may go too far , " chillingly Gordon shake 
 his dead , and reply , " how true that be ! certainly too far . " 

 apart from the congeniality of political sentiment , there be other 
 point of friendly contact between the old and young man . each be 
 an exceedingly pleasant man of the world ; and , though Leopold Travers 
 could not have plumb certain deep in Chillingly Gordon 's nature , - and 
 in every man 's nature there be deep which his able observer can not 
 fathom , - yet he be not wrong when he say to himself , " Gordon be a 
 gentleman . " 

 utterly would my reader misconceive that very clever young man , if they 
 hold he to be a hypocrite like Blifil or Joseph Surface . chillingly 
 Gordon , in every private sense of the word , be a gentleman . if he have 
 stake his whole fortune on a rubber at whist , and an undetected glance 
 at his adversary 's hand would have make the difference between loss and 
 gain , he would have turn away his head and say , " hold up your card . " 
 Neither , as I have have occasion to explain before , be he actuate 
 by any motive in common with the vulgar fortune - hunter in his secret 
 resolve to win the hand of the heiress . he recognize no inequality of 
 worldly gift between they . he say to himself , " whatever she may give 
 I in money , I shall amply repay in worldly position if I succeed , and 
 succeed I certainly shall . if I be as rich as Lord Westminster , and 
 still care about be Prime Minister , I should select she as the most 
 fitting woman I have see for a Prime Minister 's wife . " 

 it must be acknowledge that this sort of self - commune , if not that of 
 a very ardent lover , be very much that of a sensible man set high 
 value on himself , bent on achieve the prize of a public career , and 
 desirous of securing in his wife a woman who would adorn the station 
 to which he confidently aspire . in fact , no one so able as Chillingly 
 Gordon would ever have conceive the ambition of be Minister of 
 England if in all that in private life constitute the english gentleman 
 he could be fairly subject to reproach . 

 he be but in public life what many a gentleman honest in private life 
 have be before he , an ambitious , resolute egotist , by no mean without 
 personal affection , but hold they all subordinate to the object 
 of personal ambition , and with no more of other principle than that 
 of expediency in reference to his own career than would cover a silver 
 penny . but expediency in itself he deem the statesman 's only rational 
 principle . and to the consideration of expediency he bring a very 
 unprejudiced intellect , quite fit to decide whether the public 
 opinion of a free and enlightened people be for turn St. Paul 's 
 Cathedral into an Agapemone or not . 

 during the summer week he have thus vouchsafe to the turf and grove 
 of Exmundham , Leopold Travers be not the only person whose good opinion 
 Chillingly Gordon have ingratiate . he have win the warm approbation 
 from Mrs. Campion . his conversation remind she of that which she have 
 enjoy in the house of her depart spouse . in talk with Cecilia she 
 be fond of contrast he to Kenelm , not to the favour of the 
 latter , whose humour she utterly fail to understand , and whom she 
 pertinaciously describe as ' so affected . " " a most superior young man 
 Mr. Gordon , so well inform , so sensible , - above all , so natural . " such 
 be her judgment upon the unavowed candidate to Cecilia 's hand ; and 
 Mrs. Campion require no avowal to divine the candidature . even Lady 
 Glenalvon have begin to take friendly interest in the fortune of this 
 promise young man . Most woman can sympathize with youthful ambition . 
 he impress she with a deep conviction of his ability , and still more 
 with respect for their concentration upon practical object of power 
 and renown . she too , like Mrs. Campion , begin to draw comparison 
 unfavourable to Kenelm between the two cousin : the one seem so 
 slothfully determine to hide his candle under a bushel , the other so 
 honestly disposed to set his light before man . she feel also annoy and 
 angry that Kenelm be thus absent himself from the paternal home at 
 the very time of her first visit to it , and when he have so felicitous 
 an opportunity of see more of the girl in whom he know that Lady 
 Glenalvon deem he might win , if he would properly woo , the wife that 
 would well suit he . so that when one day Mrs. Campion , walk through 
 the garden alone with Lady Glenalvon while from the garden into the 
 park go Chillingly Gordon , arm - in - arm with Leopold Travers , abruptly 
 ask , " do not   you think that Mr. Gordon be smite with Cecilia , though 
 he , with his moderate fortune , do not dare to say so ? and do not   you 
 think that any girl , if she be as rich as Cecilia will be , would be 
 more proud of such a husband as Chillingly Gordon than of some silly 
 earl ? " 

 Lady Glenalvon answer curtly , but somewhat sorrowfully , " yes . " 

 after a pause she add , " there be a man with whom I do once think she 
 would have be happy than with any other . one man who ought to be 
 dearer to I than Mr. Gordon , for he save the life of my son , and who , 
 though perhaps less clever than Mr. Gordon , still have a great deal of 
 talent within he , which might come forth and make he - what shall I 
 say ? - a useful and distinguished member of society , if marry to a girl 
 so sure of raise any man she marry as Cecilia Travers . but if I be 
 to renounce that hope , and look through the range of young man bring 
 under my notice , I do not   know one , put aside consideration of rank 
 and fortune , I should prefer for a clever daughter who go heart and 
 soul with the ambition of a clever man . but , Mrs. Campion , I have not 
 yet quite renounce my hope ; and , unless I do , I yet think there be one 
 man to whom I would rather give Cecilia , if she be my daughter . " 

 Therewith Lady Glenalvon so decidedly break off the subject of 
 conversation that Mrs. Campion could not have renew it without such a 
 breach of the female etiquette of good breeding as Mrs. Campion be the 
 last person to adventure . 

 Lady Chillingly could not help be pleased with Gordon . he be light 
 in hand , serve to amuse her guest , and make up a rubber of whist in 
 case of need . 

 there be two person , however , with whom Gordon make no ground ; 
 namely , Parson John and Sir Peter . when Travers praise he one day for 
 the solidity of his part and the soundness of his judgment , the Parson 
 reply snappishly , " yes , solid and sound as one of those table you 
 buy at a broker 's ; the thickness of the varnish hide the defect in 
 the joint : the whole framework be rickety . " but when the Parson be 
 indignantly urge to state the reason by which he arrive at so harsh 
 a conclusion , he could only reply by an assertion which seem to his 
 questioner a declamatory burst of parsonic intolerance . 

 " because , " say Parson John , " he have no love for man , and no reverence 
 for God . and no character be sound and solid which enlarge its surface 
 at the expense of its support . " 

 on the other hand , the favour with which Sir Peter have at first regard 
 Gordon gradually vanish , in proportion as , act on the hint miver 
 have originally throw out but do not deem it necessary to repeat , he 
 watch the pain which the young man take to insinuate himself into 
 the good grace of Mr. Travers and Mrs. Campion , and the artful and 
 half - suppress gallantry of his manner to the heiress . 

 perhaps Gordon have not venture thus " to feel his way " till after miver 
 have depart ; or perhaps Sir Peter 's parental anxiety render he , in 
 this instance , a shrewd observer than be the man of the world , 
 whose natural acuteness be , in matter of affection , not unfrequently 
 render languid by his acquire philosophy of indifferentism . 

 More and more every day , every hour , of her sojourn beneath his roof , 
 do Cecilia become dearer to Sir Peter , and strong and strong became 
 his wish to secure she for his daughter - in - law . he be inexpressibly 
 flatter by her preference for his company : ever at hand to share his 
 customary walk , his kindly visit to the cottage of peasant or the 
 homestead of petty tenant ; wherein both be sure to hear many a 
 simple anecdote of Master Kenelm in his childhood , anecdote of whim or 
 good - nature , of considerate pity or reckless courage . 

 throughout all these variety of thought or feel in the social 
 circle around she , Lady Chillingly preserve the unmoved calm of she 
 dignified position . a very good woman certainly , and very ladylike . no 
 one could detect a flaw in her character , or a fold awry in her flounce . 
 she be only , like the god of Epicurus , too good to trouble her serene 
 existence with the care of we simple mortal . not that she be without 
 a placid satisfaction in the tribute which the world lay upon she 
 altar ; nor be she so supremely goddess - like as to soar above the 
 household affection which humanity entail on the dweller and denizen 
 of earth . she like her husband as much as most elderly wife like their 
 elderly husband . she bestow upon Kenelm a like somewhat more warm , 
 and mingle with compassion . his eccentricity would have puzzle she , 
 if she have allow herself to be puzzle : it trouble she less to pity 
 they . she do not share her husband 's desire for his union with Cecilia . 
 she think that her son would have a high place in the county if he 
 marry Lady Jane , the Duke of Clanville 's daughter ; and " that be what 
 he ought to do , " say Lady Chillingly to herself . she entertain 
 none of the fear that have induce Sir Peter to extract from Kenelm 
 the promise not to pledge his hand before he have receive his father 's 
 consent . that the son of Lady Chillingly should make a mesalliance , 
 however crotchety he might be in other respect , be a thought that it 
 would have so disturb she to admit that she do not admit it . 

 such be the condition of thing at Exmundham when the lengthy 
 communication of Kenelm reach Sir Peter 's hand . 




 BOOK VIII . 



 CHAPTER I. 

 never in his whole life have the mind of Sir Peter be so agitated as it 
 be during and after the perusal of Kenelm 's flighty composition . he have 
 receive it at the breakfast - table , and , open it eagerly , run his eye 
 hastily over the content , till he very soon arrive at sentence 
 which appal he . Lady Chillingly , who be fortunately busy at the 
 tea - urn , do not observe the dismay on his countenance . it be visible 
 only to Cecilia and to Gordon . neither guess who that letter be from . 

 " no bad news , I hope , " say Cecilia , softly . 

 " bad news , " echo Sir Peter . " no , my dear , no ; a letter on business . 
 it seem terribly long , " and he thrust the packet into his pocket , 
 muttering , ' see to it by and by . " 

 " that slovenly farmer of your , Mr. Nostock , have fail , I suppose , " 
 say Mr. Travers , look up and observe a quiver on his host 's 
 lip . " I tell you he would , - a fine farm too . let I choose you another 
 tenant . " 

 Sir Peter shake his head with a wan smile . 

 " Nostock will not fail . there have be six generation of nostock on 
 the farm . " 

 " so I should guess , " say Travers , dryly . 

 " and - and , " falter Sir Peter , " if the last of the race fail , he must 
 lean upon I , and - if one of the two break down - it shall not be - " 

 " shall not be that cross - cropping blockhead , my dear Sir Peter . this be 
 carry benevolence too far . " 

 here the tact and savoir vivre of Chillingly Gordon come to the rescue 
 of the host . possess himself of the " Times " newspaper , he utter an 
 exclamation of surprise , genuine or simulated , and read aloud an extract 
 from the lead article , announce an impending change in the Cabinet . 

 as soon as he could quit the breakfast - table , Sir Peter hurry into 
 his library and there give himself up to the study of Kenelm 's unwelcome 
 communication . the task take he long , for he stop at interval , 
 overcome by the struggle of his heart , now melt into sympathy with the 
 passionate eloquence of a son hitherto so free from amorous romance , and 
 now sorrow for the ruin of his own cherished hope . this uneducated 
 country girl would never be such a helpmate to a man like Kenelm as 
 would have be Cecilia Travers . at length , have finish the letter , 
 he bury his head between his clasp hand , and try hard to 
 realize the situation that place the father and son into such direct 
 antagonism . 

 " but , " he murmur , " after all it be the boy 's happiness that must be 
 consult . if he will not be happy in my way , what right have I to say 
 that he shall not be happy in his ? " 

 just then Cecilia come softly into the room . she have acquire the 
 privilege of enter his library at will ; sometimes to choose a book of 
 his recommendation , sometimes to direct and seal his letter , - Sir Peter 
 be grateful to any one who save he an extra trouble , - and 
 sometimes , especially at this hour , to decoy he forth into his wonte 
 constitutional walk . 

 he lift his face at the sound of her approach tread and her win 
 voice , and the face be so sad that the tear rush to her eye on 
 see it . she lay her hand on his shoulder , and say pleadingly , " Dear 
 Sir Peter , what be it , - what be it ? " 

 " ah - ah , my dear , " say Sir Peter , gather up the scatter sheet of 
 Kenelm 's effusion with hurried , tremble hand . " do not   ask , - do not   talk 
 of it ; ' tis but one of the disappointment that all of we must undergo , 
 when we invest our hope in the uncertain will of other . " 

 then , observe that the tear be trickle down the girl 's fair , pale 
 cheek , he take her hand in both his , kiss her forehead , and say , 
 whisperingly , " pretty one , how good you have be to I ! Heaven bless 
 you . what a wife you will be to some man ! " 

 thus say , he shamble out of the room through the open casement . she 
 follow he impulsively , wonderingly ; but before she reach his side 
 he turn round , wave his hand with a gently repel gesture , and 
 go his way alone through dense fir - grove which have be plant in 
 honour of Kenelm 's birth . 



 CHAPTER II . 

 KENELM arrive at Exmundham just in time to dress for dinner . his 
 arrival be not unexpected , for the morning after his father have 
 receive his communication , Sir Peter have say to Lady Chillingly - " that 
 he have hear from Kenelm to the effect that he might be down any day . " 

 " quite time he should come , " say Lady Chillingly . " have you his letter 
 about you ? " 

 " no , my dear Caroline . of course he send you his kind love , poor 
 fellow . " 

 " why poor fellow ? have he be ill ? " 

 " no ; but there seem to be something on his mind . if so we must do what 
 we can to relieve it . he be the good of son , Caroline . " 

 " I be sure I have nothing to say against he , except , " add she 
 ladyship , reflectively , " that I do wish he be a little more like other 
 young man . " 

 " Hum - like Chillingly Gordon , for instance ? " 

 " well , yes ; Mr. Gordon be a remarkably well - breed , sensible young man . 
 how different from that disagreeable , bearish father of his , who go to 
 law with you ! " 

 " very different indeed , but with just as much of the chillingly blood in 
 he . how the Chillinglys ever give birth to a Kenelm be a question much 
 more puzzling . " 

 " oh , my dear Sir Peter , do not   be metaphysical . you know how I hate 
 puzzle . " 

 " and yet , Caroline , I have to thank you for a puzzle which I can never 
 interpret by my brain . there be a great many puzzle in human nature 
 which can only be interpret by the heart . " 

 " very true , " say Lady Chillingly . " I suppose Kenelm be to have his old 
 room , just opposite to Mr. Gordon 's . " 

 " Ay - ay , just opposite . opposite they will be all their life . only 
 think , Caroline , I have make a discovery ! " 

 " dear I ! I hope not . your discovery be generally very expensive , and 
 bring we in contact with such very odd people . " 

 " this discovery shall not cost we a penny , and I do not   know any people 
 so odd as not to comprehend it . briefly it be this : to genius the 
 first requisite be heart ; it be no requisite at all to talent . my dear 
 Caroline , Gordon have as much talent as any young man I know , but he 
 want the first requisite of genius . I be not by any mean sure that 
 Kenelm have genius , but there be no doubt that he have the first requisite 
 of genius , - heart . heart be a very perplexing , wayward , irrational 
 thing ; and that perhaps account for the general incapacity to 
 comprehend genius , while any fool can comprehend talent . my dear 
 Caroline , you know that it be very seldom , not more than once in three 
 year , that I presume to have a will of my own against a will of your ; 
 but should there come a question in which our son 's heart be concern , 
 then   speak between ourselves   my will must govern your . " 

 " Sir Peter be grow more odd every day , " say Lady Chillingly to 
 herself when leave alone . " but he do not mean ill , and there be bad 
 husband in the world . " 

 Therewith she ring for her maid , give requisite order for the preparing 
 of Kenelm 's room , which have not be sleep in for many month , and then 
 consult that functionary as to the adaptation of some dress of her , 
 too costly to be lay aside , to the style of some dress less costly 
 which Lady Glenalvon have import from Paris as la derniere mode . 

 on the very day on which Kenelm arrive at Exmundham , Chillingly Gordon 
 have receive this letter from Mr. Gerald Danvers . 


 DEAR GORDON , - in the ministerial change announce as rumour in the 
 public paper , and which you may accept as certain , that sweet little 
 cherub - be to be send to sit up aloft and pray there for the life of 
 poor Jack ; namely , of the government he leave below . in accept the 
 peerage , which I persuade he to do , - create a vacancy for the 
 borough of - - - , just the place for you , far well in every way than 
 Saxborough . - - - promise to recommend you to his committee . come to 
 town at once . your , etc . 

  G. DANVERS . 


 Gordon show this letter to Mr. Travers , and , on receive the hearty 
 good - wish of that gentleman , say , with emotion partly genuine , partly 
 assume , " you can not guess all that the realization of your good - wish 
 would be . once in the House of Commons , and my motif for action be 
 so strong that - do not think I very conceited if I count upon 
 parliamentary success . " 

 " my clear Gordon , I be as certain of your success as I be of my own 
 existence . " 

 " should I succeed , - should the great prize of public life be within 
 my reach , - should I lift myself into a position that would warrant my 
 presumption , do you think I could come to you and say , ' there be an 
 object of ambition dearer to I than power and office , - the hope of 
 attain which be the strong of all my motif of action ? and in 
 that hope shall I also have the good - wish of the father of Cecilia 
 Travers ? " 

 " my dear fellow , give I your hand ; you speak manfully and candidly as a 
 gentleman should speak . I answer in the same spirit . I do not   pretend 
 to say that I have not entertain view for Cecilia which include 
 hereditary rank and establish fortune in a suitor to her hand , though 
 I never should have make they imperative condition . I be neither 
 potentate nor parvenu enough for that ; and I can never forget "   here 
 every muscle in the man 's face twitch   " that I myself marry for 
 love , and be so happy . how happy Heaven only know ! still , if you have 
 thus speak a few week ago , I should not have reply very favourably 
 to your question . but now that I have see so much of you , my answer be 
 this : if you lose your election , - if you do not   come into Parliament 
 at all , you have my good - wish all the same . if you win my daughter 's 
 heart , there be no man on whom I would more willingly bestow her hand . 
 there she be , by herself too , in the garden . go and talk to she . " 

 Gordon hesitate . he know too well that he have not win her heart , though 
 he have no suspicion that it be give to another . and he be much 
 too clever not to know also how much he hazard who , in affair of 
 courtship , be premature . 

 " ah ! " he say , " I can not express my gratitude for word so generous , 
 encouragement so cheer . but I have never yet dare to utter to Miss 
 Travers a word that would prepare she even to harbour a thought of I as 
 a suitor . and I scarcely think I should have the courage to go through 
 this election with the grief of her rejection on my heart . " 

 " well , go in and win the election first ; meanwhile , at all event , take 
 leave of Cecilia . " 

 Gordon leave his friend , and join Miss Travers , resolve not indeed 
 to risk a formal declaration , but to sound his way to his chance of 
 acceptance . 

 the interview be very brief . he do sound his way skilfully , and feel 
 it very unsafe for his footstep . the advantage of have gain the 
 approval of the father be too great to be lose altogether , by one of 
 those decide answer on the part of the daughter which allow of no 
 appeal , especially to a poor gentleman who woo an heiress . 

 he return to Travers , and say simply , " I bear with I her good - wish 
 as well as your . that be all . I leave myself in your kind hand . " 

 then he hurry away to take leave of his host and hostess , say a few 
 significant word to the ally he have already gain in Mrs. Campion , and 
 within an hour be on his road to London , pass on his way the train 
 that bear Kenelm to Exmundham . Gordon be in high spirit . at least he 
 feel as certain of win Cecilia as he do of win his election . 

 " I have never yet fail in what I desire , " say he to himself , 
 " because I have ever take pain not to fail . " 

 the cause of Gordon 's sudden departure create a great excitement in 
 that quiet circle , share by all except Cecilia and Sir Peter . 



 CHAPTER III . 

 KENELM do not see either father or mother till he appear at dinner . 
 then he be seat next to Cecilia . there be but little conversation 
 between the two ; in fact , the prevalent subject of talk be general and 
 engrossing , the interest in Chillingly Gordon 's election ; prediction 
 of his success , of what he would do in Parliament . " where , " say Lady 
 Glenalvon , " there be such a dearth of rise young man , that if he be 
 only half as clever as he be he would be a gain . " 

 " a gain to what ? " ask Sir Peter , testily . " to his country ? about which 
 I do not   believe he care a brass button . " 

 to this assertion Leopold Travers reply warmly , and be not less 
 warmly back by Mrs. Campion . 

 " for my part , " say Lady Glenalvon , in conciliatory accent , " I think 
 every able man in Parliament be a gain to the country ; and he may not 
 serve his country less effectively because he do not boast of his 
 love for it . the politician I dread most be those so rampant in France 
 nowadays , the bawl patriot . when Sir Robert Walpole say , ' all 
 those man have their price , ' he point to the man who call themselves 
 ' patriot . ' " 

 " Bravo ! " cry Travers . 

 " Sir Robert Walpole show his love for his country by corrupt it . 
 there be many way besides bribe for corrupt a country , " say 
 Kenelm , mildly , and that be Kenelm 's sole contribution to the general 
 conversation . 

 it be not till the rest of the party have retire to rest that the 
 conference , long for by Kenelm , dread by Sir Peter , take place in 
 the library . it last deep into the night ; both part with lightened 
 heart and a fonder affection for each other . Kenelm have draw so 
 charm a picture of the Fairy , and so thoroughly convinced Sir Peter 
 that his own feeling towards she be those of no pass youthful 
 fancy , but of that love which have its root in the innermost heart , 
 that though it be still with a sigh , a deep sigh , that he dismiss 
 the thought of Cecilia , Sir Peter do dismiss it ; and , take comfort at 
 last from the positive assurance that Lily be of gentle birth , and 
 the fact that her name of Mordaunt be that of ancient and illustrious 
 house , say , with half a smile , " it might have be bad , my dear 
 boy . I begin to be afraid that , in spite of the teaching of Mivers and 
 Welby , it be ' the Miller 's daughter , ' after all . but we still have 
 a difficult task to persuade your poor mother . in cover your first 
 flight from our roof I unluckily put into her head the notion of Lady 
 Jane , a duke 's daughter , and the notion have never get out of it . that 
 come of fib . " 

 " I count on Lady Glenalvon 's influence on my mother in support of 
 your own , " say Kenelm . " if so accept an oracle in the great world 
 pronounce in my favour , and promise to present my wife at Court and 
 bring she into fashion , I think that my mother will consent to allow we 
 to reset the old family diamond for her next reappearance in London . 
 and then , too , you can tell she that I will stand for the county . I will 
 go into Parliament , and if I meet there our clever cousin , and find that 
 he do not care a brass button for the country , take my word for it , I 
 will lick he more easily than I lick Tom Bowles . " 

 " Tom Bowles ! who be he ? - ah ! I remember some letter of yours in which 
 you speak of a Bowles , whose favourite study be mankind , a moral 
 philosopher . " 

 " moral philosopher , " answer Kenelm , " have so muddle their brain 
 with the alcohol of new idea that their moral leg have become shaky , 
 and the humane would rather help they to bed than give they a licking . 
 my Tom Bowles be a muscular Christian , who become no less muscular , but 
 much more Christian , after he be lick . " 

 and in this pleasant manner these two oddity settle their conference , 
 and go up to bed with arm wrap round each other 's shoulder . 



 CHAPTER IV . 

 KENELM find it a much hard matter to win Lady Glenalvon to his side 
 than he have anticipate . with the strong interest she have take in 
 Kenelm 's future , she could not but revolt from the idea of his union 
 with an obscure portionless girl whom he have only know a few week , 
 and of whose very parentage he seem to know nothing , save an assurance 
 that she be his equal in birth . and , with the desire , which she have 
 cherish almost as fondly as Sir Peter , that Kenelm might win a bride 
 in every way so worthy of his choice as Cecilia Travers , she feel not 
 less indignant than regretful at the overthrow of her plan . 

 at first , indeed , she be so provoke that she would not listen to 
 his pleading . she break away from he with a rudeness she have never 
 exhibit to any one before , refuse to grant he another interview in 
 order to re - discuss the matter , and say that , so far from use her 
 influence in favour of his romantic folly , she would remonstrate well 
 with Lady Chillingly and Sir Peter against yield their assent to his 
 " thus throw himself away . " 

 it be not till the third day after his arrival that , touch by the 
 grave but haughty mournfulness of his countenance , she yield to the 
 argument of Sir Peter in the course of a private conversation with that 
 worthy baronet . still it be reluctantly   she do not fulfil her threat 
 of remonstrance with Lady Chillingly   that she concede the point , 
 that a son who , succeed to the absolute fee - simple of an estate , have 
 volunteer the resettlement of it on term singularly generous to both 
 his parent , be entitle to some sacrifice of their inclination on a 
 question in which he deem his happiness vitally concerned ; and that he 
 be of age to choose for himself independently of their consent , but for 
 a previous promise extract from he by his father , a promise which , 
 rigidly construe , be not extend to Lady Chillingly , but confine 
 to Sir Peter as the head of the family and master of the household . the 
 father 's consent be already give , and , if in his reverence for both 
 parent Kenelm could not dispense with his mother 's approval , surely 
 it be the part of a true friend to remove every scruple from his 
 conscience , and smooth away every obstacle to a love not to be condemn 
 because it be disintereste . 

 after this conversation , Lady Glenalvon seek Kenelm , find he 
 gloomily musing on the bank of the trout - stream , take his arm , lead he 
 into the sombre glade of the fir - grove , and listen patiently to 
 all he have to say . even then her woman 's heart be not win to his 
 reasoning , until he say pathetically , " you thank I once for save 
 your son 's life : you say then that you could never repay I ; you can 
 repay I tenfold . could your son , who be now , we trust , in heaven , look 
 down and judge between we , do you think he would approve you if you 
 refuse ? " 

 then Lady Glenalvon weep , and take his hand , kiss his forehead as 
 a mother might kiss it , and say , " you triumph ; I will go to Lady 
 Chillingly at once . marry she whom you so love , on one condition : marry 
 she from my house . " 

 Lady Glenalvon be not one of those woman who serve a friend by 
 half . she know well how to propitiate and reason down the apathetic 
 temperament of Lady Chillingly ; she do not cease till that lady herself 
 come into Kenelm 's room , and say very quietly , - 

 " so you be go to propose to Miss Mordaunt , the Warwickshire 
 Mordaunts I suppose ? Lady Glenalvon say she be a very lovely girl , 
 and will stay with she before the wedding . and as the young lady be an 
 orphan Lady Glenalvon 's uncle the Duke , who be connect with the eld 
 branch of the Mordaunts , will give she away . it will be a very brilliant 
 affair . I be sure I wish you happy ; it be time you should have sow your 
 wild oat . " 

 two day after the consent thus formally give , Kenelm quit 
 Exmundham . Sir Peter would have accompany he to pay his respect to 
 the intended , but the agitation he have go through bring on a sharp 
 twinge of the gout , which consign his foot to flannel . 

 after Kenelm have go , Lady Glenalvon go into Cecilia 's room . Cecilia 
 be seat very desolately by the open window . she have detect that 
 something of an anxious and painful nature have be weigh upon the 
 mind of father and son , and have connect it with the letter which have 
 so disturb the even mind of Sir Peter ; but she do not divine what the 
 something be , and if mortify by a certain reserve , more distant than 
 heretofore , which have characterize Kenelm 's manner towards herself , 
 the mortification be less sensibly feel than a tender sympathy for the 
 sadness she have observe on his face and yearn to soothe . his reserve 
 have , however , make her own manner more reserved than of old , for which 
 she be now rather chide herself than reproach he . 

 Lady Glenalvon put her arm round Cecilia 's neck and kiss she , 
 whispering , " that man have so disappoint I : he be so unworthy of the 
 happiness I have once hop for he ! " 

 " whom do you speak of ? " murmur Cecilia , turn very pale . 

 " Kenelm Chillingly . it seem that he have conceive a fancy for some 
 penniless girl whom he have meet in his wandering , have come here to 
 get the consent of his parent to propose to she , have obtain their 
 consent , and be go to propose . " 

 Cecilia remain silent for a moment with her eye close , then she 
 say , " he be worthy of all happiness , and he would never make an 
 unworthy choice . Heaven bless he - and - and - " she would have add , 
 " his bride , " but her lip refuse to utter the word bride . 

 " Cousin Gordon be worth ten of he , " cry Lady Glenalvon , indignantly . 

 she have serve Kenelm , but she have not forgive he . 



 CHAPTER V. 

 KENELM sleep in London that night , and , the next day , be singularly 
 fine for an english summer , he resolve to go to Moleswich on foot . he 
 have no need this time to encumber himself with a knapsack ; he have leave 
 sufficient change of dress in his lodging at Cromwell Lodge . 

 it be towards the evening when he find himself in one of the prettiest 
 rural village by which 


  " wander the hoary Thames along 
  his silver - winding way . " 


 it be not in the direct road from London to Moleswich , but it be a 
 pleasanter way for a pedestrian . and when , quit the long street of 
 the sultry village , he come to the shelve margin of the river , he be 
 glad to rest a while , enjoy the cool of the rippling water , and listen 
 to their placid murmur amid the rush in the border shallow . he 
 have ample time before he . his ramble while at Cromwell Lodge have make 
 he familiar with the district for mile round Moleswich , and he know 
 that a footpath through the field at the right would lead he , in less 
 than an hour , to the side of the tributary brook on which Cromwell Lodge 
 be place , opposite the wooden bridge which conduct to Grasmere and 
 Moleswich . 

 to one who love the romance of history , english history , the whole 
 course of the Thames be full of charm . ah ! could I go back to the day 
 in which young generation than that of Kenelm Chillingly be unborn , 
 when every wave of the Rhine speak of history and romance to I , what 
 fairy should meet on thy bank , o thou our own Father Thames ! perhaps 
 some day a german pilgrim may repay tenfold to thee the tribute render 
 by the english kinsman to the Father Rhine . 

 listen to the whisper of the reed , Kenelm Chillingly feel the 
 haunt influence of the legendary stream . many a poetic incident or 
 tradition in antique chronicle , many a votive rhyme in song , dear to 
 forefather whose very name have become a poetry to we , throng dimly 
 and confusedly back to his memory , which have little care to retain such 
 graceful trinket in the treasure - house of love . but everything that , 
 from childhood upward , connect itself with romance , revive with yet 
 fresher bloom in the memory of he who love . 

 and to this man , through the first perilous season of youth , so 
 abnormally safe from youth 's most wonted peril , - to this would - be 
 pupil of realism , this learn adept in the school of a Welby or a 
 Mivers , - to this man , love come at last as with the fatal power of 
 the fabled Cytherea ; and with that love all the realism of life become 
 ideal , all the stern line of our commonplace destiny undulate into 
 curve of beauty , all the trite sound of our every - day life attune 
 into delicacy of song . how full of sanguine yet dreamy bliss be his 
 heart - and seem his future - in the gentle breeze and the soften glow 
 of that summer eve ! he should see Lily the next morn , and his lip be 
 now free to say all that they have as yet suppress . 

 suddenly he be rouse from the half - awake , half - asleep happiness that 
 belong to the moment in which we transport ourselves into Elysium , by 
 the carol of a voice more loudly joyous than that of his own heart - 


  " singing , singing , 
  lustily sing , 
  down the road , with his dog before , 
  come the Ritter of Nierestein . " 


 Kenelm turn his head so quickly that he frighten Max , who have for 
 the last minute be stand behind he inquisitively with one paw 
 raise , and sniff , in some doubt whether he recognize an old 
 acquaintance ; but at Kenelm 's quick movement the animal break into a 
 nervous bark , and run back to his master . 

 the minstrel , little heed the figure recline on the bank , would have 
 pass on with his light tread and his cheery carol , but Kenelm rise to 
 his foot , and hold out his hand , say , " I hope you do not   share Max 's 
 alarm at meet I again ? " 

 " ah , my young philosopher , be it indeed you ? " 

 " if I be to be designate a philosopher it be certainly not i. and , 
 honestly speak , I be not the same . I , who spend that pleasant day 
 with you among the field round Luscombe two year ago - " 

 " or who advise I at Tor Hadham to string my lyre to the praise of a 
 beefsteak . I , too , be not quite the same , - I , whose dog present you 
 with the begging - tray . " 

 " yet you still go through the world singing . " 

 " even that vagrant singing time be pretty well over . but I disturb you 
 from your repose ; I would rather share it . you be probably not go my 
 way , and as I be in no hurry , I should not like to lose the opportunity 
 chance have so happily give I of renew acquaintance with one who have 
 often be present to my thought since we last meet . " thus say , the 
 minstrel stretch himself at ease on the bank , and Kenelm follow his 
 example . 

 there certainly be a change in the owner of the dog with the 
 begging - tray , a change in costume , in countenance , in that indescribable 
 self - evidence which we call " manner . " the costume be not that Bohemian 
 attire in which Kenelm have first encounter the wandering minstrel , nor 
 the studied , more graceful garb , which so well become his shapely form 
 during his visit to Luscombe . it be now neatly simple , the cool and 
 quiet summer dress any english gentleman might adopt in a long rural 
 walk . and as he uncover his head to court the cool breeze , there 
 be a graver dignity in the man 's handsome Rubens - like face , a line of 
 more concentrated thought in the spacious forehead , a thread or two of 
 gray shimmering here and there through the thick auburn curl of hair 
 and beard . and in his manner , though still very frank , there be just 
 perceptible a sort of self - assertion , not offensive , but manly ; such 
 as do not misbecome one of mature year , and of some establish 
 position , address another man much young than himself , who in 
 all probability have achieve no position at all beyond that which the 
 accident of birth might assign to he . 

 " yes , " say the minstrel , with a half - suppress sigh , " the last year 
 of my vagrant holiday have come to its close . I recollect that the first 
 day we meet by the road - side fountain , I advise you to do like I , seek 
 amusement and adventure as a foot - traveller . now , see you , evidently 
 a gentleman by education and birth , still a foot - traveller , I feel as if 
 I ought to say , ' you have have enough of such experience : vagabond life 
 have its peril as well as charm ; cease it , and settle down . ' " 

 " I think of do so , " reply Kenelm , laconically . 

 " in a profession ? - army , law , medicine ? " 

 " no . " 

 " ah , in marriage then . right ; give I your hand on that . so a petticoat 
 indeed have at last find its charm for you in the actual world as well 
 as on the canvas of a picture ? " 

 " I conclude , " say Kenelm , evade any direct notice of that playful 
 taunt , " I conclude from your remark that it be in marriage you be 
 about to settle down . " 

 " ay , could I have do so before I should have be save from many 
 error , and be many year near to the goal which dazzle my sight 
 through the haze of my boyish dream . " 

 " what be that goal , - the grave ? " 

 " the grave ! that which allow of no grave , - fame . " 

 " I see - despite of what you just now say - you still mean to go through 
 the world seek a poet 's fame . " 

 " alas ! I resign that fancy , " say the minstrel , with another half - sigh . 
 " it be not indeed wholly , but in great part the hope of the poet 's fame 
 that make I a truant in the way to that which destiny , and such few 
 gift as Nature concede to I , mark out for my proper and only goal . 
 but what a strange , delusive Will - o'-the - Wisp the love of verse - making 
 be ! how rarely a man of good sense deceive himself as to other thing 
 for which he be fit , in which he can succeed ; but let he once drink 
 into his be the charm of verse - making , how the glamour of the charm 
 bewitch his understanding ! how long it be before he can believe that 
 the world will not take his word for it , when he cry out to sun , moon , 
 and star , ' I , too , be a poet . ' and with what agony , as if at the 
 wrench of soul from life , he resign himself at last to the conviction 
 that whether he or the world be right , it come to the same thing . who 
 can plead his cause before a court that will not give he a hearing ? " 

 it be with an emotion so passionately strong , and so intensely painful , 
 that the owner of the dog with the begging - tray thus speak , that Kenelm 
 feel , through sympathy , as if he himself be tear asunder by the wrench 
 of life from soul . but then Kenelm be a mortal so eccentric that , if 
 a single acute suffering endure by a fellow mortal could be bring 
 before the evidence of his sens , I doubt whether he would not have 
 suffer as much as that fellow - mortal . so that , though if there be a 
 thing in the world which Kenelm Chillingly would care not to do , it be 
 verse - making , his mind involuntarily hasten to the argument by which 
 he could well mitigate the pang of the verse - maker . 

 Quoth he : " accord to my very scanty reading , you share the love 
 of verse - making with man the most illustrious in career which have 
 achieve the goal of fame . it must , then , be a very noble love : 
 Augustus , Pollio , Varius , Maecenas , - the great statesman of their 
 day , - they be verse - maker . Cardinal Richelieu be a verse - maker ; 
 Walter Raleigh and Philip Sidney , Fox , Burke , Sheridan , Warren 
 Hastings , canning , even the grave William Pitt , - all be verse - maker . 
 verse - making do not retard - no doubt the quality essential to 
 verse - making accelerate - their race to the goal of fame . what great 
 painter have be verse - maker ! Michael Angelo , Leonardo da Vinci , 
 Salvator Rosa " - and Heaven know how may other great name Kenelm 
 Chillingly might have proceed to add to his list , if the minstrel have 
 not here interpose . 

 " what ! all those mighty painter be verse - maker ? " 

 " verse - maker so good , especially Michael Angelo , - the great painter 
 of all , - that they would have have the fame of poet , if , unfortunately 
 for that goal of fame , their glory in the sister art of painting do not 
 outshine it . but when you give to your gift of song the modest title of 
 verse - making , permit I to observe that your gift be perfectly distinct 
 from that of the verse - maker . your gift , whatever it may be , could not 
 exist without some sympathy with the non verse - make human heart . 
 no doubt in your foot travel , you have acquire not only observant 
 intimacy with external Nature in the shift hue at each hour of a 
 distant mountain , in the lengthening shadow which yon sunset cast on 
 the water at our foot , in the habit of the thrush drop fearlessly 
 close beside I , in that turf moisten by its neighbourhood to those 
 dripping rush , all of which I could describe no less accurately than 
 you , - as a Peter Bell might describe they no less accurately than a 
 William Wordsworth . but in such song of your as you have permit I 
 to hear , you seem to have escape out of that elementary accidence 
 of the poet 's art , and to touch , no matter how slightly , on the only 
 lasting interest which the universal heart of man can have in the song 
 of the poet ; namely , in the sound which the poet 's individual sympathy 
 draw forth from the latent chord in that universal heart . as for what 
 you call ' the world , ' what be it more than the fashion of the present 
 day ? how far the judgment of that be worth a poet 's pain I can not   pretend 
 to say . but of one thing I be sure , that while I could as easily square 
 the circle as compose a simple couplet address to the heart of a 
 simple audience with sufficient felicity to decoy their praise 
 into Max 's begging - tray , I could spin out by the yard the sort of 
 verse - making which characterize the fashion of the present day . " 

 much flatter , and not a little amuse , the wandering minstrel turn 
 his bright countenance , no long dim by a cloud , towards that of his 
 lazily recline consoler , and answer gayly , - 

 " you say that you could spin out by the yard verse in the fashion of 
 the present day . I wish you would give I a speciman of your skill in 
 that handiwork . " 

 " very well ; on one condition , that you will repay my trouble by 
 a speciman of your own verse , not in the fashion of the present 
 day , - something which I can construe . I defy you to construe mine . " 

 " agree . " 

 " well , then , let we take it for grant that this be the augustan age of 
 english poetry , and that the english language be dead , like the Latin . 
 suppose I be write for a prize - medal in English , as I write at 
 college for a prize - medal in Latin : of course , I shall be successful in 
 proportion as I introduce the verbal elegance peculiar to our Augustan 
 age , and also catch the prevail poetic characteristic of that 
 classical epoch . 

 " now I think that every observant critic will admit that the striking 
 distinction of the poetry most in the fashion of the present day , 
 namely , of the Augustan age , be , - first , a selection of such verbal 
 elegance as would have be most repulsive to the barbaric taste of the 
 precede century ; and , secondly , a very lofty disdain of all prosaic 
 condescension to common - sense , and an elaborate cultivation of that 
 element of the sublime which Mr. Burke define under the head of 
 obscurity . 

 " these premise concede , I will only ask you to choose the metre . blank 
 verse be very much in fashion just now . " 

 " Pooh ! blank verse indeed ! I be not go so to free your experiment 
 from the difficulty of rhyme . " 

 " it be all one to I , " say Kenelm , yawning ; " rhyme be it : heroic or 
 lyrical ? " 

 " heroic be old - fashioned ; but the Chaucer couplet , as bring to 
 perfection by our modern poet , I think the well adapt to dainty 
 leaf and uncrackable nut . I accept the modern Chaucerian . the 
 subject ? " 

 " oh , never trouble yourself about that . by whatever title your Augustan 
 verse - maker label his poem , his genius , like Pindar 's , disdain to be 
 cramp by the subject . listen , and do not   suffer Max to howl , if he can 
 help it . here go . " 

 and in an affected but emphatic sing - song Kenelm begin : - 


  " in Attica the gentle Pythias dwelt . 
  youthful he be , and pass rich : he feel 
  as if nor youth nor rich could suffice 
  for bliss . dark - eyed Sophronia be a nice 
  girl : and one summer day , when Neptune drive 
  his sea - car slowly , and the olive grove 
  that skirt Ilissus , to thy shell , Harmonia , 
  ripple , he say ' I love thee ' to Sophronia . 
  Crocus and iris , when they hear he , wag 
  their pretty head in glee : the honey - bag 
  Bees become altar : and the forest dive 
  her plumage smooth . such be the charm of love . 
  of this sweet story do ye long for more ? 
  wait till I publish it in volume four ; 
  which certain critic , my good friend , will cry 
  up beyond Chaucer . take their word for ' t. I 
  say ' trust they , but not read , - or you will not buy . ' "
