 " you have certainly keep your word , " say the minstrel , laugh ; " and 
 if this be the augustan age , and the English be a dead language , you 
 deserve to win the prize - medal . " 

 " you flatter I , " say Kenelm , modestly . " but if I , who never before 
 string two rhyme together , can improvise so readily in the style of the 
 present day , why should not a practical rhymester like yourself dash off 
 at a sit a volume or so in the same style ; disguise completely the 
 verbal elegance borrow , add to the delicacy of the rhyme by the 
 frequent introduction of a line that will not scan , and tower yet 
 more into the sublime by become yet more unintelligible ? do that , and 
 I promise you the most glowing panegyric in ' the Londoner , ' for I will 
 write it myself . " 

 " ' the Londoner ' ! " exclaim the minstrel , with an angry flush on his 
 cheek and brow , " my bitter , relentless enemy . " 

 " I fear , then , you have as little study the critical press of the 
 Augustan age as you have imbue your muse with the classical spirit of 
 its verse . for the art of write a man must cultivate himself . the art 
 of be review consist in cultivate the acquaintance of reviewer . 
 in the Augustan age criticism be cliquism . belong to a clique and you 
 be Horace or Tibullus . Belong to no clique and , of course , you be 
 Bavius or Maevius . ' the Londoner ' be the enemy of no man : it hold all 
 man in equal contempt . but as , in order to amuse , it must abuse , it 
 compensate the praise it be compel to bestow upon the member of its 
 clique by heap additional scorn upon all who be cliqueless . hit he 
 hard : he have no friend . " 

 " ah , " say the minstrel , " I believe that there be much truth in what you 
 say . I never have a friend among the clique . and Heaven know with what 
 pertinacity those from whom I , in utter ignorance of the rule which 
 govern so - call organ of opinion , have hop , in my time of struggle , 
 for a little sympathy , a kindly encouragement , have combine to crush 
 I down . they succeed long . but at last I venture to hope that I 
 be beat they . happily , Nature endow I with a sanguine , joyous , 
 elastic temperament . he who never despair seldom completely fail . " 

 this speech rather perplexed Kenelm , for have not the minstrel declare 
 that his singing day be over , that he have decide on the renunciation 
 of verse - making ? what other path to fame , from which the critic have 
 not be able to exclude his step , be he , then , now pursue , - he whom 
 Kenelm have assume to belong to some commercial moneymaking firm ? no 
 doubt some less difficult prose - track , probably a novel . everybody 
 write novel nowadays , and as the public will read novel without be 
 tell to do so , and will not read poetry unless they be tell that they 
 ought , possibly novel be not quite so much at the mercy of clique as 
 be the poem of our Augustan age . 

 however , Kenelm do not think of seek for further confidence on that 
 score . his mind at that moment , not unnaturally , wander from book and 
 critic to love and wedlock . 

 " our talk , " say he , " have digress into fretful course ; permit I 
 to return to the starting - point . you be go to settle down into the 
 peace of home . a peaceful home be like a good conscience . the rain 
 without do not pierce its roof , the wind without do not shake its 
 wall . if not an impertinent question , be it long since you have know 
 your intended bride ? " 

 " yes , very long . " 

 " and always love she ? " 

 " always , from her infancy . out of all womankind , she be design to be 
 my life 's playmate and my soul 's purifier . I know not what might have 
 become of I , if the thought of she have not walk beside I as my 
 guardian angel . for , like many vagrant from the beat high road of 
 the world , there be in my nature something of that lawlessness which 
 belong to high animal spirit , to the zest of adventure , and the warm 
 blood that run into song , chiefly because song be the voice of a joy . 
 and no doubt , when I look back on the past year I must own that I have 
 too often be lead astray from the object set before my reason , and 
 cherish at my heart , by err impulse or wanton fancy . " 

 " Petticoat interest , I presume , " interpose Kenelm , dryly . 

 " I wish I could honestly answer ' no , ' " say the minstrel , colour 
 high . " but from the bad , from all that would have permanently blast 
 the career to which I intrust my fortune , all that would have render 
 I unworthy of the pure love that now , I trust , await and crown 
 my dream of happiness , I have be save by the haunt smile in a 
 sinless infantine face . only once be I in great peril , - that hour of 
 peril I recall with a shudder . it be at Luscombe . " 

 " at luscombe ! " 

 " in the temptation of a terrible crime I think I hear a voice say , 
 ' Mischief ! remember the little child . ' in that supervention which be so 
 readily accept as a divine warning , when the imagination be morbidly 
 excited , and when the conscience , though lull asleep for a moment , be 
 still asleep so lightly that the sigh of a breeze , the fall of a leaf , 
 can awake it with a start of terror , I take the voice for that of my 
 guardian angel . think it over later , and couple the voice with the 
 moral of those weird line you repeat to I so appositely the next 
 day , I conclude that I be not mistaken when I say it be from your lip 
 that the voice which preserve I come . " 

 " I confess the impertinence : you pardon it ? " 

 the minstrel seize Kenelm 's hand and press it earnestly . 

 " pardon it ! oh , could you but guess what cause I have to be grateful , 
 everlastingly grateful ! that sudden cry , the remorse and horror of my 
 own self that it strike into I , - deepen by those rugged line which 
 the next day make I shrink in dismay from ' the face of my darling 
 sin ' ! then come the turning - point of my life . from that day , the lawless 
 vagabond within I be kill . I mean not , indeed , the love of Nature 
 and of song which have first allure the vagabond , but the hatred of 
 steadfast habit and of serious work , - that be kill . I no long 
 trifle with my calling : I take to it as a serious duty . and when I see 
 she , whom fate have reserve and rear for my bride , her face be no 
 long in my eye that of the playful child ; the soul of the woman be 
 dawn into it . it be but two year since that day , to I so eventful . 
 yet my fortune be now secure . and if fame be not establish , I be at 
 last in a position which warrant my saying to she I love , ' the time have 
 come when , without fear for thy future , I can ask thee to be mine . ' " 

 the man speak with so fervent a passion that Kenelm silently leave he 
 to recover his wonte self - possession , - not unwilling to be silent , - not 
 unwilling , in the softness of the hour , pass from roseate sunset into 
 starry twilight , to murmur to himself , " and the time , too , have come for 
 I ! " 

 after a few moment the minstrel resume lightly and cheerily , - 

 " Sir , your turn : pray have you long know - judging by our former 
 conversation you can not have long love - the lady whom you have woo 
 and win ? " 

 as Kenelm have neither as yet woo nor win the lady in question , and do 
 not deem it necessary to enter into any detail on the subject of love 
 particular to himself , he reply by a general observation , - 

 " it seem to I that the come of love be like the coming of spring : 
 the date be not to be reckon by the calendar . it may be slow and 
 gradual ; it may be quick and sudden . but in the morning , when we wake 
 and recognize a change in the world without , verdure on the tree , 
 blossom on the sward , warmth in the sunshine , music in the air , then we 
 say Spring have come ! " 

 " I like your illustration . and if it be an idle question to ask a lover 
 how long he have know the beloved one , so it be almost as idle to ask if 
 she be not beautiful . he can not but see in her face the beauty she have 
 give to the world without . " 

 " true ; and that think be poetic enough to make I remind you that I 
 favour you with the maiden speciman of my verse - making on condition 
 that you repay I by a speciman of your own practical skill in the art . 
 and I claim the right to suggest the theme . let it be - " 

 " of a beefsteak ? " 

 " tush , you have wear out that tasteless joke at my expense . the theme 
 must be of love , and if you could improvise a stanza or two expressive 
 of the idea you just utter I shall listen with yet more pleased 
 attention . " 

 " alas ! I be no improvisatore . yet I will avenge myself on your former 
 neglect of my craft by chant to you a trifle somewhat in unison with 
 the thought you ask I to versify , but which you would not stay to hear 
 at Tor Hadham   though you do drop a shilling into Max 's tray ; it be 
 one of the song I sing that evening , and it be not ill - receive by my 
 humble audience . 


  " the BEAUTY of the mistress be in the LOVER 's EYE . 

  " be she not pretty , my Mabel May ? 
  nobody ever yet call she so . 
  be not her lineament faultless , say ? 
  if I must answer you plainly , no . 

  " Joy to believe that the maid I love 
  none but myself as she be can see ; 
  Joy that she steal from her heaven above , 
  and be only reveal on this earth to I ! " 


 as soon as he have finish this very artless ditty , the minstrel rise 
 and say , - 

 " now I must bid you good - by . my way lie through those meadow , and 
 yours no doubt along the high road . " 

 " not so . permit I to accompany you . I have a lodging not far from 
 hence , to which the path through the field be the short way . " 

 the minstrel turn a somewhat surprised and somewhat inquisitive look 
 towards Kenelm . but feeling , perhaps , that have withhold from his 
 fellow - traveller all confidence as to his own name and attribute , he 
 have no right to ask any confidence from that gentleman not voluntarily 
 make to he , he courteously say " that he wish the way be long , 
 since it would be so pleasantly halve , " and stride forth at a brisk 
 pace . 

 the twilight be now close into the brightness of a starry summer 
 night , and the solitude of the field be unbroken . both these man , 
 walk side by side , feel supremely happy . but happiness be like wine ; 
 its effect differ with the differ temperament on which it 
 act . in this case garrulous and somewhat vaunting with the one man , 
 warm - colour , sensuous , impressionable to the influence of external 
 Nature , as an Aeolian harp to the rise or fall of a pass wind ; and , 
 with the other man , taciturn and somewhat modestly express , saturnine , 
 meditative , not indeed dull to the influence of external Nature , but 
 deem they of no value , save where they pass out of the domain of 
 the sensuous into that of the intellectual , and the soul of man dictate 
 to the soulless Nature its own question and its own reply . 

 the minstrel take the talk on himself , and the talk charm his 
 listener . it become so really eloquent in the tone of its utterance , in 
 the frank play of its delivery , that I could no more adequately describe 
 it than a reporter , however faithful to every word a true orator may 
 say , can describe that which , apart from all word , belong to the 
 presence of the orator himself . 

 not , then , venture to report the language of this singular itinerant , 
 I content myself with say that the substance of it be of the 
 nature on which it be say most man can be eloquent : it be personal 
 to himself . he speak of aspiration towards the achievement of a name , 
 date back to the dawn of memory ; of early obstacle in lowly birth , 
 stint fortune ; of a sudden opening to his ambition while yet in 
 boyhood , through the generous favour of a rich man , who say , " the child 
 have genius : I will give it the discipline of culture ; one day it shall 
 repay to the world what it owe to I ; " of study passionately begin , 
 earnestly pursue , and mournfully suspend in early youth . he do not 
 say how or wherefore : he rush on to dwell upon the struggle for a 
 livelihood for himself and those dependent on he ; how in such struggle 
 he be compel to divert toil and energy from the systematic pursuit 
 of the object he have once set before he ; the necessity for money 
 be too urgent to be postpone to the vision of fame . " but even , " he 
 exclaim , passionately , " even in such hasty and crude manifestation 
 of what be within I , as circumstance limit my power , I know that 
 I ought to have find from those who profess to be authoritative judge 
 the encouragement of praise . how much well , then , I should have do 
 if I have find it ! how a little praise warm out of a man the good 
 that be in he , and the sneer of a contempt which he feel to be unjust 
 chill the ardour to excel ! however , I force my way , so far as be then 
 most essential to I , the sufficing breadmaker for those I love ; and in 
 my holiday of song and ramble I find a delight that atone for all the 
 rest . but still the desire of fame , once conceive in childhood , once 
 nourish through youth , never die but in our grave . foot and hoof may 
 tread it down , bud , leaf , stalk ; its root be too deep below the surface 
 for they to reach , and year after year stalk and leaf and bud re - emerge . 
 Love may depart from our mortal life : we console ourselves ; the beloved 
 will be reunite to we in the life to come . but if he who set his heart 
 on fame lose it in this life , what can console he ? " 

 " do you not say a little while ago that fame allow of no grave ? " 

 " true ; but if we do not achieve it before we ourselves be in the grave , 
 what comfort can it give to we ? love ascend to heaven , to which we hope 
 ourselves to ascend ; but fame remain on the earth , which we shall never 
 again revisit . and it be because fame be earth - bear that the desire for 
 it be the most lasting , the regret for the want of it the most bitter , 
 to the child of earth . but I shall achieve it now ; it be already in my 
 grasp . " 

 by this time the traveller have arrive at the brook , face the wooden 
 bridge beside Cromwell Lodge . 

 here the minstrel halt ; and Kenelm with a certain tremble in his 
 voice , say , " be it not time that we should make ourselves know to 
 each other by name ? I have no long any cause to conceal mine , indeed I 
 never have any cause strong than whim , - Kenelm Chillingly , the only son 
 of Sir Peter , of Exmundham , - - -shire . " 

 " I wish your father joy of so clever a son , " say the minstrel with his 
 wonte urbanity . " you already know enough of I to be aware that I be 
 of much humbler birth and station than you ; but if you chance to have 
 visit the exhibition of the Royal Academy this year - ah ! I understand 
 that start - you might have recognize a picture of which you have see 
 the rudimentary sketch , ' the Girl with the Flower - ball , ' one of three 
 picture very severely handle by ' the Londoner , ' but , in spite of 
 that potent enemy , insure fortune and promise fame to the wandering 
 minstrel , whose name , if the sight of the picture have induce you to 
 inquire into that , you would have find to be Walter Melville . next 
 January I hope , thank to that picture , to add , ' associate of the Royal 
 Academy . ' the public will not let they keep I out of it , in spite of 
 ' the Londoner . ' you be probably an expected guest at one of the more 
 impose villa from which we see the distant light . I be go to a 
 very humble cottage , in which henceforth I hope to find my establish 
 home . I be there now only for a few day , but pray let I welcome you 
 there before I leave . the cottage be call Grasmere . " 



 CHAPTER VI . 

 the minstrel give a cordial parting shake of the hand to the 
 fellow - traveller whom he have advise to settle down , not notice how 
 very cold have become the hand in his own genial grasp . lightly he pass 
 over the wooden bridge , precede by Max , and merrily , when he have gain 
 the other side of the bridge , come upon Kenelm 's ear , through the hush 
 of the luminous night , the verse of the uncompleted love - song , - 


  " singing , singing , 
  lustily sing , 
  down the road , with his dog before , 
  come the Ritter of Nierestein . " 


 love - song , uncompleted ; why uncompleted ? it be not give to Kenelm to 
 divine the why . it be a love - song versify one of the prettiest fairy 
 tale in the world , which be a great favourite with Lily , and which 
 Lion have promise Lily to versify , but only to complete it in her 
 presence and to her perfect satisfaction . 



 CHAPTER VII . 

 if I could not venture to place upon paper the exact word of an 
 eloquent coveter of fame , the earth - bear , still less can I dare to place 
 upon paper all that pass through the voiceless heart of a coveter of 
 love , the heaven - bear . 

 from the hour in which Kenelm Chillingly have part from Walter Melville 
 until somewhere between sunrise and noon the next day , the summer 
 joyousness of that external nature which do now and then , though , for 
 the most part , deceitfully , address to the soul of man question and 
 answer all her soulless own , laugh away the gloom of his misgiving . 

 no doubt this Walter Melville be the beloved guardian of Lily ; no doubt 
 it be Lily whom he designate as reserve and rear to become his 
 bride . but on that question Lily herself have the sovereign voice . it 
 remain yet to be see whether Kenelm have deceive himself in the 
 belief that have make the world so beautiful to he since the hour of 
 their last parting . at all event it be due to she , due even to his 
 rival , to assert his own claim to her choice . and the more he recall 
 all that Lily have ever say to he of her guardian , so openly , so 
 frankly , proclaim affection , admiration , gratitude , the more 
 convincingly his reasoning allay his fear , whispering , " so might 
 a child speak of a parent : not so do the maiden speak of the man she 
 love ; she can scarcely trust herself to praise . " 

 in fine , it be not in despondent mood , nor with deject look , that , 
 a little before noon , Kenelm cross the bridge and re - enter the 
 enchant land of Grasmere . in answer to his inquiry , the servant who 
 open the door say that neither Mr. Melville nor Miss Mordaunt be 
 at home ; they have but just go out together for a walk . he be about to 
 turn back , when Mrs. Cameron come into the hall , and , rather by 
 gesture than word , invite he to enter . Kenelm follow she into the 
 drawing - room , take his seat beside she . he be about to speak , when 
 she interrupt he in a tone of voice so unlike its usual languor , so 
 keen , so sharp , that it sound like a cry of distress . 

 " I be just about to come to you . happily , however , you find I alone , 
 and what may pass between we will be soon over . but first tell I : you 
 have see your parent ; you have ask their consent to we d a girl such 
 as I describe ; tell I , oh tell I that that consent be refuse ! " 

 " on the contrary , I be here with their full permission to ask the hand 
 of your niece . " 

 Mrs. Cameron sink back in her chair , rock herself to and fro in the 
 posture of a person in great pain . 

 " I fear that . Walter say he have meet you last evening ; that you , like 
 himself , entertain the thought of marriage . you , of course when you 
 learn his name , must have know with whom his thought be connect . 
 happily , he could not divine what be the choice to which your youthful 
 fancy have be so blindly lead . " 

 " my dear Mrs. Cameron , " say Kenelm , very mildly , but very firmly , " you 
 be aware of the purpose for which I leave Moleswich a few day ago , 
 and it seem to I that you might have forestall my intention , the 
 intention which bring I ; thus early to your house . I come to say to 
 Miss Mordaunt 's guardian , ' I ask the hand of your ward . if you also woo 
 she , I have a very noble rival . with both of we no consideration for our 
 own happiness can be comparable to the duty of consult her . let she 
 choose between the two . ' " 

 " impossible ! " exclaim Mrs. Cameron ; " impossible . you know not what you 
 say ; know not , guess not , how sacred be the claim of Walter Melville 
 to all that the orphan whom he have protect from her very birth can 
 give he in return . she have no right to a preference for another : her 
 heart be too grateful to admit of one . if the choice be give to she 
 between he and you , it be he whom she would choose . solemnly I assure 
 you of this . do not , then , subject she to the pain of such a choice . 
 suppose , if you will , that you have attract her fancy , and that now you 
 proclaim your love and urge your suit , she would not , must not , the 
 less reject your hand , but you might cloud her happiness in accept 
 Melville 's . be generous . conquer your own fancy ; it can be but a pass 
 one . speak not to she , nor to Mr. Melville , of a wish which can never be 
 realize . go hence , silently , and at once . " 

 the word and the manner of the pale implore woman strike a vague 
 awe into the heart of her listener . but he do not the less resolutely 
 answer , " I can not obey you . it seem to I that my honour command I 
 to prove to your niece that , if I mistook the nature of her feeling 
 towards I , I do not , by word or look , lead she to believe mine towards 
 herself be less in earnest than they be ; and it seem scarcely less 
 honourable towards my worthy rival to endanger his own future happiness , 
 should he discover later that his bride would have be happy with 
 another . why be so mysteriously apprehensive ? if , as you say , with such 
 apparent conviction , there be no doubt of your niece 's preference for 
 another , at a word from her own lip I depart , and you will see I no 
 more . but that word must be say by she ; and if you will not permit I 
 to ask for it in your own house , I will take my chance of find she 
 now , on her walk with Mr. Melville ; and , could he deny I the right to 
 speak to she alone , that which I would say can be say in his presence . 
 Ah ! madam , have you no mercy for the heart that you so needlessly 
 torture ? if I must bear the bad , let I learn it , and at once . " 

 " learn it , then , from my lip , " say Mrs. Cameron , speak with voice 
 unnaturally calm , and feature rigidly set into stern composure . " and I 
 place the secret you wring from I under the seal of that honour which 
 you so vauntingly make your excuse for imperille the peace of the home 
 I ought never to have suffer you to enter . an honest couple , of 
 humble station and narrow mean , have an only son , who evince in early 
 childhood talent so remarkable that they attract the notice of 
 the father 's employer , a rich man of very benevolent heart and very 
 cultivate taste . he send the child , at his expense , to a first - rate 
 commercial school , mean to provide for he later in his own firm . 
 the rich man be the head partner of an eminent bank ; but very infirm 
 health , and taste much estranged from business , have induce he to 
 retire from all active share in the firm , the management of which be 
 confine to a son whom he idolize . but the talent of the protege he 
 have send to school take there so passionate a direction towards art 
 and estrange from trade , and his design in draw when show to 
 connoisseur be deem so promising of future excellence , that the 
 patron change his original intention , enter he as a pupil in the 
 studio of a distinguished french painter , and afterwards bid he 
 perfect his taste by the study of italian and flemish masterpiece . 

 " he be still abroad , when - " here Mrs. Cameron stop , with visible 
 effort , suppress a sob , and go on , whisperingly , through tooth 
 clench together - " when a thunderbolt fall on the house of the patron , 
 shatter his fortune , blast his name . the son , unknown to the 
 father , have be decoy into speculation which prove unfortunate : the 
 loss might have be easily retrieve in the first instance ; unhappily 
 he take the wrong course to retrieve it , and launch into new hazard . 
 I must be brief . one day the world be startle by the news that a firm , 
 fame for its suppose wealth and solidity , be bankrupt . Dishonesty 
 be allege , be prove , not against the father , - he go forth from 
 the trial , censure indeed for neglect , not condemn for fraud , but a 
 penniless pauper . the - son , the son , the idolize son , be remove from 
 the prisoner 's dock , a convict felon , sentence to penal servitude ; 
 escape that sentence by - by - you guess - you guess . how could he escape 
 except through death ? - death by his own guilty deed ? " 

 almost as much overpower by emotion as Mrs. Cameron herself , Kenelm 
 cover his bended face with one hand , stretch out the other blindly 
 to clasp her own , but she would not take it . 

 a dreary foreboding . again before his eye rise the old gray 
 tower , - again in his ear thrill the tragic tale of the Fletwodes . 
 what be yet leave untold hold the young man in spell - bind silence . Mrs. 
 Cameron resume , - 

 " I say the father be a penniless pauper ; he die lingeringly 
 bedridden . but one faithful friend do not desert that bed , - the youth 
 to whose genius his wealth have minister . he have come from abroad 
 with some modest saving from the sale of copy or sketch make in 
 Florence . these saving keep a roof over the head of the old man and 
 the two helpless , break - hearted woman , - pauper like himself , - his own 
 daughter and his son 's widow . when the saving be go , the young man 
 stoop from his destine calling , find employment somehow , no matter 
 how alien to his taste , and these three whom his toil support never 
 want a home or food . well , a few week after her husband 's terrible 
 death , his young widow   they have not be a year married   give birth to 
 a child , - a girl . she do not survive the exhaustion of her confinement 
 many day . the shock of her death snap the feeble thread of the poor 
 father 's life . both be bear to the grave on the same day . before they 
 die , both make the same prayer to their sole two mourner , the felon 's 
 sister , the old man 's young benefactor . the prayer be this , that the 
 new - bear infant should be rear , however humbly , in ignorance of her 
 birth , of a father 's guilt and shame . she be not to pass a suppliant 
 for charity to rich and high - bear kinsfolk , who have vouchsafe no word 
 even of pity to the felon 's guiltless father and as guiltless wife . that 
 promise have be keep till now . I be that daughter . the name I bear , 
 and the name which I give to my niece , be not ours , save as we may 
 indirectly claim they through alliance century ago . I have never 
 marry . I be to have be a bride , bring to the representative of 
 no ignoble house what be to have be a princely dower ; the wedding day 
 be fix , when the bolt fall . I have never again see my betrothed . he 
 go abroad and die there . I think he love I ; he know I love he . 
 who can blame he for desert I ? who could marry the felon 's sister ? 
 who would marry the felon 's child ? who but one ? the man who know 
 her secret , and will guard it ; the man who , care little for other 
 education , have help to instil into her spotless childhood so steadfast 
 a love of truth , so exquisite a pride of honour , that do she know such 
 ignominy rest on her birth she would pine herself away . " 

 " be there only one man on earth , " cry Kenelm , suddenly , rear his 
 face , - till then conceal and downcast , - and with a loftiness of pride 
 on its aspect , new to its wonte mildness , " be there only one man who 
 would deem the virgin at whose foot he desire to kneel and say , ' deign 
 to be the queen of my life , ' not far too noble in herself to be debase 
 by the sin of other before she be even bear ; be there only one man 
 who do not think that the love of truth and the pride of honour be 
 most royal attribute of woman or of man , no matter whether the father 
 of the woman or the man be pirate as lawless as the father of 
 Norman king , or liar as unscrupulous , where their own interest 
 be concern , as have be the crown representative of line as 
 deservedly famous as Caesars and Bourbons , Tudors and stuart ? nobility , 
 like genius , be inborn . one man alone guard her secret ! - guard a 
 secret that if make known could trouble a heart that recoil from shame ! 
 ah , madam , we Chillinglys be a very obscure , undistinguished race , but 
 for more than a thousand year we have be english gentleman . guard her 
 secret rather than risk the chance of discovery that could give she a 
 pang ! I would pass my whole life by her side in Kamtchatka , and even 
 there I would not snatch a glimpse of the secret itself with mine own 
 eye : it should be so closely muffle and wrap round by the fold of 
 reverence and worship . " 

 this burst of passion seem to Mrs. Cameron the senseless declamation 
 of an inexperienced , hot - headed young man ; and put it aside , much 
 as a great lawyer dismisse as balderdash the florid rhetoric of some 
 junior counsel , rhetoric in which the great lawyer have once indulge , 
 or as a woman for whom romance be over dismisse as idle verbiage some 
 romantic sentiment that befool her young daughter , Mrs. Cameron simply 
 reply , " all this be hollow talk , Mr. Chillingly ; let we come to the 
 point . after all I have say , do you mean to persist in your suit to my 
 niece ? " 

 " I persist . " 

 " what ! " she cry , this time indignantly , and with generous indignation ; 
 " what , even be it possible that you could win your parent ' consent to 
 marry the child of a man condemn to penal servitude , or , consistently 
 with the duty a son owe to parent , conceal that fact from they , 
 could you , bear to a station on which every gossip will ask , ' who and 
 what be the name of the future Lady Chillingly ? ' believe that the 
 who and the what will never be discover ! have you , a mere stranger , 
 unknown to we a few week ago , a right to say to Walter Melville , 
 ' resign to I that which be your sole reward for the sublime sacrifice , 
 for the loyal devotion , for the watchful tenderness of patient year ' ? " 

 " surely , madam , " cry Kenelm , more startled , more shake in soul by 
 this appeal , than by the previous revelation , ' surely , when we 
 last part , when I confide to you my love for your niece , when you 
 consent to my proposal to return home and obtain my father 's approval 
 of my suit , - surely then be the time to say , ' no ; a suitor with claim 
 paramount and irresistible have come before you . ' " 

 " I do not then know , Heaven be my witness , I do not then even suspect , 
 that Walter Melville ever dream of seek a wife in the child who have 
 grow up under his eye . you must own , indeed , how much I discourage 
 your suit ; I could not discourage it more without reveal the secret 
 of her birth , only to be reveal as an extreme necessity . but my 
 persuasion be that your father would not consent to your alliance with 
 one so far beneath the expectation he be entitle to form , and the 
 refusal of that consent would terminate all further acquaintance between 
 you and Lily , leave her secret undisclosed . it be not till you have 
 leave , only indeed two day ago , that I receive a letter from Walter 
 Melville , - a letter which tell I what I have never before conjecture . 
 here be the letter , read it , and then say if you have the heart to 
 force yourself into rivalry , with - with - " she break off , choke by she 
 exertion , thrust the letter into his hand , and with keen , eager , hungry 
 stare watch his countenance while he read . 



  - - - STREET , BLOOMSBURY . 

 MY DEAR FRIEND , - Joy and triumph ! my picture be complete , the picture 
 on which for so many month I have work night and day in this den of 
 a studio , without a glimpse of the green field , conceal my address 
 from every one , even from you , lest I might be tempt to suspend my 
 labour . the picture be complete : it be sell ; guess the price ! fifteen 
 hundred guineas , and to a dealer , - a dealer ! think of that ! it be to be 
 carry about the country exhibit by itself . you remember those three 
 little landscape of mine which two year ago I would gladly have sell 
 for ten pound , only neither Lily nor you would let I . my good friend 
 and early patron , the german merchant at Luscombe , who call on 
 I yesterday , offer to cover they with guineas thrice pile over the 
 canvas . imagine how happy I feel when I force he to accept they as a 
 present . what a leap in a man 's life it be when he can afford to say , " I 
 give ! " now then , at last , at last I be in a position which justify the 
 utterance of the hope which have for eighteen year be my solace , my 
 support ; be the sunbeam that ever shine through the gloom when my fate 
 be at the dark ; be the melody that buoy I aloft as in the 
 song of the skylark , when in the voice of man I hear but the laugh of 
 scorn . do you remember the night on which Lily 's mother besought we 
 to bring up her child in ignorance of her parentage , not even to 
 communicate to unkind and disdainful relative that such a child be 
 bear ? do you remember how plaintively , and yet how proudly , she , so 
 nobly bear , so luxuriously nurture , clasp my hand when I venture 
 to remonstrate , and say that her own family could not condemn her child 
 because of the father 's guilt , - she , the proud woman I ever know , she 
 whose smile I can at rare moment detect in Lily , raise her head from 
 her pillow , and gasp forth , - 

 " I be die : the last word of the dying be command . I command you to 
 see that my child 's lot be not that of a felon 's daughter transport to 
 the hearth of noble . to be happy , her lot must be humble : no roof too 
 humble to shelter , no husband too humble to we d , the felon 's daughter . " 

 from that hour I form a resolve that I would keep hand and heart 
 free , that when the grandchild of my princely benefactor grow up into 
 womanhood I might say to she , " I be humbly bear , but thy mother would 
 have give thee to I . " the newborn , consign to our charge , have now 
 ripen into woman , and I have now so assure my fortune that it be 
 no long poverty and struggle that I should ask she to share . I be 
 conscious that , be her fate not so exceptional , this hope of mine 
 would be a vain presumption , - conscious that I be but the creature 
 of her grandsire 's bounty , and that from it spring all I ever can 
 be , - conscious of the disparity in years,-conscious of many a past error 
 and present fault . but , as fate so ordain , such consideration be 
 trivial ; I be her rightful choice . what other choice , compatible with 
 these necessity which weigh , dear and honoured friend , immeasurably 
 more on your sense of honour than they do upon mine ? and yet mine be 
 not dull . grant , then , that you , her near and most responsible 
 relative , do not contemn I for presumption , all else seem to I clear . 
 Lily 's childlike affection for I be too deep and too fond not to 
 warm into a wife 's love . happily , too , she have not be rear in the 
 stereotype boarding - school shallowness of knowledge and vulgarity of 
 gentility ; but educate , like myself , by the free influence of Nature , 
 longing for no hall and palace save those that we build as we list , in 
 fairyland ; educate to comprehend and share the fancy which be 
 more than booklore to the worshipper of art and song . in a day or two , 
 perhaps the day after you receive this , I shall be able to escape from 
 London , and most likely shall come on foot as usual . how I long to 
 see once more the woodbine on the hedgerow , the green blade of the 
 cornfield , the sunny lapse of the river , and dearer still the tiny 
 fall of our own little noisy rill ! meanwhile I entreat you , dearest , 
 gentlest , most honored of such few friend as my life have hitherto win 
 to itself , to consider well the direct purport of this letter . if you , 
 bear in a grade so much high than mine , feel that it be unwarrantable 
 insolence in I to aspire to the hand of my patron 's grandchild , say so 
 plainly ; and I remain not less grateful for your friendship than I be 
 to your goodness when din for the first time at your father 's palace . 
 Shy and sensitive and young , I feel that his grand guest wonder why I 
 be invite to the same board as themselves . you , then court , admire , 
 you have sympathetic compassion on the raw , sullen boy ; leave those , who 
 then seem to I like the god and goddess of a heathen Pantheon , to 
 come and sit beside your father 's protege and cheeringly whisper to 
 he such word as make a low - bear ambitious lad go home light - hearted , 
 say to himself , " some day or other . " and what it be to an ambitious 
 lad , fancy himself lift by the god and goddess of a Pantheon , to 
 go home light - hearted muttering to himself , " some day or other , " I doubt 
 if even you can divine . 

 but should you be as kind to the presumptuous man as you be to the 
 bashful boy , and say , " realize be the dream , fulfil be the object of 
 your life ! take from I as her next of kin , the last descendant of your 
 benefactor , " then I venture to address to you this request . you be in 
 the place of mother to your sister 's child , act for she as a keeper now , 
 to prepare her mind and heart for the come change in the relation 
 between she and I . when I last see she , six month ago , she be still 
 so playfully infantine that it half seem to I I should be sin 
 against the reverence due to a child , if I say too abruptly , " you be 
 woman , and I love you not as child but as woman . " and yet , time be 
 not allow to I for long , cautious , and gradual slide from the 
 relationship of friend into that of lover . I now understand what the 
 great master of my art once say to I , " a career be a destiny . " by one 
 of those merchant prince who now at Manchester , as they do once at 
 Genoa or Venice , reign alike over those two civilizer of the world 
 which to dull eye seem antagonistic , Art and Commerce , an offer be make 
 to I for a picture on a subject which strike his fancy : an offer so 
 magnificently liberal that his commerce must command my art ; and the 
 nature of the subject compel I to seek the bank of the Rhine as 
 soon as may be . I must have all the hue of the foliage in the meridian 
 glory of summer . I can but stay at Grasmere a very few day ; but 
 before I leave I must know this , be I go to work for Lily or be I 
 not ? on the answer to that question depend all . if not to work for she , 
 there would be no glory in the summer , no triumph in art to I : I refuse 
 the offer . if she say , " yes ; it be for I you work , " then she become 
 my destiny . she assure my career . here I speak as an artist : nobody who 
 be not an artist can guess how sovereign over even his moral being , at 
 a certain critical epoch in his career of artist or his life of man , 
 be the success or the failure of a single work . but I go on to speak as 
 man . my love for Lily be such for the last six month that , though if 
 she reject I I should still serve art , still yearn for fame , it would 
 be as an old man might do either . the youth of my life would be go . 

 as man I say , all my thought , all my dream of happiness , distinct from 
 art and fame , be sum up in the one question , " be Lily to be my wife 
 or not ? " 

  your affectionately , 

  W. M. 


 Kenelm return the letter without a word . 

 enrage by his silence , Mrs. Cameron exclaim , " now , sir , what say you ? 
 you have scarcely know Lily five week . what be the feverish fancy of 
 five week ' growth to the lifelong devotion of a man like this ? do you 
 now dare to say , ' I persist ' ? " 

 Kenelm wave his hand very quietly , as if to dismiss all conception of 
 taunt and insult and say with his soft melancholy eye fix upon the 
 working feature of Lily 's aunt , " this man be more worthy of she than 
 I. he pray you , in his letter , to prepare your niece for that change of

 you do so ? " 

 " I have ; the night I get the letter . " 

 " and - you hesitate ; speak truthfully , I implore . and she - "

 obey the voice of that prayer - ' she seem stunned at first , muttering , 
 ' this be a dream : it can not be true , - can not ! I Lion 's wife - I - I !

 child 's laugh , and put her arm round my neck , and say , ' you be

 Lion ask I whether I would willingly say or do anything that make he

 happiness for she with another , know that she have blight the life

 the only choice she can and ought to adopt , I resolve to visit you this 
 morning , and make that appeal to your reason and your heart which I have 
 do now , - not , I be sure , in vain . hush ! I hear his voice ! " 

 Melville enter the room , Lily lean on his arm . the artist 's comely 
 face be radiant with ineffable joyousness . leave Lily , he reach 
 Kenelm 's side as with a single bound , shake he heartily by the hand , 
 say , " I find that you have already be a welcome visitor in this 
 house . long may you be so , so say I , so   I answer for she   say my fair 
 betrothed , to whom I need not present you . " 

 Lily advance , and hold out her hand very timidly . Kenelm touch rather 
 than clasp it . his own strong hand tremble like a leaf . he venture 
 but one glance at her face . all the bloom have die out of it , but the 
 expression seem to he wondrously , cruelly tranquil . 

 " your betrothed ! your future bride ! " he say to the artist , with a 
 mastery over his emotion render less difficult by the single glance 
 at that tranquil face . " I wish you joy . all happiness to you , Miss 
 Mordaunt . you have make a noble choice . " 

 he look round for his hat ; it lie at his foot , but he do not see 
 it ; his eye wander away with uncertain vision , like those of a 
 sleep - walker . 

 Mrs. Cameron pick up the hat and give it to he . 

 " thank you , " he say meekly ; then with a smile half sweet , half bitter , 
 " I have so much to thank you for , Mrs. Cameron . " 

 " but you be not go already , - just as I enter too . hold ! Mrs. Cameron 
 tell I you be lodge with my old friend Jones . come and stop a 
 couple of day with we : we can find you a room ; the room over your 
 butterfly cage , eh , Fairy ? " 

 " thank you too . thank you all . no ; I must be in London by the first 
 train . " 

 speak thus , he have find his way to the door , bow with the quiet 
 grace that characterize all his movement , and be go . 

 " pardon his abruptness , Lily ; he too love ; he too be impatient to 
 find a betrothed , " say the artist gayly : " but now he know my dear 
 secret , I think I have a right to know his ; and I will try . " 

 he have scarcely utter the word before he too have quit the room and 
 overtaken Kenelm just at the threshold . 

 " if you be go back to Cromwell Lodge , - to pack up , I suppose , - let 
 I walk with you as far as the bridge . " 

 Kenelm incline his head assentingly and tacitly as they pass through 
 the garden - gate , wind backwards through the lane which skirt the 
 garden pale ; when , at the very spot in which the day after their first 
 and only quarrel Lily 's face have be see brighten through the 
 evergreen , that day on which the old woman , quit she , say , " God 
 bless you ! " and on which the vicar , walk with Kenelm , speak of her 
 fairy charm ; well , just in that spot Lily 's face appear again , not

 of the pale moon can be say to brighten . Kenelm see , start , halt . 
 his companion , then in the rush of a gladsome talk , of which Kenelm have 
 not hear a word , neither see nor halt ; he walk on mechanically , 
 gladsome , and talk . 

 Lily stretch forth her hand through the evergreen . Kenelm take it 
 reverentially . this time it be not his hand that tremble . 

 " good - by , " she say in a whisper , " good - by forever in this world . you 
 understand , - you do understand I . say that you do . " 

 " I understand . noble child ! noble choice ! God bless you ! God comfort 
 I ! " murmur Kenelm . their eye meet . oh , the sadness ; and , alas ! oh the 
 love in the eye of both ! 

 Kenelm pass on . 

 all say in an instant . how many all be say in an instant ! Melville 
 be in the midst of some glowing sentence , begin when Kenelm drop 
 from his side , and the end of the sentence be this : 

 " word can not say how fair seem life ; how easy seem conqu of fame , 
 date from this day - this day " - and in his turn he halt , look round 
 on the sunlit landscape , and breathe deep , as if to drink into his soul 
 all of the earth 's joy and beauty which his gaze could compass and the 
 arch of the horizon bind . 

 " they who know she even the good , " resume the artist , stride on , 
 " even her aunt , never could guess how serious and earnest , under all 
 her infantine prettiness of fancy , be that girl 's real nature . we be 
 walk along the brook - side , when I begin to tell how solitary the

 be under the shadow of the church in which we shall be married that

 of love with the sanctity of religion . " 

 Kenelm shudder , - the church , the burial - ground , the old Gothic tomb , 
 the flower round the infant 's grave ! 

 " but I be talk a great deal too much about myself , " resume the 
 artist . " lover be the most consummate of all egotist , and the 
 most garrulous of all gossip . you have wish I joy on my destine

 the artist would have small claim to the essential attribute of 
 genius - namely , the intuitive sympathy of passion with passion - if that 
 secret of Kenelm 's which he have so lightly say " he have acquire the

 CHAPTER VIII . 

 so , then , but for that officious warning , utter under the balcony 
 at Luscombe , Kenelm Chillingly might never have have a rival in Walter 
 Melville . but ill would any reader construe the character of Kenelm , do 
 he think that such a thought increase the bitterness of his sorrow . 
 no sorrow in the thought that a noble nature have be save from the 
 temptation to a great sin . 

 the good man do good merely by live . and the good he do may often 
 mar the plan he form for his own happiness . but he can not regret that 
 Heaven have permit he to do good . 

 what Kenelm do feel be perhaps well explain in the letter to Sir 
 Peter , which be here subjoin : - 


 " MY DEAREST FATHER , - never till my die day shall I forget that 
 tender desire for my happiness with which , overcome all worldly 
 consideration , no matter at what disappointment to your own cherish 
 plan or ambition for the heir to your name and race , you send I 
 away from your roof , these word ring in my ear like the sound of 
 joy - bell , ' choose as you will , with my blessing on your choice . I open 
 my heart to admit another child : your wife shall be my daughter . ' it 
 be such an unspeakable comfort to I to recall those word now . of all 
 human affection gratitude be surely the holy ; and it blend itself 
 with the sweetness of religion when it be gratitude to a father . and , 
 therefore , do not grieve too much for I , when I tell you that the hope 
 which enchant I when we part be not to be fulfil . her hand be 
 pledge to another , - another with claim upon her preference to which 
 mine can not be compare ; and he be himself , put aside the accident 
 of birth and fortune , immeasurably my superior . in that thought - I mean 
 the thought that the man she select deserve she more than I do , and 
 that in his happiness she will blend her own - I shall find comfort , so 
 soon as I can fairly reason down the first all - engross selfishness 
 that follow the sense of unexpected and irremediable loss . meanwhile 
 you will think it not unnatural that I resort to such aid for change 
 of heart as be afford by change of scene . I start for the continent 
 to - night , and shall not rest till I reach Venice , which I have not yet 
 see . I feel irresistibly attract towards still canal and gliding 
 gondola . I will write to you and to my dear mother the day I arrive . 
 and I trust to write cheerfully , with full account of all I see and 
 encounter . do not , dearest father , in your letter to I , revert or 
 allude to that grief which even the tender word from your own tender 
 self might but chafe into pain more sensitive . after all , a disappointed 
 love be a very common lot . and we meet every day , man - ay , and woman 
 too - who have know it , and be thoroughly cure . the manly of our 
 modern lyrical poet have say very nobly , and , no doubt , very justly , 


  " to bear be to conquer our fate . 


  " ever your love son , 

  " K. C. " 



 CHAPTER IX . 

 nearly a year and a half have elapse since the date of my last chapter . 
 two Englishmen be - the one seat , the other recline at length - on 
 one of the mound that furrow the ascent of Posilippo . before they 
 spread the noiseless sea , bask in the sunshine , without visible 
 ripple ; to the left there be a distant glimpse through gap of 
 brushwood of the public garden and white water of the Chiaja . they be 
 friend who have chance to meet abroad unexpectedly , joined company , and 
 travel together for many month , chiefly in the East . they have be 
 but a few day in Naples . the elder of the two have important affair in 
 England which ought to have summon he back long since . but he do not 
 let his friend know this ; his affair seem to he less important than 
 the duty he owe to one for whom he entertain that deep and noble 
 love which be something strong than brotherly , for with brotherly 
 affection it combine gratitude and reverence . he know , too , that 
 his friend be oppress by a haunting sorrow , of which the cause be 
 divine by one , not reveal by the other . 

 to leave he , so beloved , alone with that sorrow in strange land , be a 
 thought not to be cherish by a friend so tender ; for in the friendship 
 of this man there be that sort of tenderness which complete a nature , 
 thoroughly manlike , by give it a touch of the woman 's . 

 it be a day which in our northern climate be that of winter : in the 
 southern clime of Naples it be mild as an english summer day , linger 
 on the brink of autumn ; the sun slop towards the west , and already 
 gather around it roseate and purple fleece ; elsewhere the deep blue 
 sky be without a cloudlet . 

 both have be for some minute silent ; at length the man recline on 
 the grass - it be the young man - say suddenly , and with no previous 
 hint of the subject introduce , " Lay your hand on your heart , Tom , and 
 answer I truly . be your thought as clear from regret as the heaven 
 above we be from a cloud ? man take regret from tear that have cease 
 to flow , as the heavens take cloud from the rain that have cease to 
 fall . " 

 " Regrets ? ah , I understand , for the loss of the girl I once love to 
 distraction ! no ; surely I make that clear to you many , many , many month 
 ago , when I be your guest at Moleswich . " 

 " ay , but I have never , since then , speak to you on that subject . I do 
 not dare . it seem to I so natural that a man , in the early struggle 
 between love and reason , should say , ' reason shall conquer , and 
 have conquer ; ' and yet - and yet - as time glide on , feel that the 
 conqueror who can not put down rebellion have a very uneasy reign . 
 answer I not as at Moleswich , during the first struggle , but now , in 
 the after - day , when reaction from struggle come . " 

 " upon my honour , " answer the friend , " I have have no reaction at all . 
 I be cure entirely , when I have once see Jessie again , another man 's 
 wife , mother to his child , happy in her marriage ; and , whether she be 
 change or not , - very different from the sort of wife I should like to 
 marry , now that I be no long a village farrier . " 

 " and , I remember , you speak of some other girl whom it would suit you 
 to marry . you have be long abroad from she . do you ever think of 
 she , - think of she still as your future wife ? can you love she ? can you , 
 who have once love so faithfully , love again ? " 

 " I be sure of that . I love emily well than I do when I leave England . 
 we correspond . she write such nice letter . " Tom hesitate , blush , 
 and continue timidly , " I should like to show you one of her letter . " 

 " do . " 

 Tom draw forth the last of such letter from his breast - pocket . 

 Kenelm raise himself from the grass , take the letter , and read slowly , 
 carefully , while Tom watch in vain for some approve smile to 
 brighten up the dark beauty of that melancholy face . 

 certainly it be the letter a man in love might show with pride to a 
 friend : the letter of a lady , well educate , well bring up , evince 
 affection modestly , intelligence modestly too ; the sort of letter in 
 which a mother who love her daughter , and approve the daughter 's 
 choice , could not have suggest a correction . 

 as Kenelm give back the letter , his eye meet his friend 's . those be 
 eager eye , - eye hunger for praise . Kenelm 's heart smite he for 
 that bad of sin in friendship , - want of sympathy ; and that uneasy 
 heart force to his lip congratulation , not perhaps quite sincere , but 
 which amply satisfy the lover . in utter they , Kenelm rise to his 
 foot , throw his arm round his friend 's shoulder , and say , " be you not 
 tired of this place , Tom ? I be . let we go back to England to - morrow . " 
 Tom 's honest face brighten vividly . " how selfish and egotistical I 
 have be ! " continue Kenelm ; " I ought to have think more of you , your 
 career , your marriage , - pardon I - " 

 " Pardon you , - pardon ! do not   I owe to you all , - owe to you Emily herself ? 
 if you have never come to Graveleigh , never say , ' be my friend , ' what 
 should I have be now ? what - what ? " 

 the next day the two friend quit Naples en route for England , not 
 exchange many word by the way . the old loquacious crotchety humour 
 of Kenelm have desert he . a dull companion than he be you could not 
 have conceive . he might have be the hero of a young lady 's novel . 
 it be only when they part in London , that Kenelm evince more secret 
 purpose , more external emotion than one of his heraldic Daces shift 
 from the bed to the surface of a waveless pond . 

 " if I have rightly understand you , Tom , all this change in you , all this 
 cure of torture regret , be wrought , work lastingly , - work so as 
 to leave you heart - free for the world 's action and a home 's peace , on 
 that eve when you see she whose face till then have haunt you , another 
 man 's happy wife , and in so see she , either her face be change or 
 your heart become so . " 

 " quite true . I might express it otherwise , but the fact remain the 
 same . " 

 " God bless you , Tom ; bless you in your career without , in your home 
 within , " say Kenelm , wring his friend 's hand at the door of the 
 carriage that be to whirl to love and wealth and station the whilom 
 bully of a village , along the iron groove of that contrivance which , 
 though now the tritest of prosaic reality , seem once too poetical 
 for a poet 's wild vision . 



 CHAPTER X. 

 a WINTER 's evening at Moleswich . very different from a winter sunset 
 at Naples . it be intensely cold . there have be a slight fall of snow , 
 accompany with severe , bright , clean frost , a thin sprinkling of white 
 on the pavement . Kenelm Chillingly enter the town on foot , no long 
 a knapsack on his back . pass through the main street , he pause a 
 moment at the door of Will Somers . the shop be closed . no , he would 
 not stay there to ask in a roundabout way for news . he would go in 
 straightforwardly and manfully to Grasmere . he would take the inmate 
 there by surprise . the soon he could bring Tom 's experience home 
 to himself , the well . he have school his heart to rely on that 
 experience , and it bring he back the old elasticity of his stride . 
 in his lofty carriage and buoyant face be again visible the old 
 haughtiness of the indifferentism that keep itself aloof from the 
 turbulent emotion and conventional frivolity of those whom its 
 philosophy pity and scorn . 

 " ha ! ha ! " laugh he who like Swift never laugh aloud , and often 
 laugh inaudibly . " ha ! ha ! I shall exorcise the ghost of my grief . I 
 shall never be haunt again . if that stormy creature whom love might 
 have madden into crime , if he be cure of love at once by a single 
 visit to the home of she whose face be change to he , - for the smile 
 and the tear of it have become the property of another man , - how much 
 more should I be leave without a scar ! I , the heir of the Chillinglys ! 
 I , the kinsman of a Mivers ! I , the pupil of a Welby ! I - I , Kenelm 
 Chillingly , to be thus - thus - " here , in the midst of his boastful 
 soliloquy , the well - remember brook rush suddenly upon eye and ear , 
 gleam and moan under the wintry moon . Kenelm Chillingly stop , 
 cover his face with his hand , and burst into a passion of tear . 

 recover himself slowly , he go on along the path , every step of 
 which be haunt by the form of Lily . he reach the garden gate of 
 Grasmere , lift the latch , and enter . as he do so , a man , touch 
 his hat , rush beside , and advance before he , - the village postman . 
 Kenelm draw back , allow the man to pass to the door , and as he thus

 hear Lily speak of her guardian . 

 the postman leave his letter , and regain the garden gate , while

 himself , " let I just see she and her happiness , and then I will knock 
 boldly at the door , and say , ' good - evening , Mrs. Melville . ' " 

 so Kenelm steal across the lawn , and , station himself at the angle of 
 the wall , look into the window . 

 Melville , in dressing - robe and slipper , be seat alone by the 
 fireside . his dog be lazily stretch on the hearth rug . one by one the 
 feature of the room , as the scene of his vanish happiness , grow out 
 from its stillness ; the delicately tinted wall , the dwarf bookcase , 
 with its feminine ornament on the upper shelf ; the piano stand in 
 the same place . Lily 's own small low chair ; that be not in its old 
 place , but thrust into a remote angle , as if it have pass into disuse . 
 Melville be read a letter , no doubt one of those which the postman 
 have leave . surely the content be pleasant , for his fair face , always 
 frankly expressive of emotion , brighten wonderfully as he read on . 
 then he rise with a quick , brisk movement , and pull the bell hastily . 

 a neat maid - servant enter , - a strange face to Kenelm . Melville give 
 she some brief message . " he have have joyous news , " think Kenelm . " he 
 have send for his wife that she may share his joy . " presently the door 
 open , and enter not Lily , but Mrs. Cameron . 

 she look change . her natural quietude of mien and movement the same , 
 indeed , but with more languor in it . her hair have become gray . Melville 
 be stand by the table as she approach he . he put the letter into 
 her hand with a gay , proud smile , and look over her shoulder while 
 she read it , point with his finger as to some line that should more 
 emphatically claim her attention . 

 when she have finish her face reflect his smile . they exchange a 
 hearty shake of the hand , as if in congratulation . 

 " ah , " think Kenelm , " the letter be from Lily . she be abroad . perhaps 
 the birth of a first - bear . " 

 just then Blanche , who have not be visible before , emerge from under 
 the table , and as Melville reseat himself by the fireside , spring into 
 his lap , rub herself against his breast . the expression of his face 
 change ; he utter some low exclamation . Mrs. Cameron take the creature 
 from his lap , stroke it quietly , carry it across the room , and put 
 it outside the door . then she seat herself beside the artist , place 
 her hand in his , and they converse in low tone , till Melville 's face 
 again grow bright , and again he take up the letter . 

 a few minute later the maid - servant enter with the tea - thing , 
 and after arrange they on the table approach the window . Kenelm 
 retreat into the shade , the servant close the shutter and draw the 
 curtain ; that scene of quiet home comfort vanish from the eye of the 
 looker - on . 

 Kenelm feel strangely perplexed . what have become of Lily ? be she indeed 
 absent from her home ? have he conjecture rightly that the letter 
 which have evidently so gladdened Melville be from she , or be 
 it possible - here a thought of joy seize his heart and hold he 
 breathless - be it possible that , after all , she have not marry her 
 guardian ; have find a home elsewhere , - be free ? he move on far 
 down the lawn , towards the water , that he might well bring before his 
 sight that part of the irregular building in which Lily formerly have she 
 sleep - chamber , and her " own - own room . " 

 all be dark there ; the shutter inexorably close . the place with which 
 the childlike girl have associate she most childlike fancy , taming and 
 tend the honey - drinker destine to pass into fairy , that fragile 
 tenement be not close against the wind and snow ; its door be 
 drearily open ; gap in the delicate wire - work ; of its dainty draperie a 
 few tattered shred hang here and there ; and on the depopulated floor 
 the moonbeam rest cold and ghostly . no spray from the tiny fountain ; 
 its basin chip and mouldering ; the scanty water therein frozen . of 
 all the pretty wild one that Lily fancy she could tame , not one . ah ! 
 yes , there be one , probably not of the old familiar number ; a stranger 
 that might have creep in for shelter from the first blast of 
 winter , and now clung to an angle in the farth wall , its wing 
 fold , - asleep , not dead . but Kenelm see it not ; he notice only the 
 general desolation of the spot . 

 " natural enough , " think he . " she have outgrow all such pretty 
 silliness . a wife can not remain a child . still , if she have belong to 
 I - " the thought choke even his inward , unspoken utterance . he turn 
 away , pause a moment under the leafless bough of the great willow 
 still dip into the brook , and then with impatient step stride back 
 towards the garden gate . 

 " no , - no , - no . I can not now enter that house and ask for Mrs. Melville . 
 trial enough for one night to stand on the old ground . I will return to 
 the town . I will call at Jessie 's , and there I can learn if she indeed 
 be happy . " 

 so he go on by the path along the brook - side , the night momently 
 colder and colder , and momently clear and clear , while the moon 
 noiselessly glide into loftier height . wrap in his abstract 
 thought , when he come to the spot in which the path split in twain , 
 he do not take that which lead more directly to the town . his step , 
 naturally enough follow the train of his thought , lead he along 
 the path with which the object of his thought be associate . he find 
 himself on the burial - ground , and in front of the old ruin tomb with 
 the effaced inscription . 

 " ah ! child ! child ! " he murmur almost audibly , " what depth of woman 
 tenderness lie conceal in thee ! in what love sympathy with the 
 past - sympathy only vouchsafe to the tender woman and the high 
 poet - didst thou lie thy flower on the tomb , to which thou didst give 
 a poet 's history interpret by a woman 's heart , little dream that 
 beneath the stone sleep a hero of thine own fallen race . " 

 he pass beneath the shadow of the yew , whose leave no winter wind 
 can strew , and pause at the ruin tomb , - no flower now on its stone , 
 only a sprinkling of snow at the foot of it , - sprinkling of snow at the 
 foot of each humbler grave - mound . motionless in the frosty air rest 
 the pointed church - spire , and through the frosty air , high and high 
 up the arch of heaven , soar the unpausing moon . around and below and 
 above she , the star which no science can number ; yet not less difficult 
 to number be the thought , desire , aspiration which , in a space of 
 time briefer than a winter 's night , can pass through the infinite deep 
 of a human soul . 

 from his stand by the Gothic tomb , Kenelm look along the churchyard 
 for the infant 's grave which Lily 's pious care have border with votive 
 flower . yes , in that direction there be still a gleam of colour ; could 
 it be of flower in that bite winter time ? - the moon be so deceptive , 
 it silver into the hue of the jessamine the green of the everlasting . 

 he pass towards the white grave - mound . his sight have dupe he ; no 
 pale flower , no green " everlasting " on its neglect border , - only brown 
 mould , wither stalk , streak of snow . 

 " and yet , " he say sadly , ' she tell I she have never break a promise ; 
 and she have give a promise to the die child . ah ! she be too happy now 
 to think of the dead . " 

 so murmur , he be about to turn towards the town , when close by 
 that child 's grave he see another . round that other there be pale 
 " everlasting , " dwarf blossom of the laurestinus ; at the four angle 
 the droop bud of a Christmas rise ; at the head of the grave be a 
 white stone , its sharp edge cut into the starlit air ; and on the 
 head , in fresh letter , be inscribe these word : - 


  to the Memory of 
  L. M. 
  Aged 17 , 
  die October 29 , a. D. 18 - , 
  this stone , above the grave to which her mortal 
  remain be consign , beside that of an infant not 
  more sinless , be consecrate by those who 
  most mourn and miss she , 
  ISABEL CAMERON , 
  WALTER MELVILLE . 
  " suffer the little child to come unto I . " 



 CHAPTER XI . 

 the next morning Mr. Emlyn , pass from his garden to the town of 
 Moleswich , descry a human form stretch on the burial - ground , 
 stir restlessly but very slightly , as if with an involuntary shiver , 
 and utter broken sound , very faintly hear , like the moan that a 
 man in pain strive to suppress and can not . 

 the rector hasten to the spot . the man be lie , his face downward , 
 on a grave - mound , not dead , not asleep . 

 " poor fellow overtake by drink , I fear , " think the gentle pastor ; and 
 as it be the habit of his mind to compassionate error even more than 
 grief , he accost the suppose sinner in very soothing tone - try to 
 raise he from the ground - and with very kindly word . 

 then the man lift his face from its pillow on the grave - mound , look 
 round he dreamily into the gray , blank air of the cheerless morn , 
 and rise to his foot quietly and slowly . the vicar be startle ; he 
 recognize the face of he he have last see in the magnificent affluence 
 of health and strength . but the character of the face be change , - so 
 change ! its old serenity of expression , at once grave and sweet , 
 succeed by a wild trouble in the heavy eyelid and tremble lip . 

 " Mr. Chillingly , - you ! be it possible ? " 

 " Varus , Varus , " exclaim Kenelm , passionately , " what hast thou do 
 with my legion ? " 

 at that quotation of the well - know greeting of Augustus to his 
 unfortunate general , the scholar recoil . have his young friend 's mind 
 desert he , - daze , perhaps , by over - study ? 

 he be soon reassure ; Kenelm 's face settle back into calm , though a 
 dreary calm , like that of the wintry day . 

 " I beg pardon , Mr. Emlyn ; I have not quite shake off the hold of a 
 strange dream . I dream that I be bad off than Augustus : he do not 
 lose the world when the legion he have trust to another vanish into 
 a grave . " 

 here Kenelm link his arm in that of the rector , - on which he lean 
 rather heavily , - and draw he on from the burial - ground into the open 
 space where the two path meet . 

 " but how long have you return to Moleswich ? " ask Emlyn ; " and how 
 come you to choose so damp a bed for your morning slumber ? " 

 " the wintry cold creep into my vein when I stand in the burial - ground , 
 and I be very weary ; I have no sleep at night . do not let I take you 
 out of your way ; I be go on to Grasmere . so I see , by the record on a 
 gravestone , that it be more than a year ago since Mr. Melville lose his 
 wife . " 

 " Wife ? he never marry . " 

 " what ! " cry Kenelm . " Whose , then , be that gravestone , - ' L. M. ' ? " 

 " alas ! it be our poor Lily 's . " 

 " and she die unmarried ? " 

 as Kenelm say this he look up , and the sun break out from the 
 gloomy haze of the morning . " I may claim thee , then , " he think within 
 himself , " claim thee as mine when we meet again . " 

 " unmarried , - yes , " resume the vicar . " she be indeed betroth to her 
 guardian ; they be to have be marry in the autumn , on his return 
 from the Rhine . he go there to paint on the spot itself his great 
 picture , which be now so famous , - ' Roland , the Hermit Knight , look 
 towards the convent lattice for a sight of the Holy Nun . ' Melville have 
 scarcely go before the symptom of the disease which prove fatal to 
 poor Lily betray themselves ; they baffle all medical skill , - rapid 
 decline . she be always very delicate , but no one detect in her the 
 seed of consumption . Melville only return a day or two before her 
 death . dear childlike Lily ! how we all mourn for she ! - not least the 
 poor , who believe in her fairy charm . " 

 " and least of all , it appear , the man she be to have marry . " 

 " he ? - Melville ? how can you wrong he so ? his grief be 
 intense - overpowering - for the time . " 

 " for the time ! what time ? " mutter Kenelm , in tone too low for the 
 pastor 's ear . 

 they move on silently . Mr. Emlyn resume , - 

 " you notice the text on Lily 's gravestone - ' suffer the little child 
 to come unto I ' ? she dictate it herself the day before she die . I be 
 with she then , so I be at the last . " 

 " be you - be you - at the last - the last ? good - day , Mr. Emlyn ; we 
 be just in sight of the garden gate . and - excuse I - I wish to see Mr. 
 Melville alone . " 

 " well , then , good - day ; but if you be make any stay in the 
 neighbourhood , will you not be our guest ? we have a room at your 
 service . " 

 " I thank you gratefully ; but I return to London in an hour or so . hold , 
 a moment . you be with she at the last ? she be resign to die ? " 

 " resign ! that be scarcely the word . the smile leave upon her lip be 
 not that of human resignation : it be the smile of a divine joy . " 



 CHAPTER XII . 

 " yes , sir , Mr. Melville be at home in his studio . " 

 Kenelm follow the maid across the hall into a room not build at the 
 date of Kenelm 's former visit to the house : the artist , make Grasmere 
 his chief residence after Lily 's death , have add it at the back of 
 the neglect place wherein Lily have encage " the soul of infant 
 unbaptized . " 

 a lofty room , with a casement partially darken , to the bleak north ; 
 various sketch on the wall ; gaunt specimen of antique furniture , 
 and of gorgeous italian silk , scatter about in confused disorder ; 
 one large picture on its easel curtain ; another as large , and half 
 finish , before which stand the painter . he turn quickly , as Kenelm 
 enter the room unannounced , let fall brush and palette , come up to he 
 eagerly , grasp his hand , droop his head on Kenelm 's shoulder , and

 " I know it ; I have see her grave . let we not speak of it . why 
 so needlessly revive your sorrow ? so - so - your sanguine hope be 
 fulfil : the world at last have do you justice ? Emlyn tell I that 
 you have paint a very famous picture . " 

 Kenelm have seat himself as he thus speak . the painter still stand with 
 deject attitude on the middle of the floor , and brush his hand 
 over his moistened eye once or twice before he answer , " yes , wait a 
 moment , do not   talk of fame yet . Bear with I . the sudden sight of you 
 unnerve I . " 

 the artist here seat himself also on an old worm - eat Gothic chest , 
 rumple and chafe the golden or tinselled thread of the embroider 
 silk , so rare and so time - wear , fling over the Gothic chest , so rare 
 also , and so worm - eat . 

 Kenelm look through half - close lid at the artist , and his lip , 
 before slightly curve with a secret scorn , become gravely compress . 
 in Melville 's struggle to conceal emotion the strong man recognize a 
 strong man , - recognize , and yet only wonder ; wonder how such a man , 
 to whom Lily have pledge her hand , could so soon after the loss of Lily
